pathways.title: Introduction to Genetic Engineering: The Process
pathways.description: This pathway outlines the process of genetic engineering with a focus on gene cloning. This pathway also provides an introduction for the ABE lab series.
learning_objectives: Identify the common features of human and bacterial cells that make genetic engineering possible.
Outline the steps of gene cloning and identify the importance of each step to the process.
Describe an example of how gene cloning is used to treat a specific disease, such as diabetes.
Apply an understanding of the gene cloning process to evaluate its use as a therapy for a particular disease.

pathways.title: Tools & Techniques in Biotechnology: Gel Electrophoresis
pathways.description: This pathway introduces gel electrophoresis, a technique used to separate biological molecules. This pathway also supports ABE lab 1.2: Gel Electrophoresis.
learning_objectives: Explain how gel electrophoresis separates biological molecules based on size and charge.
Identify and explain the role of each piece of equipment required to conduct gel electrophoresis.
Demonstrate how to separate dye molecules using gel electrophoresis.
Interpret a gel to determine the relative sizes of molecules separated by gel electrophoresis.

pathways.title: Tools & Techniques in Biotechnology: Micropipetting
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the micropipette, an instrument used to measure small volumes of liquid, and provides an opportunity to practice using this tool. This pathway also supports ABE Lab 1.1: How to Use a Micropipette.
learning_objectives: Identify the parts of a micropipette.
Describe situations when a micropipette is needed to measure volume.
Model how to set the volume and read the volume window of a micropipette.
Demonstrate how to use a micropipette to transfer small volumes of liquid.

pathways.title: Coronavirus: From Bats to Humans to Pandemic
pathways.description: What do bats have to do with coronaviruses? As it turns out, the adaptation to flight may be what turned bats into perfect virus reservoirs. One of these viruses, the coronavirus SARS-CoV-2 causing COVID-19, likely jumped the species barrier to humans causing the initial 2019 outbreak in China. This pathway reviews the history of the outbreak from the perspective of medical professionals working to accurately diagnose and treat patients, scientists racing to understand the source of the outbreak, and public health officials implementing solutions to control its spread.
learning_objectives: Summarize the coronavirus outbreak in 2019 from a public health point of view.
Describe the lifecycle of a coronavirus.
Chart the path of a coronavirus patient in a hospital.
Examine how bats became perfect reservoirs for viruses.
Explain how the adaption to flight required bats to adjust their immune system.

pathways.title: Introduction to Genetic Engineering: Recombinant Plasmids
pathways.description: This pathway provides an overview of recombinant plasmids and how they can be modified to introduce new traits into an organism. This pathway can support ABE Labs 2, 2a, 3, 4 and 4a.
learning_objectives: Describe what a plasmid is and its role in bacterial cells.
Label the key features of a recombinant plasmid.
Explain the function of each part of a plasmid vector.
Explain the role of restriction enzymes in creating a recombinant plasmid.
Design a recombinant plasmid to express a protein of interest.

pathways.title: Tools & Techniques in Biotechnology: Bacterial Transformation
pathways.description: This pathway outlines how genetic information is added to cells in the technique called transformation. This pathway also supports ABE Lab 5.
learning_objectives: Describe different ways that bacteria can acquire new DNA.
Explain how the heat shock process allows DNA to enter bacterial cells.
Judge whether a transformation experiment was successful by evaluating the growth of bacteria on selective media.

pathways.title: Building a Recombinant Plasmid: Restriction Enzymes
pathways.description: This pathway explains how restriction enzymes are used in gene cloning to obtain the DNA pieces that will create a recombinant plasmid. This pathway supports ABE labs 2, 2a, 3, 4, and 4a.
learning_objectives: Explain how restriction enzymes cut dna.
Explain why restriction enzymes are a valuable tool in genetic engineering.
Describe how conditions such as temperature and ph affect the activity of restriction enzymes.
Choose the best restriction enzyme for a given application.

pathways.title: The Scientific Method
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the scientific method as the fundamental concept of universal problem solving in general and the scientific process in particular. It illustrates how scientific inquiry should be conducted. Multiple intuitive interactions illustrate this foundational concept that applies to anyone conducting investigations or solving problems.
learning_objectives: Explain the scientific method: a systematic approach for scientific inquiry.
Justify why the scientific method is an iterative process that alternates between observation and experimentation
Develop a hypothesis to explain an observation.
Design the most appropriate experimental setup to test a hypothesis.

pathways.title: Biotechnology
pathways.description:
This pathway introduces how DNA can be manipulated using different molecular tools and techniques. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathway Biotechnology and Genomics from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Explain the use of genetic engineering techniques like gel electrophoresis and dna sequencing in analyzing dna.
Explain the use of genetic engineering techniques like pcr and gene cloning in manipulating dna.

pathways.title: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
pathways.description: Polymerase Chain Reaction - or PCR - is known as one of the most ubiquitous and powerful techniques in life science laboratories. It is a highly sensitive and selective method to produce many copies of a particular piece of DNA. This pathway details applications of PCR, its mechanism and primer design, and demonstrates how the method is used in the lab.
learning_objectives: Summarize the principle of pcr.
Explain the mechanism and primer design of pcr.
Identify the steps of experimental pcr in a method video.

pathways.title: Verifying a Recombinant Plasmid: Gel Electrophoresis
pathways.description: This pathway demonstrates how a fundamental tool in biotechnology, DNA gel electrophoresis, can be used to verify successful production of a recombinant plasmid. This pathway supports ABE labs 4 and 4a.
learning_objectives: Describe the interactions that give dna its unique helical structure.
Explain how gel electrophoresis can be used to sort dna molecules by size.
Use gel electrophoresis to evaluate whether the desired recombinant plasmid has been created from a ligation experiment.

pathways.title: Introduction to Genome Editing with CRISPR-Cas9
pathways.description: CRISPR-Cas9 is a groundbreaking gene editing tool with the promise to cure diseases, improve agriculture, and revolutionize environmental engineering. This pathway introduces the CRISPR-Cas9 mechanism, which is applied to disrupt a gene to confer HIV resistance of human immune cells.
learning_objectives: Summarize the potential applications of the gene editing tool crispr-cas9.
Recall the origin of the gene editing tool crispr-cas9 as a bacterial defense mechanism.
Examine the molecular mechanism of crispr-cas9.
Summarize crispr-triggered dna repair mechanisms.

pathways.title: Building a Recombinant Plasmid: DNA Ligase
pathways.description: This pathway describes how DNA ligase, a key enzyme in DNA replication, is used in gene cloning to create a recombinant plasmid. This pathway supports ABE lab 3: Building a Recombinant Plasmid.
learning_objectives: Explain how dna ligase joins two dna strands together.
Justify why dna ligase is an important tool in genetic engineering.
Predict the recombinant plasmids that will form when two dna fragments with complementary sticky ends are connected by dna ligase.

pathways.title: DNA Replication and Central Dogma
pathways.description:
This pathway includes an introduction to how genetic information is replicated and expressed to form proteins. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathways DNA Structure and Function and Genes and Proteins from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Describe how information flows from dna to rna to protein.
Explain how an organisms phenotype is determined by its genotype.

pathways.title: Tools & Techniques in Biotechnology: Column Chromatography
pathways.description: This pathway will show you how the technique of column chromatography can be used to purify a protein product. This pathway supports ABE lab 6.
learning_objectives: Summarize why proteins need to be purified from cells in order to be used.
Describe the role of lysis and centrifugation in the process of protein purification.
Explain how column chromatography works to separate proteins from a mixture.

pathways.title: Introduction to Genetic Engineering: The Role of Cells
pathways.description: This pathway will help learners better understand the critical role that bacterial cells play as protein production factories in the process of gene cloning. The processes of binary fission, transcription and translation are all essential for this role and are explored in detail here. For those using this pathway as part of the Amgen Biotech Experience, this pathway is a good companion to ABE Lab 5.
learning_objectives: Explain how the process of genetic cloning takes place.
Infer the scale and breadth of the potential for DNA cloning and the use of bacterial cells as "factories" to solve real world problems.

pathways.title: Critical Reading of Scientific Papers
pathways.description: An essential part of scientific literacy is the critical reading and analysis of research papers. This pathway dissects each section of a research article to help the learner understand its logic and structure.
learning_objectives: Define the purpose of scientific articles.
Outline the structure of a scientific article.
Explain the purpose of each of the eight sections of a research paper.
Judge the quality of the content of each section of a research paper.

pathways.title: Introduction to Genetic Engineering: The Product
pathways.description: This pathway is a review of the structure of proteins and how they are made within cells during the process of genetic engineering. This pathway is a good companion to ABE Lab 6.
learning_objectives: Describe how proteins are used inside cells.
Explain how protein structure relates to function.
Describe how recombinant proteins are being used in science.

pathways.title: Protein Expression in Gene-edited Cells - Western Blotting
pathways.description: Western blotting is a common technique used to visualize proteins and determine protein expression in cells. In this pathway, CRISPR-Cas9 edited macrophages are tested for the expression of the CCR5 protein.
learning_objectives: Describe and interpret protein gel electrophoresis and western blotting techniques.
Analyze the protein expression of crispr-edited cell lines with the help of a western blot.
Interpret the results of a western blot analyzing the protein expression of ccr5.

pathways.title: Macromolecules
pathways.description:
This pathway provides an overview of biological macromolecules and their properties.  The resources in this pathway can be used to introduce or review the structure and function of lipids, carbohydrates, proteins, and nucleic acids. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathway Biological Macromolecules from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Describe the properties of the monomers and the types of bonds that connect the monomers in biological macromolecules..
Explain the role of biological macromolecules in living systems.

pathways.title: Data Literacy: How to Interpret Biological Data
pathways.description: This pathway introduces users to some of the most common types of data used in biology.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Interpret gel electrophoresis data, dna sequencing chromatogram data, sequence alignments, and model figures.
Critique gel electrophoresis data, dna sequencing chromatogram data, sequence alignments, and model figures.
Predict gel electrophoresis data for a given experiment.
Locate common errors when reading gel electrophoresis data, dna sequencing chromatogram data, and model figures.
Use sequence alignment tools to generate your own alignments.
Illustrate your hypotheses using the conventions of biological model figures.

pathways.title: Enzymes
pathways.description:
This pathway provides an introduction to enzymes, their properties, and how they affect biological reactions. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathway Metabolism from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Describe the properties of enzymes.
Explain how enzymes work to affect the rate of biological reactions..
Predict the impact of environmental factors on enzyme function.

pathways.title: Teach with LabXchange
pathways.description: This pathway is designed to support educators in using the LabXchange platform with learners. The pathway provides an overview of LabXchange and tutorials for the features educators will need, such as how to create a class and enroll learners, how to find and assign relevant content for learners, and how to add their own content to their private library.
learning_objectives: Identify ways you can use LabXchange with your learners.
Compare/contrast the differences between educator and learner accounts on LabXchange.
Describe how to create and use a private class on LabXchange to assign content, monitor learner progress and facilitate online discussions.
Explain how to identify and design content relevant for your learners and learning objectives.

pathways.title: Vaccine Development
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the concept of vaccination, how vaccines are developed, and the purpose of clinical trials during vaccine development. While true for the development of vaccines against many viruses, the coronavirus SARS-CoV-2—which causes COVID-19—serves as an example.
learning_objectives: Describe what a vaccine is and the molecular basis of how vaccination works.
Summarize the importance of vaccination and the utility of different types of vaccines.
Explain the purpose of the phases of clinical and preclinical trials during vaccine development and which steps can be accelerated safely.
Describe how a vaccine prepares the immune system to combat viral infection.
Predict how to design experiments to develop a safe and effective vaccine against sars-cov-2.
Design a method to develop a coronavirus vaccine.
Interpret elisa data to evaluate the best vaccine candidate.

pathways.title: Teach Lab Skills Remotely
pathways.description: This pathway introduces you to the virtual lab simulations available on LabXchange, and how these simulations can be used to teach lab skills like gel electrophoresis.
learning_objectives: Describe how a virtual simulation can be used to help students explore and understand a lab technique like gel electrophoresis.

pathways.title: Genetic Disorders
pathways.description:
This pathway provides an introduction to genetic disorders including examining the different ways genetic information may be changed and specific examples of genetic disease. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathway Modern Understandings of Inheritance from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Explain how mutations in chromosomal dna can lead to disease.
Describe examples of different genetic diseases and their etiology.

pathways.title: Nucleic Acids
pathways.description:
This pathway provides an introduction to nucleic acids and their role in providing heritable information in cells. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathway DNA Structure and Function from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Describe the structural similarities and differences between DNA and RNA.

pathways.title: Cellular Transport
learning_objectives: Describe the fluid mosaic model of a cell membrane and the roles of each component.
Explain why cell membranes are selectively permeable.
Compare/contrast the processes of passive and active transport.
Explain how concentration gradients influence movement of materials across the membrane and how this affects both cells and organisms.

pathways.title: Gene Regulation
pathways.description:
This pathway includes an introduction to the ways that gene expression is regulated in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathway Gene Regulation from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Describe the types of interactions that regulated gene expression in prokaryotes.
Describe the types of interactions that regulated gene expression in eukaryotes.
Compare/contrast types of operons.

pathways.title: Exploring Biotechnology
pathways.description: This pathway explores innovations in biotechnology that have revolutionized aspects of modern life like agriculture, forensics and medicine.
learning_objectives: Explain explain the use of genetic engineering techniques like gel electrophoresis and dna sequencing in analyzing dna.
Explain the use of genetic engineering techniques like pcr and gene cloning in manipulating dna.
Examine some of the ethical considerations around manipulating dna.

pathways.title: Proteins
pathways.description: This pathway explores the structure and folding of proteins. Proteins are large biomolecules, or macromolecules, consisting of one or more long chains of amino acid residues. Proteins perform a vast array of functions within organisms, including catalyzing metabolic reactions, DNA replication, responding to stimuli, providing structure to cells and organisms, and transporting molecules from one location to another. Proteins differ from one another primarily in their sequence of amino acids, which is dictated by the nucleotide sequence of their genes, and which usually results in protein folding into a specific three-dimensional structure that determines its activity.
learning_objectives: Describe the four levels of protein structure.
Explain the thermodynamic factors that influence protein folding.
Discuss the kinetics of protein folding.

pathways.title: Cellular Structure and Function
pathways.description:
This pathway provides an introduction to cells as the basic unit of life and their structure and function. Prokaryotic, eukaryotic and plant cells are compared and the structure and function of different organelles are discussed. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathway Cell Structure from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Describe the structure and function of the parts of prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
Compare/contrast the structure of prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
Discuss the possible origin of eukaryotic cells from engulfed prokaryotic cells (endosymbiosis theory).

pathways.title: Research Integrity and Scientific Misconduct
pathways.description: This pathway spreads awareness of scientific misconduct and introduces how it can be countered by research integrity.
learning_objectives: Explain the purpose of research integrity.
Identify the characteristics of scientific misconduct.
Outline the loss of trust in science as the most serious consequences of breaches of scientific integrity.

pathways.title: Cell Cycle and Cancer
pathways.description:
This pathway provides an introduction to the cell cycle and how its regulation is important to normal cell division. Cancer is often caused by unregulated cell division. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathway Cell Reproduction from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Describe how the cell cycle regulates cell division in mitosis.
Describe how the cell cycle is regulated by checkpoints.
Explain how disruption of the cell cycle regulation can lead to diseases such as cancer.

pathways.title: Aseptic Techniques in the Laboratory
pathways.description: Microorganisms are found all around us; on our hands, in the air, and on surfaces. Not all microorganisms are harmful to us, but some can cause disease. It is important to limit the spread of unwanted microorganisms, especially when working in the laboratory or with susceptible patients. To do this we can use aseptic techniques. In this pathway we will provide some of the key strategies of aseptic techniques, including personal protective equipment, setting up a clean workspace, and maintaining sterility while working.
learning_objectives: Describe how to put on personal protective equipment.
Explain how aseptic techniques can be used in the lab to transfer microbes between liquid media and from a liquid medium to a solid medium.
Explain how aseptic techniques can be used in the lab to transfer a single colony from a solid medium to another solid medium.
List the different ways we can control the growth of unwanted microorganisms.

pathways.title: Biotechnology and Genomics
pathways.description: Some of the greatest accomplishments of biotechnology are in the fields of medicine and medical research. Scientists have recently developed a way to engineer human intestines from human cells using mice. This could be the breakthrough needed to help patients suffering from intestinal failure. This pathway provides an in-depth look at biotechnology; mapping genomes; whole-genome sequencing; applying genomics; and genomics & proteomics. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Name three types of gene sequencing.
Discuss whole-genome sequencing.
Define pharmacogenomics.
Describe an example of a polygenic human disease.
Identify examples of basic techniques used to manipulate genetic material (dna and rna).
Describe the difference between molecular and reproductive cloning.
Define a proteome.
Identify examples of uses of biotechnology in medicine and agriculture.
Describe a protein signature and discuss its relevance to cancer screening.
Describe genomics.
Define a genetic map.
Describe an example of a genomic mapping method.
Name three types of gene sequencing.
Discuss whole-genome sequencing.
Define pharmacogenomics.
Describe an example of a polygenic human disease.
Define a proteome.
Describe a protein signature and discuss its relevance to cancer screening.

pathways.title: Heredity
pathways.description:
This pathway provides an introduction to heredity and how the biological process of meiosis leads to genetic diversity. The foundational concepts of Mendelian genetics are also reviewed. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathways Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction, Mendel's Experiments and Heredity and Modern Understandings of Inheritance from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Explain how the process of meiosis transmits genetic information to offspring.
Compare/contrast the processes of mitosis and meiosis.
Explain how the process of meiosis leads to genetic diversity.
Describe how the inheritance of genes and traits is explained by mendel's laws.

pathways.title: Cells
pathways.description: This pathway explores the different types of cells, including eukaryotic, prokaryotic and plant cells, and their structures.
learning_objectives: Describe describe the structure and function of the parts of prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells that are common to both.
Compare/contrast compare/contrast the structure of eukaryotic, prokaryotic and plant cells.

pathways.title: Design and Testing of Guide RNAs for CRISPR-Cas9 Gene Editing
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the design of guide RNAs to disrupt a macrophage gene with the gene editing tool CRISPR-Cas9. Afterwards, a DNA cleavage assay is created to test how well the designed gRNAs work.
learning_objectives: Use your knowledge of guide rnas to design three targeting the human ccr5 gene.
Design an assay to test how well your designed grnas facilitate the cleaving of the target dna ccr5.
Devise an experiment to test how well your designed grnas facilitate the cleaving of the target dna ccr5.

pathways.title: The Atom
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the building block of all matter, the atom, and some of the key experiments that helped scientists discover the subatomic particles that give atoms their unique properties.
learning_objectives: Describe how an element is determined by the number of protons in the nucleus, which becomes the atomic number.
Predict the atomic mass from the number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus.
Explain that isotopes of an element have the same atomic number, but different atomic masses.
Calculate the electron number of a neutral atom based on the atomic number and the charge of an ion based on the number of protons and electrons.
Describe the structure of an atom and where the subatomic particles are located.

pathways.title: Water
pathways.description:
This pathway provides an introduction to the structure of water and properties of water, such as hydrogen bonding, that make it essential for life. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathway The Chemical Foundation of Life from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Explain how the properties of water that result from its polarity and hydrogen bonding affect its biological function.

pathways.title: Cell Communication
pathways.description:
This pathway provides an introduction to cellular mechanisms of communication, highlighting one of the primary mechanisms: signal transduction pathways. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathway Cell Communication from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Describe different mechanisms cells use to communicate.
Explain how a signal is propagated between the different components of a signaling cascade.
Discuss examples of responses that result from a signaling cascade.

pathways.title: Sequencing of Target DNA After CRISPR-Cas9 Gene Editing
pathways.description: Immune cell genes are sequenced after gene editing with CRISPR-Cas9. The goal is to identify a disrupted CCR5 gene in edited macrophages. Macrophages that don’t express CCR5 are resistant to HIV infection.
learning_objectives: Describe codons and reading frames and use the codon wheel.
Identify insertions, deletions, and frameshifts in gene-edited dna.
Interpret the consequences of crispr-editing concerning the reading frame of the edited gene.
Examine sequencing data to choose a cell line with a disrupted ccr5 gene after gene editing.

pathways.title: The Basics of DNA
pathways.description: Our DNA encodes the information that, in combination with our environment, makes us unique individuals. Learn about the structure of DNA and how it influences our traits and health in this pathway.

pathways.title: Biotechnology
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at cloning and genetic engineering, biotechnology in medicine and agriculture, genomics and proteomics. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain the basic techniques used to manipulate genetic material.
Explain molecular and reproductive cloning.
Describe uses of biotechnology in medicine.
Describe uses of biotechnology in agriculture.
Define genomics and proteomics.
Define whole genome sequencing.
Explain different applications of genomics and proteomics.

pathways.title: Molecular Biology
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at the structure of DNA, DNA replication, transcription, translation and how genes are regulated. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the structure of dna.
Describe how eukaryotic and prokaryotic dna is arranged in the cell.
Explain the process of dna replication.
Explain the importance of telomerase to dna replication.
Describe mechanisms of dna repair.
Explain the central dogma.
Explain the main steps of transcription.
Describe how eukaryotic mrna is processed.
Describe the different steps in protein synthesis.
Discuss the role of ribosomes in protein synthesis.
Describe the genetic code and how the nucleotide sequence determines the amino acid and the protein sequence.
Discuss why every cell does not express all of its genes.
Describe how prokaryotic gene expression occurs at the transcriptional level.
Explain that eukaryotic gene expression occurs at the epigenetic, transcriptional, post-transcriptional, translational, and post-translational levels.

pathways.title: Lab Etiquette
pathways.description: The introduction to good lab citizenship serves as a lab etiquette guide and aims to make life easier for first-time research students in the crucial first days in a new lab.
learning_objectives: Recognize how a research lab is structured.
Identify the jobs of the different people in the lab.
Recognize how to be a good lab citizen using proper lab etiquette.

pathways.title: Central Dogma
pathways.description: This pathway explores the processes of transcription and translation that allow cells to produce proteins from DNA.
learning_objectives: Describe how the information in dna is used to create a protein (central dogma).
List the steps required to create a protein from dna.
Describe how rna polymerase reads the dna duplex and transcribes dna into mrna.
Describe how a ribosome translates mrna into a protein.
Examine how mutations in dna impact transcription and translation and the structure of the protein produced.

pathways.title: How to Make a Solution
pathways.description: Making solutions is a common activity in research labs. This pathway describes the steps involved in making a solution when in a research lab, from finding the recipe and doing calculations to putting the ingredients together.
learning_objectives: Plan how to make a solution.
Calculate how much of each ingredient to add.
Explain the steps involved in making a solution.
Describe how to work safely in a lab when making a solution.

pathways.title: Teach Experimental Thinking
pathways.description: This pathway highlights how the LabXchange experimental design simulation can be used to help learners design and plan experiments to answer a research question.
learning_objectives: Examine how the experimental design simulation can be used to help students develop their ability to design and plan experiments.
Identify how the experimental design simulation can support learners with different levels of understanding of lab techniques.

pathways.title: Cell Division
pathways.description: This pathway explores division of cells through mitosis and meiosis and how disregulation of these processes can lead to disease.
learning_objectives: Describe how cells divide through mitosis.
Compare/contrast the cell division processes mitosis and meiosis.
Explain how disruption of the cell cycle regulation can lead to diseases such as cancer.

pathways.title: Acids and Bases
pathways.description: Acids and bases are substances that are able to give and receive hydrogen ions respectively. This seemingly insignificant ability gives rise to a wide range of reactions and is essential for many biological processes. This pathway provides resources related to acids and bases, the types of reactions they undergo, and the pH scale which is used to measure the concentration of hydrogen ions in a solution.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Define an acid and a base according the both the arrhenius and bronsted-lowry models.
Predict the reactions that occur between acids and bases.
Describe how water is able to autoionize to form hydronium and hydroxide ions.
Define the term ph.
Calculate the ph of a solution.

pathways.title: Cell Membranes
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the structure of cell membranes and how they function in cellular transport.
learning_objectives: Describe the composition, chemical nature and structure of an eukaryotic cell membrane.
Explain why cell membranes are selectively permeable.
Compare/contrast the processes of passive (both simple and facilitated diffusion) and active transport.

pathways.title: An Introduction to the Human Body
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at overview of anatomy & physiology, structural organization of the human body, functions of human life, requirements for human life, homeostasis, anatomical terminology, and medical imaging. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Compare/contrast anatomy and physiology, and identify several branches of each.
Describe the structure of the body, from simplest to most complex, in terms of the six levels of organization.
Identify the functional characteristics of human life.
Identify the four requirements for human survival.
Define homeostasis and explain its importance to normal human functioning.
Use appropriate anatomical terminology to identify key body structures, body regions, and directions in the body.
Compare/contrast at least four medical imagining techniques in terms of their function and use in medicine.

pathways.title: Solutions and Concentration
pathways.description: Water is essential for life as we know it, due in part to its ability to dissolve a wide range of substances. In fact, most chemical reactions occur in a dissolved state. This pathway provides resources regarding how water is able to act as a solvent.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Diagram how water is able to dissolve ionic and polar substances due to its own polar nature.
Describe the effects of concentration on conductivity.
Define the term electrolyte.

pathways.title: Biotechnology and Genomics
pathways.description: The study of nucleic acids began with the discovery of DNA, progressed to the study of genes and small fragments, and has now exploded to the field of genomics. Genomics is the study of entire genomes, including the complete set of genes, their nucleotide sequence and organization, and their interactions within a species and with other species. DNA sequencing technology has contributed to advances in genomics. This pathway provides an in-depth look at biotechnology, mapping genomes, whole-genome sequencing, applying genomics, and genomics & proteomics. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe gel electrophoresis..
Explain molecular and reproductive cloning..
Describe biotechnology uses in medicine and agriculture..
Define genomics..
Describe genetic and physical maps..

Describe three types of sequencing..
Define whole-genome sequencing..
Explain pharmacogenomics..
Define polygenic..
Explain systems biology..
Describe a proteome..
Define protein signature..

pathways.title: Properties of Water
pathways.description: This pathway explores the properties of water that make it essential for life. Key properties of water discussed in this pathway include water as a solvent, hydrogen bonding, specific heat and cohesion.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Identify the unique properties of water that are important for life.
Explain how hydrogen bonds connect liquid water molecules and how hydrogen bonds contribute to the unique properties of water.

pathways.title: Explore Content on LabXchange
pathways.description: This pathway provides an introduction to LabXchange, how it can support learners and educators, and the types of content you can find on LabXchange.
learning_objectives: Describe the different types of content available on labxchange and how they can support teaching and learning.

pathways.title: CUT! How Does CRISPR Work?
pathways.description: Learn about the CRISPR gene editing technology by creating a stop-motion video of the CRISPR-Cas9 molecular mechanism. This pathway includes a deep dive into the CRISPR mechanism and guides you through the video-making process with a brainstorming worksheet, a storyboard template, a video introduction to stop-motion animation, and an assessment rubric for your final stop-motion video. Additional resources highlight the bioethical issues surrounding gene editing.
learning_objectives: Examine the molecular mechanism of crispr-cas9 genome editing.
Build a model to visualize the molecular mechanism of crispr-cas9.
Identify the ethical implications of the crispr technology and its applications.

pathways.title: Photosynthesis and Cellular Respiration
pathways.description:
This pathway provides an introduction to cellular metabolism, including a comparison of the processes of photosynthesis and cellular respiration. For a deeper look at this topic, we recommend the pathways Metabolism, Cellular Respiration and The Biological Process of Photosynthesis from the OpenStax textbook Biology for AP® Courses.
Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this material.
learning_objectives: Describe the structures and processes that allow plant cells to transfer energy from light to macromolecules.
Explain how organisms use energy stored in macromolecules through respiration and fermentation.
Explain how cells harness energy stored in macromolecules to carry out cellular functions.

pathways.title: The Study of Life
pathways.description: Scientists believe that the first forms of life on Earth were microorganisms that existed for billions of years in the ocean before plants and animals appeared. The mammals, birds, and flowers so familiar to us are all relatively recent, originating 130 to 250 million years ago. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the science of biology, and the themes & concepts of biology. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Identify the shared characteristics of the natural sciences.
Summarize the steps of the scientific method.
Compare/contrast inductive reasoning with deductive reasoning.
Describe the goals of basic science and applied science.
Identify and describe the properties of life.
Define and interpret a phylogenetic tree.
List examples of different subdisciplines in biology.

pathways.title: Photosynthesis
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at overview of photosynthesis, the light dependent reactions of photosynthesis, and the Calvin cycle. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Summarize the process of photosynthesis.
Explain the relevance of photosynthesis to other living things.
Identify the reactants and products of photosynthesis.
Describe the main structures involved in photosynthesis.
Explain how plants absorb energy from sunlight.
Describe how the wavelength of light affects its energy and color.
Describe how and where photosynthesis takes place within a plant.
Describe the calvin cycle.
Define carbon fixation.
Explain how photosynthesis works in the energy cycle of all living organisms.

pathways.title: Create Your Online Class
pathways.description: This pathway will show you how to create a class on LabXchange, add content and enroll learners. LabXchange classes can be used to facilitate discussions and track learner progress through assigned content.
learning_objectives: Describe how to create your own class on Labxchange.
Explain how learners join and are admitted to a Labxchange class.
Support student engagement by facilitating online discussions.

pathways.title: Chemical Reactions and Stoichiometry
pathways.description: Chemical reactions are the foundation of chemistry, and understanding how elements combine and in what ratios helps us to understand the world around us. This pathway provides resources about how different chemicals react, and the ratios in which they react, a field of study known as Stoichiometry (literally: element measuring).
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Define the terms mole and molar mass.
Calculate the amounts of substances required to react with other substances.
Calculate the amount of a substance produced in a reaction.

pathways.title: The Immune System
pathways.description: The environment consists of numerous pathogens, which are agents, usually microorganisms, that cause diseases in their hosts. A host is the organism that is invaded and often harmed by a pathogen. Pathogens include bacteria, protists, fungi and other infectious organisms. We are constantly exposed to pathogens in food and water, on surfaces, and in the air. Mammalian immune systems evolved for protection from such pathogens; they are composed of an extremely diverse array of specialized cells and soluble molecules that coordinate a rapid and flexible defense system capable of providing protection from a majority of these disease agents. Vaccines were developed to reduce the chance of infection of a particular disease, such as measles, mumps, polio, or chicken pox, by assisting the body to develop immunity. However, many diseases still do not have a vaccine, such as the deadly disease caused by the Ebola virus. This pathway provides an in-depth look at innate immune response; adaptive immune response; antibodies; and disruptions in the immune system. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Identify examples of physical and chemical immune barriers.
Define the immediate and induced immune responses.
Describe natural killer cells, and explain their role in immunity.
List the features of histocompatibility class i molecules.
Explain how the proteins in a complement system function to destroy extracellular pathogens.
Discuss adaptive immunity.
Describe the difference between adaptive and innate immunity.
Compare/contrast the cell-mediated immune response and the humoral immune response.
Discuss immune tolerance.
Describe cross-reactivity.
Describe the basic structure of an antibody, and explain the functions of antibodies.
Describe how antibodies are produced.
Explain hypersensitivity.
Define autoimmunity, and identify an example of an autoimmune disease.

pathways.title: Chemical Bonding
pathways.description: Why do atoms react with some elements but not others, and why do they form compounds with such different properties? One of the reasons is the bonds that they form. This pathway provides resources relating to the reasons that atoms bond, and the three different types of bonds that form between atoms: covalent, ionic and metallic.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Name the different ypes of chemical bonds that occur between atoms.
Identify the type of bond that forms based on the electronegativity of the atoms.
Describe how electrons are shared by atoms in a covalent bond.
Describe how electrons are transferred between atoms in an ionic bond.
Explain how metal atoms form a loose arrangement via metallic bonding.

pathways.title: Making Measurements
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the units scientists use when making measurements and how to judge and express the meaning and level of confidence in a measurement.
learning_objectives: Use metric units to record measurements.
Label the significant figures in a measurement.
Describe the level of confidence in a measurement using its reported uncertainty and number of replicates.

pathways.title: Ecology
pathways.description: This pathway explores the importance of biodiversity and human impact on the environment.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Compare/contrast exponential and logistic population growth and describe the changes in growth phase based on birth rate and death rate.
Explain the differences and effect of density-dependent and density-independent factors on population growth.
Categorize community interactions between populations of organisms as either competition, parasitic, predation, or symbiotic.
Diagram a food web based on trophic levels, food chains, and community interactions in an ecosystem.

Explain the nutrient cycling of carbon and nitrogen based on key processes, such as photosynthesis, cellular respiration, decomposition, sedimentation, fixation, nitrification, denitrification and assimilation..
Discuss the disruption of human actions on climate, natural resources and sustainability.

pathways.title: Getting Started on LabXchange
pathways.description: This pathway contains guidance for how to create an account on LabXchange, and the differences between the Educator and Learner roles. You will also learn how to search the library for public content, and how to add content to your own private library.
learning_objectives: Summarize how to create a free labxchange account or log in to an existing account.
Describe the search categories in the labxchange library to find the content you're looking for.
Use your dashboard to add or create your own content.

pathways.title: A Guide to the Properties of Water
pathways.description: Water, water everywhere! In this pathway, Science Mom uses a series of demonstrations to introduce you to the properties of water.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties

pathways.title: Chemistry of Life
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at the building blocks of molecules, water, and biological molecules. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe matter and elements.
Describe the interrelationship between protons, neutrons, and electrons, and the ways in which electrons can be donated or shared between atoms.
Describe the properties of water that are critical to maintaining life.
Describe the ways in which carbon is critical to life.
Explain the impact of slight changes in amino acids on organisms.
Describe the four major types of biological molecules.
Explain the functions of the four major types of molecules.

pathways.title: MassBioEd's Redesigning Bacteria: Bacterial Transformation
pathways.description: Background and activities to support the virtual lab "Redesigning Bacteria: Bacterial Transformation" from MassBioEd.
learning_objectives: Examine the process of transformation.
Describe what a plasmid is and how plasmid function naturally in bacteria cells.
Explain how plasmids are used in biotechnology.
Identify the dna sequences required for plasmid function.
Predict how acquisition of a plasmid can change the traits of bacteria.
Construct questions and form hypotheses.
Use experimental data to make conclusions about the initial question and support or refute the stated hypothesis.

pathways.title: Genetics and Disease
pathways.description: This pathway explores how we inherit our phenotypes and the origin of genetic diseases.
learning_objectives: Explain how genotype leads to phenotype.
Describe how the inheritance of genes and traits is explained by mendel's laws.
Explain how mutations in chromosomal dna can lead to disease.
Summarize examples of different genetic diseases and how they arise.

pathways.title: Biomolecules
pathways.description: This pathway provides an overview of the four classes of biological molecules (lipids, carbohydrates, proteins and nucleotides) and how they are assembled.
learning_objectives: Identify the four major classes of biomolecules.
Compare/contrast the differences between the structure and function of each class of biomolecule.
Explain the role of biomolecules in living systems.
Describe the reactions and processes that connect monomers to form biomolecule polymers.

pathways.title: Tips for Learning Online
pathways.description: This pathway is designed to help students get the most out of their online learning experience.
learning_objectives: Identify resources available on labxchange to support learning science online.
Describe strategies that can help you make the most of your online learning experience while balancing self care.

pathways.title: Evolution
pathways.description: This pathway explores the concepts of natural selection and evolution of populations. Stories from scientists sharing their perspective on this topic and their research are embedded throughout the pathway.
learning_objectives: Describe how four factors result in natural selection and give organisms an advantage in survival and reproduction in an environment.
Explain how variation in genetic traits in a population lead to individuals with advantageous traits having a reproductive advantage.

Describe the three outcomes of natural selection that lead to either an increase in the trait in the population, speciation, or extinction.

pathways.title: DNA Structure and Function
pathways.description: Each person’s DNA is unique, and it is possible to detect differences among individuals within a species on the basis of these unique features. DNA analysis has many practical applications, including identifying criminals (forensics), determining paternity, tracing genealogy, identifying pathogens, researching archeological finds, tracing disease outbreaks, and studying human migration patterns. In the medical field, DNA is used in diagnostics, new vaccine development, and cancer therapy. It is often possible to determine predisposition to diseases by sequencing genes. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the historical basis of modern understanding; DNA structure & sequencing; the basics of DNA replication; DNA replication in prokaryotes and eukaryotes; and DNA repair. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Explain the transformation of dna.
Explain how griffith’s experiments in 1928 relate to our modern understanding of dna and how it works.
Discuss key historic experiments that helped identify dna as the genetic material.
Define chargaff’s rules of nitrogenous base pairing.
Illustrate the molecular structure of dna.
Describe the sanger method of dna sequencing.
Recall an application of dna sequencing.
Compare/contrast the similarities and differences between eukaryotic and prokaryotic dna.
Explain how the structure of dna provides for the process of replication.
Discuss how the meselson and stahl experiments supported the semi-conservative nature of replication.
Explain how dna is replicated in prokaryotes, and describe the roles of the leading and lagging strands and okazaki fragments in the process.
Explain the role of dna polymerase and other enzymes and proteins in supporting replication.

Recall the role of telomerase in dna replication.
List different types of mutations in dna and explain the significance of mutations.

pathways.title: The Periodic Table
pathways.description: The periodic table of elements is one of the greatest achievements in chemistry, and understanding its arrangement helps us to understand many aspects of chemistry. This pathway provides resources to understand several of the trends on the periodic table, such as electronegativity and the number of bonds an atom can form.
learning_objectives: Name several groups on the periodic table.
Infer the number of bonds an element will be able to form from its position on the periodic table.
Explain the trends on the periodic table regarding atomic mass, atomic radius and electronegativity.
Define the term electronegativity and describe how it relates to ionization energy and electron affinity.

pathways.title: Test of Cells for HIV Resistance After CRISPR-Cas9 Editing
pathways.description: A GFP reporter gene is used to test CRISPR-Cas9 edited macrophages for HIV resistance.
learning_objectives: Recall fluorescence microscopy as a powerful tool in cell biology.
Describe a fluorescence microscopy-based assay to test cells for hiv resistance.

pathways.title: Expanding the CRISPR-Cas9-Based Toolbox
pathways.description: The application of Cas9-fusion proteins creates a greater cellular toolbox beyond gene disruption.

pathways.title: Organic Chemistry
pathways.description: Organic chemistry is the study of the molecules that are necessary for life to exist. The key ingredient for these molecules is carbon, which is able to form complex structures and allows for the variety of different molecules in living things. This pathway provides resources for understanding the basic rules that are used to categorize these different molecules.

Identify the homologous series that a n organic molecule belongs to.

Diagram an organic molecule based on its iupac name.

pathways.title: Build Scientific Literacy
pathways.description: This pathway shows how LabXchange can help build scientific literacy skills to help you engage with the science happening around you.
learning_objectives: Describe how the resources available on labxchange can help build scientific literacy and confidence in scientific discourse.

pathways.title: Data Literacy: How to Interpret Common Charts and Plots
pathways.description: This pathway prepares users to interpret and critique the types of charts and plots commonly used to summarize and visualize scientific data.
learning_objectives: Interpret common ways of presenting data (including histograms, violin plots, boxplots, barplots, and pie charts).
Critique common ways of presenting data (including histograms, violin plots, boxplots, barplots, and pie charts).
Use principles of data visualization when designing your own graphs and interpreting others' graphs.

pathways.title: Body Systems and Homeostasis
pathways.description: This pathway provides an introduction to some of the systems of the body and the concept of homeostasis.
learning_objectives: Explain the concept of homeostasis.
Describe how blood flows through the body.
Summarize how organs perform specialized functions and work together to process food in the body.
Explain the function of hormones and how they contribute to homeostasis.
Summarize the role of joints and muscles in movement.
Describe the innate and adaptive immune systems and how they work together to fight off infections.

pathways.title: Cell Structure
pathways.description: Close your eyes and picture a brick wall. What is the basic building block of that wall? A single brick, of course. Like a brick wall, your body is composed of basic building blocks called 'cells.' Your body has many kinds of cells, each specialized for a specific purpose. Just as a home is made from a variety of building materials, the human body is constructed from many cell types. Each of these cell types plays a vital role during the growth, development, and ongoing maintenance of the body. In humans, before a cell develops into its specialized type, it is called a stem cell. A stem cell is a cell that has not undergone the changes involved in specialization. This pathway provides an in-depth look at studying cells; prokaryotic & eukaryotic cells; the endomembrane system & proteins; the cytoskeleton; and connections between cells & cellular activities. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Discuss the role of cells in organisms.
Explain the difference between light microscopy and electron microscopy.
Summarize the cell theory.
Identify the major structures of prokaryotic cells.

Compare/contrast the similarities and differences in the structure of the eukaryotic cell and the structure of the prokaryotic cell.
List the structural differences between animal and plant cells.
Illustrate the functions of the major cell structures.

Recall how the various components of the cytoskeleton perform their functions.
Identify the components of the extracellular matrix.
Discuss the roles of tight junctions, gap junctions, and plasmodesmata in allowing cells to exchange materials with the environment and communicate with other cells.

pathways.title: How To Method Videos
pathways.description: This pathway features videos that take you into the lab and show you, step-by-step, how to do various common lab procedures.

pathways.title: Basic Lab Skills - Manipulating DNA
pathways.description: This pathway shows a number of molecular biology techniques to manipulate DNA, including PCR, restriction enzyme digests, ligations, and transformations. There is a list of foundational method videos and interactives and the pathway is used as a glossary of lab techniques, rather than an ordered sequence of resources.
learning_objectives: Apply molecular biology techniques to manipulate DNA.
Recognize foundational lab protocols and equipment.
Identify the most appropriate and useful lab protocols and equipment.
Use methods and lab equipment to more easily in the real lab environment.

pathways.title: Enzymes Are Protein Catalysts
pathways.description: This pathway introduces a specific class of proteins called enzymes that are integral to catalyzing many biological reactions.
learning_objectives: Describe the properties of enzymes.
Explain how enzymes work to affect the rate of biological reactions..
Summarize the impact of environmental factors on enzyme function.

pathways.title: How to Keep a Great Lab Notebook
pathways.description: This pathway addresses how, when, and why to maintain an excellent lab notebook.
learning_objectives: Design a lab notebook that is a useful reference for both you and your labmates.
Design a lab notebook that allows your experimental findings to be easily reproduced.

pathways.title: Application of CRISPR-Cas9: Gene Disruption to Gain HIV Resistance
pathways.description: To create HIV-resistant immune cells with the gene editing tool CRISPR-Cas9, a specific macrophage gene must be disrupted.
learning_objectives: Describe the hiv life cycle.
Interpret the hiv life cycle.
Use your knowledge about the hiv life cycle to choose a macrophage gene to disrupt.

pathways.title: Gene Expression
pathways.description: Each somatic cell in the body generally contains the same DNA. A few exceptions include red blood cells, which contain no DNA in their mature state, and some immune system cells that rearrange their DNA while producing antibodies. In general, however, the genes that determine whether you have green eyes, brown hair, and how fast you metabolize food are the same in the cells in your eyes and your liver, even though these organs function quite differently. If each cell has the same DNA, how is it that cells or organs are different? Why do cells in the eye differ so dramatically from cells in the liver? This pathway provides an in-depth look at regulation of gene expression, prokaryotic gene regulation, eukaryotic epigenetic gene regulation, eukaryotic transcription gene regulation, eukaryotic post-transcriptional gene regulation, eukaryotic translational and post-translational gene regulation, and cancer & gene regulation. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Discuss why every cell does not express all of its genes all of the time.
Describe how prokaryotic gene regulation occurs at the transcriptional level.
Discuss how eukaryotic gene regulation occurs at the epigenetic, transcriptional, post-transcriptional, translational, and post-translational levels.
Describe the steps involved in prokaryotic gene regulation.
Explain the roles of activators, inducers, and repressors in gene regulation.
Explain how chromatin remodeling controls transcriptional access.
Describe how access to dna is controlled by histone modification.
Describe how access to dna is controlled by histone modification.
Discuss the role of transcription factors in gene regulation.
Explain how enhancers and repressors regulate gene expression.
Interpret rna splicing and explain its role in regulating gene expression.
Describe the importance of rna stability in gene regulation.
Interpret the process of translation and discuss its key factors.
Describe how the initiation complex controls translation.
Explain the different ways in which the post-translational control of gene expression takes place.
Describe how changes to gene expression can cause cancer.
Explain how changes to gene expression at different levels can disrupt the cell cycle.
Discuss how understanding regulation of gene expression can lead to better drug design.

pathways.title: Population and Community Ecology
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at population demographics and dynamics, population growth and regulation, the human population, and community ecology. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe how ecologists measure population size and density.
Describe three different patterns of population distribution.
Use life tables to calculate mortality rates.
Describe the three types of survivorship curves and relate them to specific populations.
Explain the characteristics of and differences between exponential and logistic growth patterns.
List examples of exponential and logistic growth in natural populations.
List examples of how the carrying capacity of a habitat may change.
Compare/contrast density-dependent growth regulation and density-independent growth regulation giving examples.
Discuss how human population growth can be exponential.
Explain how humans have expanded the carrying capacity of their habitat.
Examine the relationship between population growth and age structure to the level of economic development in different countries.
Discuss the long-term implications of unchecked human population growth.
Discuss the predator-prey cycle.
List examples of defenses against predation and herbivory.
Describe the competitive exclusion principle.
List examples of symbiotic relationships between species.
Describe community structure and succession.

pathways.title: Life Science Practices
pathways.description: This pathway provides an introduction to the scientific method in the context of the life sciences.
learning_objectives: Describe the steps of the scientific method and how they are used in life science research.
Explain the role of controls in an experiment.
List the characteristics that define life.

pathways.title: An Introduction to DNA Sequencing
pathways.description: Our DNA says a lot about us - but how do we read it? This pathway provides an introduction to DNA sequencing and the evolution of this technology.
learning_objectives: Examine the structure of dna and the way a dna sequence is constructed.

pathways.title: Protocol Simulations
pathways.description:
Virtual lab simulations offer free, unlimited access to a research lab to give users the time and space to practice developing lab skills in a low-stakes environment. In contrast to a physical lab, in a virtual lab, learners can undo steps and repeat an experiment as many times as needed. The simulations respond to actions and offer feedback along the way, revealing how actions in the lab impact results. 
Each simulation is framed by a virtual lab notebook that introduces the key concepts and equipment used in the lab. Learners are introduced to the scientific process, from making predictions to interpreting results.
learning_objectives: Develop useful lab skills through the interactive simulations.
Recall important lab safety rules and techniques.
Develop a hypothesis in different experimental scenarios.
Test your hypothesis through the simulated experiments.

pathways.title: Mitosis
pathways.description: This pathway examines how eukaryotic cells divide by mitosis and cytokinesis to yield 2 genetically identical cells.
learning_objectives: Explain the purpose of mitosis with respect to cellular reproduction.
Describe key events that occur during each phase of mitosis.
Explain how progression through the eukaryotic cell cycle is controlled and the cellular consequences if such control is lost.
Conduct an experiment to evaluate the effect of environmental factors on the rate of mitosis.

pathways.title: The Digestive System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at overview of the digestive system, digestive system processes & regulation; the mouth, pharynx, & esophagus; the stomach; the small & large intestines; accessory organs in digestion: the liver, pancreas, & gallbladder; and chemical digestion & absorption: a closer look. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: List and describe the functional anatomy of the organs and accessory organs of the digestive system.
Discuss the processes and control of ingestion, propulsion, mechanical digestion, chemical digestion, absorption, and defecation.
Discuss the roles of the liver, pancreas, and gallbladder in digestion.
Compare/contrast the digestion of the three macronutrients.

pathways.title: Diversity of Life
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at organizing life on Earth, and determining evolutionary relationships. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Discuss the need for a comprehensive classification system.

Describe how systematics and taxonomy relate to phylogeny.
