pathways.title: The Chemical Level of Organization
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at elements & atoms: the building blocks of matter, chemical bonds, chemical reactions, inorganic compounds essential to human functioning, and organic compounds essential to human functioning. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Describe the fundamental composition of matter.
Identify the three subatomic particles.
Identify the four most abundant elements in the body.
Explain the relationship between an atom’s number of electrons and its relative stability.
Compare/contrast between ionic bonds, covalent bonds, and hydrogen bonds.
Explain how energy is invested, stored, and released via chemical reactions, particularly those reactions that are critical to life.
Explain the importance of the inorganic compounds that contribute to life, such as water, salts, acids, and bases.

Compare/contrast the four important classes of organic (carbon-based) compounds—proteins, carbohydrates, lipids and nucleic acids—according to their composition and functional importance to human life.

pathways.title: The Immune System and Disease
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at viruses, innate immunity, adaptive immunity, and disruptions in the immune system. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe how viruses were first discovered and how they are detected.
Explain the detailed steps of viral replication.
Describe how vaccines are used in prevention and treatment of viral diseases.
Describe the body’s innate physical and chemical defenses.
Explain the inflammatory response.
Describe the complement system.
Explain adaptive immunity.
Describe cell-mediated immune response and humoral immune response.
Describe immune tolerance.
Describe hypersensitivity.
Define autoimmunity.

pathways.title: Theory Behind Evolution I
pathways.description: This is the first Session in the Evolution Flipped Course from iBiology. In this first session, we introduce the topic of evolution with three videos. Dr. Hale introduces Darwinian evolution and the concepts of heritable traits, genetic variation, and natural selection. She explains speciation and describes how phylogenetic trees can be constructed from fossil and DNA evidence and used to compare the relatedness of species. Dr. Hoekstra elaborates on how changes in genes can produce phenotypic variation which can be acted upon by natural selection.  And finally, Dr. Gordon discusses how interaction networks evolve in different ecological settings.

pathways.title: Rates of Reactions & Equilibrium
pathways.description: When a chemical reaction occurs in a closed system, it will reach an equilibrium state in which reactants and products are produced at the same rate. This pathway provides resources to understand the factors that affect chemical equilibrium.
learning_objectives: Define a dynamic chemical equilibrium.
Define the equilibrium constant.
Recall le chatelier's principle.
Explain how changes in temperature, concentration and pressure affect the chemical equilibrium.
Calculate the equilibrium constant for a given reaction.

pathways.title: Cell Structure and Function
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at how cells are studied, comparing prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, eukaryotic cells, the cell membrane, passive transport and active transport. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the roles of cells in organisms.
Compare/contrast light microscopy and electron microscopy.
Summarize the cell theory.
Name examples of prokaryotic and eukaryotic organisms.
Compare/contrast prokaryotic cells and eukaryotic cells.
Describe the relative sizes of different kinds of cells.
Describe the structure of eukaryotic plant and animal cells.
Describe the role of the plasma membrane.
Summarize the functions of the major cell organelles.
Describe the cytoskeleton and extracellular matrix.
Explain the fluid mosaic model of membranes.
Describe the functions of phospholipids, proteins, and carbohydrates in membranes.
Explain why and how passive transport occurs.
Describe the processes of osmosis and diffusion.
Define tonicity and describe its relevance to passive transport.
Explain how electrochemical gradients affect ions.
Describe endocytosis, including phagocytosis, pinocytosis, and receptor-mediated endocytosis.
Explain the process of exocytosis.

pathways.title: Quadratic Equations
pathways.description: This pathway aims to help students understand how to solve quadratic equations and deal with the nuances associated with quadratic calculations.
learning_objectives: Develop your skills for solving algebraic equations..
Calculate solutions to quadratic equations using different techniques..
Calculate graphs described by quadratic equations..

pathways.title: Basic Lab Skills - Working With Cells
pathways.description: This pathway gives an overview of how to work with bacterial cells, human tissue culture cells, and fluorescently labeled cells. There is a list of foundational method videos and interactives and the pathway is used as a glossary of lab techniques, rather than an ordered sequence of resources.
learning_objectives: Use methods to work in a sterile environment with bacterial and human cells.
Use fluorescence microscopy to investigate cells.
Recognise foundational lab protocols and equipment.
Identify which lab protocols are useful to them.
Use methods and lab equipment more easily in the real lab environment.

pathways.title: The Cellular Level of Organization
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at the cell membrane, the cytoplasm & cellular organelles, the nucleus & DNA replication, protein synthesis, cell growth & division, and cellular differentiation. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the structure and function of the cell membrane, including its regulation of materials into and out of the cell.
Describe the functions of the various cytoplasmic organelles.
Explain the structure and contents of the nucleus, as well as the process of dna replication.
Explain the process by which a cell builds proteins using the dna code.
List the stages of the cell cycle in order, including the steps of cell division in somatic cells.
Discuss how a cell differentiates and becomes more specialized.
List the morphological and physiological characteristics of some representative cell types in the human body.

pathways.title: The Musculoskeletal System
pathways.description: The muscular and skeletal systems provide support to the body and allow for a wide range of movement. The bones of the skeletal system protect the body’s internal organs and support the weight of the body. The muscles of the muscular system contract and pull on the bones, allowing for movements as diverse as standing, walking, running, and grasping items. Injury or disease affecting the musculoskeletal system can be very debilitating. In humans, the most common musculoskeletal diseases worldwide are caused by malnutrition. Ailments that affect the joints are also widespread, such as arthritis, which can make movement difficult and—in advanced cases—completely impair mobility. Progress in the science of prosthesis design has resulted in the development of artificial joints, with joint replacement surgery in the hips and knees being the most common. This pathway provides an in-depth look at types of skeletal systems; bone; joints & skeletal movement; muscle contraction & locomotion. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Describe the characteristics of the different types of animal skeletal systems.
Explain the roles of the various components of the human skeletal system.
Identify examples of the different types of bones and explain their functions in the human skeleton.
Discuss the roles of the different cell types and tissue types in bone.
Explain how bones develop, grow, and undergo remodeling and repair.
Describe the different types of joints on the basis of structure.
Discuss the role of joints in skeletal movement.
Identify the different types of muscle tissue.
Discuss the role of muscle contraction in locomotion.

pathways.title: From Prokaryotes to Multicellular Organisms
pathways.description: This is the sixth session of the Evolution Flipped Course from iBiology. Once prokaryotic life emerged on earth, how did it evolve into larger, more complex eukaryotic organisms? Three scientists help us understand how this may have happened. Dr. Julie Theriot speculates about the important role that cytoskeleton-associated proteins may have played in that transition (and she challenges you to prove her speculation wrong!). Dr. Miller explains how endosymbionts such as mitochondria and chloroplasts came to be the power organelles of eukaryotes. And Dr. Nicole King addresses the fascinating question of how the first multicellular animals may have evolved from our unicellular ancestors.

pathways.title: The Lymphatic and Immune System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at anatomy of the lymphatic & immune systems, barrier defenses & the innate immune response, the adaptive immune response: T-lymphocytes & their functional types, the adaptive immune response: B-lymphocytes & antibodies, the immune response against pathogens, diseases associated with depressed or overactive immune responses, and transplantation & cancer immunology. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Identify the components and anatomy of the lymphatic system.
Discuss the role of the innate immune response against pathogens.
Describe the power of the adaptive immune response to cure disease.
Explain immunological deficiencies and over-reactions of the immune system.
Discuss the role of the immune response in transplantation and cancer.
Describe the interaction of the immune and lymphatic systems with other body systems.

pathways.title: Adding Content to Your LabXchange Library
pathways.description: This pathway will show you how to use your dashboard to add or create your own content.
learning_objectives: Examine how to add your own content through your learner dashboard.

pathways.title: Introduction to Python
pathways.description: This pathway offers lessons that introduce users to Python programming and is designed to teach users the basics of Python to solve real-world science problems ranging from biology to statistics to physics. The lessons assume no prior knowledge in programming or any of these scientific fields. Programming beginners will probably want to proceed through the first lessons in order, but you should also feel free to jump to lessons that interest and excite you!
learning_objectives: Identify and implement four mathematical operations to make calculations with the appropriate python 3 syntax.
Interpret and use variables in mathematical computations.
Design if statements to output appropriate statements based on variable inputs.

Design a program that calculates a phenotypic ratio for a given sample and indicates whether that ratio is expected or unexpected.

Build interpretable histograms, box plots, and scatter plots using real biological data.

pathways.title: Viruses, Viral Morphology, Infection, and Treatment
pathways.description: Viruses are noncellular parasitic entities that cannot be classified within any kingdom. They can infect organisms as diverse as bacteria, plants, and animals. In fact, viruses exist in a sort of netherworld between a living organism and a nonliving entity. Living things grow, metabolize, and reproduce. In contrast, viruses are not cellular, do not have a metabolism or grow, and cannot divide by cell division. Viruses can copy, or replicate themselves; however, they are entirely dependent on resources derived from their host cells to produce progeny viruses—which are assembled in their mature form. This pathway provides an in-depth look at viral evolution, morphology, and classification, virus infections and hosts, prevention and treatment of viral infections, and other acellular entities (prions & viroids). The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe how viruses were first discovered and how they are detected.
Discuss three hypotheses about how viruses evolved.
Describe the general structure of a virus.
Identify the basic shapes of viruses.
Interpret past and emerging classification systems for viruses.
Describe the basis for the baltimore classification system.
List the steps of replication and explain what occurs at each step.
Describe the lytic and lysogenic cycles of virus replication.
Explain the transmission of plant and animal viruses.
Discuss some of the diseases caused by plant and animal viruses.
Discuss the economic impact of plant and animal viruses.
Identify major viral illnesses that affect humans.
Compare/contrast vaccinations and anti-viral drugs as medical approaches to viruses.
Describe prions and their basic properties.
Define viroids and their targets of infection.

pathways.title: Meiosis
pathways.description: This pathway examines how the process of meiosis in a eukaryotic cell results in 4 genetically distinct, haploid cells. It also explores the consequences of errors during meiosis and provides a comparison of mitosis and meiosis.

pathways.title: Electric Circuits
pathways.description: This pathway explores the foundational concepts of electric circuits, such as voltage, curent, resistance and capacitance. Some further applications of circuits are also included. Problems are provided to test for understanding.

pathways.title: Basic Lab Skills - Pipetting and Solutions
pathways.description: A comprehensive collection of instructional videos and interactives on essential lab techniques, focusing on pipetting and making solutions. Designed for flexible learning, this pathway serves as an extensive glossary allowing users to explore and refine their skills in a non-sequential order. Ideal for learners at all levels, this pathway enhances understanding and proficiency in foundational laboratory practices at one's own pace.

Identify the most appropriate and useful lab protocols and equipment.
Use methods and lab equipment more easily in the real lab environment.

pathways.description: Through the background and activitites in this pathway, students will determine the antibiotic resistance phenotype of bacteria through agarose gel electrophoresis
learning_objectives: Explain what an antibiotic is and what it is used fro.

pathways.title: The Cardiovascular System: Blood
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at an overview of blood, production of the formed elements, erythrocytes, leukocytes & platelets, hemostasis, and blood typing. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.

pathways.title: Mendel's Experiments and Heredity
pathways.description: Genetics is the study of heredity. Johann Gregor Mendel set the framework for genetics long before chromosomes or genes had been identified, at a time when meiosis was not well understood. Mendel selected a simple biological system and conducted methodical, quantitative analyses using large sample sizes. Because of Mendel’s work, the fundamental principles of heredity were revealed. We now know that genes, carried on chromosomes, are the basic functional units of heredity with the capability to be replicated, expressed, or mutated. Today, the postulates put forth by Mendel form the basis of classical, or Mendelian, genetics. This pathway provides an in-depth look at Mendel’s experiments & the laws of probability; characteristics & traits; and the laws of inheritance. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the scientific reasons for the success of mendel’s experimental work.
Describe the expected outcomes of monohybrid crosses involving dominant and recessive alleles.

pathways.title: Cell Structure
pathways.description: Close your eyes and picture a brick wall. What is the wall's basic building block? It is a single brick. Like a brick wall, cells are the building blocks that make up your body. Your body has many kinds of cells, each specialized for a specific purpose. Just as we use a variety of materials to build a home, the human body is constructed from many cell types. This pathway provides an in-depth look at studying cells, prokaryotic cells, eukaryotic cells, the endomembrane system and proteins, the cytoskeleton, and connections between cells and cellular activities. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.

pathways.title: DNA Structure and Function
pathways.description: The three letters “DNA” have now become synonymous with crime solving and genetic testing. DNA can be retrieved from hair, blood, or saliva. Each person’s DNA is unique, and it is possible to detect differences between individuals within a species on the basis of these unique features. DNA analysis has many practical applications beyond forensics. In humans, DNA testing is applied to numerous uses: determining paternity, tracing genealogy, identifying pathogens, archeological research, tracing disease outbreaks, and studying human migration patterns. In the medical field, DNA is used in diagnostics, new vaccine development, and cancer therapy. It is now possible to determine predisposition to diseases by looking at genes. This pathway provides an in-depth look at historical basis of modern understanding, DNA structure and sequencing, basics of DNA replication, DNA replication in prokaryotes, DNA replication in eukaryotes, and DNA repair. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.

pathways.title: Diffusion Through a Membrane

pathways.title: Energy in Cells
pathways.description: This pathway explores how cells harvest energy using cellular respiration in eukaryotic cells and photosynthesis in plant cells.

They will also consider how restriction enzymes enable them to distinguish between bitter-tasters and non-tasters. After running gels, they will explore how their gels compare with their sequencing data and their phenotype. As an application, students will explore the genetics of sickle cell anemia, and how it can be detected with restriction enzymes and gel electrophoresis.

pathways.title: The Cardiovascular System: The Heart

pathways.title: Intermolecular Forces of Attraction
pathways.description: Why are some substances liquids, while others are solids? Why are some substances harder than others? Why is water essential for life on Earth? This pathway provides resources for answering these questions by looking at the types of intermolecular forces that exist between particles, and the effect that these forces have on the macroscopic properties of substances.

pathways.title: Magnetic Fields
pathways.description: This pathway explores the essential concepts of magnets and magnetic fields. The relationship between magnets and the fields they produce is explained, as well as some of the contemporary applications of magnetism in medical diagnostics and quantum mechanics. pathways.title: What Is pH Again?
pathways.description:
pH is a measure of the concentration of protons in solution. This pathway introduces pH and the pH scale as a practical way to describe the acidity of a solution with the help of an interactive. Questions involving well-known liquids aim to familiarize the student with the pH and the related pOH scale.
Pathway Outcomes:
After completing the pathway you will be able to connect proton concentration [H+] and pH in the following way: 
pH  = -log10[H+]
An acidic solution has a proton concentration [H+] > 10-7 M and pH < 7. A basic solution has a proton concentration [H+] < 10-7 M and a pH > 7.  Neutral water has a proton concentration [H+] = 10-7 M and a pH = 7.
An acidic solution has a low pH (ranging from 0-7) and a high concentration of protons, whereas a basic solution has a high pH (ranging from 7-14) and a low concentration of protons. The mathematical shortcut of the pH scale makes it easy to describe the acidity of a solution.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: You will explain the concept of acids and bases.
You will explain the concepts of pH and pH scale.
You will use an interactive illustrating the relationship of pH and proton concentration.
You will describe the relationship of proton and hydroxide ion concentrations in aqueous solutions and the related pH and pOH values.
You will predict pH values of common liquids.

pathways.title: Viral Evolution
pathways.description: This is the tenth and final session of the Evolution Flipped Course from iBiology. Although we tend to think of evolution as something that happens slowly and over long periods of time, it is actually happening around us all the time. In this session, Drs. Turner and Malik focus on the ability of viruses to rapidly evolve and adapt to changing environments. Turner explains how some viruses have evolved to infect many hosts while others infect a single cell type. He also uses experimental evolution to study the ability of a virus to survive under different environmental conditions versus its ability to effectively reproduce. Malik studies the ongoing battle between viruses and their hosts and describes some of the tricks each use to survive attacks by the other.

pathways.title: Biological Macromolecules
pathways.description: Food provides the body with the nutrients it needs to survive. Many of these critical nutrients are biological macromolecules, or large molecules, necessary for and built by living things. muscle. The body uses fat molecules to build new cells, store energy, and for proper digestion. Carbohydrates are the primary source of the body’s energy. Nucleic acids contain genetic information. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the synthesis of biological macromolecules; carbohydrates; lipids; proteins; and nucleic acids. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.

pathways.title: The Body’s Systems
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at homeostasis and osmoregulation, digestive system, circulatory and respiratory systems, endocrine system, musculoskeletal system, and nervous system. pathways.title: The Chemical Foundation of Life
pathways.description: All matter, including living things, is made up of various combinations of elements. Some of the most abundant elements in living organisms include carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, and phosphorus. These elements form the major biological molecules—nucleic acids, proteins, carbohydrates, and lipids—that are the fundamental components of living matter. Biologists study these important molecules to understand their unique structures which determine their specialized functions. All biological processes follow the laws of physics and chemistry. Therefore, in order to understand how biological systems work, it is important to understand the underlying physics and chemistry. This pathway provides an in-depth look at atoms, isotopes, ions & molecules (the building blocks); water; and carbon. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Explain how atomic structure determines the properties of elements, molecules, and matter.

pathways.title: Prokaryotes: Bacteria and Archaea
pathways.description: Most people think of bacteria as disease-causing organisms. Although some bacteria are pathogens, many are not. For example, the commensal bacteria that inhabit our skin and gastrointestinal tract do a host of good things for us. They protect us from pathogens, help us digest our food, and produce some of our vitamins and other nutrients. These activities have been known for a long time. More recently, scientists have gathered evidence that these bacteria may also help regulate our moods, influence our activity levels, and even help control weight by affecting our food choices and absorption patterns. The Human Microbiome Project has begun the process of cataloging our normal bacteria (and archaea) so we can better understand these functions. This pathway provides an in-depth look at prokaryotic diversity; the structure of prokaryotes; prokaryotic metabolism; bacterial diseases in humans; and beneficial prokaryotes. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.

pathways.title: Energy and Phase Changes

pathways.title: Metabolism and Nutrition
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at overview of metabolic reactions, carbohydrate metabolism, lipid metabolism, protein metabolism, metabolic states of the body, energy & heat balance, and nutrition & diet. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the processes involved in anabolic and catabolic reactions.
List and describe the steps necessary for carbohydrate, lipid, and protein metabolism.
Explain the processes that regulate glucose levels during the absorptive and postabsorptive states.
Explain how metabolism is essential to maintaining body temperature (thermoregulation).
Summarize the importance of vitamins and minerals in the diet.

pathways.title: Protein Purification: Using a Nickel Column to Purify a His-tagged Protein
pathways.description: This pathway describes how a specific type of column chromatography, called affinity chromatography, can be used to purify a His-tagged protein. To learn more about this, see the interactive scrollable about Purifying a Protein.
learning_objectives: Design protein purification experiments.
Interpret sds page data summarizing a purification experiment..

pathways.title: 12 Days of Biopython
pathways.description: Bioinformatics is an important field in the biological sciences and allows scientists to work with large amounts of data; which is very useful for genetics research. In this pathway, you will be introduced to software and techniques used by bioinformaticians.
learning_objectives: You will become familiar with software used in the field of bioinformatics
You will understand the overlap between computer science and bioinformatics
You will learn how to use Python code to process biological datasets

pathways.title: Photosynthesis and Cellular Respiration
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores cellular energetics, energy capture, storage, and use. In this pathway, learners will explore using a leaf disk assay to measure the rate of photosynthesis and a microrespirometer to record the rate of cellular respiration. Through an analysis of energy in living systems, the role of ATP in cellular energetics, the complex photosynthetic processes that allow cells to capture energy from light, factors that impact the rate of photosynthesis, and the intricate processes that allow organisms to use energy stored in macromolecules to power cellular functions; learners will investigate the interconnectedness between photosynthesis and cellular respiration.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will be able to explain the role of energy in living systems.
Describe the photosynthetic processes that allow organisms to capture energy from light and transfer it to biological molecules for storage.
Compare the processes that allow organisms to use energy stored in biomolecules to power cellular processes.
Describe the relationship between variation in the number and types of molecules within cells and the overall ability of the organism to survive in a variety of environments.

pathways.title: The Integumentary System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at layers of the skin, accessory structures of the skin, functions of the integumentary system, and diseases, disorders, & injuries of the integumentary system. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the integumentary system and the role it plays in homeostasis.
Describe the layers of the skin and the functions of each layer.
Describe the accessory structures of the skin and the functions of each.
Describe the changes that occur in the integumentary system during the aging process.
Discuss several common diseases, disorders, and injuries that affect the integumentary system.
Explain treatments for some common diseases, disorders, and injuries of the integumentary system.

pathways.title: Patterns of Inheritance
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at Mendel's experiments, laws of inheritance, and extensions of the laws of inheritance. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain the scientific reasons for the success of mendel’s experimental work.
Describe the expected outcomes of monohybrid crosses involving dominant and recessive alleles.
Explain the relationship between genotypes and phenotypes in dominant and recessive gene systems.
Use a punnett square to calculate the expected proportions of genotypes and phenotypes in a monohybrid cross.
Explain mendel’s law of segregation and independent assortment in terms of genetics and the events of meiosis.
Explain the purpose and methods of a test cross.
Identify non-mendelian inheritance patterns such as incomplete dominance, codominance, multiple alleles, and sex linkage from the results of crosses.
Explain the effect of linkage and recombination on gamete genotypes.
Explain the phenotypic outcomes of epistatic effects among genes.

pathways.title: Data Literacy: Bioinformatics
pathways.description: This pathway focuses on the part of data literacy that involves dealing with databases specific to the life sciences and computer-based data mining.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of databases in life science.
Use basic programs that provide sequence information.
Use scientific literature searches.

pathways.title: Macromolecule Structure and Function
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores the chemistry of life as it relates to macromolecules. How does macromolecule structure impact biological function? In this pathway, learners will be introduced to the biochemical composition of carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids. They will also discover each macromolecule’s unique role in living systems. Through an analysis of monomers, bonds, and polymers; learners will explore the structural differences among these 4 biomolecules and interpret how changes at the subunit level can lead to overall changes in their function. 
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe the properties of monomers and the type of bonds that connect the monomers in biological macromolecules.
Explain how a change in the subunits of a polymer may lead to changes in the structure or function of the macromolecule.
Summarize the structural similarities and differences between DNA and RNA.

pathways.title: Experimental Design Simulations
pathways.description: Our experimental design simulations challenge learners to be be scientists. Rather than just following a prescribed protocol, visitors learn how to investigate a research question by designing their own experiment and validating the results through data analysis and feedback.
learning_objectives: Design your own experiments to test different research questions.
Predict the outcome of an experiment.
Develop practical lab skills and techniques.

pathways.title: Modern Understandings of Inheritance
pathways.description: The gene is the physical unit of inheritance, and genes are arranged in a linear order on chromosomes. Chromosome behavior and interaction during meiosis explain, at a cellular level, inheritance patterns that we observe in populations. Genetic disorders involving alterations in chromosome number or structure may have dramatic effects and can prevent a fertilized egg from developing. This pathway provides an in-depth look at chromosomal theory & genetic linkage, and the chromosomal basis of inherited disorders. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Discuss sutton’s chromosomal theory of inheritance.
Describe genetic linkage.
Explain the process of homologous recombination, or crossing over.
Describe chromosome creation.
Calculate the distances between three genes on a chromosome using a three-point test cross.
Describe how a karyogram is created.
Explain how nondisjunction leads to disorders in chromosome number.
Compare/contrast disorders that aneuploidy causes.
Describe how errors in chromosome structure occur through inversions and translocations.

pathways.title: Evidence of Evolution
pathways.description: This is the third session in the Evolution Flipped Course from iBiology. Some of Darwin’s earliest supporters found evidence of evolution in fossils.  Fossils continue to inform our ideas of evolution as Neil Shubin tells us.  He describes his search for a fossil link between fish and land animals and the excitement of finally finding it. In a review of session 2, Sarah Tishkoff reminds us of what fossils tell us about human evolution and migration. Further evidence of evolution comes from comparing DNA sequences. Hopi Hoekstra studies the impact of genes and the environment on the evolution of specific phenotypes. She found that populations of mice living on white sand beaches were more likely to evolve light colored fur than populations in areas with dark soil.  (Why do you think this happens?) In about half of the populations, this change was due to a single mutation in the MC1 receptor.  In an interesting example of convergent evolution, the same mutation causes coat color variation in some dogs and may have caused coat colors to vary in woolly mammoths!

pathways.title: Matter and Change
pathways.description: Chemistry is often defined as the science of matter and how it changes. This pathway introduces the composition of matter and the physical and chemical changes it can undergo.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Recall that all matter is composed of atoms.
Describe the difference between a solid, liquid and gas using the concept of energy.
Compare/contrast physical and chemical changes to matter.

pathways.title: Mendel's Experiments and Heredity
pathways.description: During the 19th century, long before chromosomes or genes had been identified, Johann Gregor Mendel set the framework for genetics by studying a simple biological system, the garden pea. He conducted methodical, quantitative analyses using large sample sizes. Mendel’s work laid the foundation for the fundamental principles of heredity. We now know that genes, carried on chromosomes, are the basic functional units of heredity with the capacity to be replicated, expressed, repressed, modified and mutated. Today, the postulates put forth by Mendel form the basis of classical, or Mendelian, genetics. Genes do not all obey the tenets of Mendelian genetics, but Mendel’s experiments serve as an excellent starting point for thinking about inheritance. This pathway provides an in-depth look at Mendel’s experiments & the laws of probability; characteristics & traits; and the laws of inheritance. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Discuss why mendel’s experimental work was so successful.
Explain how the sum and product rules of probability predicts the outcomes of monohybrid crosses involving dominant and recessive alleles?.
Describe the relationship between genotypes and phenotypes in dominant and recessive gene systems.
Discuss how a punnett square can be used to calculate expected proportions of genotypes and phenotypes in a monohybrid cross.
Discuss how phenomena such as incomplete dominance, codominance, recessive lethals, multiple alleles, and sex linkage explain deviations from mendel’s model of inheritance.
Examine the relationship between mendel’s law of segregation and independent assortment in terms of genetics and the events of meiosis.
Describe how the forked-lined method and probability rules can be used to calculate the probability of genotypes and phenotypes from multiple gene crosses.
Explain how linkage, cross-over, epistasis, and recombination violate mendel’s laws of inheritance.

pathways.title: Lab Tips
pathways.description: This collection of videos covers everything from preparing agar plates and creating a DNA master mix for PCR reactions to isolating individual bacterial colonies and troubleshooting bacterial growth. Gain a solid foundation in laboratory techniques and be equipped to conduct successful experiments.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to efficiently create a DNA master mix for multiple PCR reactions while accounting for potential pipetting errors and maintaining the mix's temperature.
You will be able to properly visualize and accurately excise DNA bands from agarose gels while minimizing the risk of contamination and damage to the gel imager.
You will be able to properly prepare agar plates for bacterial culture and incubation, isolating and growing individual bacterial colonies while minimizing contamination.

You will be able to optimize their qPCR experiments by properly diluting their samples, creating a standard curve with appropriate differences in CT values, and including a no template control to verify the CT values.

You will be able to conduct agarose gel electrophoresis experiments safely and effectively, optimizing their gel preparation, adding ethidium bromide, and understanding the differences between TAE and TBE buffers.

pathways.title: Gases
pathways.description: Gases exist across a wide range of temperature and pressures, and their properties obey some fairly simple laws. This pathway provides resources to help students understand the behaviour of gases, and the relationships that exist between their pressure, volume and temperature.
learning_objectives: Define the temperature, volume and pressure of a gas.
Explain the effect that changes in temperature, pressure or volume have on the other properties of a gas.
Calculate the properties of gases using the gas laws.
Chart the relationships that exist between the macroscopic properties of gases.

pathways.title: How Is DNA Sequenced, and What Can We Learn?
pathways.description: In this pathway, students will use a selection of TAS2R38 DNA sequences and bioinformatics software to determine the differences between the sequences. They will explore how the various genotypes relate to the taster phenotypes, and discuss how small the differences are between the DNA sequences. They will also learn what might cause a taster status to not conform with a genotype.
learning_objectives: Students will be able to describe the components of a Sanger sequencing reaction, including the product.
Students will be able to define the term chromatogram, and explain how it is useful in analyzing a DNA sample.
Students will be able to interpret data from a DNA sequencing chromatogram.
Users will understand how to align and compare DNA sequences for the TAS2R38 gene using Benchling.
Students will be able to define the term single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP).
Students will be able to identify the location of SNPs in the TAS2R38 gene.
Students will be able to describe the connection between SNPs and phenotypes.
Students will be able to use software (SNPedia, dbSNP and Ensembl) to explore the connection between SNPs in the TAS2R38 gene and bitter taste phenotypes.
Students will be able to describe the relationship between protein structure and function.
Students will be able to explain how a SNP might alter the performance of the TAS2R38 receptor protein.

pathways.title: Cell Reproduction
pathways.description: A human, as well as every sexually reproducing organism, begins life as a fertilized egg (embryo) or zygote. Trillions of cell divisions subsequently occur in a controlled manner to produce a complex, multicellular human. In other words, that original single cell is the ancestor of every other cell in the body. Once a being is fully grown, cell reproduction is still necessary to repair or regenerate tissues. All multicellular organisms use cell division for growth, maintenance, and repair of tissues. Cell division is tightly regulated, and the occasional failure of regulation can have life-threatening consequences. Single-celled organisms use cell division as their method of reproduction. This pathway provides an in-depth look at cell division; the cell cycle; control of the cell cycle; cancer; and prokaryotic cell division. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Explain the relationship between chromosomes, genes, and traits in prokaryotes and eukaryotes.
Identify the processes that occur during the three stages of interphase.
Describe how the chromosomes behave during the mitotic phase.
Identify examples of internal and external mechanisms that control the cell cycle.
Name the molecules involved in controlling the cell cycle through positive and negative regulation.
Identify the causes of uncontrolled cell growth.
Explain why uncontrolled cell growth often cause cancer.
Describe how the process of binary fission in prokaryotes differs from cell division in eukaryotes.

pathways.title: Genes and Proteins
pathways.description: The definition of gene has progressed from being an abstract unit of heredity in Mendel’s time to our current concept of a tangible molecular entity capable of replication, expression, and mutation. Currently, we can perform tests for many genetic diseases, but these tests create ethical and legal issues. Fortunately, the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act of 2008 protects American citizens from discrimination from both insurance companies and employers based on genetic information. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the genetic code; prokaryotic & eukaryotic transcription; RNA processing in eukaryotes; and ribosomes & protein synthesis. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Define the “central dogma” of protein synthesis.
Describe the genetic code, and how nucleotide sequence prescribes the amino acid and polypeptide sequence.
Identify the steps, in order, in prokaryotic transcription.
Explain how and when transcription is terminated.
Identify the steps in eukaryotic transcription.
Compare/contrast the structural and functional similarities and differences among the three rna polymerases.
Explain why helper proteins are necessary for the formation of the final protein during rna splicing in higher organisms.
Identify the different sequential steps in protein synthesis.
Explain the role of ribosomes in protein synthesis.

pathways.title: Molecular Shapes
pathways.description: The spatial arrangement of atoms in a molecule has a massive role in the properties of that substance. This pathway provides resources to explain why molecules take the shapes that they do, and how to determine what shape the molecule will take.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Identify the molecular shape based on the number of bonds and lone pairs of electrons.
List the different molecular shapes that can be formed.
Diagram lewis dot diagrams to determine the number of bonds between atoms.
Explain the effect of lone pairs of electrons on the shape of molecules.

pathways.title: Work, Energy and Simple Machines
pathways.description: This pathway explores the fundamental concept of energy, and how energy can be transferred between objects. Different types of energy are defined, and the law of conservation of energy is explained. Simple machines are explained as a way of making energy transfer between objects easier. Problems are provided to check understanding.
learning_objectives: Define the terms energy and work.
Identify different types of energy.
Explain the relationship between energy and work.
Calculate the energy of an object or the work done on the object using the work energy theorem.
Describe different types of of simple machines.

pathways.title: Cellular Respiration
pathways.description: Energy enters an organism’s body in one form and is converted into another form that can fuel the organism’s life functions. In the process of photosynthesis, plants and other photosynthetic producers take in energy in the form of light (solar energy) and convert it into chemical energy, glucose, which stores this energy in its chemical bonds. Then, a series of metabolic pathways, collectively called cellular respiration, extract the energy from the carbon–carbon bonds of glucose and convert it into a form that all living things can use—both producers, such as plants, and consumers, such as animals. Nearly all organisms perform glycolysis, the first part of both aerobic and anaerobic respiration. This pathway provides an in-depth look at energy in living systems; glycolysis; oxidation of pyruvate & the citric acid cycle; oxidative phosphorylation; metabolism without oxygen; connections of carbohydrate, protein, & lipid metabolic pathways; and regulation of cellular respiration. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Explain the importance of electrons for the transfer of energy in living systems.
Describe how atp is used by the cell as an energy source.
Identify the overall result, in terms of molecules produced, in the breakdown of glucose by glycolysis.
Describe how pyruvate, the product of glycolysis, is prepared for entry into the citric acid cycle.
List the products of the citric acid cycle.
Describe how electrons move through the electron transport chain.
Explain what happens to the energy levels of electrons as they move through the electron transport chain.
Recall how many atp molecules are produced by chemiosmosis.
Describe how a proton (h+) gradient is established and maintained by the electron transport chain.
Explain the fundamental difference between anaerobic cellular respiration and the different types of fermentation.
Recall how carbohydrate metabolic pathways, glycolysis, and the citric acid cycle interrelate with protein and lipid metabolism pathways.
Describe the mechanisms that control cellular respiration.

pathways.title: Diversity of Microbes, Fungi, and Protists
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at prokaryotic diversity, eukaryotic origins, protists and fungi. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the evolutionary history of prokaryotes.
Describe the basic structure of a typical prokaryote.
Identify bacterial diseases that caused historically important plagues and epidemics.
Describe the uses of prokaryotes in food processing and bioremediation.
Describe the endosymbiotic theory.
Explain the origin of mitochondria and chloroplasts.
Describe the main characteristics of protists.
Describe important pathogenic species of protists.
Describe the roles of protists as food sources and as decomposers.
List the characteristics of fungi.
Describe fungal parasites and pathogens of plants and infections in humans.
Describe the importance of fungi to the environment.
Summarize the beneficial role of fungi in food and beverage preparation and in the chemical and pharmaceutical industry.

pathways.title: How to Pour a Polyacrylamide Gel
pathways.description: This pathway describes how a polyacrylamide gel works and explains how to pour a polyacrylamide gel.
learning_objectives: Describe why molecules separate out in a polyacrylamide gel based on their molecular weight.
List the steps required to pour a polyacrylamide gel.
Explain the steps required to pour a polyacrylamide gel.

pathways.title: Finding a Research Idea
pathways.description: This pathway aims to help students become familiar with the ways in which scientists make observations and apply the scientific method to understand what they observed and come up with ways to apply that new insight to further their research, and improve products and processes.
learning_objectives: Plan experimental procedures to test hypotheses.
Develop your ability to think like a scientist.
Support classmates in their learning process.

pathways.title: Genes and Proteins
pathways.description: Since the rediscovery of Mendel’s work in 1900, the definition of the gene has progressed from an abstract unit of heredity to a tangible molecular entity capable of replication, expression, and mutation. Genes are composed of DNA and are linearly arranged on chromosomes. Genes specify the sequences of amino acids, which are the building blocks of proteins. In turn, proteins are responsible for orchestrating nearly every function of the cell. Both genes and the proteins they encode are absolutely essential to life as we know it. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the genetic code, prokaryotic transcription, eukaryotic transcription, RNA processing in eukaryotes, and ribosomes & protein synthesis. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain the “central dogma” of dna-protein synthesis.
Describe the genetic code and how the nucleotide sequence prescribes the amino acid and the protein sequence.
List the different steps in prokaryotic transcription.
Discuss the role of promoters in prokaryotic transcription.
Describe how and when transcription is terminated.
List the steps in eukaryotic transcription.
Discuss the role of rna polymerases in transcription.
Compare/contrast the three rna polymerases.
Explain the significance of transcription factors.
Describe the different steps in rna processing.
Interpret the significance of exons, introns, and splicing for mrnas.
Explain how trnas and rrnas are processed.
Describe the different steps in protein synthesis.
Discuss the role of ribosomes in protein synthesis.

pathways.title: Electrochemistry
pathways.description: One of the driving mechanisms in certain chemical reactions is the exchange of electrons. In these reactions, one substance gains electrons while another substance loses them. This pathway provides resources relating to electrochemistry, reduction & oxidation, and different types of electrochemical cells.
learning_objectives: Define oxidation and reduction.
Diagram the flow of electrons in an electrochemical cell.
Identify which substances are being oxidized or reduced in a chemical reaction.
Compare/contrast a galvanic cell with an electrolytic cell.

pathways.title: The Respiratory System
pathways.description: Breathing is an involuntary event. How often a breath is taken and how much air is inhaled or exhaled are tightly regulated by the respiratory center in the brain. Humans, when they aren’t exerting themselves, breathe approximately 15 times per minute on average. In addition to exhaling carbon dioxide when we breathe, scientists believe people with cancerous lung cells also exhale tiny amounts of volatile organic compounds. This pathway provides an in-depth look at systems of gas exchange; gas exchange across respiratory surfaces; breathing; transport of gases in human bodily fluids. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Explain how the lungs are protected from particulate matter.
Illustrate how air passes from the outside environment to the lungs.
Identify the names and descriptions of lung volumes and capacities.
Describe how gas pressure influences the movement of gases into and out of the body.
Illustrate how the structure of the lungs and thoracic cavity control the mechanics of breathing.
Discuss the importance of compliance and resistance in the lungs.
Explain how carbon dioxide is transported from body tissues to the lungs.
Describe how oxygen is bound to hemoglobin and transported to body tissues.

pathways.title: Muscle Tissue
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at overview of muscle tissues, skeletal muscle, muscle fiber contraction & relaxation, nervous system control of muscle tension, types of muscle fibers, exercise & muscle performance, cardiac muscle tissue, smooth muscle, and development & regeneration of muscle tissue. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain the organization of muscle tissue.
Describe the function and structure of skeletal, cardiac muscle, and smooth muscle.
Explain how muscles work with tendons to move the body.
Describe how muscles contract and relax.
Define the process of muscle metabolism.
Explain how the nervous system controls muscle tension.
Explain the connections between exercise and muscle performance.
Explain the development and regeneration of muscle tissue.

pathways.title: The Nervous System and Nervous Tissue
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at basic structure & function of the nervous system, nervous tissue, the function of nervous tissue, the action potential, and communication between neurons. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Name the major divisions of the nervous system, both anatomical and functional.
Describe the functional and structural differences between gray matter and white matter structures.
Name the parts of the multipolar neuron in order of polarity.
List the types of glial cells and assign each to the proper division of the nervous system, along with their function(s).
Identify the major functions of the nervous system: sensation, integration, and response.
Describe the components of the membrane that establish the resting membrane potential.
Describe the changes that occur to the membrane that result in the action potential.
Explain the differences between types of graded potentials.
Categorize the major neurotransmitters by chemical type and effect.

pathways.title: Gene Regulation
pathways.description: Most people know that regular exercise is important to maintain good health. It promotes cardiovascular health and helps to prevent obesity. Scientists have now discovered that long-term endurance training also changes how genes are expressed in muscle tissue. This pathway provides an in-depth look at regulation of gene expression; prokaryotic & eukaryotic epigenetic gene regulation; eukaryotic transcriptional & post-transcriptional gene regulation; eukaryotic translational & post-translational gene regulation; and cancer & gene regulation. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Explain how prokaryotic gene regulation differs from eukaryotic gene regulation.
Define operons and explain the roles of activators, inducers, and repressors in regulating operons and gene expression.
Describe the science of epigenetics and how this process is regulated.
Explain the role of transcription factors, enhancers, and repressors in gene regulation.
Summarize how gene expression is controlled through post-transcriptional modifications of rna molecules.
Describe the different ways in which translational and post-translational control of gene expression takes place.
Explain how changes in gene expression can cause cancer.
Explain how changes to gene expression at different levels can disrupt the cell cycle.

pathways.title: Claim, Evidence, Reasoning (CER) Framework in STEM
pathways.description: In this pathway, you'll learn to apply the claims, evidence and reasoning (CER) framework to analyze data and construct arguments.

pathways.title: MassBioEd's Virtual Molecular Rainbow Lab
pathways.description: Explore how molecules differ in size, shape, and charge, and learn how agarose gel electrophoresis separates them based on these properties. Through interactive activities, you'll interpret gel results, infer the relative sizes of dye molecules, analyze unknown mixtures, and predict molecular movement in a gel.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to recall molecules vary in size, shape, and charge.
Explain what agarose gel electrophoresis is and how it is used to separate molecules based on size and charge.
Interpret the results of molecular separation by gel electrophoresis.
Infer the relative size of dye molecules as a function of movement through the agarose gel.
Infer the components of an unknown dye mixture.
Predict how additional molecules would have migrated in gel electrophoresis based upon size and charge.

pathways.title: The Respiratory System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at organs and structures of the respiratory system, the lungs, the process of breathing, gas exchange, transport of gases, modifications in respiratory functions, and embryonic development of the respiratory system. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: List the structures of the respiratory system.
List the major functions of the respiratory system.
Describe the forces that allow for air movement into and out of the lungs.
Describe the process of gas exchange.
Summarize the process of oxygen and carbon dioxide transport within the respiratory system.
Diagram a flow chart illustrating how respiration is controlled.
Discuss how the respiratory system responds to exercise.
Describe the development of the respiratory system in the embryo.

pathways.title: Exploring Our DNA
pathways.description: In this pathway, students connect drug metabolism to their understanding of genetics. In continuing to learn more about the TAS2R38 gene, they extract their own DNA from their cheek cells. They then isolate and amplify a specific short segment of their DNA to determine their taster genotype. Finally, students explore how the human genome was mapped. They also read about methods that scientists use to find the locations of genes, and then use their knowledge to map fictitious traits onto a chromosome.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand that not all patients respond to medication in the same way.
Describe factors that influence an individual’s ability to process and respond to medication.
Perform a DNA extraction experiment in a systematic and precise fashion.
Perform a PCR experiment involving the TAS2R38 gene.
Explain how scientists used the inheritance patterns of genes to develop a way to map their location on a chromosome.
Use experimental evidence to identify the location of several fictitious traits on a chromosome.

pathways.title: The Nervous System
pathways.description: Videos detailing the anatomy and workings of the nervous system and its components.

pathways.title: Structure and Function of Plasma Membranes
pathways.description: The plasma membrane, the cell membrane, has many functions, but the most basic one is to define the cell's borders and keep the cell functional. The plasma membrane is selectively permeable. This means that the membrane allows some materials to freely enter or leave the cell, while other materials cannot move freely, but require a specialized structure, and occasionally, even energy investment for crossing.This pathway provides an in-depth look at components and structure, passive transport, active transport, and bulk transport. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain the cell membrane fluid mosaic model.
Describe phospholipid, protein, and carbohydrate functions in membranes.
Discuss membrane fluidity.
Explain how electrochemical gradients affect ions.
Identify and distinguish between primary active transport and secondary active transport.
Describe endocytosis, including phagocytosis, pinocytosis, and receptor-mediated endocytosis.
Identify and understand the process of exocytosis.

pathways.title: Diversity of Plants
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at the plant kingdom, seedless plants, and seed plants including gymnosperms and angiosperms. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the major characteristics of the plant kingdom.
Discuss the challenges to plant life on land.
Describe the adaptations that allowed plants to colonize land.
Describe the distinguishing traits of the three types of bryophytes.
Identify the new traits that first appear in seedless vascular plants.
Describe the major classes of seedless vascular plants.
Discuss the type of seeds produced by gymnosperms, as well as other characteristics of gymnosperms.
List the four groups of modern-day gymnosperms and provide examples of each.
Describe the main parts of a flower and their purpose.
Describe the life cycle of an angiosperm.
Discuss the two main groups into which flower plants are divided, as well as explain how basal angiosperms differ from others.

pathways.title: Ecosystems and the Biosphere
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at Waterford's energy flow through ecosystems, biogeochemical cycles, terrestrial biomes, and aquatic and marine biomes. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the basic types of ecosystems on earth.
Compare/contrast food chains and food webs and recognize the importance of each.
Describe how organisms acquire energy in a food web and in associated food chains.
Explain how the efficiency of energy transfers between trophic levels effects ecosystem.
Discuss the biogeochemical cycles of water, carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur.
Explain how human activities have impacted these cycles and the resulting potential consequences for earth.
Identify the two major abiotic factors that determine the type of terrestrial biome in an area.
Identify distinguishing characteristics of each of the eight major terrestrial biomes.
Describe the effects of abiotic factors on the composition of plant and animal communities in aquatic biomes.
Compare/contrast the characteristics of the ocean zones.
Summarize the characteristics of standing water and flowing water in freshwater biomes.

pathways.title: The Appendicular Skeleton
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at the pectoral girdle, bones of the upper limb, the pelvic girdle & pelvis, bones of the lower limb, and development of the appendicular skeleton. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Discuss the bones of the pectoral and pelvic girdles, and describe how these unite the limbs with the axial skeleton.
Describe the bones of the upper limb, including the bones of the arm, forearm, wrist, and hand.
Identify the features of the pelvis and explain how these differ between the adult male and female pelvis.
Describe the bones of the lower limb, including the bones of the thigh, leg, ankle, and foot.
Describe the embryonic formation and growth of the limb bones.

pathways.title: Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction
pathways.description: The ability to reproduce 'in kind' is a basic characteristic of all living things. 'In kind' means that the offspring of an organism closely resembles its parent or parents. Hippopotamuses give birth to hippopotamus calves, Joshua trees produce Joshua tree seedlings, and flamingos lay eggs that hatch into flamingo chicks. 'In kind' can mean exactly the same. Many unicellular organisms, such as yeast, and a few multicellular organisms, such as sponges, can produce genetically identical clones of themselves through cell division. Sexual reproduction, specifically meiosis and fertilization, introduces variation into offspring. Variation is an important component of a species evolutionary success. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the process of meiosis and sexual reproduction. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Describe how chromosomes behave during meiosis.
Discuss the cellular events that occur during meiosis.
Compare/contrast the similarities and differences between meiosis and mitosis.
Explain how the process of meiosis can generate genetic variation.
Explain why meiosis and sexual reproduction are considered evolved traits.
Discuss why variation among offspring is a potential evolutionary advantage to sexual reproduction.
Name the three different life-cycles among sexual multicellular organisms and their commonalities.

pathways.title: Basic Lab Skills - Separating Biological Molecules
pathways.description: This pathway shows how to separate DNA and proteins with gel electrophoresis and how to purify proteins from cells. There is a list of foundational method videos and interactives and the pathway is used as a glossary of lab techniques, rather than an ordered sequence of resources.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate how centrifugation separates a solution into a pellet and supernatant.
Use gel electrophoresis to separate and identify biological molecules.
Use chromatography to purify proteins.
Recognize foundational lab protocols and equipment.
Identify the most appropriate and useful lab protocols and equipment.
Use methods and lab equipment to more easily in the real lab environment.

pathways.title: Introduction to Python
pathways.description: This pathway offers interactive lessons that introduce users to programming using Python. This pathway is designed to teach users the basics of programming in Python to solve real-world science problems ranging from biology to statistics to physics. These lessons assume no prior knowledge in programming or any of these scientific fields. Programming beginners will probably want to proceed through the first lessons in order, but you should also feel free to jump to lessons that interest and excite you!
learning_objectives: Identify and implement four mathematical operations to make calculations with the appropriate python 3 syntax.
Interpret and use variables in mathematical computations.
Explain the four data types and how to convert between them.
Design if statements to output appropriate statements based on variable inputs.
Construct if, elif, and else statements in useful situations.
Design a program that calculates a phenotypic ratio for a given sample and indicates whether that ratio is expected or unexpected.
Explain the use of python packages and recognize how to import them.
Describe how to load data into python from an outside source.
Build interpretable histograms, box plots, and scatter plots using real biological data.
Identify appropriate situations to employ for loops.
Design a program to create a protein from a nucleotide sequence.
Build graphs of data using appropriate python 3 syntax.
Solve for best-fit lines using python.
Use first-order rate constants to calculate a half-life.

pathways.title: Kinematics in One Dimension
pathways.description: This pathway provides resources for understanding motion in one dimension. The fundamentals of vector and scalars are covered, and the key concepts of position, displacement, speed, velocity and acceleration are explained. The pathway concludes with a series of questions to test understanding of the topic.
learning_objectives: Define scalras and vectors, and provide examples of each of these types of quantities.
Define the terms reference frame, position, displacement, distance, velocity, speed and acceleration.
Explain the difference between speed and velocity.
Calculate various kinematic quantities, given a situation.
Interpret graphs of motion in order to describe the movment of an object.
Chart the motion of an object by drawing graphs of motion.

pathways.title: Light
pathways.description: This pathway explores the properties of light. Light is described as a wave which is able to reflect, refract and diffract when it encounters surfaces. Applications of light's wave properties such as single and double slit diffraction are discussed, as well as how different colours occur as combinations of different frequencies of light. Problems are provided to check for understanding.
learning_objectives: Recall the light has wave-like properties.
Define the terms reflection, refraction and diffraction.
Calculate the path of light rays passing between media using Snell's law.
Define huygen's principle and use it to explain how diffraction occurs.
Discuss the consequences of diffraction in cases such as single and double slit diffraction.
Explain how our colour vision arises as a combination of red, blue and green light.

pathways.title: How Can We Measure pH?
pathways.description: pH describes the acidity of a solution. pH can be measured by pH indicators, pH strips, and pH meters. This pathway will familiarize you with all of these pH measurement techniques.
learning_objectives: You will explain the concept of pH indicators as a way to measure pH.
You will interpret how the pH indicator Bromothymol blue (BTB) works.
You will use a pH indicator interactive to understand how changes in carbon dioxide change the pH of a solution.
You will describe the function of a pH meter to measure pH in a quantitative way.

pathways.title: Modern Understandings of Inheritance
pathways.description: According to the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, approximately 95% of those who commit homicide are men. While behavior is shaped by the environment one grows up and lives in, genetics also play a role. role. For example, scientists have discovered genes that appear to increase one’s tendency to exhibit aggressive behavior. One of the genes, called MAOA, is located on the X chromosome. Men only have one copy of the gene, since men only have one X chromosome. Women, however, have two copies of the X chromosome and therefore two copies of the gene. Therefore, women who inherit the variant allele will most likely also have a normal allele to counteract its effects. It is important to note that many men inherit the variant copy of MAOA and only some commit violent crimes. The environment seems to play a much more critical role. This pathway provides an in-depth look at chromosomal theory & genetic linkages and the chromosomal basis of inherited disorders. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Discuss the relationship among genetic linkage, crossing over, and genetic variation.
Interpret and illustrate the genetic consequences that result from nondisjunction and errors in chromosome structure through inversions and translocations.

pathways.title: Cell Structure and Function
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores cell structure and function. In this pathway, learners will analyze cell size dynamics, organelles that reflect common ancestry, the secretory pathway, and complex structural interactions that contribute to maintaining an internal environment within the cell. Through an analysis of subcellular components, energy capturing structures, endosymbiotic organelles, and compartmentalization, learners will explore the structural differences between eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells and interpret how these features impact overall cellular function. 
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe the structure and function of subcellular components and organelles, including endosymbiotic organelles that reflect the common ancestry of all known life.
Explain how interactions between subcellular components and organelles contribute to the function of the cell, including the maintenance of an internal environment within the cell.
Describe the structural features of a cell that allow organisms to capture, store, and use energy.
Explain the effect of surface-area-to-volume ratios on the exchange of materials between cells or organisms and the environment.
Compare compartmentalization between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.

pathways.title: Forces and Vectors
pathways.description: This pathway explores the concept of forces. The basic techniques of vector algebra are discussed, which allows for an introduction to Newton's Three Laws of Motion. These laws describe how objects react when forces are applied to them. The pathway concludes with a series of questions for students to test their understanding.
learning_objectives: Illustrate how vectors can be added together using vector diagrams.
Calculate the result of several vectors acting together.
Recall newton's three laws of motion.
Calculate the acceleration of an object when several forces are acting on it.
Identify action-rection force pairs.
Define different types of forces.

pathways.title: DNA Mutations and Genetic Diseases
pathways.description: In this pathway, you will explore the origins of genetic diseases. You will learn about different types of genetic mutations and their impacts, and get hands-on experience with bioinformatics tools to investigate why some mutations cause genetic diseases and others do not.
learning_objectives: Explain why some mutations in chromosomal dna can lead to genetic diseases.
Use bioinformatics tools and databases to investigate the cause of genetic diseases.
Identify pathogenic single nucleotide polymorphisms that result in cystic fibrosis.
Interpret database search results from the ncbi website.
Describe at least one example of a genetic disease and its effects.

pathways.title: Biological Macromolecules
