pathways.description: Food provides the body with the nutrients it needs to survive. Many of these critical nutrients are biological macromolecules, or large molecules, necessary for life. Different smaller organic molecule (monomer) combinations build these macromolecules (polymers). What specific biological macromolecules do living things require? How do these molecules form? What functions do they serve? This pathway provides an in-depth look at synthesis of biological macromolecules, carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Interpret macromolecule synthesis.
Explain dehydration (or condensation) and hydrolysis reactions.
Discuss the role of carbohydrates in cells and in the extracellular materials of animals and plants.
Explain carbohydrate classifications.
List common monosaccharides, disaccharides, and polysaccharides.
Compare/contrast saturated and unsaturated fatty acids.
Describe the four major types of lipids.
Explain the role of fats in storing energy.
Describe phospholipids and their role in cells.
Define the basic structure of a steroid and some steroid functions.
Explain how cholesterol helps maintain the plasma membrane's fluid nature.
Describe the functions proteins perform in the cell and in tissues.
Discuss the relationship between amino acids and proteins.
Explain the four levels of protein organization.
Describe the ways in which protein shape and function are linked.
Describe nucleic acids' structure and define the two types of nucleic acids.
Explain dna's structure and role.
Explain rna's structure and roles.

pathways.title: The Xchange: Perspectives on COVID-19
pathways.description: A virtual interview series from LabXchange at Harvard University, the Xchange showcased the diverse roles, projects, and initiatives that make up the intersecting fields of science, education, and health. Hosted by the late Dr. Robert Lue, the series featured a range of perspectives on the impact of COVID-19.
learning_objectives: Examine the COVID-19 pandemic from a variety of different perspectives.
Infer how the COVID-19 pandemic affected the fields of education, health care, and scientific research.

pathways.title: Structure and Function of Plasma Membranes
pathways.description: The plasma membrane, which is also called the cell membrane, has many functions; but, the most basic one is to define the borders and act as gatekeeper for the cell. The plasma membrane is selectively permeable, meaning some molecules can freely enter or leave the cell. Others require help from specialized structures, other molecules, or require energy in order to cross. This pathway provides an in-depth look at components & structure; and passive, active & bulk transport. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Illustrate how the fluid mosaic model describes the structure and components of the plasma cell membrane.
Explain how the molecular components of the membrane provide fluidity.
Identify and describe the properties of life..
Discuss why and how passive transport occurs across membranes.
Define tonicity, and explain how it is relevant to passive transport.
Discuss how electrochemical gradients affect the active transport of ions and molecules across membranes.
Compare/contrast the differences among the different types of endocytosis: (phagocytosis, pinocytosis, and receptor-mediated endocytosis) and exocytosis.

pathways.title: Evolution and Its Processes
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at discovering how populations change, mechanisms of evolution, evidence of evolution, speciation, and common misconceptions about evolution. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain how darwin’s theory of evolution differed from the current view at the time.
Describe how the present-day theory of evolution was developed.
Describe how population genetics is used to study the evolution of populations.
Describe the four basic causes of evolution: natural selection, mutation, genetic drift, and gene flow.
Explain how each evolutionary force can influence the allele frequencies of a population.
Explain sources of evidence for evolution.
Define homologous and vestigial structures.
Describe the definition of species and how species are identified as different.
Explain allopatric and sympatric speciation.
Describe adaptive radiation.

pathways.title: Conservation Biology and Biodiversity
pathways.description: In the 1980s, biologists working in Lake Victoria in Africa discovered one of the most extraordinary products of evolution on the planet. Located in the Great Rift Valley, Lake Victoria is a large lake about 68,900 km2 in area (larger than Lake Huron, the second largest of North America’s Great Lakes). Biologists were studying species of a family of fish called cichlids. They found that as they sampled for fish in different locations of the lake, they never stopped finding new species, and they identified nearly 500 evolved types of cichlids. But while studying these variations, they quickly discovered that the invasive Nile Perch was destroying the lake’s cichlid population, bringing hundreds of cichlid species to extinction with devastating rapidity. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the biodiversity crisis; the importance of biodiversity to human life; threats to biodiversity; and preserving biodiversity. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Define the different types of biodiversity.
Explain why biodiversity can be described as the equilibrium of naturally fluctuating rates of extinction and speciation.
Identify examples of historical causes of high extinction rates in earth’s history.
Describe how chemical diversity benefits humans.
Name components of biodiversity that support human agriculture.
Discuss ecosystem services.
List significant threats to biodiversity.
Describe the impacts of habitat loss, exotic species, and hunting on biodiversity.
Identify examples of early and predicted effects of climate change on biodiversity.
Recall examples of new technologies for measuring biodiversity and legislative framework for conservation.
Discuss the principles and challenges of conservation preserve design.
Identify examples of the effects of habitat restoration.
Explain the roles of zoos in biodiversity conservation.

pathways.title: Momentum
pathways.description: This pathway explores the concept of momentum. Momentum as a quantity is defined, and its relationship to force explained. The law of conservation of momentum is introduced, as well as the term impulse. Problems are included at the end of the chapter to check for understanding.
learning_objectives: Define momentum and impulse.
Discuss how momentum is conserved in a closed sytem.
Calculate masses or velocities of objects using the law of conservation of momentum.
Explain how the force applied to an object relates to its change in momentum.
Define the term elastic collision.

pathways.title: Conservation and Biodiversity
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at the importance of biodiversity, threats to biodiversity, and preserving biodiversity. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Describe biodiversity as the equilibrium of naturally fluctuating rates of extinction and speciation.
Identify benefits of biodiversity to humans.
Identify significant threats to biodiversity.
Explain the effects of habitat loss, exotic species, and hunting on biodiversity.
Identify the early and predicted effects of climate change on biodiversity.
Describe biodiversity as the equilibrium of naturally fluctuating rates of extinction and speciation.
Explain the legislative framework for conservation.
Identify the factors important in conservation preserve design.
Identify examples of the effects of habitat restoration.
Identify the role of zoos in biodiversity conservation.

pathways.title: Ecosystems, Energy Flow, and Biogeochemical Cycles
pathways.description: In 1993, an interesting example of ecosystem dynamics occurred when a rare lung disease struck inhabitants of the southwestern United States. This disease had an alarming rate of fatalities, killing more than half of early patients, many of whom were Native Americans. These formerly healthy young adults died from complete respiratory failure. The disease was unknown, and the Centers for Disease Control (CDC), the United States government agency responsible for managing potential epidemics, was brought in to investigate. The scientists could have learned about the disease had they known to talk with the Navajo healers who lived in the area and who had observed the connection between rainfall and mice populations, thereby predicting the 1993 outbreak. This pathway provides an in-depth look at ecology for ecosystems; energy flow through ecosystems; and biogeochemical cycles. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Categorize the basic types of ecosystems on earth.
Discuss the methods that ecologists use to study ecosystem structure and dynamics.
Explain the different methods of ecosystem modeling.
Explain the difference between a food chain and a food web, and the importance of each.
Describe how organisms acquire energy in a food web and associated food chains.
Explain how the efficiency of energy transfer between trophic levels affects ecosystem structure and dynamics.
List the characteristics of each trophic level in an ecosystem, and explain how ecological pyramids can be used to model them.
Describe the basic stages in the biogeochemical cycles of water, nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur.
Discuss how human activities have impacted these biogeochemical cycles, and the potential consequences for earth.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Microbial Growth
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of how microbes grow; oxygen requirements for microbial growth; the effects of ph on microbial growth; temperature and microbial growth; other environmental conditions that affect growth

pathways.title: Fluid, Electrolyte, and Acid-Base Balance
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at body fluids & fluid compartments, water balance, electrolyte balance, acid-base balance, and disorders of acid-base balance. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Identify the body’s main fluid compartments.
Define plasma osmolality and identify two ways in which plasma osmolality is maintained.
Identify the six ions most important to the function of the body.
Define buffer and discuss the role of buffers in the body.
Explain why bicarbonate must be conserved rather than reabsorbed in the kidney.
Identify the normal range of blood ph and name the conditions where one has a blood ph that is either too high or too low.

pathways.title: The Urinary System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at physical characteristics of urine, gross anatomy of urine transport, gross anatomy of the kidney, microscopic anatomy of the kidney, physiology of urine formation, tubular reabsorption, regulation of renal blood flow, endocrine regulation of kidney function, regulation of fluid volume & composition, and the urinary system & homeostasis. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the composition of urine.
Label structures of the urinary system.
Summarize the roles of each of the parts of the urinary system.
Illustrate the macroscopic and microscopic structures of the kidney.
Describe the flow of blood through the kidney.
Describe how blood is filtered in the kidney nephron.
Describe symptoms of kidney failure.
List some of the solutes filtered, secreted, and reabsorbed in different parts of the nephron.
Describe the role of a portal system in the kidney.
Explain how urine osmolarity is hormonally regulated.
Describe the regulation of major ions by the kidney.
Summarize the role of the kidneys in maintaining acid–base balance.

pathways.title: Evolution, Bioinformatics, and Phylogenetic Trees
pathways.description: This pathway investigates how genetic mutations can be used to trace evolutionary relationships between organisms. You will learn how to create and read phylogenetic trees and get hands-on experience in using the BLAST tool for sequence alignments and phylogenetic analyses.
learning_objectives: Describe the molecular mechanisms of evolution.
Examine how genetic mutations can be used to trace evolutionary relationships between organisms.
Identify dna and protein sequence similarities using the blast tool.
Build phylogenetic trees based on blast sequence alignments.

pathways.title: Viruses, Viral Evolution, Prevention & Treatment
pathways.description: No one knows exactly when viruses emerged or from where they came, since viruses do not leave historical footprints such as fossils. Modern viruses are thought to be a mosaic of bits and pieces of nucleic acids picked up from various sources along their respective evolutionary paths. Viruses are acellular, parasitic entities that are not classified within any kingdom. Unlike most living organisms, viruses are not cells and cannot divide. Instead, they infect a host cell and use the host’s replication processes to produce identical progeny virus particles. Viruses infect organisms as diverse as bacteria, plants, and animals. This pathway provides an in-depth look at viral evolution, morphology & classification; virus infection & hosts; prevention & treatment of viral infections; and other acellular entities (prions & viroids). The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Discuss how viruses were first discovered and how they are detected.
Examine how viruses are classified.
Illustrate the basic structure of a virus.
Compare/contrast three hypotheses that describe the evolution of viruses.
Identify the steps in viral replication, and describe events that occur in each.
Illustrate the difference between the lytic and lysogenic cycles of virus replication.
Describe how plant and animal viruses are transmitted.
Identify examples of virus-caused diseases in plants and animals.
Explain the economic impacts of plant viruses.
Discuss examples of major viral illnesses that affect humans.
Compare/contrast how vaccinations differ from antiviral drugs as medical approaches to viruses.
Describe prions and explain how they cause disease.
Describe viroids and discuss their targets of infection.

pathways.title: The Cardiovascular System: Blood Vessels and Circulation
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at structure & function of blood vessels; blood flow, blood pressure, & resistance; capillary exchange; homeostatic regulation of the vascular system; circulatory pathways; and development of blood vessels & fetal circulation. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Compare/contrast the anatomical structure of arteries, arterioles, capillaries, venules, and veins.
Describe the forces that account for capillary exchange.
List the major factors affecting blood flow, blood pressure, and resistance.
Describe how blood flow, blood pressure, and resistance interrelate.
Discuss how the neural and endocrine mechanisms maintain homeostasis within the blood vessels.
Describe the interaction of the cardiovascular system with other body systems.
Label the major blood vessels of the pulmonary and systemic circulations.
Identify and describe the hepatic portal system.
Describe the development of blood vessels and fetal circulation.
Compare/contrast fetal circulation to that of an individual after birth.

pathways.title: The Cellular Basis of Inheritance
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at sexual reproduction, meiosis, and errors in meiosis. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain that variation among offspring is a potential evolutionary advantage resulting from sexual reproduction.
Describe the three different life-cycle strategies among sexual multicellular organisms and their commonalities.
Describe the behavior of chromosomes during meiosis.
Describe cellular events during meiosis.
Explain the differences between meiosis and mitosis.
Explain the mechanisms within meiosis that generate genetic variation among the products of meiosis.
Explain how nondisjunction leads to disorders in chromosome number.
Describe how errors in chromosome structure occur through inversions and translocations.

pathways.title: Teaching the Genome Generation: Genetics Curriculum with Laboratory
pathways.description: Teaching the Genome Generation™ (TtGG) is designed to provide high school teachers the content knowledge, teaching strategies, and resources needed to enhance student learning in genetics, genomics, and personalized medicine. In TtGG™, students conduct classroom experiments, collect and analyze real data, and discuss the ethical complexities at the intersection of human genomics, research and medicine. This pathway contains all of the teacher guides for the TtGG lab experiments and bioinformatics activities.
learning_objectives: Learn how to properly use laboratory equipment and perform modern laboratory techniques
Perform genotyping assays for several common human alleles to demonstrate human genetic variation
Interpret DNA gels and/or DNA sequencing traces to identify genotypes for the ACE, ACTN3, CYP2C19, OXTR, and/or TAS2R38 genes
Navigate common bioinformatic databases
Reflect on how to incorporate this curriculum into your classroom

pathways.title: Population and Community Ecology
pathways.description: Imagine sailing down a river in a small motorboat on a weekend afternoon; the water is smooth and you are enjoying the warm sunshine and cool breeze when suddenly you are hit in the head by a 20-pound silver carp. This is a risk now on many rivers and canal systems in Illinois and Missouri because of the presence of Asian carp. This fish—actually a group of species including the silver, black, grass, and big head carp—has been farmed and eaten in China for over 1000 years. It is one of the most important aquaculture food resources worldwide. In the United States, however, Asian carp is considered a dangerous invasive species that disrupts community structure and composition to the point of threatening native species. This pathway provides an in-depth look at population demography; life histories & natural selection; environmental limits to population growth; population dynamics & regulation; human population growth; community ecology; and behavioral biology. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Discuss how ecologists measure population size and density.
Describe three different patterns of population distribution.
Explain how life tables can be used to calculate mortality rates.
Name the three types of survivorship curves and explain how  they relate to specific populations.
Discuss how life history patterns are influenced by natural selection.
Identify different life history patterns.
Discuss how different reproductive strategies affect species survival.
Compare/contrast the characteristics of and differences between exponential and logistic growth patterns.
Identify examples of exponential and logistic growth in natural populations.
Explain how the carrying capacity of a habitat can change.
Compare/contrast the similarities and differences between density-dependent growth regulation and density-independent growth regulation, and identify some examples of both.
Discuss how natural selection and environmental adaptation leads to the evolution of particular life-history patterns.
Explain how human population growth can be exponential.
Describe how humans have expanded the carrying capacity of their habitat.
Discuss how population growth and age structure relates to the level of economic development in different countries.
Interpret the long-term implications of unchecked human population growth.
Describe the predator-prey cycle.
Identify examples of defenses against predation and herbivory.
Describe the competitive exclusion principle.
Discuss examples of symbiotic relationship among species.
Explain community structure and succession.
Describe the difference between innate and learned behavior.
Explain how movement and migration behaviors are a result of natural selection.
List different ways members of a population communicate with each other.
Identify examples of how species use energy for mating displays and other courtship behaviors.
Discuss examples of various mating systems.
Describe different ways that species learn.

pathways.title: Development and Inheritance
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at fertilization; embryonic development; fetal development; maternal changes during pregnancy, labor, & birth; adjustments of the infant at birth & postnatal stages; lactation; and patterns of inheritance. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: List and explain the steps involved in fertilization.
Describe the major events in embryonic development.
Describe the major events in fetal development.
Discuss the adaptations of a woman’s body to pregnancy.
Describe the physiologic adjustments that the newborn must make in the first hours of extrauterine life.
Summarize the physiology of lactation.
Describe and describe the different patterns of inheritance.

pathways.title: How Cells Obtain Energy
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at energy and metabolism, glycolysis, citric acid cycle and oxidative phosphorylation, fermentation, and connections to other metabolic pathways. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain what metabolic pathways are.
Define the first and second laws of thermodynamics.
Explain the difference between kinetic and potential energy.
Describe endergonic and exergonic reactions.
Discuss how enzymes function as molecular catalysts.
Explain how atp is used by the cell as an energy source.
Describe the overall result in terms of molecules produced of the breakdown of glucose by glycolysis.
Describe the location of the citric acid cycle and oxidative phosphorylation in the cell.
Describe the overall outcome of the citric acid cycle and oxidative phosphorylation in terms of the products of each.
Describe the relationships of glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation in terms of their inputs and outputs..
Discuss the fundamental difference between anaerobic cellular respiration and fermentation.
Describe the type of fermentation that readily occurs in animal cells and the conditions that initiate that fermentation.
Discuss the way in which carbohydrate metabolic pathways, glycolysis, and the citric acid cycle interrelate with protein and lipid metabolic pathways.
Explain explain why metabolic pathways are not considered closed systems.

pathways.title: Introduction to Data Visualization
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the concepts behind data visualization, why data visualization is important, guidelines for designing high-quality data visualization, and introduces different types of data.
learning_objectives: Explain why data visualization is important.
Use principles of data visualization when designing your own graphs and interpreting others' graphs.

pathways.title: DNA Sequence Analysis with Benchling
pathways.description: This step-by-step guide from The Jackson Laboratory Teaching the Genome Generation™ curriculum will enable you to detect variants from DNA sequence files. In this pathway, you will utilize the program Benchling. Before you begin, download our example sequence files. Check out our pathways for Chromas and 4Peaks too!
learning_objectives: View and analyze DNA sequence files using Benchling
Determine sequence quality and identify genotypes for ACTN3, CYP2C19, and/or TAS2R38 genes by interpreting DNA sequencing traces
Confirm the identity of a DNA sequence using BLAST
Locate specific SNP variants within a DNA sequence

pathways.title: Introduction to Human Biology
pathways.description: This pathway helps learners understand the levels of organization found within the human body and explains the terminology used when describing parts of the body.
learning_objectives: Examine parts of human anatomy at different scales to reveal greater complexity.
Build an understanding of how all the bodily systems work together to keep you alive.

pathways.title: Evolution and Origin of Species
pathways.description: The field of biology is a diverse one that includes the study of organisms from the small and simple to the large and complex. From biological molecules to biomes, the one theme that remains consistent is evolution. All species of living organisms are descended from a common ancestor. Although it may seem that living things today stay much the same, this is not the case. Evolution is actually an ongoing process. This pathway provides an in-depth look at understanding evolution; the formation of new species; and reconnection & rates of speciation. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Describe how the present-day theory of evolution was developed.
Explain adaptation, and how adaptation relates to natural selection.
Compare/contrast the differences between convergent and divergent evolution.
Identify examples of convergent and divergent evolution that support evolution by natural selection.
Discuss examples of homologous and vestigial structures, and the evidence these structures provide to support patterns of evolution.
Identify common misconceptions about the theory of evolution.
Describe what defines a species, and how different species can be distinguished from each other.
Explain how genetic variation leads to speciation.
Explain the role of pre-zygotic and post-zygotic reproductive barriers in speciation.
Examine the difference between allopatric speciation and sympatric speciation.
Explain how adaptive radiation explains the diversification.
Name the pathways of species evolution in hybrid zones.
Explain the two major theories on rates of speciation.

pathways.title: Summary Statistics and Probability
pathways.description: This pathway covers fundamental concepts in statistics and probability that are related to data science. The statistical concepts that are covered include characterization of distributions, summary statistics, and variability in data. Probability concepts include the sum and product rules, conditional probability, Bayes’ theorem, and Simpson’s paradox.
learning_objectives: Interpret histograms as a tool for representing data (including variation and uncertainty).
Explain how histograms can be used to represent discrete or continuous distributions.

Define basic summary statistics (mean, median, etc.), as well as how to calculate them, evaluate which are most suitable for characterizing a given dataset, and how to describe a distribution using summary statistics.
Explain the differences among variance, standard deviation, and standard error, as well as interpret the meaning of error bars.
Rewrite elements of written scenarios using probability notation and language.
Calculate the probability of coinciding dependent or independent events.
Employ Bayes' theorem to demonstrate the effects of different relevant conditions on the probability of an event.

pathways.title: The Tissue Level of Organization
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at types of tissues, epithelial tissue, connective tissue supports & protects, muscle tissue & motion, nervous tissue mediates perception & response, and tissue injury & aging. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Identify the main tissue types and discuss their roles in the human body.
Identify the four types of tissue membranes and the characteristics of each that make them functional.
Explain the functions of various epithelial tissues and how their forms enable their functions.
Explain the functions of various connective tissues and how their forms enable their functions.
Describe the characteristics of muscle tissue and how these enable function.
Discuss the characteristics of nervous tissue and how these enable information processing and control of muscular and glandular activities.

pathways.title: Drug Substance
pathways.description: Drug substance process development is an extensive process that involves a number of experiments to establish the process for making a drug substance on a large scale. This pathway will explore the various steps in drug substance process development, including developing cell lines, isolating a single clone, growing cells at scale in a bioreactor, and harvesting and purifying a biologic drug substance.
learning_objectives: Describe what a drug substance is and the steps in drug substance process development.
Explain how a monoclonal antibody (mAb) is synthesized and secreted.
Describe how cell lines are generated to produce an mAb.
Explain what hydrodynamics and process controls in a bioreactor are.
Describe how an mAb is harvested and purified from cells grown in culture in a bioreactor.
Explain the purpose of each of different purification operations, including viral inactivation, viral filtration, ultrafiltration and diafiltration.

pathways.title: Introduction to Biology
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at themes and concept of biology, and the process of science. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the properties of life.
Describe the levels of organization among living things.
List examples of different sub disciplines in biology.
Identify the shared characteristics of the natural sciences.
Describe the process of scientific inquiry.
Compare/contrast inductive reasoning with deductive reasoning.
Describe the goals of basic science and applied science.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Laboratory Analysis of the Immune Response
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of polyclonal and monoclonal antibody production; detecting antigen-antibody complexes; agglutination assays; eias and elisas; fluorescent antibody techniques

pathways.title: Learn How To Strengthen Your Immune System To Fight Threats, From Infections To Cancer
pathways.description: This pathway will give you the tools to improve and strengthen your immune system, the "army" in your body, to fight threats! You will also learn more about how the immune system works to fight threats such as viruses like the novel 2019 coronavirus.
learning_objectives: Identify the role of your immune system in infections and fighting threats.
Use the knowledge you gain to strengthen your own immune system through lifestyle.

pathways.title: Biology Review: Genetics and Hardy-Weinberg
pathways.description:
This pathway reviews some concepts that are important to understand for AP Biology including Hardy-Weinberg basics, review of life cycles, and different types of inheritance. 
This excerpt is from the WestonHS Bio101x On Ramp to AP* Biology.
learning_objectives: Define the conditions necessary for hardy-weinberg equilibrium..
Explain how mathematical approaches are used to calculate changes in allele frequency.

pathways.title: Financial Aid, Scholarships, and Loans
pathways.description: This pathway details the different types of financial aid for college and how to apply to them.
learning_objectives: Identify the different sources of financial assistance for college.
Examine the pros and cons of different sources of financial assistance.
Develop a strategy for affording college with financial aid, scholarships, grants, and, if needed, low-fee loans.

pathways.title: Cell Reproduction
pathways.description: A human, like every sexually reproducing organism, begins life as a fertilized egg (embryo) or zygote. In our species, billions of cell divisions subsequently must occur in a controlled manner in order to produce a complex, multicellular human comprising trillions of cells. Thus, the original single-celled zygote is literally the ancestor of all cells in the body. However, once a human is fully grown, cell reproduction is still necessary to repair and regenerate tissues, and sometimes to increase our size! This pathway provides an in-depth look at cell division, cell cycle, control of the cell cycle, cancer & the cell cycle, and prokaryotic cell division. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the structure of prokaryotic and eukaryotic genomes.
Describe the mechanisms of chromosome compaction.
Define and distinguish between chromosomes, genes, and traits.
Describe the three stages of interphase.
Discuss the behavior of chromosomes during karyokinesis/mitosis.
Explain how the cytoplasmic content is divided during cytokinesis.
Define the quiescent g0 phase.
Interpret how the cell cycle is controlled by mechanisms that are both internal and external to the cell.
Explain how the three internal “control checkpoints” occur at the end of g1, at the g2/m transition, and during metaphase.
Describe the molecules that control the cell cycle through positive and negative regulation.
Describe the process of binary fission in prokaryotes.
Explain how ftsz and tubulin proteins are examples of homology.

pathways.title: Motion in Two Dimensions
pathways.description: This pathway expands the concepts of motion from one dimension into two dimensions. Two key applications of this concept are the pendulum and projectile motion. The pathways concludes with questions that test understanding of the topic.
learning_objectives: Calculate the motion of objects in two dimensions.
Describe the motion of an object experiencing a constant force.
Explain why all objects fall at the same rate in the absence of air resistance.
Discuss the factors that affect the period of a pendulum.
Calculate the range of a projectile.

pathways.title: Ecosystems
pathways.description: Unit 4 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. Why are there so many living organisms on Earth, and so many different species? How do the characteristics of the nonliving environment, such as soil quality and water salinity, help determine which organisms thrive in particular areas? These questions are central to the study of ecosystems—communities of living organisms in particular places and the chemical and physical factors that influence them. Learn how scientists study ecosystems to predict how they may change over time and respond to human impacts.

pathways.title: The DNA Base Pairs
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the structure and function of the nitrogenous bases present in DNA and RNA.
learning_objectives: Use the structure of the dna bases to explain why adenine (a) always pairs with thymine (t), and why guanine (g) always pairs with cytosine (c) in dna..
Use the structure of the dna bases to explain why the nucleotide sequence of a dna molecule does not affect the shape of the double helix..
Identify the watson-crick edge, the major groove edge, and the minor groove edge of a dna base pair..
Compare/contrast the structure of a dna double helix with that of an rna double helix..
Explain why proteins interact with the major groove edge of a dna molecule to determine its sequence..

pathways.title: Introduction to Lab Norms and Expectations
pathways.description: This pathway focuses on expected norms and behaviors in a research lab along with best practices when conducting scientific research. Topics include lab roles, being a good lab citizen, how to get the most out of a research experience, and how to communicate with your labmates.
learning_objectives: Summarize the roles of your lab mates and know how to communicate with them professionally.
Plan how and when to meet with your lab mentors.
Identify the questions you should ask of your lab mates to learn about your lab's culture.
Explain how to be a collaborative and supportive member of the lab.

pathways.title: The Immune System
pathways.description: The environment consists of numerous pathogens, which are agents, usually microorganisms, that cause diseases in their hosts. A host is the organism that is invaded and often harmed by a pathogen. Pathogens include bacteria, protists, fungi and other infectious organisms. We are constantly exposed to pathogens in food and water, on surfaces, and in the air. Mammalian immune systems evolved for protection from such pathogens; they are composed of an extremely diverse array of specialized cells and soluble molecules that coordinate a rapid and flexible defense system capable of providing protection from a majority of these disease agents. Components of the immune system constantly search the body for signs of pathogens. When pathogens are found, immune factors are mobilized to the site of an infection. This pathway provides an in-depth look at innate immune response, adaptive immune response, antibodies, and disruptions in the immune system. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe physical and chemical immune barriers.
Explain immediate and induced innate immune responses.
Discuss natural killer cells.
Describe major histocompatibility class i molecules.
Summarize how the proteins in a complement system function to destroy extracellular pathogens.
Explain adaptive immunity.
Compare/contrast adaptive and innate immunity.
Describe cell-mediated immune response and humoral immune response.
Describe immune tolerance.
Explain cross-reactivity.
Describe the structure and function of antibodies.
Discuss antibody production.
Describe hypersensitivity.
Define autoimmunity.

pathways.title: The Evolution of Populations
pathways.description: Evolutionary medicine is an emerging field that applies evolutionary theory to modern medicine. Rather than just seeking answers to how illness occurs, evolutionary medicine also asks why illness occurs. This approach to medicine has led to many important advances. For example, endogenous retroviruses (ERVs) are pieces of retroviruses that began invading mammalian genomes over 100 million years ago. While studying why smaller mammals tend to get cancer more frequently than larger mammals, scientists discovered that larger mammals have had fewer ERVs invade their genome. Because retroviral integration is associated with cancer, results from this research suggest the possibility that larger mammals are able to control EVR replication until they reach post-reproductive age. This pathway provides an in-depth look at population evolution & genetics; and adaptive evolution. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Discuss population genetics and how population genetics is a synthesis of mendelian inheritance and darwinian evolution.
Identify the hardy–weinberg principle, and explain how it can be applied to microevolution.
Discuss the different types of variation in a population.
Explain why only heritable variation can be acted upon by natural selection.
Discuss how genetic drift, the bottleneck effect, and the founder effect can influence allele frequencies in a population.
Explain how gene flow, mutation, nonrandom mating, and environmental variance can affect allele frequencies in a population.
Identify different ways in which natural selection can shape populations.
Discuss how these different forces can lead to different outcomes in terms of population variation.

pathways.title: The Study of Life
pathways.description: Viewed from space, Earth offers no clues about the diversity of life it harbors. The first forms of life on Earth are thought be microorganisms that existed for billions of years in the ocean before plants and animals appeared. The mammals, birds, and flowers that we see in modern times are mostly “recent” species, originating 130 to 200 million years ago. In fact, only in the last 200,000 years have humans started looking like we do today. Organisms evolve in response to each other. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the science of biology and the themes & concepts of biology. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Discuss the characteristics shared by the natural sciences.
Explain the steps of the scientific method.
Identify and describe the properties of life.
Describe the levels of organization among living things.
Illustrate and interpret a phylogenetic tree.

pathways.title: Osmotic Regulation and Excretion
pathways.description: The daily intake recommendation for human water consumption is eight to ten glasses of water. In order to achieve a healthy balance, the human body should excrete the eight to ten glasses of water every day. This occurs via the processes of urination, defecation, sweating and, to a small extent, respiration. The organs and tissues of the human body are soaked in fluids that are maintained at constant temperature, pH, and solute concentration, all crucial elements of homeostasis. The solutes in body fluids are mainly mineral salts and sugars, and osmotic regulation is the process by which the mineral salts and water are kept in balance. Osmotic balance is especially important in the brain. This pathway provides an in-depth look at osmoregulation & osmotic balance; the kidneys & osmoregulatory organs; excretion systems; nitrogenous wastes; hormonal control of osmoregulatory functions. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Explain why osmoregulation and osmotic balance are important to body functions.
Define osmolarity and describe how is it measured.
Discuss osmoregulators or osmoconformers, and how these tools allow animals to adapt to different environments.
Illustrate how the structure of the kidneys relates to its function as the main osmoregulatory organs in mammalian systems.
Describe how the nephron is the functional unit of the kidney and how it actively filters blood and generates urine.
Illustrate and explain the roles of glomerular filtration, tubular reabsorption, and tubular secretion in the formation of urine.
Describe how vacuoles, present in microorganisms, work to excrete waste.
Summarize how flame cells and nephridia in worms perform excretory functions and maintain osmotic balance.
Illustrate how insects use malpighian tubules to excrete wastes and maintain osmotic balance.
Compare/contrast the differences in the ways aquatic animals and terrestrial animals can eliminate toxic ammonia from their systems.
Compare/contrast the major byproducts of ammonia metabolism in mammals compared to fish, reptiles, birds, and insects.
Explain how hormonal cures help the kidneys synchronize the osmotic needs of the body.

Discuss how hormones and other chemical messengers including epinephrine, norepinephrine, rennin-angiotensin, aldosterone, antidiuretic hormone, and atrial natriuretic peptide help regulate waste elimination, maintain correct osmolarity, and perform other osmoregulatory functions.

pathways.title: Ecology and the Biosphere
pathways.description: Why study ecology? Perhaps you are interested in learning about the natural world and how living things have adapted to the physical conditions of their environment. Or, perhaps you’re a future physician seeking to understand the connection between human health and ecology. Humans are a part of the ecological landscape, and human health is one important part of human interaction with our physical and living environment. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the scope of ecology; biogeography; terrestrial biomes; aquatic biomes; and climate & the effects of global climate change. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Summarize ecology, and the four levels of ecological research.
Identify examples of the ways in which ecology requires the integration of different scientific disciplines.
Describe examples of biotic and abiotic components of the environment.
Define biogeography.
Describe examples of abiotic factors that affect the global distribution of plant and animal species.
Identify examples of how abiotic factors can impact aquatic and terrestrial environments.
Explain the effects of abiotic factors on net primary productivity.
Name the two major abiotic factors that determine terrestrial biomes.
List distinguishing characteristics of each of the major terrestrial biomes.
Describe the effects of abiotic factors on the composition of plant and animal communities in aquatic biomes.
Discuss the similarities and differences among the ocean zones.
Recall the characteristics of standing water and flowing water freshwater biomes.
Define global climate change.
List the effects of the industrial revolution on global atmospheric carbon dioxide concentration.

pathways.title: Coevolution
pathways.description:
This is the ninth session of the Evolution Flipped Course from iBiology. Coevolution occurs when two (or more) species influence each other’s evolution. In this session, we look at coevolution in several diverse systems. Dr. McFall-Ngai begins with an overview of different types of symbiosis, including the important interactions between hosts and their beneficial microbes. Dr. Estes studies the coevolution of sea otters, sea urchins and kelp. Using historic records, he has been able to show the impact of human over-hunting of otters on an entire ecosystem. In the last video, Dr. Baldwin describes the amazingly intertwined lives of the Nicotiana attenuata plant and the Manduca sexta moth. In a fascinating twist, defensive toxins produced by the plant to prevent predation by the M. sexta caterpillar, may in fact protect the caterpillar from predation by a spider.

For additional educator resources for this session, please visit the iBiology Evolution Flipped Course website.

pathways.title: Photosynthesis
pathways.description: In this pathway, students will explore photosynthesis in depth. This fascinating process enables plants, algae, and some bacteria to capture sunlight and convert it into essential chemical energy that is stored in molecules like carbohydrates. Photosynthesis takes place primarily in the chloroplast, a specialized organelle that is designed for this energy transformation.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will be able to explain the process of photosynthesis.
You will apply an understanding of photosynthesis to novel problems.

pathways.title: Equilibrium and Le Châtelier's Principle
pathways.description: In a reversible chemical reaction, a chemical equilibrium is established when the concentrations of products and reactants don’t change. Le Châtelier's principle predicts how an equilibrium will oppose changes to reach a new equilibrium state.
learning_objectives: You will describe what an equilibrium is.
You will describe the ways in which an equilibrium system can be stressed and perturbed.
You will predict the response of a stressed equilibrium using Le Châtelier’s principle.
You will interpret graphs depicting the progress of a chemical reaction under consideration of Le Châtelier's principle.

pathways.title: Biomolecules
pathways.description: What are the building blocks of living things? There are 4 essential biomolecules within our cells: proteins, lipids, carbohydrates, and nucleic acids. When we understand these important biomolecules, we can unlock the secrets of how living things, like us, are put together and how they work. Use the Teaching Guide: Biomolecules Pathway to create a lesson that facilitates learning in your classroom.
learning_objectives: Describe the importance of each of the 4 biomolecules at a cellular level.
Compare the structure and functions of different biomolecules.
Determine whether a diet is balanced with regards to biomolecule composition.

pathways.title: SNPs and Drug Metabolism
pathways.description: Users return to the idea that patients may respond differently to the same medications, depending on their genotype. Students relate what they’ve learned through their exploration of PTC and SNPs to better understand the role of SNPs in drug metabolism and response. Students then investigate and determine how the metabolism of codeine is affected by SNPs. Finally, students consider the pros and cons of using genetic information to better treat patients.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand that not all patients respond to medication the same way.
Explain how many factors (such as dosing, whether the dose is taken with food or not, what other drugs a patient is taking, and genetics) affect how a patient responds to a drug.
Recognize that drugs are metabolized by enzymes in the body.
Provide examples of how differences in people’s genotypes affect their reactions to medications.

pathways.title: The Beaks of Finches
pathways.description: This pathway explores how physical features of a species can change over time in response to environmental factors influencing survival. Learn about well known case studies like Darwin's finches and peppered moths, and conduct your own investigations through simulations.
learning_objectives: Examine how differences in physical features of members of a species can influence their survival.
Test the impact of physical features and environmental factors on survival within a population.
Explain how natural selection leads to changes within a population over time.

pathways.title: Logarithms
pathways.description: This pathway aims to explore the logarithm fundamentals and help learners understand how to solve logarithmic equations.
learning_objectives: Develop your skills for solving logarithmic and exponential equations.
Identify the graph described by a logarithmic function.
Calculate solutions to logarithmic equations using logarithm rules.

pathways.title: Metabolism
pathways.description: Virtually every task performed by living organisms requires energy. Energy is needed to perform heavy labor and exercise. Humans also use a great deal of energy while thinking and even during sleep. In fact, the living cells of every organism constantly use energy. Nutrients and other molecules are imported, metabolized (broken down), synthesized into new molecules, modified if needed, transported around the cell, and, in some cases, distributed to the entire organism. From where, and in what form, does this energy come? How do living cells obtain energy and how do they use it? This pathway provides an in-depth look at energy & metabolism; potential, kinetic, free & activation energy; the laws of thermodynamics; adenosine triphosphate (ATP); and enzymes. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Discuss metabolic pathways.
Compare/contrast the differences between anabolic and catabolic pathways.
Explain how chemical reactions play a role in energy transfer.
Define "energy".
Identify the difference between kinetic and potential energy.
Explain free energy, and how free energy related to activation energy.
Identify the difference between endergonic and exergonic reactions.
Describe entropy.
Discuss the difference between the first and second laws of thermodynamics.
Explain why atp is considered the energy currency of the cell.
Describe how energy is released through the hydrolysis of atp.
Discuss the role of enzymes in metabolic pathways.
Describe how enzymes function as molecular catalysts.

pathways.title: Vertebrate Animals
pathways.description: Vertebrates are among the most recognizable organisms of the Animal Kingdom, and more than 62,000 vertebrate species have been identified. The vertebrate species now living represent only a small portion of the vertebrates that have existed in the past. The best-known extinct vertebrates are the dinosaurs, a unique group of reptiles, some of which reached sizes not seen before or after in terrestrial animals. In fact, they were the dominant terrestrial animals for 150 million years, until most of them died out in a mass extinction near the end of the Cretaceous period (except for the feathered theropod ancestors of modern birds, whose direct descendents now number nearly 10,000 species). This pathway provides an in-depth look at chordates, fishes, amphibians, reptiles, birds, mammals, and the evolution of primates. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the distinguishing characteristics of chordates.
Identify the derived characters of craniates that sets them apart from other chordates.
Describe the developmental fate of the notochord in vertebrates.
Describe the difference between jawless and jawed fishes.
Discuss the distinguishing features of sharks and rays compared to other modern fishes.
Describe the important difference between the life cycle of amphibians and the life cycles of other vertebrates.
Identify and distinguish between the characteristics of urodela, anura, and apoda.
Describe the evolutionary history of amphibians.
Describe the main characteristics of amniotes.
Explain the difference between anapsids, synapsids, and diapsids, and give an example of each.
Identify the characteristics of reptiles.
Discuss the evolution of reptiles.
Describe the evolutionary history of birds.
Describe the derived characteristics in birds that facilitate flight.
Name and describe the distinguishing features of the three main groups of mammals.
Describe the likely line of evolutionary descent that produced mammals.
List some derived features that may have arisen in response to mammals’ need for constant, high-level metabolism.
Identify the major clades of eutherian mammals.
Describe the derived features that distinguish primates from other animals.
Describe the defining features of the major groups of primates.
Identify the major hominin precursors to modern humans.
Explain why scientists are having difficulty determining the true lines of descent in hominids.

pathways.title: Data Literacy: Biological Data Interpretation
pathways.description: Master the essential techniques of molecular biology and biochemistry! This pathway takes you through DNA and protein analysis, gel electrophoresis, immunoblotting, cell fractionation, DNA sequencing, chromatography, and biological data interpretation—equipping you with the skills to analyze and interpret key experimental results in the lab.
learning_objectives: Understand some of the most common data output of laboratory techniques.
Design a research investigation approach to answer scientific questions using data.
Interpret the data of some of the most common laboratory approaches.

pathways.title: Ecosystems
pathways.description: n 1993, an interesting example of ecosystem dynamics occurred when a rare lung disease struck inhabitants of the southwestern United States. This disease had an alarming rate of fatalities, killing more than half of early patients, many of whom were Native Americans. These formerly healthy young adults died from complete respiratory failure. The disease was unknown, and the Centers for Disease Control (CDC), the United States government agency responsible for managing potential epidemics, was brought in to investigate. The scientists could have learned about the disease had they known to talk with the Navajo healers who lived in the area and who had observed the connection between rainfall and mice populations, thereby predicting the 1993 outbreak. This pathway provides an in-depth look at ecology of ecosystems, energy flow through ecosystems, and biogeochemical cycles. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Discuss the biogeochemical cycles of water, carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur.
Explain how human activities have impacted these cycles and the potential consequences for earth.
Describe how organisms acquire energy in a food web and in associated food chains.
Explain how the efficiency of energy transfers between trophic levels affects ecosystem structure and dynamics.
Discuss trophic levels and how ecological pyramids are used to model them.
Compare/contrast the differences between food chains and food webs and recognize the importance of each.
Identify the different methods of ecosystem modeling.
Explain the methods that ecologists use to study ecosystem structure and dynamics.
Describe the basic ecosystem types.

pathways.title: Interpreting Gels
pathways.description: This pathway introduces foundational skills involved in analyzing and critiquing agarose gels, polyacrylamide gels, and Western blot data.
learning_objectives: Interpret agarose gel, polyacrylamide gel, and western blot data.
Critique agarose gel, polyacrylamide gel, and western blot data.
Locate common errors when reading agarose gel, polyacrylamide gel, and western blot data.

pathways.title: The Journey of a Metabolite: A Visual Lesson on Metabolic Flux for Undergraduates
learning_objectives: Describe metabolic flux in the context of common biochemical pathways of central carbon metabolism.
Examine carbon flux from glucose (a sugar) and glutamine (an amino acid) into central carbon metabolism pathways.
Interpret how metabolic flux can be altered under different conditions and its significance.
Identify ways to measure metabolic flux.

pathways.title: Cell Communication
pathways.description: Imagine what life would be like if you and the people around you could not communicate. You would not be able to express your wishes to others, nor could you ask questions about your location. Social organization is dependent on communication between the individuals that comprise that society; without communication, society would fall apart. As with people, it is vital for individual cells to be able to interact with their environment. This is true for both a one-celled organism growing in a puddle and a large animal living on a savanna. In order to properly respond to external stimuli, cells have developed complex mechanisms of communication that can receive a message, transfer the information across the plasma membrane, and then produce changes within the cell in response to the message. This pathway provides an in-depth look at signaling molecules and cellular receptors, propagation of the signal, response to the signal, and signaling in single-celled organisms. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe four types of signaling mechanisms found in multicellular organisms.
Compare/contrast internal receptors with cell-surface receptors.
Identify the relationship between a ligand’s structure and its mechanism of action.
Explain how the binding of a ligand initiates signal transduction throughout a cell.
Explain the role of phosphorylation in the transmission of intracellular signals.
Examine the role of second messengers in signal transmission.
Describe how signaling pathways direct protein expression, cellular metabolism, and cell growth.
Identify the function of pkc in signal transduction pathways.
Explain the role of apoptosis in the development and maintenance of a healthy organism.
Identify and relate the role of quorum sensing to the ability of some bacteria to form biofilms.
Describe how single-celled yeasts use cell signaling to communicate with one another.

pathways.title: Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction
pathways.description: The ability to reproduce is a basic characteristic of all organisms: Hippopotamuses give birth to hippopotamus calves; Joshua trees produce seeds from which Joshua tree seedlings emerge; and adult flamingos lay eggs that hatch into flamingo chicks. However, unlike the organisms shown above, offspring may or may not resemble their parents. Although many unicellular organisms and a few multicellular organisms can produce genetically identical clones of themselves through asexual reproduction, many single-celled organisms and most multicellular organisms reproduce regularly using another method—sexual reproduction. This highly evolved method involves the production by parents of two haploid cells and the fusion of two haploid cells to form a single, genetically recombined diploid cell—a genetically unique organism. Haploid cells that are part of the sexual reproductive cycle are produced by a type of cell division called meiosis. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the process of meiosis and sexual reproduction. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the behavior of chromosomes during meiosis, and the differences between the first and second meiotic divisions.
Describe the cellular events that take place during meiosis.
Explain the differences between meiosis and mitosis.
Explain the mechanisms within the meiotic process that produce genetic variation among the haploid gametes.
Explain that meiosis and sexual reproduction are highly evolved traits.
Identify variation among offspring as a potential evolutionary advantage of sexual reproduction.
Describe the three different life-cycle types among sexually reproducing multicellular organisms.

pathways.title: The Muscular System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at interactions of skeletal muscles; their fascicle arrangement, & their lever systems; naming skeletal muscles; axial muscles of the head, neck, & back; axial muscles of the abdominal wall & thorax; muscles of the pectoral girdle & upper limbs; and appendicular muscles of the pelvic girdle & lower limbs. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the actions and roles of agonists and antagonists.
Explain the structure and organization of muscle fascicles and their role in generating force.
Explain the criteria used to name skeletal muscles.
Identify the skeletal muscles and their actions on the skeleton and soft tissues of the body.
Identify the origins and insertions of skeletal muscles and the prime movements.

pathways.title: Cell Communication
pathways.description: Imagine what life would be like if you and the people around you could not communicate. You would not be able to express your wishes, nor could you ask questions to find out more about your environment. Social organization is dependent on communication between the individuals; without communication, society would fall apart. As with people, it is vital for a cell to interact with its environment. This is true whether it is a unicellular organism or one of many cells forming a larger organism. organism. While the necessity for cellular communication in larger organisms seems obvious, even single-celled organisms communicate with each other. Cell signaling is vital to the survival of organisms. This pathway provides an in-depth look at signaling molecules & cellular receptors; propagation of the signal; response to the signal; and signaling in single-celled organisms. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: List the four types of signaling that are found in multicellular organisms.
Summarize the differences between internal receptors and cell-surface receptors.
Explain the relationship between a ligand’s structure and its mechanism of action.
Describe how the binding of a ligand initiates signal transduction throughout a cell.
Explain the role of second messengers in signal transduction.
Discuss how signaling pathways direct protein expression, cellular metabolism, and cell growth.
Explain the role of apoptosis in the development and maintenance of a healthy organism.
Describe how single-celled yeasts use cell signaling to communicate with each other.

pathways.title: The Biological Process of Photosynthesis
pathways.description: All biological processes require energy. To get this energy, many organisms access stored energy by eating, that is, by ingesting other organisms. But where does the stored energy in food originate? Almost all of this energy can be traced back to photosynthesis. Photosynthetic organisms are the basis for almost all of the food webs on the planet. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the light-dependent reaction of photosynthesis and using light to make organic molecules. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Explain the relevance of photosynthesis to living organisms.
Identify the main cellular structures involved in photosynthesis.
Identify the substrates and products of photosynthesis.
Explain how plants absorb energy from sunlight.
Explain the differences between short and long wavelengths of light.
List the wavelengths used in photosynthesis.
Describe how and where photosynthesis occurs within a plant.
Identify the reactions in the calvin cycle described as the light-independent reactions.
Explain why the term "carbon fixation" describes the products in the calvin cycle.
Discuss the role of photosynthesis in the energy cycle of all living organisms.

pathways.title: Model Organisms: Cultured Cells
pathways.description: The ability to grow cell cultures is one of the most crucial practices in modern biotechnology, medical research, and microbiology. In this pathway, discover how the ability to grow cells and tissues in 3 dimensions in microgravity holds the potential for the creation of entire organs.
learning_objectives: Examine how microgravity allows for unique experiments that would not be possible on earth's surface.

pathways.title: DNA Sequence Analysis with Chromas
pathways.description: This step-by-step guide from The Jackson Laboratory Teaching the Genome Generation™ curriculum will enable you to detect variants from DNA sequence files. In this pathway, you will utilize the program Chromas. Before you begin, download our example sequence files. Check out our pathways for Benchling and 4Peaks too!
learning_objectives: View and analyze DNA sequence files using Chromas
Determine sequence quality and identify genotypes for ACTN3, CYP2C19, and/or TAS2R38 genes by interpreting DNA sequencing traces
Confirm the identity of a DNA sequence using BLAST
Locate specific SNP variants within a DNA sequence

pathways.title: Population and Community Ecology
pathways.description: Imagine sailing down a river in a small motorboat on a weekend afternoon; the water is smooth and you are enjoying the warm sunshine and cool breeze when suddenly you are hit in the head by a 20-pound silver carp. This is now a risk on many rivers and canal systems in Illinois and Missouri because of the presence of Asian carp. This fish—actually a group of species including the silver, black, grass, and big head carp—has been farmed and eaten in China for over 1000 years. It is one of the most important aquaculture food resources worldwide. In the United States, however, Asian carp is considered a dangerous invasive species that disrupts community structure and composition to the point of threatening native species. This pathway provides an in-depth look at population demography, life histories & natural selection, environmental limits to population growth, population dynamics & regulation, human population growth, community ecology, and behavioral biology (proximate & ultimate causes of behavior). The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe how ecologists measure population size and density.
Describe three different patterns of population distribution.
Use life tables to calculate mortality rates.
Describe the three types of survivorship curves and relate them to specific populations.
Describe how life history patterns are influenced by natural selection.
Explain different life history patterns and how different reproductive strategies affect species’ survival.
Explain the characteristics of and differences between exponential and logistic growth patterns.
Illustrate /give examples of exponential and logistic growth in natural populations.
Describe how natural selection and environmental adaptation led to the evolution of particular life history patterns.
Illustrate /give examples of how the carrying capacity of a habitat may change.
Compare/contrast density-dependent growth regulation and density-independent growth regulation, giving examples.
Illustrate exponential and logistic growth in wild animal populations.
Describe how natural selection and environmental adaptation leads to the evolution of particular life-history patterns.
Discuss exponential human population growth.
Explain how humans have expanded the carrying capacity of their habitat.
Discuss the long-term implications of unchecked human population growth.
Identify and relate population growth and age structure to the level of economic development in different countries.
Discuss the predator-prey cycle.
Describe the competitive exclusion principle.
Illustrate /give examples of defenses against predation and herbivory.
Illustrate /give examples of symbiotic relationships between species.
Describe community structure and succession.
Identify differences between various mating systems.
Describe different ways that species learn.
Illustrate /give examples of how species use energy for mating displays and other courtship behaviors.
Discuss the different ways members of a population communicate with each other.
Discuss how movement and migration behaviors are a result of natural selection.
Compare/contrast innate and learned behavior.

pathways.title: Photosynthesis and the Use of Light Energy
pathways.description: The metabolic processes in all organisms—from bacteria to humans—require energy. To get this energy, many organisms access stored energy by eating, that is, by ingesting other organisms. But where does the stored energy in food originate? All of this energy can be traced back to photosynthesis. This pathway provides an in-depth look at an overview of photosynthesis, the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis, and using light energy to make organic molecules. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain the significance of photosynthesis to other living organisms.
Describe the main structures involved in photosynthesis.
Identify the substrates and products of photosynthesis.
Explain how plants absorb energy from sunlight.
Describe short and long wavelengths of light.
Describe how and where photosynthesis takes place within a plant.
Describe the calvin cycle.
Define carbon fixation.
Explain how photosynthesis works in the energy cycle of all living organisms.

pathways.title: DNA Sequence Analysis with 4Peaks
pathways.description: This step-by-step guide from The Jackson Laboratory Teaching the Genome Generation™ curriculum will enable you to detect variants from DNA sequence files. In this pathway, you will utilize the program 4Peaks. Before you begin, download our example sequence files. Check out our pathways for Chromas and Benchling too!
learning_objectives: View and analyze DNA sequence files using 4Peaks
Determine sequence quality and identify genotypes for ACTN3, CYP2C19, and/or TAS2R38 genes by interpreting DNA sequencing traces
Confirm the identity of a DNA sequence using BLAST
Locate specific SNP variants within a DNA sequence

pathways.title: Cell Division
pathways.description: This pathway defines the problems that a cell faces as it prepares for division and describes some of the ways it solves them. This pathway is the seventh session of the Cell Biology Flipped Course from iBiology.

pathways.title: Yamicia Connor, MD, PhD - Physician Scientist
pathways.description: Dr. Yamicia Connor, a scientist and physician, bridges the gap between science and surgery. She is interested in using medicine and science to improve the lives of women.

pathways.title: Waves and Energy Transfer
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the concept of waves. Properties of waves are defined, such as frequency, wavelength and period. Examples of waves in different media are given, as well as observable properties. Problems are provided to check for understanding.
learning_objectives: Define the terms pulse, amplitude, frequency, period and wavelength.
Describe the effect of constructive and destructive interference of waves.
Calculate the properties of waves.

pathways.title: Atomic Physics
pathways.description: This pathway explores the nature of atoms and their substructures, as well as how these substructures determine the behavior of the atom, such as the absorption and emission of energy by electrons within an atom. It also relates the stories of how the various properties of an atom were discovered through clever and imaginative experimentation (such as the Millikan oil drop experiment) and interpretation (such as Brownian motion). The pathway further discusses the probabilistic description used to describe the nature of electrons and how the wave model is used to understand the energy levels in an atom and the properties of electrons.
learning_objectives: Describe the basic structure of the atom, the basic unit of all matter.
Describe how electrons were discovered.
Explain the millikan oil drop experiment.
Describe rutherford’s gold foil experiment.
Describe rutherford’s planetary model of the atom.
Describe the mysteries of atomic spectra.
Explain bohr’s theory of the hydrogen atom.
Explain bohr’s planetary model of the atom.
