Illustrate the energy state using the energy-level diagram.
Describe the triumphs and limits of bohr’s theory.
Define x-ray tube and its spectrum.
Identify and show the x-ray characteristic energy.
List and specify the use of x rays in medical observations.
Explain the use of x rays in ct scanners in diagnostics.
Define and discuss fluorescence.
Define metastable.
Describe how laser emission is produced.
Explain population inversion.
Define and discuss holography.
Explain bohr’s model of the atom.
Define and describe quantization of angular momentum.
Calculate the angular momentum for an orbit of an atom.
Define and describe the wave-like properties of matter.
Describe and discuss the zeeman effect.
Define orbital magnetic field.
Define orbital angular momentum.
Define space quantization.
Define quantum number.
Calculate the angle of an angular momentum vector with an axis.
Define spin quantum number.
Identify and specify the shell and subshell symbols and their positions.
Define the composition of an atom along with its electrons, neutrons, and protons.
Explain the pauli exclusion principle and its application to the atom.
Define the position of electrons in different shells of an atom.
Describe the position of each element in the periodic table according to shell filling.

pathways.title: Diffusion and Transpiration
pathways.description: This pathway introduces users to the concepts of diffusion and osmosis and the factors that affect how quickly diffusion and osmosis occur. The concept of water potential is also introduced and applied to transpiration.

pathways.title: Cellular Respiration
pathways.description: In the process of photosynthesis, plants and other photosynthetic producers take in energy in the form of light (solar energy) and convert it into chemical energy in the form of glucose, which stores this energy in its chemical bonds. Then, a series of metabolic pathways, collectively called cellular respiration, extracts the energy from the bonds in glucose and converts it into a form that all living things can use.This pathway provides an in-depth look at energy in living systems; glycolysis; oxidation of pyruvate & the citric acid cycle; oxidative phosphorylation; metabolism without oxygen; connections of carbohydrate, protein, & lipid metabolic pathways, and regulation of cellular respiration. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Discuss the importance of electrons in the transfer of energy in living systems.
Explain how atp is used by cells as an energy source.
Describe the overall result in terms of molecules produced during the chemical breakdown of glucose by glycolysis.
Compare/contrast the output of glycolysis in terms of atp molecules and nadh molecules produced.
Explain how a circular pathway, such as the citric acid cycle, fundamentally differs from a linear biochemical pathway, such as glycolysis.
Describe how pyruvate, the product of glycolysis, is prepared for entry into the citric acid cycle.
Describe how electrons move through the electron transport chain and explain what happens to their energy levels during this process.
Explain how a proton (h+) gradient is established and maintained by the electron transport chain.
Discuss the fundamental difference between anaerobic cellular respiration and fermentation.
Describe the type of fermentation that readily occurs in animal cells and the conditions that initiate that fermentation.
Describe how feedback inhibition would affect the production of an intermediate or product in a pathway.
Identify the mechanism that controls the rate of the transport of electrons through the electron transport chain.

pathways.title: Data Visualization
pathways.description: From choosing a visualization type to labeling axes and intervals, this pathway covers the fundamentals of communicating and interpreting data through graphs and charts.
learning_objectives: Describe fundamental principles of data visualization (Tufte’s principles) and apply them in the creation of their own figures.
Describe the elements of each type of graph (bar graph, scatter plot, histogram or boxplot).
Illustrate a given dataset using the most relevant type of graph.
Use verbal and mathematical descriptors to characterize the rate at which a dependent variable is changing at different points in a line graph.

pathways.title: Cell Size
pathways.description: This pathway provides an overview of how cell size affects the movement of molecules into and out of the cell, as well as inside the cell.
learning_objectives: Calculate surface area and volume of a cube-shaped cell to determine how cell size affects cell function.
Explain how cell size affects movement of molecules into or out of the cell.
Explain how cell size affects the movement of molecules inside the cell.

pathways.title: Biology Lab Investigations
learning_objectives: Formulate a testable question and design an investigation with appropriate controls and variables to explore a biological phenomenon.
Collect, represent, and analyze experimental data using appropriate graphs, tables, and statistical methods to identify patterns, trends, and sources of error.
Construct a biological explanation or argument that is supported by experimental evidence, reasoning, and scientific principles.

pathways.title: Diversity of Animals
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at features of the animal kingdom, sponges, cnidarians, flatworms, nematodes, arthropods, mollusks, annelids, echinoderms, chordates and vertebrates. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: List the features that distinguish the animal kingdom from other kingdoms.
Explain the processes of animal reproduction and embryonic development.
Describe the hierarchy of basic animal classification.
Compare/contrast the embryonic development of protostomes and deuterostomes.
Describe the organizational features of the simplest animals.
Describe the organizational features of cnidarians.
Describe the structure and systems of flatworms.
Describe the structural organization of nematodes.
Compare/contrast the internal systems and the appendage specialization of arthropods.
Describe the unique anatomical features of mollusks.
Describe the features of an animal classified in phylum annelida.
Describe the distinguishing characteristics of echinoderms.
Describe the distinguishing characteristics of chordates.
Describe the difference between jawless and jawed fishes.
Explain the main characteristics of amphibians, reptiles, and birds.
Describe the derived characteristics in birds that facilitate flight.
Name and describe the distinguishing features of the three main groups of mammals.
Describe the derived features that distinguish primates from other animals.

pathways.title: Hands-on Biotechnology: An Introduction to Enzymes
pathways.description: Enzymes are critical to regulating many biochemical reactions in organisms ranging from microbes to plants to humans. In this pathway you will learn how enzymes change chemical reaction rates and the ways in which enzymes can be inactivated.
learning_objectives: Test how increasing the concentration of an enzyme changes the reaction rate.
Examine how acidic and basic solutions alter enzymatic activity.
Test whether an enzyme can be inactivated by heat.

pathways.title: Hands-on Biotechnology: An Introduction to DNA & Inheritance
pathways.description: DNA provides the instructions for the structure and function of all living organisms. In this pathway, you will get a hands-on appreciation for how DNA is structured, what it looks like on the microscopic scale, how it can be extracted for research and medical purposes, how it stores information, and how the inheritance of specific sequences of DNA lead to individuals' traits.
learning_objectives: Construct a model of dna.
Describe how nucleotides pair.
Use simple laboratory techniques to extract dna from fruit.
Illustrate the connection between an organism's genotype and phenotype.

pathways.title: Prokaryotes: Bacteria and Archaea
pathways.description: In the recent past, scientists grouped living things into five kingdoms—animals, plants, fungi, protists, and prokaryotes—based on several criteria, such as the absence or presence of a nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles, the absence or presence of cell walls, multicellularity, and so on. In the late 20th century, the pioneering work of Carl Woese and others compared sequences of small-subunit ribosomal RNA (SSU rRNA), which resulted in a more fundamental way to group organisms on Earth. Based on differences in the structure of cell membranes and in rRNA, Woese and his colleagues proposed that all life on Earth evolved along three lineages, called domains. The domain Bacteria comprises all organisms in the kingdom Bacteria, the domain Archaea comprises the rest of the prokaryotes, and the domain Eukarya comprises all eukaryotes—including organisms in the kingdoms Animalia, Plantae, Fungi, and Protista. Two of the three domains—Bacteria and Archaea—are prokaryotic. This pathway provides an in-depth look at prokaryotic diversity, structure of prokaryotes (bacteria and archaea), prokaryotic metabolism, bacterial diseases in humans, and beneficial prokaryotes. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the evolutionary history of prokaryotes.
Discuss the distinguishing features of extremophiles.
Explain why it is difficult to culture prokaryotes.
Describe the basic structure of a typical prokaryote.
Describe important differences in structure between archaea and bacteria.
Identify the macronutrients needed by prokaryotes, and explain their importance.
Describe the ways in which prokaryotes get energy and carbon for life processes.
Describe the roles of prokaryotes in the carbon and nitrogen cycles.
Identify bacterial diseases that caused historically important plagues and epidemics.
Describe the link between biofilms and foodborne diseases.
Explain how overuse of antibiotics may be creating “super bugs”.
Explain the importance of mrsa with respect to the problems of antibiotic resistance.
Explain the need for nitrogen fixation and how it is accomplished.
Describe the beneficial effects of bacteria that colonize our skin and digestive tracts.
Identify prokaryotes used during the processing of food.
Describe the use of prokaryotes in bioremediation.

pathways.title: Ecology and the Biosphere
pathways.description: Why study ecology? Perhaps you are interested in learning about the natural world and how living things have adapted to the physical conditions of their environment. Or, perhaps you’re a future physician seeking to understand the connection between your patients' health and their environment. Humans are a part of the ecological landscape, and human health is one important part of human interaction with our physical and living environment. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the scope of ecology, biogeography, terrestrial biomes, aquatic biomes, and climate & the effects of global climate change. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Define ecology and the four basic levels of ecological research.
Describe examples of the ways in which ecology requires the integration of different scientific disciplines.
Compare/contrast abiotic and biotic components of the environment.
Describe the relationship between abiotic and biotic components of the environment.
Define biogeography.
List and describe abiotic factors that affect the global distribution of plant and animal species.
Compare/contrast the impact of abiotic forces on aquatic and terrestrial environments.
Summarize the effects of abiotic factors on net primary productivity.
Name distinguishing characteristics of each of the eight major terrestrial biomes.
Identify the two major abiotic factors that determine terrestrial biomes.
Describe the effects of abiotic factors on the composition of plant and animal communities in aquatic biomes.
Compare/contrast the characteristics of the ocean zones.
Summarize the characteristics of standing water and flowing water freshwater biomes.
Define global climate change.
Summarize the effects of the industrial revolution on global atmospheric carbon dioxide concentration.
Describe three natural factors affecting long-term global climate.
List two or more greenhouse gases and describe their role in the greenhouse effect.

pathways.title: Intro to the Lab Bench
pathways.description: If you're a molecular biologist looking to start your career in the lab, you won't want to miss this series. In this safety protocol lesson, learn everything you need to know to protect yourself when working in a lab. You will learn everything you need to know to get started with water baths and pippettes
learning_objectives: In this pathway you'll learn how to stay safe in the lab, so you can focus on making groundbreaking discoveries.
You will learn everything you need to know to get started with water baths, including how to clean and fill them properly, what type of water to use, and how to set the temperature.
You will be able to properly select, use, and read a single-channel pipette for accurate and precise measurement of liquid volumes.

pathways.title: Theory Behind Evolution II
pathways.description: This is the second session in the Evolution Flipped Course from iBiology. This session continues our introduction to evolution. Dr. Ramakrishnan studies how species are distributed across regions. She expands on the concept of speciation and explains why islands and mountain ranges are often regions of increased biodiversity. Dr. Tishkoff investigates the migration of hominids out of Africa and the evolution of modern humans. She defines many key concepts in evolution including genetic drift, neutral evolution, founder effect, and more. She explains how mitochondrial DNA can be used to study relatedness of humans and other populations. Dr. Hadly finishes with a short, clear, explanation of a genetic bottleneck.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Microbial Metabolism
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of energy, matter, and enzymes; catabolism of carbohydrates; cellular respiration; fermentation; catabolism of lipids and proteins; photosynthesis; biogeochemical cycles.

pathways.title: Photosynthesis and pH
pathways.description: Photosynthesis of a water plant changes the pH of the surrounding solution, which can be measured as a color change of the pH indicator BTB.
learning_objectives: You will interpret the color change of the pH indicator BTB as a result of photosynthesis.
You will use the interactive to establish a link between ongoing photosynthesis, carbon dioxide consumption, and oxygen production.

pathways.title: DNA Structure
pathways.description: This pathway introduces DNA by highlighting the scientists and research that helped to determine its structure. It also provides an overview of what happens when DNA mutates and explores different ways to model DNA.
learning_objectives: Describe how the structure of DNA is related to its function.
Investigate the effects of changes in DNA sequences.

pathways.title: Racism and the History of Science
learning_objectives: Discuss examples of racist and unethical practices within the history of science and medicine.
Describe how European naturalists and physicians used inaccurate theories about race to oppress racially marginalized communities.
Describe the contributions of Black Americans to science and medicine during and after slavery.
Explain how historical acts of racism in the fields of science and medicine continue to affect racially marginalized communities.
Explain how racist discourses have functioned in public health crises throughout US history to the present.

pathways.title: The Reproductive System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at anatomy and physiology of the male reproductive system, anatomy and physiology of the female reproductive system, and development of the male and female reproductive systems. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the anatomy of the male and female reproductive systems, including their accessory structures.
Explain the role of hypothalamic and pituitary hormones in male and female reproductive function.
Describe the path of a sperm cell from its initial production through fertilization of an oocyte.
Explain the events in the ovary prior to ovulation.
Describe the development and maturation of the sex organs and the emergence of secondary sex characteristics during puberty.

pathways.title: Medical Mystery
pathways.description:
Why don’t all patients respond the same way to different medications? This pathway explores how a patient's genetic makeup can impact the effectiveness of certain drugs, and how treatments can be tailored using an approach called precision medicine.
This pathway complements Activity 1 from the Amgen Biotech Experience Exploring Precision Medicine curriculum. To learn about the digital supports for this curriculum, see the Amgen Biotech Experience Exploring Precision Medicine Cluster. For more information on this classroom and lab-based curriculum, see the Teaching and Learning Materials from the Amgen Biotech Experience.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand that people respond to medications in different ways.
Define the terms gene and trait.
Describe the connection between genes, the environment, and traits.
Use evidence from text to make conjectures.

pathways.title: Mendelian Genetics
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores evidence for common ancestry and the inheritance of traits. In this pathway, learners will examine how shared biological features support the concept that all organisms share a common origin. Learners will also be introduced to Mendel’s laws of segregation and independent assortment to explain how genes and traits are passed from parents to offspring. By analyzing how fertilization, probability, and inheritance patterns influence outcomes, learners will connect Mendel’s principles to genetic variation and the prediction of traits across generations. Coming soon: new interactive content on autosomal modes of inheritance!
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe how shared, conserved, fundamental processes and features provide evidence of common ancestry for all organisms.
Explain how Mendel’s laws explain the inheritance of genes and traits from one generation to the next.

pathways.title: How Can We Begin to Understand Race?
pathways.description:
What is race, really? In this pathway, you’ll explore the myth of biological race in humans, how it developed, and the complex social meanings that the term “race” holds. You’ll also examine how the myth of biological race in humans has been used throughout history to justify the exploitation and enslavement of certain groups of people, and the very real social consequences this history still has today. 
This pathway forms part of the Racism as a Public Health Crisis cluster. See our equity pledge as part of this initiative. Please note: This pathway contains sensitive content related to racial violence and discrimination.
learning_objectives: Identify how and why definitions of race have changed over the course of human history.
Describe why race in humans is not biological, but is socially constructed.
Recognize the sociocultural and political forces that underpin the myth of biological race in humans.
Explain how the myth of biological race in humans has historically been used to justify the enslavement and exploitation of certain groups of people.

pathways.title: Heat and Heat Transfer Methods
pathways.description: In this pathway, the ideas of temperature and thermal energy are used to examine and define heat, how heat is affected by the thermal properties of materials, and how the various mechanisms of heat transfer function. These topics are fundamental and practical, and will be returned to in subsequent pathways.
learning_objectives: Define heat as transfer of energy.
Describe heat transfer and change in temperature and mass.
Calculate final temperature after heat transfer between two objects.
Examine heat transfer.
Calculate final temperature from heat transfer.
Discuss the different methods of heat transfer.
Calculate thermal conductivity.
Describe conduction of heat in collisions.
Explain thermal conductivities of common substances.
Discuss the method of heat transfer by convection.
Discuss heat transfer by radiation.
Explain the radiant power of different materials.

pathways.title: Experimental Design, Data, and Variables
pathways.description: This pathway covers fundamental concepts in experimental design that are related to data science, such as asking testable research questions, independent and dependent variables, generating hypotheses, designing positive and negative controls, data types, determining correlation versus causation, and sampling.
learning_objectives: Generate testable research questions and hypotheses, and classify variables as dependent or independent.
Develop questions and experiments designed to reduce bias & display the data in a clear, ethical manner.
Recognize patterns and confounding variables which can affect the story told by the data (such as correlation vs causation).

pathways.title: The Nervous System
pathways.description: While you’re reading this book, your nervous system is performing several functions simultaneously. The visual system is processing what is seen on the page, the motor system controls the turn of the pages (or click of the mouse), and the prefrontal cortex maintains attention. Even fundamental functions, like breathing and regulation of body temperature, are controlled by the nervous system. A nervous system is an organism’s control center: it processes sensory information from outside (and inside) the body and controls all behaviors—from eating to sleeping to finding a mate. This pathway provides an in-depth look at neurons & glial cells; how neurons communicate; the central & peripheral nervous systems; and nervous system disorders. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Illustrate the four main types of neurons.
Describe the functions of the structural components of a neuron.
Compare/contrast the functions of different types of glial cells.
Compare/contrast the similarities and differences between chemical and electrical synapses.
Describe the stages of an action potential, and how action potentials are propagated.
Discuss long-term potentiation and long-term depression, and how both relate to transmission of impulses across synapses.
Discuss the basis of the resting membrane potential.
Identify the major areas of the brain.
Explain the primary functions of the spinal cord, cerebral lobes, cerebellum, and brainstem.
Illustrate the organization and functions of the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems.
Discuss the organization and function of the sensory-somatic nervous system.
Identify examples of symptoms, causes, and treatments for several examples of nervous system disorders.

pathways.title: Phylogenies and the History of Life
pathways.description: A bee and echinacea flower could not look more different, yet they are related, as are all living organisms on Earth. By following pathways of similarities and changes—both visible and genetic—scientists seek to map the evolutionary past of how life developed from single-celled organisms to the tremendous variety of creatures that have germinated, crawled, floated, swam, flown, and walked on this planet. This pathway provides an in-depth look at organizing life on Earth; determining evolutionary relationships; and perspectives on the phylogenetic tree. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Explain why scientists need a comprehensive classification system to study living organisms.
Identify the different levels of the taxonomic classification system.
Explain how systematics and taxonomy are related to phylogeny.
Discuss the components and purpose of a phylogenetic tree.
Explain the difference between homologous and analogous traits.
Describe how homologous and analogous traits are used when determining evolutionary relatedness.
Define cladistics.
Discuss how a cladogram differs from a phylogenetic tree.
Explain parsimony.
Discuss horizontal gene transfer and its significance in constructing phylogenetic trees.
Describe how prokaryotes and eukaryotes transfer genes horizontally.
Illustrate other models of phylogenetic relationships and discuss how they differ from the original phylogenetic tree concept.

pathways.title: NCBI for Beginners
pathways.description: This pathway introduces how to create a My NCBI account, how to use My Bibliography, how to save search results in Collections, and how to find a gene's sequence (including an explanation of RefSeqGene) in NCBI.
learning_objectives: Use an NCBI account to save searches and maintain a bibliography.
Use NCBI to find the DNA sequence of gene.

pathways.title: High School Biology
pathways.description: Presentations introducing the major concepts covered in high school biology, ranging from defining biology to an in-depth discussion of human physiology.
learning_objectives: Define what an organism is and the 5 characteristics of living things.
Develop an understanding of the scientific method, controlled experiments, scientific tools, theories and laws.
Categorize relationships between organisms and their environment.
Describe energy sources, cycles, and release of energy in plants and animals.
Discuss the importance of biodiversity and conservation.
Define covalent, ionic, and hydrogen bonds.
Describe the properties of water.
Discuss the history of cell theory and the workings of the cell.
Explain the structure and functions of the skin.
Explain the structure and funtions of the skeletal system.
Describe skeletal muscles; their types and workings.
Describe the functions of the digestive system.
Discuss nutrition and how the body deals with nutrients, calories, and metabolism.
Discuss the endocrine system and how endocrine glands control functions in the body through the release of hormones.

pathways.title: The College Essay
pathways.description: This pathway reviews the types of college essays and presents strategies to craft a successful one.
learning_objectives: Identify types of college essays.
Define the qualities of successful college essays.
Develop strategies to brainstorm and compose a successful college essay.

pathways.title: Anatomy of the Nervous System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at the embryologic perspective, the central nervous system, circulation & the central nervous system, and the peripheral nervous system. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain the relationship between the developmental processes of the embryonic nervous system and the adult structures.
Name the major regions of the adult nervous system.
Locate regions of the cerebral cortex on the basis of anatomical landmarks common to all human brains.
Describe the regions of the spinal cord in cross-section.
List the cranial nerves in order of anatomical location and provide the central and peripheral connections.
List the spinal nerves by vertebral region and by which nerve plexus each supplies.

pathways.title: How Do Carbon Dioxide Levels Affect Photosynthesis?
pathways.description: Carbon dioxide levels affect the rate of photosynthesis of a water plant. This can be followed by monitoring its oxygen production.
learning_objectives: You will test the effect of carbon dioxide levels on the photosynthesis of a water plant by measuring oxygen with an electrochemical gas sensor.
You will interpret the effect of changing carbon dioxide levels on the rate of the plant’s photosynthesis.
You will examine why, in this case, measuring oxygen production is a better method to follow the photosynthesis of the water plant than using a pH indicator.

pathways.title: Organizing Atoms and Electrons: The Periodic Table
pathways.description: Through its organization, the periodic table makes clear the underlying chemical and physical trends among the elements. These characteristics—reactivity, atomic radius, electronegativity, and density—are linked to the distribution of electrons around the nucleus. This pathway forms Unit 4 of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Identify elements as metals, non-metals, or metalloids.
Predict the electron configuration and the reactivity of any element based on its position on the periodic table.
Identify trends in effective nuclear charge, atomic size, ionization energy, electron affinity, ionic size, and electronegativity.
Name any ionic compound and provide its formula.

pathways.title: Electrodynamics
pathways.description: This pathway explores how the properties of magnets and charges relate to produce electromagnetism. The basic principles of electromagnetism are discussed, with a specific focus on Faraday's law as a mechanism for producing an emf by moving charges in a magnetic field. Applications of electromagnetism such as motors and generators are explained. Problems are provided to check for understanding.
learning_objectives: Define the concept of magnetic flux.
Explain how a changing magnetic flux through a loop gives rise to an emf.
Identify the factors that influence the induced emf in a wire.
Define lenz's law and faraday's law.
Discuss how motors and generators function.
List some of the applications of electromagnetism.

pathways.title: How Does Light Intensity Affect Photosynthesis?
pathways.description: The rate of photosynthesis depends on the intensity of the light source. This effect can be measured by the color change of the pH indicator BTB.
learning_objectives: You will test the effect of the intensity of light on photosynthesis using the color change of the pH indicator BTB.
You will interpret the effect on the rate of photosynthesis when the intensity of the light source shining on the plant is changed.
You will use the interactive to establish a link between the rate of photosynthesis and oxygen production.

pathways.title: Water and the Elements of Life
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores the chemistry of life as it relates to water and the elements of life (C,H, N, O, P, S). In this pathway, learners will be introduced to water’s unique properties, its polarity, and the biochemical properties contributing to hydrogen bonding. Learners will also discover the composition of macromolecules and explore carbon’s molecular diversity through an analysis of isomers and functional groups.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe how the properties of water that result from its polarity and hydrogen bonding impact its biological function.
Explain the composition of macromolecules found in living organisms, including carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, and sulfur.
Summarize how carbon's molecular diversity (isomers and functional groups) contributes to the structure of biological molecules.

pathways.title: Cell Signalling
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores how cells communicate to coordinate life processes. Learners are introduced to the 3 stages of cell signaling: reception, transduction, and cellular response. They will examine how signals are detected by receptors, how transduction pathways amplify and relay information within the cell, and how responses can vary from changes in gene expression to alterations in cell behavior. This pathway also highlights how modifications in signal transduction can affect the efficiency or outcome of cellular responses, providing insight into the regulation of biological systems. 
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe how cells communicate with one another.
Identify the components of a signal transduction pathway and explain their roles in producing a cellular response.
Explain how environmental factors influence signal transduction pathways and the types of cellular responses they elicit.
Analyze how structural changes in signaling molecules or pathway components can alter signal transduction activity and outcomes.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Control of Microbial Growth
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of controlling microbial growth; using physical methods to control microorganisms; using chemicals to control microorganisms; testing the effectiveness of antiseptics and disinfectants

pathways.title: Introduction to Statistics
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the types of summary statistics commonly used to describe data sets.
learning_objectives: Define mean, median, mode, range, interquartile range (iqr), and standard deviation (sd).
Examine when using mean, median, or mode best describes the center of a data set.
Examine when using range, interquartile range (iqr), or standard deviation (sd) best describes the range of a data set.

pathways.title: Prime Factorizations
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the concept of prime factorization and explains how to solve problems using this method.
learning_objectives: Use prime factorization to solve mathematical problems.
Define a prime number and a prime factor.
Explain how to find the least common multiple and greatest common factor of different numbers.

pathways.title: Making the Most of High School
pathways.description: This pathway reviews how extracurricular activities, academic performance, and mentorship during high school play a role in the college admissions process.
learning_objectives: Identify decisions made during high school that impact college admissions.
Develop an understanding of how mentorship is valuable during high school.
Define ways to challenge yourself academically outside of high school courses.

pathways.title: The Circulatory System
pathways.description: Most animals are complex multicellular organisms that require a mechanism for transporting nutrients throughout their bodies and removing waste products. The circulatory system has evolved over time from simple diffusion through cells in the early evolution of animals to a complex network of blood vessels that reach all parts of the human body. This extensive network supplies the cells, tissues, and organs with oxygen and nutrients, and removes carbon dioxide and waste, which are byproducts of respiration. At the core of the human circulatory system is the heart. Understanding the heart’s basic anatomy and function is important to understanding the body’s circulatory and respiratory systems. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the circulatory system; components of the blood; the mammalian heart & blood vessels; blood flow & blood pressure regulation. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Identify the difference between an open circulatory system and a closed circulatory system.
Describe the components of interstitial fluid and hemolymph.
Discuss the differences and similarities among the organization and evolution of different vertebrate circulatory systems.
Illustrate the basic components of blood.
Explain the differences between red blood cells and white blood cells.
Explain the difference between blood plasma and blood serum.
Describe the structure of the heart and how cardiac muscle differs from other muscles.
Discuss the events in the cardiac cycle.
Illustrate the structure of arteries, veins, and capillaries, and how blood flows through the body.
Explain how blood pressure is regulated.

pathways.title: Introduction to Sequence Alignments
pathways.description: This pathway prepares users to interpret sequence alignments and to generate sequence alignments of multiple protein or nucleic acid sequences using Clustal Omega.
learning_objectives: Interpret a sequence alignment.
Define conservative, semi-conservative, and non-conservative substitutions.
Use sequence alignment tools to generate your own alignments.

pathways.title: BLAST for Beginners
pathways.description: This pathway prepares learners to use the Basic Local Alignment Search Tool (BLAST) program offered by the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI). This includes accessing BLAST, aligning two (or more) sequences using BLAST, and an explanation of the BLAST Results page.

pathways.title: Basic Lab Skills - Measuring pH and Quantitative Measurements
pathways.description: This pathway introduces how to measure pH and the quantitative measurements of temperature, pressure, oxygen, and carbon dioxide. Here, you’ll find a list of foundational method videos and interactives. Use this pathway as a glossary of lab techniques rather than a linear sequence.
learning_objectives: Use reagents and equipment to measure pH.
Understand the purpose of the standard curve when determining pH with a spectrophotometer.
Use equipment to quantify DNA, temperature, pressure, and amounts of oxygen and carbon dioxide.
Recognize foundational lab protocols and equipment.
Identify which lab protocols are useful.
Use methods and lab equipment more easily in the real lab environment.

pathways.title: The Biochemical Process of Metabolism
pathways.description: Organisms require energy to perform heavy labor and exercise, but humans also use considerable energy while thinking, and even during sleep. Every organism's living cells constantly use energy. Organisms import nutrients and other molecules. They metabolize (break down) and possibly synthesize into new molecules. If necessary, molecules modify, move around the cell and may distribute themselves to the entire organism. This pathway provides an in-depth look at energy & metabolism; potential, kinetic, free & activation energy; the laws of thermodynamics; ATP (adenosine triphosphate); and enzymes. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain metabolic pathways and describe the two major types.
Discuss how chemical reactions play a role in energy transfer.
Define “energy”.
Explain the difference between kinetic and potential energy.
Discuss the concepts of free energy and activation energy.
Describe endergonic and exergonic reactions.
Discuss the concept of entropy.
Explain the first and second laws of thermodynamics.
Explain atp's role as the cellular energy currency.
Describe how energy releases through atp hydrolysis.
Describe the role of enzymes in metabolic pathways.
Explain how enzymes function as molecular catalysts.
Discuss enzyme regulation by various factors.

pathways.title: Evolution of Vertebrates
pathways.description: This is the seventh session in the Evolution Flipped Course from iBiology. In this session, we dig more deeply into the evolution of the complex behaviors and patterning found in vertebrates. We begin with an whiteboard video which investigates why sexual reproduction provides an evolutionary advantage over asexual reproduction, in most cases. In the second video, we wonder why you jump when someone startles you. Melina Hale explains how her research into the startle response in fish may help us to understand the evolution of neural circuits in vertebrates. And in the last video, Neil Shubin explains how studying the expression of hox genes in fish and mice may help to explain the evolution of limb development from fish, to early tetrapods like Tiktaalik, to modern mice and humans.

pathways.title: Biodiversity & Relationship
pathways.description: This pathway explores how structural and molecular evidence can be used to determine relationships between plant species.
learning_objectives: Identify major structures of a plant.
Describe how comparing and contrasting external and internal plant structures can help characterize the relationship between different plant specimen.
Explain how restriction enzymes and gel electrophoresis can be used to analyze and compare dna from plant species.
Examine the importance of biodiversity to human and environmental health.

pathways.title: Transpiration and Water Potential
pathways.description: This pathway explains how differences in water potential enable plants to absorb water from the soil to replace the water that is lost to the atmosphere via transpiration.
learning_objectives: Describe what is meant by the term 'transpiration'.
Describe what is meant by the term 'water potential'.
Describe diffusion and osmosis.
Explain how water potential relates to transpiration in plants.
Explain how water potential can be used to determine the direction of diffusion and osmosis.
Describe how solutes and pressure affect the water potential of a solution.

pathways.title: COVID Transmission and Economic Networks
pathways.description: In this pathway, we use a network model to understand how virus transmission can affect individuals and the economy. We also explore the effects of social distancing policies such as lockdown on case numbers, economic transactions, and income inequality.
learning_objectives: Explain how we can use models to understand complex real-world phenomena.
Use a network model to illustrate how a virus can spread throughout a population, and how people in an economy interact with each other.
Examine the effects of social distancing policies on health and economic outcomes.
Discuss covid-19-related public health policies adopted around the world.

pathways.title: Probability
pathways.description: This pathway introduces probabilities and provides useful information on how to solve probability problems.
learning_objectives: Calculate probabilities in different scenarios.
Construct venn and tree diagrams to represent probabilities.
Define dependent and independent events.

pathways.title: Dynamics: Force and Newton's Laws of Motion
pathways.description: This pathway explores the essential ideas in dynamics, which deals with the causes of objects motion, and the interactions between different objects in a system, known as forces.
learning_objectives: Identify and understand the definition of force.
Identify and understand newton's first law of motion.
Define mass and inertia.
Define net force, external force, and system.
Identify and understand newton’s second law of motion.
Define and apply newton’s second law to determine the weight of an object.
Identify and understand newton's third law of motion.
Define and apply newton's third law to define systems and solve problems of motion.
Define normal and tension forces.
Define and apply newton's laws of motion to solve problems involving a variety of forces.
Use trigonometric identities to resolve weight into components.
Define and apply a problem-solving procedure to solve problems using newton's laws of motion.
Define and apply problem-solving techniques to solve for quantities in more complex systems of forces.
Identify and integrate concepts from kinematics to solve problems using newton's laws of motion.
Identify and understand the four basic forces that underlie the processes in nature.

pathways.title: Cellular Reproduction
pathways.description: Heritable information provides the basis for the continuity of life. This pathway explores the process of cellular reproduction in prokaryotes and eukaryotes, and how genetic information is passed on to newly formed cells.
learning_objectives: Explain how prokaryotic cells divide by binary fission.
Explain how eukaryotic cells divide by mitosis followed by cytokinesis.
Describe what happens in each phase of the cell cycle.
Explain how chromosome and DNA content changes throughout the cell cycle.

pathways.title: Lab Etiquette and Safety
pathways.description: This pathway focuses on expected norms and behaviors in a research lab along with best practices when conducting scientific research. Topics include lab roles, being a good lab citizen, how to get the most out of a research experience, and how to communicate with your labmates. Lab safety is an integral part of being a good lab citizen.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of each person in a life science laboratory.
Recall basic lab safety measures.
Practise to be a good lab citizen.

pathways.title: The Jackson Laboratory Teaching the Genome Generation (TtGG™): Human Genetic Variation Virtual Lab Curriculum
pathways.description:
This pathway will introduce you to key concepts and procedures in the study of human genetics, genomics and personalized medicine. As part of the TtGG™: Human Genetic Variation Virtual Lab curriculum, you will conduct virtual experiments, analyze real data, and touch on the ethical complexities at the intersection of human genomics, research and medicine.
For teachers who require supportive guides for the TtGG™ lab experiments and bioinformatics activities, please visit the Teaching the Genome Generation: Genetics Curriculum with Laboratory pathway.
learning_objectives: Perform modern laboratory techniques and properly use laboratory equipment.
Illustrate human genetic variation by performing genotyping assays for several common human alleles.
Interpret DNA gels and/or DNA sequencing traces to infer genotypes for the ACE, ACTN3, CYP2C19, OXTR and/or TAS2R38 genes.

pathways.title: Chromosome Variation
pathways.description: This pathway explores ploidy, variations in the number and structure of chromosomes, and the role that chromosome variations play in agriculture and evolution.
learning_objectives: Define ploidy.
Determine a cell’s haploid number and ploidy.
Compare the 3 classes of chromosomal mutations.
Determine the total number of chromosomes in a given aneuploid or polyploid cell type.

pathways.title: The Nervous System
pathways.description: When you’re reading this book, your nervous system is performing several functions simultaneously. The visual system is processing what is seen on the page; the motor system controls the turn of the pages (or click of the mouse); the prefrontal cortex maintains attention. Even fundamental functions, like breathing and regulation of body temperature, are controlled by the nervous system. A nervous system is an organism’s control center: it processes sensory information from outside (and inside) the body and controls all behaviors—from eating to sleeping to finding a mate. This pathway provides an in-depth look at neurons & glial cells, how neurons communicate, the central nervous system, the peripheral nervous system, and nervous system disorders. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: List and describe the functions of the structural components of a neuron.
List and describe the four main types of neurons.
Compare/contrast the functions of different types of glial cells.
Describe the basis of the resting membrane potential.
Explain the stages of an action potential and how action potentials are propagated.
Explain the similarities and differences between chemical and electrical synapses.
Describe the similarities and differences between chemical and electrical synapses.
Identify the spinal cord, cerebral lobes, and other brain areas on a diagram of the brain.
Describe the basic functions of the spinal cord, cerebral lobes, and other brain areas.
Describe the organization and functions of the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems.
Describe the organization and function of the sensory-somatic nervous system.
Describe the symptoms, potential causes, and treatment of several examples of nervous system disorders.

pathways.title: Fungi Characteristics, Classifications and Ecology
pathways.description: Being eukaryotes, a typical fungal cell contains a true nucleus and many membrane-bound organelles. The kingdom Fungi includes an enormous variety of living organisms collectively referred to as Eumycota, or true Fungi. While scientists have identified about 100,000 species of fungi, this is only a fraction of the 1.5 million species of fungus likely present on Earth. This pathway provides an in-depth look at characteristics of fungi, classifications of fungi, ecology of fungi, fungal parasites and pathogens, and importance of fungi in human life. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: List the characteristics of fungi.
Describe the composition of the mycelium.
Describe the mode of nutrition of fungi.
Explain sexual and asexual reproduction in fungi.
Identify fungi and place them into the five major phyla according to current classification.
Describe each phylum in terms of major representative species and patterns of reproduction.
Describe the role of fungi in various ecosystems.
Describe mutualistic relationships of fungi with plant roots and photosynthetic organisms.
Describe the beneficial relationship between some fungi and insects.
Describe some fungal parasites and pathogens of plants.
Describe the different types of fungal infections in humans.
Explain why antifungal therapy is hampered by the similarity between fungal and animal cells.
Describe the importance of fungi to the balance of the environment.
Summarize the role of fungi in agriculture and food and beverage preparation.
Describe the importance of fungi in the chemical and pharmaceutical industries.
Discuss the role of fungi as model organisms.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Modern Applications of Microbial Genetics
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of microbes and the tools of genetic engineering; visualizing and characterizing DNA, RNA, and protein; whole genome methods & pharmaceutical applications of genetic engineering; and gene therapy

pathways.title: Reproduction at the Cellular Level
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at the genome, the cell cycle, cancer and the cell cycle, and prokaryotic cell division. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the prokaryotic and eukaryotic genome.
Compare/contrast chromosomes, genes, and traits.
Describe the three stages of interphase.
Discuss the behavior of chromosomes during mitosis and how the cytoplasmic content divides during cytokinesis.
Define the quiescent g0 phase.
Explain how the three internal control checkpoints occur at the end of g1, at the g2–m transition, and during metaphase.
Explain how cancer is caused by uncontrolled cell division.
Describe how proto-oncogenes are normal cell genes that, when mutated, become oncogenes.
Describe how tumor suppressors function to stop the cell cycle until certain events are completed.
Explain how mutant tumor suppressors cause cancer.
Describe the process of binary fission in prokaryotes.
Explain how ftsz and tubulin proteins are examples of homology.

pathways.title: Invertebrate Animals
pathways.description: A brief look at any magazine pertaining to our natural world, such as National Geographic, would show a rich variety of vertebrates, especially mammals and birds. To most people, these are the animals that attract our attention. Concentrating on vertebrates, however, gives us a rather biased and limited view of animal diversity, because it ignores nearly 97 percent of the animal kingdom—the invertebrates—animals that lack a cranium and a defined vertebral column or spine. This pathway provides an in-depth look at phylum porifera; phylum cnidaria; superphylum lophotrochozoa (flatworms, rotifers, & nemerteans); superphylum lophotrochozoa (molluscs & annelids); superphylum ecdysozoa (nematodes & tardigrades); superphylum ecdysozoa (arthropods), and superphylum deuterostomia.. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the organizational features of the simplest multicellular organisms.
Explain the various body forms and bodily functions of sponges.
Compare/contrast structural and organization characteristics of porifera and cnidaria.
Describe the progressive development of tissues and their relevance to animal complexity.
Identify the two general body forms found in the cnidaria.
Describe the identifying features of the major cnidarian classes.
Describe the unique anatomical and morphological features of molluscs and annelids.
Describe the formation of the coelom.
Identify an important extracoelomic cavity in molluscs.
Describe the major body regions of mollusca and how they vary in different molluscan classes.
Discuss the advantages of true body segmentation.
Describe the features of animals classified in phylum annelida.
Describe the structural organization of nematodes.
Describe the importance of caenorhabditis elegans in research.
Describe the features of tardigrades.
Compare/contrast the internal systems and appendage specializations of phylum arthropoda.
Discuss the environmental importance of arthropods.
Discuss the reasons for arthropod success and abundance.
Describe the distinguishing characteristics of echinoderms.
Describe the distinguishing characteristics of chordates.

pathways.title: Astrobotany: Food
pathways.description: How do astronauts grow fresh edible plants in space? This pathway explores some of the efforts to grow nutritional crops on the International Space Station and beyond.
learning_objectives: Identify the challenges of growing plants in space.
Examine successful attempts to grow crops in space.

pathways.title: Attribute Sciences
pathways.description: Attribute sciences collectively refers to the study of the molecular properties of a drug and the development of the tools that are used for the screening and characterizing of a drug to ensure appropriate quality for human use. This pathway will explore the various areas that fall under attribute sciences, including purity, impurities, potency, higher order structure, and raw materials.
learning_objectives: Describe the quality attributes of a biologic that are monitored by laboratory methods during product testing.
Describe the different types of monoclonal antibody (mAb) variants and types of impurities that are evaluated during product testing.
List the techniques that can be used to screen for mAb variants and impurities.
Describe what a biological activity test is and how one is designed specifically to assess the potency of a protein-based drug.
Describe how the higher order structure of a mAb is evaluated during product testing.
Describe the different types of raw materials used to manufacture a biologic and how standards for their attributes are established.

pathways.title: Introduction to FairOpportunityX
pathways.description: This pathway gives an overview of the contents, objectives, and resources of the FairOpportunityX cluster.
learning_objectives: Identify the components of the fairopportunityx cluster.
Examine some common misconceptions about applying to college.
Produce a reflection on what you know about college and why you are interested in attending.

pathways.title: Reasoning: What Does It Mean to Reason in Science and Everyday Life?
pathways.description: Follow this pathway to explore the concept and crucial functions of reasoning in science and everyday life. You’ll acquire basic vocabulary for discussing reasoning and arguments, and practice translating examples of reasoning from everyday life into precisely stated arguments. There are two main kinds of reasoning: deducing and theorizing. You’ll explore the connection between an argument's logical form and its deductive validity, and will apply these concepts to clarify some of the key steps of the scientific method.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will be able to describe the characteristics of deductive and non-deductive reasoning.
Identify arguments from everyday life and scientific contexts, and state them in standard form, including their implicit premises.
Distinguish valid and invalid deductive arguments on the basis of their logical forms.

pathways.title: Seed Plants
pathways.description: The lush palms on tropical shorelines do not depend on water for the dispersal of their pollen, fertilization, or the survival of the zygote—unlike mosses, liverworts, and ferns living within the same terrain. These palms are seed plants, which have broken free from the need to rely on water for their reproductive needs. The seed plants play an integral role in all aspects of life on the planet, shaping the physical terrain, influencing the climate, and maintaining life as we know it. For millennia, human societies have depended on seed plants for nutrition and medicinal compounds. Somewhat more recently, seed plants have served as a source of manufactured products such as timber and paper, dyes, and textiles. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the evolution of seed plants, gymnosperms, angiosperms, and the role of seed plants. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the two major innovations that allowed seed plants to reproduce in the absence of water.
Explain when seed plants first appeared and when gymnosperms became the dominant plant group.
Discuss the purpose of pollen grains and seeds.
Describe the significance of angiosperms bearing both flowers and fruit.
Discuss the type of seeds produced by gymnosperms, as well as other characteristics of gymnosperms.
Identify the geological era dominated by the gymnosperms and describe the conditions to which they were adapted.
List the four groups of modern-day gymnosperms and provide examples of each.
Describe the life cycle of a typical gymnosperm.
Explain why angiosperms are the dominant form of plant life in most terrestrial ecosystems.
Describe the main parts of a flower and their functions.
Examine the life cycle of a typical gymnosperm and angiosperm.
Discuss the similarities and differences between the two main groups of flowering plants.
Explain how angiosperm diversity is due, in part, to multiple complex interactions with animals.
Describe ways in which pollination occurs.
Discuss the roles that plants play in ecosystems and how deforestation threatens plant biodiversity.

pathways.title: Deciding on College
pathways.description: This pathway offers perspective on what college offers and how to make the decision of whether college is right for you.
learning_objectives: Identify and dispel common misconception about college.
Explain factors that should be considered when deciding on college.
Develop goals for your future self and relate those goals to the question of whether college is right for you.

pathways.title: Addressing Systemic Racism in STEM Education
learning_objectives: Recognize why it is important to address systemic racism in STEM education.
Define systemic racism and the forms of racism that create and support it.
Discuss the learning barriers that systemic racism creates for racially diverse students.
Identify strategies to address the effects of systemic racism at an institutional level in STEM education.

pathways.title: Sensory Systems
pathways.description: In more advanced animals, the senses are constantly at work, making the animal aware of stimuli—such as light, or sound, or the presence of a chemical substance in the external environment—and monitoring information about the organism’s internal environment. All bilaterally symmetric animals have a sensory system, and the development of any species’ sensory system has been driven by natural selection; thus, sensory systems differ among species according to the demands of their environments.This pathway provides an in-depth look at sensory processes, somatosensation, taste & smell, hearing & vestibular sensation, and vision. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Identify the general and special senses in humans.
Describe three important steps in sensory perception.
Explain the concept of just-noticeable difference in sensory perception.
Describe four important mechanoreceptors in human skin.
Describe the topographical distribution of somatosensory receptors between glabrous and hairy skin.
Explain why the perception of pain is subjective.
Explain in what way smell and taste stimuli differ from other sensory stimuli.
Identify the five primary tastes that can be distinguished by humans.
Explain in anatomical terms why a dog’s sense of smell is more acute than a human’s.
Describe the relationship of amplitude and frequency of a sound wave to attributes of sound.
Identify the structures of the vestibular system that respond to gravity.
Identify and trace the path of sound through the auditory system to the site of transduction of sound.
Explain how electromagnetic waves differ from sound waves.
Identify and trace the path of light through the eye to the point of the optic nerve.
Explain tonic activity as it is manifested in photoreceptors in the retina.

pathways.title: How Evolution is Measured
pathways.description: This is the fourth session of the Evolution Flipped Course from iBiology. In this session, we investigate some of the methods used by scientists to measure evolution. A whiteboard animation introduces Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (HWE) and explains how it can be used to calculate frequencies of specific alleles in a population. This whiteboard also suggests reasons why allele frequencies in a population may evolve or change over time. Three short video clips (from Drs. Hale, Tishkoff, and Newman) explain methods for generating phylogenetic trees and what they can tell us about the relatedness of species and how long ago they diverged. In our last video, David Haussler shares his excitement about the chance to compare multiple sequenced genomes and identify the genetic innovations that made us who we are today.
learning_objectives: Understand how to use the hardy-weinberg equilibrium to solve population genetics problems.
Describe how fossils and dna analyses are used to build phylogenetic trees.
Describe how phylogenetic trees can be used to represent the diversity of life on earth and trace population migration.
Understand how dna sequence alignments allow us to uncover the genetic innovations that comprise our own genomes.

pathways.title: Inspiring the Scientists of Tomorrow: Learning Resources Co-Developed With Amgen Scientists
pathways.description: LabXchange is sponsored by the Amgen Foundation, which seeks to advance excellence in science education to inspire the next generation of innovators. Our team works with Amgen staff scientists to co-develop learning content on a variety of educational topics. This pathway features several of these interactives and other learning resources!

pathways.title: G-proteins
pathways.description: Ras is both a key molecule in regulating normal cell growth and an oncogene in unregulated cancer cell growth. In this pathway, Dr. Alfred Wittinghofer (MPI) discusses the structure of Ras and how solving this structure led to insights into disease mechanisms associated with GTPases. This pathway is session 3 of the Cell Biology Flipped Course from iBiology.

pathways.title: BLAST Tutorial Series
pathways.description: In this tutorial series from the Teaching the Genome Generation™ (TtGG) program at The Jackson Laboratory, learn to use the online NCBI Basic Local Alignment Search Tool (BLAST) to compare DNA and protein sequences. For practice applying these techniques, check out the TtGG Cancer Genetics and Sequence Comparison & Identity modules.
learning_objectives: Compare DNA and protein sequences using BLAST, an online bioinformatics tool.
Use sequence comparison to identify a gene from a short nucleotide sequence.

pathways.title: Sound
pathways.description: This pathway explores the concept of sound. Sound is described in terms of its wave properties, and properties of sound are explained, such as interference, and the Doppler Effect. There is additional information on how hearing works.
learning_objectives: Explain that sound is a longitudinal wave.
Describe how the doppler effect arises due to the relative motion of a sound source and observer.
Explain how ultrasound is used in medical diagnostics.
Describe how hearing occurs when sound waves reach the ear.

pathways.title: Plant Reproduction
pathways.description: Plants have evolved different reproductive strategies for the continuation of their species. Some plants reproduce sexually, and others asexually, in contrast to animal species, which rely almost exclusively on sexual reproduction. Plant sexual reproduction usually depends on pollinating agents, while asexual reproduction is independent of these agents. Flowers are often the showiest or most strongly scented part of plants. With their bright colors, fragrances, and interesting shapes and sizes, flowers attract insects, birds, and animals to serve their pollination needs. Other plants pollinate via wind or water; still others self-pollinate. This pathway provides an in-depth look at reproductive development & structure, pollination and fertilization, and asexual reproduction. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the two stages of a plant’s lifecycle.
Compare/contrast male and female gametophytes and explain how they form in angiosperms.
Describe the reproductive structures of a plant describe the components of a complete flower.
Describe the components of a complete flower.
Describe the development of microsporangium and megasporangium in gymnosperms.
Describe what must occur for plant fertilization.
Explain cross-pollination and the ways in which it takes place.
Describe the process that leads to the development of a seed.
Define double fertilization.
Compare/contrast the mechanisms and methods of natural and artificial asexual reproduction.
Describe the advantages and disadvantages of natural and artificial asexual reproduction.
Discuss plant life spans.

pathways.title: Work, Energy, and Energy Resources
pathways.description: In this pathway, energy is loosely defined as the ability to do work, admitting that in some circumstances not all energy is available to do work. Because of the association of energy with work, we begin the pathway with a discussion of work. Work is intimately related to energy and how energy moves from one system to another or changes form. The work-energy theorem, introduced in this pathway, establishes the relationship between work done on an object by an external force and changes in the object’s kinetic energy. Similarly, systems can do work on each other. This pathway also discusses how to calculate kinetic, gravitational, and elastic potential energy in order to determine the total mechanical energy of a system; and the conservation of energy.
learning_objectives: Explain how an object must be displaced for a force on it to do work.
Explain how relative directions of force and displacement of an object determine whether the work done on the object is positive, negative, or zero.
Explain work as a transfer of energy and net work as the work done by the net force.
Explain and apply the work-energy theorem.
Explain gravitational potential energy in terms of work done against gravity.
Describe that the gravitational potential energy of an object of mass m at height h on earth is given by peg = mgh.
Describe how knowledge of potential energy as a function of position can be used to simplify calculations and explain physical phenomena.
Define conservative force, potential energy, and mechanical energy.
Explain the potential energy of a spring in terms of its compression when hooke’s law applies.
Use the work-energy theorem to show how having only conservative forces leads to conservation of mechanical energy.
Define nonconservative forces and explain how they affect mechanical energy.

Describe how the principle of conservation of energy can be applied by treating the conservative forces in terms of their potential energies and any nonconservative forces in terms of the work they do.
Explain the law of the conservation of energy.
Describe some of the many forms of energy.
Define efficiency of an energy conversion process as the fraction left as useful energy or work, rather than being transformed, for example, into thermal energy.
Calculate power by calculating changes in energy over time.
Examine power consumption and calculations of the cost of energy consumed.
Explain the human body’s consumption of energy when at rest versus when engaged in activities that do useful work.
Calculate the conversion of chemical energy in food into useful work.
Describe the distinction between renewable and nonrenewable energy sources.
Explain why the inevitable conversion of energy to less useful forms makes it necessary to conserve energy resources.

pathways.title: Is My Sense of Taste Controlled by My Genes?
pathways.description: In this pathway, you will chart the specific tastes of a variety of foods, including vegetables in the Brassica genus. Next, you will learn about variations in the TAS2R38 gene, and how this gene may impact whether you are able to taste certain bitter compounds. You will also learn more about the process of polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and explore the connection between genes and traits.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will be able to identify taste receptors as proteins that bind to molecules from our food.
Identify patterns in your personal taste preferences for different fruits or vegetables.
Understand how your preference for bitter tasting foods is in part determined by your TAS2R38 genotype.
Describe the relationship between genes and traits, including the key role of proteins.
Explain the role of chelating beads during DNA extraction.
Explain the purpose and process of PCR.
Discuss the evidence presented for the connection between PTC tasting and bitter food preferences.

pathways.title: How Can We Begin to Understand Human Genetic Diversity?
learning_objectives: Recognize human genetic diversity as small, biologically continuous, discordant, and greater within populations.
Explain the influences of evolution and the environment on the patterns of human genetic diversity.
Demonstrate how race in humans has no biological basis and is not representative of genetic diversity in the human species.

pathways.title: Slider Interactives
pathways.description: These interactive images explore how different conditions impact proteins and DNA, and how DNA is synthesised and sequenced. The interactive sliders allow you to examine the state of different proteins and DNA in a variety of scenarios.
learning_objectives: Use the interactive sliders to adjust the scenarios.
Identify how different conditions impact proteins and dna.
Examine the processes of dna synthesis and sanger sequencing.

pathways.title: Overview of Meiosis in Animal Cells
pathways.description: This pathway provides an overview of meiosis in animal cells.
learning_objectives: You will explain the structure of a chromosome, including the centromere, kinetochore, chromatid, sister chromatids, and non-sister chromatids.
You will describe the different stages of meiosis I and meiosis II, and the role they play in the development of new organisms.
You will explain how meiosis results in genetic variation.

pathways.title: Experimental Design
pathways.description: This pathway puts emphasis on asking scientific questions and defining problems. It introduces the experimental setup as a key part of planning and carrying out investigations to answer scientific questions. In particular, this pathway illustrates the importance of experimental controls through a number of examples.
learning_objectives: Justify that, by applying the scientific method, experimentation is the best way to support or reject hypotheses.
Generate research questions and hypotheses.
Use controls to validate an experiment.

pathways.title: Teaching Guides for the Exploring Precision Medicine Cluster
pathways.description:
Use these teacher guides along with the learner pathways in the cluster to supplement an in-person classroom or lab experience.
These are adaptations of the Amgen Biotech Experience Exploring Precision Medicine Curriculum. This provides pacing, ideas for in-person instruction and discussion, as well as links to digital supports for a hybrid experience.
To learn about digital supports for this curriculum see the Amgen Biotech Experience Exploring Precision Medicine Cluster. For more information on this classroom and lab-based curriculum see the Teaching and Learning Materials from the Amgen Biotech Experience.
learning_objectives: In this pathway, you will understand that people respond to medications in different ways.
Describe the relationship between genes and traits, including the key role of proteins.
Understand how your preference for bitter-tasting foods is partly determined by your TAS2R38 genotype.
Explain the purpose and process of PCR.
Explain how restriction enzymes and gel electrophoresis can be used to analyze DNA.
Implement the Amgen Biotechnology Experience Precision Medicine Curriculum in a classroom or hybrid setting.

pathways.title: Fermentation
pathways.description: This pathway covers cellular respiration and fermentation, which are aerobic and anaerobic pathways by which organisms derive chemical energy from glucose. A hands-on lab component provides the opportunity to conduct fermentation experiments with kimchi, yogurt, and yeast.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Explain how organisms derive energy from glucose via cellular respiration and fermentation.
Describe the differences between cellular respiration and fermentation.
Conduct a fermentation experiment.
Describe how lactic acid fermentation is different to ethanol fermentation.

pathways.title: Immune System: Structure, Function, and Health Strategies
pathways.description: Explore how the immune system defends against pathogens, the role of vaccines, and the significance of memory cells in adaptive immunity. Understand the science behind immune responses, vaccination, and maintaining health through engaging videos, interactive content, and case studies.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to explain the structure and function of the immune system, including its cellular and molecular components.
Analyze the process of immune response to pathogens, including the roles of innate and adaptive immunity.
Evaluate the impact of immunodeficiency disorders, autoimmune diseases, and allergies on health.
Describe strategies for maintaining a healthy immune system, such as vaccination and lifestyle choices.
Apply knowledge of immunology to understand the principles of infection control and disease prevention.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To How We See the Invisible World
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of the properties of light; peering into the invisible world; instruments of microscopy; staining microscopic specimens.

pathways.title: The College Application: Timeline, Content, and Process
pathways.description: This pathway details the components of the college application and how to organize your application.
learning_objectives: Examine the components of a college application.
Construct a strategy for organizing the college application.
Build a college list.

pathways.title: Interpreting Commonly Used Charts and Plots
pathways.description: This pathway prepares users to interpret and critique the types of charts and plots commonly used to summarize and visualize scientific data in papers, posters, and textbooks.
learning_objectives: Illustrate data sets using histograms, barplots, boxplots, violin plots, and pie charts.
Interpret data sets using histograms, barplots, boxplots, violin plots, and pie charts.
Critique common ways of presenting data (including histograms, violin plots, boxplots, barplots, and pie charts).

pathways.title: Introduction to Energy in Cells
pathways.description: How do organisms get the energy they need to perform their vital processes? Every cell, whether prokaryotic or eukaryotic, needs energy, but the means by which they obtain this energy can vary significantly. While some organisms gain energy from consuming other living beings, others have the remarkable ability to harvest energy directly from their environment.
learning_objectives: Classify organisms as autotrophs or heterotrophs.
Classify organisms as phototrophs or chemotrophs.
Determine whether a metabolism is aerobic or anaerobic.

pathways.title: Interpreting Model Figures
pathways.description: This pathway introduces foundational skills involved in interpreting model figures, which are commonly used to describe scientific models and hypotheses.
