learning_objectives: Illustrate your hypotheses using the conventions of biological model figures.

pathways.title: Scientific Writing
pathways.description: The clear communication of findings, arguments, data, and models is central to science. Writing in an engaging, concise, and well-structured way will help you to craft an email, advance a scientific argument, or structure a research paper. Knowing your audience is especially important: more and more, scientists need to communicate clearly with science experts as well as the general public and other non-experts.
learning_objectives: Understand the basics of writing scientific content and tailor it to your audience.
Explain how to structure a scientific argument.
Identify the structural elements, including the abstract, of a scientific paper.
Learn how to create graphic content, such as slides and posters, that visually supports and enhances your writing in an accessible way.
Communicate with scientists and the general public in writing.

pathways.title: Deep Learning Basics with Pytorch
pathways.description: A comprehensive introduction to the principles and techniques of deep learning using PyTorch, a popular open-source machine learning framework. You will learn to work with tensors, manipulate data, and build powerful neural networks to solve real-world problems. Check out the tutorial on the Neuromatch website.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will learn about PyTorch and tensors.
You will be able to perform tensor computations.
You will be able to describe how to train a neural network.
You will discuss the data requirements of deep learning.

pathways.title: Evolution and the Origin of Species
pathways.description: All living organisms, from bacteria to baboons to blueberries, evolved at some point from a different species. Although it may seem that living things today stay much the same, that is not the case—evolution is an ongoing process. The theory of evolution is the unifying theory of biology, meaning it is the framework within which biologists ask questions about the living world. Its power is that it provides direction for predictions about living things that are borne out in ongoing experiments. This pathway provides an in-depth look at understanding evolution, formation of new species, and reconnection & speciation rates. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe how scientists developed the present-day theory of evolution.
Define adaptation.
Explain convergent and divergent evolution.
Describe homologous and vestigial structures.
Discuss misconceptions about the theory of evolution.
Define species and describe how scientists identify species as different.
Describe genetic variables that lead to speciation.
Identify prezygotic and postzygotic reproductive barriers.
Explain allopatric and sympatric speciation.
Describe adaptive radiation.
Describe pathways of species evolution in hybrid zones.
Explain the two major theories on rates of speciation.

pathways.title: Protein Kinases
pathways.description: This pathway provides an overview of protein kinase structure and function using cyclic AMP dependent kinase (PKA) as a prototype for this enzyme superfamily. This pathway is the eighth session of the Cell Biology Flipped Course from iBiology.

pathways.title: Water Cycle
pathways.description: This pathway provides an overview of the water cycle and introduces some of the processes involved. A hands-on activity demonstrates how to make a miniature model of the water cycle.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Explain the major processes of the water cycle.
Identify several water cycle processes represented in a miniature water cycle model.
Describe the different states of matter for water.

pathways.title: NASA | The Origin of Stars and Planets
pathways.description: Embark on a cosmic journey of discovery as you unravel the mysteries of star formation, witness the breathtaking birth of stars and learning the celestial recipe for crafting entire planets with the James Webb Space Telescope.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you should be able to define the basic concepts of star and planet formation
Explain the key stages in the formation of stars and planets
Apply knowledge of star and planet formation to analyze and interpret real-world examples
Understand the principles of spectroscopy used to identify stars and planets in the universe

pathways.title: Climate Models
pathways.description: This pathway explains and demonstrates the use of climate models with increasing complexity, and explains why scientists develop different climate models.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Develop your understanding of climate models and how they are created.
Examine the different levels of complexity associated with building climate models.
Explain how climate scientists use climate models to measure and simulate the effects of climate change.

pathways.title: Axial Skeleton
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at divisions of the skeletal system; the skull; the vertebral column; the thoracic cage; and embryonic development of the axial skeleton. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the functions of the skeletal system and define its two major subdivisions.
Identify the bones and bony structures of the skull, the cranial suture lines, the cranial fossae, and the openings in the skull.
Discuss the vertebral column and regional variations in its bony components and curvatures.
Describe the components of the thoracic cage.
Discuss the embryonic development of the axial skeleton.

pathways.title: Plant Form and Physiology
pathways.description: Plants are as essential to human existence as land, water, and air. Without plants, our day-to-day lives would be impossible because without oxygen from photosynthesis, aerobic life cannot be sustained. From providing food and shelter to serving as a source of medicines, oils, perfumes, and industrial products, plants provide humans with numerous valuable resources. When you think of plants, those that first come to mind are likely to be vascular plants. While each vascular plant species is unique, all are made up of a plant body consisting of stems, roots, and leaves. They also all transport water, minerals, and sugars produced through photosynthesis through the plant body using the same mechanism, and they all respond to environmental factors, such as light, gravity, competition, temperature, and predation. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the plant body; stems; roots; leaves; the transport of water & solutes in plants; and plant sensory systems & responses . The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Describe the differences between the shoot organ system and the root organ system.
List differences between meristematic tissues and permanent tissues.
Identify the three regions where plant growth occurs.
Discuss the roles of dermal tissues, vascular tissues, and ground tissues.
Compare/contrast the difference between simple plant tissues and complex plant tissues.
Describe the main function and illustrate the basic structure of a plant stem.
Explain the difference between primary growth and secondary growth in stems.
Identify the origin of annual rings in stems.
Describe how annual rings are used to approximate the age of a tree.
Identify examples of modified stems.
Define the two types of root system.
Illustrate the three zones of the root tip and describe the role of each in root growth.
Illustrate the structure of the root.
Identify examples of modified roots.
Illustrate the parts of a typical leaf.
Describe the internal structure and function of a leaf.
Compare/contrast the differences/similarities between simple leaves and compound leaves.
Identify examples of modified leaves.
Explain water potential, and how it is influenced by solutes, pressures, gravity, and the matric potential.
Discuss how water potential, evapotranspiration, and stomatal regulation influence how water is transported in plants.
Describe how photosynthates are transported in plants.
Explain how red and blue light affect plant growth and metabolic activities.
Explain gravitropism.
Identify examples of plant hormones, and describe how they affect plant growth and development.
Explain the differences between thigmotropism, thigmonastism, and thigmogenesis.
Discuss how plants defend themselves against predators and respond to wounds.

pathways.title: The Blueprint of Life: Exploring DNA and Fingerprinting
pathways.description: The resources in this pathway provide a deep dive into the science of DNA, including its structure, function, and the process of creating DNA fingerprints. Learners will explore the building blocks of DNA through interactive activities, build virtual DNA ladders, and participate in a hands-on project to understand the uniqueness of DNA fingerprints. This pathway offers a dynamic way to connect theoretical concepts with real-world applications in genetics and forensic science.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the structure of DNA.
Understand the process of translation and transcription to form a protein.
Understand the applications of DNA fingerprinting.

pathways.title: Acids and Bases: The Voyage of the Proton
pathways.description: This unit introduces the key concepts of acids and bases. The acidity of a solution is a measurement of the concentration of hydrogen ions it contains, and the acidity has a great impact on the properties of the solution. Pure water contains a certain concentration of hydrogen ions. Dissolving acids in water raises the concentration of hydrogen ions, while dissolving bases in water lowers the concentration. Acidity is measured in pH units, and the acidity of a solution depends both on the concentration of acid dissolved and the strength (degree of dissociation) of the acid. Chemists use indicators to measure pH as well as titration to determine the concentration and strength of an acid. This unit forms part of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Examine the dissociation of water molecules into hydronium and hydroxide ions and explain the equilibrium constant (kw) for that reaction.
Define acids and bases using the arrhenius and brønsted-lowry (conjugate acid/base pairs) definitions of acids and bases.
Define the pka of an acid and understand its relationship to the acid’s strength.
Calculate the ph of a solution.
Explain the titration curves of monoprotic acids.
Define buffer and explain how one works.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Microbial Biochemistry
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of organic molecules; carbohydrates; lipids; proteins and using biochemistry to identify microorganisms.

pathways.title: Spectroscopy 101
pathways.description: What is spectroscopy, anyway? Through this engaging pathway, you will acquire an understanding of spectroscopy and its real-world applications. Join us on this journey as we unveil the hidden wonders of the universe through the lens of spectroscopy. Whether you're a budding scientist or simply curious about the world around you, Spectroscopy 101 will illuminate the path to scientific discovery.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the basics of spectroscopy and its role in scientific research and space exploration.
Explain the interaction between light and matter and understand the concepts of absorption and emission.
Identify and explain the characteristics of different types of spectra.
Describe the spectral patterns of elements, and understand the connection between spectral lines and atomic structure.
Explore the different applications of spectroscopy beyond determining temperature and composition.

pathways.title: Careers in Genetics and Genomics at JAX
pathways.description: In this pathway, explore jobs in genetics and genomics to find ones that fit your skills and interests. Hear from scientists, educators, designers, and more about their work and career paths.
learning_objectives: Explore the variety of different types of career paths available in genetics and genomics.
Identify jobs in genetics and genomics that fit your skills and interests.

pathways.title: Fluorescence Microscopy
pathways.description: This pathway shows a simple animated diagram of a fluorescence microscope, illustrates the light path in an upright microscope, and shows how CCDs (charged couple device) are used to generate a charge that can be read by electronics and turned into a digital copy.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Digestive System Infections
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of anatomy & normal microbiota of the digestive system; microbial diseases of the mouth & oral cavity; bacterial infections of the gastrointestinal tract; viral infections of the gastrointestinal tract; protozoan infections of the gastrointestinal tract; and helminthic infections of the gastrointestinal tract.

pathways.title: Teaching the Genome Generation: Cancer Genetics Module
pathways.description: This Cancer Genetics module is part of a suite of curriculum from The Jackson Laboratory which aims to teach quantitative and data analysis skills in the context of genetics and bioinformatics. The module centers around a futuristic theme on newborn sequencing. Fast forward many years in the future to a time when newborns receive whole genome sequencing which generates a Newborn Genomic Report that is added their medical records. Gene variants associated with increased risk for cancer would be detailed on the report and an individual with such a gene variant would be at risk for hereditary cancer. The first lesson includes an activity in which you will use a publicly available database for cancer genomics called the cBioPortal to analyze real patient data. In the second activity, you will use BLAST to identify gene variants carried by different members within a family, emphasizing inheritance and taking a backwards approach to pedigrees. An extension activity focuses on the concept that cancer is complex disease, illustrating the interaction between genes and the environment. The module concludes with discussion prompts centered around the bioethics of cancer genetic testing. Additional resources for this module include cBioPortal video tutorials and BLAST video tutorials. If you are a teacher interested in implementing this module with your students, check out our teacher guide and webinar describing the module.
learning_objectives: Navigate a cancer database and explore data from a cancer research study.
Calculate and analyze the range and median age of diagnosis for individuals with potentially inherited gene variants.
Discuss concepts such as germline inheritance, predisposition for cancer, and how genetics could influence age of diagnosis.
Interpret data and graphs to illustrate how environmental factors contribute to cancer risk.
Compare gene sequences to uncover if individuals carry a gene variant associated with hereditary skin cancer.
Discuss the ethical implications of genetic testing for cancer risk.

pathways.title: Which College is Right For Me?
pathways.description: This pathway reviews ways to explore college options, learn about particular colleges, and start creating your college list.
learning_objectives: Develop a plan to learn about your college options.
Identify misconceptions that cause students to apply to colleges that are unaffordable or that they wouldn't like.
Use strategies to find colleges that fit you best.

pathways.title: Voices in Science
pathways.description: This pathway offers diverse voices in science from people of all backgrounds. It helps the student to see themselves in the role of scientist and that everyone can be part of the scientific endeavor. The collected stories in science provide a broad overview of the different scientific journeys, different backgrounds, different inspirations, and ultimately different career choices in STEM. The display of scientists in different fields and roles in science encourages the student to think about their own genuine interest in science.
learning_objectives: Find diverse voices and experiences in science.
Relate to scientists of all backgrounds.
Understand that diversity makes for better science.

pathways.title: Research Presentations
pathways.description: Effective slides support a presentation; they don't replace the speaker. Master the art of scientific communication with our guide on crafting visually appealing presentation slides, ensuring your research resonates with diverse audiences. Elevate your storytelling skills and make every data point count.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will develop and refine their research presentation skills by crafting clear, focused slides and practice delivery to communicate scientific concepts effectively.
Create engaging presentations through designing supportive slides and using effective public speaking techniques.
Learn how to analyze the audience to set relevant goals, and develop core messages for better communication.
Tailor presentations to assess audience traits for improved engagement and understanding.

pathways.title: Atmospheric Pollution
pathways.description: Unit 11 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. Many forms of atmospheric pollution affect human health and the environment at levels from local to global. These contaminants are emitted from diverse sources, and some of them react with one another to form new compounds in the air. Industrialized nations have made important progress toward controlling some pollutants in recent decades, but air quality is much worse in many developing countries, and global circulation patterns can transport some types of pollution rapidly around the world. In this unit, discover the basic chemistry of atmospheric pollution and learn which human activities have the greatest impacts on air quality.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming

pathways.title: Teaching the Genome Generation: Sequence Comparison & Identity
pathways.description:
This Sequence Comparison & Identity module is part of a suite of curriculum from The Jackson Laboratory which aims to teach quantitative and data analysis skills in the context of genetics and bioinformatics. In this module, students use bioinformatics tools BLAST, UniProt, and PolyPhen-2 to answer the question: what can we learn from comparing sequences across individuals and species?
The first part of the module introduces applications of sequence comparison using the five genes from the Teaching the Genome Generation™ (TtGG) laboratory curriculum. The second part of the module centers around a real research study on a rare neurodevelopmental disorder. First, you will read a scenario that introduces you to some of the participants in the research study. Next, you will use BLAST to identify variants in the patients’ DNA that could be contributing to their disorder. Then you will use bioinformatics tools PolyPhen-2, UniProt, and BLAST to evaluate the potential impact of one of the patient’s DNA variants on protein structure and function. The module concludes with bioethics discussion prompts centered around rare disease research and health care access.
Additional resources for this module include tutorials for BLAST, UniProt, and PolyPhen-2. If you are a teacher interested in implementing this module with your students, check out our teacher guide and webinar describing the module.
learning_objectives: Calculate sequence percent identity to compare DNA, RNA, and protein sequences across individuals and species.
Effectlvely utilize bioinformatics tools BLAST, PolyPhen-2, and UniProt to find and interpret biological data.
Use sequence comparisons and percent identity calculations to demonstrate that changes in the genetic code can affect changes in protein sequence and structure, which can change protein function.
Utilize percent identity calculations to support hypotheses about the evolutionary relationships between species.
Discuss ethical considerations for rare disease research.

pathways.title: Data Literacy: Statistical Research Tools
pathways.description: Build a strong foundation in data science and data literacy! This pathway explores key concepts like distributions, variability, and centrality, alongside hands-on practice in data visualization, correlation vs. causation, and experimental design. Learn to interpret biological and chemical data through graphs, histograms, and decision trees—turning raw data into meaningful insights!
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of data literacy in life science.
Use statistics to interpret data in life science.
Interpret visualized data.

pathways.title: Conservation Biology and Biodiversity
pathways.description: In the 1980s, biologists working in Lake Victoria in Africa discovered one of the most extraordinary products of evolution on the planet. Located in the Great Rift Valley, Lake Victoria is an enormous and deep lake about 68,900 km2 in area (larger than Lake Huron, the second largest of North America’s Great Lakes). Biologists were studying species of a family of fish called cichlids. When they sampled for fish in different locations of the lake, the researchers identified over 500 evolved species in total. However, the scientists soon discovered that the invasive Nile Perch was destroying the lake’s cichlid population, bringing hundreds of cichlid species to extinction with devastating rapidity. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the biodiversity crisis, the importance of biodiversity to human life, threats to biodiversity, and preserving biodiversity. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Define biodiversity in terms of species diversity and abundance.
Describe biodiversity in terms of species diversity and abundance.
Identify historical causes of high extinction rates in earth’s history.
Identify chemical diversity benefits to humans.
Identify biodiversity components that support human agriculture.
Describe ecosystem services.
Identify significant threats to biodiversity.
Explain the effects of habitat loss, the introduction of exotic species, and hunting on biodiversity.
Identify the early and predicted effects of climate change on biodiversity.
Identify new technologies and methods for describing biodiversity.
Explain the legislative framework for conservation.
Describe principles and challenges of conservation preserve design.
Identify examples of the effects of habitat restoration.
Discuss the role of zoos in biodiversity conservation.

pathways.title: Pubmed for Beginners
pathways.description: This pathway introduces PubMed, a database developed and maintained by the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) at the U.S. National Library of Medicine (NLM), which contains more than 33 million citations and abstracts of biomedical literature. In this pathway, users will learn how to use PubMed to find research articles by author, topic, from a citation, or by journal.

pathways.title: Open Science
pathways.description: Open Science is a model that maximizes community participation in the formulation of investigations, collection of knowledge, and dissemination of data. This pathway gives some examples of Open Science and Open Data initiatives at NASA.
learning_objectives: Identify the benefits of the open science model.
Examine NASA's open science datasets.

pathways.title: NCBI for Experienced Users
pathways.description: This pathway prepares you to use NCBI to find the curated sequence of a gene, along with many other important data about the gene, including its associated phenotype and expression patterns, homologs, SNPs, and research published about the gene.
learning_objectives: Locate files providing the sequence of a gene.
Locate information about a gene's expression patterns, homologs, and allelic variation.
Identify research articles written about a gene or protein.
Design PCR primers specific for a particular gene or locus.
Locate and view the 3D structure(s) of a protein or nucleic acid.

pathways.title: Biotech Software Tools in Use
pathways.description: Digital tools are transforming biotech labs—and biological technicians use specialized software to model molecules, simulate experiments, and visualize results. This pathway introduces essential software skills used in biotechnology research, including molecular modeling, simulation setup, and interaction analysis. Explore how specialized digital tools help scientists predict molecular behavior, run experiments virtually, and visualize complex biological processes that are difficult to observe directly in a laboratory setting.
learning_objectives: Understand the fundamentals of molecular modeling.
Navigate the specialized software interface.
Set up and run basic simulations.
Understand how to analyze molecular interactions.

pathways.title: Universal Gravitation
pathways.description: This pathways explores one of the fundamental forces of nature: the Law of Universal Gravitation, first formulated by Isaac Newton. There are explanations of how to use the law to calculate forces between different bodies, as well as some applications of this law. There are questions at the end of the pathway to check for understanding of the topic.
learning_objectives: Recall newton's law of universal gravitation.
Solve problems involving the gravitational force between two objects.
Explain some of the implication of the law of gravitation for astronomers.

pathways.title: Noor Al-Alusi, MS - Epidemiologist
pathways.description: Noor Al-Alusi is an epidemiologist, lifelong adventurer, has a black belt in Taekwondo, and is passionate about serving her communities.

pathways.title: Mendelian Inheritance
pathways.description: This pathway examines Mendel’s landmark studies of pea plants that allowed him to discover the basic principles of heredity. Topics covered include different types of genetic crosses (specifically monohybrid, dihybrid, multihybrid, and reciprocal crosses, as well as testcrosses), the concept of dominance, and Mendel’s laws of segregation and independent assortment. It also explores the use of branch diagrams and the rules of probability to solve genetic crosses.
learning_objectives: Describe the key characteristics of an ideal model organism.
Explain the purpose of each step in a genetic cross.
Predict the outcome of a genetic cross following one or more traits that are each controlled by a single gene.
Describe the concept of dominance.
Explain each of Mendel's Laws.
Use a branch diagram to determine the results of a genetic cross involving 2 or more characteristics.
Predict the outcome of monohybrid, dihybrid, and multihybrid crosses using basic rules of probability.

pathways.title: Unlocking the Secrets of Protein Synthesis: From DNA to Proteins
pathways.description: Dive into the fascinating world of protein synthesis! This learning pathway is designed to assist Grade 12 learners through the journey of protein synthesis; from DNA to proteins, unravelling the roles of RNA, nucleotides, and amino acids. Through engaging lessons and interactive activities, discover how genetic blueprints are transformed into vital proteins. Start your adventure in molecular biology today!
learning_objectives: By the end of the learning pathway, learners will be able to identify the key differences between DNA and RNA.
Describe the role of RNA in protein synthesis.
Summarize the steps of translation and where it takes place in the cell.
Use a virtual simulation to model the entire process of protein synthesis, from transcription to protein folding.
Distinguish between the roles of mRNA, tRNA, and ribosomes in protein synthesis.
Compare and contrast the structure and function of DNA and RNA.
Illustrate how mRNA codons correspond to amino acids during translation with the use of a virtual simulation.

pathways.title: Oscillatory Motion and Waves
pathways.description: This pathway introduces oscillation, the regular variation in the position of a system about a central point accompanied by trasfer of energy and momentum. It opens by discussing the forces that govern oscillations and waves, and goes on to discuss important concepts such as simple harmonic motion, uniform harmonic motion, and damped harmonic motion. It also explores energy in simple harmonic motion and how it changes from kinetic to potential, and how the total sum, which would be the mechanical energy of the oscillator, remains constant or conserved at all times. The pathway also discusses characteristics of waves, such as their frequency, period of oscillation, and the forms in which they can exist, i.e., transverse or longitudinal. The pathway ends by discussing what happens when two or more waves overlap and how the amplitude of the resultant wave changes, leading to the phenomena of superposition and interference.
learning_objectives: Explain newton’s third law of motion with respect to stress and deformation.
Describe the restoring force and displacement..
Use hooke’s law of deformation, and calculate stored energy in a spring.
Identify and relate recurring mechanical vibrations to the frequency and period of harmonic motion, such as the motion of a guitar string.
Describe a simple harmonic oscillator.
Identify and relate physical characteristics of a vibrating system to aspects of simple harmonic motion and any resulting waves.
Describe and determine the period of oscillation of a hanging pendulum.
Describe the changes in energy that occur while a system undergoes simple harmonic motion.
Compare/contrast simple harmonic motion with uniform circular motion.
Compare/contrast and discuss underdamped and overdamped oscillating systems.
Explain critically damped systems.
Compare/contrast and differentiate between different types of damping.
Explain resonance.
Describe various characteristics associated with a wave.
Compare/contrast and differentiate between between transverse and longitudinal waves.
Describe and determine the resultant waveform when two waves act in superposition relative to each other.
Explain standing waves.
Describe the mathematical representation of overtones and beat frequency.
Calculate the intensity and the power of rays and waves.

pathways.title: Rotational Motion
pathways.description: This pathway explores the principles of rotational motion, such as rotational intertia, torque and angular velocity. Angular kinematics are explained in a manner analogous to one dimentional kinematics, and the principle of conservation of angular momentum is discussed. Applications of these principles are provided, as well as problems to check for understanding.
learning_objectives: Define the basic terms of angular kinematics.
Calculate angular quatities using the angular kinematic equations.
Define the principle of conservation of angular momentum.
Explain how applying a torque to a rotating object will change its angular velocity.
Discuss applications of rotational motion to everyday phenomena.

pathways.title: Eukaryotic Chromosomes
pathways.description: This pathway provides an overview of the general characteristics of a eukaryotic cell and explores how their genomic content is organized by examining key features of eukaryotic chromosomes.
learning_objectives: Identify the key structural features of a eukaryotic cell.
Describe key features of eukaryotic chromosomes.
Explain the organization of chromatin and the anatomy of a condensed chromosome.

pathways.title: How Does the Distance From the Light Source Affect the Rate of Photosynthesis?
pathways.description: The rate of photosynthesis depends on a plant’s distance from a light source. This effect can be measured by the color change of the pH indicator Bromothymol BTB.
learning_objectives: You will interpret the effect on the rate of photosynthesis of a water plant when it is moved relative to a light source by measuring the pH of the surrounding solution.
You will use the interactive to establish a link between the rate of photosynthesis and oxygen production.

pathways.title: How Does Temperature Affect the Rate of Photosynthesis?
pathways.description: Temperature affects the rate of photosynthesis of a water plant. This can be followed by monitoring the pH surrounding the plant with the help of the pH indicator BTB.
learning_objectives: You will test the effect of temperature on the photosynthesis of a water plant using the color change of the pH indicator BTB.
You will interpret the effect of changing temperature on the rate of the plant’s photosynthesis.
You will use the interactive to establish a link between the rate of photosynthesis and oxygen production.

pathways.title: Particle Physics
pathways.description: This pathway divides elementary particles into fundamental particles as objects that do not have internal structure and composed particles whose properties are defined by their substructures. The magnetic dipole moment, related to the properties of spin (angular momentum) and charge, is an intrinsic property of some fundamental particles such as the electron. This property is the fundamental source of magnetic behavior in matter.
learning_objectives: Recall and state the heisenberg uncertainty principle.
Define yukawa particle.
Describe a pion.
Identify and estimate the mass of a pion.
Explain what a meson is.
Recall and state the four basic forces.
Define qed.
Explain the feynman diagram for the exchange of a virtual photon between two positive charges.
Describe the feynman diagram for the exchange of a photon between a proton and a neutron.
Recall and state the principle of a cyclotron.
Recall and state fermilab's accelerator principle.
Describe the voltage needed by an accelerator between accelerating tubes.
Define matter and antimatter.
Summarize and outline the differences between hadrons and leptons.
Recall and state the differences between mesons and baryons.
Summarize and outline the quark composition of hadrons.
Define fundamental particle.
Describe quark and antiquark.
List flavors of quarks.
Calculate quantum numbers from quark composition.
Recall and state the grand unified theory.
Explain the electroweak theory.
Define gluons.
Describe the principle of quantum chromodynamics.
Define the standard model.

pathways.title: Levels of Organization
pathways.description: This pathway describes the complex, hierarchical system that is the human body and breaks down each level of organization. The human body is looked at from an atomic level to the level of organ systems.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Develop your understanding of how molecular interactions influence the nature of larger bodily structures.
Categorize physiological processes by their level of organization.

pathways.title: Cell Structures and Functions
pathways.description: How do eukaryotic cells work? This pathway explores essential cellular structures and functions that are crucial for life.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate how the structure of an organelle relates to its function.
Categorize organelles based on their structures and functions.
Use a model to describe a function of a cell and how its parts contribute to that function.

pathways.title: Cancer Genetics Research Experience Activity
pathways.description: This cancer genetics research experience provides students with an opportunity to engage in a mock research project and explore a variety of health and science careers. In completing this activity, students will have the opportunity to “join” three different research groups: a clinical research lab, a bioinformatics research lab, and a mouse research lab. Students can keep track of their work using this spreadsheet.
learning_objectives: Define cancer and explain how cancer varies across individual patients
Identify the roles of animal researchers, clinical researchers, and bioinformaticians in clinical cancer research
Learn how to navigate a cancer database to identify patients based on demographic or clinical information
Graph and interpret tumor size data to determine the effectiveness of different cancer treatments
Infer which patients will likely respond to treatments based on genomic data
Discuss the bioethics of cancer research, including patient informed consent and humane practices for animal testing
Describe how targeted therapies can be personalized to yield a precision medicine approach to cancer treatment

pathways.title: Bone Tissue and the Skeletal System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at the functions of the skeletal system; bone classification; bone structure; bone formation & development; fractures: bone repair; exercise, nutrition, hormones, & bone tissue; and calcium homeostasis: interactions of the skeletal system & other organ systems. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: List and describe the functions of bones.
Describe the classes of bones.
Discuss the process of bone formation and development.
Explain how bone repairs itself after a fracture.
Discuss the effect of exercise, nutrition, and hormones on bone tissue.
Describe how an imbalance of calcium can affect bone tissue.

pathways.title: Can Plants Hear? Using the Scientific Method to Explore Plant Senses
pathways.description: This pathway guides the learner from a real-life observation to a current discovery in plant science—can plants "hear"? By applying the scientific method in an intuitive and logical way, anyone—even without any scientific background—can arrive at a conclusion that was published in scientific journals by plant scientists.
learning_objectives: Relate how the scientific method is used to investigate real-world observations to arrive at conclusions.
Describe how the iterative process of the scientific method helps to refine hypotheses to arrive at increasingly accurate explanations of a phenomenon.
Identify how a hypothesis allows you to make predictions that can be tested with experiments.
Explain how the results from testing a first hypothesis can inform a better second hypothesis.
Analyze the results of an experiment to decide whether a hypothesis should be accepted or rejected.

pathways.title: Viral Chromosomes
pathways.description: This pathway highlights key features of viral genomes and how viruses are grouped according to the Baltimore classification system.
learning_objectives: Describe key characteristics and structural features of viruses.
Differentiate between the 7 Baltimore groups of viruses.
Describe the genome organization and subgroups of DNA viruses, RNA viruses, and reverse-transcribing viruses.

pathways.title: Animal Reproduction and Development
pathways.description: Animal reproduction is necessary for the survival of a species. In the animal kingdom, there are innumerable ways that species reproduce. Asexual reproduction produces genetically identical organisms (clones), whereas in sexual reproduction, the genetic material of two individuals combines to produce offspring that are genetically different from their parents. This pathway provides an in-depth look at reproduction methods; fertilization; human reproductive anatomy & gametogenisis; hormonal control of human reproduction; fertilization & early embryonic development; organogenesis & vertebrate formation; and human pregnancy & birth. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Identify the advantages and disadvantages of asexual and sexual reproduction.
Describe examples of methods of asexual reproduction among animals.
Describe are examples of methods of sexual reproduction among animals.
Explain the differences between internal and external methods of fertilization.
Identify examples of methods animals use for development of offspring during gestation.
Identify examples of anatomical adaptations that evolved in animals to facilitate reproduction.
List the features of the human male and female reproductive anatomies.
Name the events associated with the human sexual response?.
Compare/contrast the differences and similarities between spermatogenesis and oogenesis?.
Explain the roles of male and female reproductive hormones.
Recall the link between the ovarian and menstrual cycles.
Name the events associated with menopause.
Describe how fertilization occurs.
Explain how the embryo forms from the zygote.
Describe the roles of cleavage and fertilization in animal development.
List the stages in the process of organogenesis in vertebrate animals.
Explain the anatomical axes in vertebrates and their significance in development.
List the stages of fetal development during the first three trimesters of gestation.
Identify the events in labor and delivery.
List the efficacy and duration of various types of contraception.
Discuss the causes of infertility and the therapeutic options available.

pathways.title: Animal Nutrition and the Digestive System
pathways.description: All living organisms need nutrients to survive. Animals obtain their nutrients by the consumption of other organisms. The conversion of the food consumed to the nutrients required is a multi-step process involving digestion and absorption.This pathway provides an in-depth look at digestive systems, processes and regulation; nutrition and energy production. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Explain the differences between digestion and absorption.
Compare/contrast the different types of digestive systems in invertebrates and vertebrates.
Describe the specialized functions of the organs involved in processing food in the human body.
Explain how organs work together to digest food and absorb nutrients.
Discuss why an animal must have a balanced diet.
List the primary components of food.
List examples of essential nutrients required for cellular function that cannot be synthesized by the animal body.
Explain how energy is produced through diet and digestion.
Describe how excess carbohydrates and energy are stored in the body.
Describe the process of digestion.
List the steps that are involved in digestion and absorption.
Recall what elimination is.
Describe the roles of the small and large intestines in absorption.
Explain how neural regulation is involved in the digestive processes.
Explain how hormones regulate digestion.

pathways.title: Animal Reproduction and Development
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at how animals reproduce, development and organogenesis, and human reproduction. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.

pathways.title: Immune System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at the immune system and explores the processes that happen during the innate and adaptive immune response. It takes a closer look at the key players of the immune system and discusses the importance of antibody specificity in the context of vaccines. Autoimmune disorders, as a result of a misguided immune response, are also covered.
learning_objectives: Explain the difference between the innate and adaptive immune response.
Identify the major players (immune cells) in the immune system.
Describe the importance of antibody specificity for vaccines.
Define what an autoimmune disorder is.

pathways.title: Interpreting Chromatograms
pathways.description: This pathway introduces foundational skills involved in analyzing and critiquing chromatograms. In particular, this pathway focuses on DNA sequencing chromatograms.
learning_objectives: Interpret DNA sequencing and protein purification chromatogram data.
Critique DNA sequencing and protein purification chromatogram data.
Locate common errors when reading DNA sequencing and protein purification chromatogram data.

pathways.title: Science Coloring Pages
pathways.description: We invite children of all ages to create something colorful with these printable coloring pages!

pathways.title: The Future of the Future
pathways.description: As technology alters every aspect of our modern society, so too does it change the ancient art of prediction. Today, advances in artificial technology, simulation, and even human thought have forced thinkers to reckon with a changing conception of what prediction is -- and who we are and will be.

pathways.title: Atoms and Light: Exploring Atomic and Electronic Structure
pathways.description: In this unit, we will follow the gradual change from considering the atom as a single indivisible particle to a later understanding of the atom composed of its constituent subatomic parts, including the electron, the first subatomic particle to be discovered, the proton, and the neutron. It forms part of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.
learning_objectives: Compare/contrast and compare the different models of the atom.
Describe the experiments of the major atomic theorists and the progression of the models of the atom over time.
Discuss the evidence that allowed scientists to infer the internal structure of the atom.

pathways.title: Joining a LabXchange Classroom
pathways.description: This short pathway demonstrates how to register on LabXchange and how to join a class.
learning_objectives: Creating a LabXchange account.
Joining a LabXchange classroom.

pathways.title: How Racism Influences Social and Environmental Determinants of Health
learning_objectives: Recognize how social and environmental factors influence people’s health outcomes.
Discuss the history of residential segregation in the US and its impact on the health outcomes of racially marginalized groups.
Describe how historical and ongoing racism contributes to negative social and environmental determinants of health, which result in racial health disparities.
Examine case studies that reveal how inequalities in social and environmental determinants of health result in racial health disparities in the present day US.

pathways.title: The Behavior of Atoms: Phases of Matter and the Properties of Gases
pathways.description: This unit covers the properties of solids, liquids, and gases in terms of the behavior of invisible particles of matter that interact at the atomic scale and forms part of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.
learning_objectives: Describe phase changes based on kinetic energy.
Discuss pressure, volume, and temperature.
Interpret phase diagrams.

pathways.title: The Autonomic Nervous System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at divisions of the autonomic nervous system, autonomic reflexes & homeostasis, central control, and drugs that affect the autonomic system. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the components of the autonomic nervous system.
Compare/contrast the structures of the sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions in the autonomic nervous system.
Name the components of a visceral reflex specific to the autonomic division to which it belongs.
Predict the response of a target effector to autonomic input on the basis of the released signaling molecule.
Describe how the central nervous system coordinates and contributes to autonomic functions.

pathways.title: When Chemicals Meet Water: The Properties of Solutions
pathways.description: This unit addresses the physical and chemical properties of solutions. Although several topics addressed are common to all solution-phase systems, most of the examples are based specifically on aqueous systems since these are fundamental to life and common in inorganic chemistry. This unit forms part of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.
learning_objectives: Identify the solvent and solute(s) in different types of solutions.
Describe differences in solubility behaviors for solid, liquid, and gas solutes.
Calculate concentrations of solutions and understand the differences between different concentration units.
Discuss how solutions’ physical properties differ from those of pure solvents, and how concentration affects the extent of these differences.

pathways.title: What Is a Gene and a Genome?
pathways.description: This pathway offers an exploration of fundamental genetic concepts, including the transmission of genetic information, Mendel’s laws, and the role of DNA in heredity. Through a combination of simulations, videos, and infographics, students are introduced to topics such as gene linkage, the one gene–one protein hypothesis, and the structure of chromosomes. Interactive reviews and pathway explorations enhance understanding, which makes this collection an essential resource for learning about genetics and genome organization.
learning_objectives: Understand the structure and function of genes and genomes: Learners will be able to define what a gene and a genome are, explain their roles in heredity, and describe how genes are organized within chromosomes.

Explain Mendel's laws and genetic inheritance: Learners will be able to apply Mendel's laws to predict genetic inheritance patterns and explain how genetic information is transmitted from one generation to the next.

Analyze the relationship between genes, proteins, and DNA: Learners will be able to describe the one gene–one protein hypothesis and explain the structural and functional correspondence between genes, proteins, and DNA.

pathways.title: Techniques in Biotechnology
pathways.description: Biological technicians are often responsible for running PCR reactions, culturing cells, and preparing DNA for analysis. Explore the principles and procedures behind genetic engineering, polymerase chain reaction (PCR), DNA transformation, and CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing. Through hands-on simulations, expert-led videos, and interactive visuals, gain both theoretical insight and practical guidance in setting up reactions, handling reagents, maintaining sterile technique, and analyzing results.
learning_objectives: Understand and demonstrate the use of PCR to amplify DNA for analysis and problem-solving.
Utilize cell culture methods to grow and analyze cells in controlled environments.
Use genetic engineering to modify DNA for research, therapy, or product development.

pathways.title: Mathematics: Plane and Simple
pathways.description: Dr Sam Ballas is based at Florida State University in the US. His research focus is on geometry and surfaces, which is a branch of mathematics. Sam’s passion for the subject shows that although maths can be challenging, it can also be extremely rewarding.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you will understand Dr. Sam Ballas' research on geometry and surfaces, including topology and convex projective manifolds.
Identify real-life applications of Sam's work, such as using geometric objects to build expander graphs for computer networks.
Explore key questions in mathematics, like the Riemann hypothesis and the P vs NP problem, and recognize the collaborative nature of mathematical research.

pathways.title: The Chemical Foundation of Life
pathways.description: Some of the most abundant elements in living organisms include carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, and phosphorus. These form the nucleic acids, proteins, carbohydrates, and lipids that are the fundamental components of living matter. Biologists must understand these important building blocks and the unique structures of the atoms that comprise molecules, allowing for cells, tissues, organ systems, and entire organisms to form. All biological processes follow the laws of physics and chemistry, so in order to understand how biological systems work, it is important to understand the underlying physics and chemistry. This pathway provides an in-depth look at atoms, isotopes, ions, and molecules (the building blocks), water, and carbon. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Define matter and elements.
Describe the interrelationship between protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Compare/contrast the ways in which electrons can be donated or shared between atoms.
Explain the ways in which naturally occurring elements combine to create molecules, cells, tissues, organ systems, and organisms.
Describe the properties of water that are critical to maintaining life.
Explain why water is an excellent solvent.
Discuss the role of acids, bases, and buffers in homeostasis.
List and provide examples of water’s cohesive and adhesive properties.
Explain why carbon is important for life.
Describe the role of functional groups in biological molecules.

pathways.title: Data Analysis and Visualization
pathways.description: Histograms are particularly useful for understanding the shape of a data distribution. This pathway builds essential data science and biostatistics skills. Topics include frequency distributions, summary statistics, pH effects on enzyme activity, bacterial growth analysis, and graph interpretation.
learning_objectives: Understand how to organize and visualize data (using appropriate graphs).
Create graphs and learn to analyze data using spreadsheet software.
Analyze data and derive insights.

pathways.title: BLAST for Experienced Users
pathways.description: This pathway prepares learners to use the Basic Local Alignment Search Tool (BLAST) program offered by the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI). This includes a brief discussion of Expect Values in BLAST, and a guide to how to use NCBI's Primer-BLAST online tool.

pathways.title: Maintaining Precision in the Lab
pathways.description: Reliable science starts with accurate instruments. This pathway introduces critical laboratory practices, including instrument calibration, routine maintenance, and basic troubleshooting. Explore how to calibrate common lab instruments for accurate measurements, perform essential maintenance to ensure equipment reliability, and troubleshoot common issues to maintain data accuracy and experimental integrity.
learning_objectives: Calibrate common laboratory instruments for accurate measurements.
Perform key maintenance to ensure instrument reliability.
Troubleshoot issues to maintain accuracy.

pathways.title: Reviewing and Taking Action on What You’ve Learned
pathways.description: This pathway reviews key concepts from FairOpportunityX and shares additional resources for help during the college application process.
learning_objectives: Recall key concepts learned during fairopportunityx.
Examine a timeline of the college application process.
Identify additional resources for support during the college application and financial aid process.

pathways.title: Chemical Evolution
pathways.description: This is the fifth session in the Evolution Flipped Course from iBiology. Scientists have wondered for years how the very first life form may have evolved.  How did the Earth go from an environment of simple molecules to primitive cells? Dr. Szostak presents evidence for the “RNA world” hypothesis and explains how lipids, nucleotides and amino acids could all have formed from molecules in the early atmosphere if energy (for example lightening) was added. Once formed, these molecules could have been enclosed in a membrane to form a primitive, self-replicating protocell. After several billion years of evolution, prokaryotes ruled the Earth. In a short video clip, Newman explains how ancient rock formations provide insights into the critical role that photosynthesizing bacteria played in the evolution of the Earth’s atmosphere and modern life.

pathways.title: Desktop Research Skills
pathways.description: Research and laboratory roles require strong skills in locating, reading, and evaluating peer-reviewed scientific articles. This pathway introduces digital literacy, the anatomy of research papers, strategic reading approaches, PubMed search techniques, and citation management using My NCBI. Gain hands-on experience with scientific databases, journal structure, evidence analysis, and bioinformatics tools, all essential for effective desktop research in biology.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of credible scientific information in the biotechnology field.
Recognize and interpret the structure of scientific research articles.
Read and extract information from scientific literature using strategic reading techniques.
Search effectively for relevant peer-reviewed articles using PubMed.
Manage, organize, and cite scientific research using tools like My NCBI.
Explore gene-level data and associated variation using RefSeqGene and related resources.

pathways.title: Electric Charge and Electric Field
pathways.description: This pathway begins the study of electromagnetic phenomena at a fundamental level. The pathway begins with the study of electric phenomena due to charges that are at least temporarily stationary, called electrostatics, or static electricity. It introduces several very important concepts of charge, electric force, and electric field, as well as defining the relationships between these concepts.
learning_objectives: Define electric charge, and describe how the two types of charge interact.
Describe three common situations that generate static electricity.
Explain the law of conservation of charge.
Define conductor and insulator, explain the difference, and give examples of each.
Describe three methods for charging an object.
Explain what happens to an electric force as you move farther from the source.
Define polarization.
List the three properties of a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium.
Explain the effect of an electric field on free charges in a conductor.
Explain why no electric field may exist inside a conductor.
Describe the electric field surrounding earth.
Explain what happens to an electric field applied to an irregular conductor.
Describe how a lightning rod works.
Explain how a metal car may protect passengers inside from the dangerous electric fields caused by a downed line touching the car.
Explain coulomb's law in terms of how the electrostatic force changes with the distance between two objects.
Calculate the electrostatic force between two point charges, such as electrons or protons.
Compare/contrast the electrostatic force to the gravitational attraction for a proton and an electron; for a human and the earth.
Describe a force field and calculate the strength of an electric field due to a point charge.
Calculate the force exerted on a test charge by an electric field.
Explain the relationship between electrical force (f) on a test charge and electrical field strength (e).
Calculate the total force (magnitude and direction) exerted on a test charge from more than one charge.
Describe an electric field diagram of a positive point charge and of a negative point charge with twice the magnitude of the positive charge.
Illustrate the electric field lines between two points of the same charge and between two points of opposite charge.
Describe how a water molecule is polar.
Explain electrostatic screening by a water molecule within a living cell.
Name several real-world applications of the study of electrostatics.

pathways.title: Cellular Transport
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content delves into the intricacies of plasma membranes and their critical role in maintaining cellular function. In this pathway, learners will examine membrane permeability, mechanisms of transport, and the dynamic processes that regulate the movement of substances across the membrane. By exploring facilitated diffusion, tonicity, osmoregulation, and various transport mechanisms, learners will gain insight into how membranes manage homeostasis. Through the analysis of these processes, students will interpret the interplay between membrane structure and function, and how these interactions support the cellular environment. 
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Explain how the components of the cell membrane, based on the fluid mosaic model, work together to maintain the cell’s internal environment.
Describe how the structure of biological membranes enables selective permeability and regulates molecular movement.
Discuss how the cell wall contributes to maintaining cell structure and function in various organisms.
Explain how organisms maintain solute and water balance, including the mechanisms used for transporting large molecules across the plasma membrane.
Analyze how a molecule’s structure, concentration gradients, and osmoregulatory mechanisms influence its ability to cross membranes and contribute to organism survival.

pathways.title: Protists and the Ecology of Protists
pathways.description: Humans have been familiar with macroscopic organisms (organisms big enough to see with the unaided eye) since before there was a written history, and it is likely that most cultures distinguished between animals and land plants, and most probably included the macroscopic fungi as plants. Therefore, it became an interesting challenge to deal with the world of microorganisms once microscopes were developed a few centuries ago. Many different naming schemes were used over the last couple of centuries, but it has become the most common practice to refer to eukaryotes that are not land plants, animals, or fungi as protists. This pathway provides an in-depth look at eukaryotic origins, characteristics of protists, groups of protists, and the ecology of protists. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: List the unifying characteristics of eukaryotes.
Describe what scientists know about the origins of eukaryotes based on the last common ancestor.
Explain the endosymbiotic theory.
Describe the cell structure characteristics of protists.
Describe the metabolic diversity of protists.
Describe the life cycle diversity of protists.
Describe the role that protists play in the ecosystem.
Describe important pathogenic species of protists.
Describe representative protist organisms from each of the six presently recognized supergroups of eukaryotes.
Identify the evolutionary relationships of plants, animals, and fungi within the six presently recognized supergroups of eukaryotes.
Identify defining features of protists in each of the six supergroups of eukaryotes.

pathways.title: Inside the Cell: Structure, Function, and Organization
pathways.description: Cells are the building blocks of life, containing specialized structures that enable them to function and respond to their environment. This pathway explores cell structure, organelles, protein functions, and cellular interactions. Through engaging videos, interactive simulations, and real-world narratives, you will gain a deeper understanding of how cells are studied, how they maintain life processes, and how they contribute to larger biological systems.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the fundamental structures and functions of cell organelles in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
Explain how proteins contribute to cellular functions, including transport, energy capture, and communication.
Differentiate between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell division.
Analyze how scientists study cells using microscopy and modern techniques.
Explore real-world applications of cell biology in research and medicine.

pathways.title: Marinna Madrid, PhD - Biophysicist
pathways.description: Dr. Marinna Madrid is a lover of Mexican food, a dancer, and a biophysicist who co-invented several laser-based techniques and co-founded Cellino Biotech which combines lasers, machine learning, and stem cell biology to make different types of cells.

pathways.title: Gel Electrophoresis: From Theory to Practice
pathways.description: This pathway introduces gel electrophoresis, a key technique in biotechnology used to separate and analyze DNA, RNA, or proteins. Learners will explore the principles behind gel electrophoresis, including how electric fields are used to move molecules through a gel matrix based on size and charge. The pathway offers interactive modules and hands-on projects that allow students to simulate and apply gel electrophoresis techniques, providing a deeper understanding of molecular biology and its practical applications in research and diagnostics.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain how gel electrophoresis separates biological molecules based on size and charge.
Identify and explain the role of each piece of equipment required to conduct gel electrophoresis.
Know how to build your own gel electrophoresis chamber.
Interpret a gel to determine the relative sizes of molecules separated by gel electrophoresis.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Adaptive Specific Host Defenses
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of overview of specific adaptive immunity; major histocompatibility complexes & antigen-presenting cells; t lymphocytes & cellular immunity; b lymphocytes & humoral immunity; and vaccines.

pathways.title: Climate and Climate Change
pathways.description: How well could you describe what climate is if someone were to ask you? In this pathway, learn the characteristics of different climates and what factors can cause local or global climate change.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: In this pathway, you will learn about the characteristics of different climates and how they lead to specific vegetation patterns.
You will learn why some climates are characterized as extreme.
You will build an ecosystem model to see how modelling helps us understand ecosystems.
You will understand which emissions affect the ozone layer the most.
You will learn how to create a mini ecosystem in a mason jar.
You will discover the techniques scientists use to study ancient climates.
You will see how climate forecasting works and why scientists use it.

pathways.title: What Are Health Disparities and What Causes Them?
pathways.description:
Why do different populations—whether categorized on the basis of race, ethnicity, or class—have different health outcomes? This pathway will introduce you to the concept of “health disparities” and explore which populations are more likely to suffer from health disparities and why. You will also examine how unequal access to positive social and environmental determinants of health can result in health disparities, and how we can seek to address these inequalities.
This pathway forms part of the Racism as a Public Health Crisis cluster. See our equity pledge as part of this initiative. Please note: This pathway contains sensitive content related to racial violence and discrimination.
learning_objectives: Define health disparities.
Explain why a unique set of health disparities—called racial health disparities—is not caused by genetic differences between racial groups.
Describe how environmental and social determinants of health contribute to health disparities.
Demonstrate how negative environmental and social determinants of health contribute to asthma, type 2 diabetes, and COVID-19-related health disparities.
Identify how negative social and environmental determinants of health can be addressed in order to reduce health disparities.

pathways.title: Anatomical Terms: Foundations in Body Orientation and Planes
pathways.description: Explore the body's planes and regions in this pathway! Learn about the directional terms used to describe the body and the importance of using common terms when referencing the human body.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define anatomical terms related to body surfaces, planes, and directions, such as anterior, posterior, superior, inferior, medial, lateral, proximal, and distal.
Identify and describe the major body planes, including sagittal, frontal (coronal), and transverse (horizontal) planes.
Apply knowledge of body orientation and terminology to describe the location and relationships of anatomical structures.
Demonstrate the ability to use anatomical directions and planes to communicate effectively in medical and scientific contexts.
Analyze how understanding body orientation and terminology enhances communication and collaboration in healthcare professions.

pathways.title: Joints
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at classification of joints, fibrous joints, cartilaginous joints, synovial joints, types of body movements, anatomy of selected synovial joints, and development of joints. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Discuss both functional and structural classifications for body joints.
Describe the characteristic features for fibrous, cartilaginous, and synovial joints and give examples of each.
Define and identify the different body movements.
Discuss the structure of specific body joints and the movements allowed by each.
Explain the development of body joints.

pathways.title: What Role Should Race Have in Medicine?
pathways.description:
Should physicians consider a patient’s race when making diagnoses and treatment decisions? In this pathway, we explore this question by looking at two approaches to medical research and practice: racialized medicine and race-conscious medicine.
While racialized medicine views race as a biological factor that can influence people’s health, race-conscious medicine recognizes that the concept of race in humans is something created by our societies, leading to inequalities between groups of people.
By understanding the historical roots of racialized medicine and the consequences its practices have for the health of racially marginalized communities, we can begin to address some of the injustices that still exist in our health-care systems. We can also support race-conscious practices that recognize how systemic racism affects people’s health, and by doing so, work towards achieving health equity.
This pathway forms part of the Racism as a Public Health Crisis cluster. See our equity pledge as part of this initiative. Please note: This pathway contains sensitive content related to racial violence and discrimination.
learning_objectives: Define the practices of racialized medicine and race-conscious medicine.
Describe the historical assumptions about race in humans that underpin racialized medicine.
Analyze examples of racialized medicine that have contributed to racial health disparities.
Demonstrate how race-conscious medicine, as an alternative to racialized medicine, promotes health equity.

pathways.title: Magnetism
pathways.description: This pathway explores the essential idea that both the internal properties of an object and the movement of charged particles can generate a magnetic field, and why all magnetic fields have a north and south pole. The pathway also discusses how magnetic fields exert forces on objects, resulting in the magnetic alignment that makes a compass work. In addition, the pathway explores how we use this principle to weigh the smallest of subatomic particles with precision and contain superheated plasma to facilitate nuclear fusion.
learning_objectives: Define ferromagnet.
Describe the role of magnetic domains in magnetization.
Define ferromagnet.
Describe the role of magnetic domains in magnetization.
Explain the significance of the curie temperature.
Describe the relationship between electricity and magnetism.
Define magnetic field and describe the magnetic field lines of various magnetic fields.
Describe the effects of magnetic fields on moving charges.
Use the right-hand rule 1 to determine the velocity of a charge, the direction of the magnetic field, and the direction of magnetic force on a moving charge.
Calculate the magnetic force on a moving charge.
Describe the effects of a magnetic field on a moving charge.
Calculate the radius of curvature of the path of a charge that is moving in a magnetic field.
Describe the hall effect.
Calculate the hall emf across a current-carrying conductor.
Describe the effects of a magnetic force on a current-carrying conductor.
Calculate the magnetic force on a current-carrying conductor.
Describe how motors and meters work in terms of torque on a current loop..
Calculate the torque on a current-carrying loop in a magnetic field.
Calculate current that produces a magnetic field.
Use the right-hand rule 2 to determine the direction of current or the direction of magnetic field loops.
Describe the effects of the magnetic force between two conductors.
Calculate the force between two parallel conductors.
Describe some applications of magnetism.
Describe how magnetic poles interact with each other.

pathways.title: The Endocrine System
pathways.description: An animal’s endocrine system controls body processes through the production, secretion, and regulation of hormones, which serve as chemical “messengers” functioning in cellular and organ activity and, ultimately, maintaining the body’s homeostasis. The endocrine system plays a role in growth, metabolism, and sexual development. In humans, common endocrine system diseases include thyroid disease and diabetes mellitus. In organisms that undergo metamorphosis, the process is controlled by the endocrine system. This pathway provides an in-depth look at types of hormones; how hormones work; regulation of body processes; regulation of hormone production; endocrine glands. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Discuss the different types of hormones.
Explain the role of hormones in maintaining homeostasis.
Describe how hormones work.
Discuss the role of different types of hormone receptors.
Explain how hormones regulate the excretory system.
Discuss the roles hormones play in the reproductive system.
Describe how hormones regulate metabolism.
Discuss the role of hormones in different diseases.
Explain how hormone production is regulated.
Identify examples of different stimuli that control hormone levels in the body.
Discuss the roles of different glands in the endocrine system.
Explain how different glands work together to maintain homeostasis.

pathways.title: Biotechnology Overview
pathways.description: Biological technicians need a strong foundation in cell biology, gene therapy, and stem cell research to support cutting-edge biomedical work. This pathway introduces essential biotechnology concepts—like CRISPR, pluripotent stem cells, gene augmentation, and tissue engineering—while highlighting real-world applications in genetic disease treatment and regenerative medicine.
learning_objectives: Know how stem cells and cell types like osteoblasts are used in biotechnology research.
Understand how gene therapy and gene editing are used to treat genetic diseases.
Explore real-world applications and career pathways in biotechnology and cell biology.

pathways.title: The Periodic Table and Chemical Bonding
pathways.description: The pathway explores key concepts like periodic trends, ionic and covalent bonding, electronegativity, and molecular structures. This topic equips students with foundational knowledge of chemical properties and interactions, setting the stage for the deeper understanding of chemical reactions and the properties of matter.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the periodic table's purpose and its role in organizing elements.
Recognize the arrangement of the periodic table, focusing on periods and groups.
Describe the fundamental structure of an atom, including protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Analyze how atomic structure influences an element's position within the periodic table.

pathways.title: Reducing Barriers for Students in STEM
learning_objectives: Define stereotype threat, bias, and microaggressions.
Describe how these phenomena impact Black, Hispanic, and Indigenous STEM students.
Reflect on instances of stereotype threat, bias, and microaggressions in STEM classrooms.
Apply strategies to address and reduce the effects of these phenomena in STEM classrooms.

pathways.title: Growth Mindset and Metacognitive Approaches to Teaching
learning_objectives: Recognize fixed and growth mindsets as different perspectives on student intelligence.
Explain how educators’ mindsets shape their teaching approaches and interactions with students.
Understand metacognition and its benefits for STEM learning.
Use strategies that integrate growth mindset and metacognitive practices into STEM lessons.

pathways.title: DNA Exonerations
pathways.description: This lesson sequence centers around a traditional forensics lab, but instead of students using DNA evidence to determine who committed a crime, they examine the innocence of someone who was wrongfully convicted. Students begin the lesson sequence by looking at real data about the criminal justice system to generate questions about the inequities they see around race and mass incarceration. These questions lead students to the case studies of real exonerees that they use to look into the inequities around wrongful convictions.
