Students work together to analyze the post-conviction DNA samples for their case studies and use Short Tandem Repeats (STRs) to help determine the innocence of the subject of their case study. Afterward, students use their new knowledge to advocate for criminal justice reform strategies of their choosing through Student Action Projects.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will analyze real data about the criminal justice system to identify and generate questions about racial inequities and mass incarceration.
Evaluate the case studies of real exonerees to investigate the inequities surrounding wrongful convictions.
Apply forensic techniques, such as analyzing post-conviction DNA samples and using Short Tandem Repeats (STRs), to assess the innocence of the subjects in the case studies.
Advocate for criminal justice reform by developing and presenting strategies through Student Action Projects based on the analysis of wrongful convictions.

pathways.title: Electro Adventurers: Exploring Science through Electrochemistry
pathways.description: Welcome to "Electro Adventurers: Exploring Science through Electrochemistry," an engaging and fun learning journey designed for students eager to dive into the wonders of electrochemistry. This pathway will lead you through the basics of electrochemical principles and their wide-ranging applications, unveiling the power and excitement inherent in this dynamic field. From the tiny reactions powering our batteries to the large-scale processes driving renewable energy technologies, electrochemistry is a cornerstone of modern science and technology.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will gain a comprehensive understanding of oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions and master the techniques for balancing redox equations.
Learn to interpret and utilize the standard reduction potential table for predicting the direction of redox reactions.
Explore the concepts of electrode potentials and cell potentials, understanding their significance in electrochemical reactions.
Delve into the workings of galvanic cells, learning how to calculate and interpret electrode potentials in practical scenarios.
Discover the real-world applications of electrochemistry, including the principles and functioning of batteries, the mechanisms of corrosion, and the processes involved in electrolysis.

pathways.title: Undergrad Crash Course: Research in the Lab
pathways.description: This is a crash course for undergraduate biology students and early career graduate students just getting introduced to research in the lab. It covers the core fundamentals of experimental design: developing hypotheses, tracking variables, setting up proper controls, understanding noise and variation, and understanding replication. For each key concept, students watch a video and then follow it up with a short reading. The videos are brief and engaging, featuring scientists from diverse backgrounds giving explanations, insights, and strategies on each topic. There are no assessments included here; if you’d like to test comprehension through self-reflective prompts, you can access the full course experience on the Science Communication Lab's courses platform. The multimedia content was originally designed by Science Communication Lab as part of a larger course on experimental design called “Let’s Experiment: A Guide for Scientists Working at the Bench.”
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand that being a scientist is an act of self-expression, and that having different perspectives in science is critical to its progress.
You will understand the definition of a hypothesis and its role in answering a scientific question.
You will learn about the different types of variables in an experiment, why they are important, and how to think through them as you plan your experiment.
You will learn what controls are and how they are an essential part of an experiment.
You will learn the sources of variation and how random variation creates “noise” in an experiment.
You will learn about the different types of replication, why they are important, and how they are applied.

pathways.title: Electric Potential and Electric Field
pathways.description: This pathway examines the relationship between voltage and electrical energy and begins to explore some of the many applications of electricity by introducing the concept of electric potential and describing the relationship between electric field and electric potential. The pathway presents the concept of equipotential lines (lines of equal potential) as a way to visualize the electric field. An analogy between the isolines on topographic maps for gravitational field and equipotential lines for the electric field is used to develop a conceptual understanding of equipotential lines. The relationship between the magnitude of an electric field, change in electric potential, and displacement is stated for a uniform field and extended for the more general case using the concept of the “average value” of the electric field.
learning_objectives: Define electric potential and electric potential energy.
Describe the relationship between potential difference and electrical potential energy.
Explain electron volt and its usage in submicroscopic processes.
Identify and determine electric potential energy given potential difference and amount of charge.
Identify and derive an expression for the electric potential and electric field.
Calculate electric field strength given distance and voltage.
Compare/contrast and distinguish between electric potential and electric field.
Explain point charges and express the equation for electric potential of a point charge.
Calculate the electric potential of a point charge given charge and distance.
Describe the relationship between voltage and electric field.
Explain equipotential lines (also called isolines of electric potential) and equipotential surfaces.
Describe the action of grounding an electrical appliance.
Compare/contrast electric field and equipotential lines.
Describe the action of a capacitor and define capacitance.
Explain parallel plate capacitors and their capacitances.
Discuss the process of increasing the capacitance of a capacitor with a dielectric.
Calculate capacitance given charge and voltage.
Identify and derive expressions for total capacitance in series and in parallel.
Identify series and parallel parts in the combination of connection of capacitors.
Calculate the effective capacitance in series and parallel given individual capacitances.
List some uses of capacitors.
Identify and express in equation form the energy stored in a capacitor.
Explain the function of a defibrillator.

pathways.title: Communication Skills
pathways.description: As a scientist, it is vital to be able to clearly communicate your data, findings, arguments and models. This pathway introduces you to best practices of scientific writing and presenting. It also introduces the lab notebook as the means to document the planning, protocols, recorded data, and conclusions of experiments conducted in the lab.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of clear communication in science.
Summarize the structure and function of the lab notebook.
Outline the different ways to communicate science most effectively.

pathways.title: The Evolution of Populations
pathways.description: All life on Earth is related. Evolutionary theory states that humans, beetles, plants, and bacteria all share a common ancestor, but that millions of years of evolution have shaped each of these organisms into the forms we see today. Scientists consider evolution a key concept to understanding life. It is one of the most dominant evolutionary forces. Natural selection acts to promote traits and behaviors that increase an organism’s chances of survival and reproduction, while eliminating those traits and behaviors that are detrimental to the organism. However, natural selection can only, as its name implies, select—it cannot create. This pathway provides an in-depth look at population evolution, population genetics, and adaptive evolution. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Define population genetics and describe how scientists use population genetics in studying population evolution.
Define the hardy-weinberg principle and discuss its importance.
Describe the different types of variation in a population.
Explain why only natural selection can act upon heritable variation.
Describe genetic drift and the bottleneck effect.
Explain how each evolutionary force can influence a population's allele frequencies.
Explain the different ways natural selection can shape populations.
Describe how these different forces can lead to different outcomes in terms of the population variation.

pathways.title: Search for Life
pathways.description: The search for life is revealing important details about our own place in the universe – where we came from, how life came about, and perhaps, where we’re headed. Learn more about the elements involved in the search for life.
learning_objectives: Develop an understanding of what biosignatures are and how to search for them.
Describe the search for life; why we search, where we search, and what we expect to find.

pathways.title: Build an Online Research Community
pathways.description: This pathway highlights some of the features and content types on LabXchange that allow researchers and those interested in research to connect and share their experiences.
learning_objectives: Describe how learners and researchers can share their stories to a broader community on labxchange.
Summarize how to engage or become a mentor on labxchange.
Justify the importance of a digital community in inspiring and supporting the next generation of scientists.

pathways.title: Drug Product
pathways.description: Drug product process development refers to the process by which a drug substance is formulated and stored in a designated container to create a final, marketable product for patient use. This pathway will explore topics related to drug product process development, including types of formulations, formulation stability, the drug product inspection process, routes of administration, and combination drug products.
learning_objectives: Define what a drug product is.
Describe what a drug formulation is.
List examples of the different types of stressors that are involved in the manufacturing of a therapeutic protein and the excipient that is used to counteract each.
Differentiate between the 3 different ways that a drug product can be visually inspected.
Compare and contrast the 3 main parenteral routes of drug administration.
Describe different types of combination drug products.

pathways.title: Operations
pathways.description: Manufacturing and delivering safe and effective medicine to patients around the world. These staff take promising new medicine from R&D and make sure it is safe for and available to patients who need it. They answer safety questions, conduct clinical trials and manufacture the medicine.​
learning_objectives: You will understand that science is a vast career field with many opportunities.
You will discover how advancements made in technology and computer sciences are improving health care.
You will understand how important it is to have a mentor to talk with about your career path.

pathways.title: Human Evolution
pathways.description: This is the eighth session of the Evolution Flipped Course from iBiology. Sarah Tishkoff studies the evolution and migration of human populations. She describes work from her lab looking at genotypic and phenotypic variation among populations speaking different African languages. Her results provide genetic insights into the history of African ethnic groups and African Americans. Tishkoff’s last talk focuses on the importance of natural selection in human evolution. For instance, mutations that are associated with disease in modern African populations may have been advantageous to the same populations at some point in their past.

pathways.title: Five Teaching Ideas for a New Semester
pathways.description: The teaching guides and videos in this pathway will help you get ready for a new semester or school year. Or, you can refer back to these resources whenever you need guidance or inspiration.

pathways.title: Pubmed for Experienced Users
pathways.description: This pathway further explores PubMed, a database developed and maintained by the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) at the U.S. National Library of Medicine (NLM), which contains more than 33 million citations and abstracts of biomedical literature. In this pathway, users will learn tips for using PubMed, how a PubMed search works, how to save PubMed searches, and how to find the latest treatment for a disease or disorder.

pathways.title: Chromatography Essentials
pathways.description: Chromatography is a powerful technique used to separate and study complex biomolecules based on their chemical properties. This pathway introduces four essential chromatography methods used to purify and analyze biomolecules: size exclusion chromatography (SEC), affinity chromatography, hydrophobic interaction chromatography (HIC), and ion exchange chromatography (IEX). Explore how each method separates molecules based on different properties—size, binding specificity, hydrophobicity, and charge—while gaining hands-on experience through interactive simulations and real lab demonstrations. You’ll also build foundational skills in automated protein chromatography using AKTA systems, the industry-standard platform for precise and scalable biomolecule purification.
learning_objectives: Learn about ion exchange chromatography (IEX) for charge-based separation.
Apply size exclusion chromatography (SEC) for biomolecule separation.
Use Hydrophobic Interaction Chromatography (HIC) for protein purification.
Implement affinity chromatography for target molecule capture.

pathways.title: How Can We Read Genetic Codes in a Single Piece of DNA Quickly?
pathways.description: Gene sequencing is an incredibly useful tool for reading the vast amounts of information DNA contains. With a cutting-edge technique called ‘nanopore gene sequencing,’ this can be done using just a single piece of DNA. However, one crucially important part of this process can only be done extremely slowly. Dr Lei Jiang, at Indiana University Bloomington in the USA, aims to solve this challenge, using the latest techniques in computer science.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will deepen your understanding of gene sequencing and base calling processes, and develop your knowledge and skills in neural networks, algorithms, and hardware design.

You will also gain insights into the interdisciplinary nature of cutting-edge research and the importance of collaboration between computer scientists and geneticists in advancing scientific discoveries.
You will engage in critical thinking by analyzing the challenges and limitations of base calling techniques in gene sequencing.

pathways.title: Geology and Our Solar System
pathways.description: Planetary geology and the more familiar Earth-based geology are introduced in this pathway. Learn what the planets in our solar system are made of and which processes carve and mold rock formations.
learning_objectives: In this pathway, you will learn about the factors that cause planets to be different sizes and have different compositions.
You will see some of the largest geologic formations in our solar system.
You will learn about the layers within the Earth.
You will test your knowledge of key geologic processes.
You will understand the rock cycle and the role it plays in nature.
You will learn about how fossils are formed.

pathways.title: The Human Circulatory System
pathways.description: If all your blood vessels were lined up, they would stretch around the Earth two and a half times! The circulatory, digestive, and lymphatic systems work together to transport nutrients, oxygen, and immune cells throughout the body. This pathway explores their structures and functions, including blood flow, metabolism, and immune defense.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify the components of blood and describe their functions, including the role of red and white blood cells, platelets, and plasma in circulation and immunity.
Explain the structure and function of the heart, blood vessels, and the circulatory system, including blood flow through pulmonary and systemic circuits.
Define digestion and describe the processes of mechanical and chemical digestion, listing the organs involved and their roles in nutrient absorption.
Analyze the function of the liver in metabolism, detoxification, digestion, and maintaining blood glucose levels.
Describe the organs of the lymphatic system and their role in fluid balance, immunity, and disease defense.

pathways.title: Wave Optics
pathways.description: This pathway covers the essential ideas relating to wave optics by presenting explanations and examples of many phenomena that can only be explained by the wave aspect of light. The pathway further explores how only waves can exhibit diffraction and interference patterns that we observe in light. It also discusses how superposition causes variations in the resultant wave amplitude. Based on an understanding of diffraction and interference of light, this pathway also explains experimental observations that occur when light passes through an opening or set of openings with dimensions comparable to the wavelength of the light⁠—specifically the effects of double-slit, multiple-slit, and single-slit  openings. Another aspect of light waves discussed in this pathway is polarization, a phenomenon in which light waves all vibrate in a single plane.
learning_objectives: Discuss the wave character of light.
Identify the changes when light enters a medium.
Discuss the propagation of transverse waves.
Discuss huygens’s principle.
Explain the bending of light.
Explain the phenomena of interference.
Define constructive interference for a double slit and destructive interference for a double slit.
Discuss the pattern obtained from diffraction grating.
Explain diffraction grating effects.
Discuss the single slit diffraction pattern.
Discuss the rayleigh criterion.
Discuss the rainbow formation by thin films.
Discuss the meaning of polarization.
Discuss the property of optical activity of certain materials.
Discuss the different types of microscopes.

pathways.title: Enzymes
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores the foundational principles of enzymes and their critical role in facilitating biochemical reactions. In this pathway, learners will investigate enzyme structure, the specificity of active sites, and the role of enzyme-substrate interactions in catalysis. By analyzing environmental factors such as temperature, pH, and substrate concentration, students will gain an understanding of how enzymes function under varying conditions and the consequences of these changes on reaction rates. Through exploration, learners will interpret how enzymes drive biological processes that are essential for maintaining life.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Explain the role of energy in sustaining life in organisms.
Analyze the photosynthetic processes that enable organisms to capture and store energy.
Describe how cells convert light energy into chemical energy stored in biological molecules.
Explain how organisms utilize energy stored in macromolecules to drive cellular activities.
Interpret how differences in the number and types of cellular molecules impact an organism’s ability to survive and reproduce in various environments.

pathways.title: Electrostatics, Electric Fields, and Coulomb's Law
pathways.description: This pathway explores the fundamental force of electrostatics, which is described by Coulomb's law. The concept of an electric field is introduced as the force per unit charge. Problems are provided to check for understanding.
learning_objectives: Explain that like charges repel and opposite charges attract.
Calculate the strength of the forces between charges using coulomb's law.
Define the term electric field.
Diagram the electric field that exists around charges.
Calculate the strength of an electric field around a charge.

pathways.title: Cell Cycle and Cancer
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores the regulation and progression of the cell cycle, a tightly controlled series of events that allow cells to grow, divide, and reproduce. In this pathway, learners will examine the sequential stages of the cell cycle—interphase (G1, S, G2), mitosis, and cytokinesis—as well as the G0 phase, where cells can exit the cycle temporarily or permanently. Learners will discover how mitosis ensures the accurate transmission of chromosomes from a parent cell to 2 genetically identical cells, supporting growth, tissue repair, and asexual reproduction. They will also explore the role of internal checkpoints and the interactions between cyclins and cyclin-dependent kinases in regulating cell cycle progression. Finally, learners will investigate how disruptions to the cell cycle can lead to apoptosis or diseases such as cancer. 
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe how mitosis ensures the accurate transmission of chromosomes to resulting cells during cell division.
Describe the stages of the cell cycle and explain the role of checkpoints in regulating cell cycle progression.
Explain how disruptions to the cell cycle can affect cellular function and impact organismal health.

pathways.title: The Mystery of Pink Lemonade
pathways.description:
Ready to step into the amazing world of pH, pH scale, and indicators? In this pathway, you'll get to explore how acidity and basicity are part of our everyday lives with the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (290-320 minutes). You'll get to do fun experiments, testing stuff from your home to see their pH using pH indicators. So, let's get started and uncover these exciting scientific mysteries together!



Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 30 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 60 minutes, items 3-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 60 minutes, items 5-6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 60 minutes, item 7-8 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 50 minutes, item 9 of the pathway.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify if something is an acid, a base or neutral.
You will learn how to use color-changing pH indicators to find out the pH of different liquids around the house.
You will be able to investigate the relationship between pH and the concentration of hydrogen ions (H+) in a solution, recognizing that high H+ ion concentration indicates an acidic solution.

pathways.title: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores how organisms use feedback mechanisms to regulate internal processes and respond to environmental changes. Learners are introduced to both positive and negative feedback mechanisms and examine examples of each. They will discover how negative feedback helps maintain homeostasis by returning systems to a set point, while positive feedback amplifies responses and drives processes further from equilibrium. Learners will also analyze examples of feedback mechanisms such as blood glucose regulation and wound healing. 
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Explain the mechanisms of positive and negative feedback in biological systems.
Describe how negative feedback maintains homeostasis.
Describe how positive feedback impacts homeostasis.

pathways.title: NASA | The History of Martian Exploration
pathways.description: This pathway provides information on each of the past Mars missions, including missions still currently in operation.
learning_objectives: List the past mars missions.
Identify current mars missions.

pathways.title: The Circulatory System
pathways.description: This pathway uses videos to explain the physiology of the circulatory system from the flow of blood through the heart to cardiovascular-related diseases.

pathways.title: Simple Harmonic Motion
pathways.description: This pathway explores the concept of simple harmonic motion. The pendulum and spring are used as foundational examples that are used to develop more complex models. Advanced application such as resonance are discussed.
learning_objectives: Define the term simple hamonic motion.
Discuss how the physical properties of a system affect its periodic motion.
Calculate the properties of simple harmoic oscillators.
Explain how simple harmic motion arises as a result of a restoring force.
Discuss forced harmoic motion and resonance.

pathways.title: The Endocrine System
pathways.description: An animal’s endocrine system controls body processes through the production, secretion, and regulation of hormones, which serve as chemical “messengers” functioning in cellular and organ activity and, ultimately, maintaining the body’s homeostasis. The endocrine system plays a role in growth, metabolism, and sexual development. In humans, common endocrine system diseases include thyroid disease and diabetes mellitus. In organisms that undergo metamorphosis, the process is controlled by the endocrine system. This pathway provides an in-depth look at types of hormones, how hormones work, regulation of body processes, regulation of hormone production, and endocrine glands. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: List the different types of hormones.
Explain their role in maintaining homeostasis.
Explain how hormones work.
Discuss the role of different types of hormone receptors.
Explain how hormones regulate the excretory system.
Discuss the role of hormones in the reproductive system.
Describe how hormones regulate metabolism.
Explain the role of hormones in different diseases.
Explain how hormone production is regulated.
Discuss the different stimuli that control hormone levels in the body.
Describe the role of different glands in the endocrine system.
Explain how the different glands work together to maintain homeostasis.

pathways.title: DNA, RNA, and Proteins
pathways.description: How do the instructions coded for in DNA lead to our physical and functional traits? This learning sequence covers the central dogma of biology, including the processes of transcription and translation.
learning_objectives: Summarize the central dogma of biology.
Describe the processes of transcription and translation.
Use a codon chart to determine an amino acid sequence.
Explain how a protein's structure affects its function.

pathways.title: Protein Synthesis
pathways.description: Proteins are synthesized through transcription and translation, but misfolding can lead to diseases. In this pathway, you explore how proteasomes degrade misfolded proteins, preventing harmful aggregates and neurodegenerative disorders.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the structure and role of DNA in genetic coding.
Illustrate the processes of transcription and translation.
Describe the role of protein folding and degradation in cellular function.
Demonstrate the process of protein purification using chromatography.
Outline the steps of polymerase chain reaction (PCR).
Explain the principles and applications of PCR.
Analyze DNA fragments using gel electrophoresis.

pathways.title: Making Molecules: Lewis Structures and Molecular Geometries
pathways.description: Understanding how atoms combine to make molecules allows scientists to predict many of the physical and chemical properties of substances. This pathway forms part of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Predict the lewis dot structure for covalent or ionic molecules.
Identify the geometry of molecules.
Describe a compound’s physical properties, such as boiling point and solubility, based on intermolecular forces.

pathways.title: Mammalian Dive Response
pathways.description: Enhance students' understanding of biological concepts such as cellular respiration, cell biology, animal physiology and adaptations. During this activity, students analyze how their heart rate changes as they hold their breath underwater to model the mammalian dive response.
learning_objectives: Interpret data.

pathways.title: Mars Rocks: The Geology of Mars
pathways.description: This pathway provides resources to understand the key features of the geology of Mars.
learning_objectives: Summarize the key geological features of mars.
Compare/contrast the carbon content of mars, earth and venus.
Examine a space geologist's path to this new stem career.
Explain some of the contemporary evidence for water on mars.

pathways.title: Anatomy & Physiology: Tissues
pathways.description: Embark on an exciting expedition in this collection of videos to discover the hidden heroes of our bodies—connective tissues! Imagine a world inside you that's like a bustling city, with different materials and workers all coming together to keep everything running smoothly. Just like cities have various building materials like steel, wood, and glass, our bodies have an amazing variety of connective tissues, each with a special role to play.
learning_objectives: In this pathway you will discover how cells join forces to create tissues and help muscles and nerves keep us balanced and active.
Identify tissues that resemble skin, explore their roles, and learn how they protect and function differently.
Understand connective tissue, its purpose, and how disorders like Marfan Syndrome impact its function and health.
Learn to sort various connective tissues, explain their roles and where they're found in bodies and food.

pathways.title: Sets & Venn Diagrams
pathways.description: This pathway will help learners understand the concept of sets and how to use Venn diagrams when trying to calculate the degree of overlap between sets.
learning_objectives: Develop an understanding of how and when to use venn diagrams.
Calculate the amount of overlap between sets.
Use activities to assess your understanding of venn diagrams and associated calculations.

pathways.title: The Central Dogma of Molecular Biology
pathways.description:
This AP Biology pathway explores how genetic information is stored, copied, and expressed to determine the traits of an organism. Learners will investigate the structures responsible for passing hereditary information from one generation to the next, including DNA and RNA. They will explore the unique properties of DNA and  mechanisms of DNA replication. Learners will also follow the flow of genetic information from DNA to RNA to protein, analyzing transcription, RNA processing, and translation, and understanding how these processes link an organism’s genotype to its phenotype.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Identify the structures involved in passing hereditary information from one generation to the next.
Explain the characteristics of DNA that allow it to be used as the hereditary material.
Explain the processes by which genetic information is accurately copied and expressed, including the flow from DNA to RNA to protein.
Analyze how an organism’s genotype determines its phenotype.

pathways.title: Origins of Life
pathways.description: Jack Szostak discusses the origin of life on earth and the components of a simple protocell. This is the first session of the Cell Biology Flipped Course from iBiology.

pathways.title: Fire Forensics: The Investigators Academy
pathways.description:
This pathway is designed to provide students with the understanding of fire, fire dynamics, and fire behavior so that they can read a fire scene and build a claim for the fire’s location of origin and cause. As fire burns, it leaves clues in its path — evidence visible only to trained fire investigators. With an understanding of fire science, fire investigators hunt for clues, collect evidence and report on what happened.
Join us at UL’s Fire Lab to work side by side with fire scientists through a series of videos and investigations. Students learn how fire is defined, how it develops, and how the evidence left behind suggests how and where it started. Students also practice using claims, evidence, and commentary to build an argument.
This pathway can be used in the classroom or virtually, and as a self-guided student inquiry or a series of classroom activities. Comprehensive student and teacher guides are included.
learning_objectives: Examine the science of fire dynamics and behavior.
Develop claims, evidence and reasoning skills.

pathways.title: How to Build a Mars Rover
pathways.description: This pathway explores some of the incredible engineering that has gone into building the Mars Perseverance rover, and how they enable it to search for signs of life.
learning_objectives: Identify the various systems of the perseverance rover.

pathways.title: Feeding Relationship
pathways.description: Ecosystems are dynamic networks of interactions, vital for sustaining biodiversity. Food chains and food webs illustrate these interactions, while human impacts like overhunting and climate change disrupt balance. Understanding trophic levels, nutrient cycles, and ecosystem services is essential for evaluating ecological resilience and promoting sustainable development in regions like the South African savanna and Gambian biomes.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Identify the feeding stages in a food chain.
Identify the producer, secondary and tertiary consumers.
Define a food chain and food web.

pathways.title: Plant Form and Physiology
pathways.description: Plants are as essential to human existence as land, water, and air. Without plants, our day-to-day lives would be impossible because without oxygen from photosynthesis, aerobic life cannot be sustained. From providing food and shelter to serving as a source of medicines, oils, perfumes, and industrial products, plants provide humans with numerous valuable resources. This pathway provides an in-depth look at the plant body, stems, roots, leaves, the transport of water & solutes in plants, and plant sensory systems & responses. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the shoot organ system and the root organ system.
Identify and describe the three regions where plant growth occurs.
Summarize the roles of dermal tissue, vascular tissue, and ground tissue.
Compare/contrast simple plant tissue with complex plant tissue.
Identify and distinguish between meristematic tissue and permanent tissue.
Describe the main function and basic structure of stems.
Compare/contrast the roles of dermal tissue, vascular tissue, and ground tissue.
Identify and distinguish between primary growth and secondary growth in stems.
Summarize the origin of annual rings.
List and describe examples of modified stems.
Identify the two types of root systems.
Describe the three zones of the root tip and summarize the role of each zone in root growth.
Describe the structure of the root.
List and describe examples of modified roots the roots of seed plants have three major functions: anch.
Identify the parts of a typical leaf.
Describe the internal structure and function of a leaf.
Compare/contrast and contrast simple leaves and compound leaves.
List and describe examples of modified leaves.
Define water potential and explain how it is influenced by solutes, pressure, gravity, and the matric potential.
Describe how water potential, evapotranspiration, and stomatal regulation influence how water is transported in plants.
Explain how photosynthates are transported in plants.
Describe how red and blue light affect plant growth and metabolic activities.
Discuss gravitropism.
Interpret how hormones affect plant growth and development.
Describe thigmotropism, thigmonastism, and thigmogenesis.
Explain how plants defend themselves from predators and respond to wounds.

pathways.title: Animal Reproduction and Development
pathways.description: Animal reproduction is necessary for the survival of a species. In the animal kingdom, there are innumerable ways that species reproduce. Asexual reproduction produces genetically identical organisms (clones), whereas in sexual reproduction, the genetic material of two individuals combines to produce offspring that are genetically different from their parents. This pathway provides an in-depth look at reproduction methods, fertilization, human reproductive anatomy and gametogenesis, hormonal control of human reproduction, human pregnancy and birth, fertilization and early embryonic development, and organogenesis and vertebrate formation. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe advantages and disadvantages of asexual and sexual reproduction.
Discuss asexual reproduction methods.
Discuss sexual reproduction methods.
Discuss internal and external methods of fertilization.
Describe the methods used by animals for development of offspring during gestation.
Describe the anatomical adaptions that occurred in animals to facilitate reproduction.
Describe human male and female reproductive anatomies.
Discuss the human sexual response.
Describe spermatogenesis and oogenesis and discuss their differences and similarities.
Describe the roles of male and female reproductive hormones.
Discuss the interplay of the ovarian and menstrual cycles.
Describe the process of menopause.
Explain fetal development during the three trimesters of gestation.
Describe labor and delivery.
Compare/contrast the efficacy and duration of various types of contraception.
Discuss causes of infertility and the therapeutic options available.
Discuss how fertilization occurs.
Explain how the embryo forms from the zygote.
Discuss the role of cleavage and gastrulation in animal development.

pathways.title: Animal Nutrition and the Digestive System
pathways.description: All living organisms need nutrients to survive. While plants can obtain the molecules required for cellular function through the process of photosynthesis, most animals obtain their nutrients by the consumption of other organisms. At the cellular level, the biological molecules necessary for animal function are amino acids, lipid molecules, nucleotides, and simple sugars. However, the food consumed consists of protein, fat, and complex carbohydrates. Animals must convert these macromolecules into the simple molecules required for maintaining cellular functions, such as assembling new molecules, cells, and tissues. This pathway provides an in-depth look at digestive systems, nutrition and energy production, digestive system processes, and digestive system regulation. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Explain the processes of digestion and absorption.
Compare/contrast different types of digestive systems.
Explain the specialized functions of the organs involved in processing food in the body.
Describe the ways in which organs work together to digest food and absorb nutrients.
Explain why an animal’s diet should be balanced and meet the needs of the body.
Define the primary components of food.
Describe the essential nutrients required for cellular function that cannot be synthesized by the animal body.
Explain how energy is produced through diet and digestion.
Describe how excess carbohydrates and energy are stored in the body.
Describe the process of digestion.
Identify the steps involved in digestion and absorption.
Define elimination.
Explain the role of both the small and large intestines in absorption.
Discuss the role of neural regulation in digestive processes.
Explain how hormones regulate digestion.

pathways.title: Basic Water Facts
pathways.description: Water is everywhere, from huge oceans to invisible water molecules making up water vapor in the air. But how much water is there on, in, above, and below the Earth, and where is it located?
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Gain an overview of the physical properties of water
Develop an understanding of the distribution of water in the water cycle
Learn the definitions of terms linked to water and the water cycle.

pathways.title: Synthetic Biology
pathways.description: Long term space exploration requires the production of valuable nutrients and resources in space. This pathway discusses how synthetic biology techniques are being used to produce these resources as well as how genetically engineered organisms can help researchers design more complex and sensitive experiments for spaceflight.
learning_objectives: Examine synthetic biology techniques that aim to produce nutrients for space travel.
Identify the importance of generating nutrients in space.

pathways.title: Cellular Metabolism
pathways.description:
How do cells get the energy to do work?
Energy cannot be created or destroyed, but it can change forms. Cells can only use energy in particular forms, so they need to have methods of transforming the energy that’s available in their environment into a form that they can use. Two primary processes, photosynthesis and cellular respiration, drive the cycling of matter and the flow of energy in cells.
learning_objectives: Identify the kinds of energy that are used in cells.
Compare and contrast the structures and functions of the chloroplast and the mitochondria.
Model the role of photosynthesis in the cycling of matter and flow of energy into and out of organisms.

pathways.title: Quantifying Chemical Reactions: Stoichiometry and Moles
pathways.description: In this unit, we will explore atomic mass, the limiting reactant (the first reactant to be used up), and the yield of a reaction. This pathway forms part of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Explain the difference between empirical and molecular formulas and understand how they are determined experimentally.
Calculate chemical quantities from measurable quantities using mole ratios.
Explain how limiting reagents and incomplete reactions influences reaction yields.

pathways.title: Space Biology Introduction
pathways.description: Space biology focuses on investigating how spaceflight affects living organisms. This pathway gives an overview of the space biology research happening at NASA.
learning_objectives: Develop an understanding of the objectives of space biology research.

pathways.title: Ryoji Amamoto, PhD - Neurobiologist
pathways.description: Dr. Ryoji Amamoto is a born adventurer, an avid sports fan, and is studying how brains grow and regenerate, while also working to bridge the gap and improve inequities in science.

pathways.title: Osmotic Regulation and Excretion
pathways.description: The daily intake recommendation for human water consumption is eight to ten glasses of water. In order to achieve a healthy balance, the human body should excrete the eight to ten glasses of water every day. This occurs via the processes of urination, defecation, sweating and, to a small extent, respiration. The organs and tissues of the human body are soaked in fluids that are maintained at constant temperature, pH, and solute concentration, all crucial elements of homeostasis. The solutes in body fluids are mainly mineral salts and sugars, and osmotic regulation is the process by which the mineral salts and water are kept in balance. This pathway provides an in-depth look at osmoregulation & osmotic balance, the kidneys & osmoregulatory organs, excretion systems, nitrogenous wastes, and hormonal control of osmoregulatory functions. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Define osmosis and explain its role within molecules.
Explain why osmoregulation and osmotic balance are important body functions.
Describe active transport mechanisms.
Explain osmolarity and the way in which it is measured.
Describe osmoregulators or osmoconformers and how these tools allow animals to adapt to different environments.
Explain how the kidneys serve as the main osmoregulatory organs in mammalian systems.
Describe the structure of the kidneys and the functions of the parts of the kidney.
Describe how the nephron is the functional unit of the kidney and explain how it actively filters blood and generates urine.
Discuss the three steps in the formation of urine: glomerular filtration, tubular reabsorption, and tubular secretion.
Explain how vacuoles, present in microorganisms, work to excrete waste.
Describe the way in which flame cells and nephridia in worms perform excretory functions and maintain osmotic balance.
Explain how insects use malpighian tubules to excrete wastes and maintain osmotic balance.
Compare/contrast the way in which aquatic animals and terrestrial animals can eliminate toxic ammonia from their systems.
Compare/contrast the major byproduct of ammonia metabolism in vertebrate animals to that of birds, insects, and reptiles.
Explain how hormonal cues help the kidneys synchronize the osmotic needs of the body.

Describe how hormones like epinephrine, norepinephrine, renin-angiotensin, aldosterone, anti-diuretic hormone, and atrial natriuretic peptide help regulate waste elimination, maintain correct osmolarity, and perform other osmoregulatory functions.

pathways.title: Introduction to Micropipettes
pathways.description: Accurately measuring and transferring a desired volume of liquid is an important skill in a laboratory. This pathway introduces the essential tools and techniques for using micropipettes effectively. Through video demonstrations, illustrations, interactive simulations, and expert tips, you will become proficient in selecting the correct pipette size, setting volumes accurately, attaching and ejecting tips safely, and transferring liquids with care.
learning_objectives: Identify the components of a micropipette.
Select the appropriate micropipette based on volume requirements.
Adjust and verify pipette settings for specific measurements.
Attach and eject pipette tips using sterile techniques.
Operate pipettes using first and second stops for accurate aspiration and dispensing.
Transfer liquid from tubes and wells without disturbing samples.
Use a multichannel pipette to load a 96-well plate with consistency.

pathways.title: Seedless Plants
pathways.description: Mosses may grow on a tree trunk, and horsetails may display their jointed stems and spindly leaves across the forest floor. Today, seedless plants represent only a small fraction of the plants in our environment; yet, 300 million years ago, seedless plants dominated the landscape and grew in the enormous swampy forests of the Carboniferous period. Their decomposition created large deposits of coal that we mine today. This pathway provides an in-depth look at early plant life, green algae (precursors of land plants), bryophytes, and seedless vascular plants. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Discuss the challenges to plant life on land.
Describe he adaptations that allowed plants to colonize the land.
Describe the timeline of plant evolution and the impact of land plants on other living things.
Describe the traits shared by green algae and land plants.
Explain why charophytes are considered the closest algal relative to land plants.
Explain how current phylogenetic relationships are reshaped by comparative analysis of dna sequences.
Identify the main characteristics of bryophytes.
Describe the distinguishing traits of liverworts, hornworts, and mosses.
Chart the development of land adaptations in the bryophytes.
Describe the events in the bryophyte lifecycle.
Identify the new traits that first appear in seedless tracheophytes.
Discuss how each trait is important for adaptation to life on land.
Identify the classes of seedless tracheophytes.
Describe the life cycle of a fern.
Explain the role of seedless plants in the ecosystem.

pathways.title: Cell Cycle
pathways.description: This pathway provides an overview of the cell-cycle control system and the role that cyclin-dependent kinases play in initiating cell cylce events. This pathway is the ninth session of the Cell Biology Flipped Course from iBiology.

pathways.title: From Genotype to Phenotype
pathways.description: This pathway explores the connection between genotype and phenotype in the context of the flow of information within a cell from genes to proteins.
learning_objectives: Explain the relationship between genotype and phenotype.
Describe the flow of information in a cell from genes to proteins.
Provide examples of how allelic variation can give rise to phenotypic variation.

pathways.title: Therapeutic Monoclonal Antibodies
pathways.description: Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) are a class of biologics that can be used to treat different diseases. These proteins work by binding to a specific target in the body, such as an antigen on the surface of a disease cell. This pathway will explore what biologics and mAbs are, review the 4 different classes of laboratory-made mAbs, give examples of their different therapeutic uses, examine their structure, and explain how mAbs can work as therapeutic proteins.
learning_objectives: Define what a biologic is.
Describe what a monoclonal antibody (mAb) is.
Compare the 4 different classes of laboratory-made mAbs.
List examples of different uses for therapeutic mAbs.
Describe the main structural features of a mAb.
Describe the mechanism by which a disulfide bond is formed.
Explain how a mAb can work as a therapeutic protein.

pathways.title: Plasmids 101
pathways.description: Are you fascinated by the world of molecular biology and genetic engineering? Then you will certainly be intrigued by the role of plasmids in these fields. So, join us in this exciting journey of discovering plasmids and their potential applications in the field of molecular biology!
learning_objectives: In this pathway you will learn all about the origins of plasmids, their structure, and how they are used in molecular biology labs.
You will learn about the origin of replication in plasmids and its importance for replication.

pathways.title: David Kelley, PhD - Computational Biologist
pathways.description: Dr. David Kelley is a computational biologist who uses machine learning to train computers to analyze large datasets. He is a trained geneticist and computer scientist who understands how the human genome works and how to write code to ask questions about it.

pathways.title: Color and Spectrum of the pH Indicator BTB
pathways.description: A solution of the pH indicator bromothymol blue (BTB) changes color according to pH. Shining white light through a BTB solution reveals a spectrum that changes when the pH changes.
learning_objectives: You will interpret how the color of the pH indicator BTB changes with pH.
You will use an interactive to explain how the spectrum of BTB changes with pH.

pathways.title: Sensory Systems
pathways.description: All bilaterally symmetric animals have a sensory system, the development of which has been driven by natural selection. Thus, sensory systems differ among species according to the demands of their environments. Animals’ senses are constantly at work, making them aware of stimuli, such as light, or sound, or the presence of a chemical substance in the external environment. They also monitor information about the organism’s internal environment. The content in this chapter is not within the scope of AP® other than to recognize the link between the sensory organs and the nervous system. The ability to detect and respond to information is critical to an organism’s survival and fitness. This pathway provides an in-depth look at sensory processes; somatosensation; taste & smell; and hearing & vestibular sensation. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Describe the general and special senses in humans.
Identify three important steps in sensory perception.
Explain the concept of just-noticeable difference in sensory perception.
Illustrate four important mechanoreceptors in human skin.
Describe the topographical distribution of somatosensory receptors between glabrous and hairy skin.
Explain why the perception of pain is subjective.
Discuss how smell and taste stimuli differ from other sensory stimuli.
Identify the five primary tastes that can be distinguished by humans.
Explain in anatomical terms why a dog’s sense of smell is more acute than a human’s.
Illustrate the relationship of amplitude and frequency of a sound wave to the attributes of sound.
Describe the path that sound travels within the auditory system to the site of transduction of sound.
Identify the structures of the vestibular system that respond to gravity.
Explain how electromagnetic waves differ from sound waves.
Describe the path that light takes as it travels through the eye to the point of the optic nerve.
Discuss tonic activity as it is manifested in photoreceptors in the retina.

pathways.title: Matter and Energy Flow in Organisms
pathways.description:
Explore how molecules and energy sustain life with this 7-day (270-305 minutes) pathway using images, texts, videos, simulations, and a student guide. Model simple molecules, understand the role of macromolecules and water, and analyze how plants and consumers obtain matter and energy in food webs.


Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 30-40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 90 minutes, items 3-5 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4-5, 90 minutes, item 6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 6, 40-50 minutes, items 7-9 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 7, 20-35 minutes, found in the Student Guide.
learning_objectives: At the end of this pathway you will, be able to model/draw simple molecules based on atomic properties.
Understand how macromolecules and water are essential for sustaining living organisms.
Model how plants and consumers obtain matter and energy in food webs.

pathways.title: The Somatic Nervous System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at sensory perception, central processing, and motor responses. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the components of the somatic nervous system.
Name the modalities and submodalities of the sensory systems.
Compare/contrast general and special senses.
Describe regions of the central nervous system that contribute to somatic functions.
Explain the stimulus-response motor pathway.

pathways.title: How Can Targeted Antibodies and Vaccines Be Used to Treat Cancer?
pathways.description: Causing nearly one in six deaths worldwide in 2020, cancer is one of the biggest health challenges of our time. Advancements in technology have led to improved treatments, one of which is using the body’s immune system. Dr Zachary Hartman of Duke University’s School of Medicine in the US is researching how to improve this approach and whether it has the potential to treat a variety of cancers.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify and recall the key components of the immune system that are involved in fighting cancer, including T-cells and immune checkpoints.
Explain the principle of how immunotherapy works to treat cancer, including the role of T-cells and how they are activated or enhanced in this treatment approach.

Apply knowledge of immunotherapy to distinguish between different types of cancer treatments, such as comparing traditional chemotherapy to targeted immune checkpoint inhibitors, and discuss the potential benefits and limitations of each.

pathways.title: Research and Development
pathways.description: Research and development covers all the science involved from discovering a pathway to testing a potential medicine in humans. These staff conduct experiments and analyze data to discover the biology behind a disease and ways to change a person’s biology in order to stop or slow a disease. ​
learning_objectives: You will discover that not all career paths are linear or traditional.
You will understand that it is important to build relationships with your colleagues.
You will understand how important experience is for your career progression.

pathways.title: How Can I Afford College?
pathways.description: This pathway reviews how to estimate the cost of college and make college affordable.
learning_objectives: Identify misconceptions about the cost of college.
Define strategies to make college affordable.
Calculate the estimated cost of college.

pathways.title: Combining Content on LabXchange for Guided Learning
pathways.description:
This pathway will show you how to use your dashboard to add or create combinations of content to create a guided learning experience.
For comprehensive answers to all your LabXchange questions, visit the LabXchange Help Center.
learning_objectives: Describe how to create a customized learning journey called a pathway.
Develop an understanding of how to create an online teaching guide to scaffold learning and activities.

pathways.title: Climate Change Projections
pathways.description: This pathway explains and demonstrates the use of climate models to predict the changes in various climatic patterns.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Examine the various applications of climate models.
Develop an understanding of what goes into model production and their levels of complexity.

pathways.title: Solution Dilution for Lab Work
pathways.description: Accurate solution preparation is a fundamental skill for any scientist working in a laboratory. Whether extracting plant metabolites or preparing reagents for physiological experiments, the ability to dilute stock solutions with precision ensures reliable and reproducible results. Explore the principles and practices of solution dilution, including the use of stock and working solutions, calculating molarity and molality, applying serial dilution techniques, understanding saturation and solubility limits, and filtering solutions for experimental use.
learning_objectives: Describe the key components of a solution, including the roles of solute and solvent.
Explain the difference between molarity and molality.
Calculate the amount of solute required to prepare a solution of a given concentration.
Prepare stock solutions and use them to create working solutions through accurate dilution methods.
Perform serial dilutions to achieve a wide range of concentrations from a single stock solution.

pathways.title: The Sensory System
pathways.description: This pathway uses videos to explain the visual and auditory sensory systems.

pathways.title: Practical Advice
pathways.description: This pathway gives you practical advice about how to find research experience to prepare you for a career in STEM.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will be able to outline how to find your research interest.
Outline how to get research experience.
Understand what online research platforms and citizen science are.
List careers in STEM.

pathways.title: Corporate Functions
pathways.description: In this pathway, you will learn more about how corporate functions provide company-wide services that support internal business practices.
learning_objectives: You will understand how important it is to develop your public speaking skills.
You will understand how important it is to build relationships with your community and colleagues.

pathways.title: Rewriting Life: CRISPR Base Editing
pathways.description:
The CRISPR-Cas9 technology, and the subsequent research-based innovations stemming from its discovery, integrate chemistry and evolution to program and re-program biology. David Liu’s lab has taken the basics of CRISPR and innovated the technology from cutting DNA to actually chemically swapping its component base pairs.
This pathway includes video and curriculum materials to support student learning, featuring the Chemistry Shorts film, "Rewriting Life."
learning_objectives: You will learn about CRISPR-Cas9 technology, and the subsequent research-based innovations stemming from its discovery, integrate chemistry and evolution to program and re-program biology, by watching REWRITING LIFE.
You will be investigating DNA and looking at how spelling mistakes, and editing tools, work with these instructions to enable or disable healthy development and functions within organisms.
You will investigate the molecular composition of DNA, by assembling DNA strands and measuring the molecules.
You will build and edit double stranded DNA sequences for genetic diseases.

pathways.title: Building Bones Using Stem Cells and Gene Therapy
pathways.description: Dr Pascale V. Guillot is based at the University College London Elizabeth Garrett Anderson Institute for Women’s Health. She is leading a team of cell biologists who are using human stem cells to grow both healthy and brittle bone tissue in the lab. Why? So they can investigate the causes of a brittle bone disease called osteogenesis imperfecta and develop innovative treatments to make bones stronger.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway students will be able to analyze the potential applications of advanced techniques in cell biology and evaluate their impact on healthcare and scientific research.
Students will be able to explain the role of osteoblasts, osteoclasts, and collagen in bone remodeling.

Understand the implications of genetic mutations in osteogenesis imperfecta, and describe the innovative techniques such as cellular reprogramming to pluripotency and CRISPR for developing potential therapies for bone fragility disorders.

pathways.title: Urban Farming for Urban Families
pathways.description: Our well-being is intimately linked to the food that we eat and the places where it is grown. Healthy soils produce nutrient-dense food, which helps us stay healthy, both in body and in mind. For those of us who live in cities, it can be difficult to find fresh, good quality, local food. At 15th Street Farm in Florida, USA, Emmanuel Roux and Professor David Himmelgreen run an education project to help local families learn about their local food systems and make healthier food choices.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will be able to explain the crucial role of soil microorganisms in maintaining soil health and promoting plant growth.
Learners will recognize the importance of urban farming and local food systems in reducing food insecurity and promoting community involvement.
Learners will evaluate the role of food security in human health and well-being, understanding its ties to political, economic, and social inequalities.
Learners will appreciate the potential career paths within anthropology, and the diverse ways anthropologists can contribute to academia, research, business, charity sector, and government.

pathways.title: Spreading the Joy of Mathematics
pathways.description: Dr James Tanton is the founder of the Global Math Project, a worldwide movement of teachers committed to inspiring a love of mathematics in students and spreading the joy of this amazing subject to all corners of the globe!
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you should learn about the impact of The Global Math Project and how it encourages learner engagement.
Understand different approaches to teaching mathematics.
Learn about Exploding Dots and the importance of visualization in learning mathematics.

pathways.title: Evidence: What Is Evidence, and How Do Good Reasoners Respond to It?
pathways.description: This pathway focuses on the central role of evidence in reasoning. Arguments can be logically flawless, but unless they are supported by good evidence, you can’t use them to reason well. Evidence consists of all the information that is currently available to you, either from your environment or stored as background knowledge. Experience is our primary source of evidence. In perception, you gain information about your immediate surroundings using your senses. And through other people’s testimony, you can also access a great deal of evidence about the broader world. The best way to use your evidence to reason well is to proportion your beliefs to your evidence. This means letting your evidence determine how confident you are in your beliefs—the stronger your evidence, the more confident you can be.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of evidence in reasoning by recognizing different types of evidence (perception, testimony, experience) and how they shape our beliefs.
Evaluate and apply confidence levels by learning how to proportion your beliefs to the strength of available evidence.
Critically assess testimony and sources of information by identifying reliable and unreliable sources, and understanding the importance of expert assurance.
Analyze the connection between beliefs and actions by understanding how accurate, evidence-based beliefs enable successful decision-making and reasoning.

pathways.title: How Does the Southern Ocean Help Protect Our Planet?
pathways.description: The Southern Ocean circulation system is vital for redistributing heat, carbon and nutrients around the world. It also plays a significant role in absorbing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, having sequestered ~40% of anthropogenic CO2 absorbed by the oceans. Associate Professor Julie Trotter and Professor Malcolm McCulloch from The University of Western Australia, and Dr Paolo Montagna from the Institute of Polar Sciences in Bologna, Italy, are working to understand the complex interactions between the Southern Ocean and global climate.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway learners will be able to understand the challenges and rewards of cross-disciplinary research in marine geochemistry.
Learners will understand the impact of the Southern Ocean on global marine biological production and nutrient distribution.
Learners will evaluate the impact of increased CO2 levels on the Southern Ocean and its potential consequences for global ecosystems.

Learners will know about the importance of cross-disciplinary collaboration in marine geochemistry and its role in obtaining a holistic understanding of ocean interactions with the atmosphere, cryosphere, and biosphere.

pathways.title: Scott Edwards, PhD - Ornithologist
pathways.description: Dr. Scott Edwards is a professor, researcher, ornithologist and the curator at the Harvard Museum of Comparative Zoology. He's interested in the evolution of life on Earth and the processes that have created our huge diversity of species.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Circulatory and Lymphatic System Infections
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of anatomy of the circulatory & lymphatic systems; bacterial infections of the circulatory & lymphatic systems; viral infections of the circulatory & lymphatic systems; and parasitic infections of the circulatory & lymphatic systems.

pathways.title: Characteristics of Living Things
pathways.description: What defines life? This learning sequence explores the characteristics of living things and introduces learners to different types of cells. More resources are coming soon!
learning_objectives: Identify whether an organism is living or nonliving.
Distinguish between prokaryote and eukaryote cells.
Compare plant and animal cells.
Investigate the levels of organization in multicellular organisms.

pathways.title: Cellular Respiration
pathways.description: In this learning sequence, students will delve deeper into the process of cellular respiration. Students are introduced to the critical contributors to the process, including ATP—the cell’s energy currency—and mitochondria, where much of this activity occurs. They’ll learn how energy from food is converted into energy that cells can use through the process of cellular respiration.
learning_objectives: Explain the process of cellular respiration
Explain the structure and function of ATP
Describe how food is transformed through chemical reactions into molecules that support growth or release energy.
Analyze how cellular respiration is impacted by environmental factors

pathways.title: Non-Mendelian Genetics
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores why some traits don’t follow simple Mendelian ratios. In this pathway, learners will investigate linked genes, sex-linked traits, codominance, incomplete dominance, pleiotropy and polygenic traits. Learners will also explore non-nuclear inheritance, such as how mitochondria and chloroplasts are passed from mother to offspring. By analyzing data and real-world examples, learners will see how these exceptions expand our understanding of heredity. Coming soon: new content on linked genes, sex-linked inheritance, and sex determination in humans.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Explain exceptions to Mendel’s principles of inheritance.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Microbial Mechanisms of Pathogenicity
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of characteristics of infectious disease; how pathogens cause disease; virulence factors of bacterial and viral pathogens; virulence factors of eukaryotic pathogens.

pathways.title: Coordinates & Equations
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the coordinate system and linear equations, and explains how to solve these equations graphically.
learning_objectives: Use the coordinate system to determine the coordinates of a point.
Solve equations using the coordinate system.

pathways.title: Chromatography
pathways.description: Chromatography is a useful lab technique for separating the parts of a mixture. It works by moving the mixture through a material (the stationary phase) using a liquid or gas (the mobile phase). Because different substances move at different speeds, they can be identified, purified, or measured. Scientists use chromatography in many fields, like chemistry, biology, and environmental science.
learning_objectives: Understand the basic principle of chromatography and how it separates components based on differential affinities toward stationary and mobile phases.
Identify different types of chromatography techniques (e.g., paper, thin-layer, column) and their specific applications.

pathways.title: Pythagoras' Theorem
pathways.description: This pathway explores the principles of Pythagoras' Theorem and teaches learners how to solve problems involving right-angled triangles.
learning_objectives: Describe the mathematical relationship between the legs and hypotenuse of a right triangle as pythagorean theorem.
Use the pythagorean theorem to determine side lengths in right triangles.
Describe what a pythagorean triple is.

pathways.title: Time, Speed & Distance
pathways.description: This pathway aims to help learners understand the mathematical relationships between time, speed, and distance while providing exercises and problems to solve.
learning_objectives: Describe the mathematical relationship between time, distance, and speed.

pathways.title: The Energy in Chemical Reactions: Thermodynamics and Enthalpy
pathways.description: Thermodynamics—the study of how and why energy moves—governs what can happen in a chemical reaction. By applying the laws of thermodynamics, chemists can measure, predict, and control the heat and energy of chemical reactions. This pathway forms part of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Mechanisms of Microbial Genetics
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of the functions of genetic material; dna replication; rna transcription; protein synthesis (translation); mutations; how asexual prokaryotes achieve genetic diversity; gene regulation: operon theory

pathways.title: Combinatorics
pathways.description: This pathway aims to help learners understand the algebraic principles of the combination and enumeration of sets.
learning_objectives: Develop skills for solving combinatorics and permutation problems.
Explain what a factorial is and its relation to combinatorics.

pathways.title: Biotechnology
pathways.description:
This AP Biology pathway explores genetic engineering and the molecular tools used to manipulate DNA. Learners will investigate DNA cloning, recombinant DNA, and plasmids, and see how restriction enzymes and DNA ligase enable scientists to cut, join, and recombine DNA. They will explore bacterial transformation, gel electrophoresis, PCR, and DNA sequencing to understand how genetic material is analyzed and modified. Learners will also examine genetically modified organisms (GMOs) and the ethical considerations of gene editing, highlighting the real-world applications of biotechnology in research, medicine, and agriculture.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe the use of genetic engineering techniques in analyzing or manipulating DNA.
Explain the application of the following techniques used to analyze and manipulate DNA and RNA: electrophoresis, PCR, bacterial transformation, DNA sequencing.

pathways.title: Earth’s Motion: How Rotation and Revolution Cause The Seasons
pathways.description:
Embark on an exploration of Earth's orbit and rotation with this pathway and student guide. The recommended learning time is 5 days (190-210 minutes), analyzing data and building models to explain the seasons and solar as well as lunar eclipses. Create an engaging video to share your astronomical findings and consider STEM careers linked to earth sciences.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2 & 3, 40-60 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 9-10 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 10 minutes, item 11 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to collect and analyze data about the Earth’s rotation and revolution.
Demonstrate an understanding of patterns of movement in our solar system by creating an accurate model.

Engage in virtual experiments and collaborative discussions to enhance critical thinking skills, explore the relationships between Earth’s movement and the seasons, undertake related career exploration, and ask relevant questions.

pathways.title: Agriculture
pathways.description: Unit 7 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. Demographers project that Earth's population will peak during the 21st century at approximately ten billion people. But the amount of new cultivable land that can be brought under production is limited. In many nations, the need to feed a growing population is spurring an intensification of agriculture—finding ways to grow higher yields of food, fuel, and fiber from a given amount of land, water, and labor. This unit describes the physical and environmental factors that limit crop growth and discusses ways of minimizing agriculture's extensive environmental impacts.

pathways.title: Gravitation and Uniform Circular Motion
pathways.description: This pathway deals with the simplest form of curved motion, uniform circular motion, which is motion in a circular path at constant speed. As an object moves on a circular path, the magnitude of its velocity remains constant, but the direction of the velocity is changing. This means there is an acceleration that we will refer to as a “centripetal” acceleration caused by a net external force, also called the “centripetal” force. The centripetal force is the net force totaling all external forces acting on the object. Studying this topic illustrates most of the concepts associated with rotational motion and leads to many new topics we group under the name rotation. This motion can be described using kinematics variables, but in addition to linear variables, the pathway introduces angular variables. Various ways are used to describe motion, namely: verbally, algebraically and graphically. This pathway also discusses how gravitational mass is an important property of an object or a system.
learning_objectives: Define arc length, rotation angle, radius of curvature, and angular velocity.
Calculate the angular velocity of a car wheel spin.
Define and establish the expression for centripetal acceleration.
Explain the centrifuge.
Calculate coefficient of friction on a car tire.
Calculate ideal speed and angle of a car on a turn.
Discuss the inertial frame of reference.
Discuss the non-inertial frame of reference.
Describe the effects of the coriolis force.
Explain earth's gravitational force.
Describe the gravitational effect of the moon on earth.
Discuss weightlessness in space.
Identify and understand the cavendish experiment.
Identify and state kepler's laws of planetary motion.
Identify and derive kepler's third law for circular orbits.
Discuss the ptolemaic model of the universe.

pathways.title: Why Is Biodiversity Threatened?
pathways.description: Human population growth and resource utilization contribute to biodiversity loss through land-use changes, pollution, and resource exploitation. Balancing human needs with ecosystem health is crucial for a sustainable future and preserving species richness. Explore urgent threats to our planet's ecosystem, including global warming fueled by rising greenhouse gases, with assessments to evaluate your understanding of human impact on the ecosystem. For more content about biodiversity, take the Biodiversity Course.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you define the terms "introduced species," "native species," "invasive species" and "cryptogenic species."
