you will identify the factors contributing to exponential human population growth.
You will explore the root causes of local threats, including human activities, land use changes, and industrial practices, to understand the factors driving biodiversity decline.
You will examine the historical trends in atmospheric carbon dioxide levels and the role of the greenhouse effect in causing Earth's warming.
You will analyze the consequences of global temperature change on biodiversity, including shifts in habitat ranges, species distribution, and adaptations.

You will demonstrate the ability to assess the significance of human activities on the planet, evaluate the impacts of introduced species, and analyze the effects of human population growth on biodiversity through self-assessment.

pathways.title: Introduction to Animal Diversity
pathways.description: Animal evolution began in the ocean over 600 million years ago with tiny creatures that probably do not resemble any living organism today. Since then, animals have evolved into a highly diverse kingdom. Although over one million extant (currently living) species of animals have been identified, scientists are continually discovering more species as they explore ecosystems around the world. The number of extant species is estimated to be between 3 and 30 million.This pathway provides an in-depth look at features of the animal kingdom, features used to classify animals, animal phylogeny, and the evolutionary history of the animal kingdom. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: List the features that distinguish the kingdom animalia from other kingdoms.
Explain the processes of animal reproduction and embryonic development.
Describe the roles that hox genes play in development.
Explain the differences in animal body plans that support basic animal classification.
Compare/contrast the embryonic development of protostomes and deuterostomes.
Interpret the metazoan phylogenetic tree.
Describe the types of data that scientists use to construct and revise animal phylogeny.
List some of the relationships within the modern phylogenetic tree that have been discovered as a result of modern molecular data.
Describe the features that characterized the earliest animals and approximately when they appeared on earth.
Explain the significance of the cambrian period for animal evolution and the changes in animal diversity that took place during that time.
Describe some of the unresolved questions surrounding the cambrian explosion.
Discuss the implications of mass animal extinctions that have occurred in evolutionary history.

pathways.title: The Endocrine System
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at an overview of the endocrine system, hormones, the pituitary gland & hypothalamus, the thyroid gland, the parathyroid glands, the adrenal gland, the pineal gland, gonadal & placental hormones, the endocrine pancreas, organs with secondary endocrine functions, and development & aging of the endocrine system. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Identify the contributions of the endocrine system to homeostasis.
Discuss the chemical composition of hormones and the mechanisms of hormone action.
Summarize the site of production, regulation, and effects of the hormones of the pituitary, thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal, and pineal glands.
Discuss the hormonal regulation of the reproductive system.
Explain the role of the pancreatic endocrine cells in the regulation of blood glucose.
Identify the hormones released by the heart, kidneys, and other organs with secondary endocrine functions.
Discuss several common diseases associated with endocrine system dysfunction.
Discuss the embryonic development of, and the effects of aging on, the endocrine system.

pathways.title: Surface Tension and Capillary Action
pathways.description: This pathway takes an in-depth look at the forces at play between water molecules. Cohesion and adhesion forces as well as hydrogen bonding between water molecules are discussed and processes like surface tension and capillary action are explored. In addition, this pathway includes experiments that show how animals use water tension, how to measure surface tension, and how to observe capillary action in action.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Describe the difference between cohesion and adhesion.
Explain what causes surface tension in water.
Explain why capillary action occurs.
Name at least three examples of surface tension and capillary action.

pathways.title: Phylogenies and the History of Life
pathways.description: The bee and the Echinacea flower could not look more different, yet they are related, as are all living organisms on Earth. By following pathways of similarities and changes—both visible and genetic—scientists seek to map the evolutionary past of how life developed from single-celled organisms to the tremendous collection of creatures that have germinated, crawled, floated, swum, flown, and walked on this planet. This pathway provides an in-depth look at organizing life on earth, determining evolutionary relationships, and perspectives on the phylogenetic tree. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Discuss the need for a comprehensive classification system.
List the different levels of the taxonomic classification system.
Describe how systematics and taxonomy relate to phylogeny.
Discuss a phylogenetic tree's components and purpose.
Compare/contrast homologous and analogous traits.
Discuss the purpose of cladistics.
Describe maximum parsimony.
Describe horizontal gene transfer.
Illustrate how prokaryotes and eukaryotes transfer genes horizontally.
Identify the web and ring models of phylogenetic relationships and describe how they differ from the original phylogenetic tree concept.

pathways.title: Protection from Pathogens
pathways.description: This pathway looks at the integumentary, and immune systems to provide insight into how the human body defends itself against pathogens.
learning_objectives: Describe the main function of each layer of the integumentary system.
Describe the function of melanin and discuss the consequences of reduced melanin.
Describe the body’s innate physical and chemical defenses.
Explain how the immune system responds to maintain homeostasis in the body.

pathways.title: Radioactivity and Nuclear Physics
pathways.description: This pathway explores radioactivity and nuclear physics including the structure and properties of a nucleus and the forces that govern the behavior of the nucleus, including the weak force and the strong force. This explains that interactions can be described by forces, such as the strong force between nucleons holding the nucleus together. The pathway also discusses the conservation laws associated with nuclear physics, such as conservation of energy, conservation of charge, and conservation of nucleon number and the processes that can be described using conservation laws. Also explored in this pathway are the consequences of charge conservation during radioactive decay and during interactions between nuclei, and how conservation of nucleon number determines which nuclear reactions can occur. Types of nuclear radiation, radioactivity, and the binding energy of a nucleus are explained. In addition, the pathway explores how probability can describe the behavior of quantum mechanical systems and the process of radioactive decay, which can be described by probability theory. Examples demonstrating spontaneous radioactive decay as a probabilistic statistical process are provided, making a connection between modeling matter with a wave function and probabilistic description of the microscopic world.
learning_objectives: Explain nuclear radiation.
Explain the types of radiation – alpha emission, beta emission, and gamma emission.
Explain the ionization of radiation in an atom.
Define the range of radiation.
Explain the working principle of a geiger tube.
Define and discuss radiation detectors.
Define and discuss the nucleus in an atom.
Define atomic number.
Define and discuss isotopes.
Calculate the density of the nucleus.
Explain nuclear force.
Define and discuss nuclear decay.
Describe the conservation laws.
Explain parent and daughter nucleus.
Calculate the energy emitted during nuclear decay.
Define half-life.
Define dating.
Calculate the age of old objects by radioactive dating.
Define and discuss binding energy.
Calculate the binding energy per nucleon of a particle.
Define and discuss tunneling.
Define and discuss tunneling.
Explain quantum tunneling.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Prokaryotic Diversity
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of prokaryote habitats, relationships, & microbiomes; proteobacteria; nonproteobacteria gram-negative bacteria & phototrophic bacteria; gram-positive bacteria; deeply branching bacteria and archaea.

pathways.title: Calorimetry and Food Calories
pathways.description: This pathway explores calorimetry, which is the process of measuring the amount of heat created by a chemical or physical change. It includes building a calorimeter that will be used to investigate the Calorie content (amount of energy) in different types of food.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Explain the technique of calorimetry.
Understand the processes that allow a calorimeter to measure the energy stored in food.
Know how to calculate the specific energy (energy content) from calorimetry data.
Desribe the relationship between calories, energy, and good nutrition.

pathways.title: How do Environmental Factors Affect Populations?
pathways.description: Use this pathway to explore how environmental factors impact population size and composition.
learning_objectives: Describe how environmental factors can impact a population
Describe how mutations impact a population changing over time

pathways.title: Cracking Chirality: The Mystery of Mirror Molecules
pathways.description:
How does chemistry transform into life? Watch a video to investigate how the symmetry of molecules may have played a role in the development of early Earth's chemistry. Then explore the mysteries of molecular chirality and its role in life's origins with our engaging lessons on stereochemistry for high school students.
This pathway includes video and curriculum materials to support student learning around chirality, featuring the Chemistry Shorts film, "Cracking Chirality."
learning_objectives: You will investigate the properties of chiral molecules and their role in the origin of life, exploring how environmental factors like magnetism may have influenced early biochemical processes.
You will explore molecular chirality and its implications for the origin of life, applying scientific reasoning to understand the molecular-level structure's role in biological functions.
You will construct and analyze molecular models to understand chirality and its significance in molecular interactions and biological processes.

pathways.title: Cell Division: Meiosis
pathways.description: Meiosis is a cellular process resulting in the division of a cell into four daughter cells, each containing half the number of chromosomes as the parent cell. It involves two stages, known as meiosis I and meiosis II. This then leads to formation of gametes.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will identify the stages of meiosis, distinguishing between meiosis I and meiosis II.
Describe the process of chromosome reduction during meiosis, explaining how it leads to the formation of daughter cells with half the chromosome number of the parent cell.
Analyze the significance of meiosis in sexual reproduction, particularly in the formation of gametes and the maintenance of genetic diversity.
Compare and contrast meiosis with mitosis, highlighting their similarities and differences in terms of chromosome behavior and the number of daughter cells produced.

pathways.title: Nervous System: Structure, Function, and Neurological Health
pathways.description: The nervous system is the body's command center, possessing incredible complexity and capability. Discover its structure, functions, and neurodevelopmental disorders, including their effects, causes, and treatments. Gain insights into various neuroscience career paths along the way.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and function of the nervous system, including the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) and peripheral nervous system.
Explain the basic principles of neuronal communication, including the generation and transmission of nerve impulses.
Analyze the organization and function of the major divisions of the brain, including the cerebrum, cerebellum, and brainstem.
Evaluate common neurological disorders and conditions, such as stroke, Alzheimer's disease, and multiple sclerosis.
Demonstrate an understanding of the role of the nervous system in sensory perception, motor control, and cognitive function.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: Matter and the Rise of Atomic Theory: The Art of the Meticulous
pathways.description: This unit explores how chemistry evolved from adapting materials for practical purposes to science that systematically offers solutions to the world’s challenges and forms part of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.
learning_objectives: Identify the scientific method and the use of models in chemistry.
Define chemistry broadly as the accumulated knowledge, skills, methods, procedures, and theoretical framework that people use to manipulate matter to serve human needs.
Discuss how the work in the late 1700s and early 1800s set the stage for the chemical revolution that followed.
Discuss how phlogiston and other now-discredited chemical theories came to be replaced by a more scientific model of matter based on particles.

pathways.title: Science Communication
pathways.description: Dive into dynamic science communication strategies, embracing networking, interviewing, and public speaking as components of effective communication. Navigate ethical dilemmas when discussing animal research with diverse groups, while discovering the power of captivating storytelling to boost public understanding and engagement with complex scientific ideas, shaping decision-making and fostering active involvement.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will develop an understanding of effective science communication strategies, including networking, interviewing, and public speaking, to enhance career advancement opportunities.
Analyze the ethical considerations and communication strategies used in discussing animal research with diverse audiences.
Evaluate the impact of effective science communication, narrative techniques and storytelling to enhance public understanding and engagement with scientific concepts.
Assess the role of science communication and storytelling in influencing public engagement and decision-making related to scientific issues.

Explore the deficit model's limitations in science education and learn how storytelling in science communication bridges the gap, enhancing public engagement and understanding of complex scientific concepts.

pathways.title: Wealth
pathways.description: The global economy today runs on prediction. From stock market modeling to macroeconomic predictions of global trade, money and prediction go hand-in-hand. Learn from this growing collection of resources on prediction and finance, starting with a discussion of a historically difficult area of prediction -- human behavior and the economy.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming

pathways.title: Elephants and Cancer
pathways.description: Elephants are the largest land mammals, with huge bodies that are made up of exponentially more cells than smaller organisms – cells that could mutate over their long lifespan. And yet, elephants rarely get cancer. This is thought to be due in part to their extra TP53 tumor suppressor genes, which code for the p53 protein. Through a comparison of elephants, humans, and four other organisms, students learn why elephants’ genetic makeup makes them less susceptible to cancer. Includes a gel electrophoresis lab.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain how genetic and environmental factors influence the development of cancer, with examples.
Understand that the structure of a protein determines its function and that mutations can change the structure.
Explain how a phylogenetic tree and/or cladogram can be used to infer evolutionary relatedness.
Explain the use of genetic engineering techniques in analyzing or manipulating DNA.

pathways.title: Lessons Learned from Amgen Scholar Alumni
pathways.description:
This pathway features interviews where former Amgen Scholars share lessons they learned from their research experiences. Topics include: how and why they became scientists, advice for getting into research and how to communicate with your lab mentors, common struggles we all go through in research, and advice on how to make the most of your ASP experience. 
The Amgen Scholars Program is an initiative that provides undergraduate students from diverse backgrounds with hands-on research experiences in biotechnology and life sciences at leading universities and research institutions. Amgen Scholars are talented and motivated students who are selected to participate in this program, where they engage in cutting-edge research, receive mentorship from experienced scientists, and develop essential skills for careers in STEM. The program aims to inspire the next generation of scientific leaders and enhance diversity within the scientific community.
learning_objectives: Summarize lessons learned from Amgen Scholar alumni and apply it to your own research projects..

pathways.title: Mitosis and Chromosomal Instability
pathways.description: This pathway uses videos to illustrate cell division with multiple centrosomes, resulting in normal chromosome segregation, cell division with multiple centrosomes, showing merotelic and syntelic attachments, resulting in micronucleus formation, and the role of dyneins in yeast mitosis.

pathways.title: Molecular Mars
pathways.description: Even on a distant planet, everything is made from the basic building blocks of atoms, compounds and molecules. This pathway explores some of the chemical investigations that are taking place on Mars.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Identify some of the important chemical features of mars.
Describe some of the instruments on the mars rovers used for chemical investigations.
Discuss the importance of the search for organic compounds on mars.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Diseases of the Immune System
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of hypersensitivities, autoimmune disorders, organ transplantation & rejection, immunodeficiency, cancer immunobiology, and immunotherapy.

pathways.title: Immunology
pathways.description: Antibodies can recognize over 10 billion different antigens. This pathway explores the immune system, pathogens, vaccines, autoimmune disease, and HIV research, linking microbiology, immunology, and biotechnology to disease prevention, health, and scientific inquiry. Gain insights into immunity, vaccination, infection processes, and global health challenges.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will define immunology and describe the structure and function of the immune system in protecting the body against pathogens.
Differentiate between innate and adaptive immunity and explain the roles of immune cells, antibodies, and memory cells in disease prevention.
Explain how vaccines work and evaluate their importance in controlling infectious diseases, including emerging and re-emerging diseases such as COVID-19 and HIV.
Analyze immune system disorders, including autoimmune diseases, allergies, and immune deficiencies, and discuss their impact on human health.
Investigate the role of biotechnology in immunology, including recombinant protein vaccines, monoclonal antibodies, and engineered immune cells, in advancing medical treatments.
Evaluate global health challenges related to immunity and infection, including HIV/AIDS, and propose strategies for disease prevention and public health.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To The Eukaryotes of Microbiology
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of unicellular eukaryotic parasites; parasitic helminths; fungi; algae and lichens.

pathways.title: What Are the Different Levels of Biodiversity?
pathways.description: Explore biodiversity at genetic, ecosystem, and evolutionary levels. Delve into the critical interplay between genetic diversity and ecosystems, examining their profound impact on conservation and the sustainability of life. Investigate the interconnectedness of all living organisms through the Tree of Life, gaining insights into how biodiversity shapes the web of life. Engage with questions on evolutionary relationships and genetic variation among species. Discover risks like shallow gene pools, human activities, and coral reefs impacting species' ability to thrive. For more content about biodiversity, take the Biodiversity Course.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define the gene pool as the entire genetic information within a population.

You will analyze the consequences of decreasing genetic diversity within threatened species, recognizing its correlation with the likelihood of extinction and the challenges faced by species in small, isolated populations.
You will describe the importance of DNA in phylogenetic analyses and how molecular data enhances our understanding of evolutionary relationships.
You will examine the role of phylogenetics in understanding the evolutionary distinctiveness and diversity of species within ecosystems.
You will evaluate the limitations and complexities involved in real-life conservation decisions
You will evaluate the significance of preserving ecosystems, taking into account their impact on biodiversity and the overall health of the ecosystem.

pathways.title: Applied Chemistry – Solutions, Materials, and Industry
pathways.description: Chemistry is everywhere, from solution preparation in labs to creating new materials that shape industries. This pathway explores how solutions are made, the properties of materials, and chemistry’s role in real-world industries like medicine and manufacturing. You will gain practical chemistry skills through interactive activities and career insights.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain how solutions are prepared and measured in laboratory settings.
Perform calculations related to molar mass and solution concentrations.
Analyze material properties and how atomic structures affect functionality.
Evaluate chemistry’s impact on industry, from medicine to engineering.
Explore career paths in chemistry and how professionals use these concepts daily.

pathways.title: Human Population Dynamics
pathways.description: Unit 5 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. Which factors influence human population growth trends most strongly, and how does population growth or decline impact the environment? Does urbanization threaten our quality of life or offer a pathway to better living conditions? What are the social implications of an aging world population? Discover how demographers approach these questions through the study of human population dynamics.
subject_tags: Economics:Firms, Work, and the Labor Market

pathways.title: Creating an Inclusive Classroom
learning_objectives: Identify the characteristics of an inclusive classroom.
Recognize how an inclusive classroom can create a more equitable STEM learning environment, especially for racially diverse students.
Use inclusive teaching activities in STEM lessons to provide equitable learning opportunities.
Apply inclusive approaches to classroom management and arrangement.

pathways.title: The Great Backyard Bird Count
pathways.description:
Each year, people from around the world come together in February to watch, learn about, count, and celebrate birds in the Great Backyard Bird Count. This free, fun, and easy event engages bird watchers of all ages in counting birds to create a real-time snapshot of bird populations. Enrich your experience by browsing some of the great bird-related resources on LabXchange, from the importance of spending time in nature to Charles Darwin's finches!
The Great Backyard Bird Count is an inter-organizational effort between the Cornell Lab of Ornithology, National Audubon Society, and Birds Canada.
learning_objectives: Describe the evolutionary history of birds.
Describe the derived characteristics in birds that facilitate flight.
Discuss career paths that incorporate an interest in nature.
Discuss the factors involved in preserving the endangered hawaiian honeycreeper.

pathways.title: PCR, Transformation, Electrophoresis, and Purification
pathways.description: PCR is a method used to make many copies of a piece of DNA. The copied DNA can be put into plasmids and added to bacteria. Scientists grow the bacteria on special plates to find the ones with the new DNA. They use gel electrophoresis to check the DNA and special dyes to see it. The DNA is then cleaned and ready for other experiments.
learning_objectives: Explain the principles and steps of the Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) for amplifying specific DNA sequences.
Describe the purpose and process of bacterial transformation using competent cells and plasmid DNA
Perform agarose gel electrophoresis to separate and analyze DNA fragments by size.
Interpret gel electrophoresis results to confirm successful DNA amplification or transformation.
Apply DNA purification techniques to isolate clean DNA for downstream applications
Understand the role of each technique in molecular cloning workflows.
Use proper laboratory techniques and safety practices throughout the procedures.
Identify common troubleshooting strategies for issues in PCR, transformation, or electrophoresis.
Explain the significance of controls and selective markers in transformation experiments.
Connect these molecular techniques to real-world applications in biotechnology, medicine, and research.

pathways.title: Multicellular Model Organisms
pathways.description: Microgravity, radiation, and other space environment stressors all pose risks to human health. This pathway explores the current and past research efforts conducted on multicellular model organisms in space or simulated space environments, with the goal of providing insight into how humans respond in such conditions. Learn about these studies and the insight they provide.
learning_objectives: Explain the importance of multicellular model organisms in studying biological processes.
Predict the effects of the space environment on human health.
Understand why studying a range of model organisms is important for ensuring human survival in space

pathways.title: Trigonometry Application
pathways.description: Learn how to solve ambiguous cases in oblique triangles using the Law of Sines. This math pathway explores finding missing angles and sides in cases where two triangles are possible. Understanding these concepts helps students apply geometry skills in real-world scenarios like navigation.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to apply the Law of Sines and Law of Cosines to solve problems involving oblique triangles, including cases with two possible solutions (ambiguous cases).
Analyze the use of bearings to solve real-world navigation problems, demonstrating an understanding of directional systems and trigonometry.
Solve geometric word problems involving distance, angles, and directions by constructing and interpreting diagrams.
Evaluate the relationships between angles and side lengths in oblique triangles, utilizing trigonometric formulas to calculate unknown values.
Demonstrate problem-solving skills in interpreting and applying trigonometric concepts to both theoretical and practical problems, including navigation and bearings.

pathways.title: Data Science in Clinical Trials
pathways.description: This learning pathway provides a comprehensive overview of clinical trials. Explore essential concepts such as trial design, data collection, and the significance of randomized control trial design. You will gain the skills needed to critically evaluate clinical trial outcomes and explain the statistical analysis methods in clinical research.
learning_objectives: Outline the fundamental steps of clinical trials and randomized control trials, describe the types of data collected, and explain the significance of these trials in assessing the efficacy and value of drugs or treatments.

Interpret key statistical concepts such as statistical hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, statistical significance, effect size, and goodness of fit, and apply appropriate statistical tests to evaluate clinical trial data effectively.

Assess the quality of clinical trial designs and outcomes by understanding the critical aspects of trial design, such as randomization and blinding, and evaluating statistical analyses used in trials to predict the practical relevance of the treatment(s).

pathways.title: Cell Motility
pathways.description: In this pathway, Dr. Julie Theriot (Standford) discusses the biochemical basis of actin-based cell motility using the bacterium Literia as a model system for this process. This pathway is the fifth session in the Cell Biology Flipped Course from iBiology.

pathways.title: Promoting Student Persistence in STEM
learning_objectives: Understand the concept of persistence and its role in promoting the success of racially diverse STEM students.
Describe the factors that influence persistence, including self-efficacy and STEM identity.
Identify teaching strategies and approaches that promote students’ persistence by encouraging self-efficacy and STEM identity.

pathways.title: Separate Biomolecules
pathways.description: Separating proteins and DNA by size is a key analytical task in gel electrophoresis. This pathway introduces essential gel electrophoresis techniques, including SDS-PAGE and agarose gel electrophoresis. Explore how to separate proteins and nucleic acids by size, set up and run gels accurately, and interpret banding patterns to analyze experimental results.
learning_objectives: Conduct agarose gel electrophoresis to separate nucleic acids.
Perform SDS-PAGE to separate proteins by size.

pathways.title: Protein Translocation Across the ER Membrane
pathways.description: This pathway uses animations to illustrate the various steps involved in cotranslational translocation of proteins across the ER membrane.

pathways.title: What’s in a Light? How Do Scientists Use Light Spectra to Learn about the Stars?
pathways.description:
Embark on an astronomical adventure with this comprehensive learning pathway, complete with a student guide. With a recommended learning time of 180-215 minutes, this pathway delves into the universe's diverse stars, their spectral classifications, and techniques to measure their vast distances. Through engaging videos and readings, comprehend how light reveals the cosmos's secrets and how these principles intersect with real-world analysis. Additionally, consider a STEM career that navigates the celestial realm and its mysteries.



Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 30-45 minutes, items 3-7 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60-80 minutes, item 8 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 10 minutes, items 9-10 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to classify various types of stars and understand their spectral characteristics using key astronomical concepts and tools.
Explain the methods used to measure the distance of stars from Earth, including your practical applications in the field of astrophysics.
Apply your knowledge of the atomic light spectrum and flame emission spectroscopy to identify different elements present in celestial bodies.

pathways.title: Proteasome Structure and Function
pathways.description: This pathway uses animations to describe the structure of proteasomes, mechanism of action of proteasome, and proteasome rocking.

pathways.title: Recording and Interpreting Lab Data
pathways.description: Supporting scientific discovery requires precision, organization, and careful interpretation of lab data. This pathway equips learners with essential documentation skills used in microbiology and biotechnology, including how to maintain a complete lab notebook, record experimental protocols and results, and visualize bacterial growth data.
learning_objectives: Record experimental data accurately.
Compute data to determine growth rates in cell cultures.
Interpret data and draw conclusions from experimental results.

pathways.title: Global Commercial Operations
pathways.description: With the aim of marketing and selling medication around the world, these staff share information with doctors and patients about medicines by writing medical articles, creating product brochures, visiting doctors’ offices and providing technical support.​
learning_objectives: You will understand how important outreach programs are in helping students choose their future career.

pathways.title: Is it Possible to Be a Scientist and an Artist?
pathways.description: If you ever wondered if choosing science meant you couldn’t do arts—Sunetra Gupta is proof that you can do both! From using mathematics to outwit the flu virus to writing novels, watch Professor Gupta share in this video her career journey and fascinating research.

pathways.title: Inheritance of Traits: How Are Living Things Made With DNA?
pathways.description:
Some evidence of common ancestry comes from the discovery of fossils, the analysis of DNA, and the presence of similar anatomical traits in humans and animals. Explore DNA structure, protein-coding, and genetic mutations in this pathway which spans 6 days (245-v305 minutes using videos, texts, case studies, and the student guide. Create family trees with BLAST and model how mutations create beneficial or harmful traits.




Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2: 45-60 minutes, items 3-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3-4: 70-90 minutes, item 5 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5: 40-50 minutes, items 6-7 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 6: 40-60 minutes, item 8 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will, model the structure of DNA and understand how DNA codes for proteins.
Create a family tree using BLAST to show similarities in an individual’s genome.
Explain the types of mutations in order to model how they occur in the gene to create beneficial or harmful traits.

pathways.title: The Cell: Understanding the Building Blocks of Life
pathways.description: Explore the fundamental unit of life: the cell. This pathway covers cell components, their functions, differences between cell types, and essential processes like cellular respiration and signaling.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you should be able to define the cell and explain its importance as the fundamental unit of life.
Identify key organelles in the cell and describe their functions.
Differentiate between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, including their structures and processes.
Explain cellular respiration as a process to generate energy for cell functions.
Explore the role of cell signaling in communication and its impact on cell growth, metabolism, and structure.

pathways.title: Linear Deep Learning
pathways.description: This pathway will provide you with a thorough understanding of how deep linear models work, including how to optimize them using gradient descent, how to build and analyze computational graphs, and how to use PyTorch for implementing and training models. Check out Tutorial 1 on the Neuromatch website.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will develop an intuitive understanding of deep Learning.
You will learn about building computational graphs.
You will be able to explain how neural networks learn using gradient descent and the backpropagation algorithm.

pathways.title: Organization of Cytoplasm
pathways.description: Dr. Tony Hyman discusses how molecules within a eukaryotic cell interact to produce order during cell division. He also describes the use of RNAi as a technology to investigate cellular organization. This pathway is session 2 from the Cell Biology Flipped Course from iBiology.

pathways.title: Should Universities Use Differential Treatment to Admit Students?
pathways.description: At first glance, a student’s exam scores might seem like an uncomplicated way to measure academic ability. However, Dr Emil Temnyalov, an economist at the University of Technology Sydney in Australia, highlights the effects of socio-economic influences on these scores. The idea that exam results should be assessed differently based on a student’s socio-economic background is known as differential treatment, and Emil is investigating whether such policies can improve equality and efficiency in education and labour markets.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will better understand the concept of differential treatment in economic and social policies, such as positive discrimination and affirmative action,
Learners will better understand that economists use mathematical models to describe economic problems and theories
Learners will be able to Recognize the value of a precise methodology in addressing major social issues and public policy problem

pathways.title: Data Skills for Biological Technicians
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the essentials of data managment and analysis skills used by biological technicians, including how to import and organize large datasets, optimize software settings, conduct basic data analysis, and create clear data visualization. Explore how these skills support accurate experimental reporting, streamline research workflows, and communicate finding s effectively in laboratory and industry settings.
learning_objectives: Use basic data analysis techniques for summarizing data.
Import and organize large datasets.
Optimize software settings for managing large datasets.
Visualize and report findings clearly using software tools.

pathways.title: DNA Replication
pathways.description: This pathway explores the fundamental process of DNA replication—the mechanism by which genetic information is accurately copied during cell division. You will examine key concepts such as the models of DNA replication, the functions of essential DNA replication enzymes, and the mechanisms of leading and lagging strand synthesis. This pathway also introduces DNA topology, highlighting how supercoiling and topoisomerase activity maintain the structural integrity of DNA during replication. This pathway provides a cohesive understanding of the molecular foundations and precise coordination of the DNA replication process.
learning_objectives: Explain how DNA is replicated during cell division, including the overall purpose of replication and the key steps in the process.
Identify the major enzymes involved in DNA replication and describe what each one does, such as helicase, DNA polymerase, ligase, and topoisomerase.
Compare how the leading and lagging strands are synthesized, and explain how DNA topology (supercoiling and topoisomerase activity) helps maintain DNA structure during replication.

pathways.title: Our Atmosphere
pathways.description: The air we breathe has many components and is responsible for more than keeping us alive. Air pressure gradients, temperature gradients and the spin of the Earth come together to form weather and our climate. Learn about the complexities of the atmosphere in this pathway.
learning_objectives: In this pathway, you will learn about the different layers of the atmosphere.
You will learn about the chemical composition of the atmosphere.
You will understand the importance of having carbon dioxide in the atmosphere, despite its low abundance.
You will understand the various effects low and high pressure can have on your body.
You will experiment with the forces associated with atmospheric pressure.
You will understand how storms are created.
You will test your knowledge of atmospheric properties.

pathways.title: Kinematics
pathways.description: This pathway explores essential ideas in kinematics, the study of the motion of objects, with no consideration as to the causes of this motion. The ideas are initially limited to kinematics in one dimension, but the concepts can be expanded to additional dimensions.
learning_objectives: Define position, displacement, distance, and distance traveled in a particular frame of reference.
Explain the relationship between position and displacement.
Define and distinguish between displacement and distance traveled.
Calculate displacement and distance given initial position, final position, and the path between the two.
Define and distinguish between scalar and vector quantities.
Identify and assign a coordinate system for a scenario involving one-dimensional motion.
Explain the relationships between instantaneous velocity, average velocity, instantaneous speed, average speed, displacement, and time.
Calculate velocity and speed given initial position, initial time, final position, and final time.
Chart / derive a graph of velocity vs. time given a graph of position vs. time.
Interpret a graph of velocity vs. time.
Define and distinguish between instantaneous acceleration and average acceleration.
Calculate acceleration given initial time, initial velocity, final time, and final velocity.
Calculate displacement of an object that is not accelerating, given initial position and velocity.
Calculate final velocity of an accelerating object, given initial velocity, acceleration, and time.
Calculate displacement and final position of an accelerating object, given initial position, initial velocity, time, and acceleration.
Identify and apply problem-solving steps and strategies to solve problems of one-dimensional kinematics.
Identify and apply strategies to determine whether or not the result of a problem is reasonable, and if not, determine the cause.
Describe the effects of gravity on objects in motion.
Discuss the motion of objects that are in free fall.
Calculate the position and velocity of objects in free fall.
Describe a straight-line graph in terms of its slope and y-intercept.
Calculate / determine average velocity or instantaneous velocity from a graph of position vs. time.
Calculate / determine average or instantaneous acceleration from a graph of velocity vs. time.
Chart / derive a graph of velocity vs. time from a graph of position vs. time.
Chart / derive a graph of acceleration vs. time from a graph of velocity vs. time.

pathways.title: Structure of the Cell
pathways.description: This pathway is about a fascinating topic that explores the basic unit of life. Cells are microscopic entities that contain various components and perform various functions. Depending on the type of organism, cells can be classified into two major groups: prokaryotic and eukaryotic. Prokaryotic cells are simpler and lack a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, while eukaryotic cells are more complex and have a nucleus and various organelles.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will be able to define the term "cell" and differentiate cells from organelles, tissues, organs and organ systems.
Describe the structure and function of a cell.
State the similarities and differences between plant and animal cells.
Prepare temporary slides of plant cells (onion epidermal cells) and observe it under a light microscope.

pathways.title: Variation of Traits: Aren’t We All Mutants?
pathways.description:
Genetic traits are inherited through DNA, which determines an organism's characteristics. Explore how genetic inheritance works with this 5-day (185-225 minutes) pathway using pedigrees, videos, texts, simulations, and a student guide. Create Punnett squares, extract DNA, and examine the benefits of genetic counseling.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40-45 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 45-60 minutes, items 3-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 40-50 minutes, items 5-6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 30-40 minutes, item 7 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 30 minutes, item 8 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will create a pedigree/Punnett square that follows a trait.
Extract DNA in order to analyze it like a scientist/genetic counselor.
Explain why genetic counseling is beneficial for families with genetic disorders.

pathways.title: Meiosis and Genetic Diversity
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores meiosis and its role in inheritance and genetic diversity. Learners will examine meiosis I and II, the formation of haploid gametes, and the similarities and differences between mitosis and meiosis. They will explore how homologous chromosomes separate, how crossing over and random assortment occur, and how these processes generate variation while ensuring chromosomes are transmitted to the next generation. Coming soon: new content on oogenesis, linked genes, genetic maps, and crossover frequencies in Sordaria fimicola.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe how meiosis results in the transmission of chromosomes from one generation to the next.
Identify the similarities and differences between the stages and outcomes of mitosis and meiosis.
Explain how meiosis produces genetic variation among offspring.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Antimicrobial Drugs
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of history of chemotherapy and antimicrobial discovery; fundamentals of antimicrobial chemotherapy; mechanisms of antibacterial drugs; mechanisms of other antimicrobial drugs; drug resistance; testing the effectiveness of antimicrobials; current strategies for antimicrobial discovery

pathways.title: Atmosphere
pathways.description: Unit 2 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. The atmosphere is what makes the Earth habitable. Heat-trapping gases allow ecosystems to flourish. While the NOAA Global Monitoring Project documents the fluctuations in greenhouse gases worldwide, MIT's Kerry Emanuel looks at the role of hurricanes in regulating global climate.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming

pathways.title: Viruses
pathways.description: This pathway takes a closer look at viruses and how they can cause diseases. It looks into the scale of cells and viruses and discusses how we can protect ourselves from viral infections using hand-washing and vaccines.
learning_objectives: Define what a virus is.
Explain how viruses can cause infections.
Describe the scale of a virus.
Explain how hand-washing and vaccines can protect people from viral infections.

pathways.title: Cell Biology: Structure, Function, and The Cell Cycle
pathways.description: In biology, cells are the fundamental units of life, forming the structure and function of all living organisms. Whether in eukaryotic or prokaryotic forms, cells share essential features like the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus. Understanding these structures is key to learning about processes like growth, metabolism, and reproduction. The videos and texts in this pathway will help you to understand of structure, function, and types of cells, compare prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, and explain the cell cycle, enhancing their knowledge of cellular biology and its scientific importance.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify and describe the structure and function of cells and their key components, such as the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus, and organelles.
Explain the differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, including size, structure, and examples of organisms in each category.
Discuss the cell theory, outlining its main principles and historical development.
Illustrate and label the parts of animal and plant cells, highlighting their unique features and similarities.
Analyze the role of the cell cycle in growth and reproduction, with emphasis on mitosis and its significance in cancer research.
Investigate how surface-area-to-volume ratios affect cellular efficiency, particularly in nutrient and waste exchange processes.

pathways.title: One Gene, Multiple Factors
pathways.description: This pathway explores factors that act at individual loci and can alter the phenotypic ratios that Mendel’s principles predict. These factors include different types of dominance relationships, multiple alleles, pleiotropy, lethal alleles, penetrance, and expressivity.
learning_objectives: Describe how different factors at a single locus can affect the results of a genetic cross.
Predict the result of a genetic cross for a characteristic that is controlled by a single gene but follows non-Mendelian inheritance.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Nervous System Infections
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of anatomy of the nervous system; bacterial diseases of the nervous system; acellular diseases of the nervous system; and fungal & parasitic diseases of the nervous system

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Respiratory System Infections
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of anatomy & normal microbiota of the respiratory tract; bacterial infections of the respiratory tract; viral infections of the respiratory tract; and respiratory mycoses.

pathways.title: Support Systems of the Body
pathways.description: This pathway describes the systems that provide support to the human body, namely the skeletal and muscular systems.
learning_objectives: Identify the major structures and functions of the skeletal system.
Identify the major structures and functions of the muscular system.
Explain how the skeletal and muscular systems provide support to the body.

pathways.title: Exploring the Scientific Method
pathways.description: The resources in this pathway introduce the scientific method, guiding learners through the essential steps of formulating hypotheses, conducting experiments, and analyzing data. Students will explore how to design controlled experiments, interpret results, and draw evidence-based conclusions. The pathway also includes a hands-on project that allows learners to apply the scientific method to reinforce their learning.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will know the steps associated with the scientific method.
You will know how to formulate a valid hypothesis.
You will understand the requirements for designing a controlled experiment.
You will practice applying the principles of the scientific method through a hands-on activity.

pathways.title: NASA | Galaxy Formation and Evolution
pathways.description: NASA's James Webb Space Telescope is the premier observatory of the next decade, serving thousands of astronomers worldwide. It studies every phase in the history of our Universe, ranging from the first luminous glows after the Big Bang, to the formation of solar systems capable of supporting life on planets like Earth, to the evolution of our own Solar System. Discover how the James Webb Space Telescope is pushing the frontiers of astronomy to answer the mysteries of galactic composition and structure in this pathway.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to define what a galaxy is, identify different types of galaxies and recognize their characteristics
Explain the concept of galaxy evolution, describe how galaxies change over cosmic time, and identify key factors influencing their evolution
Apply your knowledge of spinning black holes to explain their role in the dynamics of galaxies, emphasizing the impact on galactic structure and the surrounding environment
Analyze the characteristics of the center of our galaxy, including the presence of a supermassive black hole, star populations, and the impact on the Milky Way's structure
Apply knowledge and understanding of astronomy technology, focusing on how space telescopes operate

pathways.title: Introduction to Quantum Physics
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the basic principles of quantum mechanics. Quantum mechanics is the branch of physics needed to deal with submicroscopic objects. Because these objects are smaller than those that we can observe directly with our senses, such as computers, books, or cars⁠—and so generally must be observed with the aid of instruments⁠—parts of quantum mechanics seem as foreign and bizarre as the effects of relative motion near the speed of light. Yet through experimental results, quantum mechanics has been shown to be valid. Truth is often stranger than fiction. The pathway also discusses blackbody radiation and the photoelectric effect, the correspondence principle and Heisenberg's undertainty principle.
learning_objectives: Explain max planck’s contribution to the development of quantum mechanics.
Explain why atomic spectra indicate quantization.
Describe a typical photoelectric-effect experiment.

Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons ejected by photons of one energy or wavelength, when given the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons for a different photon energy or wavelength.
Explain the relationship between the energy of a photon in joules or electron volts and its wavelength or frequency.
Calculate the number of photons per second emitted by a monochromatic source of specific wavelength and power.

Identify and relate the linear momentum of a photon to its energy or wavelength, and apply linear momentum conservation to simple processes involving the emission, absorption, or reflection of photons.
Identify and account qualitatively for the increase of photon wavelength that is observed, and explain the significance of the compton wavelength.
Explain what the term particle-wave duality means, and why it is applied to em radiation.
Describe the davisson-germer experiment, and explain how it provides evidence for the wave nature of electrons.
Use both versions of heisenberg’s uncertainty principle in calculations.
Explain the implications of heisenberg’s uncertainty principle for measurements.
Explain the concept of particle-wave duality, and its scope.

pathways.title: Types of Eukaryotic Cells
pathways.description: Why do different types of cells exist? This learning sequence introduces the concept of cell differentiation and explores different types of eukaryotic cells. More resources are coming soon!
learning_objectives: Describe the levels of organization in multicellular organisms.
Classify specialized cells according to their structure and function.
Formulate an evidence-based theory on the significance of certain specialized cell types.

pathways.title: DNA Replication and Mitosis
pathways.description: How is genetic information passed on to new cells within an organism? This learning sequence covers DNA replication and mitosis—processes that ensure each cell in an organism has the appropriate DNA.
learning_objectives: Explain how and why DNA replication occurs.
Describe the stages of mitosis.
Predict the consequences of errors occurring during DNA replication or cell division.

pathways.title: Microorganisms: The Invisible World Around Us
pathways.description: Microorganisms are everywhere—inside our bodies, in the environment, and even shaping global ecosystems. This pathway explores different types of microorganisms, their roles in nature and human health, and how we study them using microscopes. Through videos and texts, you will learn about bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protists, their beneficial and harmful effects, and how microscopes have allowed us to explore this hidden world.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify and classify microorganisms.
Understand microbial structure and function.
Comprehend microbial growth and reproduction.

pathways.title: Electric Current, Resistance, and Ohm's Law
pathways.description: This pathway explores the essential ideas relating to electric charge as a property of a system that affects its interaction with other charged systems, whereas electric current is fundamentally the movement of charge through a conductor and is based on the fact that electric charge is conserved within a system. The conservation of charge also leads to the concept of an electric circuit as a closed loop of electrical current. In addition, this pathway discusses examples showing that the current in a circuit is resisted by the elements of the circuit and the strength of the resistance depends on the material of the elements. The pathway also describes how the interaction of systems of objects can result in changes in those systems. The unifying theme of the physics curriculum is that any changes in the systems due to interactions are governed by laws of conservation. This pathway applies the idea of energy conservation to electric circuits and connects concepts of electric energy and electric power as rates of energy use.
learning_objectives: Define electric current, ampere, and drift velocity.
Describe the direction of charge flow in conventional current.
Use drift velocity to calculate current and vice versa.
Explain the origin of ohm's law.
Calculate voltages, currents, and resistances with ohm's law.
Explain the difference between ohmic and non-ohmic materials.
Describe a simple circuit.
Explain the concept of resistivity.
Use resistivity to calculate the resistance of specified configurations of material.
Use the thermal coefficient of resistivity to calculate the change of resistance with temperature.
Calculate the power dissipated by a resistor and the power supplied by a power supply.
Calculate the cost of electricity under various circumstances.
Explain the differences and similarities between ac and dc current.
Calculate rms voltage, current, and average power.
Explain why ac current is used for power transmission.
Define thermal hazard, shock hazard, and short circuit.
Explain what effects various levels of current have on the human body.
Explain the process by which electric signals are transmitted along a neuron.
Explain the effects myelin sheaths have on signal propagation.
Explain what the features of an ecg signal indicate.

pathways.title: Moon Observation and Orbit
pathways.description: The Moon is the closest celestial body to Earth. Through regular observations of the Moon, one can gain insight into its phases, topography, and other features. Whether using a telescope or studying photographs, learning about the Moon's orbit can be a great way to deepen one's knowledge of astronomy and space science. Explore concepts like lunar phases, tidal locking, libration, and motion synchronous rotation.
learning_objectives: In this pathway, you will explore the lunar surface as seen from Earth.
You will develop an understanding of Moon-related terminology.
You will examine how the Earth and Moon affect each other.

pathways.title: Why Is Biodiversity Important?
pathways.description: Biodiversity is critical to sustaining ecosystems that provide essential services to human populations. Biodiversity ensures resilience and adaptability to environmental changes, making ecosystems more robust and capable of withstanding challenges. This pathway covers ecosystem function and services, species richness, and ecological resilience, with questions to test your understanding of species interactions and ecosystem dynamics. For more content about biodiversity, take the Biodiversity Course.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the concept of biodiversity and species richness and their significance in the natural world.
You will analyze and categorize different types of interactions among organisms, such as predation, parasitism, mutualism, commensalism, and competition.
You will learn to identify key species within ecosystems and understand their significance in maintaining ecological balance.
You will evaluate the impact of human activities, such as over-hunting on ecosystem function.
You will describe how biodiversity is crucial to humans: direct services, indirect services, and aesthetic or ethical effects.
You will test your knowledge of biodiversity, ecosystem function, and services through self-assessment.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To The Cell
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of spontaneous generation; foundations of modern cell theory; unique characteristics of prokaryotic cells and unique characteristics of eukaryotic cells.

pathways.title: Healthy Eating, Plant Foods and Vitamins
pathways.description: This pathway will teach you why you should eat more plant-based foods and how they can improve your health and provide you with vitamins that your body needs.
learning_objectives: Summarize vitamins, what they are and why they are important to health.
Identify foods that contain important vitamins.
Summarize why fruits and vegetables are good for you and how many you need daily.

pathways.title: Equilibrium and Advanced Thermodynamics: Balance in Chemical Reactions
pathways.description: Unit 9 introduces the concept of entropy and its role as the driving force for chemical reactions. Students will learn the Second Law of Thermodynamics in terms of entropy rather than energy and work as it had been previously presented in Unit 7. The middle sections of the unit explain spontaneity and Gibb’s free energy, explaining why some chemical reactions occur spontaneously while others do not. Even non-spontaneous reactions can still occur when coupled with spontaneous reactions. The final sections introduce chemical equilibrium, the equilibrium constant, and equilibrium shifts (Le Chatelier’s Principle). This unit forms part of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.
learning_objectives: Define microstate, macrostate, and entropy.
Use the gibbs free energy equation to determine if a reaction is spontaneous.
Explain how non-spontaneous reactions can still happen when they are coupled with a spontaneous reaction.
Define the equilibrium state as the one where the gibbs free energy is at a minimum.
Describe the equilibrium constant expression for a chemical reaction.
Predict the direction a reaction will shift when: reactants/products are added/removed, the reaction vessel is compressed, or the temperature changes.

pathways.title: Introduction to "Rethinking the Economy After Coronavirus"
pathways.description: The “Rethinking the economy after the coronavirus” series has been initiated by the EPOG+ Master programme during the first lockdown period, with the objective of understanding the socio-economic consequences of the COVID and the perspectives for policy makers to reorientate our development model. Taking benefit of a very qualified network of scholars, this series contains several lectures, with very diversified but totally complementary contents, coping with some of the aspects we collectively understood as vital aspects for the understanding of the current crisis, not only in its surface, but in its structural dimension.

pathways.title: The Path to Newton on LabXchange
pathways.description: An experience where the interactive Path to Newton website is enriched with other web resources from The Prediction Project and Crash Course videos.
learning_objectives: Examine the work that came before newton that ultimately informed his theory of gravity..

pathways.title: Hydrology and Water Systems
pathways.description: Life on Earth would not be possible without water, but water does more than keep you hydrated or give you a place to swim. In this pathway, learn about the role water plays in carving and shaping our planet, and how it behaves under different conditions.
learning_objectives: In this pathway, you will learn about all the different places water can be found on Earth.
You will learn about ocean currents and the tides.
You will learn about groundwater and how it forms caves.
You will test your knowledge of weather and hydrological processes.
You will be able to characterize different bodies of water.

pathways.title: Electromagnetic Waves
pathways.description: This pathway explores essential ideas about electromagnetic waves. The list of the various types of electromagnetic waves, ranging from radio transmission waves to nuclear gamma-rays (γ-rays), is interesting in itself. Even more intriguing is that all of these widely varied phenomena are different manifestations of the same thing—electromagnetic waves. What electromagnetic waves are, how they are created, how they travel, how to understand their widely varying properties, and what their relationship is to electric and magnetic effects are some of the questions about electromagnetic waves that are explored in this pathway.
learning_objectives: Recall and restate maxwell’s equations.
Describe the electric and magnetic waves as they move out from a source, such as an ac generator.
Explain the mathematical relationship between the magnetic field strength and the electrical field strength.
Calculate the maximum strength of the magnetic field in an electromagnetic wave, given the maximum electric field strength.
Identify and draw a simplified electromagnetic spectrum, indicating the relative positions, frequencies, and spacing of the different types of radiation bands.
List three “rules of thumb” that apply to the different frequencies along the electromagnetic spectrum.
Explain why the higher the frequency, the shorter the wavelength of an electromagnetic wave.
List and explain the different methods by which electromagnetic waves are produced across the spectrum.
Explain how the energy and amplitude of an electromagnetic wave are related.
Calculate (given its power output and the heating area) the intensity of a microwave oven’s electromagnetic field, as well as its peak electric and magnetic field strengths.

pathways.title: Solutions and Concentrations
pathways.description: These resources cover the science of solutions and concentrations. Through simulations and a hands-on activity, learners will explore the concepts of solubility and concentration, experimenting with how different substances dissolve in water. A hands-on experiment to determine the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in various solvents, reinforces student understanding through practical application.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the molecular process of dissolving.
You will know why water is described as the universal solvent.
You will understand how to reach the ideal concentration of solute for your purposes.

pathways.title: Lipids and Fats
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the four different types of lipids and takes a closer look at triglycerides, which are often called "fats". A hands-on activity provides the opportunity to extract fats from different foods.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Name the four different types of lipids.
Describe the molecular structure of triglycerides (fats).
Explain the difference between saturated, unsaturated, and trans fats.
Explore how fats can be extracted from foods.

pathways.title: Intro to Cancer: Leukemia and Hina's Story
pathways.description: This is a cancer unit, intended for high school biology classes, focused on cell growth, cell cycle, and mutations. Over ten lessons, students investigate the case of Hina Marsey, an eleven-year old girl, who is diagnosed with a rare form of leukemia. As the unit unfolds, students develop conceptual models on cell growth, cancer, and treatments for leukemia. The unit builds toward a wet lab in which students conduct a gel electrophoresis lab to understand what it means to look for a "match" for transplantation.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand that cellular division (mitosis) and differentiation produce and maintain complex organisms.
Discover that cancer is the result of multiple mutations that alter specific locations or genes in a cell’s DNA.
Understand that cancer affects all population groups in the United States, but certain groups may bear a disproportionate burden of cancer compared with other groups.

pathways.title: Rodrigo Braga, PhD - Cognitive Neuroscientist
pathways.description: Dr. Rodrigo Braga is a musician and soccer-playing cognitive neuroscientist who studies the human brain to better understand what makes humanity unique.

pathways.title: Physics of Hearing
pathways.description: In this pathway, the concept of waves is specifically applied to the phenomena of sound. Sound waves carry energy and momentum from one location to another without the permanent transfer of mass. This energy is carried through vibrations caused by disturbances in air pressure. As air pressure increases, amplitudes of vibration and energy transfer do as well. This explains why a very loud sound can break glass. The pathway continues the fundamental analysis of waves addressed in the previous pathway. Sound waves are periodic, and can therefore be expressed as a function of position and time. Furthermore, sound waves are described by amplitude, frequency, wavelength, and speed. The relationship between speed and frequency is analyzed further, as the frequency of sound depends upon the relative motion between the source and observer.
learning_objectives: Define sound and hearing.
Describe sound as a longitudinal wave.
Define pitch.
Describe the relationship between the speed of sound, its frequency, and its wavelength.
Describe the effects on the speed of sound as it travels through various media.
Describe the effects of temperature on the speed of sound.
Define intensity, sound intensity, and sound pressure level.
Calculate sound intensity levels in decibels (db)..
Define doppler effect, doppler shift, and sonic boom.
Calculate the frequency of a sound heard by someone observing doppler shift.
Describe the sounds produced by objects moving faster than the speed of sound.
Define antinode, node, fundamental, overtones, and harmonics.
Identify instances of sound interference in everyday situations.
Describe how sound interference occurring inside open and closed tubes changes the characteristics of the sound, and how this applies to sounds produced by musical instruments.
Calculate the length of a tube using sound wave measurements.
Define hearing, pitch, loudness, timbre, note, tone, phon, ultrasound, and infrasound.
Compare/contrast loudness to frequency and intensity of a sound.
Identify structures of the inner ear and explain how they relate to sound perception.
Define acoustic impedance and intensity reflection coefficient.
Describe medical and other uses of ultrasound technology.
Calculate acoustic impedance using density values and the speed of ultrasound.
Calculate the velocity of a moving object using doppler-shifted ultrasound.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Innate Nonspecific Host Defenses
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of physical defenses; chemical defenses; cellular defenses; pathogen recognition and phagocytosis; inflammation and fever.

pathways.title: What Effect Does Carbon Dioxide Have On the pH Indicator BTB?
pathways.description: Bromothymol blue (BTB) is a pH indicator that changes color depending on the pH of the solution it’s added to. A fun and simple way to observe this phenomenon is by blowing through a straw into a glass of water containing a few drops of BTB. The carbon dioxide in your breath changes the pH of the solution, which can be qualitatively measured by the color change of the BTB.
learning_objectives: You will explain how carbon dioxide changes the pH of a solution.
You will interpret how the pH indicator Bromothymol blue (BTB) works.
You will use a pH indicator interactive to understand how changes in carbon dioxide change the pH of a solution.

pathways.title: Urinary System: Structure, Health, and Balance
pathways.description: Learn about the structure and function of the kidneys, ureters, bladder, and urethra, and gain an understanding of the processes involved in urine formation, as well as fluid and electrolyte balance. Learn to analyze common urinary disorders such as UTIs, kidney stones, and renal failure. Develop skills in performing basic urinary assessments and discover career opportunities in nephrology and urology.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will describe the structure and function of the urinary system, including the kidneys, ureters, bladder, and urethra.
Explain the process of urine formation, including filtration, reabsorption, and secretion.
Evaluate the role of the urinary system in maintaining fluid and electrolyte balance, as well as acid-base balance.
Analyze common urinary disorders, such as urinary tract infections (UTIs), kidney stones, and renal failure.
Demonstrate the ability to perform basic urinary assessments, such as urinalysis and measuring urine output.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Biochemistry of the Genome
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of using microbiology to discover the secrets of life; structure and function of DNA; structure and function of RNA; structure and function of cellular genomes

pathways.title: How Do Enzymes Speed up Chemical Reactions?
pathways.description: Enzymes are the catalysts of life. They accelerate chemical reactions inside cells to incredible speeds. Enzymes are so extraordinary that we extract them from organisms, like bacteria, and use them for countless processes, from cleaning our clothes and making cheese, to blood clot dissolution and improving antibiotics. Professor Judith Klinman at the University of California, Berkeley, USA, and her laboratory team, the Klinman Group, are focused on examining how enzymes generate these enormous rate accelerations.
learning_objectives: After completing this pathway, you will understand the catalytic properties of enzymes and their significance in accelerating biochemical reactions, both in cellular processes and industrial applications.
You will know how scientists are investigating the factors contributing to the remarkable catalytic rates of enzymes.

pathways.title: Portable Electrical Power
pathways.description: Lithium-ion batteries are used to power our most essential devices including laptops, mobile phones, and electric vehicles. Their chemistry is well-suited to supply portable electrical power. As with anything, this chemistry comes with trade-offs of risks versus benefits. What is a lithium-ion battery and how does it work? What are the benefits and the risks? How are both considered when applying this technology to solve some of our most pressing energy issues? Let's find out.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Explain the phenomenon of thermal runaway.
Describe the risks and benefits of lithium-ion batteries.
Describe tests safety scientists use to test lithium-ion batteries.

pathways.title: Model Organisms: Microbes
pathways.description: Studying the inner workings of microbes is complicated because there is so much variation in how different microbes work. That is why scientists focus on specific species when studying microbial physiology. Check out this pathway to discover how scientists research microbes in space, with the hopes of understanding how they (and we) respond to an environment without perceived gravity, with intense radiation, and under the influence of other space stressor conditions.
learning_objectives: Develop your understanding of the relationship between gravity and physiological processes.
Examine the differences between research conducted in space and on earth.

pathways.title: Cytoskeletal Motor Proteins
pathways.description: Molecular motor proteins are fascinating enzymes that power much of the movement performed by living organisms. This pathway explores how motor proteins function and the tools researchers use to determine their mechanisms of action. This pathway is the sixth session of the Cell Biology Flipped Course from iBiology.

pathways.title: Deciphering Earth's Complex Climate and Human Influence
pathways.description:
Explore climate change in Earth's history, by delving into natural and human-induced factors, and enhance your understanding of our planet's climate evolution with this pathway and accompanying student guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (190-210 minutes) focusing on climate change, human population dynamics, population growth, and the environment. Try out virtual experiments where you can predict future climate and explore the greenhouse effect.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 40-60 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 10-12 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 10 minutes, items 13-14 of the pathway.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will better understand climate change and learn the notion that our planet has a complex climate history that left geologic evidence.
Learn how population growth can be studied to predict Earth’s carrying capacity, and how population growth affects the environment, the economy, and society.
Explore how human activities can affect climate change.

Learners will be able to engage in virtual experiments and collaborative discussion to enhance critical thinking skills, explore the relationships between humans' use of natural resources and Climate change, patterns of movement of human populations, related careers exploration, and ask relevant questions.

pathways.title: Cell Structures, Functions and Specialisation
pathways.description: The nucleus, a membrane-bound organelle in eukaryotic cells, safeguards genetic material (DNA) and regulates protein synthesis, ensuring cellular function and replication.
