Understand the structure, function, and collaboration of cellular organelles in maintaining life processes, including protein synthesis, genetic regulation, and cell communication.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and function of animal cell organelles, including the nucleus, endoplasmic reticulum, mitochondria, and Golgi apparatus, and their roles in maintaining cellular processes
Explain the collaborative processes of organelles in protein synthesis, energy production, and intracellular transport, highlighting their interdependence within the cell.
Analyze the relationship between cell structure and function, including the implications for tissue specialization and medical applications, such as understanding diseases and treatments.
Interpret microscopy images of cells and tissues, identifying organelles and cellular components, and relate them to their specific functions in maintaining life processes.
Apply knowledge of cell structures and functions to real-world contexts, including advancements in medical research, biotechnology, and healthcare innovations.

pathways.title: Extreme Environments
pathways.description: Space is a dangerous place, where "no one can hear you scream". This pathway describes five hazards involved in space travel and life in space, and how these hazards are addressed so that (hopefully) no screaming is required.
learning_objectives: Identify the five hazards of spaceflight.
Examine the ways in which nasa alleviates these hazards.

pathways.title: The Surprising Organic Chemistry
pathways.description: One of the most important and interesting areas in science is organic chemistry. This is the study of carbon and its compounds. Organic chemistry is a big part of our everyday life because it covers more than 90% of all known substances, both natural and man-made. It also connects closely with many other fields of science. In this pathway, we will explore the basics of organic chemistry, including its origins, the compounds it studies, their properties, and their uses. Let's dive into the fascinating world of organic chemistry!
learning_objectives: Assess the importance of organic chemistry, from the description of its implications in technological, economic, ecological development and the discussion of the relationships it maintains with other sciences such as inorganic chemistry, medicine, biochemistry.

Describe the importance of functional groups in determining the physical-chemical properties of organic compounds, from their identification in open formulas, the interpretation of the concept of isomerism, the molecular representation and the application of the IUPAC system for their nomination.
Classify chemical functions from the recognition of their nomenclature, their particular properties and the observation and identification of said properties in laboratory experiences.

Recognize the importance of hydrocarbons in the industry, from the description of their properties, classes, methods of obtaining, forms of representation and nomenclature; and the observation, identification and interpretation of their characteristics in experimental processes in which they will work with alkanes, alkenes and alkynes.

pathways.title: The Animal Body: Basic Form and Function
pathways.description: The structures of animals consist of primary tissues that make up more complex organs and organ systems. Homeostasis allows an animal to maintain a balance between its internal and external environments. The arctic fox is an example of a complex animal that is well adapted to its environment and illustrates the relationships between an animal’s form and function. According to researchers, animals living millions of years ago in the Himalayan Mountains of Tibet are ancestors to many of today’s cold-adapted animals. For example, a type of Tibetan fox from 3–5 million years ago is the ancestor to the arctic fox. This pathway provides an in-depth look at animal form & function; animal primary tissues; and homeostasis. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions. Advanced Placement® and AP® are trademarks registered and/or owned by the College Board, which is not affiliated with, and does not endorse, this textbook.
learning_objectives: Illustrate and describe the various types of body plans that occur in animals.
Explain the limits on animal size and shape.
Discuss how bioenergetics relate to body size, levels of activity, and the environment.
Describe the characteristics of epithelial tissues.
Compare/contrast differences among the three types of muscle tissue.
Describe the characteristics of nervous tissue.
Define homeostasis.
Identify factors that affect homeostasis.
Discuss the differences between negative and positive feedback mechanisms used in homeostasis.
Compare/contrast the differences between thermoregulation mechanisms in endothermic and ectothermic animals.

pathways.title: The Nervous System
pathways.description: The nervous system, with its 86 billion neurons, regulates everything from reflexes to cognition. Explore neuronal communication, glial cells, sensory processing, and nervous system disorders to understand how the central and peripheral systems maintain body functions.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway; you will be able to explain the structure and functions of the nervous system, including the central and peripheral divisions, and their roles in sensory input, integration, and motor output.
Describe the structure and function of neurons, including dendrites, axons, synapses, and glial cells, and their role in neuronal communication.
Classify neurons based on structure and function, distinguishing between multipolar, bipolar, and pseudounipolar neurons, and explaining their roles in sensory, motor, and interneuron pathways.
Analyze the anatomy and physiology of the ear and eye, explaining their roles in hearing, balance, and vision, and how sensory receptors process stimuli.

pathways.title: Beyond the Red Planet
pathways.description: This pathway explores some of the missions that NASA is undertaking for future explorations of the solar system and beyond.
learning_objectives: List the future missions that nasa has planned within the solar system and beyond.
Summarize the aims of the artemis mission.

pathways.title: Pandemics and Human-Nature Relations: The First Crisis Of the Anthropocene
pathways.description:
Pandemics, conservation and human nature relations
Recent responses to the COVID-19 pandemic has spurred reactions to how access to wild animals and their products may spread infectious disease from such animals to humans. This has unleashed an onslaught of debate in the scientific and related policy literature on nature conservation, human and animal rights, and efforts to alleviate global poverty among forest-dependent peoples. The most serious pandemics in history originated in Asia, assuming disastrous proportions on reaching Europe and the Americas. This origin appears related to the density of human populations and their growing proximity to rapidly dwindling wilderness areas. Encroachment on such areas by farmers, herders, and hunters in search of protein derived from bush meat exposed them and their domestic animals to zoonoses that made the leap to human hosts, thus facilitating the spread of infectious disease. But wildlife consumption was only one route that brought increased exposure to such pathogens. Chief drivers that have led to the liberation of disease-causing pathogens from the Brazilian Amazon include human migration, vector dynamics, roads and transportation, habitat loss, and climate change, among others. Similar studies modeling such interactions in S.E. Asia showed that the most prominent factors were land-use change (31%), agricultural industry changes (15%), international travel and commerce (13%). Nevertheless, as the most proximate vectors of emerging disease caused by the transmission of pathogens from animals to humans, wildlife has borne the brunt of proposals for policy adjustment to avert such outbreaks.  A wide-ranging ban on the consumption of wild meat has been proposed, as a rationale for the conservation of threatened wildlife and natural resources. Further discussion on the assumptions that drive such proposals from a rights perspective has been advanced, considering forest dependency and the right to food while embracing a landscape perspective on the need for conservation of biological diversity with adequate food security and nutrition. Solutions demand that we first renew our relationship with nature, recognizing interdependence and finding ways to cushion the overwhelming pressures of teeming humanity while we adapt to nature’s response in the guise of a pandemic.  Different cultures in the Amazon provide us with examples that could inspire and enable a future rooted in a respectful and intuitive relationship with the natural world. By co-evolving with, rather than dominating and destroying we may be able to uncover and nourish the collective instinct toward preservation necessary to keep humans and other species alive.
The first crisis of the Anthropocene (by the AFD) 
The COVID crisis is a triple crisis. Aside from the international health crisis, which has a strong ecological connection and which has so far specifically affected so-called developed economies, the moment has become a global financial crisis and an oil and commodities crisis of acute intensity in the developing world. This course will highlight the interconnectedness of these three dimensions. The first lecture will concentrate on the immediate impacts of the crisis. The health crisis is itself the combination of accelerated ecological degradations due to deeply unbalanced modes of development, and the starving of public health systems. The disinvestment in public goods by national and international public bodies observed in the last decades strikes back. This socio-ecological crisis has generated a full-blown pandemic with financial and economic impacts at a scale not seen in at least a century. Its uncertain timing, completely dependent on the virus dynamics, enters in deep contradiction with the current governance system, making any assumptions about a recovery dynamics highly speculative. This global shock of biological and economic notions of time certainly makes it a systemic crisis of the Anthropocene. In the developing world, the crisis triggered massive international capital flows movement away from emerging markets and toward developed countries. Moreover, the sudden decrease in oil and commodities demand exacerbated tensions notably among oil market actors driving the price for short-term WTI futures in negative territories. Consequently, many emerging markets and developing economies are now facing potential disruptive impacts such as exchange rate tensions, speculative attacks, and possible debt restructuring. The macrofinancial mechanisms at hand will be highlighted.

pathways.title: Electricity and Circuits: Understanding Voltage and Current
pathways.description: Electric current, defined as the flow of charge, powers technologies from electric circuits to advanced systems like electric vehicles. Discover the principles of voltage and electric current, and learn how to use instruments to measure them. Uncover how these concepts power technologies, from circuits to clean energy systems, while highlighting careers in electrical engineering.voltage, electric current, circuits, clean energy, electrical engineering
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to use simulations to construct basic electrical circuits, incorporating components such as batteries, resistors, and switches.
Correctly connect and use a voltmeter and ammeter to measure voltage and current in a circuit.
Record voltage and current measurements and analyze their relationships within a circuit.
Plot and interpret data on a line graph to understand trends and relationships in electrical systems.
Explain how principles of voltage, current, and resistance apply to technologies like electric vehicles and renewable energy systems.
Identify career opportunities in electrical engineering and the importance of electricity in sustainable development.

pathways.title: Energy Challenges
pathways.description: Unit 10 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. Global energy use increases by the day. Polluting the atmosphere with ever more carbon dioxide is not a viable solution for our future energy needs. Can new technologies such as carbon sequestration and ethanol production help provide the energy we need without pushing the concentrations of carbon dioxide to dangerous levels?

pathways.title: Introduction to Cellular Transport: Diffusion and Osmosis
pathways.description: Cells rely on cellular transport to move water, nutrients, and other substances across their membranes. This pathway explores diffusion and osmosis, two essential processes that regulate movement in and out of cells. You will learn about how plant and animal cells transport water and solutes, the role of concentration gradients, and how tonicity impacts cells. Through videos, interactive images, experiments, and quizzes, you will develop a deeper understanding of how cellular transport supports life.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and function of plant and animal cells in relation to cellular transport.
Explain the differences between diffusion and osmosis and their roles in cell function.
Analyze how concentration gradients affect cellular transport.
Apply knowledge of osmosis through hands-on experiments and real-world examples.

pathways.title: Galactic Explorers: Design Your Own Interactive Solar System Exhibit!
pathways.description:
Travel through the solar system with this pathway and accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (190-210 minutes) and focuses on exploring celestial bodies' physical characteristics, location, and motion. Understand the relationship between gravity and mass when it comes to orbits and design an interactive model or a celestial body. Plus, explore a potential STEM career related to these topics. 
 

Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 40-60 minutes, items 3-11 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 12-14 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 10 minutes, item 15 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will be able to recognize and explain the physical characteristics, location, and motion of different celestial bodies in our solar system.
Use your knowledge of scientific investigation and experiment design to study these celestial bodies.
Evaluate and interpret scientific data, like graphs and charts, about the motion and location of these celestial bodies.
Create an interactive model of a chosen celestial body, showing your understanding of its physical traits and movement.
Share your scientific discoveries through the interactive model, helping others understand more about celestial bodies.

pathways.title: The Neurological Exam
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth look at overview of the neurological exam, the mental status exam, the cranial nerve exam, the sensory & motor exams, and the coordination & gait exams. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the major sections of the neurological exam.
Explain the benefits of rapidly assessing neurological function.
Compare/contrast anatomical structures of the nervous system to specific functions.
Diagram the connections of the nervous system to the musculature and integument involved in primary sensorimotor responses.
Compare/contrast the somatic and visceral reflexes with respect to how they are assessed through the neurological exam.

pathways.title: Laboratory Communication
pathways.description: Science is a highly social and communal endeavor. Scientists depend on each other for ideas and inspiration, information, feedback, and training. A research lab facilitates this vital communication with your lab mates through lab meetings, journal clubs, one-to-one training, and the collaborative preparation of manuscripts and research talks. Here, we help you to practice each format of lab communication.
learning_objectives: Explain the different formats of communication in the lab and how they make scientific research possible.
Plan and run an effective lab meeting to present your research to your lab mates.
Lead a journal club to inform and teach the lab about interesting and relevant research from other labs.
Give and receive constructive critical feedback on scientific talks, posters, and manuscripts.

pathways.title: Understanding Acids: Properties, Strength, and Reactions
pathways.description: Discover the differences between strong and weak acids through concepts like ionization, pH, and equilibrium constants (Ka). Learn how acids dissociate in water, form hydronium ions, and influence conductivity. Explore key indicators and tools such as pH meters to test acid strength. Perfect for high school students studying acid-base properties and chemical behavior.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will define weak acids using the Bronsted-Lowry theory and describe their behavior in water, including proton donation and hydronium ion formation.
Compare the strength of weak acids and strong acids based on their ionization, pH, and equilibrium constants (Ka).
Analyze the relationship between acid strength and pKa values to determine the degree of ionization.
Calculate the acid dissociation constant (Ka) for weak acids using equilibrium concentrations of products and reactants.
Determine the pH of weak acids and relate it to their ionization extent, strength, and concentration.
Investigate acid-base reactions using tools like pH meters, conductivity probes, and indicators to identify the strength and concentration of acids.
Apply knowledge of acid reactions to real-life examples, such as the effervescence observed when okra reacts with sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃).

pathways.title: Risk Exposure, and Health
pathways.description: Unit 6 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. We are exposed to numerous chemicals every day from environmental sources such as air and water pollution, pesticides, cleaning products, and food additives. Some of these chemicals are threats to human health, but tracing exposures and determining what levels of risk they pose is a painstaking process. How do harmful substances enter the body, and how do they damage cells? Learn how dangers are assessed, what kind of regulations we use to reduce exposures, and how we manage associated human health risks.

pathways.title: Isolation and Confinement
pathways.description: Expedition crews are expected to stay on board the space station for missions that go up to 12 months. Being isolated and confined in the space station, along with the space environment's stressors, has psychological and behavioral impacts on the flight crew. This pathway discusses the physiological and psychological impacts of isolation and confinement, and what measures researchers and astronauts take to mitigate its effects.
learning_objectives: Explain the hazards of isolation and confinement, and the psychological and behavioral implications of isolation.
List coping mechanisms to deal with isolation and confinement.
Examine the effect of spaceflight on biological systems, including the brain, to better understand the effects on astronauts.
Develop an understanding of the mitigations employed by nasa in preparing astronauts and missions for success.

pathways.title: Data Mining
pathways.description: Data mining is a powerful approach for uncovering patterns, extracting insights, and supporting decision making across science, technology, and data-driven fields. This pathway introduces supervised and unsupervised machine learning techniques—such as regression, decision trees, clustering, and neural networks—used in analytics, statistics, and biotechnology.
learning_objectives: Identify patterns in data using classification and clustering methods, including k-means and hierarchical clustering, to segment data for targeted actions and scientific discovery.

Apply foundational data mining techniques—including decision trees, regression, clustering, and neural networks—to extract insights and support decision-making in business, health, and scientific contexts.

pathways.title: Microbes, Health, and Ecosystems
pathways.description: The digestive system breaks down food into essential nutrients, fueling energy and growth. Explore digestion, absorption, enzyme activity, and metabolism. Ideal for core instruction or additional learning, it supports understanding of human biology, health, and homeostasis through key processes like peristalsis and nutrient assimilation.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to differentiate bacteria and viruses based on their structure, reproduction, and role in human health, emphasizing their beneficial and harmful effects.
Describe interactions between bacteria and viruses, including bacteriophage infections and their significance in biological and medical research.
Examine the role of bacteria and viruses in ecosystems, focusing on their contribution to decomposition, nutrient cycles, and population dynamics.
Evaluate the importance of bacteria and viruses in public health and medicine, discussing disease transmission, immune responses, vaccination, and antimicrobial resistance.

pathways.title: Analyze and Evaluate Lab Assays
pathways.description: Laboratory professionals play a key role in documenting assays and determining analyte concentrations to support precise lab results. This pathway introduces essential assay evaluation techniques used in laboratory settings, including how to document procedures, generate standard curves, and determine the concentration of analytes in a sample. Explore how assays are set up, measured, and interpreted to support real-world lab investigations.
learning_objectives: Document assay procedures and results and evaluate the results, e.g., determine amount of analyte in quantitative assay.

pathways.title: How Can We Discover New Antibiotics?
pathways.description: First discovered in the 1920s, antibiotics have revolutionised the way we treat infectious disease. However, the discovery of new antibiotics has been declining since the mid-1960s and bacteria are gradually developing resistance to existing ones, posing an urgent threat to global health. At Princeton University in the US, Professor Mohammad Seyedsayamdost has developed a new method to identify novel natural products, potentially fast-tracking the development of new antibiotics.
learning_objectives: After completing the pathway, you will understand the historical significance of antibiotics, the challenges in their discovery, and the rise of antibiotic resistance.
Describe the importance of natural products as a source of drugs, the concept of silent gene clusters, and their potential for discovering new natural products.
Analyze the impact of Mo's research on activating silent gene clusters, comparing lab and bacterial behaviors, and exploring potential uses of natural products.

pathways.title: PPE Essentials for Laboratory Work
pathways.description: Using appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) and practicing good ergonomics help ensure safety and productivity in laboratory environments. This pathway introduces the proper selection, use, and maintenance of PPE in scientific settings. Explore how different types of PPE—such as gloves, goggles, and lab coats—protect against specific hazards, and how ergonomic and safety checks support a safe, functional workspace. These practices are essential for anyone preparing to work with chemicals, biological samples, and laboratory equipment.
learning_objectives: Understand and demonstrate the correct selection and use of PPE for different tasks and hazards in a lab setting.
Implement ergonomic practices to maintain safe and comfortable work positions during lab and field tasks.
Conduct regular checks and ensure PPE and ergonomic tools are in good working condition.

pathways.title: Speaking About Science
pathways.description: When presenting your scientific findings, it is important to clearly define your goal, connect with your audience, and tell a good story. Whether you need to address experts in the field or a group of high school students, we will help you to engage and interest the audience by creating an effective and concise slide presentation, designing a science poster, or even delivering a 30-second elevator pitch.
learning_objectives: Understand how to use oral presentations to effectively communicate science with different audiences in mind.
Understand how to create a clear and concise message in short presentations.
Make a poster that gets your scientific content across fast and effectively.
Design constructive slides and give an engaging slide presentation with an emphasis on making graphic and data content more accessible.
Understand how to use storytelling to engage an audience.

pathways.title: Visualization and Reporting
pathways.description: Clear and ethical data visualization is essential for communicating insights across science, technology, and data-driven fields. This pathway introduces key skills in data visualization, statistical analysis, Python programming, and geospatial mapping, while emphasizing accessibility, source attribution, and reporting integrity.
learning_objectives: Select appropriate chart types—such as bar charts, line graphs, histograms, or heatmaps—to match the shape, distribution, and story of a dataset.
Use Python libraries like Pandas, Matplotlib, Seaborn, and GeoPandas to generate clear, purpose-driven data visualizations.
Evaluate the accuracy and reliability of data visualizations by identifying misleading design choices and verifying source credibility.
Design visually accurate and inclusive charts, maps, and dashboards that follow accessibility guidelines and ethical principles.
Communicate insights through visual storytelling by constructing infographics, spatial heatmaps, and geospatial dashboards that highlight patterns, trends, or outliers.

pathways.title: Ecological Challenges from a Multidisciplinary Perspective (EPOG+)
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming

pathways.title: Does Alien Life Exist?
pathways.description: Are we alone in the Universe? Is there life out there somewhere? If there is life, what is it like? This pathway explores some of these big questions.

pathways.title: The Framework for Predictive Systems
pathways.description: The Framework for Predictive Systems helps us classify and understand similarities and differences amongst predictive methods across time and cultures. It is used throughout PredictionX to think about classes of inputs to predictive systems, the systems themselves, and processes for refining systems (e.g. to improve their accuracy).

pathways.title: Hybrid Zones: Evolution's Testing Grounds
pathways.description:
Students will learn that hybrids are not flukes—they’re commonly found in the wild and our grocery stores! By playing and reflecting on Foraging Frenzy (a research-inspired memory game) students will appreciate how climate change affects species ranges and the direction of evolution. This pathway is aimed at Grades 8-12 and consists of three 45-minute activities.
Driving Questions:
• What is hybridization and how does it impact evolution?
• Is hybridization good, bad or neutral?
• How is climate change impacting hybridization and evolution?
Students will move around, play a memory game, and have many opportunities for discussion as they explore how hybridization, evolution, and climate change are intertwined in diverse organisms.
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to distinguish between pure species and hybrids using scientific definitions and naming conventions.
Explore the mechanisms and conditions under which hybridization occurs between two species.
Reflect on how common hybrids are in everyday life, especially in the foods we eat and the impact on biodiversity.
Simulate natural selection and evolution by adopting the roles of chickadees with different foraging strategies, competing for resources in varying environmental settings.
Analyze how environmental factors influence the success of different species.
Collaborate to reflect on the outcomes of varying environments and explore how these results relate to concepts of evolution and hybridization.
Analyze data and construct an explanation on how traits affect survival in different environments.
Evaluate environmental effects on species success and develop game changes to better demonstrate evolution across generations.

pathways.title: How Small Changes in a Protein Can Have Large Impacts on Human Health
pathways.description: In the Tan Lab at Stony Brook University School of Medicine, USA, Dr Dongyan Tan is leading a team of early career scientists who are uncovering the structures and functions of chromatin. These DNA-protein complexes are essential for life. Slight variations in their structure can result in diseases, so it is critical for scientists to learn more about them.
learning_objectives: After completing the pathway, you will understand the vital role of chromatin in DNA packaging and transcription regulation, and how slight variations in its structure can lead to diseases.
Explore the techniques used in structural biology, particularly single-particle cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM), to investigate chromatin structures and functions.
Describe the significance of histone variants in influencing chromatin structure and function and their potential impact on human health and disease.

pathways.title: Foundations of Chemistry – The Atom and The Scientific Method
pathways.description: Chemistry is the science of matter and change, and it all begins with atoms. This pathway introduces the scientific method, atomic structure, periodic trends, and molecular bonding. Through videos, simulations, and interactive activities, you will develop a strong foundation in chemistry and learn how matter behaves at the atomic level.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the scientific method and how it applies to chemistry.
Describe atomic structure, including protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Analyze the periodic table and identify element trends.
Understand isotopes, valence electrons, and molecular bonding.
Build atoms and molecules using interactive simulations.

pathways.title: The Power of X-Rays in Materials Science
pathways.description: The X-ray Materials Science (XMaS) project uses a synchrotron facility, a massive doughnut-shaped structure that accelerates electrons to high velocities. These electrons emit X-ray radiation, which can be used to study the tiniest intricate structures within all kinds of materials. What does this mean for society and our future?
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to explain the function of a synchrotron and its relevance in modern science and technology.
Describe the components of a synchrotron, such as the storage ring, beamlines, and equipment used in experiments, and their roles in scientific research.

Students will be able to explain the benefits of maximizing surface area at the microscopic level, by designing and conducting experiments to test the relationship between surface area and time to dissolve for a Vitamin C tablet.

pathways.title: Why Do We Need to Study Chromosomes?
pathways.description: Dr Lakxmi Subramanian, based at Queen Mary University of London in the UK, is investigating how chromosomes segregate during cell division. Her findings could lead to the development of effective treatment strategies against disorders caused by chromosome mis-segregation.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the importance of studying chromosomes and their accurate segregation during cell division.
Explore the research questions in biochemistry related to the formation and function of centromeres and the epigenetic inheritance of these regions.
Describe the interdisciplinary nature of biochemistry, which combines biology and chemistry, and understand its significance in the study of the molecular basis of life.

pathways.title: UniProt Tutorial Series
pathways.description: In this tutorial series from the Teaching the Genome Generation™ (TtGG) program at The Jackson Laboratory, learn to use the Universal Protein Resource (UniProt) to search for information about a protein of interest. For practice applying these techniques, check out the TtGG Sequence Comparison & Identity module.
learning_objectives: Search UniProt for a protein of interest.
Locate information about protein function in a UniProtKB entry.

pathways.title: Genes and Proteins in Action
pathways.description: DNA carries genetic instructions for building proteins, the molecules essential for life. Explore how genetic information is stored, inherited, and used to build proteins. Learn how DNA, RNA, and codons guide the processes of transcription and translation, forming the molecular basis of heredity and passing traits from parents to offspring.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define nucleic acids and describe the structure and function of DNA and RNA.
Explain the steps of protein synthesis, including the roles of DNA, mRNA, tRNA, codons, transcription, and translation.
Describe the principles of heredity, including how traits are inherited through genes, using examples from Mendel’s experiments.

pathways.title: Biodiversity and Humans: How Can Humans Prevent The Reduction Of Species Due To Climate Change?
pathways.description:
Explore the importance of biodiversity with this 5-day (235 minute) pathway using videos, texts, interactives, and a student guide. Analyze biodiversity data, and human impacts, and propose solutions to protect ecosystems.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 10-15 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3: 60-70 minutes, item 2 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3: 20 minutes, item 3 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4: 45-60 minutes items 4-5 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 30 minutes, item 6 of the pathway.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will brainstorm and describe the benefits that humans receive from ecosystems with high biodiversity.
Analyze and interpret data on biodiversity and human impacts in regions on Earth.
Propose a solution to prevent the reduction of biodiversity using textual evidence.

pathways.title: Cancer Fighting Proteins
pathways.description: This pathway investigates the driving question: “How does protein structure determine its function?” Throughout the pathway, students investigate how peptides (small proteins) derived from nature are being studied as possible treatments for cancer and other rare diseases.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will understand how nature-sourced peptides are candidates for cancer treatments.
Recognize how researching and understanding proteins can help us find treatments for diseases like cancer.

pathways.title: Can We Engineer Bacteria to Regenerate Tissues?
pathways.description: Dr Christopher Contag, a biomedical engineer and microbiologist at Michigan State University in the US, is creating engineered endosymbionts – bacterial cells that can be delivered into cells of other organisms where they can persist and control cellular functions. Since these engineered endosymbionts have the potential to guide the regeneration of our organs and tissues, Chris hopes his work will help to rebuild tissues in patients with damaged or diseased organs.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will be able to define and explain the concepts of symbiosis, endosymbionts, eukaryotes, and prokaryotes in the context of biomedical engineering.
Learners will gain an appreciation for the importance of mathematics and quantitative sciences in biomedical engineering, reinforcing the relevance of these subjects in real-world applications.
Learners will gain insights into the future of biomedical engineering, with a focus on the potential of genetic modification and stem cells in tissue regeneration and organ transplantation.

pathways.title: Deep Multilayer Perceptrons (MLP)
pathways.description: This pathway discusses Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) and their mathematical and practical applications. The learners will explore various MLP architectures, including deep and wide models, and how they relate to the transfer function used. Learners will also learn about the crucial role of initialization and analyze two methods for smart initialization from a mathematical perspective. Check out Tutorial 2 on the Neuromatch website.
learning_objectives: You will contrast deep linear networks with shallow linear networks.
You will describe the different architectures of deep Multilayer perceptrons.
You will explain the importance of initialization in deep learning

pathways.title: Validating Models in Data Science
pathways.description: In data-rich scientific and technical fields, validating models is essential for ensuring reliable and meaningful results. This pathway introduces key techniques used to evaluate and refine data models, including logistic regression, k-means and DBSCAN clustering, confusion matrices, cross-validation, and sensitivity analysis.
learning_objectives: Construct and interpret confusion matrices for both binary and multiclass classification problems, using them to compare actual and predicted outcomes and identify true/false positives and negatives.
Compute and interpret classification metrics—including accuracy, precision, recall, and the F1 score—to assess the performance and reliability of supervised learning models.

Compare the performance of different models using a range of validation metrics such as cross-validation scores, confusion matrices, AIC/BIC, and sensitivity analysis to make evidence-based decisions about model selection.

pathways.title: Evidence of Common Ancestry and Diversity: How Are Humans Related to Other Animals in the Kingdom?
pathways.description:
Explore how phylogenetic trees reveal evolutionary relationships with this 8-day (320-345 minutes) pathway using texts, videos, simulations, and a student guide. Analyze patterns and evidence to create and interpret phylogenetic trees.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40-60 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-4: 120 minutes, items 2-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4-6: 90 minutes, found in the student guide.
• EXPLAIN: Day 7: 40-45 minutes, found in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 8: 30 minutes, item 5 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how and why phylogenetic trees are made.
Present information on types of evidence collected to determine common ancestors.
Analyze patterns (genes, anatomical structures, etc.) in groups of animals to create a phylogenetic tree.

pathways.title: Deep Linear Neural Networks
pathways.description: This pathway is an introduction to advanced deep linear neural networks, covering topics such as their implementation and learning dynamics. It also delves into the use of singular value decomposition for understanding these dynamics, as well as the use of representational similarity analysis for analyzing neural representations. The concept of illusory correlation and its potential impact on deep linear neural networks is also discussed, along with strategies for mitigating these biases. Check out Tutorial 3  on the Neuromatch website.
learning_objectives: You will be able to explain how representation learning is used in deep linear neural networks.
You describe dynamics and singular value decomposition
You will explain representational similarity analysis
You will examine illusory correlations using a toy hierarchy.

pathways.title: Learning Hyperparameters
pathways.description: This pathway will introduce hyperparameters and teach you how to navigate the training landscape of shallow and deep neural networks. You will explore the effect of depth on network performance and learn how to optimize the learning rate and initialization of your models. Check out Tutorial 2 on the Neuromatch website.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will have gained knowledge of the training landscape.
You will describe the effect of depth on neural networks.
You will be able to determine the learning rate of neural networks.
You will discuss the importance of initialization in training deep neural networks.

pathways.title: Water Properties Questions & Answers
pathways.description: Water is everywhere, from huge oceans to invisible water molecules making up water vapor in the air. Of course you can see and feel the physical properties of water, but there are also many chemical, electrical, and atomic-scale properties of water that affect all life and substances on Earth.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Gaining a clear understanding of the properties of water
Exploring aspects that can affect water quality

pathways.title: Growth and Development of Organisms: How Do Human Offspring Grow and Develop Into Healthy Adults?
pathways.description:
Explore how reproduction sustains life with this 6-day (205-245 minutes) pathway using timelines, videos, texts, simulations, and a student guide. Model human fertilization, analyze infertility impacts, and discuss sexual selection's role in offspring success.




Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45-55 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 40 minutes, items 3-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3-4, 60-80 minutes, item 5 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 20-30 minutes, found in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 40 minutes, item 6 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to differentiate between the types of reproduction.
Create a timeline on human fertilization and development and explain how infertility impacts these processes.
Discuss how sexual selection is influenced by animal behavior/appearance to produce successful offspring.

pathways.title: Exponents & Radicals
pathways.description: This pathway tackles the concept of exponents and radicals by explaining how to approach different types of exponent calculations.
learning_objectives: Solve exponent calculations using the exponent rules.
Simplify exponential expressions.

pathways.title: Cassandra Extavour, PhD - Developmental Biologist
pathways.description: Dr. Cassandra Extavour is a singing, dancing Harvard developmental biologist who wants to understand where we come from. She studies crickets, spiders, and other insects to better understand how some of our important cells work.

pathways.title: The Muscular System
pathways.description: This pathway uses videos to explain the contraction of skeletal muscles.

pathways.title: Foundations of Atomic Theory: Exploring the Structure, Experiments, and Quantum Principles of the Atom
pathways.description: In this pathway, students will explore the fundamentals of atomic structure, including key theories about atoms and subatomic particles. From Dalton’s atomic theory, which introduced atoms as the smallest units of matter, to the contributions of J.J. Thomson, Rutherford, and Bohr, learners will gain a deep understanding of how atoms form compounds and the principles of quantum mechanics.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be ale to understand the fundamentals of atomic structure, including the roles of protons, neutrons, and electrons in defining elements and their properties.
Describe the development of atomic theory, including Dalton’s theory, its limitations, and contributions from J.J. Thomson, Rutherford, and Bohr.
Analyze the outcomes of key experiments, such as Rutherford’s alpha scattering and J.J. Thomson’s cathode ray tube experiments, and their implications for atomic models.
Identify and represent atoms, molecules, and ions, using symbols and formulas, and explain their significance as the building blocks of matter.
Apply concepts of quantization in atomic models, understanding Bohr's model and the principles of energy levels, electron transitions, and the emission spectrum of hydrogen.

pathways.title: Oceans
pathways.description: Unit 3 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. Oceans cover three-quarters of the Earth's surface, but many parts of the deep oceans have yet to be explored. Learn about the large-scale ocean circulation patterns that help to regulate temperatures and weather patterns on land, and the microscopic marine organisms that form the base of marine food webs.

pathways.title: Linear Momentum and Collisions
pathways.description: This pathway explores the concept of momentum and the relationship between momentum and force (both vector quantities) applied over a time interval. The pathway also discusses the interaction of objects (like a glass and the floor or two automobiles) results in forces, which in turn result in changes in the momentum of each object. At the same time, it is shown how the law of momentum conservation can be applied to a system to help determine the outcome of a collision.
learning_objectives: Define linear momentum.
Explain the relationship between linear momentum and force.
Describe newton’s second law of motion in terms of linear momentum.
Calculate linear momentum given mass and velocity.
Define impulse.
Describe effects of impulses in everyday life.
Define and determine the average effective force using graphical representation.
Calculate average force and impulse given mass, velocity, and time.
Define and derive an expression for the conservation of momentum.
Describe the law of conservation of linear momentum.
Explain conservation of momentum with examples.
Explain the law of conservation of momentum as it relates to atomic and subatomic particles.
Define and derive an expression for conservation of internal kinetic energy in a one-dimensional collision.
Define and determine the final velocities in an elastic collision given masses and initial velocities.
Describe an elastic collision of two objects in one dimension.
Define internal kinetic energy.
Define inelastic collision.
Explain perfectly inelastic collisions.
Describe and apply an understanding of collisions to sports.
Define and determine recoil velocity and loss in kinetic energy given mass and initial velocity.
Define and derive an expression for conservation of momentum along the x-axis and y-axis.
Define and determine the magnitude and direction of the final velocity given initial velocity and scattering angle.
Discuss two-dimensional collisions as an extension of one-dimensional analysis.
Define point masses.
Describe elastic collisions of two objects with equal mass.
Describe newton’s third law of motion.
Explain the principle involved in propulsion of rockets and jet engines.
Define and derive an expression for the acceleration of the rocket.
Discuss the factors that affect the rocket’s acceleration.
Describe the function of a space shuttle.

pathways.title: The Circulatory System
pathways.description: Most animals are complex multicellular organisms that require a mechanism for transporting nutrients throughout their bodies and removing waste products. The circulatory system has evolved over time from simple diffusion through cells in the early evolution of animals to a complex network of blood vessels that reach all parts of the human body. This extensive network supplies the cells, tissues, and organs with oxygen and nutrients, and removes carbon dioxide and waste, which are byproducts of respiration. At the core of the human circulatory system is the heart. This pathway provides an in-depth look at an overview of the circulatory system, components of the blood, mammalian heart & blood vessels, and blood flow & blood pressure regulation. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe an open and closed circulatory system.
Describe interstitial fluid and hemolymph.
Compare/contrast the organization and evolution of the vertebrate circulatory system.
List the basic components of the blood.
Compare/contrast red and white blood cells.
Describe blood plasma and serum.
Describe the structure of the heart and explain how cardiac muscle is different from other muscles.
Describe the cardiac cycle.
Explain the structure of arteries, veins, and capillaries, and how blood flows through the body.
Describe the system of blood flow through the body.
Describe how blood pressure is regulated.

pathways.title: Building Blocks and States of Matter
pathways.description: Enhance your scientific literacy by grasping the basics of chemistry with atoms, molecules, matter states, and energy interactions. This pathway equips you to investigate smoke's origin without fire, applying principles to real-world applications with the comprehensive Where There Is Smoke...Is There Always Fire? learning pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will be able to identify and describe the basic structure of atoms, the concept of elements, and how atoms bond to form molecules, recognizing the unique properties that result from these molecular structures.
You will be able to differentiate between the three states of matter and describe their characteristics.
You will be able to explain the characteristic behaviors of molecules in each state and how these behaviors relate to phenomena such as smoke production.
You will be able to explain the process of chemical reactions, demonstrating how atoms rearrange to create new substances with distinct properties, and identifying the signs of a chemical reaction.

You will be able to  articulate the principles of potential, kinetic, and thermal energy, applying their understanding to how energy is conserved and transferred between objects or systems in various scenarios.
You  will be able to create models to represent atoms combining to form molecules.

pathways.title: Photosynthesis
pathways.description: This pathway explores photosynthesis, the remarkable process plants use to convert sunlight into chemical energy to fuel their growth and survival. Powered by sunlight and facilitated by chloroplasts in plant leaves, photosynthesis begins when light energy splits water molecules into oxygen and hydrogen. The oxygen is released into the air, supporting life on Earth, while the hydrogen drives the transformation of carbon dioxide into glucose, the primary source of energy and growth for plants. This essential process underpins the energy cycle of nearly all living organisms on our planet.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define the term "photosynthesis" and describe its significance in converting solar energy into chemical energy.
Identify and describe the main structures involved in photosynthesis, including chloroplasts, chlorophyll, and leaf anatomy.
Write and interpret the balanced chemical equation for photosynthesis.
Explain the importance of photosynthesis for sustaining life on Earth and its role in supporting other living organisms.
Identify the substrates (reactants) and products of photosynthesis, and describe their roles in the process.

pathways.title: Genetics and Evolution Through Simulations
pathways.description: Discover how the Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium helps geneticists determine whether a population is evolving or in equilibrium. Through simulations, students will explore genetic drift, natural selection, mutations, and inheritance patterns, gaining insights into evolution and population dynamics.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium and how it predicts genetic stability or evolution within populations.
Analyze genetic crossing patterns, including monohybrid and dihybrid crosses, and predict inheritance outcomes using simulations.
Explain the role of mutations and how they contribute to genetic variation and evolutionary processes.
Examine the mechanisms of natural selection, genetic drift, and migration in shaping population genetics.
Use simulations to investigate the effects of population size, environmental factors, and selection on gene frequency.
Understand the principles of gene editing through CRISPR-Cas9 and explore its applications in modern genetics.
Evaluate evolutionary processes through the study of allele frequencies and the impact of different selection pressures on species.

pathways.title: Gene Regulation
pathways.description:
This AP Biology pathway explores how gene expression is regulated to produce diverse traits in cells and organisms. Learners will investigate the types of interactions that control gene expression, including regulatory DNA sequences, transcription factors, and epigenetic modifications. They will examine how the location of regulatory sequences influences their function in both prokaryotic operons and eukaryotic gene networks. Through real-world examples, learners will examine how binding of transcription factors to promoters can activate or inhibit gene expression, and how these processes lead to differential gene expression, cell differentiation, and phenotypic diversity.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe the different types of interactions that control gene expression.
Explain how the position of regulatory sequences influences their function.
Explain how transcription factors binding to promoter regions affect gene expression and/or an organism’s phenotype.
Analyze the connection between the regulation of gene expression and phenotypic differences in cells and organisms.

pathways.title: Transpiration and Water Movement in Plants
pathways.description: Amazingly, giant trees are able to absorb water from the soil and transport it to their leaves against gravity. Explore how this works by following the process of transpiration which enables the movement of water across water potential gradients.
learning_objectives: Describe the process of transpiration.
Explain the role of water potential in determining the direction of water movement within plants.
Illustrate how cohesion and adhesion enable the formation of a continuous water column in the xylem.
Analyze how differences in water potential between the soil, plant tissues, and the atmosphere drive transpiration.
Use a potometer to measure the rate of transpiration.
Interpret data from instruments such as the LI-COR to assess photosynthesis and water loss.

pathways.title: Last Digits
pathways.description: This pathway explains the simple math skills needed for learners to find the last digit of any large number.
learning_objectives: Describe what last digits or unit digits are.
Calculate the last digit of a power.
Predict the last digit of any given number.

pathways.title: The Respiratory System
pathways.description: This pathway uses videos to explain changes in the body when breathing and how oxygen enters the body to be transported by hemoglobin.

pathways.title: The Respiratory System
pathways.description: Breathing is an involuntary event. How often a breath is taken and how much air is inhaled or exhaled are tightly regulated by the respiratory center in the brain. Humans, when they aren’t exerting themselves, breathe approximately 15 times per minute on average. Canines, like the dog in Figure 39.1, have a respiratory rate of about 15–30 breaths per minute. With every inhalation, air fills the lungs, and with every exhalation, air rushes back out. That air is doing more than just inflating and deflating the lungs in the chest cavity. This pathway provides an in-depth look at systems of gas exchange, gas exchange across respiratory surfaces, breathing, and the transport of gases in human bodily fluids. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the passage of air from the outside environment to the lungs.
Explain how the lungs are protected from particulate matter.
Name and describe lung volumes and capacities.
Identify and understand how gas pressure influences how gases move into and out of the body.
Describe how the structures of the lungs and thoracic cavity control the mechanics of breathing.
Explain the importance of compliance and resistance in the lungs.
Discuss problems that may arise due to a v/q mismatch.
Describe how oxygen is bound to hemoglobin and transported to body tissues.
Explain how carbon dioxide is transported from body tissues to the lungs.

pathways.title: Where Is Biodiversity Found?
pathways.description: Where does biodiversity bloom? Find out as you journey through Earth's biodiversity hotspots with this pathway. Featuring case studies from California to Sao Tomé, it explains unique ecosystems, endemic species, and conservation challenges. Explore species origins, adaptations, and human impacts on biodiversity, and engage with interactive assessments to deepen your understanding of life's intricate distribution patterns. For more content about biodiversity, take the Biodiversity Course.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define the term "biodiversity hotspot." and identify the criteria used to designate an area as a biodiversity hotspot.
You will describe the three main factors influencing the distribution of life on Earth: physical, biotic, and evolutionary factors.
You will develop an understanding of how different species adapt to their unique environments, recognizing the adaptive mechanisms that drive species' survival in various ecosystems.
You will evaluate the impact of human activities on extreme ecosystems, considering potential threats and conservation measures.

pathways.title: Experimental Studies of Gene Structure Using Bacteriophages
pathways.description: This pathway explores the fascinating world of bacteriophage T4, a key model in molecular biology research. You’ll gain experience with lab techniques that are used to study the life cycle of this virus, from how it attaches to cells and replicates to how it spreads. By the end, you’ll understand important methods for studying viruses and have gained practical skills to contribute to virology research.
learning_objectives: Understand the bacteriophage T4 life cycle: Explain key stages of the T4 life cycle, including attachment, penetration, replication, and release, and their significance in molecular biology.
Master assay techniques for viral study: Perform and analyze common assays used to investigate bacteriophage dynamics, gaining practical skills applicable to virology research.

pathways.title: Electromagnetic Induction, AC Circuits, and Electrical Technologies
pathways.description: This pathway illustrates how electric and magnetic changes can take place in a system due to interactions with other systems. The symmetry between electricity and magnetism is elaborated upon. Specifically, we know that a current creates a magnetic field. If nature is symmetric in this case, then perhaps a magnetic field can create a current. The basic process of generating EMFs (electromotive forces), and hence currents, with magnetic fields is known as induction; this process is also called “magnetic induction” to distinguish it from charging by induction, which utilizes the Coulomb force. Explanations and examples in this pathway support the understanding of current induction via magnetic interactions in mechanical systems and how the behavior of AC circuits depends strongly on the effect of magnetic fields on currents.
learning_objectives: Calculate the flux of a uniform magnetic field through a loop of arbitrary orientation.
Describe methods to produce an electromotive force (emf) with a magnetic field or a magnet and a loop of wire.
Calculate emf, current, and magnetic fields using faraday’s law.
Explain the physical results of lenz’s law.
Calculate emf, force, magnetic field, and work due to the motion of an object in a magnetic field.
Explain the magnitude and direction of an induced eddy current, and the effect this will have on the object it is induced in..
Describe several applications of magnetic damping.
Calculate the emf induced in a generator.
Calculate the peak emf that can be induced in a particular generator system.
Explain what back emf is and how it is induced.
Explain how a transformer works.
Calculate voltage, current, and/or number of turns given the other quantities.
Explain how various modern safety features in electric circuits work, with an emphasis on how induction is employed.
Calculate the inductance of an inductor.
Calculate the energy stored in an inductor.
Calculate the emf generated in an inductor.
Calculate the current in an rl circuit after a specified number of characteristic time steps.
Calculate the characteristic time of an rl circuit.
Diagram or sketch the current in an rl circuit over time.
Diagram or sketch voltage and current versus time in simple inductive, capacitive, and resistive circuits.
Calculate inductive and capacitive reactance.
Calculate current and/or voltage in simple inductive, capacitive, and resistive circuits.
Calculate the impedance, phase angle, resonant frequency, power, power factor, voltage, and/or current in an rlc series circuit.
Illustrate or draw the circuit diagram for an rlc series circuit.
Explain the significance of the resonant frequency.

pathways.title: It's Not NOT Rocket Science
pathways.description: This pathway explores some of the physics that enables us to send a rocket from Earth to Mars and the new technological developments that will be employed.
learning_objectives: Describe the main phases of the rocket's journey to mars.
Explain the techniques used to launch the rocket, put it on an orbit to mars, and slow down once it arrives at the red planet.
Examine some of the big challenges to sending a rocket to mars.

pathways.title: Metacognition: How to Study Human Biology
pathways.description: This pathway is designed to help students study effectively and retain the maximum amount of information and understand concepts.
learning_objectives: Plan out your learning strategy before you start studying.
Develop skills that will make you a more effective learner.
Test yourself to see whether the learning process has been effective.

pathways.title: Special Relativity
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the theory of special relativity, which was first described by Albert Einstein in the year 1905. The pathway opens with a discussion of Einstein’s postulates that form the basis of special relativity and also explains an essential physics framework that is used to describe the observations and measurements made by an observer in what is called the “inertial frame of reference”.
learning_objectives: Identify and explain both of einstein’s postulates.
Explain what an inertial frame of reference is.
Describe one way the speed of light can be changed.
Describe simultaneity.
Describe time dilation.
Calculate γ.
Compare/contrast proper time and the observer’s measured time.
Explain why the twin paradox is a false paradox.
Describe proper length.
Calculate length contraction.
Explain why we do not notice these effects at everyday scales.
Calculate relativistic velocity addition.
Explain when relativistic velocity addition should be used instead of classical addition of velocities.
Calculate relativistic doppler shift.
Calculate relativistic momentum.
Explain why the only mass it makes sense to talk about is rest mass.
Identify and compute the total energy of a relativistic object.
Identify and compute the kinetic energy of a relativistic object.
Describe rest energy, and explain how it can be converted to other forms.
Explain why objects with mass cannot travel at c, the speed of light.

pathways.title: The Search for Life
pathways.description: This pathway explores some of the ways that astronomers determine whether life exists on a planet, and how NASA's Mars mission is attempting to detect life on the Red Planet.
learning_objectives: List some of the key features of life.
Discuss the recent history of the search for life in the universe.
Explain the goals and techniques used in the current search for life on mars.

pathways.title: Human Impact on Ocean Ecosystems
pathways.description:
Dive into the deep blue and gain an in-depth understanding of ocean ecosystems with this pathway and accompanying student guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (190-210 minutes) and focuses on the impact of human activities on ocean ecosystems, global climate change, and possible sustainable solutions through a series of videos, texts, simulations, and interactives.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 40-60 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 9-11 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 40 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: 10-15 minutes, items 12-13 of the pathway.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to collect and analyze data about the human impact on marine ecosystems.
Students will be able to demonstrate an understanding of possible solutions to correct the negative impacts.
Students  will be able to engage in virtual experiments and collaborative discussions to enhance critical thinking skills and explore the relationships between humans and environmental impacts.
Students will be able to engage with related career exploration and ask relevant questions.

pathways.title: Multiple Genes, One Phenotype
pathways.description: This pathway examines different types of interactions between genes at different loci that can contribute to the determination of a single phenotypic characteristic, specifically additive gene interactions and epistatic gene interactions.
learning_objectives: Describe how genes at different loci contribute to the determination of a single phenotypic characteristic in a gene interaction.
Explain how an additive gene interaction between 2 genes controlling a single characteristic can produce novel phenotypes.
Explain how the expression of one gene can be masked by an epistatic gene at a different locus.
Predict the result of a genetic cross involving a gene interaction.

pathways.title: The Water Cycle
pathways.description: The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Explore how different components of the water cycle contribute to and affect the water cycle.
learning_objectives: Define the water cycle
Recognize the components of the water cycle and how they contribute to the water cycle
Define the stages of the water cycle
Understand the effect humans can have on the water cycle
Explore the effect of climate change on the water cycle

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Acellular Pathogens
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of viruses; the viral life cycle;  isolation, culture, & identification of viruses and viroids, virusoids, & prions.

pathways.title: Soil and Plant Nutrition
pathways.description: Cucurbitaceae is a family of plants first cultivated in Mesoamerica, although several species are native to North America. The family includes many edible species, such as squash and pumpkin, as well as inedible gourds. In order to grow and develop into mature, fruit-bearing plants, many requirements must be met and events must be coordinated. Seeds must germinate under the right conditions in the soil; therefore, temperature, moisture, and soil quality are important factors that play a role in germination and seedling development. Soil quality and climate are significant to plant distribution and growth. This pathway provides an in-depth look at nutritional requirements of plants, the soil, and nutritional adaptations of plants. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe how plants obtain nutrients.
List the elements and compounds required for proper plant nutrition.
Describe an essential nutrient.
Describe how soils are formed.
Explain soil composition.
Describe a soil profile.
Identify the nutritional adaptations of plants.
Describe mycorrhizae.
Explain nitrogen fixation.

pathways.title: Understanding Climate Change
pathways.description: Through interactive learning experiences, students will become climate-savvy changemakers. They'll dig into the science of climate impacts, connect with peers worldwide, and use data to spark innovative solutions that make a real difference in addressing our planet's environmental challenges.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, students will be able to define and differentiate between weather and climate, using examples to illustrate the differences.
Analyze and interpret how climate change is impacting their local community, using real-world data and observations.
Use data to predict future impacts of climate change, demonstrating their understanding of scientific modeling and forecasting.
Identify and evaluate actions that individuals, organizations, and governments can take to address the climate crisis.
Design and implement a climate action plan, incorporating feedback and collaboration, to understand and address the causes and effects of climate change.

pathways.title: Cell Division in Plants
pathways.description: Explore the 2 main types of cell division—mitosis and meiosis—to learn more about how plants grow and reproduce.
learning_objectives: Identify the different phases of mitosis in microscope images of onion root tips.
Identify the different phases of meiosis in microscope images of  flower anthers.
Compare and contrast mitosis and meiosis.

pathways.title: Let’s Experiment
pathways.description: Discover the power of the scientific method through the lens of photosynthesis and ecology! This pathway guides you through virtual experiments, qualitative tests, and ecological principles, helping you to analyze energy flow in living systems. Engage with interactive activities and deepen your understanding of experimentation and data interpretation in biology.
learning_objectives: Apply the steps of the scientific method to a photosynthesis and cellular respiration experiment.
Predict the results of the photosynthesis and cellular respiration experiment.
Analyze the data gathered from the photosynthesis and cellular respiration experiment.

pathways.title: Working toward Health Equity
learning_objectives: Define health equity and its associated features.
Recognize the importance of health equity in a society.
Identify successful examples of past and present health equity interventions.
Examine how different parts of society can work toward health equity.

pathways.title: Space Radiation
pathways.description: Human space exploration will expose astronauts to space radiation. Space radiation consists of Solar Particle Events and Galactic Cosmic rays which are a significant risk to human health. This pathway discusses some of the different physiological effects of space radiation and research into protecting astronauts from these effects.
learning_objectives: Develop an understanding of the inhospitable nature of space.
Examine how scientists protect astronauts from radiation in space.

pathways.title: Digestive System: Anatomy, Health, and Nutritional Balance
pathways.description: Explore the digestive system through this learning pathway, covering digestion, nutrient metabolism, gut microbiota, and careers in dietetics and food science. This comprehensive collection includes videos, readings, biographical pieces, and a case study. Learn about digestive organ structure and function, mechanical and chemical digestion, and nutrient absorption. Evaluate diet's role in digestive health, analyze disorders like GERD, IBS, and celiac disease, and discover careers in dietetics, nutrition science, and food technology.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and function of the organs involved in digestion, absorption, and nutrient metabolism.
Explain the process of digestion, including mechanical and chemical digestion, and nutrient absorption.
Evaluate the role of diet and nutrition in maintaining digestive health and preventing digestive disorders.
Analyze common digestive disorders, such as gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), and celiac disease.
Demonstrate the ability to assess nutritional status and provide dietary recommendations for promoting digestive health.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: Evidence for Evolution
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores the wide range of evidence scientists use to understand evolution and the shared ancestry of life on Earth. Learners will examine how molecular, morphological, geological, and fossil data reveal patterns of change over time and provide support for common ancestry across all domains of life. They will investigate the conserved cellular and molecular features shared by all organisms, as well as the specific structural traits, such as membrane-bound organelles, linear chromosomes, and introns, that unite all eukaryotes. Learners will also analyze how evolution continues in modern species, including antibiotic resistance. Finally, they will explore how evolutionary relationships are inferred using multiple lines of evidence and how phylogenetic trees and cladograms can be used to represent relatedness, common ancestry, and speciation.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe the different types of evidence that support the theory of evolution, including molecular, cellular, anatomical, and fossil data.
Explain how shared molecular and cellular features across all domains of life provide evidence of common ancestry.
Analyze the structural and functional evidence which shows that all eukaryotes share a common ancestor.
Explain how evolution is an ongoing process observed in all living organisms.
Describe how evolutionary relationships can be inferred using various types of evidence and how phylogenetic trees or cladograms represent those relationships.

pathways.title: Acids, bases, and pH of solutions.
pathways.description: This lesson aims to introduce students to the fundamental concepts of acids, bases, and pH. Through practical demonstrations and experiments, students will learn to measure and interpret the pH levels of various solutions, understand the behavior of acids and bases in aqueous solutions, and apply this knowledge in practical contexts. Discussions and activities will focus on the impact of acids and bases on the environment and human health, as well as making informed decisions in using everyday products based on their pH level.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to identify and classify acids and bases as distinct classes of inorganic compounds.
Students will measure the pH level of various everyday products and interpret the results to determine their degree of acidity or alkalinity.
Students will analyze the behavior of acids and bases in different contexts and evaluate their impact on the environment and human health.
Students will apply the knowledge gained to conduct practical experiments and make informed decisions regarding product usage based on their pH level.
Students will understand fundamental concepts related to pH and communicate the results of their experiments and their conclusions clearly and coherently.

pathways.title: The Structure of the DNA
pathways.description: In order to understand the biological function of DNA, you first need to understand its molecular structure. This requires learning the vocabulary for talking about the building blocks of DNA, and how these building blocks are assembled to make DNA molecules.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define DNA and its role as the genetic material in living organisms.
Identify the components of a nucleotide, including the sugar, phosphate group, and nitrogenous bases.
Explain the concept of complementary base pairing and its significance in DNA structure.
Outline the relationship between genes, DNA, and chromosomes.

pathways.title: DNA: Structure and Function
pathways.description: Do you know the molecule which is the blueprint of life? Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) is the blueprint of life because it contains instructions for organisms to grow, develop, reproduce, and survive. Explore the structure, function, and replication of DNA, from nucleotide composition to enzymatic processes like helicase and DNA polymerase. Learn how DNA replication ensures genetic continuity and discover lab techniques for DNA extraction, essential for understanding heredity and cell division.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and composition of DNA, including nucleotides, base pairing, and the double-helix model.
Explain the functions of DNA in heredity, genetic continuity, and protein synthesis.
Analyze the process of DNA replication, including the role of enzymes such as helicase, primase, and DNA polymerase.
Demonstrate the key steps of DNA extraction, applying laboratory techniques to isolate and purify DNA.
Discuss the significance of DNA extraction in medical research, biotechnology, and forensic science.
Compare DNA and RNA, highlighting structural differences, nitrogenous bases, and their respective roles in cells.

pathways.title: Your Complex Brain
pathways.description: The human brain is a complex network of neurons that define its structure and function and influence the entire body. This neural network adapts and evolves, molding our life experiences. In this pathway, we will explore how different sensory stimuli can affect the brain's development over time, and maintain healthy brain function. We will delve into the myriad of neurons present in the human brain, the importance of their connectivity, the evolution of the brain, and the impact of diseases and aging on brain circuits.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will know the number of neurons in the human brain.
