You will understand that brain function arises from connectivity.
You will understand how the brain evolved within the animal kingdom.
You will understand how disease and aging change brain circuits.

pathways.title: Endocrine Systems
pathways.description: The endocrine system controls metabolism, growth, and homeostasis through hormone secretion and gland interactions. This pathway explores the thyroid, hypothalamus, and adrenal glands, emphasizing hormonal regulation and physiological balance.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify and describe the structure and functions of major endocrine glands, including the hypothalamus, pituitary, thyroid, adrenal glands, pancreas, and gonads.
Differentiate between endocrine and exocrine glands, explaining their modes of secretion and physiological roles.
Explain hormone regulation and feedback mechanisms, including the role of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis and the negative feedback loop in maintaining homeostasis.
Analyze the impact of hormonal imbalances and deficiency diseases, such as hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, and iodine deficiency, on human health.
Evaluate the role of hormones in metabolism, growth, and reproduction, including their effects on energy regulation, development, and stress response.

pathways.title: Health
pathways.description: The future of health is difficult to predict in many ways. With the looming spectre of climate change, the world stopping global pandemic caused by COVID-19, and the first sighting of declining life expectancies among portions of American society due to rampant obesity and opioid abuse, it is strange to think that despite this global health is improving more quickly than ever before. Developments in genomics bring new possibilities of wiping out previously unstoppable genetic diseases, but also add new ethical questions and worrying implications about so-called “designer babies” and what it truly means to be human. Mobile health will enable more people than ever to gain access to healthcare regardless of income or geography, yet many in even the most wealthy nations still lack proper healthcare support. The contradictory nature of major trends in the future of health makes formulating concrete predictions of what health will look like in coming years extremely difficult, but these interviews highlight scientists who continue to try to both understand and improve human health in the face of these challenges.

pathways.title: Natural Selection
pathways.description: A detailed lesson sequence to teach Natural Selection to middle-school learners through fun activities and interactions. Within this sequence you will also find printable student worksheets for learners to use in class.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will be able to compare and contrast populations of a species, analyze their anatomical differences, and predict the reasons behind these differences, based on the principles of natural selection and adaptation to specific environments.
Demonstrate an understanding of the concept of natural selection and adaptation by designing, building, and testing an "organism" in a simulated environment.
Comprehend the significance of genetic diversity in populations and its impact on their likelihood of survival and adaptation.
Gain a comprehensive understanding of natural selection and its role in causing organisms to change over time.
Synthesize their understanding of genetic diversity and its role in natural selection by composing a well-structured mini essay.
Differentiate between natural selection and artificial selection by evaluating the design process they engaged in and justifying whether it resembles natural or artificial selection.
Conduct research on artificially selected organisms to assess their impact on the environment and human society.

pathways.title: Investigating Human Genetic Variation
pathways.description: What is race? Students learn about the role of race as an important part of social identity before examining race as a social and political construct influenced and reinforced by bias and structural racism. Our current scientific understanding of the biological basis of human genetic variation demonstrates that there are no biological features that are present in all members of one "race" group and not in others. While race is not a genetically meaningful category, it can still impact biology through the enactment of racist policies and practices - which result in inequities in areas such as healthcare.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to analyze the historical origins of hierarchical and biological ideas of race and explain how these concepts have been used to reinforce structural racism and inequity.

Articulate why race is not a genetically meaningful concept by examining the distribution of human genetic variation and demonstrating how most variation exists within, rather than between, so-called "race" groups.
Develop a personal action plan to actively address and disrupt racism in healthcare and other areas, using an understanding of the role of race and racism in perpetuating inequities.

pathways.title: Control and Regulation, An Introduction
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the concepts of control and regulation in the human body and explores these concepts by describing the features and functions of the nervous system.
learning_objectives: Identify the major features and functions of the nervous system.
Explain how the nervous system contributes to the control and regulation of the human body.

pathways.title: Genes in Action: Understanding Meiosis and Mutations
pathways.description: The resources in this pathway offer an in-depth exploration of genetics, covering topics such as sexual versus asexual reproduction, Mendel’s experiments, and the process of meiosis. Through simulation, learners will also investigate the causes and effects of mutations, including their role in genetic diseases. Through interactive modules and hands-on projects, students will gain a deeper understanding of genetic inheritance, the impact of genetic errors, and how mutations influence health and disease.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will be able to explain how the process of meiosis transmits genetic information to offspring.
Understand how the process of meiosis leads to genetic diversity.
Understand how mutations can lead to genetic diseases.

pathways.title: Muscular System: Structure, Function, and Movement
pathways.description: Unlock the secrets of the muscular system! Perfect for aspiring healthcare professionals, sports scientists, and fitness enthusiasts, this pathway dives into muscle anatomy, function, and health. From understanding muscle contraction to exploring career opportunities, you’ll gain a robust foundation in muscular science.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and function of the muscular system, including skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle tissues.
Explain the mechanisms of muscle contraction and relaxation, including the role of actin, myosin, and neurotransmitters.
Analyze the types of muscle movements and their coordination by the nervous system.
Evaluate common muscular disorders and conditions, such as muscular dystrophy, strains, and sprains.
Demonstrate an understanding of muscle anatomy and physiology through the identification of major muscles and muscle groups.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: Further Applications of Newton's Laws: Friction, Drag, and Elasticity
pathways.description: This pathway further explores applications of Newton's laws of motion. The pathway discusses examples of specific contact forces, such as friction, air or liquid drag, and elasticity that may affect the motion or shape. It also discusses the nature of forces on both macroscopic and microscopic levels of an object. Newton's laws are applied to describe the motion of an object and to examine relationships between contact forces and other forces exerted on an object. The examples in this pathway provide practice in using vector properties of forces and free-body diagrams to determine net force.
learning_objectives: Discuss the general characteristics of friction.
Describe the various types of friction.
Calculate the magnitudes of static and kinetic frictional forces.
Define drag force and model it mathematically.
Discuss the applications of drag force.
Define terminal velocity.
Calculate and find terminal velocity.
Identify and state hooke's law.
Explain hooke's law using graphical representation between deformation and applied force.
Discuss the three types of deformations such as changes in length, sideways shear, and changes in volume.
Describe with examples the young's modulus, shear modulus, and bulk modulus.
Calculate the change in length given mass, length, and radius.

pathways.title: Introduction: The Nature of Science and Physics
pathways.description: This pathway explores the history of the formal study of physics, beginning with natural philosophy and the ancient Greeks, and leading up through a review of Sir Isaac Newton and the laws of physics that bear his name. The pathway also introduces the standards scientists use when they study physical quantities and the interrelated system of measurements most of the scientific community uses to communicate in a single mathematical language. Finally, the pathway discusses the limits of our ability to be accurate and precise, and the reasons scientists go to painstaking lengths to be as clear as possible regarding their own limitations.
learning_objectives: Explain the difference between a principle and a law.
Explain the difference between a model and a theory.
Identify and perform unit conversions both in the si and english units.
Explain the most common prefixes in the si units and be able to write them in scientific notation.
Identify and determine the appropriate number of significant figures in both addition and subtraction, as well as multiplication and division calculations.
Calculate the percent uncertainty of a measurement.
Calculate reasonable approximations based on given data.

pathways.title: Go With The Flow! How Do Earth’s Systems Produce and Maintain the Changes Needed for All Organisms to Survive?
pathways.description:
Take a journey through Earth's natural cycles with this pathway and accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (180-190 minutes) and focuses on water, carbon, and rock cycles through interactive videos and readings. Understand how the sun's energy affects our planet and how everything is connected. Try out experiments and see how these cycles impact real-world issues. Plus, explore a potential STEM career related to these topics.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 30-40 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 9-11 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 10 minutes, item 12 of the pathway.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will understand the interconnectedness of the water, carbon, and rock cycles and how the sun's energy influences these processes.
Students will have an understanding of the richness of life on Earth and the strategies needed for preserving biodiversity in the face of conservation challenges.
Students will comprehend how energy moves from the sun to producers and consumers within ecosystems, and its impact on ecosystem productivity.
Students will grasp the causes and effects of climate change, learn about sustainable practices, and explore potential careers in environmental science and sustainability.

pathways.title: Environmental Health: Risks, Resilience, and Sustainability
pathways.description: Explore how pollution, climate change, and natural disasters impact health in this Environmental Risk Factors pathway. Learn how vulnerable populations, including children, face disproportionate health hazards, while examining mitigation strategies. This pathway emphasizes planetary health, sustainable development, and policy-based interventions to address environmental challenges and promote resilience in ecosystems and human health.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe environmental risk factors, such as pollution, climate change, and natural disasters, and their impact on health.
Analyze the disproportionate burden of environmental health hazards on vulnerable populations, including children and marginalized communities.
Evaluate strategies for mitigating environmental risks and promoting sustainable development practices.
Discuss the concept of planetary health and the interconnectedness of human health with ecosystem health.
Propose policy and community-based interventions to address environmental health challenges and promote resilience.

pathways.title: How Is Biodiversity Studied?
pathways.description: Embark on a journey through time and explore how researchers have documented the natural world. Discover how scientists recorded information about plants and animals. Dive into multidisciplinary field expeditions, exploring the necessary equipment and logistical support scientists require in field research to study biodiversity. Explore the impact of technology and citizen science on the future of biodiversity research with questions testing your understanding of binomial nomenclature, taxonomy, and genetic sequencing, highlighting the relevance of these concepts in biodiversity research. For more content about biodiversity, take the Biodiversity Course.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will be able to recall the historical roots of biodiversity documentation, starting with wunderkammers.
You will describe the significance of binomial nomenclature in taxonomy.
You will evaluate the significance of collaboration and open partnerships in the success of biodiversity expeditions.
You will analyze the challenges and considerations involved in planning expeditions for different environments.
You will evaluate your understanding of biodiversity expeditions, factors in expedition preparation, and species classification through self-assessment.

pathways.title: Mastering Chemical Equilibrium: Principles and Applications
pathways.description: Explore the principles of chemical equilibrium and Le Chatelier's Principle to understand how reactions achieve balance and respond to stress. This course examines their applications in industrial processes like the Haber process, biological systems such as blood buffering, and environmental science. Analyze reaction rates and predict changes in dynamic systems for real-world contexts.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able describe a reversible reaction in terms of the interconversion of products and reactants.
Explain chemical equilibrium as a dynamic state where the forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates.
Predict the effect of changes in concentration, pressure, and temperature on the equilibrium position.
Use Le Chatelier's Principle to explain how equilibrium shifts to oppose stresses introduced to a system.
Explain the role of chemical equilibrium and Le Chatelier's Principle in industrial processes, such as the Haber process, to optimize production.
Describe how compromised conditions of temperature, pressure, and catalysts are used to balance efficiency and cost in industrial reactions.
Explain how buffer systems, such as the carbonic acid-bicarbonate buffer in blood, maintain pH balance and homeostasis.
Analyze the physiological role of buffers in mitigating health risks caused by pH imbalances.
Explain how carbon monoxide poisoning can be treated using Le Chatelier's Principle.
Analyze the impact of tobacco smoke chemicals, such as nicotine and carbon monoxide, on biological systems and how equilibrium principles aid in recovery.

pathways.title: Control and Regulation 2
pathways.description: This pathway describes the functioning of the human reproductive system and the endocrine system processes involved in maintaining homeostasis.
learning_objectives: Describe how the endocrine system maintains homeostasis.
Identify the major features and functions of the human reproductive system.

pathways.title: Electromagnetism
pathways.description: Electromagnetism is the branch of physics that studies the interactions between electrically charged particles and their associated fields. It is an interaction that occurs between particles with electric charge via electromagnetic fields. The electromagnetic force is one of the four fundamental forces of nature. It is the dominant force in the interactions of atoms and molecules.
learning_objectives: To understand the fundamental concepts of electricity and magnetism
To identify applications of these principles in daily life
To define electromagnetic induction
To understand the principles of electric motors and generators
To explain the role of electromagnets

pathways.title: Astrobotany: Systems and Hardware
pathways.description: Microgravity and confined spaces are some of the challenges that make growing food in space vastly different than land-based farming. In this pathway, learn about the innovative hardware and systems used to support plant survival in space.
learning_objectives: Examine some of the technology being used to grow plants in space.

pathways.title: Pardis Sabeti, MD, PhD - Medical Geneticist
pathways.description: Dr. Pardis Sabeti is a musician, Photoshop-enthusiast, and a Professor of Immunology and Infectious Diseases at Harvard School of Public Health. She studies the genetic factors of disease to better understand how they've evolved, how people are put at risk and how people can be better protected. Dr. Sabeti uses mathematics and computers to invent brand new tools to answer important questions.

pathways.title: PolyPhen-2 Tutorial Series
pathways.description: In this tutorial series from the Teaching the Genome Generation™ (TtGG) program at The Jackson Laboratory, learn to use the Polymorphism Phenotyping v2 (PolyPhen-2) tool to predict the effect of a DNA variant on protein function. For practice applying these techniques, check out the TtGG Sequence Comparison & Identity module.
learning_objectives: Submit a query about a variant to PolyPhen-2, an online bioinformatics tool, using either a protein identifier, a SNP identifier, or a protein sequence in FASTA format
Access and interpret a PolyPhen-2 results report, including variant effect prediction results

pathways.title: Our Climate, Our Future
pathways.description: Get the real facts on climate change. Let Our Climate Our Future take you into a future that we can create and to scales so small you need to be a molecule to see what’s happening. Join us on a journey to learn why the story of climate change isn’t just about melting glaciers or disappearing polar bears, and not just about a more dangerous world for far-off future generations. Climate change is really a story about us. Our Climate Our Future is an award-winning video experience that educates young people on the science of climate change and empowers them to take action.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming

pathways.title: The Science Behind DNA Replication
pathways.description: DNA replication is the biological process by which a cell copies its DNA before cell division. It ensures that each daughter cell receives an identical copy of genetic information. In this pathway, you will study DNA replication, including enzymes such as helicase and DNA polymerase, as well as the differences between eukaryotic and prokaryotic replication, to gain an understanding of genetic inheritance and cell division.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure of DNA and its role in genetic information storage and transmission.
Explain the semi-conservative nature of DNA replication and its significance in cell division.
Outline the step-by-step process of DNA replication, including initiation, elongation, and termination.
Understand the importance of DNA replication accuracy and the role of proofreading mechanisms in preventing mutations.

pathways.title: SITN Seminar Series
pathways.description: This pathway showcases a number of different topics as part of the SITN seminar series.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Examine a number of different scientific areas and perspectives.

pathways.title: Vision and Optical Instruments
pathways.description: This pathway builds an understanding of vision and optical instruments on the idea that waves can transfer energy and momentum without the transfer of matter. The way light waves travel is addressed using both conceptual and mathematical models. Throughout this unit, the direction of this travel is manipulated through the use of instruments like microscopes and telescopes.
learning_objectives: Explain the image formation by the eye.
Explain why peripheral images lack detail and color.
Examine the accommodation of the eye for distant and near vision.
Identify and discuss common vision defects.
Explain nearsightedness and farsightedness corrections.
Explain laser vision correction.
Explain the simple theory of color vision.
Summarize the coloring properties of light sources.
Describe the retinex theory of color vision.
Describe different types of microscopes.
Explain how an image is formed in a compound microscope.
Summarize the invention of the telescope.
Describe the working of a telescope.
Describe optical aberration.

pathways.title: Circuits, Bioelectricity, and DC Instruments
pathways.description: This pathway takes the topic of electric circuits a step beyond simple circuits by addressing both changes that result from interactions between systems and constraints on such changes due to laws of conservation. When the circuit is purely resistive, everything in this pathway applies to both DC and AC. However, matters become more complex when capacitance is involved. The pathway does consider what happens when capacitors are connected to DC voltage sources. In addition, a number of important DC instruments, such as meters that measure voltage and current, are covered in this pathway. Information and examples presented in the pathway examine cause-effect relationships inherent in interactions involving electrical systems.
learning_objectives: Diagram a circuit with resistors in parallel and in series.
Use ohm’s law to calculate the voltage drop across a resistor when current passes through it.
Compare/contrast the way total resistance is calculated for resistors in series and in parallel.
Explain why total resistance of a parallel circuit is less than the smallest resistance of any of the resistors in that circuit.
Calculate total resistance of a circuit that contains a mixture of resistors connected in series and in parallel.
Compare/contrast the voltage and the electromagnetic force of an electric power source.
Describe what happens to the terminal voltage, current, and power delivered to a load as internal resistance of the voltage source increases.
Explain why it is beneficial to use more than one voltage source connected in parallel.
Examine a complex circuit using kirchhoff’s rules, applying the conventions for determining the correct signs of various terms.
Explain why a voltmeter must be connected in parallel with the circuit.
Diagram an ammeter correctly connected in a circuit.
Describe how a galvanometer can be used as either a voltmeter or an ammeter.
Calculate the resistance that must be placed in series with a galvanometer to allow it to be used as a voltmeter with a given reading.
Explain why measuring the voltage or current in a circuit can never be exact.

pathways.title: Hazard Handling in Laboratory Settings
pathways.description: Safe handling, storage, and disposal of hazardous materials are essential responsibilities in laboratory environments. In this pathway, learners explore how science and laboratory staff follow EPA and OSHA guidelines to minimize risk, including using personal protective equipment (PPE), working within regulated safety zones, and managing both chemical and electrical hazards. Discover best practices for storing chemicals such as methylene chloride, using grounded outlets and GFCIs, and inspecting lab equipment for frayed wires or insulation wear. Examine real laboratory protocols that help ensure safe practices are followed at every step.
learning_objectives: Know and disposal and recycling procedures for hazardous, flammable. and combustible materials.
Learn to handle and store hazardous materials safely, following EPA and OSHA guidelines.

Demonstrate safety practices and procedures for working with and around electrical hazards, such as using ground fault circuit interrupters (GFCIs), inspecting for frayed wiring, and understanding grounding requirements for lab equipment.

pathways.title: The ABCs of DNA
pathways.description: Prokaryotes have a single circular chromosome in a nucleoid. This pathway builds genetics and cell biology skills, including identifying cell organelles, matching plant cell types, explaining DNA as genetic material, distinguishing between chromosomes/genes/traits, describing chromatin compaction and mitosis, and connecting gene-regulation enhancers to animal multicellularity.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify major organelles in plant/animal cells and relate structure to function, using simulations and micrographs.
Distinguish plant cell types/tissues (e.g., dermal, ground, vascular) by illustration and function; match examples to roles.
Describe the differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic genomes; define chromosome, gene, allele, trait; distinguish haploid vs. diploid.
Explain chromosome organization and chromatin compaction (histones, nucleosomes, 30-nm fiber) and why compaction matters for gene expression.
Outline key experiments establishing DNA as the genetic material (e.g., Avery–MacLeod–McCarty; Hershey–Chase) and summarize the discovery timeline.
Describe the cell cycle (interphase, mitosis) and relate S-phase DNA replication to sister chromatids, centromeres, and accurate chromosome segregation.
Interpret simple karyotypes and use genotype/phenotype language to predict traits in basic cases (e.g., ABO blood group).
Compare core gene-regulatory mechanisms (histone marks, transcription-factor networks, regulatory RNAs) across unicellular relatives and animals to infer evolutionary continuity.
Explain the role of distal cis-regulatory elements (enhancers) in multicellularity and cell-type specialization; connect to animal development examples.
Practice scientific skills emphasized in the framework: using interactives/simulations, analyzing diagrams, and communicating evidence-based explanations.

pathways.title: Culturally Relevant Pedagogies
learning_objectives: Understand culturally relevant pedagogy and its components—student achievement, cultural competence, and critical consciousness.
Describe the benefits of using culturally relevant approaches in STEM classrooms.
Discover culturally relevant strategies that educators can use to promote student achievement, cultural competence, and critical consciousness in STEM lessons.

pathways.title: Cell Physiology
pathways.description: Explore how cells work, studying how materials move in and out through processes like osmosis and diffusion. Understand how the cell membrane's structure affects its functions, like letting certain materials pass through. Learn about diffusion and osmosis and their importance in cells. Perform practical virtual experiments in the lab to see these processes in action, to understand theory and practice.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will be able to state the meaning of cell physiology
Correlate membrane structure with cell physiology in relation to permeability
Differentiate between diffusion and osmosis
Carry out experiments on diffusion and osmosis

pathways.title: Understanding Atoms: Building Blocks of Matter and Their Interactions
pathways.description: Welcome to an exciting exploration into the heart of matter—atoms! In this pathway, we're delving into the fundamental building blocks of everything around us. Get ready to put on your scientific goggles as we engage in a hands-on experiment and classroom activity to build an atom from scratch.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure of the atom.
Differentiate between the terms “element“ and “atom.”
Describe the location, mass, and charge of subatomic particles.
Use the element name, mass, and charge to determine the number of protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Use the number of protons, neutrons, and electrons to draw a model of the atom, identify the element, and determine the mass and charge.
Predict how addition or subtraction of a proton, neutron, or electron will change the element, the charge, and the mass.

pathways.title: Model Organisms: Arabidopsis
pathways.description: Arabidopsis thaliana is a model plant species because of its ability to grow in a short time, in small places, self-pollinating capability, and relatively small genome. In this pathway, learn about how researchers use Arabidopsis to make research conclusions when cultivating plants in the space environment.
learning_objectives: Examine the effects of microgravity on plant growth and development.
Compare/contrast the developmental patterns of plants in space with plants on earth.

pathways.title: Untapped Potential: Ensuring a Safe Water Supply
pathways.description:
Gain a comprehensive understanding of the challenges associated with creating a safe water supply in the 21st century and how the chemical sciences, including modern chemistry, desalination, and advanced oxidation processes, are pivotal in addressing these challenges. 
This pathway includes a video and curriculum materials to support student learning, featuring the Chemistry Shorts film, Untapped Potential.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to discover how chemists are helping to secure a safe water supply by watching the video, "Untapped Potential"."
Explore the challenges facing our water supply and potential solutions to address them.
Learn about Lewis dot structures, valence electrons, and covalent bonds.
Investigate how freezing affects saltwater as a potential method for desalination.

pathways.title: Christine Ortiz, PhD - Materials Scientist
pathways.description: Dr. Christine Ortiz is a lover of sci-fi, adventure, and is also a professor and materials scientist at MIT. In her area of research she studies biological materials like fish scales and bird feathers to create new materials. She has expanded her work to focus on building a new style of college that helps students from all backgrounds get the most they can out of school.

pathways.title: Water Cycle Unit
pathways.description: Help your students to explore the interconnectedness of the water cycle, transpiration, deforestation, climate change, and extreme weather. Through hands-on experiments, research projects, and discussions, students will uncover how human activities impact these natural processes and what they can do to take positive action.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to describe the water cycle and analyze how climate change disrupts its natural processes using models and real-world examples.
Explain the role of transpiration in cooling urban environments, evaluate its connection to climate change, and develop proposals to improve tree equity in your community.
Investigate how deforestation and erosion affect the water cycle, soil quality, and ecosystems, and create cause-and-effect diagrams to demonstrate your understanding.
Analyze how climate change intensifies extreme weather events, create infographics on local impacts, and propose community-based solutions to mitigate these effects.

pathways.title: NASA | Evolution of the Early Universe
pathways.description: NASA's James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) peers into the dawn of our universe to reveal the light stemming from the Big Bang that first illuminated the cosmos we know today. Jump into this cosmic time machine as James Webb takes us to the beginning of the universe.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the James Webb Space Telescope's role in early universe exploration
Analyze the concept of photons' journey through space, their interaction with matter, and their role in providing insights into the universe's history
Define and describe the universe's "dark ages" and identify the key events and transitions that occurred during this cosmic epoch
Assess the significance of reionization in the early universe and its impact on the formation and evolution of galaxies
Apply your knowledge of the JWST's capabilities to elucidate how it can specifically study the early universe

pathways.title: Data Visualization for Science Communication
pathways.description: Visualizing an entire dataset is an act of transparency that enhances clarity and encourages critical thinking. This pathway introduces science communicators and educators to key data literacy concepts, including data visualization, infographics, cleaning, analysis, and presentation. Ideal for enhancing science communication skills, these resources blend practical strategies with real-world examples to support engaging and evidence-based storytelling.
learning_objectives: Learn to visualize and present scientific data clearly and effectively using graphs, infographics, and slide presentations tailored to specific audiences.
Develop critical thinking and data literacy skills by identifying misleading statistics and interpreting complex datasets in STEM contexts.
Apply best practices for cleaning, analyzing, and preparing real-world data to ensure accuracy, reliability, and ethical scientific communication.
Collaborate on scientific investigations by collecting, organizing, and communicating experimental data in ways that support transparency and stakeholder engagement.

pathways.title: Modern Materials and the Solid State: Crystals, Polymers, and Alloys
pathways.description: In this closing chapter, we see chemistry not only as an excellent entry point to predicting how new materials behave, but also as a continuous process of innovation and discovery.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Explain the difference between solid structures in terms of atomic interactions and order (or lack thereof) in their structure.
Identify the conditions that influence phase changes in materials.
Identify and characterize the different allotropes of carbon.
Identify the different types of alloys in terms of their constituents and behavior.
Identify the various naturally occurring and synthetic polymers.

pathways.title: Trigonometric Foundations and Applications
pathways.description: Mastering trigonometric identities like sine, cosine, and tangent is crucial for solving right-angled triangle problems. You will apply these functions and the Pythagorean theorem to calculate unknown side lengths, angles, and graph trigonometric functions, building a strong foundation for advanced geometry and pre-calculus topics.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to apply the Pythagorean theorem to calculate unknown side lengths in right-angled triangles, including identifying the hypotenuse and calculating using different side lengths.
Solve right-angled triangle problems using trigonometric ratios (sine, cosine, and tangent) to calculate unknown angles and side lengths, including applying complementary angles in triangles.
Demonstrate proficiency in solving trigonometric equations using algebraic manipulation and the Pythagorean identity to simplify complex expressions involving sine, cosine, and tangent.
Graph trigonometric functions (sine, cosine, and tangent) using the unit circle, identifying key features like periodicity, amplitude, and asymptotes.
Verify trigonometric identities by applying knowledge of basic trigonometric properties, ratios, and algebraic operations.
Analyze real-world force problems using vectors, applying trigonometric concepts to decompose forces into components and calculate the necessary conditions for motion using Newton’s second law.
Evaluate and apply pre-calculus formulas such as the quadratic formula, trigonometric identities, and vector equations to solve more complex mathematical problems in preparation for advanced topics.

pathways.title: The Power of Modeling, Simulation, and Machine Learning
pathways.description: This pathway explains to learners how advanced computational techniques are integrated into biotechnology. By completing this pathway, you will gain foundational knowledge on the application of simulations and machine learning, learn to adjust computational simulation parameters to understand epidemic spread, and master interpreting linear regressions along with their coefficients of determination.
learning_objectives: Describe the basic capabilities of simulation and machine learning and how they can be applied to biomedical research.
Manipulate the parameters of a computational simulation to draw conclusions about epidemic propagation.
Interpret a linear regression and the significance of the associated coefficient of determination.

pathways.title: Understanding the Skeletal System: From Anatomy to Function
pathways.description: Discover the human skeletal system, focusing on the axial and appendicular skeleton, joint types, and their functions. Learn how bones like the humerus, scapula, and pelvis enable movement, protect organs, and adapt to physical demands. This pathway provides insights into skeletal anatomy, joint mechanics, and developmental processes, which are essential for understanding human biology and physical health.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to recognize the major components of the axial and appendicular skeleton, including bones, joints, and their specific roles in the human body.
Analyze the structural and functional roles of bones and joints in movement, protection, and support, emphasizing their adaptations to physical demands.
Classify bones and joints based on their structure and function, and relate these to specific movements and regions of the human body.

pathways.title: Space Harvest: Building a Garden in the Stars
pathways.description:
Embark on an interstellar botany adventure with this pathway and the accompanying Student Guide! Discover how NASA grows space veggies, design your own soilless plant habitat, and explore the science that could feed astronauts on Mars and beyond. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (190-210 minutes). Get ready to grow!
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 40-60 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 9-11 of the pathway
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, (found in the Student Guide)
• EXTEND: 10 minutes, item 12 of the pathway
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to collect and analyze data about how well plants grow in soilless systems.
You will be able to demonstrate an understanding of plants' basic needs by completing simulations.
You will be able to engage in virtual experiments to enhance critical thinking skills.
You will explore the relationships between plants and human survival.
You will explore related STEM careers in the field of astrobiology, and ask relevant questions.

pathways.title: Review and Synthesis: Putting It All Together
pathways.description: This pathway examines the principles of human biology by summarising the functions of the specific organ systems and integrating the themes of human biology into a coherent understanding of how the human body works.
learning_objectives: Predict how a dysfunctional organ system will affect the rest of the body.
Describe how different body systems interact with each other.

pathways.title: Francesca Dominici, PhD - Biostatistician
pathways.description: Dr. Francesca Dominici is marathon-loving, biostatistician, and a champion of women who analyzes big data looking for patterns to improve public health. Her particular interests are in the area of pollution which can mean air quality or the level of noise in an environment.

pathways.title: Frosty Formulations: The Chemistry of Ice Cream
pathways.description:
Follow along with Dr. Maya Warren, Ice Cream Scientist, as she leads you through the chemistry of ice cream! Like bubbles colliding and connecting, the interactions between the molecular components of ice cream are responsible for the dreamy qualities of texture and mouthfeel that we all enjoy so much. Those same interactions reveal important chemistry concepts that touch our lives daily. "Frosty Formulations" is aimed at high school to early college students and can be used as a starting point for discussions around emulsions, hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity, and states of matter.
This pathway includes a video, classroom curriculum, and experiment guide from Chemistry Shorts.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: You will investigate how making ice cream is connected to chemistry.
You will learn about the three states of matter: solid, liquid, and gas.
You will learn about intermolecular interactions and how emulsifiers work.

pathways.title: Fallacies: How Can We Recognize Bad Arguments and Resist Their Pull?
pathways.description: This pathway develops the idea of background assumptions in reasoning and defines an informal fallacy as an argument that rests on a false background assumption. A variety of common examples, such as ad hominem, appeal to authority, and false dilemma, are introduced by way of the false background belief(s) that they rely upon.
learning_objectives: Define a fallacy and explain how fallacies undermine good reasoning.
Distinguish between fallacies that are purely logical in nature (formal fallacies) and fallacies that involve weak or irrelevant evidence (informal fallacies).
Recognize common fallacies, such as ad hominem, straw man, slippery slope, and so on, and identify the faulty reasoning they involve.
Apply knowledge of common fallacy types to spot questionable assumptions in everyday discourse and argumentation.

pathways.title: Anatomy & Physiology: The Nervous System
pathways.description: Explore the intricate network of the human nervous system, from the rapid-fire communication of neurons to the delicate balance of brain functions. Unravel the mysteries of nerve signals, the impact of stress, and the harmonious symphony of the autonomic system, including the vital roles of the cranial nerves. Dive into a journey of discovery about how our bodies perceive, react, and adapt to the world around us.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, explore how your nervous system, including the central and peripheral parts, controls your body and senses.
Discover how neurons communicate with electric signals, the role of ions, and the sodium-potassium pump in messaging.
Learn about synapses, where neurons send messages, and how brain chemicals can change with different substances.
Understand brain injuries, their effects on abilities, and the brain regions responsible for language, movement, and perception.
Dive into how your nerves detect pain, your body's reflexes, and how pain is processed in the brain.
Examine the automatic nervous system's two parts: one for action and one for relaxation, and their functions.
Explore the immediate effects of stress on your body, the sympathetic nervous system's response, and chronic stress impacts.
Get familiar with the nervous system's calming side and the 12 cranial nerves that help your body function.

pathways.title: Atom and Its Subatomic Particles
pathways.description: In this learning experience, students will define an atom and identify its subatomic particles. They will also engage with interactive simulation materials on atoms and their particles, demonstrating how electrons revolve around the atomic shells.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to correctly define atom.
Describe the three subatomic particles in an atom.
Explain the differences between proton, neutron, and electron.
State the characteristics of elements in the periodic table.
Play and interact with simulation exercises on atoms and its subatomic particles, and how electrons revolves round the shells of an atom.

pathways.title: The Human Skeleton System
pathways.description: The human skeleton has 206 bones, divided into the axial and appendicular skeletons, supporting movement, protection, and blood cell production. Describe the structure, function, and classification of the human skeletal system, including major bones, connective tissues, and the processes involved in bone growth, maintenance, and movement.
learning_objectives: By the end of pathway, you will be able describe the major functions of the human skeletal system, including support, movement, protection, blood cell production, and mineral storage.
Distinguish between the axial and appendicular skeletons and list the major bones found in each division.
Identify and locate major bones of the human skeleton using diagrams and anatomical terminology.
Explain the roles of ligaments, tendons, and cartilage in supporting joint movement and skeletal function.
Outline the process of bone growth, repair, and remodeling, including the roles of osteoblasts, osteoclasts, and osteocytes.

pathways.title: Magnetic Poles Mystery on the Moon: Protecting Lunar Base Equipment
pathways.description:
Become a junior space engineer and join a space adventure! Figure out how to shield important Moon base tools from weird space magnetism in this learning pathway, with the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (270 minutes) - experiment, brainstorm, and solve real-world challenges while exploring the Moon's unique magnetic field through hands-on activities and simulations.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 50 minutes. Items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 60 minutes. Items 3-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 40 minutes. Item 5 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 60 minutes. Item 6-7 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 60 minutes. Item 8 of the pathway.
Before we begin, let's gear up with "Magnetic Mysteries: Basics of Magnetism" to make sure we're all charged up to take your magnetic knowledge to the next level!
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will investigate and explain how magnetic fields influence nearby objects and materials, understanding the effects on magnetically susceptible and non-susceptible materials.
Identify characteristics of magnetic materials through experiments and hands-on activities.
Analyze the interactions between magnets and various lunar base materials, observing and recording the behaviors that result from magnetic attraction and repulsion.
Develop an understanding of how magnetic fields lead to attraction and repulsion in different materials.
Describe the unique characteristics of the Moon's magnetic field and compare it to Earth's magnetic field.
Understand why the Moon lacks a strong magnetic field due to factors like its small core, lack of convective currents, and absence of tectonic activity.
Explain the impact of the absence of a magnetic field on lunar equipment and materials.

pathways.title: Anatomy & Physiology: Sensory Systems
pathways.description: Embark on a journey through the human sensory system, exploring the intricate processes of smell, hearing, and vision, and discover how our brains interpret the world around us through case studies, anatomical analysis, and the science of perception.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you'll get to see how Olivia's story shows us what happens when someone loses their sense of smell from getting hurt. You'll discover how smelling works, how it's linked to tasting, and why being able to smell is a big deal in our lives.
Find out how our ears help us hear sounds and keep our balance. You'll learn how ears catch sound waves, turn those waves into messages for our brain, and let us hear different pitches and loudness.

Dive into how we see by looking at how the eye is put together, the part light plays, and the way our brain makes sense of what we see. We'll use cool optical illusions to show how our eyes can trick our brains.

pathways.title: Sensory System: Anatomy, Physiology, and Perception
pathways.description: Discover the anatomy and physiology of the sensory organs involved in vision, hearing, taste, smell, and touch. Sensory transduction and the role of sensory receptors in detecting stimuli are explained and students will analyze neural pathways and processing centers involved in sensory perception and integration, and evaluate some sensory disorders like when senses mix. Included are hands-on activities to demonstrate sensory physiology principles and career descriptions that contextualize research with the senses.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will be able to describe the anatomy and physiology of the sensory organs involved in vision, hearing, taste, smell, and touch.
Be able to explain the process of sensory transduction and the role of sensory receptors in detecting environmental stimuli.
Analyze the neural pathways and processing centers involved in sensory perception and integration.
Evaluate common sensory disorders and conditions, such as vision impairment, hearing loss, and anosmia.
Demonstrate an understanding of the principles of sensory physiology through hands-on activities and sensory perception experiments.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: COVID-19 Data Analysis
pathways.description: The COVID-19 pandemic is the perfect example of a case where the ways in which data are analyzed significantly influences the types of decisions made at varying levels of society. This pathway provides case studies and associated activities that aim to help the user familiarize themself with various data analysis skills.
learning_objectives: Develop skills to assess data and identify trends.
Critique the effectiveness of different covid-19 management measures.
Discuss your views on policies with class mates.

pathways.title: What Are Data and Data Science?
pathways.description: Python and R are among the most commonly used programming languages in data science, offering tools for data manipulation, statistical analysis, and visualization. This content introduces foundational data science skills—including identifying data types, using Python (Pandas, Matplotlib), analyzing datasets, and creating visualizations. This pathway is Chapter 1 of the Principles of Data Science textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the data science cycle and explain how data is collected, prepared, analyzed, managed, and reported to generate insights and support evidence-based decision-making.

Explain how data science integrates multiple disciplines and supports innovation across fields such as business, health, policy, and engineering, while addressing emerging ethical, privacy, and societal challenges.
Classify and interpret different types, structures, and formats of data in datasets to support data analysis and evidence-based reasoning across scientific and real-world contexts.
Describe how various software tools and programming languages support data manipulation, analysis, and visualization to draw evidence-based conclusions and communicate scientific information.
Use Python to load, analyze, and visualize data, applying computational tools and mathematical thinking to identify patterns and communicate evidence-based findings from large datasets.

pathways.title: A One-Dimensional Journey: Learning the Parameters of Kinematics
pathways.description:
This learning pathway offers a complete immersion in the concepts of kinematics related to one-dimensional motion. Through informative texts, interactive simulations, and challenging questions, students will be guided on an educational journey that will allow them to deeply understand the fundamental principles of kinematics and their application in the analysis of one-dimensional motion.
Worksheets to use with the simulations included on the pathway:
For "Vector addition": Vectors
For "Equilibrium forces": Equilibrium of forces
For "The moving man": Moving man
learning_objectives: Identify and define the fundamental concepts of kinematics in one-dimensional motion.
Apply the concepts of kinematics learned through solving questions and problems related to one-dimensional motion.
Use formulas and principles to calculate and predict position, velocity, and acceleration in different situations.
Analyze and evaluate real-world situations or simulated scenarios by posing challenging questions that require the synthesis of acquired knowledge and the creative application of kinematics.

pathways.title: Safety Readiness for Laboratory Work
pathways.description: Knowing where emergency equipment is and how to use it is essential for anyone working in laboratory environments. This pathway introduces the location and function of critical emergency equipment used in scientific settings, such as eyewash stations, fire extinguishers, safety showers, and first aid kits. Explore how quickly identifying and accessing emergency resources helps prevent injuries and supports safe lab practices—an essential skill for learners preparing for careers in science.
learning_objectives: Identify and locate the positions of critical emergency equipment, including first aid kits, emergency action and response plan, eyewash stations and shower facilities, sinks, fire extinguishers and fire blankets, telephones, master power switches and emergency exits.
Recognize and comply with OSHA standards for labeling and signage of emergency equipment.

pathways.title: Can Artificial Intelligence Detect Hidden Heart Attacks?
pathways.description: Analysing medical data to detect health problems can be incredibly difficult, but advances in artificial intelligence (AI) mean it is now possible for computers to spot subtle patterns in complex patient data. Dr Salah Al-Zaiti and Dr Christian Martin-Gill, from the University of Pittsburgh in the US, and Dr Ervin Sejdić, from the University of Toronto in Canada, are pioneering this technology to identify patients who have had hidden heart attacks, ensuring they receive medical treatment as quickly as possible.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway learners will be able to define and explain key terms such as Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS), Electrocardiogram (ECG), Ischemia, and Artificial Intelligence (AI).
Learners will be able to evaluate the challenges and ethical considerations in implementing machine learning in healthcare, such as patient privacy and access to medical data.
Learners will be able to identify potential career opportunities in machine learning in medicine, ranging from direct healthcare roles to positions in pharmaceutical and bioengineering companies.

pathways.title: An Educational Journey Through Cell Biology
pathways.description: Dr Kristina Ames is the Assistant Director for Cancer Research Training and Education Coordination (CRTEC) at the Albert Einstein College of Medicine in New York, USA. As a biomedical scientist and passionate educator, Kristina talks us through key concepts of cell biology and how understanding of them shapes scientific research and impacts society.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway students will learn about the role and importance of PI3K in the cellular recycling process, enhancing their understanding of cell biology.
Learn about the central dogma of molecular biology, understanding its role in the flow of genetic information from DNA to RNA to proteins.
Be able to explain the structure and function of DNA, enhancing their understanding of the fundamental blueprint of life.
Be able to explain the process of DNA transcription and its significance in protein synthesis.
Be able to explain the process of RNA to protein translation and its importance in cell function and disease development.

pathways.title: Wireless Charging
pathways.description:
Explore how electric currents generate magnetic fields and how changing magnetic fields produce electric currents in this pathway and accompanying student guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (215 minutes) and focuses on electromagnetism, energy transfer, and practical applications. 
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 25 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 30 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 2-3, 75 minutes, items 1-6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 45 minutes, in the student guide and item 7 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 40 minutes, items 8-10 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how electric currents can produce a magnetic field and that a changing magnetic field can produce an electric current.
You will also demonstrate how electric and magnetic fields transfer energy.

pathways.title: Driving Reactions: Solving Plastic Waste
pathways.description: Explore the potential of directed evolution in using enzymes as molecular machines to address sustainability challenges, especially plastic recycling. Ideal for high school to college students, since it introduces concepts of polymer chemistry, enzyme reactions, directed evolution, and DNA applications.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to explore how chemists are improving plastic recycling through directed evolution by watching the video Driving Reactions.
Understand how enzymes function and how they can be engineered to address global challenges.
Learn about catalysis and the factors that can influence the rate of catalytic reactions.
Investigate the relationship between polymers and monomers.
Observe enzymes in action through a hands-on experiment using bananas.

pathways.title: The Biological Hazards of Space
pathways.description: Space is a place of great exploration. However, it is threatening to humans and our biology. This pathway takes a deeper dive into the biological challenges faced by the human body in space, and the research being done to minimize the effects of these challenges.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will learn about the hazards of space travel.
You will understand how scientists are trying to combat the hazards of space travel on the human body.
You will learn about the effects of microgravity on an astronaut's cardiovascular system, vision and bones.
You will understand the effects of space radiation on the human body.

pathways.title: Predictive Analytics
pathways.description: Predictive analytics helps professionals across science, technology, and data-driven fields turn past data into informed expectations about the future. This learning pathway equips you with essential skills in predictive analytics—the science of using historical data to forecast outcomes and drive smarter decisions. You’ll learn how to prepare and clean real-world datasets, visualize trends, and build foundational predictive models using decision trees and logistic regression. Through real-world examples across industries, learners will explore how to evaluate model performance and apply predictive reasoning to drive data-informed decisions in business, healthcare, government, and beyond.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of predictive analytics in data-driven decision-making.
Visualize and summarize data to uncover patterns and relationships.
Prepare and clean datasets for predictive modeling.
Understand and evaluate a basic predictive model using decision trees and logistic regression.
Evaluate predictive models using proper training/testing methods.
Compare how data science is used in different industries to support predictive decision-making.

pathways.title: Introduction to Ecology: Understanding Life and the Environment
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the fundamental concepts of ecology, the branch of biology that studies the interactions between living organisms and their environment. You will explore ecosystems, biotic and abiotic factors, nutrient cycles, and how biology influences climate. Through videos, texts, and interactive quizzes, you will gain a deeper understanding of how ecosystems function and why ecological balance is essential for sustainability.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify the living (biotic) and non-living (abiotic) components that make up an ecosystem.
Explain how nutrients, such as carbon and nitrogen, cycle through ecosystems.
Analyze the role of living organisms in climate regulation and environmental sustainability.

pathways.title: Data Streams - A Crash Course in Data Literacy Using Stream Ecology
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to use a protocol to analyze the information in a graph.
Apply the PPSTT data literacy framework to a novel analysis of data using the Google Ngram Viewer.
Explain how the scale of a graph can be manipulated to show different trends.
Analyze streamflow data to recognize patterns and differences that raise further questions.
Synthesize information presented as a graph, table, photo, and map to fill in gaps in the data.
Make a claim based on the evidence from several data sources about whether a stream is wet or not.

pathways.title: How to Identify Plants
pathways.description: Discover the diversity of major plant families by exploring their unique structures, from legumes and composite flower heads to crucifer petals and grass spikelets. Through dissections and real examples, you’ll gain practical skills in plant identification, morphology, and classification.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify the major plant families Fabaceae, Asteraceae, Brassicaceae, and Poaceae by recognizing characteristic morphological traits such as flower structure, fruit type, and leaf arrangement.
Describe the structure and function of flowers, fruits, and leaves, explaining how these features contribute to reproduction and classification in plants.
Compare and contrast composite flowers, legumes, crucifers, and grasses to highlight similarities and differences in plant morphology and adaptations.
Analyze the role of plant families in biodiversity, ecosystems, and agriculture, connecting structural traits to ecological and human uses.
Apply observational and classification skills, including dissections, to determine the family of an unknown plant specimen.

pathways.title: Database Usage
pathways.description: Databases play a critical role across science, technology, and data-driven fields, supporting how large and complex datasets are stored, accessed, and analyzed. This pathway builds essential skills in data storage, retrieval, and compression, while introducing cloud computing concepts and hands-on experience navigating scientific databases such as Ensembl, NCBI, and UCSC.
learning_objectives: Explain how databases store and retrieve information with a focus on data persistence and query efficiency.
Describe the advantages of using databases for handling diverse and large-scale data types in analytical tasks.
Demonstrate an understanding of basic database operations, including data input, querying, and generating reports.

pathways.title: Mission Moon: Blast Off!! How Does NASA Get Rockets into Space?
pathways.description:
Embark on a cosmic expedition with this educational pathway, complete with a student guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (180-200 minutes), delving into the diversity of stars, their spectral classifications, and techniques for measuring cosmic distances. Learn about the atomic light spectrum and how flame emission spectroscopy aids in element detection. Engage with interactive videos and readings, conduct experiments, and comprehend the interconnectedness of these astronomical phenomena. Additionally, explore a STEM career tied to astrophysics and spectroscopy.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2 & 3, 40-60 minutes, items 3-6 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 7-9 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 30 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 10 minutes, item 10 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to classify various types of stars using spectral classification and explain how these classifications correlate with stellar temperatures and compositions, demonstrating knowledge and comprehension.

Students will analyze and interpret data from flame emission spectroscopy to identify different elements, applying their understanding of the atomic light spectrum and showcasing their analytical abilities.

Students will evaluate the methods used to measure astronomical distances and create a model to demonstrate the concept, synthesizing information to solve problems and communicate their understanding of the scale of the universe.

pathways.title: Cognitive Bias: How Does Cognitive Bias Undermine Good Reasoning, and How Can We Counteract Its Influence?
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the concept of cognitive bias and identifies heuristic reasoning and motivated reasoning as the two main sources of cognitive bias. Learners will become familiar with confirmation bias, key heuristic biases (availability and representativeness), and a variety of motivated biases (self-serving bias, groupthink, and more). Since minimizing the influence of cognitive bias is a central goal of the scientific method, learners will explore how standard scientific practices are designed to mitigate bias. These techniques can also help to improve our everyday reasoning. Learners will explore how a difficult college admissions decision can be distorted by cognitive bias and identify a variety of reasoning strategies that are effective tools for minimizing the effect of bias on complex reasoning and decision-making tasks.
learning_objectives: Define heuristic reasoning, describe a variety of common cognitive heuristics, and explain how they give rise to cognitive biases.
Define motivated reasoning and describe a variety of common cognitive biases that are rooted in motivated reasoning.
Identify signs and triggers for cognitive biases in both scientific and everyday contexts.
Explain how contemporary scientific practices mitigate the influence of cognitive bias on scientific reasoning.
Describe and apply strategies to minimize the influence of cognitive bias on everyday reasoning.

pathways.title: Energy to Spare: Building Better Batteries
pathways.description:
Lithium-ion batteries make headlines: from the positives of smart watches, glasses, and phones that hold a charge for days, to the negatives of battery fires and environmental cleanup. They’re also a critical component in storing and distributing the energy that powers our world. But, as we bowl towards a future that depends more on batteries as key players in renewable technology, we’re going to need chemists to strike up bigger, safer, and more efficient solutions. "Energy to Spare," a seven-minute film from Chemistry Shorts®, uses exploding batteries, bowling showdowns, and electrifying animations to give a primer on how batteries work and explore how scientists are revolutionizing battery technology for the renewable energy age.
This pathway leads classrooms through a video, curriculum, and hands-on experiment to explore battery science.
learning_objectives: Battery Science

pathways.title: Growing Human Liver Cells ‘in a Dish’
pathways.description: Full liver transplants are the main treatment option for severe liver disease, but suitable donors are in short supply. Could stem cell technology, in which healthy liver cells are grown in the lab, be a solution to this problem? Dr Dave Hay from the University of Edinburgh’s Centre for Regenerative Medicine thinks it could
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway students should be able to describe the characteristics of stem cells, including their undifferentiated nature.
Understand the consequences of liver damage on overall health and well-being.
Formulate informed opinions on the potential impact of stem cell research on healthcare and patient outcomes.

pathways.title: cBioPortal Tutorial Series
pathways.description: In this cBioPortal Tutorial Series from the Teaching the Genome Generation™ (TtGG) Cancer Genetics module, you will learn to navigate the cBioPortal in order to identify individual cancer studies by tissue type, search for a specific gene within a single study, reveal demographic data for clinical studies and calculate the range and median of diagnosis age for a group of patients.
learning_objectives: Navigate a cancer database and explore data from a cancer research study.

pathways.title: Cycles of Matter and Energy in Ecosystems: Why Is a Healthy Diet Important?
pathways.description:
Explore food and energy with this 5-day (190–230 minute) pathway using videos, texts, simulations, and a student guide. Analyze nutrient absorption, and ATP production, and discuss solutions to US food deserts.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 15-20 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 60 minutes, item 2 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 45-60 minutes, items 3-4 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 20-30 minutes, item 5 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 30 minutes, items 6-7 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will explain how food is broken down into nutrients needed for the body.
Collect data on the production of ATP and absorption of molecules based on amount/size.
Discuss the causes and solutions to food deserts in the US.

pathways.title: Telomeres: The Chromosome Tips That Stave off Ageing
pathways.description: The ends of our chromosomes are capped with structures called telomeres, which protect our DNA when cells divide. Over time, chromosomes get shorter, and scientists think this erosion plays a role in the ageing process. Professor Julie Cooper and her team at the University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus in the US are investigating the wide range of enigmatic functions of telomeres, which has implications for our understanding of cancer, reproduction and how we grow older.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms such as chromosome, telomere, telomerase, meiosis, mitosis, and senescence.
You identify the end replication problem and how it affects DNA replication.
You will explain how telomeres prevent chromosome degradation and fusion during mitosis and meiosis.
You will compare and contrast the role of telomerase in germline cells, stem cells, and somatic cells.
You will debate whether extending telomere length artificially could have positive or negative consequences.

pathways.title: Ecological Economics
pathways.description: Gain insights into climate change's societal influence and an alternative economic perspective that prioritizes health and the environment. Learn to navigate the complex challenges of our modern era, from carbon taxes to global survival.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will learn about the fundamental principles of the intersection of climate science and economics, equipping them with the knowledge to analyze and critique traditional microeconomic models.
Explore an alternative economic perspective that prioritizes health and the environment in the modern era.
Explore the potential implications for policy and decision-making, fostering a more sustainable and equitable future.
Understand the role of regulatory frameworks and international agreements in facilitating or hindering the transformation of the global financial system.

pathways.title: César Hidalgo, PhD - Network Scientist
pathways.description: Dr. César Hidalgo is a network scientist who studies how different systems interact; his work is to solve real world problems by studying patterns in everyday life. He develops new ways to look at data to make it easier for anyone to understand.

pathways.title: Data Preparation and Statistics
pathways.description: Data preparation and statistical reasoning are foundational skills across science, technology, and data-driven fields, supporting how raw information is transformed into meaningful insights. This pathway builds essential skills in analyzing data distributions, calculating measures of center (mean, median, mode) and spread (range, IQR, standard deviation), identifying outliers, interpreting percentiles and z-scores, and visualizing data using Python.
learning_objectives: Identify appropriate data collection methods and distinguish between observational, transactional, and experimental data sources for statistical analysis.
Clean datasets by handling missing values, removing duplicates, managing outliers, and applying normalization or standardization techniques for statistical preprocessing.
Implement data storage and retrieval strategies for large datasets, including chunking, compression, indexing, and cloud-based database systems.

pathways.title: A New Paradigm in Antibiotic Research
pathways.description: Used for treating bacterial infections, antibiotics are an essential part of modern medicine. However, this crucial treatment option is in jeopardy. Antibiotic resistance – where bacteria become resistant to antibiotics – led to more than 1 million deaths worldwide in 2019. At the University of Basel in Switzerland, Professor Christoph Dehio leads the National Centre of Competence in Research AntiResist, which is applying new research methods to speed up the discovery and development of new antibiotics and complementary anti-infective strategies, thereby helping to reduce the problem of antibiotic resistance.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms related to antibiotics and antimicrobial resistance, such as antibiotics, antimicrobials, pathogens, and antibiotic resistance.
Explain the difference between antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and antibiotic resistance.
Compare and contrast in vitro and in vivo methods used in the AntiResist project.
Examine the factors contributing to the rapid increase of antibiotic resistance, including poor hygiene and over-prescription.

pathways.title: Natural Selection: How Does Human Impact on the Climate Affect the Survival of Species?
pathways.description:
Explore natural selection with this pathway which spans 7 days or 310-375 minutes. Using simulations, images, videos, texts, and a student guide, investigate environmental pressures, analyze adaptive traits, and explain their role in evolutionary fitness.




Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40-60 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 90-120 minutes, items 2-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4-5, 90 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXPLAIN: Day 6, 45-60 minutes, items 5-6 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 7, 45 minutes, item 7 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will explain natural selection and give examples of natural selection in humans and other organisms.
Investigate how environmental pressures induce the emergence of traits that are most fit for the environment.
Explain how the emergence of traits in human appearance helps us to live in many different environments.

pathways.title: Sending Phone Calls Globally
pathways.description:
Investigate how communications data are sent worlwide by understanding the properties of light with this pathway and accompanying student guide. The recommended learning time spans 6 days (250 minutes). Discover how light can be described as both a particle and a wave, and how we collect data using fiber optic cables.




Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 30 minutes, found in the student guide.
• EXPLORE: Day 1-2, 60 minutes, items 1-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3-4, 100 minutes, item 5-9 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 30 minutes, item 10 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 30 minutes, found in the student guide.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how the material that light travels through can affect its speed and wavelength, but the frequency of the light remains unchanged.
Understand that light can be described as a particle or as a wave and that both representations have their uses when trying to understand how objects interact with light.
Describe how we send communication signals around the globe.

pathways.title: Can We Harness the Power of the Oceans?
pathways.description: Professor Jennifer Franck leads a team of mechanical engineers at the University of Wisconsin-Madison in the US who are working on a device that could turn the immense power of ocean tides into a sustainable electricity supply
learning_objectives: After completing this pathway, you will understand the concept of marine energy as a reliable and predictable source of renewable energy.
You will understand how innovative technologies are used to harness the movement of water and generate clean and sustainable electricity.

pathways.title: SciJourneys - Building Resilience in Science
pathways.description:
Help students sharpen their skills of inquiry and critical thinking and see that science is all around them! This student-centered, active learning pathway helps students build resilience and growth mindsets as they approach scientific methods. Features 3 early career women in STEM! This pathway is aimed at Grades 6-10 and consists of three 45 minute activities.
Driving Question(s):
• Understand that how science works is diverse, dynamic, and requires resilience.
• Practices and approaches that scientists take are not distinct from, and often draw upon, skills cultivated through arts and humanities
Students will build question webs, draw comics, create hypotheses, and plan studies, all based on their own interests. Supported by videos that feature exciting new research, crafted specifically to interact with this learning experience.
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to practice forming and sharing higher-level, scientific questions.
