Build questioning capacity through learning about the story and scientific work of ethnobotanist Rose Bear Don't Walk.
Understand several challenges in asking higher-level, scientific questions.
Reflect upon your own challenges and triumphs when asking questions in science.
Understand the definition of a hypothesis and what makes a hypothesis testable.
Appreciate the creativity and ingenuity involved in developing hypotheses through the story of biomechanical engineer Ritu Raman.
Generate numerous hypotheses connected to a research question.
Evaluate if hypotheses are specific, testable, and answered with data.
Analyze the role of hypotheses in scientific investigations and understand situations where data collection might proceed without a hypothesis.
Develop strategies to adapt and continue data collection in the face of unexpected challenges.
Collaborate to design a comprehensive data collection plan and anticipate potential challenges.
Identify and describe various types of data that can be collected to understand phenomena.

pathways.title: Water’s Role in Environmental and Societal Processes
pathways.description:
Water is not only vital for life but also a key player in environmental and societal challenges. This pathway explores water’s role in the environment, from addressing acid rain and heavy metal pollution to harnessing renewable energy. By connecting water chemistry to real-world applications, you will appreciate its importance in solving global problems and advancing sustainable development.
For a deep dive into the molecular structure and properties of water, visit The Chemistry of Life – Properties and Functions of Water.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will investigate water’s role in environmental systems, such as the water cycle, acid rain, and heavy metal pollution.
Analyze innovative solutions to water-related challenges, including water purification and renewable energy technologies.
Reflect on the societal and environmental impact of water and its management in a global context.

pathways.title: Can Artificial Intelligence Help Prevent Heart Failure?
pathways.description: Depending on its application – and your opinion – advances in artificial intelligence (AI) can seem impressive, daunting or both! Though AI raises many thought-provoking questions about its use and potential, researchers all over the world are finding many positive uses for AI that are likely to benefit humanity greatly. One such researcher, Dr Linwei Wang from the Rochester Institute of Technology in New York, USA, is using AI to develop new and innovative ways of detecting heart disease.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway learners will be able to comprehend the dual nature of AI, exploring both disruptive and beneficial applications.
Learners will be able to evaluate the application of AI in healthcare, by examining the development of in-home monitoring devices.
Learners will examine the challenges and concerns associated with AI, including issues of trustworthiness, fairness, bias, and error prevention.

pathways.title: Sharing Computational Skills to Address Neglected Diseases in Latin America
pathways.description: Tropical diseases are a significant source of deaths across Latin America, but, until recently, there has been little interest in developing drugs and vaccines to tackle them. As part of the innovative CABANA project, Dr Rodrigo Ochoa from the University of Antioquia, in Colombia, uses his skills in biochemistry and bioinformatics to discover new drugs and improve the status of biochemical research in Latin America.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how computational methods are used to analyse the structures of proteins.
You will know some career options in the field of biochemistry.
You will understand what defines a neglected tropical disease.

pathways.title: Kinetics and Nuclear Chemistry – Rates of Reaction
pathways.description: In this unit, we will learn how the rate of a chemical reaction is affected by a number of factors, including temperature and the concentration of reactants at the beginning of the reaction. This unit forms part of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.
learning_objectives: Describe the different factors that influence the rate of a chemical reaction, referencing the basics of collision theory.
Explain what a rate law is and what kind of data one needs to determine what the rate law for a reaction is.
Explain potential energy diagram, define activation energy, and explain how higher temperatures and catalysts speed up a reaction.
Explain reaction mechanisms and rate-limiting steps.
Describe the history of the discovery of radioactivity.
Describe the different types of radioactive decay and write balanced nuclear equations..
Explain the concept of half-life and describe the process of radiometric dating.

pathways.title: Thermodynamics
pathways.description: This pathway discusses thermodynamics in practical contexts including heat engines, heat pumps, and refrigerators, and that interactions between systems can result in changes in those systems. As systems either do work or have work done on them, the total energy of a system can change. These ideas are based on the previous understanding of heat as the process of energy transfer from a higher temperature system to a lower temperature system. The pathway also discusses the first law of thermodynamics, the second law of thermodynamics, and entropy.
learning_objectives: Define the first law of thermodynamics.
Describe how conservation of energy relates to the first law of thermodynamics.
Identify instances of the first law of thermodynamics working in everyday situations, including biological metabolism.
Calculate changes in the internal energy of a system, after accounting for heat transfer and work done.
Describe the processes of a simple heat engine.
Explain the differences among the simple thermodynamic processes—isobaric, isochoric, isothermal, and adiabatic.
Calculate total work done in a cyclical thermodynamic process.
Explain the expressions of the second law of thermodynamics.
Calculate the efficiency and carbon dioxide emission of a coal-fired electricity plant, using second law characteristics.
Describe and define the otto cycle.
Identify a carnot cycle.
Calculate maximum theoretical efficiency of a nuclear reactor.
Explain how dissipative processes affect the ideal carnot engine.
Describe the use of heat engines in heat pumps and refrigerators.
Describe and demonstrate how a heat pump works to warm an interior space.
Explain the differences between heat pumps and refrigerators.
Calculate a heat pump's coefficient of performance.
Define entropy.
Calculate the increase of entropy in a system with reversible and irreversible processes.
Explain the expected fate of the universe in entropic terms.
Calculate the increasing disorder of a system.
Identify probabilities in entropy.
Examine statistical probabilities in entropic systems.

pathways.title: Heat Energy
pathways.description: Heat energy, also known as thermal energy, is the energy associated with the movement of particles within a substance. It represents the internal kinetic energy of the particles in a material. The faster the particles move, the higher the temperature and, consequently, the greater the heat energy.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners should be able to explain the different forms of heat transfer.
Perform experiments on heat-related activities.
State and explain the first law of thermodynamics.
Calculate specific heat capacity.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Disease and Epidemiology
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of the language of epidemiologists; tracking infectious diseases; modes of disease transmission; global public health.

pathways.title: Reasoning, Planning and Solving Problems
pathways.description: Contrary to popular belief, emotions play a pivotal role in problem-solving and decision-making, not just logic and reasoning. This learning pathway explores the mechanisms of reasoning, planning, and problem-solving, with a special focus on their emotional foundations. You'll learn to distinguish between belief and fact-based reasoning in brain processing, understand how emotional responses are shaped by past experiences, and learn how solving a problem engages multiple brain circuits. By the end, you will appreciate the role of learning in strengthening memory and cognitive abilities.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how emotional responses are based on past experience.
You will appreciate the role of learning in building memory and cognitive strength.
You will be able to give an example of how solving a problem uses multiple brain circuits.
You will be able to distinguish a belief from fact-based reasoning in brain processing.

pathways.title: Exploring Molecules in 3D: An Introduction to VSEPR Theory
pathways.description: Uncover the principles of molecular geometry with the VSEPR model, exploring how electron repulsion shapes 3D molecules. Predict molecular structures, bond angles, and hybridization, including linear, trigonal planar, and tetrahedral geometries.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to predict the shapes of molecules using the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) model, identifying geometries such as linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, and octahedral.
Explain how bonding and lone pairs of electrons affect molecular geometry and bond angles in 3D structures.
Identify bond angles and hybridization states of central atoms in molecules, demonstrating the relationship between electron domains and geometry.
Distinguish between electron domain geometry and molecular geometry, providing examples of each in real-world molecules.
Utilize interactive simulations to model molecular shapes and analyze how adding bonds or lone pairs alters geometry.
Describe how electron pair repulsions influence molecular shapes, using examples like ammonia and water to demonstrate the effects of lone pairs.

pathways.title: Respiratory System: Structure, Health, and Environmental Impact
pathways.description: The respiratory system is crucial for gas exchange between the body and the environment. Explore how playing wind instruments may impact lung function and expiratory capacity, focusing on peak expiratory flow and environmental health effects on respiratory diseases.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and function of the respiratory system, including the organs involved in ventilation and gas exchange.
Explain the process of respiration, including inspiration, expiration, and gas transport.
Evaluate common respiratory disorders, such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and pneumonia, and their impact on health.
Analyze the effects of environmental factors, such as air pollution, on respiratory health.
Demonstrate proper techniques for assessing respiratory function, such as measuring lung capacity and conducting spirometry tests.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: Periodic Table of Elements
pathways.description: This pathway provides resources to understand the periodic table of elements. Explore periodic classification, atomic structure, and element properties through the lens of transition metals, lanthanides, and actinides. Learn how electron configurations and periodic trends influence chemical behavior, color, and reactivity.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the layout and classification of the periodic table, including groups, periods, and blocks (s, p, d, and f).
Explain the trends in the periodic table, such as atomic size, ionization energy, metallic and non-metallic character, and reactivity, across periods and down groups.
Interpret the relationship between electron configuration and the position of an element in the periodic table, including the concept of valence electrons, orbital types, and quantum numbers.
Compare the properties of different types of elements, including metals, nonmetals, metalloids, transition metals, lanthanides, and actinides.
Analyze the chemical and physical properties of transition metals, such as variable oxidation states, colored compounds, catalytic behavior, and complex ion formation.
Identify the characteristic features of lanthanides and actinides, including their placement in the f-block, common oxidation states, and technological applications.
Explain the concept of electron orbitals and quantum numbers, and how these relate to atomic structure and periodicity.
Use the periodic table to predict chemical behavior and bonding tendencies of elements, including formation of ions and types of chemical reactions.
Relate the position of an element to its real-world applications, such as the use of actinides in nuclear energy or lanthanides in electronics.
Interpret and construct electron configurations and orbital diagrams, applying the Aufbau principle and rules for orbital filling.

pathways.title: Cardiovascular System: Anatomy, Health, and Disease Prevention
pathways.description: Explore how kidneys influence cardiovascular health by filtering blood, regulating blood pressure, and linking chronic kidney disease to cardiovascular disease. Discover preventive measures, translational research, and careers in nephrology and cardiovascular medicine.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to explain the structure and function of the heart, blood vessels, and blood components.
Analyze the role of the cardiovascular system in maintaining homeostasis.
Evaluate common cardiovascular diseases, risk factors, and preventive measures.
Demonstrate the ability to measure and interpret vital signs related to cardiovascular health.
Apply knowledge of cardiovascular anatomy and physiology to real-life scenarios, such as emergency response situations.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: Experience Shapes Your Brain
pathways.description: Learn more about the fascinating interplay of genes and environment in shaping the brain. In this pathway, you will discover how activities like exercise or learning new skills can physically expand your brain circuitry, and how personal experiences can alter your brain's memory. By comparing the architecture of a child's brain with that of an adult and an aging brain, you'll gain insights into the brain's dynamic nature and lifelong adaptability. By the end of this pathway, you'll appreciate the brain's incredible capacity for growth and change.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand that both genes and environment shape the brain.
You will show how new exercise or skill learning literally grows new brain circuits.
You will give an example of how your experience changes your brain's memory.
You will compare/contrast brain architecture in the child, adult, and aging brain.

pathways.title: The Metallic World: Electrochemistry and Coordination Compounds
pathways.description: This unit provides an overview of both electrochemistry and basic transition metal chemistry. Oxidation-reduction reactions, also known as redox reactions, drive electrochemistry. In redox reactions, one compound gains electrons (reduction) while another one loses electrons (oxidation). This unit forms part of Chemistry: Challenges and Solutions.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Describe the difference between spontaneous and nonspontaneous redox processes in terms of both cell emf (electromotive force) and dg.
Identify everyday applications of spontaneous redox processes as well as applications that depend on the forcing of a process to run in the non-spontaneous direction.
Describe the basics of physiological redox processes, and recognize some of the enzymes that facilitate electron transfer.
Compare/contrast basic transition-metal chemistry to main-group chemistry.

pathways.title: BLOCK 1 - The ecological roots of Coronacrisis
pathways.description:
Welcome to this first MOOC series on “Rethinking the economy after the coronavirus”. This series has been initiated by the EPOG+ Master programme during the first lockdown period, with the objective of understanding the socio-economic consequences of the COVID and the perspectives for policy makers to reorientate our development model. Taking benefit of a very qualified network of scholars, this MOOC series contains several lectures, with very diversified but totally complementary contents, coping with some of the aspects we collectively understood as vital aspects for the understanding of the current crisis, not only in its surface, but in its structural dimension. First of all, it is crucial to discuss the ecological roots of Coronocrisis. Connected to that, we have lectures highlighting that Coronacrisis is a multidimensional crisis, having deep impacts on the world economy and society, and allowing some scholars to claim that it will possibly provoke structural changes. Finally, it is essential to discuss the economic policies that are being implemented worldwide to face the Coronocrisis. As you can see, it is a very dense and extremely instigating MOOC series and we hope you will enjoy it!
BLOCK 1 - The ecological roots of Coronacrisis
BLOCK 2 – Impacts and structural changes provoked by Coronacrisis
BLOCK 3 – The economy and the coronavirus in the global South
BLOCK 4 – Rethinking economic policy after the coronavirus

pathways.title: Hemoglobin: Studying the T to R Transition
pathways.description: Hemoglobin, a tetrameric protein found in high concentrations in red blood cells, is responsible for binding and transporting oxygen in the body. Each hemoglobin protein is made up of four subunits - two alpha subunits and two beta subunits - and each subunit is capable of binding to an oxygen molecule via its heme group. This animated pathway shows conformational changes upon oxygen binding, focusing first on a heme group and then zooming out to see the structure of the tetramer and then shows conformation changes from T to R states, and finally allosteric regulation by BPG.

pathways.title: Where Did the Sun Go?
pathways.description: In this pathway, students will analyze and interpret data from the investigation to find patterns in how their location in North America will determine if they will see a total or partial solar eclipse on April 8, 2024. Students will develop a model to communicate how the Earth, sun, moon system align to cause a solar eclipse. This pathway is designed for NGSS standard, MS-ESS1-1.
learning_objectives: Students analyze and interpret data from the investigation to find patterns in how their location in North America will determine if they will see a total or partial solar eclipse on April 8.
Students develop a model to communicate how the Earth, sun, moon system align to cause a solar eclipse.

pathways.title: Lungs and Pulmonary System
pathways.description: This pathway investigates how our lungs and pulmonary system work. It includes making a lung model which will be used to explore how lung infections influence breathing.
learning_objectives: List the external and internal body parts of a human body used for breathing.
Use a model to explain how humans breathe.
Explain how lung infections can influence breathing.

pathways.title: UV-Visible Light Absorption Spectroscopy and Its Applications
pathways.description: By studying of UV-visible absorption spectroscopy, students will explore how molecules interact with ultraviolet and visible light. They will develop the ability to analyze the absorbance spectra of various molecules and  colour of various substances. It will also help to understand the principles behind the Beer-Lambert law. This knowledge will enable them to calculate the concentration of unknown substances using spectroscopic data.
learning_objectives: Explain the principles of UV-visible spectroscopy and how molecules absorb light at specific wavelengths.
Analyze absorbance spectra to identify characteristics of different substances.
Apply the Beer-Lambert law to determine the concentration of substances from spectroscopic data.

pathways.title: Transport of Fluids within the Body
pathways.description: This pathway looks at the cardiovascular, lymphatic, and urinary systems to provide an understanding of fluid circulation throughout the human body.
learning_objectives: Describe the structure of the heart and explain how cardiac cycle works..
Describe the major structures of the lymphatic system..
Describe the major functions of the urinary system.
Identify the major functions of the urinary system.

pathways.title: The Animal Body: Basic Form and Function
pathways.description: The arctic fox is an example of a complex animal that has adapted to its environment and illustrates the relationships between an animal’s form and function. The structures of animals consist of primary tissues that make up more complex organs and organ systems. Homeostasis allows an animal to maintain a balance between its internal and external environments. This pathway provides an in-depth look at animal form & function, animal primary tissues, and homeostasis. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Describe the various types of body plans that occur in animals.
Describe limits on animal size and shape.
Identify and relate bioenergetics to body size, levels of activity, and the environment.
Describe epithelial tissues.
Discuss the different types of connective tissues in animals.
Describe three types of muscle tissues.
Describe nervous tissue.
Define homeostasis.
Describe the factors affecting homeostasis.
Discuss positive and negative feedback mechanisms used in homeostasis.
Describe thermoregulation of endothermic and ectothermic animals.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Skin and Eye Infections
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of anatomy & normal microbiota of the skin & eyes; bacterial infections of the skin & eyes; viral infections of the skin & eyes; mycoses of the skin; protozoan and helminthic infections of the skin & eyes.

pathways.title: Exchange with the Environment
pathways.description: In this section, learn about how the digestive and respiratory systems function and interact with the environment.
learning_objectives: Describe the major functions of the digestive system.
Identify digestive organs based on position and structure.
Describe major functions and processes of the respiratory system.
Discuss the mechanisms of gas exchange in the lungs & tissues.

pathways.title: How Neurons Communicate
pathways.description: Neural communication is the cornerstone of brain function and shapes our perception of life. This pathway explains the intricacies of how neurons transmit signals, the implications of disrupted brain communication, and the promising research aimed at treating neurological disorders. By the end, you'll gain a comprehensive understanding of brain region interactions and the neural circuit in the cerebellum.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain how electrical signals propagate in brain axons.
You will understand how sensory stimuli convert to electrical signals.
You will understand how myelin accelerates brain axonal communication.
You will have a deeper understanding of the canonical neural circuit in cerebellum.
You will have a holistic understanding of how brain regions communicate with one another.

pathways.title: Respiration and Energy in the Human Body
pathways.description: Oxygen moves from the alveoli into capillaries due to partial pressure differences, ensuring efficient gas exchange. Explain the mechanisms of gas exchange, cellular respiration, and oxygen transport in the human body, highlighting their biochemical processes and physiological significance in sustaining life.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and function of the human respiratory system, identifying key organs and tissues involved in breathing and gas exchange.
Explain the process of gas exchange and oxygen transport, including the role of hemoglobin, partial pressure differences, and diffusion.
Analyze the relationship between respiration and energy production, describing how oxygen is used in aerobic cellular respiration to generate ATP.

pathways.title: Scientific Illustration
pathways.description: One important aspect of data and science literacy is understanding and designing scientific illustrations that show experimental results or models. This pathway helps to appreciate and interpret scientific illustrations.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of visualizing data and illustrating models in biology.
Explain scientific illustrations.

pathways.title: Brain Anatomy: Structure, Function, and Health Impacts
pathways.description: Dive into the complexities of the brain with this pathway, where you'll understand the anatomy of key structures like the cerebral cortex and brainstem, and their roles in everything from emotion to motor control. Become familiar with how neurotransmitters affect neural communication and how brain disorders impact mental health and well-being. Through neuroimaging and case studies, you'll translate theory into real-world understanding and finally, you'll discover career opportunities that stem from the study of neuroscience.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will be able to describe the anatomical structures and regions of the brain, including the cerebral cortex, limbic system, and brainstem.
Explain the functions of different brain regions in sensory perception, motor control, emotion, cognition, and behavior.
Analyze the role of neurotransmitters and synaptic transmission in neural communication and synaptic plasticity.
Evaluate the impact of brain injuries, neurodegenerative diseases, and psychiatric disorders on brain function and mental health.
Develop an understanding of brain anatomy and function through an introduction to neuroimaging techniques.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: Daniele Foresti, PhD - Biomechanical Engineer
pathways.description: Dr. Foresti is a mechanical engineer and researcher at Harvard University where he works in a lab filled with 3-D printers, bottles and jars of different liquids. He uses scientific principles to invent a way to use sound waves to create precise droplets of nearly any liquid.

pathways.title: Cell Cycle Regulation: Timing is Everything
pathways.description:
Dive into the intricacies of the cell cycle with this informative pathway and accompanying student guide. Spanning lessons over 10 days (420 minutes) you will study the stages of the cell cycle, uncover the role of cyclins and growth factors in cell regulation, explore how cell cycle irregularities can lead to cancer, and examine cell division's function in healing injuries. Engage with interactive content to grasp these vital biological concepts and consider a STEM career in this fascinating field.

















Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1; 45 minutes, item 1 in the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-4; 135 minutes, items 2-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 5-6; 60 minutes, items 9-11 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 7-9; 135 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 10; 45 minutes, items 12-13 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the phases of the cell cycle (G1, S, G2, and M) and the hallmark events of each.
You will be able to explain how cyclins and growth factors work together to regulate cellular progression through the phases of the cell cycle.
You will be able to explain how dysfunctional regulation of the cell cycle may result in tumor formation and cancer.
You will be able to model the role of cell division in repairing an injury in order to maintain a complex organism.

pathways.title: Fazed by the Moon
pathways.description: In this pathway, students will plan and carry out an investigation to determine changes in the position of the moon over the course of about a month and how this affects the moon's appearance on Earth. Students will develop and use a model to show how the pattern of motion of the moon causes lunar phases. This pathway is designed for NGSS Standard, MS-ESS1-1.
learning_objectives: Students will plan and carry out an investigation to determine changes in the position of the moon over the course of about a month and how this affects the moon's appearance on Earth
Students will develop and use a model to show how the pattern of motion of the moon causes lunar phases.

pathways.title: Protecting Biodiversity and Natural Resources
pathways.description: The IUCN Red List tracks endangered species to support global biodiversity protection. Learn how conservation biology, habitat restoration, and international legislation help reduce extinction risks. Analyze the causes and consequences of biodiversity loss and evaluate conservation strategies, energy choices, and human impacts on ecosystems to promote environmental sustainability. Describe ways of conserving and protecting natural resources and the environment.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the causes and effects of biodiversity loss, including habitat destruction, pollution, and resource exploitation.
Explain the importance of conservation efforts, such as wildlife reintroduction, habitat restoration, protected areas, and international legislation.
Identify the role of human population growth and land-use change in environmental degradation.
Differentiate between renewable and nonrenewable energy sources and evaluate their environmental impacts.
Analyze how energy choices and pollution contribute to ecosystem change and climate impacts.
Describe strategies for sustainable use and protection of natural resources.

pathways.title: Atomic Structure and Chemical Bond
pathways.description:
This lesson demonstrates that atoms are composed of a very small, positively charged nucleus, which is surrounded by a cloud of negatively charged electrons. Despite the nucleus being less than one ten-thousandth the size of the atom, it holds more than 99.9% of the atom’s mass.
Additionally, the atoms in ceramic materials are bonded by chemical forces. A chemical bond is the attractive force that holds atoms or ions together. This attraction creates a more stable system compared to individual atoms. Valence electrons play a crucial role in forming these chemical bonds.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this lesson, learners should understand the composition of atoms and their fundamental properties.
Learners should understand that atoms or molecules are held together by bonds of varying types and strengths, depending on their nature and the forces that attract them.
Learners should understand that molecules and compounds form through chemical bonds, which play a crucial role in the existence of living organisms.
Learners should understand that atoms are the fundamental building blocks of compounds and molecules.

pathways.title: NASA | Phase Changes
pathways.description: Phase changes happen when heat is transferred into or out of a substance. By understanding the nature of this heat transfer in different environments and under different circumstances, scientists are able to develop more efficient refrigeration technology. Learn how the science of heat transfer is used to develop new technologies on the International Space Station.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how the addition or removal of heat energy changes the state of materials.
You will understand how temperature and pressure influence the state of a material.
You will learn why the efficiency of heat transfer is important for refrigeration.
You will see how new technology is created through understanding the process of heat transfer between substances.

pathways.title: Temperature, Kinetic Theory, and the Gas Laws
pathways.description: In this pathway, several concepts related to heat are discussed—what is heat, how is it related to temperature, what are heat’s effects, and how is it related to other forms of energy and to work. In spite of the richness of the phenomena, there is a small set of underlying physical principles that unite the subjects and tie them to other fields. This pathway also discusses the “ideal gas law” which relates the temperature, pressure, and volume of an ideal gas using a simple equation.
learning_objectives: List and convert temperatures between the celsius, fahrenheit, and kelvin scales.
Define temperature.
Define thermal equilibrium.
Describe the zeroth law of thermodynamics.
Define and describe thermal expansion.
Calculate the linear expansion of an object given its initial length, change in temperature, and coefficient of linear expansion.
Calculate the volume expansion of an object given its initial volume, change in temperature, and coefficient of volume expansion.
Calculate thermal stress on an object given its original volume, temperature change, volume change, and bulk modulus.
Define the ideal gas law in terms of molecules and in terms of moles.
Use the ideal gas law to calculate pressure change, temperature change, volume change, or the number of molecules or moles in a given volume.
Define the ideal gas law in terms of molecular mass and velocity.
Define thermal energy.
Use avogadro’s number to convert between number of molecules and number of moles.
Calculate the kinetic energy of a gas molecule, given its temperature.
Describe the relationship between the temperature of a gas and the kinetic energy of atoms and molecules.
Interpret a phase diagram.
Define dalton’s law.
Identify and describe the triple point of a gas from its phase diagram.
Describe the state of equilibrium between a liquid and a gas, a liquid and a solid, and a gas and a solid.
Explain the relationship between vapor pressure of water and the capacity of air to hold water vapor.
Explain the relationship between relative humidity and partial pressure of water vapor in the air.
Calculate vapor density using vapor pressure.
Calculate humidity and dew point.

pathways.title: Groundwater Questions & Answers
pathways.description: Our planet is covered in water. We see it in our oceans and on land we see it in our lakes and rivers. But did you know that there is a hundred times more water in the ground than in all the Earth's lakes and rivers? Though you cannot see it there is a plethora of groundwater beneath your feet. Here at the Water Science School we have the answers to your questions about groundwater.
learning_objectives: Gain an understanding of the role and importance of groundwater
Identify aspects which can affect groundwater volume and quality
Explore the science of measuring groundwater

pathways.title: Making X-Rays Safer – And More Efficient
pathways.description: Explore how to make X-rays safer and more efficient with our expert guide. Discover the latest techniques and technologies that are transforming X-ray imaging, and how they can benefit both patients and medical professionals.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway students will be able to define CT scan, micro, micrometre, nano, nanomanipulator, nanometre, nanoscience, surface area, X-ray, and X-ray photodetector.
Understand the risks associated with X-rays and CT scans, including the increased risk of cancer due to radiation exposure.
Discuss the potential benefits of using perovskite-based X-ray photodetectors, including improved sensitivity and lower levels of radiation exposure.

pathways.title: Understanding Photosynthesis
pathways.description: The resources in this pathway offer a comprehensive look at photosynthesis, exploring its key processes and importance in plant biology. Learners will study the structures involved in photosynthesis, including light-dependent and light-independent reactions, and understand how plants convert light energy into chemical energy. The pathway includes a hands-on project where students use floating leaf disks to observe photosynthesis in action, providing a practical, interactive experience to reinforce theoretical concepts.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will be able to describe the structures and processes that allow plant cells to transfer energy from light to macromolecules.
Measure photosynthetic activity using plant leaves.

pathways.title: Sexual Reproduction and Gametogenesis
pathways.description: This pathway focuses on sexual reproduction in eukaryotes. It explores the different sexual life cycles (namely, haplontic, diplontic, and haplo-diplontic) and how they contribute to genetic variation. It also examines how gametes are produced in humans through the processes of spermatogenesis (sperm production) and oogenesis (oocyte production).
learning_objectives: Describe key steps in the sexual reproduction cycle in eukaryotes.
Outline the sources of genetic variation that occur during meiosis.
Outline the basic steps that occur during gamete formation in the male and female germ line.

pathways.title: Cell Division and Reproduction
pathways.description: Cells spend most of the cell cycle in interphase, growing and replicating DNA before division. Explain the processes and significance of the cell cycle, including interphase, mitosis, and meiosis, in growth, repair, reproduction, and the transmission of genetic information in organisms.
learning_objectives: Describe the stages and purpose of the cell cycle, including interphase, mitosis, and meiosis.
Differentiate between mitosis and meiosis in terms of chromosome behavior, resulting cells, and biological roles.
Illustrate and label the phases of mitosis and meiosis using models or diagrams.
Distinguish between asexual and sexual reproduction based on the type of cell division and genetic outcomes.
Explain how errors in the cell cycle can lead to diseases such as cancer.

pathways.title: Reproductive System: Anatomy, Health, and Development
pathways.description: Witness the miracle of life as a tiny cell transforms into a new being! Explore the remarkable journey of male and female bodies in creating life. Learn about the importance of contraception and the prevention of sexually transmitted infections (STIs) to maintain reproductive health and well-being. Along the way, discover exciting careers in reproductive health and medicine.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will describe the structure and function of the male and female reproductive systems, including the organs involved in reproduction and hormone regulation.
Explain the process of gametogenesis, fertilization, and embryonic development.
Evaluate the factors that influence reproductive health, including contraception, sexually transmitted infections (STIs), and infertility.
Demonstrate knowledge of reproductive anatomy and physiology in the context of prenatal care, childbirth, and postnatal development.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: Energy, Behavior, and Populations
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content examines how organisms acquire, use, and compete for energy—and how their behavioral and physiological strategies influence survival, reproductive fitness, and population success. Learners will analyze how changes in energy availability impact populations and trophic levels, affecting species interactions, population density, and long-term growth dynamics. They will also investigate how autotrophs and heterotrophs drive the flow of energy through ecosystems, connecting individual-level adaptations to ecosystem-wide patterns. 
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe how the behavioral responses of organisms impact their overall fitness and may contribute to the success of the population.
Explain the strategies organisms use to obtain and utilize energy.
Analyze how changes in energy availability affect populations and ecosystems.
Describe how the activities of autotrophs and heterotrophs support the flow of energy within an ecosystem.
Analyze how resource availability and population density interact to shape population growth dynamics.

pathways.title: Humans on the Moon
pathways.description: The exploration of the Moon is a global endeavor that necessitates investment from many nations. This pathway highlights the Apollo program, a previous lunar mission, and discusses NASA's efforts to design human landers that will transport astronauts to the Moon, with long-term goals of global lunar access and a reusable landing system.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will have an understanding of the Apollo missions and the astronauts who walked on the moon.
You will be able to describe how robotic and human explorers will build infrastructure for a long-term, sustainable presence on the Moon.
You will learn about how the human landing system will support future space travel.
You will design and land your own spacecraft on a specific target.

pathways.title: NASA | Designing the James Webb Space Telescope
pathways.description: Embark on an exciting journey through the universe and the brilliant minds behind the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), a groundbreaking instrument designed to revolutionize astrophysics and our understanding of galaxies. Discover the vision of James Webb and the innovative technology that powers this groundbreaking space mission, revealing the mysteries of space along the way.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will recall James Webb's contributions to space exploration and explain the mission of the James Webb Space Telescope.
You will analyze the scientific goals of the James Webb Space Telescope and evaluate the engineering challenges faced during its development.
You will compare and contrast key components like gold mirrors, beryllium, Kapton, and carbon, assessing their roles in Webb's construction and functioning.
You will explain how Dr. Lugaro's experiences and insights as an astrophysicist related to the interdisciplinary character of space exploration.
You will design a paper model of the James Webb Space Telescope, showcasing your ability to identify its critical components and explain their functions within the system

pathways.title: Water Properties Information
pathways.description: Looking at water, you might think that it's the most simple thing around. Pure water is practically colorless, odorless, and tasteless. But it's not at all simple and plain and it is vital for all life on Earth. Where there is water there is life, and where water is scarce, life has to struggle or just "throw in the towel." Continue on to learn about dozens of water properties.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Explore the physical properties of water
Explore the chemical properties of water

pathways.title: NASA | Density and Buoyancy
pathways.description: Join us in exploring the core concepts of fluid dynamics, focusing on density and buoyancy. This pathway covers the fundamental forces in fluids, both on Earth and in space, and includes engaging activities to deepen your understanding of these principles.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will define key terms like density, buoyancy, and microgravity.
Explain the principles of fluid mechanics and how they apply to different environments.
Compare and contrast the behavior of liquids in gravity and microgravity conditions.

pathways.title: Ayanna Thomas, PhD - Psychologist
pathways.description: Dr. Ayanna Thomas is a psychologist, researcher, mentor, and teacher interested in how our understanding of memory can inform classrooms, courtrooms, and other parts of everyday life.

pathways.title: COVID-19 Lockdowns and Extreme Inequality (4 Parts)
pathways.description: The module considers the interactions between covid-19 lockdown measures, also known as non-pharmaceutical interventions, and high levels of inequality. The goal of this module is that at the end of it, you, the learner, should be able to (1.) explain how inequality impacts the effectiveness of lockdowns; (2) explain how inequality interacts with the economic effects of lockdowns and ; (3) review the effectiveness and impact of lockdown policies implemented to stop Covid-19 in the city of Cape Town, as an example for other major cities in the Global South that are characterised by extreme inequality.

pathways.title: NASA | Exploring Telescopes in Space
pathways.description: Explore the wonders of the cosmos through some of the most advanced space telescopes ever created. In this engaging pathway, you will discover the Hubble Space Telescope, James Webb Space Telescope, NASA's Fermi Satellite, and more. You will learn how these cutting-edge instruments work, what celestial mysteries they're designed to unveil, and will have the opportunity to conduct a fun and educational activity at home or in the classroom.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will analyze how telescopes construct our understanding of the universe.
You will differentiate between the Hubble and James Webb Space Telescopes.
You will assess the benefits of designing telescopes capable of detecting diverse wavelengths, providing insights into the advantages of multi-wavelength observation.
You will explore careers accessible to those with an interest in space technology, gaining knowledge of potential professional opportunities.
You will perform your own celestial observations either at home or in the classroom, fostering practical experience in astronomical observation.

pathways.title: Hostile or Closed Environments
pathways.description: The microbial ecosystem inside spacecrafts plays a big role in everyday astronaut life in space. Microbes can change characteristics in space, and micro-organisms that naturally live on the human body are transferred more easily from person to person in closed habitats, such as the space station. Stress hormone levels are also elevated and the immune system is altered, which could lead to increased susceptibility to allergies or other illnesses. This pathway discusses the impacts of spacecraft's hostile and closed environment on astronaut immune systems, and moreover, how they can prevent and combat disease in space.
learning_objectives: Develop an understanding of the hostile and closed environment of space as well as the implication of this for space travel.
Examine microbes in space, considering their effect on astronauts and the space station.
Describe the effects of space travel on the human immune system.
Develop an understanding of the life support systems used for space travel.

pathways.title: Exploring Exothermic and Endothermic Reactions
pathways.description: Discover exothermic and endothermic reactions, key energy concepts in chemical processes. Learn about activation energy, Gibbs free energy, and enthalpy through real-world examples like bonfires and melting snow, while analyzing energy profiles and applications.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to, define key energy concepts such as exothermic and endothermic reactions, activation energy, enthalpy, and Gibbs free energy.
Differentiate between exothermic and endothermic reactions based on energy transfer and their effects on the surroundings.
Interpret and analyze energy profile diagrams, including activation energy and enthalpy changes, for various chemical reactions.
Identify and explain real-world examples of energy changes in chemical reactions, such as combustion and phase changes.
Explore the role of catalysts in lowering activation energy and their impact on reaction rates.
Apply scientific reasoning to energy changes in physical and chemical processes, aligning with practical applications and theoretical understanding.
Evaluate the relationship between energy changes and reaction spontaneity, considering endergonic and exergonic reactions in real-life contexts.

pathways.title: Skeletal System: Anatomy, Health, and Common Disorders
pathways.description: Explore the wonders of our skeletal system! Uncover how bones and joints keep us standing strong, safeguard our organs, and help us move, while investigating careers linked to evolution of the human skeleton.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and function of the skeletal system, including bones, joints, cartilage, and connective tissues.
Explain the role of the skeletal system in providing support, protection, movement, and mineral storage.
Analyze common skeletal disorders and conditions, such as osteoporosis, arthritis, fractures, and scoliosis.
Evaluate the impact of nutrition, exercise, and lifestyle choices on bone health and skeletal development.
Demonstrate an understanding of skeletal anatomy and physiology through the identification of major bones and bone landmarks.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: Integumentary System: Structure, Health, and Protection Strategies
pathways.description: Embark on a journey through your skin! Discover its layers, functions, and how it works tirelessly to protect you, all while exploring exciting careers in skincare science.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and function of the integumentary system, including the skin, hair, nails, and associated glands.
Explain the role of the integumentary system in protecting the body from physical, chemical, and microbial threats.
Analyze common disorders and conditions of the integumentary system, such as acne, eczema, burns, and skin cancer.
Evaluate strategies for maintaining skin health and preventing skin disorders, including proper hygiene, sun protection, and nutrition.
Demonstrate an understanding of the integumentary system's role in thermoregulation, sensation, and vitamin D synthesis.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: Earth's Changing Climate
pathways.description: Unit 12 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. Earth's climate is a sensitive system that is subject to dramatic shifts over varying time scales. Today human activities are altering the climate system by increasing concentrations of heat-trapping greenhouse gases in the atmosphere, which raises global temperatures. In this unit, examine the science behind global climate change and explore its potential impacts on natural ecosystems and human societies.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming

pathways.title: The Chemistry of Life – Properties and Functions of Water
pathways.description:
Dive into the unique properties of water that make it indispensable for life on Earth. From its molecular structure and hydrogen bonding to its role as a solvent and its behavior across states of matter, this pathway explores the chemistry of water and its relevance to biological processes. You will develop a deep understanding of water's essential properties and how they sustain life.
To explore how water's unique chemistry impacts environmental systems and societal challenges, visit Water’s Role in Environmental and Societal Processes.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to comprehend the molecular structure of water, including its bonding, polarity, and hydrogen bonding.
Analyze the physical and chemical properties of water, such as cohesion, adhesion, high specific heat, and phase transitions.
Explain the significance of water's properties in biological and ecological processes.

pathways.title: Introduction to Photosynthesis
pathways.description: Plants, algae and even some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy through a process known as photosynthesis. Explore how plants capture, convert, and store light energy from the sun, by focusing on both the light-dependent and light-independent reactions that drive this complex system. Investigate the specialized structures within leaves that facilitate photosynthesis, examine how environmental factors influence photosynthetic rate, and apply experimental techniques to observe photosynthesis in action.
learning_objectives: Identify the main structures within plants and leaves that support photosynthesis.
Describe the process of photosynthesis.
Explain how plants capture energy from sunlight using photosynthetic pigments.
Distinguish between light-dependent and light-independent reactions, including their location and outputs.
Evaluate environmental factors that affect photosynthetic rate, including light intensity, carbon dioxide levels, and temperature.
Apply experimental techniques, such as the floating leaf disk assay and pH-based methods, to investigate photosynthesis.

pathways.title: Energy: Forms, Changes, and Conservation
pathways.description: This pathway is designed for Senior Secondary School 1 Physics in alignment with the Nigerian BEC curriculum. It introduces the concept of energy, its various forms, and how it transforms from one type to another. You will explore energy conservation, perform interactive experiments, and apply energy principles to real-world scenarios. By the end of this pathway, you will understand energy conversions and be able to analyze and answer questions related to energy conservation.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you should be able to define and explain its different forms.
Perform virtual experiments to observe energy transformations.
Answer questions related to energy conservation and conversions.

pathways.title: Patterns in the Sky: Eclipses
pathways.description:
Throughout history, eclipses have struck a chord of wonder and amazement in people who witness them. Without understanding the scientific background of these phenomena, a disappearing Sun or a darkening Moon seemed scary in many cultures. In this learning pathway which spans 6 days (310 minutes) and is accompanied by a student guide, you will explore some of the legends that have been crafted over time to explain these strange phenomena and learn more about how the interactions between the Sun, Moon, and Earth cause lunar and solar eclipses. You will model both solar and lunar eclipses to get a really good idea of how they work and how often they occur. You will use your knowledge to explain this science along with a historical legend in a brochure, showing how science can “debunk” the legend.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1: 50 minutes in class, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2: 50 minutes in class, items 3-7 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3; 50 minutes in class, items 8-9 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4-5; 100 minutes in class, item 10 of the pathway and in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 6; 60 minutes in class, items 11-12 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the rotation of the Moon around Earth and will be able to draw the relative positions of the Earth, Moon, and Sun during a full moon phase and during a new moon phase.
Be able to model a solar eclipse with a model Sun and model Moon and will be able to explain why seeing a full solar eclipse is very rare.
Model a lunar eclipse with a model that includes the plane of the orbital path of the Moon and be able to use this model to explain why lunar eclipses do not occur at each new Moon phase.

pathways.title: Where Does Biodiversity Come From?
pathways.description: The process of natural selection, shaped by genetic variations, has led to the incredible biodiversity we witness today, showcasing the adaptability and resilience of life on Earth. Assess your knowledge of gene flow, evolutionary processes, and DNA mutations, gaining insights into species responses to evolutionary pressures. Delve into the complexities of biodiversity, including species dispersal, historical extinctions, and the current crisis driven by human activities. For more content about biodiversity, take the Biodiversity Course.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will recall the key concepts of evolution, including natural selection, adaptation, and the role of variations in populations.
You will be able explain the significance of evolutionary trees and phylogenetic systematics in depicting relationships among organisms over time.
You will analyze the impact of mutations on the formation of alleles, explaining how different alleles lead to variations in traits among individuals within a population.

You will explore the implications of speciation and extinction for the diversity of species and ecosystems, as well as their impact on the conservation of biodiversity and the management of endangered species.
You will Explore the practical implications of these evolutionary causes for species variation and the significance of preserving this diversity in the context of conservation efforts.
You will investigate the historical extinctions and the current crisis caused by human activities, gaining insights into the impact of human actions on Earth's biodiversity and ecosystems.

You will Evaluate your understanding of natural selection, phylogenetic systematics, extinction, and mutation through self-assessment, fostering a deeper understanding of their significance in evolutionary processes.

pathways.title: Global Change
pathways.description: This pathway describes and explains global changes in the environment, the causes of these changes, and their consequences. Learners will be asked to explain and evaluate the cause and effect of climate change on a global spectrum using environmental science and data science principles.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Identify how the Earth’s temperature, oceans, and biodiversity are changing globally.
Explain how human activity has an impact on global warming, ocean warming and acidification, and biodiversity.
Manipulate climate-related parameters to draw conclusions regarding carbon dioxide emissions and global climate change.
Formulate data-driven hypotheses and explanations for how to reduce global climate change.

pathways.title: Mighty Mice in Space
pathways.description: This series of lessons and videos from The Jackson Laboratory allows teachers and students to learn about the Mighty Mice in Space research project and immerse themselves in the research process. The three lessons were created for high school students at the introductory biology level. The lessons are not content driven, but rather emphasize skill development in areas such as prediction, experimental design, data interpretation and graphing.
learning_objectives: Identify possible outcomes of the Mighty Mice experiments
Conduct data analysis by calculating averages and graphing the muscle masses of Mighty Mice and non-engineered mice
Interpret and discuss the experimental results for the Mighty Mice experiments
Conduct online research into human bone or muscle disorder
Create and present a poster describing a human bone or muscle disorder and how the Mighty Mice experiments could help treatment of the disorder

pathways.title: Solving System Equations
pathways.description: Master solving systems of linear equations by graphing. Learn how to find intersection points and interpret slope and y-intercept. Gain proficiency in algebraic methods such as substitution and elimination to solve real-world problems.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to graph and solve systems of linear equations by finding the intersection points of two lines.
Interpret slope and y-intercept in the context of linear equations and their solutions.
Apply algebraic methods such as substitution and elimination to solve systems of linear equations.
Analyze solutions to systems of equations to determine if they have one solution, no solution, or infinite solutions.
Use mathematical reasoning to solve real-world problems involving linear equations in two variables.

pathways.title: Flowering Plants: Structure, Reproduction, and Growth
pathways.description: Flowering plants, or angiosperms, are among the most diverse and important plants on Earth. This pathway explores the structures, reproduction, and functions of flowering plants, including the differences between monocots and dicots, reproductive structures, germination types, and the roles of roots, stems, and leaves. Through videos, texts, and thought-provoking questions, you will gain a comprehensive understanding of plant biology and its significance in ecosystems.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to differentiate between monocot and dicot plants based on structural and physiological traits.
List the reproductive structures of flowering plants and explain their roles in reproduction.
Discuss the two types of germination (epigeal and hypogeal) and their significance.
Describe the structure and functions of roots, stems, and leaves, including leaf arrangement.

pathways.title: Sam Spaulding - Robotics Scientist
pathways.description: Sam Spaulding is a robotics engineer who hopes to help develop new types of social robots that serve a purpose in education, art, and keeping people company.

pathways.title: The Economy and the Coronavirus - Macroeconomic and Financial Issues
pathways.description:
The pathway adresses various challenges for the economy is facing in times of coronovirus. It is also discussing the perspectives for an post-coronavirus economy.
This sub-pathway deals in particular with maconomic and financial issues.

pathways.title: Energy
pathways.description: Energy is a key concept that explains how physical systems behave, and the principle of energy conservation is essential in understanding various phenomena in physics and engineering. This pathway provides a collection of interactive simulations that demonstrate energy conservation, energy transformations, and kinetic energy. Additionally, explore energy in action with examples of renewable energy sources used to generate power.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will be able to identify the different forms of energy.
Conduct experiments on various forms of energy.
Explain the law of conservation of energy.
Recognize energy uses and conversions in real world examples.

pathways.title: Structure and Topology of DNA
pathways.description: This pathway provides an in-depth exploration of the intricate structure and topology of DNA, unraveling the fundamental principles that determine its shape, function, and interactions. Key concepts such as base pairing, helical geometry, chromatin organization, and topological constraints are examined to provide a comprehensive understanding of DNA architecture. The role of topoisomerases and higher-order chromatin structures in maintaining genetic stability and function is also explored in depth.
learning_objectives: Explain the primary and secondary structures of DNA, including base pairing, helical geometry, and chromatin organization.
Describe topological constraints such as supercoiling, knotting, and looping, and explain how they affect DNA function and stability.
Identify the functions of Class I and Class II topoisomerases in DNA topology maintenance and explore the hierarchical organization of chromatin and nucleosomes.

pathways.title: Mastering Simple Harmonic Motion: Forces, Waves, and Energy in Motion
pathways.description: Explore the physics of oscillations and energy transformation by understanding simple harmonic motion and uniform circular motion. Using pendulums, springs, and simulations, you’ll observe how forces interact with mass and displacement, revealing the underlying patterns of motion.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will be able to explain the principles of simple harmonic motion (SHM) and uniform circular motion and describe their interconnection in real-world systems like pendulums and springs.
Analyze the factors influencing the period (T) of oscillation, including mass, spring constant, and displacement, and predict how these variables affect motion.
Apply the concept of Hooke's Law to simple harmonic systems to understand force, displacement, and potential energy in mechanical systems.
Evaluate real-world applications of simple harmonic motion, such as pendulums, car suspension systems, and vibrating systems, emphasizing their role in engineering and technology.
Interpret energy transformations in oscillating systems, identifying how kinetic and potential energy are conserved and exchanged during motion.

pathways.title: Meet the Martians
pathways.description: This pathway introduces several of the team members that were involved in the mission that sent the Perseverance rover to Mars.
learning_objectives: Identify some of the members of the mars perseverance mission.

pathways.title: Solving Real-World Problems with Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
pathways.description: A system of equations consists of two or more equations with the same variables. The solution set includes all ordered pairs that satisfy each equation. Master solving systems of linear equations using graphing and elimination techniques, and apply these methods to real-world scenarios like travel times and cost analysis.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to visualize and interpret the solution of a system of linear equations using graphical methods to identify intersection points on a Cartesian plane.
Solve systems of linear equations in two variables using substitution and elimination methods, applying algebraic principles.
Analyze and solve real-world problems involving systems of equations, such as travel times, cost analysis, and mixtures, to develop mathematical reasoning and application skills.

pathways.title: Creating a Clear Image of Myopia: Discovering the Causes and Developing Treatments
pathways.description: Myopia, also known as near-sightedness, causes blurry distance vision and is estimated to affect over 30% of the world’s population. At Emory University School of Medicine in the US, Professor Machelle Pardue and her research team are working to better understand the underlying causes of myopia. They hope their discoveries will identify novel therapeutic interventions for treating myopia in children.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define axial length and its significance in ophthalmology.
Recall the definition of emmetropisation and its role in achieving clear vision.
Explain how myopia occurs, including the imbalance between optical power and axial length.
Analyze the impact of genetic and environmental manipulations on eye growth.

pathways.title: Biodiversity Decline
pathways.description: Unit 9 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. Living species on Earth may number anywhere from 5 million to 50 million or more. Although we have yet to identify and describe most of these life forms, we know that many are endangered today by development, pollution, over-harvesting, and other threats. Earth has experienced mass extinctions in the past due to natural causes, but the factors reducing biodiversity today increasingly stem from human activities. In this unit, we see how scientists measure biodiversity, how it benefits our species, and what trends might cause Earth's next mass extinction.

pathways.title: First Stars
pathways.description: Explore the birth and evolution of stars, the universe's expansion through redshift, and the cosmic timeline from the first light to the formation of complex elements. This pathway of images illuminates the awe-inspiring mass and composition of celestial bodies, guiding you through the universe's grand timeline. For more information, read What Were the First Stars Like?
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to describe what redshift is and explain how the James Webb Space Telescope uses this phenomenon to observe the light from the earliest stars and galaxies.

Students will learn about the life cycle of stars, particularly massive Population III stars, and understand how successive generations of stars have contributed to the increasing complexity of elements in the universe.

Students will be able to outline the key events in the timeline of the universe, including the formation of the first stars, the period of Recombination, the era of Reionization, and how these events relate to the concepts of ionization and mass of early stars.

pathways.title: Frontiers of Physics
pathways.description: This pathway investigates the mystery, surprise, adventure, and discovery in exploring new frontiers. The search for answers is that much more intriguing because the answer to any question always leads to new questions. As our understanding of nature becomes more complete, nature still retains its sense of mystery and never loses its ability to awe us. Looking through the lens of physics allows us to look both backward and forward in time, and we can discern marvelous patterns in nature with its myriad rules and complex connections. Moreover, we continue looking ever deeper and ever further, probing the basic structure of matter, energy, space, and time, and wondering about the scope of the universe, its beginnings, and its future. The essential ideas that have been supported and justified in the study of physics are now used as a framework to investigate and justify new ideas.
learning_objectives: Discuss the expansion of the universe.
Explain how the big bang gave rise to the universe we see today.
Explain the effect of gravity on light.
Discuss black holes.
Explain quantum gravity.
Define superstring theory.
Explain the relationship between superstring theory and the big bang.
Discuss the evidence for the existence of dark matter.
Explain neutrino oscillations and the consequences thereof.
Explain complex systems.
Discuss chaotic behavior of different systems.
Identify superconductors and their uses.
Discuss the need for a high-tc superconductor.
Identify sample questions to be asked on the largest scales.
Identify sample questions to be asked on the intermediate scale.
Identify sample questions to be asked on the smallest scales.

pathways.title: Atomic Structure
pathways.description: Electrons behave as both waves and particles, influencing atomic structure and spectroscopy. Explain atomic structure by identifying subatomic particles, modeling atoms, interpreting isotopic data, and analyzing electron behavior using quantum theory and spectroscopy, in alignment with modern atomic theory.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the development and limitations of different atomic models, including those proposed by Thomson, Rutherford, Bohr, and the quantum mechanical model.
Describe the structure of an atom by identifying subatomic particles, calculating atomic number, mass number, isotopes, and understanding ions and their charges.
Interpret the concept of atomic orbitals and electron configuration using principles of quantum mechanics, including wave-particle duality and Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle.

pathways.title: Capacitance in Action: Voltage, Charge, and Energy
pathways.description:
Welcome to an exciting journey into the world of Physics! In this pathway, we're going to explore a fundamental concept that is crucial to understanding the physics of our everyday lives: Capacitance. Application of the capacitance concept is key in our day-in and day-out activities.
So, let's get our scientific thinking caps on with a vocabulary building activity around essential concepts.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you should be able to explain the relationships between voltage, charge, stored energy, and capacitance in electrical systems.
Describe the factors affecting capacitance, including plate area, plate separation, and dielectric material, using simulations and experiments.
Investigate how capacitors store and discharge energy in simple circuits, such as an RC circuit connected to a light bulb.
Analyze data from simulations to predict changes in stored energy and capacitance when circuit parameters are altered.
Demonstrate an understanding of capacitance in real-world contexts by explaining its role in various electronic devices.
Develop critical thinking skills by evaluating and comparing different configurations of capacitors in the simulation environment.

pathways.title: Living in a Material World: The Importance of Materials Science and Engineering
pathways.description: Interested in the science of making, characterizing, and testing materials? Materials Science and Engineering (MSE) explores the relationship between a material structure and properties on all levels and seeks to develop new materials and applications through innovative and creative thinking.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the importance of testing materials and how this can inform new materials' development.
You will learn the significance of a material's mechanical and chemical properties.
You will understand the importance of microstructure in determining a material's properties.
You will have some insight into the career opportunities associated with Materials Science.

pathways.title: Identifying Acids and Bases
pathways.description: Explore the fascinating world of pH, acids, and bases. Discover how to measure and predict pH, analyze acid-base strength and concentration, and connect molecular structure to solution behavior.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to identify whether a solution is acidic, basic, or neutral using pH measurements and indicators.
Compare the strength and concentration of acids and bases and their effects on pH.
Demonstrate how dilution and molecular properties influence the behavior of acids and bases.

pathways.title: Cellular Respiration in Plants
pathways.description: How do plants transform light into the energy that fuels life? In this pathway, explore how cellular respiration—alongside photosynthesis—keeps plants alive and powers growth, reproduction, and even innovations like biofuels. Using interactive simulations, experimental protocols, and compelling visualizations, investigate the molecules, organelles, and environmental conditions that shape respiration in plant cells.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Outline the chemical equation for cellular respiration and identify the inputs and outputs.
Describe the role of glucose and ATP in providing usable energy for cellular processes.
Identify the organelles responsible for cellular respiration, and explain their roles.
Compare aerobic and anaerobic respiration in plants.
Analyze how cellular respiration supports plant functions such as growth, fruiting, and response to environmental conditions.
Investigate how environmental variables, such as oxygen availability and temperature, affect the rate of cellular respiration in plants.
Use qualitative and quantitative tools (e.g., pH indicators and spectrophotometers) to measure photosynthesis and cellular respiration.

pathways.title: How Can Biodiversity Be Protected?
pathways.description: Explore the critical role of conservation in protecting Earth's diverse life forms through legislation, global agreements, and habitat preservation. Navigate the IUCN Red List to gain insights into the conservation status of threatened species. Discover the link between economic pressures, legislation, and biodiversity protection, and learn essential strategies for preserving Earth's biodiversity. This pathway offers a deep dive into environmental science, featuring engaging activities, assessments, and tutorials on safeguarding biodiversity. For more content about biodiversity, take the Biodiversity Course.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will examine the significance of legislation and global agreements in biodiversity conservation.
you will explore the active role that individuals can play in biodiversity conservation.
you will identify key biodiversity champions and their significant contributions to biodiversity conservation.
You will recognize the interconnectedness of international policies, individual actions, and technological tools in promoting and protecting biodiversity.
You will self-assess your ability to analyze the significance of the IUCN Red List and global strategies in guiding global biodiversity conservation initiatives through a set of questions.

pathways.title: Abel Sanchez, PhD - Information Engineer
pathways.description: Dr. Abel Sanchez is natural-born explorer, lover of music, and an information engineer at MIT. His work involves a lot of travel, working with computers and big data processors, teaching students, and meeting with other people and working together to solve problems.

pathways.title: Cellular Structure and Function
pathways.description: Explore the fascinating world of cells—the building blocks of life! In this pathway, you’ll discover the structure and function of prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, learn how organelles work together to sustain life, and investigate the limits of cell size. Through interactive simulations, real-world case studies, and inspiring stories, you’ll connect cellular biology to cutting-edge research and practical applications. This journey will deepen your understanding of how cells power the living world and inspire you to think about their role in science and innovation.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the structure and function of components common to both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
Differentiate between the structural features of eukaryotic, prokaryotic, and plant cells.
Analyze the relationship between cell size and function, focusing on the surface-area-to-volume ratio.
Understand the roles of macromolecules, membranes, and organelles in supporting cell function.

pathways.title: Plant Tropisms
pathways.description: Plants possess remarkable ways of sensing and responding to their environment. This pathway explores how plants perceive and respond to stimuli such as light, gravity, and touch.
learning_objectives: Define tropism in plants.
Explain the process of phototropism, including the role of auxin and photoreceptors like phototropins and phytochromes.
