Describe how plants respond to gravity through gravitropism, and outline the function of amyloplasts and the distribution of auxin in this process.
Discuss thigmotropism, thigmonastic movements, and thigmomorphogenesis as responses to mechanical stimuli such as touch or wind.
Identify how plant tropisms contribute to survival and adaptation, particularly in response to environmental challenges like shade, poor soil anchorage, or herbivore damage.
Compare the effects of auxin in shoots versus roots and explain why auxin promotes growth in one and inhibits it in the other.

pathways.title: Logarithms: Properties and Types
pathways.description:
Learn about logarithms, the properties of logarithms, and how they are used in solving exercises.
A logarithm is a mathematical function that determines the exponent to which a given base must be raised to produce a specified number. It is denoted as logb​(x), where b is the base and x is the number whose logarithm is being calculated. The logarithm of a number x to base b is the power to which b must be raised to equal x. In equation form, if y=logb​(x), then x=by. Logarithms are commonly used in various scientific disciplines to simplify calculations involving exponential growth, decay, and complex multiplicative relationships.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define the concept of logarithm.
Apply the properties of the logarithmic function in solving exercises.

pathways.title: Including More Student Voices in the Conversation
pathways.description: Equitable classrooms include a variety of viewpoints and voices in class conversations. Encouraging students from all backgrounds to contribute to the classroom discourse takes a little extra work but brings innumerable rewards. In this pathway, we’ll look at ways to get more students actively engaged during class, whether the setting is the smallest seminar or the largest lecture.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will recall key strategies such as cold calling, warm calling, and the use of physical proximity to foster inclusive classroom discussions.
Articulate the purpose behind various inclusive techniques and how they serve to engage a broader range of student voices.
Implement strategies like partnered work and alternative participation avenues in a lesson plan to enhance classroom inclusivity.
Examine classroom dynamics to pinpoint obstacles to equal participation and evaluate which strategies might effectively overcome these challenges.
Assess the impact of inclusive teaching methods on student engagement by gathering and reflecting on student feedback and participation data.
Develop a comprehensive approach that integrates multiple inclusive teaching strategies to actively promote equity in classroom discussions.

pathways.title: Digital Innovation and Technology
pathways.description: In this pathway, you will learn and understand how staff use technology and business processes to organize data into useful information, answer questions and solve problems.​
learning_objectives: You will understand how advancements made in technology are changing our world.

pathways.title: Sex-Linked Characteristics
pathways.description: This pathway examines how the inheritance of characteristics determined by genes located on sex chromosomes differs from that determined by genes located on autosomes.
learning_objectives: State the Chromosome Theory of Inheritance.
Use 𝘋𝘳𝘰𝘴𝘰𝘱𝘩𝘪𝘭𝘢 genetic nomenclature proficiently.
Explain how the Chromosome Theory of Inheritance was supported by the discovery of a sex-linked gene in 𝘋𝘳𝘰𝘴𝘰𝘱𝘩𝘪𝘭𝘢.
Perform genetic crosses involving the inheritance of sex-linked genes.
Explain the purpose of X-chromosome inactivation.

pathways.title: Atomic Structure and Models
pathways.description: All atoms of an element share the same number of protons. In this pathway, explore atomic structure, isotopes, ions, and atomic models, including Dalton’s and Rutherford’s theories, and learn to calculate relative atomic mass using isotopic abundances.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure of atoms in terms of protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Explain Dalton’s atomic theory and Rutherford’s model of the atom.
Differentiate between isotopes, ions, and nuclide symbols.
Calculate relative atomic mass from isotopic abundances.

pathways.title: Looking Forward: Our Global Experiment
pathways.description: Unit 13 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. Emerging technologies offer potential solutions to environmental problems. Over the long-term, human ingenuity may ensure the survival not only of our own species but of the complex ecosystems that enhance the quality of human life. In this unit, examine the wide range of efforts now underway to mitigate the worst effects of man-made environmental change, looking toward those that will have a positive impact on the future of our habitable planet.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming

pathways.title: The Science of Sound and Music
pathways.description: Explore the fundamental principles of sound, vibrations, and music. Students will investigate how sound is produced, how amplitude and frequency affect volume and pitch, and how sound can be recorded and visualized. The pathway ends with an interactive activity where students will use beakers to play a musical piece, applying their knowledge of frequency and tuning. To explore these concepts in action, visit the project at Listening to Waves - Science of Music.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to understand sound production by learning how sound is created through vibrations, and how different objects produce sound.
Investigate how frequency affects the pitch of a sound.
Examine how changing the amplitude of a signal influences the loudness of a sound.
Use spectrograms and oscilloscopes to analyze and compare different sound frequencies.
Tune beakers to specific frequencies and play a song using scientific principles of sound and pitch.

pathways.title: Water Quality Questions & Answers
pathways.description: What is in that water that you just drank? Is it just hydrogen and oxygen atoms? Is it safe for drinking? All water is of a certain "quality" (and you can't tell by just looking), but what does "water quality" really mean? It can be thought of as a measure of the suitability of water for a particular use based on selected physical, chemical, and biological characteristics. Here at the Water Science School, we have the answers to your questions about water quality.
learning_objectives: Explore aspects which can affect water quality
Identify qualities of water
Clarify the definition of clean water

pathways.title: Human Exposure to Space Radiation
pathways.description: Ionizing radiation has dire effects on all materials and biological organisms in space. This means that any spacecraft or human has to be well-prepared and protected from exposure to radiation while on their journey beyond the Earth's atmosphere. In this pathway, learn about the biological effects of space radiation and the research being conducted as part of the Artemis I mission to protect astronauts.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the difference between the radiation on Earth and radiation in space.
You will learn about NASA's research into the effects of ionizing radiation on human health and material integrity.
You will discover the various strategies used to protect astronauts from space radiation.

pathways.title: The Human Reproductive System
pathways.description: The human body is made up of intricate systems that work together to support life, growth, and reproduction. This pathway will introduce you to tissues and reproductive systems. It highlights the structure and function of major body systems and connects these to real-world health and developmental processes. You will explore the menstrual cycle and birth control.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify the four main tissue types and their roles in the body.
Describe the structure and function of male and female reproductive systems, including phases of the menstrual cycle.
Understand contraceptive methods and their effectiveness.

pathways.title: Guide to Water
pathways.description: Water is a vital resource that sustains all forms of life on Earth, and its unique properties make it a fascinating subject of study. This pathway is designed to help you understand the various properties of water and how they contribute to its unique behavior. You will conduct experiments to observe water's properties, such as cohesion, surface tension, capillary action, and high specific heat. For further information on the properties and behavior of water, please check out Science Mom's Guide to Water videos.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you'll be able to explain how water's unique properties, such as cohesion, surface tension, and capillary action, contribute to its distinct behavior.
You will perform a series of experiments to observe the different properties of water.
You'll explain how water, as a universal solvent, can dissolve a broad range of substances.
You will explain the importance of water's specific heat capacity in regulating temperature and its role in natural phenomena and human applications.

pathways.title: The Journey of Reproduction: How Life Begins
pathways.description: Have you ever wondered how animals multiply or give birth to their own kind? This happens through reproduction, the biological process by which new life is created. There are two main types: sexual and asexual reproduction. While procreation is often used synonymously with reproduction, it specifically refers to sexual reproduction, which involves both a male and female parent. In this pathway, you will explore how sexual reproduction works in humans and animals, learn about reproductive organs, fertilization, and fetal development, and understand how different species ensure the survival of their kind.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the concept of reproduction and its importance in the continuation of life.
Identify and describe the functions of male and female reproductive organs.
Understand the process of fertilization, fetal development, and birth in animals and humans.
Answer fundamental questions related to reproduction and reproductive health.
Investigate how different species reproduce and compare reproductive strategies in nature.

pathways.title: Surface Water Basics
pathways.description: Surface water is the most recognizable part of the water cycle that we can see. Lakes and rivers may be the first types of surface water that come to mind but don't forget those snowbanks in your front yard! We think of oceans when we think of surface water but did you remember to add glaciers to your list?
learning_objectives: Identify the characteristics of various surface waters
Explore environmental problems in surface water and the probable causes
Gain an understanding of the importance and use of streamflow information
Recognize the role of surface water in the water cycle
Understand the effect of climate change on the Earth's surface water

pathways.title: Taxonomy and Biodiversity
pathways.description: How do we classify and organize the many different kinds of creatures on Earth? This learning sequence explores the systems used to categorize species and introduces key groups of plants, animals, and fungi.
learning_objectives: Explain how organisms are named.
Use a phylogenetic tree to determine relationships between species.
Classify organisms into kingdoms.

pathways.title: Environmental Effects on Phenotype
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores how environmental factors influence gene expression and contribute to phenotypic diversity. In this pathway, learners will investigate the concept of phenotypic plasticity, where individuals with the same genotype can exhibit different phenotypes under varying environmental conditions. Learners will examine examples of how light, temperature, nutrition, stress or other environmental cues can alter gene expression, leading to observable changes in traits. Coming soon: new content on phenotypic variation in humans.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe how the same genotype can result in multiple phenotypes under different environmental conditions.

pathways.title: Introduction to Soil: Ecosystems, Nutrients, Structure
pathways.description: Soil is a living, evolving ecosystem that shapes plant health, ecosystem dynamics, and agricultural productivity. This pathway introduces the physical, chemical, and biological properties of soil, from its formation and structure to the complex web of life it supports.
learning_objectives: Describe the composition and physical structure of soil.
Describe sources of soil organic matter, including decayed plant and animal material.
Explain how soils form.
Recognize and interpret soil horizons within a soil profile.
Describe the role of decomposers such as fungi and insects in the breakdown of organic matter, and explain how their activity contributes to nutrient cycling and soil fertility.
Describe the biodiversity found in soil ecosystems and explain how biodiversity enhances soil quality and plant productivity.
Evaluate the environmental and agricultural consequences of soil degradation, including salinization, erosion, and contamination, and propose strategies for soil conservation and rehabilitation.

pathways.title: Faster than Light
pathways.description:
People are often told that light is the fastest thing in the universe but, is this always the case? Investigate the findings of a Harvard research group that slowed light to sub-highway speeds, in this pathway. There is also an accompanying student guide. The recommended learning time spans 6 days of lessons (200 minutes).
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes, found in the student guide.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 50 minutes, items 1-5 in the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4, 45 minutes, item 6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 30 minutes, items 7-10 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 30 minutes, found in the student guide.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to understand that the material through which light travels can affect its speed and wavelength, but the frequency of the light remains unchanged.
Create a model of how light interacts with matter.
Understand how waves interact.

pathways.title: Preparing Artemis for the Moon
pathways.description: The Moon, Earth's only natural satellite, is the focus of NASA's Artemis program. This pathway highlights the various characteristics of the Moon, including its size, distance from Earth, composition, and unique features, like ice at its South Pole. Through videos, podcasts, and educational resources, the NASA Artemis program aims to increase public understanding of the Moon and its role in humanity's future in space.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be familiar with the history of lunar exploration and discovery.
You will understand the basic characteristics of Earth's Moon, including its size, distance, composition, and geology.
You will be able to calculate the distance between Earth and the Moon, and the diameter of the Moon.
You will understand the scientific and technological challenges of lunar exploration and the Moon's role in deep space exploration.

pathways.title: Plant Structure, Growth, and Adaptation
pathways.description: Botany professor Dr. Joyce Onyenedum guides you through the anatomy and development of major plant organs—roots, shoots, stems, and leaves. Across four episodes, the series explains how growth processes and tissue organization support plant function, environmental adaptation, and resilience, linking structure to evolution, ecology, and real-world applications.
learning_objectives: Understand how stem cells (meristems) drive continuous plant growth and organ formation.
Analyze how vascular tissue organization affects transport efficiency and structural support.
Recognize how anatomical adaptations reflect evolutionary responses to environmental pressures.
Connect plant structure–function principles to applications like agriculture, climate science, and biotechnology.

pathways.title: Energy
pathways.description: This core idea focuses on understanding the interactions of objects and how they transfer energy from one object or system of objects to another. Explaining how the total energy within a defined system changes only by transferring energy into or out of the system - conservation of energy - is also emphasized in this core idea. This pathway is designed for NGSS standard, MS-PS3.
learning_objectives: Construct and interpret graphical displays of data to describe the relationships of kinetic energy to the mass of an object and to the speed of an object. (MS-PS3-1)
Develop a model to describe that when the arrangement of objects interacting at a distance changes, different amounts of potential energy are stored in the system. (MS-PS3-2)
Apply scientific principles to design, construct, and test a device that either minimizes or maximizes thermal energy transfer. (MS-PS3-3)

Plan an investigation to determine the relationships among the energy transferred, the type of matter, the mass, and the change in the average kinetic energy of the particles as measured by the temperature of the sample. (MS-PS3-4)
Construct, use, and present arguments to support the claim that when the kinetic energy of an object changes, energy is transferred to or from the object. (MS-PS3-5)

pathways.title: The Second Law of Thermodynamics and Specific Heat
pathways.description:
Heat always moves from warmer to cooler areas. In this 255 minute lesson, you will explore specific heat capacity, entropy, and thermal energy transfer through videos, simulations, a case study, and the accompanying student guide. Then, you'll investigate how energy spreads within a closed system based on the Second Law of Thermodynamics.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 30 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 60 minutes, items 2-5 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 60 minutes, item 4 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 60 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 45 minutes, item 5 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain how the Second Law of Thermodynamics relates to temperature differences, heat flow, and entropy, and define specific heat capacity by exploring videos and simulations.
Plan and conduct an investigation to demonstrate how the specific heat capacity of a substance affects its ability to change temperature.

Plan and conduct an experiment to show how thermal energy transfers when two objects at different temperatures are combined in a closed system, leading to a more even distribution of energy, as explained by the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

pathways.title: Genetics and Heredity
pathways.description: A gene is a DNA segment carrying instructions for proteins. Explore how genes, alleles, and mutations influence inheritance and variation, using simulations to analyze genetic crosses, genotyping, and chromosomal disorders.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will define and apply key genetics terms, including gene, allele, chromosome, genotype, and phenotype.
Explain how DNA carries genetic information and how mutations can alter traits.
Demonstrate understanding of Mendelian inheritance patterns using dominant and recessive alleles.
Analyze genetic crosses and predict outcomes using simulations.
Evaluate chromosomal disorders caused by nondisjunction and their biological consequences.

pathways.title: How Were the Chemical Elements Born?
pathways.description: Stars are a cooking pot for all the naturally occurring chemical elements. Dr Maria Lugaro, of the Konkoly Observatory at the Research Centre for Astronomy and Earth Sciences in Hungary, is working out the stars’ recipes and what they can tell us about the birth of our solar system.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the nuclear physics processes involved in the creation of heavy elements in the cosmos, including nuclear fusion and neutron capture.
Describe the use of radioactive nuclei found in meteorites as a clock to trace back the history of the formation of the Solar System.
Explain the processes involved in the formation of elements in stars and how they can be used to understand the history of the Universe.

pathways.title: Fields and Fungicides: Mixing Microbiology and Social Science
pathways.description: Farmers often use fungicides to protect their crops from fungal diseases. However, there are worries that these fungicides can damage the environment if they leak into water sources and may even build antimicrobial resistance against treatments for human diseases. A unique team of early-career researchers from the University of Exeter and the University of Bristol in the UK is addressing this concern through environmental tests and direct engagement with farmers.
learning_objectives: Students will learn how researchers from different backgrounds and skills can collaborate to research one problem, and find potential solutions.
Learn what that Microbiology is a field of science that involves the study of microbes.
Learn that Social Sciences is a board range of disciplines that focuses on how people behave in society, and what drives their behavior.

pathways.title: Nergis Mavalvala, PhD - Astrophysicist
pathways.description: Dr. Nergis Mavalvala is a mom, lover of puzzles, sport, and an MIT astrophysicist. She was part of the research team that first detected gravitational waves. Her research interests have moved into quantum mechanics and at the same time STEAM education, breaking stereotypes and encouraging the study of both art and science.

pathways.title: NASA | Evidence of Life
pathways.description: For years, we have sought to answer this question: Are we alone in the universe? Teams working on the James Webb Space Telescope may be able to provide some answers. This pathway looks at the methods used to determine whether exoplanets have the necessary building blocks for life, as well as the evidence of potential life elsewhere in the universe that has already been found.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will have a greater understanding of the methods that the James Webb Space Telescope uses to find evidence of life on other planets.
You will learn about the different factors that the James Webb Space Telescope looks at to determine if planets can support life.
You will gain understanding about evidence that has been found that could indicate a possibility of life existing elsewhere in the universe.

pathways.title: Investigating Coral Bleaching: For Teachers
pathways.description: Explore this newly updated module for grades 6–12, launched in August 2024. Using current and historic data—like ocean surface temperature and accumulated heat stress—students will uncover how scientists monitor and evaluate the health of coral reefs around the world. This 5-level module includes interactive maps, graphs, and videos, offering a deep dive into the science of coral bleaching and its far-reaching global impacts.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to use maps to identify coral reef locations and understand factors affecting their distribution.
Analyze temperature data to assess heat stress on coral reefs and predict bleaching risk.
Monitor coral reef health using quadrat sampling to measure coral bleaching.
Evaluate bleaching predictions for coral reefs in the Florida Keys using real-time data.
Design and conduct an investigation using coral reef data to answer a research question.

pathways.title: Exploring Electric Fields: Forces, Charges, and Applications
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the fundamental concept of electric fields, their properties, and their interactions with charged objects. Through videos, simulations, and interactive exercises, you will explore how electric fields are generated, how their intensity is measured, and how they influence forces between charged bodies. By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define electric fields, calculate electric field strength, and analyze real-world applications of electrostatic forces.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain  the concept of an electric field and how it is generated by charged objects.
Describe electric field intensity and how it influences the forces acting on charged bodies.
Calculate electric field strength using mathematical formulas and simulations.
Analyze the effect of multiple charges on electric fields and predict their interactions.
Apply knowledge of electric fields to real-world scenarios, such as capacitors, conductors, and electrostatic forces.

pathways.title: The Lunar Surface: Science on the Moon
pathways.description: Discover various aspects of the lunar surface and the potential for future human exploration and habitation on the Moon. Topics include the geology of the surface, the challenges of growing food in a lunar environment, and the scientific significance of impact craters.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will learn about geological features and processes of the Moon.
You will learn about the potential for sustaining life on the Moon and the challenges of growing food on the Moon.
You will understand the scientific and technological innovations necessary to explore and use lunar resources.
You will discover the different fields of science that contribute to learning everything we need to know about the Moon.

pathways.title: Measuring Substances in a Lab
pathways.description: Accurate measurement, preparation, and handling of substances is essential for any scientist working in a laboratory. Explore the foundational techniques for making solutions and handling common lab glassware through interactive guides, step-by-step demonstrations, practical lab tips, and a close-up look at essential equipment.
learning_objectives: Identify common laboratory glassware and their appropriate uses, including beakers, flasks, pipettes, test tubes, and graduated cylinders.
Select the appropriate labware based on the level of accuracy required.
Apply best practices for safe and efficient laboratory setup, including the use of personal protective equipment (PPE).
Perform accurate mass and volume measurements using balances, weigh boats, pipettes, and cylinders.
Describe how to make a solution in a lab, including finding a recipe for the solution, doing the necessary calculations, and measuring and mixing the ingredients.

pathways.title: The Hierarchy and Diversity of Living Things
pathways.description: Carl Linnaeus developed taxonomy to classify organisms by similarities in form, structure, and genetics. Explore how scientists classify living organisms based on similarities in structure, function, and genetic makeup. This pathway covers taxonomy, binomial nomenclature, and phylogenetic trees, highlighting the role of morphology, DNA, and historical developments in grouping life on Earth.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe how living organisms are classified based on structural, functional, and genetic similarities.
Identify and compare the major domains and kingdoms of life, including their characteristics and examples.
Explain the structural and functional characteristics of unicellular organisms in different environments.
Interpret phylogenetic trees to understand evolutionary relationships among living organisms.

pathways.title: Exploring Physics Concepts with a Gravity Well
pathways.description: This pathway explores various concepts related to gravity using gravity well. It covers topics such as Newton's first law of motion, the direction of planetary orbits, orbital speeds of planets, the Moon's orbits around planets, the pull of gravity on objects, gravity assistance for spacecraft, and the formation of planets. It offers a hands-on learning experience to understand these fundamental principles of physics.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand Newton's first law of motion and how gravity affects the trajectory of objects.
You will explain the direction of planetary orbits and the factors that contribute to their alignment.
You will explore the concept of gravity assist and its application in space missions.
You will be able to describe the formation of planets and stars from the accumulation of matter.

pathways.title: How Electricity Flows in Circuits
pathways.description: Learn how electricity flows through simple circuits using accessible tools. Explore electric current, voltage, and resistance through interactive simulations and videos. Apply concepts from physics to construct, measure, and analyze basic electrical circuits while understanding Ohm’s law and safe electrical practices.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the basic components of an electric circuit, including cells, wires, switches, and resistors.
Explain the relationship between electric current, voltage, and resistance using Ohm’s Law.
Construct and test simple series and parallel circuits to observe and measure current and voltage.
Apply principles of current electricity to real-life situations and demonstrate safe handling of electrical components.

pathways.title: Bringing Revolutionary Gene Editing Techniques to Undergraduates
pathways.description: In recent years, the emergence of a new genetic modification technique called CRISPR-Cas9 has made gene editing a dramatically faster and easier process for scientists. As part of the CRISPR in the Classroom Network, Dr Anil Challa from the University of Alabama at Birmingham and Associate Professor Dr Lynn Kee from Stetson University, both in the US, are bringing CRISPR-Cas9 to the university classroom, teaching undergraduates how to use this important tool.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will be able to define CRISPR-Cas9 and explain its components.
Explain the concept of gene editing and its significance in genetic modification.
Summarize the process of CRISPR-Cas9 technology and its application in genetic modification.
Compare and contrast the advantages and disadvantages of CRISPR-Cas9 technology with previous genetic modification methods.
Critique the potential risks and benefits of implementing CRISPR technology in society.

pathways.title: Global Health Ethics: Rights and Equity
pathways.description: This learning pathway explores the critical role of ethical decision-making in healthcare, emphasizing the human right to health and covers key principles of healthcare ethics, including informed consent and justice, including their application in real-world scenarios. We examine how ethical considerations influence patient care, policy development, and resource allocation.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will be familiar with the principles of human rights.
You will analyze ethical dilemmas and challenges in global health practice, such as cultural relativism, informed consent, and resource allocation.
You will evaluate the role of governmental policy in protecting health-related human rights and outcomes.
You will discuss the concept of health equity and the ethical imperative to address social determinants of health.
You will apply ethical frameworks to analyze real-world case studies and propose solutions to health-related ethical dilemmas.

pathways.title: The Beautiful Brain
pathways.description: The brain is a fascinating mystery. How does such a complex thing develop? The Beautiful Brain is a video series that uncovers the key steps of brain development using masterful art as the analogy.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the main steps involved in brain development, including the roles of stem cells, neuron differentiation, and the formation and migration of brain cells.

Compare and contrast the process of brain development with artistic concepts, such as the construction of the Sagrada Familia and the skill of grapevine pruning, illustrating the parallels between natural and human-made masterpieces.
Identify the structure and function of neurons, including the formation of axons, dendrites, and synapses, and the importance of these connections in the brain's neural network.
Understand the process of synaptic pruning and the role of microglia in maintaining and refining neural connections, especially during periods of brain development.

pathways.title: Energy Transfer in Motion
pathways.description:
Join Astronaut Alex as a junior space engineer to solve energy puzzles! Over five days, experiment and discover why a rover's energy changes, using fun activities and simulations in this hands-on science journey, with the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (230 minutes) - experiment, brainstorm, and solve real-world challenges while exploring energy transformation.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 60 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 40 minutes, items 3-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3-4, 80 minutes, items 5-6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 50 minutes, item 7 of the pathway and the Student Guide.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will be able to identify factors influencing kinetic energy such as mass, height, friction, and gravity.
Understand and describe energy conservation, giving examples of how energy changes from one form to another.
Solve practical problems using knowledge of energy concepts, focusing on situations involving kinetic energy.
Analyze how various factors influence kinetic energy in an energy system and recognize patterns in these relationships.
Use your understanding of kinetic energy to examine and explain why the rover's energy levels change as it traverses different areas of the lunar surface.

pathways.title: Dental Discoveries: How Is Dental Research Improving Oral Health?
pathways.description: Many people do not enjoy going to the dentist, but regular dental check-ups are a key aspect of healthcare. Maintaining good oral hygiene is important not only for your teeth, but also for your body and mind. Dental scientists at the Iowa Institute of Oral Health Research at the University of Iowa, USA, are studying various aspects of oral health and healthcare, to improve dental practices and keep our teeth and mouths healthy.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will understand the significance of oral health and its connection to overall well-being.
You will comprehend the link between oral health and conditions such as periodontitis, heart disease, and diabetes.
You will apply knowledge of genetic mutations to understand conditions like cleft lip, cleft palate, and dental anomalies.
You will reflect on personal oral health practices and identify areas for improvement.

pathways.title: How Ice Sheets in the Geological Past Can Inform Us of Sea Level Rise in the Future
pathways.description: The future of the earth’s sea levels due to climate change is incredibly difficult to predict. Dr Ed Gasson, at the University of Exeter, and Professor Carrie Lear, at Cardiff University, in the UK, are Earth and environmental scientists researching how the Antarctic ice sheet changed during the Earth’s past, in order to predict how it will influence future changes to sea levels.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: After completing the pathway, you will understand the potential impacts of the melting Antarctic Ice Sheet on global communities.
Examine the methods used by scientists to reconstruct past climates and understand the changes in ice sheets over geological time.

pathways.title: Artemis I: Mission Design
pathways.description: The Artemis I mission will enable NASA’s next era of space exploration, and therefore requires a lot of careful thought and planning. This pathway takes you through the meticulous design process of the Artemis I mission.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will have a better understanding of the mission design choices involved in the Artemis I mission.
You will examine the route that the Artemis I rocket will take to the Moon.
You will discover additional projects taking place alongside the Artemis I mission.
You will learn about the design and recovery of the Orion spacecraft that will land astronauts on the lunar surface.

pathways.title: Seedless and Seed Plants
pathways.description: This pathway explores seedless plants (bryophytes and pteridophytes) as well as seed plants (gymnosperms and angiosperms). Core concepts covered include plant anatomy, reproduction, phylogeny, and seed dispersal.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Identify and describe the key anatomical features and vascular structures that distinguish seedless vascular plants from non-seedless plants.
Compare the reproductive life cycles of ferns, gymnosperms, and angiosperms with attention to gametophyte/sporophyte generations, fertilization methods, and dispersal strategies.
Compare angiosperms, gymnosperms, and ferns based on their structural complexity, reproductive mechanisms, and evolutionary placement on the plant phylogenetic tree.
Evaluate the evolutionary advantages of seeds, flowers, and fruits in gymnosperms and angiosperms compared to the spore-based reproduction of ferns.

pathways.title: Using SDS in Laboratory Settings
pathways.description: People working in laboratories rely on Safety Data Sheets (SDS) to understand chemical hazards and follow safety documentation requirements. This pathway introduces the structure, purpose, and effective use of Safety Data Sheets. Explore how to identify chemical hazards, locate critical safety information, and meet documentation requirements to ensure proper handling and emergency response in laboratory environments.
learning_objectives: Identify, describe, and demonstrate the effective use of Safety Data Sheets (SDS) to meet documentation requirements.

pathways.title: Plant-Like Organisms and Photosynthesis: Understanding Algae, Mosses, Ferns and Fungi
pathways.description: Plant-like organisms such as algae, mosses, and ferns are non-flowering organisms that have evolved unique adaptations for survival. This pathway explores the diversity of these organisms, their reproduction, their ecological significance, and their relationship to photosynthesis. Additionally, it covers fungi, their classification, nutrition, and their impact on human life and agriculture. By the end of this pathway, learners will understand how plants produce energy through photosynthesis and the vital roles of algae, bryophytes, and fungi in ecosystems.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the characteristics, adaptations, and ecological importance of algae, mosses, and ferns.
Explain the different types of fungal nutrition and their roles in ecosystems.
Understand the process of photosynthesis and its role in sustaining plant life and ecosystems.
State and analyze the balanced chemical equation for photosynthesis.
Examine how environmental factors impact biological activity, such as phytoplankton populations and fungicide use in agriculture.

pathways.title: Project-Based Learning
pathways.description:
To develop a deep conceptual understanding of abstract concepts, particularly in the sciences, students need to do more than just read textbooks or listen in lecture halls. They need to find ways to actively develop their understanding, observe, and reflect on how these concepts introduced in class actually operate in the real world. In classes that utilize a Project-Based Learning approach, students try to solve challenging everyday problems by learning about and applying key class concepts. In a well-designed problem, students are meaningfully engaged in an authentic social problem that can only be solved if they deeply understand what is to be learned in the class. While projects can range in scope and complexity - with some lasting only a few days and others taking a full semester -- all project-based learning challenges students to be self-directed and take ownership of their learning.
How do we design projects that can engage students in authentic and meaningful applications of class concepts? How do we scaffold students’ design processes so that they are intrinsically motivated to learn more about fundamental class concepts? How do we enable students to track and assess their own learning throughout project development? How do we ensure that students value the process of applying class concepts to the real world over how effectively their project works? In this pathway, featured instructor Eric Mazur discusses his approach and strategies for using project-based learning with undergraduates at Harvard’s School of Engineering and Applied Sciences.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will analyze how collaborative assessments and fairs boost motivation and learning.
Master organizing and running a public project fair.
Use design notebooks to improve problem-solving in project-based learning.
Document and reflect on learning to foster iterative thinking.
Assess problem-based learning's focus on critical thinking.
Apply strategies to strengthen student resilience in problem-solving.
Design projects with real-world challenges for student engagement.
Connect academic concepts to hands-on experiences for societal impact.

pathways.title: Reading the Rainbow
pathways.description: How do astronomers learn about something in space that is very far away? In addition to images, they study the light from cosmic objects like planets, stars, and galaxies. Astronomers spread light out to look at the detailed rainbow spectrum (plural: spectra). Light carries a lot of information, if you learn to “read” its rainbow!
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will identify how astronomers use the light spectrum to determine star temperatures.
Explain how the red-shifted spectrum helps measure galaxy distances.
Describe how spectrum shifts toward blue or red indicate the speed of gas and dust orbiting black holes.
Interpret light spectra to understand the atmospheric conditions of distant planets.

pathways.title: Climate Change and Ecological Impacts
pathways.description: Dive into the Anthropocene era, explore the importance of energy efficiency, witness France's ongoing energy transition, and rethink traditional energy transition theories. Uncover the profound impact of climate change, learn about sustainable energy solutions, and discover the complexities of historical energy dynamics. Join us in shaping a brighter future for our planet.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the profound impact of human-induced climate change during the Anthropocene era.
Have acquired the knowledge needed to make informed choices for sustainable energy consumption, aligning with global efforts to reduce environmental impact.
Learn about the application of heterodox economic principles to climate economy modeling and the role of these models in shaping climate policy.

Be able to identify key indicators such as shifting bird migrations, vegetation changes, and rising greenhouse gases, and recognize the urgency for action to safeguard our planet's ecological balance.
Gain insight into the significance of the critical zone in the context of environmental understanding and policy-making in the Anthropocene era.
Gain insights into the consequences of environmental degradation, climate change, and resource depletion on public health.

pathways.title: What if Microbes Could Live In Extreme Environments Beyond Earth?
pathways.description: Dr Jay Nadeau, based at Portland State University, USA, is on a quest to find life in extreme environments. Her findings could help inform the search for bugs in space.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, students should learn the definition of microbiology and the characteristics of microbes, including their small size and their presence in various environments.
Learn about the investigation of extreme environments and "extraterrestrial analogue" sites.
Gain an understanding of the incredible diversity of microorganisms found in extreme environments on Earth

pathways.title: The Need for Antimicrobial Peptides in a World of Antibiotic Resistance
pathways.description: Terri Camesano is a professor of chemical engineering at Worcester Polytechnic Institute in the US. Much of her research is concerned with peptides, specifically their antimicrobial and wound healing properties. Her findings could lead to the development of strategies to treat bacterial infections.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you should be able to identify the role of antibiotics in fighting bacterial infections and understand their significance in revolutionizing medicine.
Describe the mechanism of action of antimicrobial peptides on bacterial cells and their ability to kill various types of microorganisms.
Learn about the general field of chemical engineering and the pathways to the career.

pathways.title: How Mice Can Reveal the Secrets of Rare Diseases
pathways.description: Even though medicine has advanced a lot over the last century, there are still many unanswered questions. This is especially true for rare diseases. Dr Kevin Marchbank of Newcastle University is using mouse models to investigate the genetics behind two rare diseases that affect the kidneys – and his findings could be key to developing treatments.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how the similarities in biology allow mice to be used as replacements for humans in the laboratory
Understand the key terms and phrases associated with complement biology
Discover how investigating rare diseases give us insight into how the human body functions.

pathways.title: Communicating Responsibly: Lab Safety Regulations
pathways.description: Lab safety training gone wrong can cause real harm—even during demonstrations, as a Massachusetts explosion showed. This pathway equips aspiring science communicators with essential lab safety knowledge, covering PPE, chemical waste disposal, eyewash station protocols, and risk assessment. Through videos, infographics, articles, and posters, learners explore how to communicate and model safe lab practices across media—an essential part of effective science outreach.
learning_objectives: Understand key laboratory safety principles to support accurate and responsible science communication in professional and educational settings.
Recognize the importance of regulatory compliance, including OSHA and ANSI standards, when discussing laboratory safety practices with diverse audiences.
Identify appropriate strategies for communicating lab safety regulations through multimedia formats such as videos, posters, and infographics.
Explore methods for clearly conveying risk assessment, hazardous material handling, and PPE protocols in science outreach and stakeholder communication.

pathways.title: Predicting the Speed of a Roller Coaster
pathways.description:
Over 8 days (345–450 minutes total), you will explore how energy transforms rather than being created or destroyed. Through videos, simulations, interactive activities, readings, and the accompanying student guide, you’ll investigate potential and kinetic energy while applying the Law of Conservation of Energy to roller coasters.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 60 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 60-90 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4-5, 90-120 minutes, item 8 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 6-7, 90-120 minutes, item 9-10 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 8, 45-60 minutes, item 11 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will explore potential and kinetic energy and the Law of Conservation of Energy by experimenting with a roller coaster simulation.
Create a computational spreadsheet to calculate the speed of an object at the bottom of a roller coaster using the Law of Conservation of Energy.
Design and build a roller coaster, predict the speed at the bottom using your spreadsheet, and test your prediction by measuring the actual speed with photogates.

pathways.title: Unveiling the Atom: The Building Blocks of Everything!
pathways.description:
Have you ever wondered what makes up everything around you—from the air you breathe to the screen you’re reading this on? Welcome to The Atom: Nature’s LEGO Bricks, where we dive deep into the unseen world of atoms!
In this pathway, you will unlock the secrets of atomic structure, explore how protons, neutrons, and electrons shape matter, and discover why atoms behave the way they do. Through interactive simulations, hands-on experiments, and real-world connections, you’ll see how atoms are the foundation of chemistry, physics, and even life itself!
By the end of this journey, you'll not only understand atoms—you’ll think like a scientist, ready to explore the universe at its most fundamental level!
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure of an atom by identifying protons, neutrons, and electrons and explaining their roles.
Differentiate between atomic number, mass number, and isotopes, and explain their significance in identifying elements.
Model the arrangement of electrons in energy levels (shells) and predict how electron configurations influence chemical behavior.
Explain the forces that hold an atom together, including the role of the nucleus and electrostatic interactions.
Analyze the role of atoms in chemical bonding, distinguishing between ionic and covalent bonds.

pathways.title: Saving Plants From Disease
pathways.description: To prevent outbreaks of disease destroying wheat and other vital food crops, scientists from the Rothamsted Research and European Bioinformatics Institutes are sharing genetic and genomic information on disease-causing microorganisms.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you should understand the importance of genetic and genomic information in devising ways to prevent diseases
Be able to identify the problems caused by plant diseases
Learn about the potential impact of PHI-base and PhytoPath in preventing plant diseases
Explain how PHI-base and PhytoPath databases help researchers discover more about organisms that cause disease (plant pathogens)

pathways.title: How Can Scientists Clean Up Hydrogen Production to Help Tackle Climate Change?
pathways.description: Hydrogen could be an important clean energy source in future, reducing greenhouse gas emissions and helping to combat climate change. However, currently, producing hydrogen fuel is not a clean process – most of the time, it generates greenhouse gases. Dr Nicolas Boscher and his team at the Luxembourg Institute of Science and Technology are using chemical engineering techniques – and taking inspiration from photosynthesis occurring in plants – to develop new polymers with the ability to produce hydrogen in a clean way
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the key terms used when discussing material chemistry
Learn about the applications of material chemistry to produce clean hydrogen using processes similar to photosynthesis
Apply this knowledge in a simulated research environment

pathways.title: From Simple to Complex: Harnessing the Laws of Nature
pathways.description: Though life and the universe are pretty complex, there are underlying rules that govern how they work. Uncovering them is no easy task, but Dr Serim Ilday and her team at Bilkent University in Turkey believe they have cracked an important one. Using simple ingredients – water, polystyrene balls and an ultra-fast laser – they have uncovered some fascinating universal truths, which have very promising real-world applications.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you will better understand the physical rule that will determine how particles will aggregate.
Understand how Colloids are mixtures, where one substance is microscopically dispersed through another.
Learn more about what a career in physics entails, and the opportunities of pursuing a career in physics.

pathways.title: Surface Water Events and Hazards
pathways.description: Flooding occurs in known floodplains when prolonged rainfall over several days, intense rainfall over a short period of time, or a debris jam causes a river or stream to overflow and flood the surrounding area. Read on to learn about flood intervals and monitoring.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Define terms related to floods
Identify potential causes and contributors to floods
Explore how floods are documented and studied

pathways.title: NASA | Bioenergetics
pathways.description: Discover how cells generate and manage energy to power life, from the role of ATP as the cell’s fuel source to the systems that regulate energy flow and transformation. Understand how this process can break down in extreme environments, such as space, and why this matters for health and survival on Earth and beyond.
learning_objectives: Strengthen understanding of work, energy, and power and their interconnections.
Explore how plants and animals generate and utilize energy.
Examine how heat and energy behave in the unique conditions of space.

pathways.title: Be Prepared: Predicting Catastrophic Events and Natural Hazards
pathways.description:
Investigate how different catastrophic events could affect the community in this learning pathway, spanning 5 days (210 minutes). Students will use texts, videos, simulations, and the accompanying student guide, to enhance understanding of natural disasters and foster disaster resilience.





Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 60 minutes in class, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes in class, items 9-11 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 40 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 10 minutes, items 12-13 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway students will be able to collect and analyze data about past catastrophic events.
Students will be able to analyze the importance of data collection, community engagement, and predictive modeling in mitigating the impacts of disasters and enhancing resilience.
Students will be able to apply the knowledge gained from readings, videos, and interactive simulations to real-world scenarios, such as preparing for and responding to natural disasters.

pathways.title: On the Frontline of the Biomedical Revolution
pathways.description: The 21st century has seen a boom in biomedical advances, aided by increased recognition of the importance of working across different scientific disciplines. Materials and methods for the detection and treatment of diseases are better than ever, and a diverse array of scientists at the University of Technology Sydney (UTS), Australia, led by Professor Dayong Jin, are at the forefront of this continuous development.
learning_objectives: Learn the importance of working across different disciplines in the scientific field.
Learn more about the boom in biomedical advances in the 21st century.

pathways.title: What Can You Do With a Science Degree?
pathways.description: Dr. Boothman has used her scientific training in a range of roles from medicine regulation to health policy. Watch the video to hear how she managed her career changes.

pathways.title: Allyship and Advocacy in STEM Education
pathways.description:
Like their students, educators from racially diverse backgrounds face systemic challenges in STEM education environments, which can make it more difficult for them to thrive. How can STEM educators support their colleagues from underrepresented groups to ensure that they all have the resources and opportunities to succeed?
This pathway introduces allyship—a powerful practice where those from privileged groups advocate for underrepresented or marginalized communities. Educators will learn the key characteristics of allyship and explore how these can be applied in STEM education contexts to create more equitable opportunities for students and colleagues.
This pathway forms part of the Advancing Equity in STEM through Inclusive Teaching cluster. See our equity pledge as part of this initiative.
learning_objectives: Recognize the challenges educators from racially diverse groups face in STEM education.
Describe allyship and its associated characteristics and strategies.
Explore how these strategies can be applied in STEM education contexts to better support educators from racially diverse groups.

pathways.title: Collecting and Preparing Data
pathways.description: Large datasets, known as big data, often require specialized techniques like data cleaning, preprocessing, normalization, web scraping, and cloud computing for accurate analysis. This pathway introduces core data science skills, including survey design, sampling methods, data compression, and database management. This pathway is Chapter 2 of the Principles of Data Science textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will describe how data collection methods, such as surveys, experiments, observation, and transactional data recording, are used to gather evidence for answering scientific questions and solving real-world problems.

Design surveys that minimize bias, select appropriate sampling methods, and apply strategies to ensure reliable, valid data collection for addressing scientific questions and solving real-world problems.
Collect, parse, and process web data using Python, regular expressions, and web scraping techniques to support scientific inquiry, data analysis, and evidence-based conclusions.
Clean, preprocess, and standardize data by handling missing values, outliers, and noise to improve the quality, accuracy, and reliability of datasets for scientific analysis.
Manage large datasets by applying storage, compression, indexing, and chunking techniques, and evaluate database systems and cloud computing for efficient data organization, retrieval, and analysis.

pathways.title: Editing Genes to Benefit Sickle Cell Disease Patients
pathways.description: Dr Natalia Rivera-Torres is conducting sickle cell research at ChristianaCare’s Gene Editing Institute in Delaware in the US. The institute is an aspirational and supportive community composed of people at different stages of their career – all of whom do ground-breaking work
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you should understand the basic concept of genes and their role in determining inherited features and characteristics.
Learn about the principles and potential applications of gene editing, specifically focusing on the CRISPR technology.
Learn about the concept of genetic mutations and their impact on health conditions and diseases, using sickle cell anemia as an example.

pathways.title: Creating Software That Works for Everyone
pathways.description: From monitoring our health to finding a parking spot, there are now apps to help with every task in life. However, as we become ever-more reliant on software, ensuring it is accessible for all of society is a growing necessity. At Monash University in Australia, the Human-Centric Software Engineering (HumaniSE) Lab is developing software that can be used by everyone.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway students will be able to explain the importance of human-centric software engineering and how it can improve the accessibility and inclusivity of software.
Students will be able to describe the use of personas and user stories in software development and how they can be used to identify the different requirements of end users.
Create personas and accessibility features for a software application that caters to the needs of a diverse range of end users.

pathways.title: How Do I Prepare for University Applications?
pathways.description: Professor Viding shares insider advice on what to think about before you start your A-levels and how to approach your UCAS application.

pathways.title: Understanding Haemophilia, One Amino Acid at a Time
pathways.description: The hereditary blood disorder haemophilia B can have huge health implications, but it is still far from fully understood. At the University of Washington in the US, Dr Jill M. Johnsen is using the latest DNA sequencing techniques to dig down into the changes to one gene that lead to haemophilia B, examining the role of every amino acid within the protein it encodes. This will create a comprehensive ‘map’ of the gene, leading to an in-depth genetic understanding of the disease.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms relevant to haematology, including coagulation factors, haemophilia, DNA sequencing, and deep mutational scanning.
Recall how multiplex assay of variant effect (MAVE) is used in genetic research.
Explain how genetic mutations in the F9 gene lead to haemophilia B.
Summarize the differences between haemophilia A and haemophilia B in terms of affected coagulation factors.

pathways.title: Adaptation: How Does Our Environment (Biomes) Impact Our Traits?
pathways.description:
Discover how environmental changes shape trait diversity with this 5-day (180–200 minute) pathway using, images, videos, simulations, and a student guide. Model trait adaptation, analyze habitat impacts, and design habitat-specific birds.




Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40-45 minutes, item 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 25-30 minutes, item 3 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 40 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 30 minutes, items 4-7 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 45 minutes, item 8 of the pathway.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to  identify cause-and-effect relationships of trait diversity and habitat/environmental disruption.
Model and explain how habitat and predation impact the presence of traits.
Design birds with traits that adapt to the habitat.

pathways.title: Polar Star: Teaching Science as a Whole
pathways.description: The POLAR STAR project is designed to help teachers successfully introduce steam in their classes. It combines state-of-the-art pedagogies and exciting activities to teach ‘science as a whole’ and help teachers answer the “why do we have to learn this?” question.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you should understand the POLAR STAR project's methodology and tools
Learn about how  this methodology can increase students' understanding of science concepts and facilitate knowledge retention

pathways.title: Short and Bushy, Long and Spindly: How Plants Make Their Shapes
pathways.description: Dr Charlotte Kirchhelle, based at the University of Oxford, is investigating the role of cell geometry in shaping plants’ organs – namely, the roots, leaves, stem and reproductive organs. Understanding the fundamental cellular mechanisms driving organ shape could lead to new ways of manipulating and improving crops around the world
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you should understand the concept of molecular plant biology and morphogenesis
Learn key terms associated with plant sciences
Learn about how the study of morphogenesis can be applied in the real world

pathways.title: Wetlands
pathways.description: Wetlands provide an interesting ecological environment. Learn more about how a wetland is defined, the unique flora and fauna which inhabit wetlands, and why wetlands are so important.
learning_objectives: Learn and understand what makes a landscape a wetland.
Explore the various ways in which scientists study the waters found in wetlands.
Learn and understand the important role soil plays in wetlands.
Learn more about the hydrophytic vegetation found in wetlands.

pathways.title: Write Like a Lab Professional
pathways.description: Laboratory professionals must tailor their writing to communicate scientific ideas clearly to peers, researchers, and the public. This pathway introduces the foundations of audience-focused writing, a key communication skill in scientific and laboratory settings. Explore how to adjust tone, style, and vocabulary to communicate scientific ideas clearly and effectively to different audiences, from technical peers to the general public.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate communicating effectively in writing as appropriate for the needs of the audience.

pathways.title: Evaluating Evidence: Cell Phone Radiation
pathways.description:
Evaluate how different frequencies of electromagnetic radiation impact matter with this pathway and accompanying student guide. The recommended learning time spans 6 days (240 minutes). Discover photon energy across wavelengths, emphasizing the critical analysis of scientific claims and data reliability.




Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 20 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 1-2, 60 minutes, items 2-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3-4, 100 minutes, item 5 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 30 minutes, item 6 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 30 minutes, found in the Student Guide.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will evaluate the validity and reliability of claims in published materials about the effects that different frequencies of electromagnetic radiation have when absorbed by matter.
Learn about the relative energy of photons of different wavelengths.

pathways.title: The Legacy and Science of the Rubin Observatory
pathways.description: This pathway explores the Rubin Observatory and its impact on astronomy. From the observatory’s role in studying cosmic phenomena to its namesake Vera Rubin’s legacy, learners will gain insight into the Solar System’s mysteries, the significance of supernovae, and how data collected from space enhances our understanding of the Universe.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the purpose and significance of the Rubin Observatory in modern astronomy.
Learn how light from space is converted into valuable scientific data and how this data contributes to space research.
Explore the history of the Solar System, the discovery of exoplanets, and the role of supernovae in mapping the Universe.

pathways.title: Using Adsorbents to Help Society
pathways.description: You will likely have heard of absorption before, but have you heard of adsorption? At the University of Notre Dame in Indiana, USA, Dr Yamil Colón is a chemical and biomolecular engineer studying this important chemical process. His work could help make huge breakthroughs in healthcare, climate change, environment and water scarcity research.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will be able to differentiate between adsorption and absorption, understanding their unique roles in various scientific and industrial processes.
Be able to articulate the key concepts and applications of chemical and biomolecular engineering, emphasizing its role in solving global issues.
Bbe able to identify the influences and experiences that can shape a career in science, using Dr. Yamil Colón's journey as an example.

pathways.title: What Can We Learn From Plant Proteins?
pathways.description: Metabolism is an essential feature of life. All biological processes, such as photosynthesis, respiration and digestion, depend on various metabolic pathways, which in turn rely upon enzymes to carry out their work. Dr Charles Stewart Jr., of the Macromolecular X-ray Crystallography Facility at Iowa State University in the US, is seeking to improve our understanding of the function of these enzymes by examining their 3D molecular structure.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway learners will be able to understand the principles and applications of X-ray crystallography in structural biology.

Understand the importance of structural biology in studying the 3D structure of biological molecules, particularly proteins, and its role in understanding their functions and implications in diseases.

Students will gain an understanding of the importance of protein structure, the techniques used to determine protein structure, and the broad applications of structural biology in various scientific fields.

pathways.title: NASA STEM Forward to the Moon Activity Guide
pathways.description: This series of classroom activities and guided demonstrations allows students to learn about foundational ideas in physics and chemistry while also understanding some of the challenges that astronauts and scientists face when planning the future of lunar exploration.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how distant selestial bodies are from each other.
You will understand how invisible forces like gravity curve the trajectory of moving objects.
You will be able to build a simple water filtration device.
You will understand the difficulties associated with living and innovating in the lunar environment.

pathways.title: Effects of Urban Development on Streams and Aquatic Life
pathways.description: This collection of videos provides a comprehensive look at the impact of urban development on natural streams and the biological communities that depend on them. From deforestation due to agricultural and urban land use, to the dangers of urban runoff and the importance of stormwater management.
learning_objectives: Learn and understand the effects that urbanization has on streams and aquatic ecosystems.
Learn about the Nationwide Urban Runoff Project, the Biological Conditions Gradient, and success stories in urban restoration.

pathways.title: Optimization
pathways.description: Focus on the essential aspect of optimization in creating Deep Learning models that are guaranteed to converge. Students will learn about commonly used optimization techniques such as Stochastic Gradient Descent and Momentum. Additionally, students will delve into adaptive hyperparameter tuning through RMSProp, which utilizes a per-dimension learning rate. This pathway also highlights the significance of choosing appropriate optimization objectives to prevent unforeseen, undesirable consequences. Check out Tutorial 1 on the Neuromatch website.
learning_objectives: By the end of this path, you will understand why optimization is necessary when creating deep learning models.
You will list commonly used optimization techniques.
You will explore optimization in non-convex loss landscapes.
You will be able to use PyTorch to compute the gradient of the loss function of a model.
You will cover ethical concerns in machine learning systems.

pathways.title: Programming Logic and Systems Thinking
pathways.description: Programming logic and systems thinking are foundational skills across science, engineering, and technology, enabling professionals to design decision-making workflows, solve problems efficiently, and communicate clearly with technical systems. This Python-based pathway introduces core concepts in computer science, Boolean logic, and systems design. Learners explore variables, data types, conditional structures, logic gates, flowcharting, pseudocode, and development methodologies such as the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) and Agile—skills that support problem solving in many STEM careers.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of logic in programming and how it supports problem-solving in data analysis.
Identify and use core logic components such as variables, data types, branching, and looping.
Grapple with flowcharting and pseudocode to plan, visualize, and structure logical workflows.
Describe and compare systems development methodologies including SDLC and Agile.
Design and implement logical workflows using Python to solve defined problems and model decision-making.

pathways.title: What Kind of Rocks are On Mars?
pathways.description:
Embark on a NASA-inspired quest to discover Mars' geology and ancient life clues through engaging videos and hands-on experiments. Determine the best rock for a Martian habitat, explore space geology careers, and protect future astronauts from radiation—all while comparing Earth's rocks to those on the Red Planet. This learning pathway spans 9 days (400 minutes) and is accompanied by a student guide.








Pathway breakdown:








• ENGAGE: Day 1; 50 minutes, items 1-3 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3; 100 minutes, items 4-7 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4-5; 70 minutes, items 8-9 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 6-8; 150 minutes, items 10-11 the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 9; 30 minutes, item 12 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the three main categories of rocks and their origin.
Compare and contrast the geological surface of Mars with that of Earth.
Analyze rock samples from Mars and look for clues of their origin.

pathways.title: The Links Between Sleep and Alzheimer’s Disease
pathways.description: The causes of Alzheimer’s disease, a brain disorder, are still far from fully understood. At the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill in the US, Dr Graham Diering and Dr Shenée Martin believe that the quality of our sleep could be a big contributor. Their experiments are taking a close look at the biomolecular and neurological changes that happen when our sleep is disrupted, and whether this could be a trigger for the development of Alzheimer’s.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define the concept of synaptic connections and their role in learning and memory consolidation.
Identify key factors contributing to sleep disruption and its impact on brain function.
Summarize the theories regarding the benefits of sleep on memory consolidation and brain health.
Evaluate the evidence supporting the hypothesis that poor sleep quality accelerates the progression of Alzheimer's disease.

pathways.title: Prostheses for People: Matching the Person and Their New Limb
pathways.description: Worldwide, the majority of amputees do not have access to a useful prosthesis. This is especially true in lower and middle income countries (LMICs) where limb loss may be more common and services less accessible. Mechanical engineers Professor Laurence Kenney and Dr Alex Dickinson are working with people in different parts of the world to make prostheses as accessible and useful as possible
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to recognize the challenges involved in developing prosthetic devices that are accessible, useful, and tailored to individual users.
Students will be able to analyze and evaluate the impact of incorporating user experiences and interdisciplinary collaboration in designing and developing prosthetic devices.
Students will be able assess the benefits of incorporating user feedback and interdisciplinary collaboration in improving the functionality, comfort, and overall design of prosthetic devices.

pathways.title: What Can Worms Tell Us About Ageing in Humans?
pathways.description: A nematode worm called C. Elegans has a lifespan of about three weeks, so what can it tell us about how and why we age? Biogerontologist Dr Jennifer Tullet, based at the University of Kent, is investigating lifespan and the ageing process using these worms. Their similarity to mammals means that these studies can also be applied to humans
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you should learn about the process of aging in different organisms.
Learn about the field of biogerontology and its focus on studying the biology of ageing.
Learn about the proteins RNA polymerase III and SKN-1 and their roles in controlling lifespan and food-related behavior.

pathways.title: Does Asking Questions Make You Feel Nervous?
pathways.description: To get the information you need as a scientist or a student you will need to ask questions.

pathways.title: Short term macroeconomic responses to Coronavirus in Italy and the Eurozone (4 parts)
pathways.description: The different macroeconomic measures that can be adopted in the emergency and their funding (loans and ‘monetization’). Policies in the Eurozone: EU institutions and national governments initiatives. Increases in the debt to GDP ratio and economic policy after the pandemic: a return to austerity? The case of Italy

pathways.title: Learning to Learn Strategies
pathways.description: Learning to learn strategies teach students to be aware of how they learn and how to gain control over their learning, helping them move from an attitude that leaves little room for change to an attitude that promotes self-awareness and resilience. This pathway will focus on the creation of a positive emotional environment for learning, promoting both individual and collaborative learning experiences, and establishing effective feedback mechanisms to support student growth.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the connection between self-management and successful learning outcomes.
You will be able to explain how fostering self-awareness, resilience, and a growth mindset empowers students to take control of their learning journey.
You will know how to model effective learning habits, foster student engagement, and encourage self-reflection in the classroom.

pathways.title: Basics of Insulation and Energy Transfer
pathways.description:
Explore the concept of insulation and energy transfer with our learning pathway.  Learn how energy moves and keeps things warm or cold!
After mastering these concepts, you'll be ready to tackle the Thermal Insulation Trouble pathway, where we'll apply what you've learned to real-world scenarios on the Moon!
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you should be able to explain the concept of energy transfer.
Understand the role of insulation in energy conservation.
Apply this knowledge to create practical solutions for thermal management challenges.

pathways.title: Be a Rockstar! What Can Moon Rocks Teach Us About the Universe?
pathways.description:
Embark on a lunar quest to analyze Moon rocks, understand the Moon's terrain, and create a rock collection device, while engaging in critical thinking and exploring careers in space science through interactive experiments. Discover the importance of the lunar surface with this learning pathway and the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (220 minutes) and focuses on astronaut training, the lunar surface, Moon rocks, and crater formation.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2 & 3, 60 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
