• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 9-19 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 45 minutes, found in the Student Guide.
• EXTEND: 10 minutes, item 11 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will collect data like real scientists to understand how Moon rocks are gathered and sorted.
Explore and describe the Moon's surface, just like lunar explorers do.
Perform an experiment to understand how lunar craters are formed.
Discover stories that Moon rocks tell about what happened on the Moon long ago.
Learn about cool jobs in space science and think up your own big questions about the Moon.

pathways.title: How Can We Unravel the Complex History of Networks?
pathways.description: Infectious diseases, such as COVID-19, can spread rapidly from person to person, resulting in a vast and complex network of infected individuals. Throughout your life, you will meet new people, make new friends and form new relationships, building an ever-evolving social network. At Rutgers University in the US, Dr Min Xu has developed a model that can unpick the history of networks such as these, helping everyone from epidemiologists to counter-terrorism investigators.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will be able to identify and describe key elements of a network, such as nodes, edges, weight, and root node, enhancing their understanding of network structures.
Learners will recognize the potential biases in data interpretation, enhancing their critical thinking skills and promoting more informed decision-making.
Learners will be able to appreciate the creative potential of mathematics, understanding its role beyond traditional rules and formulas.
Learners will have the knowledge to construct and visualize their own social network, reinforcing their understanding of network theory and its practical applications.

pathways.title: Managing Data Lifecycle and Provenance
pathways.description: Managing data from collection to use—and understanding where that data comes from—is essential across science, technology, and data-driven fields. This pathway introduces the full data lifecycle, data provenance, and ethical data management practices that support trustworthy analysis and decision making.
learning_objectives: Define data provenance and explain its importance in ensuring data traceability, integrity, and accountability.
Explain the stages of the data lifecycle and their role in supporting organizational workflows.
Describe how system design principles and enterprise network architectures support data lifecycle policies.
Apply traceability practices and tools to manage data securely and ethically across the data lifecycle.

pathways.title: Data Analytics and Modeling
pathways.description: From clicks to conversions, discover how businesses use data to make smart decisions and predict what’s next. Learn how web analytics, big data, and AI tools uncover trends, target customers, and boost success. Explore how you can turn data into insights—and power every decision with evidence. This pathway is Chapter 8 of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to outline the steps in the business analytics process and how data supports decisions.
Compare types of analytics and use models like regression, clustering, and decision trees.
Describe key business intelligence (BI) tools and ethical issues in handling big data.
Explain how web analytics tools collect and measure online behavior.
Identify ways businesses use analytics to improve marketing, performance, and customer engagement.

pathways.title: Global Health Initiatives: Partnerships and Collaboration
pathways.description: Discover how cancer health disparities impact diverse populations globally. From access to screening in Sub-Saharan Africa to collaborative health interventions in East and Southern Africa, learn about advancements in cancer research, early detection, and the role of regional hubs. Explore how multi-stakeholder partnerships enhance global health equity and drive innovative solutions.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify key stakeholders and partners involved in global health initiatives, including governments, NGOs, academia, and the private sector.
Analyze successful examples of international cooperation and collaboration in addressing global health challenges.
Evaluate the role of diplomacy, advocacy, and funding mechanisms in supporting global health efforts.
Discuss the importance of cultural competency and sensitivity in global health partnerships and interventions.
Propose strategies for enhancing coordination and synergy among different actors to achieve collective impact in global health.

pathways.title: Sustainability and Resource Management
pathways.description: From rethinking energy transitions to delving into the intricacies of digital sufficiency, these videos offer fresh perspectives. Discover innovative approaches to renewable energy, historical energy patterns, and the intersection of environmental and social concerns. Join the conversation, engage, and enrich your knowledge.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will gain a nuanced perspective on past energy dynamics to challenge conventional thinking in the field of energy transition.
Explore the benefits and challenges of renewable energy communities and co-ownership models.
Delve into the history of energy development and consumption.
Grasp the concept of digital sufficiency and its role in ecological and economic contexts

pathways.title: Investigating El Niño For Teachers
pathways.description: Interested in engaging your students with real-time NOAA data? In this module, designed for 6-12th grade classrooms, students evaluate changes in sea surface temperature over space and time and investigate how El Niño affects phytoplankton & species distribution in the eastern Pacific Ocean. The module offers lessons at five different levels, beginning with basic graph interpretation (Levels 1 & 2) and building towards activities that challenge students to ask questions and develop their own data investigations (Levels 4 & 5).
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to identify and interpret sea surface temperature (SST) on a false-color map.
Compare SST data in map and graph formats, explaining their uses.
Distinguish between normal and El Niño conditions using SST data.
Analyze SST and chlorophyll data to explain El Niño's impact on ocean productivity.
Design and conduct a research investigation on El Niño using real data.

pathways.title: Rubin Voices
pathways.description: Rubin Voices is an initiative by Rubin Observatory that highlights and celebrates the wide diversity of people who make the observatory and its science possible. Browse the narratives below to meet the staff, scientists, and educators who are part of the observatory or use its data—and listen to what they sound like too!
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will explore careers in astronomy and identify the diverse roles of scientists, engineers, and educators contributing to the Rubin Observatory.
Recognize the human side of science and understand the personal journeys, backgrounds, and inspirations of individuals involved in Rubin Observatory’s work.
Appreciate diversity in STEM and reflect on how people from different backgrounds collaborate to advance astronomy and data science.

pathways.title: Individual and Collaborative Learning
pathways.description: Individual and collaborative learning occurs when a lesson incorporates tasks that encourage students to work on their own and in groups. This pathway defines individual and collaborative learning and provides an opportunity to see these types of learning in practice in a classroom.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will have a comprehensive understanding of individual and collaborative learning.
You will be able to identify examples of individual and collaborative learning that can be used in your class.
You will know how to utilize group work to foster collaboration and active learning among students.

pathways.title: What Chemical Biology Is Revealing About Chronic Stress and Disease
pathways.description: Chronic stress can lead to changes at the cellular level, resulting in disease. Dr Gabriela Chiosis of New York’s Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center is investigating the chemical pathways behind this process, and how treatments could address the issue.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway students will be able to define key terms related to chemical biology, such as chaperome, chaperone, chemical probe, and epichaperome, and explain their significance in understanding cellular processes and disease development.

Analyze the impact of chemical probes and innovative drug candidates on medical research and propose potential future advancements in the field of chemical biology for diagnosis and treatment strategies.
Students will be able to analyze the potential impact of chemical biology on diagnosing and treating diseases, with a specific focus on Alzheimer's disease

pathways.title: Where There Is Smoke...Is There Always Fire?
pathways.description:
Embark on an interstellar journey to discover the generation of smoke without fire using this pathway and accompanying Student Guide. Spanning 6 days and 290 minutes of lessons, students will engage in activities and guided lessons about chemical reactions, atomic structures, and states of matter to unravel the mystery of how smoke can be produced in the absence of fire.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 50 minutes. Items 1-3 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 60 minutes. Item 4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3-4, 60 minutes. Item 5-9 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4-5, 60 minutes. Item 10-12 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 60 minutes. Item 13-14 of the pathway.
Before we begin, let's gear up with Building Blocks and States of Matter to make sure we're all set to solve this smoke mystery!
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure of matter, recognizing that substances are composed of various types of atoms and their arrangements contribute to the properties of those substances.
You will demonstrate an understanding of how the composition and interactions of matter are governed by learning standards related to the structure and properties of matter and chemical reactions.

You will identify the properties of matter, comprehend their changes during chemical reactions, and describe the processes, including the rearrangement of atoms into new molecules with distinct properties.
You will define and differentiate between energy release and storage in chemical reactions, identifying examples and comprehending the associated energy transformations.
You will better understand thermal energy transfer's connection to temperature differences, its role in chemical reactions and other processes.

pathways.title: Detecting Deepfakes: How Can We Ensure That Generative AI Is Used for Good?
pathways.description: The field of generative artificial intelligence (AI) is advancing at an astronomical pace. As a result, deepfakes – manipulated pieces of media using generative AI technology and designed to trick their viewers – are becoming more convincing, prevalent and problematic. Professor Siwei Lyu, based at University at Buffalo, The State University of New York in the US, is determined to halt the advance of deepfake media and ensure that generative AI is used for the good of society.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define key terms related to media forensics, such as artefacts, deepfake, disinformation, and generative AI.
Recall the potential risks associated with deepfake technology, including personal security and democratic processes.
Explain how generative AI creates deepfake media by using algorithms trained on large datasets.
Describe the basic principles behind the detection of deepfakes, including the identification of artefacts or noise.
Compare and contrast the benefits and risks of generative AI in different contexts, such as medical rehabilitation and disinformation.

pathways.title: Waves
pathways.description: Sound and light travel as waves. Explore the basics of wave properties, observe vibrations in slow motion, and adjust frequency and amplitude with hands-on experiments.
learning_objectives: By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to describe the basic features of waves, such as wavelength, amplitude, frequency, and speed.
Learn the difference between transverse and longitudinal waves.
Learn how changing the frequency and amplitude changes the wave patterns.
Understand how waves carry energy through different materials like water, air, and solids.
Recreate Thomas Young's double-slit experiment to show how the patterns formed in this experiment prove the wave theory of light.

pathways.title: The Molecules Behind Plant Growth
pathways.description: Plants coordinate their growth through hormones – chemical messengers that instruct cells to do certain activities. Dr Alexander Jones and his team at the University of Cambridge are investigating how one particular plant hormone, gibberellin, affects cells’ behaviour, and what this means for the crops of the future.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the role of plant hormones in regulating growth and development.
Describe the function of gibberellin, a key phytohormone, in plants and how it affects cells' behavior.
Discuss the significance of gibberellin research for improving crop yields and food security.
Learn about techniques used in plant science, such as biosensors and protein engineering, and their applications in understanding plant growth and development.

pathways.title: Earth
pathways.description: Prediction on Earth comes on many forms. For this course, much of the material focuses on prediction about climate change, one of the most important efforts of modern prediction. Climate change is an essential predictive effort as it is poses an existential threat to humanity, while being caused by one of the most difficult to predict areas that we know of -- human behavior. The following course materials contain videos and essays discussing various areas of climate and Earth science prediction with eminent experts in the field.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming

pathways.title: Fluid Dynamics and Its Biological and Medical Applications
pathways.description: This pathway explores essential ideas in fluid dynamics and its biological and medical applications. The equations governing fluid dynamics are derived from the same equations that represent energy conservation. One of the most powerful equations in fluid dynamics is Bernoulli's equation, which governs the relationship between fluid pressure, kinetic energy, and potential energy. This pathway further discusses how Bernoulli's equation explains the pressure difference that provides lift for airplanes and provides the means for fluids (like water or paint or perfume) to move in useful ways.
learning_objectives: Calculate flow rate.
Define units of volume.
Explain incompressible fluids.
Explain the consequences of the equation of continuity.
Explain the terms in bernoulli's equation.
Explain how bernoulli's equation is related to conservation of energy.
Explain how to derive bernoulli's principle from bernoulli's equation.
Calculate with bernoulli's principle.
List some applications of bernoulli's principle.
Calculate using torricelli's theorem.
Calculate power in fluid flow.
Define laminar flow and turbulent flow.
Explain what viscosity is.
Calculate flow and resistance with poiseuille's law.
Explain how pressure drops due to resistance.
Use the reynolds number for a system to determine whether it is laminar or turbulent.
Calculate the reynolds number for an object moving through a fluid.
Explain whether the reynolds number indicates laminar or turbulent flow.
Describe the conditions under which an object has a terminal speed.
Define diffusion, osmosis, dialysis, and active transport.
Calculate diffusion rates.

pathways.title: Monocots and Eudicots (Dicots)
pathways.description: Monocots and dicots (now known as eudicots) exhibit different characteristics such as leaf veneration, vascular tissue, and root systems. This pathway supports botanists in analyzing root, stem, and leaf anatomy, plant classification, tissue differentiation, and structural adaptations—key for research, taxonomy, and applied plant science.
learning_objectives: Explain structural and functional adaptations of plant organs—including stems, roots, and leaves—in various environments.
Differentiate between monocots and eudicots based on leaf venation, root system, stem structure, and vascular organization.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To An Invisible World
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of what our ancestors knew; a systematic approach and types of organisms.

pathways.title: Unstoppable Us Detective Game: The Missing Code
pathways.description: Join Professor Darwina in UNSTOPPABLE US: The Detective Game: The Missing Code! This interactive game, paired with a quiz and teaching guide, helps Grades 4–7 learners explore DNA, heredity, and evolution. Solve puzzles, uncover clues, and discover how humans and animals are connected while developing scientific thinking and problem-solving skills.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to understand key facts about the similarities between humans and other animals.
Explain why humans and animals share common traits, using the framework of evolutionary theory.
Recognize that, according to scientific evidence, multiple human species once coexisted on Earth.
Be able to interpret the evolutionary tree diagram, including: how animals are grouped, and how species evolve and change over time.

pathways.title: Geometric Optics
pathways.description: This pathway explores the essential ideas relating to the behavior of light as an electromagnetic wave including what makes a diamond sparkle, how images are formed by lenses for the purposes of magnification or photography, why objects in some mirrors are closer than they appear, and why clear mountain streams are always a little bit deeper than they appear to be. The pathway also examines different ways of thinking about and modeling light and when each method is most appropriate.
learning_objectives: List the ways by which light travels from a source to another location.
Explain reflection of light from polished and rough surfaces.
Calculate the index of refraction, given the speed of light in a medium.
Explain the phenomenon of total internal reflection.
Describe the workings and uses of fiber optics.
Examine the reason for the sparkle of diamonds.
Explain the phenomenon of dispersion and discuss its advantages and disadvantages.
List the rules for ray tracking for thin lenses.
Illustrate the formation of images using the technique of ray tracing.
Calculate the power of a lens given the focal length.
Illustrate image formation in a flat mirror.
Explain with ray diagrams the formation of an image using spherical mirrors.
Calculate focal length and magnification given radius of curvature, distance of object, and distance of image.

pathways.title: The Musculoskeletal System
pathways.description: The muscular and skeletal systems provide support to the body and allow for a wide range of movement. The bones of the skeletal system protect the body’s internal organs and support the weight of the body. The muscles of the muscular system contract and pull on the bones, allowing for movements as diverse as standing, walking, running, and grasping items. Injury or disease affecting the musculoskeletal system can be very debilitating. In humans, the most common musculoskeletal diseases worldwide are caused by malnutrition. Ailments that affect the joints are also widespread, such as arthritis, which can make movement difficult and—in advanced cases—completely impair mobility. This pathway provides an in-depth look at types of skeletal systems, bone, joints & skeletal movement, and muscle contraction & locomotion. The pathway also provides vocabulary support for these topics, as well as opportunities for learners to self-assess their understanding by analyzing figures and answering questions.
learning_objectives: Name and classify the different types of bones in the skeleton.
Explain the role of the different cell types in bone.
Explain how bone forms during development.
Name and classify the different types of joints on the basis of structure.
Explain the role of joints in skeletal movement.
Name and classify the different types of muscle tissue.
Explain the role of muscles in locomotion.

pathways.title: Population Evolution
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content explores how random events, population conditions, and mathematical models shape the genetic makeup and evolution of populations. Learners will investigate how population size, migration, mutation, mating patterns, and selection influence whether a population is evolving. Through interactive analysis and exploration of Hardy–Weinberg mathematical modeling, learners will describe how changes in these conditions impact genetic diversity, create differences between populations, and provide evidence for evolution over time.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe how random events can influence the evolution of a population and change its genetic makeup over time.
Identify the conditions that cause allele and genotype frequencies to shift in a population.
Explain how a population will be affected when any of the Hardy-Weinberg conditions are not met.

pathways.title: Microgravity
pathways.description: Being in microgravity long-term is an entirely new experience for the human body that results in many physiological changes. In this pathway, discover the effects microgravity has on the body and the research that scientists are doing to minimize the negative health effects that come with prolonged weightlessness.
learning_objectives: Develop an understanding of how the human body reacts to the absence of gravity.
Compare/contrast the challenges associated with running experiments on earth and in space.

pathways.title: A Degrowth Perspective For a Post-Covid19 World (3 Parts)
pathways.description: Governments around the world are designing new growth strategies to revive their economies, as a response to the economic crisis that covid19 is triggering. Degrowth has been arguing for many years that economic growth is not, and cannot be, a viable solution to the problems of our times. Basing a post-covid recovery on growth policies does not have the power to address the social and ecological problems, because it is not dealing with the fundamental causes of the ongoing crises: it is merely treating symptoms. This is the topic of the first video. The second video then introduces some of the key principles of degrowth, such as autonomy, conviviality, decommodification, care, democracy and solidarity, and how these notions invite us to rethink the foundation of our economies. The third and last video explores three interconnected strategies, that can be summarised as follows: produce less, share more, and decide together.

pathways.title: Can Soil Microbes Improve Agricultural Sustainability?
pathways.description: Synthetic fertilisers are currently necessary for producing enough food for the global population to survive, but they are known to contribute to greenhouse gas levels, contaminate water sources and increase soil acidity. At Washington State University in the US, Dr Nathaniel Boyer is a biochemist working to find a more environmentally friendly solution.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will be able to explain the role of nitrogen in agriculture and understand the process of nitrogen fixation, highlighting its importance in crop growth.
Learners will gain knowledge about Biological Nitrogen Fixation (BNF), understanding the role of diazotrophs and the symbiotic relationship between these microorganisms and legume plants.
Learners will be able to analyze the various opportunities and pathways available for a career in biochemistry, including the importance of laboratory experience and computational tools.

Learners will assess the potential of biofertilizers as an alternative to synthetic fertilizers, fostering critical thinking and problem-solving skills in the context of environmental science and sustainability.

pathways.title: Fluid Statics
pathways.description: This pathway explores how the arrangement and interaction of the particles—atoms and molecules—that make up a fluid define many of its macroscopic characteristics, like density and pressure. While the number of particles in a fluid is often immense, it is possible to use a probabilistic approach in order to explain how a fluid affects its environment in a variety of ways. From the pressure a fluid places on the walls of a hydraulic system to a variety of biological and medical applications, understanding a fluid's properties begins with understanding its internal structure. Objects and systems have properties such as mass and charge. Systems may have internal structure. This pathway also explores how the mathematics of probability can be used to describe the behavior of complex systems and to interpret the behavior of quantum mechanical systems.
learning_objectives: Describe the common phases of matter.
Explain the physical characteristics of solids, liquids, and gases.
Describe the arrangement of atoms in solids, liquids, and gases.
Define density.
Calculate the mass of a reservoir from its density.
Compare/contrast and contrast the densities of various substances.
Define pressure.
Explain the relationship between pressure and force.
Calculate force given pressure and area.
Describe and understand applications of pascal's principle.
Define and derive relationships between forces in a hydraulic system.
Define pressure.
Define pascal's principle.
Define gauge pressure and absolute pressure.
Describe and understand the working of aneroid and open-tube barometers.
Define buoyant force.
Describe archimedes' principle.
Describe and understand why objects float or sink.
Describe and understand the relationship between density and archimedes' principle.
Describe and understand cohesive and adhesive forces.
Describe and understand capillary action.
Define surface tension.
Explain the concept of pressure in the human body.
Explain systolic and diastolic blood pressures.
Describe pressures in the eye, lungs, spinal column, bladder, and skeletal system.

pathways.title: Natural and Artificial Selection
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content examines how natural selection, environmental change, and human activities shape the evolution and diversity of populations. Learners will explore the causes of natural selection, how differential survival and reproductive success drive evolutionary change, and why phenotypic variation is essential for adapting to shifting selective pressures. Learners will also investigate convergent evolution and analyze how artificial selection allows humans to influence variation in other organisms. 
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe the causes of natural selection and how natural selection changes populations over time.
Describe why phenotypic variation within a population is important for survival and adaptation.
Explain how human activities can increase or decrease genetic diversity in a population.
Explain how environmental changes can lead to evolutionary changes in a population.

pathways.title: Rotational Motion and Angular Momentum
pathways.description: This pathway explores how the same physics describes the exhilarating spin of a skater and the wrenching force of a tornado, the importance of conservation laws, and their role in determining how changes happen in a system. It also presents how a change of a conserved quantity is always equal to the transfer of that quantity between interacting systems for both energy and angular momentum. The conservation of angular momentum in relation to the external net torque parallels that of linear momentum conservation in relation to the external net force. This pathway also introduces the concept of rotational inertia: a concept that takes into account not only the mass of an object or a system, but also the distribution of mass within the object or system. Changes in the rotational inertia of a system could lead to changes in the motion of the system. The pathway further describes how all important aspects of rotational motion either have already been defined for linear motion or have exact analogues in linear motion, and that force, energy, and power are associated with rotational motion.
learning_objectives: Describe uniform circular motion.
Explain nonuniform circular motion.
Calculate angular acceleration of an object.
Describe the link between linear and angular acceleration.
Describe the kinematics of rotational motion.
Define and derive rotational kinematic equations.
Interpret problem solving strategies for rotational kinematics.
Describe and understand the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration.
Examine the turning effect of force.
Discuss the analogy between force and torque, mass and moment of inertia, and linear acceleration and angular acceleration.
Define and derive the equation for rotational work.
Calculate rotational kinetic energy.
Define and demonstrate the law of conservation of energy.
Describe and understand the analogy between angular momentum and linear momentum.
Describe the relationship between torque and angular momentum.
Define and apply the law of conservation of angular momentum.
Describe collisions of extended bodies in two dimensions.
Examine collisions at the point of percussion.
Describe the right-hand rule to find the direction of angular velocity, momentum, and torque.
Explain the gyroscopic effect.
Examine how earth acts like a gigantic gyroscope.

pathways.title: NASA | Physical Sciences Program
pathways.description: Dive into the fascinating world of space physics with this pathway. Explore how the unique space environment unlocks new insights in physics and engineering, focusing on Fluid Physics, Combustion Science, and Soft Matter. Discover the collaborative model that brings together community participation, from formulating investigations to sharing groundbreaking data.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand how the Space Physical Science Program uses the space environment as a tool to provide insights in physics and engineering science, and how physical systems respond to spaceflight environments.
Explore three of six Physical Sciences Programs: Fluid Physics, Combustion Science and Soft Matter.
Investigate the model that enables community participation in the formulation of investigations, collection of knowledge, and dissemination of data.

pathways.title: Virus Alarm Lesson
pathways.description: A fun, and interactive lesson that will teach students more about virology and epidemiology. The Virus Alarm Game takes students on a journey through the world of virology by putting them in the shoes of a city government during the outbreak of a novel disease.
learning_objectives: Students understand that viruses spread exponentially when no countermeasures are taken (vaccination, physical distancing, hygiene precautions).
Students understand the concept of the basic reproduction number (R0).
Students practice interpreting data and creating graphs and learn basic concepts of mathematical modeling of infectious diseases.
Students discuss the basic reproduction number, hospitalization rate, and lethality of different viral diseases (e. g. SARS-CoV-2).
Students discuss which strategies are effective in containing a virus or slowing its spread.
Students learn to distinguish between the terms basic reproduction number (R0) and net reproduction number (R or Reff).
Students understand the need for physical / social distancing.
Students discuss the societal consequences (economic downturn, unemployment, learning loss) of various epidemiological measures.
The students simulate how vaccination affects the viral spread.
The students understand how different vaccination rates affect viral spread and the number of sick people.
The students understand the concept of herd immunity.

pathways.title: Research Benefits Human Health
pathways.description: Neuroscience research has a profound impact on combating brain diseases and neurodegeneration. This pathway will guide you through the significant contributions of neuroscience in treating various brain disorders. You'll gain insights into the crucial role of human clinical trials in the development of safe new drugs, and understand the importance of comprehensive research in the creation and application of effective therapies.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to provide examples of successful neuroscience discoveries.
You will explore how neuroscience research has positively impacted neurodegeneration and treatments for brain disease.
You will be able to explain the role of human clinical trials in developing safe new drugs.
You will understand the importance of thorough research in the development and use of therapies.

pathways.title: Medical Applications of Nuclear Physics
pathways.description: This pathway focuses on medical applications of nuclear physics. The sections on fusion and fission address the ideas that objects and systems have properties, such as mass, and that interactions between systems can result in changes in those systems. The changes that occur as a result of interactions always satisfy conservation laws. The mass conservation and energy conservation are replaced by the law of conservation of mass-energy.
learning_objectives: Explain the working principle behind an anger camera.
Describe the spect and pet imaging techniques.
Define various units of radiation.
Describe rbe.
Explain the concept of radiotherapy and list typical doses for cancer therapy.
Define food irradiation, low dose, and free radicals.
Define nuclear fusion.
Discuss processes to achieve practical fusion energy generation.
Define nuclear fission.
Discuss how fission fuel reacts and describe what it produces.
Describe controlled and uncontrolled chain reactions.
Discuss different types of fission and thermonuclear bombs.
Explain the dangers and health impacts of nuclear explosions.

pathways.title: Many Planets, One Earth
pathways.description: Unit 1 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. Astronomers have discovered dozens of planets orbiting other stars, and space probes have explored many parts of our solar system, but so far scientists have only discovered one place in the universe where conditions are suitable for complex life forms: Earth. In this unit, examine the unique characteristics that make our planet habitable and learn how these conditions were created.

pathways.title: Two-Dimensional Kinematics
pathways.description: This pathway explores essential ideas in kinematics, the study of the motion of objects, extended to two dimensions.
learning_objectives: Test and observe that motion in two dimensions consists of horizontal and vertical components.
Explain the independence of horizontal and vertical vectors in two-dimensional motion.
Explain the rules of vector addition, subtraction, and multiplication.
Identify and apply graphical methods of vector addition and subtraction to determine the displacement of moving objects.
Explain the rules of vector addition and subtraction using analytical methods.
Define and apply analytical methods to determine vertical and horizontal component vectors.
Define and apply analytical methods to determine the magnitude and direction of a resultant vector.
Identify and explain the properties of a projectile, such as acceleration due to gravity, range, maximum height, and trajectory.
Calculate and determine the location and velocity of a projectile at different points in its trajectory.
Define and apply the principle of independence of motion to solve projectile motion problems.
Define and apply principles of vector addition to determine relative velocity.
Explain the significance of the observer in the measurement of velocity.

pathways.title: BLOCK 4 - Rethinking economic policy after the Coronacrisis
pathways.description:
Welcome to this first MOOC series on “Rethinking the economy after the coronavirus”. This series has been initiated by the EPOG+ Master programme during the first lockdown period, with the objective of understanding the socio-economic consequences of the COVID and the perspectives for policy makers to reorientate our development model. Taking benefit of a very qualified network of scholars, this MOOC series contains several lectures, with very diversified but totally complementary contents, coping with some of the aspects we collectively understood as vital aspects for the understanding of the current crisis, not only in its surface, but in its structural dimension. First of all, it is crucial to discuss the ecological roots of Coronocrisis. Connected to that, we have lectures highlighting that Coronacrisis is a multidimensional crisis, having deep impacts on the world economy and society, and allowing some scholars to claim that it will possibly provoke structural changes. Finally, it is essential to discuss the economic policies that are being implemented worldwide to face the Coronocrisis. As you can see, it is a very dense and extremely instigating MOOC series and we hope you will enjoy it!
BLOCK 1 - The ecological roots of Coronacrisis
BLOCK 2 – Impacts and structural changes provoked by Coronacrisis
BLOCK 3 – The economy and the coronavirus in the global South
BLOCK 4 – Rethinking economic policy after the coronavirus

pathways.title: Excellent Energy Engineering
pathways.description:
With the increased focus on engineering and design instruction, teachers are looking for more activities that incorporate these concepts into their curriculum. Many NEED activities lend themselves well to being modified for engineering and design challenges. In this sampler we have modified six of our lessons to demonstrate how seamlessly you can incorporate engineering and design into your science content lessons.
The key to developing a good design lesson is “less is more.” Give your students as few directions as possible; provide them with just enough information to know what the purpose of the challenge is and how they can get started with their design. It can be difficult as a teacher to let loose of the reins a bit and allow students more freedom in their explorations. However, we feel confident that your students will be able to learn what they need to learn with your guidance, while being able to develop the skills necessary to problem-solve and design solutions to well-defined challenges.
None of the parameters set in the activities within this booklet are magical or set in stone. As you read through them, you may have adifferent idea about how you will challenge your students or how to best level the challenge to meet your students’ needs. Add, delete, oredit parameters as necessary. We have also provided a generic rubric for assessment of engineering and design and science process skillson pages 13-14. Use and edit as needed.
Each activity in this sampler is based on more cookbook-style activities already in print in other NEED curriculum guides, and each isreferenced in the individual activity. We recommend you review those activities to become familiar with them. The other important item toremember is to trust yourself and your students. If you give them well-defined parameters, they will meet or exceed your expectations. Thekey to quality student work is to tell students what is expected. If an activity is too open-ended, students become overwhelmed and aren’tsure where to begin.
Each activity includes an introduction, suggested design and testing parameters, and “Teachers’ Cheats”, along with additional extensionand enrichment opportunities. Teachers’ Cheats provide helpful tips for when your students become bogged down or get stuck on onearea of the challenge. It is suggested that you do not share these tips with students prior to beginning the challenges.
learning_objectives: Design a wind turbine to lift as many paper clips as possible.
Build a house using energy efficient principles.
Construct a simple circuit.
Design a battery using a piece of fruit.
Construct a solar oven.

pathways.title: Evaporative Cooling
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the concept of evaporative cooling and explains the processes involved. Hands-on activities included in the pathway allow students to investigate several aspects of evaporative cooling.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Explain how evaporative cooling works.
State at least two examples of evaporative cooling.
Apply knowledge to build an evaporative cooler.

pathways.title: Mutations and Viruses
pathways.description:
This AP Biology pathway explores how changes in DNA contribute to genetic variation and influence phenotypes. Learners will investigate the different types of mutations and how alterations in DNA sequences can affect the function or amount of gene products. Learners will also examine how changes in genotype produce phenotypic variation that can be acted on by natural selection. This includes the effects of chromosomal changes, polyploidy, and human disorders, as well as mechanisms in prokaryotes—such as transformation, transduction, conjugation, and transposition—that increase genetic diversity. Through these examples, learners will see how mutations and other sources of variation drive evolution and shape the diversity of life. Coming soon: new interactive content on small scale mutations, horizontal gene transfer, viral cycles and repairing DNA errors.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Identify the various types of mutation.
Describe how changes in genotype may result in changes in phenotype.
Analyze how alterations in DNA sequences contribute to variation that can be subject to natural selection.

pathways.title: Reproduction in Humans
pathways.description: In humans, sexual reproduction combines male and female gametes to form a zygote. This pathway explores the human reproductive system, including fertilization and the birth process. Study how male and female gametes unite to form a zygote and examine reproductive anatomy, hormones, and developmental stages.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and functions of the male and female reproductive systems in humans.
Explain the processes of gametogenesis, fertilization, and zygote formation.
Outline the stages of embryonic development and the birth process.
Discuss the roles of hormones in regulating human reproduction.
Analyze the importance of reproduction in heredity and the continuity of life.

pathways.title: Think Like a Martian
pathways.description: This pathway provides activities that can be tried at home to get a hands-on experience of solving problems like a scientist.
learning_objectives: Design a system for landing a rover on mars.
Build a prototype mars rover.
Test some of the factors that impact space balloon design.
Build an astronaut lander.
Construct a mars helicopter.

pathways.title: NASA | Cohesion and Adhesion
pathways.description: Engage with the fascinating world of surface tension, adhesion, and cohesion, and explore how these water properties enable vital processes like capillary action in plants and fluid behavior in space, enhancing our understanding of microgravity physics.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will compare the properties of adhesion and cohesion and their effects on water behavior.
Explain the role of surface tension in everyday phenomena and its importance in the environment.
Explore the process of capillary action and its significance in plant hydration.
Analyze the behavior of fluids in microgravity and its implications for space exploration.

pathways.title: Endocrine System: Structure, Regulation, and Homeostasis
pathways.description: Imagine a system that keeps your body running smoothly, controlling your energy, mood, and more. That's the endocrine system! Learn about its key roles, structures, and the exciting world of STEM careers related to it.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and function of the endocrine system, including glands, hormones, and target organs.
Explain the mechanisms of hormone synthesis, secretion, and regulation by feedback loops.
Analyze the roles of major endocrine glands, such as the pituitary, thyroid, adrenal, and pancreas, in maintaining homeostasis.
Evaluate common endocrine disorders and conditions, such as diabetes, thyroid disorders, and hormonal imbalances.
Demonstrate an understanding of the interplay between the endocrine system and other body systems in coordinating physiological responses.
Explore different careers related to this topic or those that may be adjacent to this topic.

pathways.title: How Substances Move: Exploring Diffusion and Its Factors
pathways.description: Explore how substances move from one place to another through diffusion. Learn how the size of molecules, temperature, and concentration gradients affect diffusion, and discover how semipermeable membranes control what enters and exits cells.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain what diffusion is and how it works.
Describe semipermeable membranes and their role in diffusion.
Explain how the state of matter affects the rate of diffusion.
Describe how molecule size influences the rate of diffusion.
Explain how the concentration gradient impacts the rate of diffusion.
Describe the effect of temperature on the rate of diffusion.
Identify all factors that affect the rate of diffusion.

pathways.title: Introduction to Cell, Cell Structure and Division
pathways.description: Cell division in prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells involves four key events: cell division signal, DNA replication, DNA segregation, and cytokinesis. Explore cell structure, mitosis, and plant cell walls made of cellulose, gaining skills to compare organisms, explain genetic material organization, and link cell biology to growth, reproduction, and disease prevention.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the basic structure and functions of prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, including subcellular organelles.
Compare and contrast the processes of cell division in prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, identifying key similarities and differences.
Explain the stages of mitosis and the role of cytokinesis in producing genetically identical cells.
Identify the composition and functions of the plant cell wall, including its role in structural support.
Analyze the importance of cell signaling and environmental factors in initiating cell division.

pathways.title: Raven Reddy, PhD - Pharmaceutical Scientist
pathways.description: Dr. Raven Reddy is a pharmaceutical scientist who loves a good game of golf, pop punk rock, and his dog Oakley. He studies the inner workings of cells and his work supports teams of scientists and engineers and acts as a bridge between the worlds of research and business.

pathways.title: NASA | Spacecraft Fire Safety
pathways.description: Ensuring spacecraft fire safety is vital for the success of astronaut missions. Discover how fire behaves in microgravity through NASA's innovative Saffire experiments. Explore the challenges and breakthroughs in mitigating fire risks in space, and learn how these advancements enhance safety for both space and Earth-based environments.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will have gained knowledge of the various Spacecraft Fire (Saffire) experiments.
Understand how fire behaves in microgravity, and how different materials propagate flames in space.

pathways.title: Chromosomal Inheritance and Genetic Disorders
pathways.description:
This AP Biology pathway explores how chromosomal inheritance drives genetic variation in sexually reproducing organisms. Learners will examine how processes such as segregation, independent assortment, and random fertilization create diversity in offspring. They will also investigate how variations in chromosome number or structure, as well as single-gene mutations, can lead to genetic disorders. Through real-world examples, learners will see how the patterns of gene transmission influence traits in humans and other organisms. Coming soon: new interactive content on chromosomal mutations and small scale mutations.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Describe how sexual reproduction creates genetic variation through processes such as segregation, independent assortment, and random fertilization.
Explain how genetic disorders can result from the inheritance of a single affected allele, specific chromosomal change, or nondisjunction.

pathways.title: The Science of Sound Waves
pathways.description: Explore the science of sound through animations and simulations that reveal how transverse and longitudinal waves transfer energy. Investigate key acoustic concepts like frequency, amplitude, wave speed, and how sound travels in different media.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe acoustics as the branch of physics dealing with sound and explore its real-world applications, such as in music, communication, and engineering.
Identify and describe wave parameters—frequency, amplitude, wavelength, period, and speed—as they relate to sound waves.
Use interactive simulations to investigate the properties and behavior of transverse and longitudinal waves, especially in the context of sound propagation.

pathways.title: How Can Intelligent Systems Revolutionise Healthcare?
pathways.description: Explore how intelligent systems are revolutionizing healthcare by enhancing efficiency and improving patient outcomes through the use of advanced AI and machine learning technologies.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you will learn about the fundamental principles of machine learning and its practical applications.
Explore ethical considerations related to AI in healthcare.
Gain insights into the challenges and rewards of working in AI and electrical engineering.
Analyze the impact of artificial intelligence in healthcare, understanding its potential benefits and ethical considerations.

pathways.title: Black Holes: The Meeting of Gravity and Quantum Physics
pathways.description: We know that black holes exist through a mix of complex mathematics and astrophysics but linking mathematical ideas to what we can observe in the Universe is no easy task. Dr Daniel Terno and his team at Macquarie University in Sydney, Australia, are building a framework of characteristics that can be used to search for black holes in the Universe, but their findings may challenge our understanding of the fundamental laws of physics.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you will understand the interdisciplinary nature of quantum physics and its applications in understanding phenomena like black holes.
Identify key concepts and terminology related to black holes, such as event horizon, singularity, and ultra-compact objects (UCOs).
Evaluate the significance of using quantum mechanics to understand black hole physics and its potential implications for our understanding of the fundamental laws of physics.

pathways.title: Statics and Torque
pathways.description: The first two sections of this pathway focus on the two conditions necessary for equilibrium. They distinguish between stationary bridges and cars moving at constant velocity, and introduce a second equilibrium condition, this time involving rotation. Exploring the second equilibrium condition, torque is discussed. Much like a force, torque provides the capability for acceleration; however, with careful attention, torques may also be balanced and equilibrium can be reached. The remainder of this pathway discusses a variety of interesting equilibrium applications. From the art of balancing, to simple machines, to the muscles in the body, the world relies upon the principles of equilibrium to remain stable. This pathway explores just how closely related these events truly are.
learning_objectives: Describe the first condition for equilibrium.
Explain static equilibrium.
Explain dynamic equilibrium.
Describe the second condition that is necessary to achieve equilibrium.
Explain torque and the factors on which it depends.
Describe the role of torque in rotational mechanics.
Describe the types of equilibrium.
Describe stable and unstable equilibriums.
Describe neutral equilibrium.
Discuss the applications of statics in real lif.
Identify and discuss various problem-solving strategies in statics.
Describe different simple machines.
Calculate the mechanical advantage.
Explain the forces exerted by muscles.
Describe how a bad posture causes back strain.
Discuss the benefits of skeletal muscles attached close to joints.
Discuss various complexities in the real system of muscles, bones, and joints.

pathways.title: Electrostatics and Electric Fields
pathways.description: These resources introduce the concepts of electrostatics and electric fields, explaining key terms like electric charge and how static electricity works. Through interactive simulations and hands-on activities, such as using balloons to explore static electricity, learners will engage in experiments to visualize electric charge behavior and better understand its real-world applications in science and technology.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand what an electric field is and how it is generated.
You will understand how static electricity causes balloons to stick to a surface.
You will gain insight into the difference between insulators and conductors when they come into contact with a charged object.
You will be able to name several real-world applications of the study of electrostatics.

pathways.title: NASA | International Space Station Facilities
pathways.description: Explore the International Space Station (ISS), focusing on its advanced research facilities, including the Combustion Integrated Rack, Fluids Integrated Rack, Microgravity Science Glovebox and Maintenance Work Area. These facilities enable scientists to conduct experiments in the ISS's unique microgravity environment, pushing the boundaries of science and technology.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will identify the key research facilities aboard the ISS, such as the CIR, FIR, and MSG.
Describe the unique research capabilities provided by the ISS’s microgravity environment and how they differ from Earth-based laboratories.
Examine the role of the Combustion Integrated Rack (CIR), Fluids Integrated Rack (FIR), and Microgravity Science Glovebox (MSG) in advancing scientific research.

pathways.title: How Do Our Kidneys Influence Our Cardiovascular Health?
pathways.description: The human body is a complex and interconnected system. In the Human Translational Nephrology Lab at the University of Iowa, USA, Dr Diana Jalal is investigating the links between the kidneys and the cardiovascular system. Patients with chronic kidney disease are at higher risk of developing cardiovascular disease, so Diana hopes to discover new therapies that will improve their health outcomes.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define cardiovascular disease (CVD) and chronic kidney disease (CKD).
Recall the functions of kidneys in maintaining blood composition and waste removal.
Comprehend the mechanisms behind endothelial dysfunction and vascular stiffness in patients with CKD.
Assess the dual role of a clinical nephrologist in patient care and research.

pathways.title: NASA | What is a Fluid?
pathways.description: Embark on an exciting journey to uncover the mysteries of fluids with this pathway! Explore the fascinating properties of fluids through interactive articles and engaging videos, delving into fluid dynamics, thermodynamics, and the behavior of fluids in microgravity. Witness amazing experiments and gain a comprehensive understanding of how fluids play a crucial role both on Earth and in space!
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the fundamental properties and behaviors of fluids, including the principles of fluid dynamics and thermodynamics.
Explore the unique behavior of fluids in microgravity environments, using real-life experiments conducted on the International Space Station.
Analyze the applications and implications of fluid physics in both terrestrial and space contexts.

pathways.title: Nutrition and Food Security: Health and Policy
pathways.description: Learn how nutrition impacts health and prevents chronic diseases in this Nutritional Risk Factors pathway. Examine the global burden of malnutrition, food insecurity, and micronutrient deficiencies, and discover how agriculture and food systems shape dietary patterns. Propose interventions to improve food security and nutrition at individual, community, and policy levels.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the importance of nutrition in promoting health and preventing chronic diseases.
Analyze the global burden of malnutrition, including undernutrition, micronutrient deficiencies, and obesity.
Evaluate factors contributing to food insecurity and inequities in access to nutritious food.
Discuss the role of agriculture, food systems, and food policies in shaping dietary patterns and nutritional outcomes.
Propose interventions to improve nutrition and food security at the individual, community, and policy levels.

pathways.title: Facing Coronacrisis in Peripheral Countries: The Economic Policy and the Political Economy Constraints (4 Parts)
pathways.description: In many countries, the national governments are responding to Coronacrisis with atypical economic policies, notably aiming to assure a minimal income for the whole population, and to save companies and banks from bankruptcy. Interestingly, this effort involves a clear alleviation on the ordinary constraints to economic policies. Nevertheless, in addition to these usual economic policies constraints, we cannot neglect the existence of violent political economy restrictions, especially in peripheral countries. In this module, we will therefore discuss the restrictions for the implementation of macroeconomic policy responses to Coronacrisis in peripheral countries, with focus on those related to the asymmetries of the International Monetary and Financial System (IMFS) – i.e., the currency hierarchy.

pathways.title: Water Is Critical to Life! Do Your Part to Protect the Water Cycle in Your Area
pathways.description:
Learn how humans can restore and protect waterways with this pathway and accompanying student guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (195-215 minutes) focusing on watersheds, runoff, and how humans can restore waterways. Meet scientists in the field to learn about the field of hydrology and what as a career in water scientist entails.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2 & 3, 40-60 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 9-11 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, in the student pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 15 minutes, items 12-13 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will understand how water moves through Earth’s system.
Students will be able to demonstrate understanding of patterns of movement in our Earth’s systems by accurately creating a presentation.

Students will be able to engage in virtual experiments and collaborative discussion to enhance critical thinking skills, explore the relationships between life and Earth’s water cycle, related careers exploration, and ask relevant questions.

pathways.title: Higher Order Structures
pathways.description: This pathway explains how higher-order structures work to perform complex metabolic processes and maintain the body's internal environment.
learning_objectives: Develop your understating of higher order biological structures.
Test your understanding of how different levels of organization feed into one anther and operate together.

pathways.title: NASA | Boiling Experiments
pathways.description: Boiling is an efficient way of transferring heat energy out of a material. With this pathway, learn how liquids boil differently in a microgravity environment compared to on the Earth's surface and see how the science of boiling is used to develop new cooling technologies on the International Space Station.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand why boiling is an effective way of removing heat from a material.
You will see how differently water boils on Earth vs in a microgravity environment.
You will learn about the various boiling experiments conducted on the space shuttle and on ISS.

pathways.title: Passive Transport of Molecules in Substances (Diffusion and Osmosis)
pathways.description: Water moves across a semipermeable membrane by osmosis, balancing solute concentrations inside and outside the cell. Examine how water, gases, ions, and nutrients cross selectively permeable membranes to maintain balance and support homeostasis in living organisms.
learning_objectives: Define diffusion and osmosis and describe their roles in the transport of substances across cell membranes.
Differentiate between diffusion, osmosis, facilitated diffusion, and active transport using biological and real-life examples.
Explain how concentration gradients influence the direction and rate of molecular movement across selectively permeable membranes.
Investigate diffusion and osmosis through simple laboratory activities or simulations and record observations accurately.
Relate the importance of membrane transport processes to maintaining homeostasis in living organisms.

pathways.title: NASA | What is Combustion?
pathways.description: Become familiar with the fundamentals of combustion with this learning pathway. Uncover the connection between useful energy and combustion, and understand how combustion is used to drive internal combustion engines in space crafts.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand what fire (or combustion) is.
You will understand why combustion is studied, including why it is studied in low gravity environments.
You will see that combustion can be used for a variety of purposes besides propulsion, through a hands-on activity.

pathways.title: Macroevolution
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content examines how speciation, extinction, and environmental change shape the evolution and diversity of life on Earth. Learners will explore the conditions and mechanisms that drive new species formation. They will analyze how genetic diversity, environmental change, and human activity influence extinction risk and overall ecosystem diversity. Finally, learners will analyze the scientific evidence supporting current models for the origin of life, connecting early Earth conditions to the processes that generate biodiversity today.
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Explain how new species form, including the conditions and mechanisms that drive speciation.
Describe how evolutionary and speciation rates vary across different ecological and environmental conditions.
Explain the factors that lead to population extinction and how environmental change influences extinction risk and opportunities for adaptive radiation.
Analyze how species’ diversity in ecosystems results from the balance between speciation, extinction, and genetic diversity within populations.
Summarize the scientific evidence supporting current models for the origin of life on Earth.

pathways.title: BLOCK 3 – The economy and the coronavirus in the global South
pathways.description:
Welcome to this first MOOC series on “Rethinking the economy after the coronavirus”. This series has been initiated by the EPOG+ Master programme during the first lockdown period, with the objective of understanding the socio-economic consequences of the COVID and the perspectives for policy makers to reorientate our development model. Taking benefit of a very qualified network of scholars, this MOOC series contains several lectures, with very diversified but totally complementary contents, coping with some of the aspects we collectively understood as vital aspects for the understanding of the current crisis, not only in its surface, but in its structural dimension. First of all, it is crucial to discuss the ecological roots of Coronocrisis. Connected to that, we have lectures highlighting that Coronacrisis is a multidimensional crisis, having deep impacts on the world economy and society, and allowing some scholars to claim that it will possibly provoke structural changes. Finally, it is essential to discuss the economic policies that are being implemented worldwide to face the Coronocrisis. As you can see, it is a very dense and extremely instigating MOOC series and we hope you will enjoy it!
BLOCK 1 - The ecological roots of Coronacrisis
BLOCK 2 – Impacts and structural changes provoked by Coronacrisis
BLOCK 3 – The economy and the coronavirus in the global South
BLOCK 4 – Rethinking economic policy after the coronavirus

pathways.title: Louise Nuijens, PhD - Atmospheric Scientist
pathways.description: Dr. Louise Nuijens is an atmospheric scientist who loves to travel, cook and go backpacking. She studies how clouds form, behave and interact with aspects of the atmosphere. Recently her work has become important to understanding climate change.

pathways.title: Newton's Second Law
pathways.description:
Over five days (255 minutes total), you will explore Newton’s Second Law of Motion and its connection to force, mass, and acceleration. Through videos, a simulation, a case study, and the accompanying student guide, you’ll uncover common misconceptions and apply Newton’s Laws to real-world situations.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 60 minutes, items 2-5 of the pathway and activities in the student guide.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 60 minutes, item 6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 60 minutes, item 7-10 of the pathway and acitivies in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 30 minutes, item 11 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will analyze a real-world phenomenon that connects key concepts and prepares you for a hands-on design challenge.
Identify common misconceptions about Newton’s Laws and use data to demonstrate how Newton’s Second Law mathematically relates force, mass, and acceleration.

Compare and contrast factors related to Newton’s Second Law using a Venn diagram, support scientific arguments with a Claim-Evidence-Reasoning (CER) chart, and test your understanding with a final assessment.

pathways.title: Oral Health: Anatomy, Hygiene, Health Equity, and Careers
pathways.description: Explore oral health's impact on well-being, recognize common issues, and learn hygiene techniques. Discover career paths in oral health and analyze disparities in access, proposing solutions for improving equity.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the anatomy and physiology of the oral cavity.
Recognize the importance of oral health in maintaining overall well-being.
Identify common oral health issues and their causes.
Demonstrate proper oral hygiene techniques, including brushing, flossing, and regular dental check-ups.
Analyze factors contributing to disparities in oral health care access and propose solutions for improving equity.
Explore various career opportunities in oral health and related fields.

pathways.title: Biodiversity - Ecological basics
pathways.description: Natural ecosystems are sustainable systems. Many properties and mechanisms underlying ecological sustainability have been identified. Maintaining them is a prerequisite for the reconquest of Planet viability. They should be known by everyone. They should be considered as major constraints and resources for the design of public policies and economic development. But, what are these concepts? Are they a relevant source of inspiration for building the “next world”? Do they open new avenues for innovation? A range of key core concepts, such as trade-off, evolution, diversity, competition, etc. will be discussed: What is behind? What are their consequences for the functioning of the system? What do they tell us about climate change, biodiversity crisis, disease dynamics, etc.

pathways.title: NASA | Gaseous Fuel
pathways.description: Discover how NASA is improving efficiency and reducing pollutant emissions in practical terrestrial combustions via the ACME experiments. This pathway delves into NASA's groundbreaking experiments aboard the ISS, studying how gaseous fuels behave in microgravity and advancing crucial fire prevention research.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will develop an understanding of the various experiments that utilized and studied gaseous fuels on ISS to understand their behavior in microgravity.

Explore advanced combustion research through NASA's ACME project, focusing on the behavior of laminar diffusion flames and the implications of microgravity experiments for improving combustion models and understanding flame stability and extinction.

pathways.title: How Do Sugar Gliders Glide?
pathways.description:
Explore the sugar glider's world, learn about adaptations, Newton's Third Law, and engage in hands-on experiments with balloon rockets and paper gliders. This learning pathway spans 9 days (390 hours) and is accompanied by a student guide. Through the collection of videos, articles, and activities, you will gain and demonstrate an understanding of natural selection adaptations driven by physics culminating in the creation of storybook to share your knowledge.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 30 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2 & 3, 100 minutes, items 2-5 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4, 80 minutes, item 6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5 & 8, 150 minutes, found in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 9, 30 minutes, item 7 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to recognize the forces acting on the sugar glider when it glides.
Model how Newton’s Third Law explains how the sugar glider is able to glide through the air.
Define and identify an adaptation as either behavioral or physical.
Identify adaptations of an organism and draw conclusions about the benefits of the adaptation to the organism.
Carry out a scientific experiment, collect and analyze data and draw conclusions.

pathways.title: Chemical Reactions and Energy Essentials
pathways.description:
Dive into the exciting basics of chemistry! Learn about chemical reactions, energy changes, and element properties through interactive videos, glossaries, and engaging examples
After mastering these chemistry concepts, you'll be ready to tackle the "Mastering Thermal Energy Management with Chemistry" learning pathway, where we'll apply what you've learned to real-world scenarios on the Moon!
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify reactants and products, differentiate reversible and irreversible reactions, and understand balanced equations and coefficients.
Learn to calculate percent yield and understand theoretical yield, recognizing factors that affect reaction completion.
Explore energy transfer in chemical reactions, including exothermic and endothermic processes, and the basics of thermodynamics.

pathways.title: On the Moon
pathways.description: A collection of lessons ranging 1-2 hours aimed at Grades 3-12. The hands-on activities are related to NASA's Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter and NASA's Lunar Crater Observation and Sensing Satellite missions. These fun challenges will inspire learners to think like engineers and create excitement about NASA’s missions to the Moon. Check out the cluster, NASA | Atremis I: Mission to the Moon.
learning_objectives: In this pathway, you will engage in the engineering design process including concepts like brainstorm, design, build, test, evaluate, and redesign.
You will develop skills in problem-solving, critical thinking, and collaboration.
You will develop an understanding of the core concepts of technology.
You will develop an understanding of the role of troubleshooting, research and development, invention and innovation, and experimentation in problem solving.
You will understand measurable attributes of objects and units, systems, and processes of measurement.
You will apply appropriate techniques, tools, and formulas to determine measurements.
You will learn about potential and kinetic energy.
You will learn about the effects of gravity and air resistance.

pathways.title: Artemis I: Engineering
pathways.description: Artemis I is the first in a series of increasingly complex missions that will eventually result in human exploration of the Moon and Mars. This pathway will take you through some of the engineering marvels that make this mission possible.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will have an understanding of the engineering behind the Space Launch System (SLS).
You will examine multiple engineering strategies used to ensure a successful mMoon voyage.
You will learn about the challenges that the SLS needs to overcome in space.
You will design your own rocket and crew module.

pathways.title: Global Disease: Impact, Prevention, and Epidemiology
pathways.description: Explore the critical impacts of major infectious and non-communicable diseases on global populations with this learning pathway. Uncover the social, economic, and environmental drivers of disease and learn about strategies for effective prevention, surveillance, and control.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify major infectious and non-communicable diseases affecting global populations and their impact on public health.
Analyze the social, economic, and environmental determinants of disease transmission and injury occurrence.
Evaluate strategies for disease prevention, surveillance, and control at the global, regional, and local levels.
Discuss the concept of the global burden of disease and its implications for resource allocation and health policy.
Apply epidemiological principles to analyze disease trends and patterns in different regions of the world.

pathways.title: Nidhi Santen, PhD - Energy Systems Scientist
pathways.description: Dr. Nidhi Santen is an energy sytems scientist who studies how different sources of energy can be used and energy systems built to meet our need for electricity more responsibly now and in the future.

pathways.title: The Urinary System
pathways.description: The kidneys filter about 120–140 liters of blood daily, maintaining homeostasis by regulating water, ions, and metabolic waste. Learn how nephron function, glomerular filtration, and urinary system health prevent conditions like UTIs and kidney infections.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and functions of the excretory system, particularly the urinary system, and its role in maintaining homeostasis.
Explain the process of urine formation, including filtration, reabsorption, and secretion in the nephron.
Analyze common diseases and infections of the urinary system, including their causes, symptoms, prevention, and treatment.
Evaluate the role of hormones and blood pressure regulation in kidney function and urine production.
Interpret urinalysis results to assess kidney health and detect urinary system disorders.

pathways.title: Linked Genes
pathways.description: This pathway explores how linked genes do not assort independently of one another, and how the relative distance between linked genes can be determined.
learning_objectives: Identify gametes or progeny as being parental or recombinant.
Explain how a testcross can provide evidence for or against genetic linkage.
Describe the relationship between recombination frequency and the map distance separating 2 loci on a chromosome.
Determine the relative positions of genes using two-point cross data to construct a genetic map.

pathways.title: Evolution: Changes Over Time
pathways.description: Why do organisms on the planet look different today than they did millions of years ago? This learning sequence explores the evidence for evolution through natural selection.
learning_objectives: Use mathematical representations to explain how natural selection can increase or decrease specific traits in populations over time.
Describe how genetic variations of traits in a population increase some individuals’ chances of surviving and reproducing in a specific environment.
Compare patterns in fossils, embryos and anatomy that document the existence, diversity, extinction, and change of life forms throughout Earth’s history.
Gather and synthesize information about technologies that have changed how humans influence the inheritance of desired traits in organisms.

pathways.title: How Your Brain Processes Information
pathways.description: The brain's ability to process information and adapt to new environments is a complex yet fascinating phenomenon. Here, we invite you to learn more about the brain's strengths and vulnerabilities in information processing—from coordinating reflex behavior to understanding the role of dopamine neurons in reward processing.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how the brain processes information and coordinates reflex behavior.
You will understand that complex behavior arises from multiple circuits in concert.
You will be able to illustrate a visual illusion that reveals circuit-based information processing.
You will understand how dopamine neurons work to process reward information.

pathways.title: Patterns in the Sky: Moon Phases
pathways.description:
People throughout history have noticed patterns in the sky. They have used those patterns to keep track of time, guide navigation, and understand more about our planet and other bodies in space.
