One pattern we notice is the changing shape of the Moon. Why does the moon’s shape appear to change each night? What can this tell you about the interactions between the Earth, Moon, and Sun?
Explore lunar phases in this learning pathway which is accompanied by a student guide and spans 6 days (310 minutes). Through observation, interactive models, and creative expression, culminating in evidence-based explanations of the Moon's changing appearance relative to Earth's position, you will gain a clearer understanding of the Moon's orbit and its place in the solar system.
Before starting this pathway complete a daily moon observation journal each night for at least two weeks. Moon Journal Worksheet (Thirty days of observations would be even better, but not necessary.)
Pathway breakdown:

• ENGAGE: Day 1, 50 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 100 minutes, items 3-5 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4, 60 minutes, items 6-7 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 40 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 60 minutes, items 8-11 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will explain that the Moon, which is constantly 50% illuminated by the sun, revolves (orbits) around the Earth about once every 28 days and rotates (spins around its axis) once during each revolution, causing the same side of the Moon to always face Earth.

Connect the viewers’ perspective of the Moon’s illuminated portion throughout each of its revolutions around the Earth to the lunar phases we see on Earth. Explain that a viewer’s perspective is changed by the changing angle between the Earth, Moon, and Sun.
Model the repeating patterns in lunar phases. Use the repeating patterns to predict lunar phases over time.

pathways.title: Plant Hormones
pathways.description: Explore the role of hormones in helping plants grow and adapt to environmental stimuli.
learning_objectives: Describe the function of major plant hormones, including auxin, gibberellins, cytokinins, abscisic acid, and ethylene.
Examine how hormones allow plants to respond to changing environments.
Interpret the role of environmental signals (light, temperature, water, nutrients, and wind) in triggering hormonal responses.
Analyze how plant hormones coordinate life processes such as seed germination, growth, flowering, fruit development, and senescence.
Evaluate commercial uses of plant hormones in agriculture, such as weed control and fruit ripening.

pathways.title: Conservation of Momentum
pathways.description:
Momentum stays the same during collisions. Through videos, a simulation, and the accompanying student guide, you’ll explore elastic and inelastic collisions, apply the Law of Conservation of Momentum, and use math to calculate speed after a collision, in this 255-minute lesson.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes, item 1-2 of the pathway and activity in the student guide.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 60 minutes, items 3-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 60 minutes, item 5-6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 60 minutes, item 7 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 30 minutes, in the student guide.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to understand key concepts and calculations related to momentum, collisions, and the Law of Conservation of Momentum, using these principles to determine speed after a collision.
Conduct experiments to test the Law of Conservation of Momentum in both perfectly elastic and perfectly inelastic linear collisions between two objects.
Use mathematical representations to demonstrate that the total momentum of a system remains constant when no external force is acting on it.

pathways.title: Plants Making Food? Find Out How Photosynthesis Makes This Possible!
pathways.description: In this pathway, you will explore the fascinating process of photosynthesis, learning how plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to produce the energy they need to grow and thrive. Discover the critical roles of light, water, and air in this process, and understand how photosynthesis sustains life on Earth by supporting ecosystems and providing the oxygen we breathe.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define photosynthesis and explain its importance in the ecosystem.
Describe the light-dependent and light-independent reactions.
Identify the reactants and products of photosynthesis
Understand the factors that affect photosynthesis (light, temperature, water, CO2).
Apply knowledge of photosynthesis to real-world scenarios (e.g., crop growth, forest ecosystems).
Evaluate the impact of human activities on photosynthesis and the ecosystem (e.g., deforestation, climate change).

pathways.title: Photosynthesis: The Green Machine Behind Every Meal
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the process of photosynthesis, where plants convert sunlight to synthesize food from carbon dioxide and water. Explore the process of carbon fixation and two stages: the light-dependent reaction that captures sunlight and the light-independent reaction that creates glucose. Along the way, discover the unique adaptations of C3, C4, and CAM pathways and how plants thrive in diverse environments to fuel the plant.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the key stages of photosynthesis, including light-dependent reactions, Calvin cycle, and the role of carbon fixation in energy production.
Explore how plants utilize C3, C4 and CAM pathways to adapt their photosynthetic process to different environments.
Recognize the critical role of photosynthesis in sustaining life on the earth by supporting energy flow in the ecosystem.

pathways.title: Thinking in Scale: Our Earth and Moon
pathways.description:
Explore the vastness of space and scale with this learning pathway on celestial distances, which is accompanied by this student guide, and spans 5 days (290-300 minutes). Delve into the science of the cosmos by predicting and comparing the distances from Earth to the Moon and Mars using a relatable scale model, while reading texts and watching videos to deepen your understanding of spatial relationships in the solar system. This activity will ignite your curiosity and deepen your understanding of our solar system's vastness, all while providing a captivating educational experience for aspiring astronomers and space explorers.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1-2: 80 minutes, item 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2: 30-40 minutes, items 3-7 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3; 60 minutes, items 8-9 of the pathway
• EXPLAIN: Day 4; 60 minutes, item 10 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5; 60 minutes, items 11-14 of the pathway
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will compare the distances between the Earth and Moon, the Earth and the International Space Station, and the Earth and Mars.

Explain that the diameter of the Moon is ¼ of the diameter of the Earth and that the distance between the Earth and the Moon is about 9 ½ times the circumference of Earth and about 30 times the diameter of the Earth.
Create an original and accurate scale model (in 2D or 3D) of the Earth and Moon and prove the model’s accuracy with a Claim, Evidence, and Reasoning paragraph.

pathways.title: Monitoring Estuarine Water Quality For Teachers
pathways.description: Interested in engaging your students with real-time NOAA data? In this module, designed for grades 6-8, students engage with an interactive graphing tool to explore parameters such as nutrients, temperature, salinity, pH, dissolved oxygen, and in some cases, contaminants. Students are guided through the use of these data to understand relationships between parameters, and the effect that water quality has on estuarine organisms. The module offers lessons at five different levels, beginning with basic graph interpretation (Levels 1 & 2) and building towards activities that challenge students to ask questions and develop their own data investigations (Levels 4 & 5).
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to analyze water temperature data in estuaries to identify daily and seasonal patterns.
Examine the relationship between water temperature and dissolved oxygen using real data.
Investigate how salinity varies in an estuary and its impact on aquatic species.
Evaluate the impact of water quality on the spawning of Atlantic sturgeon using real data.
Design and conduct an independent investigation using estuarine water quality data.

pathways.title: Introduction to Ecology and Ecosystems
pathways.description: Explore key ecological concepts including ecosystems, habitats, and biodiversity. Learn how biotic and abiotic factors influence energy flow, food chains, and environmental balance.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you should be able to, define and explain key ecological terms such as ecology, ecosystem, habitat, biotic, and abiotic factors.
Identify and describe various types of habitats and their characteristic organisms.
Explain the roles of producers, consumers, and decomposers in an ecosystem.
Illustrate energy flow through food chains and food webs.
Analyze how human activities impact biodiversity and ecosystem stability.
Suggest ways to conserve natural resources and promote environmental sustainability.

pathways.title: How Climate Change Affect Future Life
pathways.description: In this pathway, build essential skills to understand how climate change affects individuals, communities, and societies. Explore meaningful ways to take action—both personally and collectively—to help address this urgent global challenge.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define climate and global climate change.
Identify the characteristics of global climate and how with time they are changing.
Identify human activities which are responsible in the change of climate.
Asses the impact of the change of climate to the society.
Choose which are the best ways to preserve the environment for future generation.

pathways.title: Understanding the Behaviour of Gases With Boyle’s Law
pathways.description: This pathway explores the principles of Boyle’s Law, which describes the relationship between the pressure and volume of a gas. Through interactive simulations, videos, and problem-solving exercises, you will gain a deeper understanding of how gases behave under different conditions. By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain Boyle’s Law, conduct virtual experiments, solve related mathematical problems, and apply the concept to real-world situations.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you  will be able to define Boyle’s Law and describe its significance in gas behavior.
Identify the variables involved in Boyle’s Law and explain their relationship.
Conduct simple experiments using interactive simulations to observe Boyle’s Law in action.
Solve mathematical problems involving Boyle’s Law to analyze gas pressure and volume relationships.
Apply Boyle’s Law to real-life situations, such as using a syringe.

pathways.title: Engaging With the Science Behind Food
pathways.description: The preparation of food is biochemically complex. At North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University in the US, Dr Salam A. Ibrahim has established the Food Microbiology and Biotechnology Lab. Here, researchers and students collaborate with the food industry to address real-world challenges in food production and safety by uncovering the secrets of food microbiology. By providing students with opportunities to learn about and participate in food science research, the lab is also inspiring the next generation of food scientists.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will learn about the role of beneficial bacteria in food safety and human health, gaining a deeper understanding of food microbiology.
Learners will be able to explain the role of microorganisms in food production and safety, highlighting the importance of good hygiene and proper food preparation.
Learners will gain insight into the diverse opportunities within the field of food science, inspiring them to explore various areas such as food microbiology, chemistry, safety, and more.

pathways.title: A Superpowered Model of the Whole Universe
pathways.description: The mysterious substance of dark matter and the enigmatic force of dark energy may hold the key to how the Universe formed. But how can scientists study something that cannot be seen or explained? At Durham University in the UK, Dr Sownak Bose is using techniques from computational cosmology to uncover the mysteries of the early Universe.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand more about dark matter, and its importance in the formation of galaxies and the universe.
You will understand how simulations and computer modelling are used to better understand the properties and behavior of dark matter.
You will understand the relationship between dark matter and dark energy.

pathways.title: NASA | Exploring Jupiter's Ocean World with Europa Clipper
pathways.description: Embark on a fun and educational journey to explore Europa, Jupiter’s icy moon, with NASA’s Europa Clipper mission. Through videos, readings, hands-on activities, this pathway guides learners in discovering the mission’s goals, the search for life, and exciting space exploration careers.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to understand the mission goals of NASA's Europa Clipper and why Europa is a key focus in the search for extraterrestrial life.
Identify the main scientific instruments on the Europa Clipper spacecraft and their roles in exploring Europa.
Explore the evidence for a hidden ocean on Europa and its potential to support life.
Engage in hands-on activities to learn how scientists detect water and other life-supporting elements on distant worlds.
Inspire interest in space exploration careers by hearing from real-world NASA scientists and career paths.

pathways.title: Heredity and Inheritance
pathways.description: This pathway explores how genetic information is passed from parents to offspring and shows how to use a Punnett square to predict traits in the next generation.
learning_objectives: Explain how eukaryotic organisms have 2 copies of each gene and how these genes are passed on to offspring.
Demonstrate the difference between dominant and recessive traits.
Use a Punnett square to predict the expected ratio of offspring based on parental genotypes.

pathways.title: What Happens When Our Blood Production System Fails?
pathways.description: A healthy adult produces billions of new blood cells every single day. Yet for some people, the process that generates these cells does not function properly, potentially resulting in blood cancer. At the Albert Einstein College of Medicine in New York, USA, Dr Kristina Ames has been investigating why blood production problems occur and has discovered a potential treatment that could restore the function of blood production cells.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway learners will be able to recall the definition of cell biology and identify cells as the functional units of life.
Learners will comprehend the importance of studying cells in the broader context of the whole organism.
Learners will understand the impact of disrupted PI3K signaling on blood cell production.
Learners will comprehend the consequences of disrupted haematopoiesis, leading to conditions like myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) and acute myeloid leukaemia (AML).

pathways.title: Plant Nomenclature and Classification
pathways.description: Collecting and preserving plant specimens is crucial for understanding plant classification and diversity. This pathway provides botanists with foundational knowledge in herbarium methods, as well as taxonomy, systematics, and phylogenetic analysis.
learning_objectives: Follow best practices for collecting, documenting, and preserving plant specimens.
Understand how to analyze a phylogenetic tree to determine evolutionary relationships among plant species.
Apply the principles of plant taxonomy and systematics in the identification and classification of plant specimens.
Utilize herbarium records to support plant identification and classification.
Critically evaluate multiple systems of plant classification, including contributions from Indigenous Knowledge, to inform ethical and inclusive scientific practice.

pathways.title: Crafting Inclusive Activities and Resources
pathways.description: In inclusive classrooms, instructors take the time to plan activities and create resources that will help all students learn. Students come to the classroom with a wide range of experiences, backgrounds, knowledge, and skills, all of which influence how they interact with the course material and each other. With this diversity in mind, inclusive instructors devise a variety of activities so that there are inviting and meaningful learning opportunities for everyone. Then, within each activity, instructors can create scaffolds that help all students accomplish the task while also supporting their different needs and interests. In this pathway, we’ll explore a variety of activities and scaffolds that can help diverse groups of students learn.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will identify and describe the purpose of graphic organizers and how they can be used to record and visualize learning.
Summarize how presenting material in various ways can support an inclusive classroom environment.
Apply inclusive teaching strategies by presenting material through anecdotes and diverse instructional methods to accommodate different learning styles.
Analyze the effectiveness of lecture handouts in supporting students with different levels of expertise and learning needs.
Assess the impact of project-based learning on student engagement and comprehension of course concepts.
Design and implement lesson plans that incorporate graphic organizers, project-based learning, and varied presentation methods to cater to diverse learning needs.

pathways.title: Can We Build Perpetual Motion Machines?
pathways.description: This pathway is designed to establish a strong knowledge foundation for learners, enabling a deep understanding of the intricate relationships between forces, work, energy, and movement in the realm of physics. Through a combination of text-based learning, interactive simulations, and video demonstrations, students will embark on a journey to unravel the fundamental principles that govern the dynamics of objects in motion.
learning_objectives: Understand the concept of energy and its various forms, including kinetic, potential, and thermal energy.
Explore the relationship between work and energy, including the calculation of work done by forces and the transfer of energy through work.
Investigate the principle of conservation of energy and apply it to analyze physical systems and phenomena.
Identify and analyze conservative forces in different contexts, including gravitational and elastic forces, and understand their role in conserving mechanical energy.
Examine the role of frictional forces in dissipating energy and their effects on mechanical systems, including the calculation of work done against friction.
Apply the concepts of energy, work, conservative and frictional forces to solve practical problems and analyze real-world situations, fostering critical thinking and problem-solving skills.

pathways.title: NASA | Future Space Exploration with James Webb
pathways.description: Glimpse the findings the James Webb Space Telescope will unveil with its powerful infrared vision; exploring galaxies, star formation, exoplanets, and the universe's ancient history.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will grasp cosmic history, from early stars to the universe's origins, advancing their understanding of the cosmos.
Learn how Webb's infrared sensitivity helps compare ancient and modern galaxies, enhancing comprehension of galaxy evolution over billions of years.
Grasp Webb's infrared abilities to observe stars and planetary systems in dusty regions, unveiling insights into star and planetary formation, addressing key astronomy questions.
Learn how Webb's infrared capabilities study exoplanet atmospheres and search for life's building blocks, advancing knowledge of planet formation and habitability.

Create your own virtual Universe: experiment with factors like dark matter and star mass to create and compare different virtual universes, aiming to understand the conditions leading to a Universe similar to our own.

pathways.title: Can We Unlock the Secrets Hidden Deep Within the Nucleus of an Atom?
pathways.description: Everything is made of atoms, which are comprised of electrons orbiting a nucleus. Understanding the nucleus and its substructure is at the heart of nuclear physics. At Lebanon Valley College in the US, Dr Daniel Pitonyak is hoping to uncover the mysteries held deep within the nucleus of an atom.
learning_objectives: After competing this pathway, you will understand the fundamental structure of atoms.
You will understand how quarks and gluons contribute to the internal structure of hadrons.

pathways.title: How Is Mathematics Applied to Real World Problems?
pathways.description: Mathematics is essential to our way of life. It is used all around us, from weather forecasting, engineering and finance to modelling how diseases spread. Professor Anotida Madzvamuse, an applied mathematician at the University of Sussex, in the UK, and the University of British Columbia, in Canada, led the UK-Africa Postgraduate Advanced Study Institute in Mathematical Sciences.
learning_objectives: Explore how math can be applied in areas like biology, social sciences, computer animation, and finance.
Understand the power of mathematical modeling.
Learn about the value of interdisciplinary collaboration.

pathways.title: Touchdown on the Moon: The Orion Spacecraft
pathways.description: Orion is an integral part of NASA's Artemis program, which aims to conduct more complex missions to explore the lunar surface and lay the groundwork for sending astronauts to Mars. This pathway compares the Apollo and Orion spacecraft and discusses various lunar landing sites for suitability as a potential human base camp.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how learnings from the Apollo missions have influenced the design of the Artemis missions.
You will be able to list landing sites on the lunar surface.
You will describe the difference between the Orion and Apollo spacecraft systems.
You will build and land your own spacecraft on the Moon.

pathways.title: Corona Crisis, Firms and Global Value Chains
pathways.description:
The schockwaves of the Corona crisis on global value chains
This MOOC proposes a stylized analysis of the three shockwaves of the COVID-19 epidemics on global value chains.
The first shockwave, in January and February 2020, triggered immediate global recessionary forces as the disruption of supply chains resulting for the lockdown in some Chinese regions shocked the activity of some manufacturing facilities across the globe.
The second wave followed the geographical amplification of the restrictions on human circulation, which resulted in asymmetric reductions of supply and demand, leading to a combination of bottlenecks and gluts with severe medium term implications for the recovery.
The third wave results from the interplay of two diverging forces. On the one hand, the acknowledgment of strategic vulnerabilities resulting from just-in-time global sourcing will foster a wave of reshoring of manufacturing activities through a combination of revisited business strategies and politically-driven institutional change. This phenomenon will be all the more important that the balance between the gains and losses from offshore manufacturing were already tilting towards the second as a result of the rise of labor costs in manufacturing hubs of the global south and the prospects of further automation in some area. On the other hand, in the absence of significant regulatory change, the learning effects resulting from the shift en masse to teleworking during the corona lockdown will dramatically accelerate global sourcing in services. Overall, the balance of the corona crisis on globalization dynamics remains uncertain and will depend of the relative strengths and shapes of two forces: securing the socioeconomic existence through a territorial re-embeddness and geographical de-specialization versus the acceleration of a shift of economic activity to a partially de-territorialized digital landscape.
Rethinking the firm
During the last decades we have witnessed an increasing transformation in the productive activities carried out by transnational firms, going from almost fully-integrated, self-sufficient powerhouses to an increasing specialization centered on “core activities” which manifested in the appearance and extension of the so-called global value chains. This process has benefited leading firms at the expense of defunded national states and squeezed labor. It has also decoupled the availability of funds by these firms from the transformation of those funds into productive resources. On the contrary, these monies have been used in multiple, non-productive ways, fostering inequality and social unrest. Behind all the troubles and suffering brought about by the covid-19 pandemic, it has also shown at least two broad lessons directly related to those trends: a) have a good dose of skepticism regarding firms willingness to change and b) national states still have a good margin of maneuver.

pathways.title: How Does the Inner Ear Develop Into a Sensitive Hearing and Balance Organ?
pathways.description: Our inner ears contain specialised hair cells that give us the ability to hear a range of sounds and to orientate ourselves in three dimensions. At Baylor College of Medicine in the US, Professor Andy Groves is leading a team of biologists to uncover how these hair cells develop as an embryo grows and how they have evolved in different organisms.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define and describe the function of hair cells in the inner ear and their role in hearing and balance.
Explain the process of mechanotransduction by which hair cells convert mechanical vibrations into electrical signals.
Analyze and interpret experimental data to identify how specific gene mutations can affect hair cell development and function.

pathways.title: Cyber Security for the AI Age
pathways.description: As the digital world grows in power and complexity, so do the threats it faces. There is a constant arms race between cyber security and cyber criminals, both rushing to outcompete the other in sophistication. This means that the field of cyber security is rapidly growing and hungry for new talent that can understand the evolving threat landscape and respond accordingly by embracing new technologies, says Dr Burcu Bulgurcu at Toronto Metropolitan University in Canada.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will be able to understand and explain the basic concepts and terminologies related to cybersecurity.
Learn about  the role of human behavior in cybersecurity, identifying how human errors contribute to security breaches and the importance of education and awareness in preventing such incidents.
Discover the various career options in cybersecurity, recognizing the skills and qualifications needed for different roles in this field.

pathways.title: The History of Economic Crisis As Academic Fiction and Political Drama (4 Parts)
pathways.description: On March 26th, 2020, the governor of the US Federal Reserve System, Jerome Powell, made an extraordinary declaration: ‘We’re not going to run out of ammunition’. In doing so, he sought to reassure Americans that the country’s central bank stood ready to take any action necessary to stem the mounting economic problems associated with the COVID-19 pandemic. He was not exaggerating: in a period of less than 3 months from late February 2020, the Fed injected nearly $3 trillion dollars into the US economy, more than it had injected in the five years following the global financial crisis of 2008-2009. Such bold action, taken with only a modicum of democratic oversight, seems to define a new normal not only for the Fed but for many of the world’s richest central banks. For those with a taste for dark humour, there is a profound irony to be enjoyed from excavating the origins of this type of radical monetarist interventionism. In this session, we will trace the intellectual justification for quantitative easing by central banks as a response to economic crisis to the arguments and evidence that Milton Friedman and Anna Schwartz mobilised in their 1963 book, A Monetary History of the United States. Second, we will review how their account gained extraordinary influence, fostering a remarkable consensus among prominent economists and economic historians about the lessons of the Great Depression. Third, we will consider how these lessons were substantially modified and adapted to new circumstances, initially during the financial crisis of 2008-2009 and subsequently in the context of the ongoing health crisis. In this way, we will think about the implications of our understanding of historical crises for dealing with crises in the present.

pathways.title: Plant Pathogens
pathways.description: Plant pathogens have devastating effects on ecosystems, global food security, and agricultural livelihoods. Examine pathogen-host interactions, plant immunity, and diagnostic methods, and discover how scientists use bioinformatics, experimental design, and field data to protect plant species.
learning_objectives: Identify deadly plant pathogens that are a major threat to global agriculture.
Explain how fungal, bacterial, and protist pathogens cause disease in plants.
Explain what plants can do to defend themselves against pathogens.
Understand how PHI-base and PhytoPath are used to investigate pathogen gene functions and support strategies for disease prevention.
Explain the research being conducted on citrus greening, cranberry fruit rot, and other significant plant health threats.
Evaluate how environmental stressors and shifting pathogen habitats impact plant health.
Examine how multidisciplinary teams analyze big data, develop predictive models, and inform quarantine or breeding programs.

pathways.title: Forms of Energy
pathways.description: All energy in living systems originates from the Sun, powering photosynthesis to create glucose for cellular respiration. Understand how energy is transformed, conserved, and utilized in biological and physical systems, including photosynthesis, respiration, and energy transfer between potential, kinetic, and chemical forms.
learning_objectives: Define and distinguish between the different forms and types of energy.
Explain how energy is transformed and conserved in living and non-living systems, using examples such as photosynthesis, respiration, and hydropower.
Describe the role of the Sun as the primary source of energy on Earth and trace how solar energy flows through biological systems.
Differentiate between renewable and nonrenewable energy sources and evaluate their environmental and economic impacts.
Use examples to illustrate the principle of conservation of energy in real-world systems, including mechanical, electrical, and biological processes.
Analyze energy changes during chemical and physical processes, such as phase changes, exergonic and endergonic reactions, and bond formation or breaking.

pathways.title: What Is the Atom
pathways.description: Atoms are the basic units of matter and define chemical elements. This pathway traces their discovery from Democritus to modern quantum models, exploring protons, neutrons, electrons, isotopes, ions, and nuclear physics. Learn how atomic structure and interactions determine the properties of substances, building core skills in chemistry and physics.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the discovery of the atom from early Greek ideas to modern evidence, including contributions by Dalton, Rutherford, Bohr, and Einstein.
Explain the structure of the atom, identifying protons, neutrons, electrons, the nucleus, and electron arrangements.
Differentiate between atomic properties, including atomic number, mass number, isotopes, and ions.
Interpret atomic models, from the plum pudding model to the quantum mechanical model, and explain their role in advancing scientific understanding.
Explore the nucleus and subatomic particles, analyzing the role of nuclear physics in explaining matter’s fundamental structure.

pathways.title: Eat a Burger - Doctor’s Orders!
pathways.description:
Explore how food is made up of macromolecules with this pathway and accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 4 days of lessons (210 minutes) and focuses on how food is made up of macromolecules like carbohydrates, fats, and proteins, which are composed of atoms from the periodic table.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 30 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 60 minutes, items 2-3 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 60 minutes, item 4 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 60 minutes, item 5 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, students will explain the ways in which naturally occurring atoms combine to create simple molecules and macromolecules.
Students will classify the different types of macromolecules based on atomic structure.
Students will identify the different types of carbohydrates and fats.

pathways.title: Transforming Energy From One Form to Another
pathways.description: Discover how energy cannot be created or destroyed, but it can change from one form to another.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define energy and identify its various forms.
Explain the uses of energy in daily life.
Describe how energy is converted from one form to another.

pathways.title: How Is Yeast Helping Us to Understand the Mysteries of Cell Biology?
pathways.description: In Dr Fabrice Caudron’s lab at Queen Mary University of London, yeast is helping scientists to understand the inner workings of cells. By studying the “dating behaviour” of these microscopic creatures, Fabrice and his team made a discovery which could eventually help us combat diseases such as Parkinson’s or Alzheimer’s.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you will better awareness of the role of cell and molecular biologists in developing new treatments and medicines for diseases and disorders caused by cellular or molecular problems.
Learn how scientist are studying yeast cells to learn more about prions, and other proteins found in other organisms.

Understanding the basic building block of life, the cell, and its importance in the biology of all creatures with an appreciation of the complexity of cell biology and the need to understand how molecules are organized within cells to fulfill their functions.
Gain insight into the path to becoming a cellular and molecular biologist and what the career entails.

pathways.title: How Can We Make Medicines With Fewer Nasty Side Effects?
pathways.description: Professor Ijeoma Uchegbu is a designer. She designs medicines to have fewer side effects by researching and developing ways to deliver drugs to different areas of the body. Watch the video to learn about pharmaceutical nanoscience and to hear her inspiring story with a key message — if you are working hard and it’s getting to be too much, ask for help.

pathways.title: A Gravity Slingshot to Mars
pathways.description:
NASA's Orion spacecraft and Space Launch System use gravity slingshot maneuvers for deep space travel. This lesson spans 255–315 minutes to explore gravitational and magnetic forces through videos, simulations, text, a student guide, and engineering design, applying these concepts to optimize spacecraft acceleration and fuel efficiency.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 60-90 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 45-60 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 45-60 minutes, items 9-10 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 60 minutes, items 9 and 11 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand NASA’s Orion spacecraft and the Space Launch System rocket designed for future deep space explorations beyond the moon, which will require the employment of a gravity slingshot maneuver to accelerate the craft to greater speeds while conserving fuel.
Explore how the gravitational force between a pair of objects increases in strength as the objects get closer to each other.

Apply the similarities of magnetic and gravitational forces between objects to an engineering design meant to simulate the gravity slingshot maneuver by modulating the trajectory of a steel ball deployed from a ramp toward a magnet.

pathways.title: Cell and Its Environment
pathways.description: Diffusion is the movement of molecules from high to low concentration, driven by factors like temperature, molecular size, and viscosity. Understanding diffusion and osmosis is crucial in biology, from cellular processes to ecosystems. Learn how these principles impact physiological systems, including the urinary system and soil salinization.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define osmosis and diffusion.
State the importance of osmosis and diffusion in both plants and animals.
Give three examples of diffusion and osmosis in both plants and animals.

pathways.title: Why Do Some People Become Psychopaths?
pathways.description: Professor Essi Viding’s job is to research why psychopathology develops and what we can do to prevent it. In the videos below, Professor Viding shares how she became a developmental psychopathologist and introduces several of the psychological tests she uses in her research.

pathways.title: Introducing Plant Propagation Techniques
pathways.description: Plant propagation is a foundational skill for every botanist, whether working in the field, in a greenhouse, or a research lab. Explore how to propagate plants using both sexual and asexual methods. Through real-world examples, gain a working knowledge of key techniques such as grafting, cuttings, and seed propagation.
learning_objectives: Understand the significance of grafting and transplanting for perennial plant species, including their role in agricultural sustainability and accelerated fruit production.
Describe the biological processes involved in both sexual and asexual plant reproduction, including pollination, fertilization, and vegetative propagation.
Identify and apply propagation methods such as budding, grafting, cuttings, and seed-based techniques in both natural and controlled environments.
Explain the principles and applications of artificial selection and cultivar cloning in the development of desirable plant traits.
Discuss the challenges and innovations in plant propagation for space agriculture, and how environmental conditions influence propagation strategies.
Evaluate the advantages and limitations of sexual and asexual propagation, especially in the context of genetic diversity, plant health, and horticultural goals.

pathways.title: Bioinspired Design: Looking to Biology for Better Solutions
pathways.description:
Students will identify a problem with an existing product and investigate biological functions to inspire a product redesign. Through independent research using AI bots and scholarly sources, students will gain a new perspective on STEM, biology, and the human interface between.
Driving Questions:
• How can we use organismal adaptations to design better engineering solutions? 
• What products in our daily lives could be more sustainable, functional, or aesthetically pleasing?
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of the learning pathway, students will be able to define the concept of bioinspired design and explain its importance in engineering and product development.
Identify key biological adaptations that have inspired engineering solutions.
Recall the steps of the bioinspired design process.
Explain the relationship between an organism’s traits and their potential application in design/engineering.
Use mind-mapping techniques to brainstorm and visually organize design challenges.
Utilize AI chatbots and scholarly sources to research biological models relevant to engineering problems.
Compare and contrast multiple biological models, evaluating their pros, cons, and tradeoffs.
Justify the selection of a biological model by evaluating its effectiveness in solving a given design problem.
Develop a clear and well-supported problem statement for a product redesign.

pathways.title: Can We Decipher the Mechanisms That Allow Plant Cells to Communicate?
pathways.description: Humans depend on plants for their survival. Plants provide the oxygen we breathe and the food we eat, but much of their functioning remains a mystery to us. At Professor Dave Jackson’s lab at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory (CSHL) in New York, USA, scientists are studying the mechanisms that co-ordinate plant development. They have recently identified an important protein that facilitates the communication underlying this process.
learning_objectives: After completing this pathway, you will understand the importance of cell-cell communication in plants during development, growth, and response to environmental changes.

Explore key molecules like plasmodesmata, chaperone molecules, mRNA, and RNA binding proteins and their roles in facilitating intercellular communication, coordinating plant responses to environmental changes, and protecting against pathogens.

pathways.title: What Determines the Speed of a Wave?
pathways.description:
Wave speed depends on frequency, wavelength, and the medium. This 270–360 minute lesson explores wave properties through videos, interactive activities, simulations, text and an accompanying student guide. As an extension activity, you wil analyze echolocation as a real-world application of wave physics.




Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1: 30-45 minutes, items 1-3 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3: 90-120 minutes, items 4-7 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4-5: 90-120 minutes, item 8 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 6: 30-45 minutes, item 9 of the pathway, and activities in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 7, 30 minutes, item 10 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand basic concepts and definitions involved with waves, including the different types of waves and their components, and figure out the physics equation describing the speed of a wave through experimentation on a simulation.

Write a claim-evidence-reasoning argument to describe the mathematical relationship between the velocity, frequency, and wavelength of a wave and how the material a wave travels through also affects its velocity.
Examine the physics involved with echolocation as a means of studying a biological example of an organism that integrates auditory information from sound waves with its visual system.

pathways.title: Hydroelectric Power: The Next Generation
pathways.description:
Explore the relationship between electricity and magnetism in this pathway (210-300 minutes) through videos, texts, simulations, a case study, and a student guide. Investigate how changing magnetic fields generate electrical currents and apply this knowledge to hydroelectric power design.








Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 60 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 60-90 minutes, items 3-10 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4-5, 90-120 minutes, item 10 of the pathway and student guide.
• EXPLAIN: Day 6-7, 90-120 minutes, items 11-12 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 8, 45-60 minutes, item 13 of the pathway.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will plan and conduct an investigation to provide evidence that an electric current can produce a magnetic field and that a changing magnetic field can produce an electric current by using an online simulation.

Revise an original design plan for the relationship between electricity and magnetism based on the types, amount, and accuracy of required data to produce reliable measurements as well as considering limitations on the precision of the data (e.g., number of trials, cost, risk, and time).
Apply knowledge of generating electrical currents from a changing magnetic field to design a device that generates hydroelectric power.

pathways.title: An Impulse for Safe Landings
pathways.description:
Explore momentum and impulse in this pathway (230-275 minutes), using simulations and experiments to understand the relationship between impulse, mass, velocity, and impact force. Learn how these concepts apply to injury reduction during collisions. Design a protective device for an egg, applying the principles of impact force and impact time. A Guided Experiment Worksheet accompanies this pathway to assist with the experiments and design challenge.










Pathway breakdown:








• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes in class, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 45-60 minutes in class, items 2-5 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3-4, 70-90 minutes in class, item 6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 40-50 minutes in class, items 7-9 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 30 minutes in class, items 10-11 of the pathway
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand momentum and impulse (change in momentum) and the mathematical relationship between impulse, mass, and change in velocity, as well as between impulse, impact force, and impact time during a collision.
Relate how impact force and impact time during a collision are important factors to consider when designing devices that are meant to reduce the likelihood of injury during a crash or fall.

Design a device that is meant to protect an egg from breaking during a floor collision by reducing the impact force during the moment of collision, and applying these design concepts to engineer ways that reduce the likelihood of injury during a fall or collision.

pathways.title: Disrupting Traditional Classroom Hierarchies
pathways.description: Classrooms have traditionally been built on a number of hierarchies: Instructors hold more knowledge and power than students, the right answers count for more than the wrong ones,  and students with more experience in and knowledge of the subject are “smarter” than their peers with less experience and subject knowledge. In this pathway, we’ll learn how to challenge these traditional hierarchies to create classrooms that center students as knowledge holders and creators, turn the “wrong” answer into a learning opportunity, and highlight the knowledge and strengths that all students bring to the classroom.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will grasp the core concepts of inclusive education and appreciate its role in nurturing a welcoming classroom culture.
Learn to set clear expectations and model appropriate behaviors to ensure a cooperative and respectful classroom.
Develop techniques to identify and honor the individual strengths of every student, promoting self-esteem and engagement.
Acquire methods to encourage a variety of student interactions, supporting a dynamic and collaborative learning experience.
Gain a skill in cultivating trust and togetherness among students by regularly coordinating and leading structured team-based exercises.

pathways.title: How Does Climate Change Affect Water Quality?
pathways.description: Clean water is our most important natural resource. We need a reliable supply of drinking water to live. We also need water for industry, agriculture and even recreation. However, many of these uses put an incredible amount of pressure on water resources – a problem likely to get worse due to climate change. Professor Li Li, from Pennsylvania State University in the US, develops computer models to understand and predict how water quality is influenced by climate change and man-made changes to the environment
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the significance of water quality as a crucial natural resource and its importance for various aspects of human life, such as drinking, industry, agriculture, and recreation.
Explore the concepts of biogeochemistry, hydrology, and ecology and their relevance in understanding water quality and the impact of climate change on freshwater systems.
Identify the skills and attributes required for a successful career in environmental engineering, including problem-solving, collaboration, and creative thinking.

pathways.title: How to Design a Biology and Math App for Students, by Students
pathways.description: Maths may not always be a favourite area for biologists, but it is crucial to understanding science. After seeing biology students struggling to apply maths to their studies, Dr Susan Safford, at Lincoln University in the US, decided to develop an app to tackle the problem. To do this, she assembled an interdisciplinary team of professors, including Dr Tiffanie Smith, and students from across biology, maths and computer science.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway students will be able to identify and calculate the surface area and volume of a cuboid and sphere, as well as understand the relationship between surface area, volume, and diffusion in biological organisms.
Understand the importance of math in biology and how it is applied to solve biological problems.
Understand the benefits of a collaborative approach to solve problems and challenges experienced.

pathways.title: What Landscapes Are Hidden Deep Within the Earth?
pathways.description: Beneath the Earth’s crust lies the mysterious mantle, and beneath that, the core. Although these layers form 99% of Earth’s volume, studying them is no easy task. Dr Paula Koelemeijer, a seismologist at the University of Oxford, UK, uses seismic waves to ‘X-ray’ our planet, enabling her to build a picture of the structures that exist deep in the mantle and to interpret these in terms of the processes that have shaped our planet.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the structure of the Earth's interior, including the core, mantle, and crust.
Learn about the techniques used in seismology to study the Earth's interior.
Explore the concept of convection and its role in the movement of material in the Earth's mantle and core.
Discover how seismic waves can be used to 'X-ray' the Earth's interior and build 3D models of its structures.

pathways.title: Improving Attention and Focus in Classroom Environments
pathways.description: Maintaining students’ concentration has often been a challenge, but for many teachers, this is now especially true thanks to the rise of technology and the consequences of disrupted schooling leftover from the COVID-19 pandemic. At the University of California Los Angeles (UCLA) in the US, Dr Agatha Lenartowicz and Dr Jennie Grammer are combining the fields of neuroscience and education to monitor and improve student focus in classrooms.
learning_objectives: Explain how brain activity, including alpha oscillations measured by EEG, is related to attention control and sensory processing in the visual cortex.
Analyze how mobile EEG technology is used to measure attention in classroom settings and compare its advantages and limitations to traditional observation methods.
Evaluate why findings about attention in laboratory settings may differ from those observed in real-world classroom environments.
Evaluate research evidence about active learning, independent work, and behavioral indicators (such as fidgeting) to determine what supports sustained attention.
Design or propose evidence-based strategies to improve attention and persistence in classroom environments, including considerations for students with ADHD.

pathways.title: Future Foods
pathways.description:
Food and climate are deeply connected. Students will dig into data to discover how our food choices impact greenhouse gas emissions. They will propose solutions to reduce their carbon “foodprint,” both as current consumers and as future food engineers. Students will eat this up in class and savor it for years to come!
Driving Question(s):
• What is a carbon footprint?
• What stages of production (from farm to table) have the biggest impact on the carbon footprint of foods?
• What are possible solutions to minimize the impact of our food supply on climate change?
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Understand that the sustainability of our food system has environmental, economic, and social aspects.
Evaluate the carbon footprints of commonly consumed foods.
Draw evidence-based conclusions about what makes certain foods more sustainable than others.

pathways.title: Exploring the Nature of Light: Understanding Its Properties and Behaviors
pathways.description: This pathway explores the fascinating world of light. Learn about its dual nature as both a wave and a particle, and study key behaviors such as reflection, refraction, and diffraction. Discover how light interacts with matter and shapes our understanding of the physical universe.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to understand the dual nature of light
Describe the electromagnetic spectrum.
Explore how light interacts with matter.
Identify the relationship between wavelength and frequency.

pathways.title: NASA | Droplet Combustion
pathways.description: Embark on a fiery journey with NASA's groundbreaking experiments in microgravity, conducted aboard zero-gravity planes and the International Space Station. Explore the FLEX and CLD Flame studies, which investigate the unique behaviors of flames in the absence of gravity. These experiments aim to enhance our understanding of combustion, potentially improving energy efficiency and safety in both space and Earth environments.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will identify the key experiments, such as FLEX and CLD Flame, that focus on droplet combustion in microgravity environments.
Explain the scientific principles underlying the behavior of flames and droplet combustion in the absence of gravity.
Analyze the experimental data from the FLEX and CLD Flame studies to deduce how changes in environmental conditions affect flame behavior in microgravity.
Evaluate the implications of these combustion experiments for advancements in energy efficiency and safety protocols in both space and terrestrial settings.

pathways.title: Nutrition and Food Preservation
pathways.description: Discover how rapid freezing preserves food quality, the role of metabolism in energy use, and how microbial infections and foodborne illnesses impact health. Explore key principles of food preservation, nutrition, and disease prevention to make informed dietary choices and promote food safety.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the role of metabolism in nutrition and energy balance.
Identify essential nutrients, their functions, and their impact on health.
Explain the principles of a balanced diet and its role in disease prevention.
Analyze the effects of microbial infections and foodborne illnesses on human health.
Evaluate food preservation methods, including freezing, and their impact on food safety and nutrition.

pathways.title: How Do Plants Protect Themselves From Diseases?
pathways.description: It is estimated that plant diseases cost the global economy roughly $220 billion each year. Understanding how plants fight pathogens is, therefore, crucial to protecting crops and combatting food insecurity. At Mississippi State University in the US, Dr Sorina Popescu is studying the mechanisms that underlie plants’ immune systems. Her research may help to identify new ways of protecting crop species from disease.
learning_objectives: After completing this pathway, you will gain an understanding of the impact of climate change on global food security.
You will understand the role of plant immune responses in fighting pathogens and adapting to a changing environment.

pathways.title: Fire Trackers: How Can We Use Modelling Techniques to Predict Where Wildfires Will Occur?
pathways.description: Climate change is making many parts of the world hotter and drier. As a result, wildfires are becoming increasingly prevalent and pose a serious threat to human life, local communities and natural ecosystems. Researchers from the FLARE Wildfire Research Group at The University of Melbourne in Australia are developing models that can help predict where wildfires are most likely to occur and how fires behave under different weather conditions.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will to understand the impact of climate change on the frequency and severity of bushfires, and how this affects human life, local communities, and natural ecosystems.

Explore how fire management strategies such as prescribed burns, fuel management, and fire suppression techniques can be used to reduce the risk of damage to human values while maintaining or improving environmental values in the landscape.
Investigate bushfire behavior and develop predictive models to determine the most likely areas of wildfire occurrence.

pathways.title: Can We Control the Electrical Activity in Our Brains?
pathways.description: Neuroscientists aim to understand how our brains work and what happens when they are not functioning properly. At the University of Oxford in the UK, Dr Adam Packer and his team are exploring how neurons transmit electrical signals and discovering how they cause us to feel sensations, by manipulating the neural activity in mice. This research has the potential to deepen our understanding of the human brain, paving the way for treatments of conditions such as epilepsy and schizophrenia.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you should understand the key concepts and terms involved in the research Adam and his team are conducting
Learn about the applications of the study of neurons and how this benefits society
Learn about neuroscience as a potential career path

pathways.title: Why Do Some Children Fail to Communicate?
pathways.description: Watch Professor Dame Frith tell the story of how she used experimental psychology to help her understand autistic children and develop the mind blindness theory.

pathways.title: Circuits, Bioelectricity, and DC Instruments
pathways.description: This pathway takes the topic of electric circuits a step beyond simple circuits by addressing both changes that result from interactions between systems and constraints on such changes due to laws of conservation. When the circuit is purely resistive, everything in this pathway applies to both DC and AC. However, matters become more complex when capacitance is involved. The pathway does consider what happens when capacitors are connected to DC voltage sources. In addition, a number of important DC instruments, such as meters that measure voltage and current, are covered in this pathway. Information and examples presented in the pathway examine cause-effect relationships inherent in interactions involving electrical systems.

pathways.title: En-ROADS Simulator: Land Use, Industry Emissions, Carbon Removal, and Climate Impacts
pathways.description: Explore how the En-ROADS simulator models land use, industry emissions, and carbon removal to understand their impacts on climate change, supporting effective climate action and systems thinking skills development.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to analyze the impacts of different emissions and carbon sequestration strategies on global warming.
Evaluate policy and action scenarios for emissions reduction and climate resilience.
Apply systems thinking to understand the interconnectedness of land, industry, and climate impacts.
Use simulation results to inform effective climate action planning.

pathways.title: Descriptive Statistics: Statistical Measurements and Probability Distributions
pathways.description: Descriptive statistics and probability theory serve as foundational tools in data science, empowering professionals to interpret data, assess uncertainty, and make informed, evidence-based decisions. Delve into key statistical concepts such as measures of central tendency, variability, and position, along with core probability principles and widely used distributions including binomial, Poisson, and normal. Develop practical skills in Python to organize, analyze, and visualize data, and apply statistical reasoning to solve real-world problems across fields like business, healthcare, and technology. This pathway is Chapter 3 of the Principles of Data Science textbook.
learning_objectives: Summarize and interpret data using measures of center, variation, and position.
Apply Python to compute and visualize descriptive statistics for real-world datasets.
Explain and use probability concepts (theoretical, empirical, conditional, Bayes’ Theorem) to assess uncertainty.
Distinguish between discrete and continuous distributions, and apply binomial, Poisson, exponential, and normal models to solve problems.
Evaluate uncertainty in data science using statistical measures and probability models to inform decisions.

pathways.title: Hydrogen and Energy Transformations in Chemistry
pathways.description: Electrolysis splits water into hydrogen and oxygen, showing energy changes in chemical bonds. Explore hydrogen’s occurrence, laboratory preparation, testing, properties, and industrial manufacture. Study electrolysis, fuel cells, biomass, and nuclear energy, linking bonding, redox reactions, and energy transformations to sustainability and real-world applications.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the occurrence, laboratory preparation, and tests for hydrogen gas.
Explain the physical and chemical properties of hydrogen and relate them to its bonding and energy transformations.
Analyze the principles of electrolysis and perform simple stoichiometric calculations for electrolytic processes.
Evaluate the industrial manufacture of hydrogen and its applications in fuel cells, energy storage, and sustainable technologies.
Assess the role of hydrogen and alternative energy sources such as biomass and nuclear energy in addressing global and Tanzanian energy challenges.

pathways.title: Analog to Digital
pathways.description:
Investigate how we transmit and store analog and digital signals, and discover their benefits with this pathway and accompanying student guide. The recommended learning time spans 6 days of lessons (215 minutes).




Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 30 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 50 minutes, found in the student guide.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3-4, 60 minutes, item 2 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 45 minutes, item 3 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 30 minutes, items 4-6 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe how analog data can be digitized.
Construct an argument about the benefits of analog and digital information.
Evaluate the advantages of using digital transmission and storage of information.

pathways.title: NASA | Solid Fuels
pathways.description: Discover how NASA is pushing the boundaries of space exploration by tackling one of its most critical challenges—fire safety in space. This pathway delves into NASA's groundbreaking experiments aboard the ISS, studying how solid fuels behave in microgravity and advancing crucial fire prevention research.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will identify and describe ISS experiments like SoFIE, BASS, and BASS-M, explaining their role in studying solid fuel behavior in microgravity.
Compare combustion and fire suppression behaviors in microgravity versus normal gravity using data from NASA's SoFIE and BASS experiments.
Evaluate NASA's flammability standards in preventing space fire hazards and suggest improvements based on experimental insights.

pathways.title: Soil, Energy and Ecology - Patterns in Nature
pathways.description: Discover how over 1,500 gigatonnes of carbon stored in soil connects to energy flow, population dynamics, and ecological succession. Explore how these processes interact to shape ecosystems and reveal patterns in nature.
learning_objectives: Describe the formation, composition, and classification of soil, and explain its role in carbon storage and plant growth.
Explain the processes and types of weathering and their importance in soil formation and landscape change.
Analyze the flow of energy through ecosystems, including the roles of producers, consumers, decomposers, and trophic levels.
Interpret ecological relationships such as parasitism, predation, and succession, and explain how disturbances affect biodiversity.

pathways.title: The Atomic Nucleus: Greater than the Sum of Its Parts
pathways.description: Dr Jacek Dobaczewski, based at the University of York, is looking at ways to simplify a complex physics problem: how to better describe the atomic nucleus – and it’s a question he’s willing to spend a lifetime answering.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will delve into the study of atoms, focusing on the nucleus which houses protons and neutrons, and discover newly identified elements such as Oganesson-294.
See how physics uses creativity and teamwork to figure out how things work together in big systems.
Get to know different elements and how their atoms are put together, find out about new elements that scientists have found, and understand how studying nuclear physics can help in all kinds of jobs.

pathways.title: Using Multispectral Imaging to “See” Space Objects
pathways.description:
Explore the intricacies of wave physics with this student guide, designed to deepen your understanding of amplitude, wavelength, and frequency across the electromagnetic spectrum. With a recommended learning time of 390 minutes over 5 days, delve into the spectrum's diverse applications and health implications, and learn to harness multispectral imaging to inform and improve decision-making in various fields.








Pathway breakdown:








• ENGAGE: Day 1; 50 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3; 100 minutes in class, items 2-5 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4; 40 minutes in class, items 6-7 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5-7; 150 minutes in class, the student guide and item 8 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 8-9; 50 minutes in class, items 9-13 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify the amplitude, wavelength and frequency of a wave.
Compare and contrast wavelengths represented in the electromagnetic spectrum.
Describe the relative difference in frequency and danger to humans of wavelengths represented in the electromagnetic spectrum.
Use your knowledge of the electromagnetic spectrum to describe how wavelengths are used to create multispectral imagery and explain the benefits of this imagery.

pathways.title: How Does Climate Change Affect Aquaculture?
pathways.description: One significant effect of climate change is that the ocean is becoming increasingly acidic. This has implications for marine life, including farmed shellfish such as oysters and mussels. Dr Susan Fitzer at the University of Stirling is investigating what climate change means for shellfish farming, and how aquaculture could adapt to keep thriving.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to analyze the effects of ocean acidification on shellfish and explore the potential strategies, such as selective breeding, to mitigate the impact of climate change on bivalve farming.

You will be able to explain the concept of aquaculture, understand the impact of ocean acidification on shellfish farming, and recognize the potential of selective breeding as a strategy for sustainable shellfish aquaculture in the face of climate change.

Students will be able to explain the effects of ocean acidification on shellfish, understand the concept of selective breeding in aquaculture, and discuss the importance of addressing climate change in the aquaculture industry.

pathways.title: Great Potential: Why China and the UK Are Working Together in Gravitational-Wave Research
pathways.description: The Gravitational-wave Excellence through Alliance Training (GrEAT) Network is bringing scientists in the UK and China together, and their collaboration is opening a new window into the universe. By identifying industrial partners, providing training for researchers, and advancing detector technologies, GrEAT paves the way for groundbreaking discoveries in the field of gravitational waves. This pathway explores the significance of gravitational waves, the practical outcomes of research, career opportunities, and the importance of international collaboration in advancing scientific knowledge.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to understand the fundamental concepts of gravitational waves, including their origin and detection.
Explore the potential practical applications of gravitational-wave research in various industries, such as technology, healthcare, and energy.
Identify the key objectives of the Gravitational-wave Excellence through Alliance Training (GrEAT) Network and its role in fostering collaboration between the UK and China.
Comprehend the career opportunities available in the field of gravitational-wave research, including academic and industrial paths.

pathways.title: The Blood Circulatory System
pathways.description: The human circulatory system transports oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and waste through the body using the heart, blood, and blood vessels. This pathway explains heart structure, blood components, and circulation, building foundational knowledge in human biology and health science.
learning_objectives: Describe the role of the circulatory system in transporting gases, nutrients, medicines, and metabolic wastes in the human body.
Differentiate between open and closed circulatory systems in animals, using examples.
Explain the structure and function of the human heart, including chambers, valves, and blood flow pathways.
Describe the structure and function of blood vessels (arteries, veins, and capillaries) in relation to transport and exchange.
Identify the main components of blood and explain their roles in transport, immunity, and clotting.
Relate the circulatory system to human health, including how factors such as activity, age, and medication influence circulation.

pathways.title: Introduction to Plant Inheritance
pathways.description: Understanding how traits are inherited is fundamental to the work of a botanist. Explore the principles of plant inheritance, from Mendel’s classic experiments with pea plants to modern tools that allow scientists to manipulate plant genes.
learning_objectives: State the genotypic and phenotypic ratios of the F1 and F2 generations that result from a monohybrid cross and a dihybrid cross.
Describe the basic approach that Gregor Mendel used in his landmark studies of heredity and how they apply to plant traits.
Describe how the Central Dogma explains the relationship between genotype and phenotype.
Describe the flow of genetic information within a cell, and how such a flow connects a plant's genotype with its phenotype.
Explain how to extract DNA from strawberries.
Explain how scientists use molecular tools to study plant genes.

pathways.title: NASA | Earth Observation
pathways.description: Earth is the most studied planet in our universe, and for good reason: we live here! Researchers around the globe observe Earth from the ground, air, and space to better understand our changing planet. See what satellite imagery reveals about our planet with this pathway.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the importance of Earth observation and monitoring.
You will be able to identify the key indicators of Earth's health, such as carbon dioxide levels, ozone variations, and sea level.
You will understand how collected Earth data can be used to create visualizations and analyze conditions on the planet.

pathways.title: Practice Uncovering Arguments
pathways.description: This pathway provides you with eight step-by-step exercises that you can use to build key skills and confidence in your ability to uncover arguments that are expressed in everyday language and translate them into standard form.
learning_objectives: Recognize the essential content of simple ordinary language arguments by identifying their conclusions and their explicit and implicit premises, and by eliminating their non-essential rhetorical elements.
Use this analysis to translate ordinary language arguments into standard form.

pathways.title: Hack Your Brain
pathways.description: Ever wonder what goes on inside your head? Now’s your chance to delve deep into the workings of the brain and discover its secrets. Because understanding your mind isn’t just interesting—it’s the key to a happier, healthier life! Each teaching guide is packed with hands-on investigations and brain-teasing challenges. Immerse yourself in the captivating story of a young scientist and robot, paired with thought-provoking educational resources that will expand your understanding of neuroscience in an engaging and interactive way.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify how different parts of the brain control movement, memory, attention, and emotions.
Explain how brain chemicals like dopamine and endorphins affect mood, learning, and stress.
Compare short-term and long-term memory and explain how sleep helps you remember things.
Describe how practice and brain flexibility (neuroplasticity) help you learn new skills.
Explain how stress affects your body and brain, like making your heart beat faster or muscles tighten.
Try strategies like breathing exercises and mindfulness to manage stress.
Understand how your brain focuses on certain things and blocks out others.
Learn how to avoid distractions and improve focus during tasks.
Explain how the brain’s reward system encourages good habits and sometimes bad ones.
Explore how games and rewards can help you stay motivated to learn.
Try experiments to measure reaction time, test your memory, and learn how attention works.
Solve puzzles and design solutions to improve focus, memory, and stress management.

pathways.title: The Nervous System: Your Body’s High-Speed Communication Network
pathways.description: Your nervous system is like a supercharged communication network, sending messages across your body at lightning speed. It helps you think, move, feel, and even learn new skills! In this pathway, you’ll explore the central and peripheral nervous systems, learn their functions, and test your understanding of how neurons transmit signals.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define the nervous system and its role in the human body.
Identify the central and peripheral nervous systems and describe their main features and functions.
Explore how signals are transmitted through neurons.
Demonstrate knowledge of the nervous system through questions and interactive activities.
Reflect on the importance of studying the brain through a personal narrative.

pathways.title: Remembering Resistance: Nongenetic Memory in Bacteria
pathways.description: Typically, the skills and properties developed through adaptation during a lifetime may not be directly passed onto the next generation as encoded in genes – they are learnt. However, Dr Hanna Salman and Dr Andrew Mugler, from the University of Pittsburgh in the US, are finding evidence that bacteria can pass on ‘memories’ to their offspring, over several generations. Understanding more about this process is crucial for the fight against the rise of antibiotic resistance in bacteria.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define key terms such as antibiotic, bacteria, daughter cells, mitosis, mRNA, and non-genetic memory.
Explain the process of mitosis and how it leads to the formation of daughter cells.
Describe how non-genetic memory in bacteria can contribute to antibiotic resistance.
Illustrate the role of mRNA in protein synthesis and its potential impact on cellular memory.
Analyze the implications of non-genetic memory on the effectiveness of antibiotic treatments.

pathways.title: Space Gear: How Spacesuits Keep Astronauts Safe
pathways.description:
Explore the intricacies of designing a spacesuit to prevent space radiation from harming astronauts with this pathway and accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 6 days of lessons (210-230 minutes) and focuses on space radiation, spacesuit design, and the different systems found in the human body and how radiation can negatively affect them. Plus, explore a potential STEM career related to these topics.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 40-60 minutes, items 3-6 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 7 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, found in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 30 minutes, found in the student guide and item 8 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will learn how to collect and analyze data about space radiation and its effect on humans.
You will be able to demonstrate an understanding of the difficulties space travel has on humans.

You will be able to engage in virtual experiments and collaborative discussions to enhance critical thinking skills, explore the relationships between mental and physical health, explore related careers, and ask relevant questions.

pathways.title: What Causes Problems With the Lower Urinary Tract?
pathways.description: Our bodies are fascinatingly interconnected. Did you know that urinating a lot at night might be due to a heart problem? Or that a condition such as diabetes might affect urination? In the US, a team of doctors and researchers, including Professor Cindy L. Amundsen from Duke University School of Medicine, have come together to study lower urinary tract dysfunction, which affects almost 70% of people over 60 years old. The scientists’ aim is to understand what causes lower urinary tract symptoms and how treatment outcomes for these conditions can be improved.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will gain an understanding of the complexity of LUTD and challenges in diagnosis and treatment.
