You will identify the importance of interdisciplinary research collaborations in advancing medical research knowledge.
You will acquire an understanding of the diverse responsibilities of gynecologists', including clinical practice, research, and education.
You will gain insights into the pivotal role that technological advancements play in enhancing precision, flexibility, and early detection in gynaecological procedures.

pathways.title: Investigating the Root of Inflammatory Diseases
pathways.description: Inflammation is an essential part of our immune system, but when it goes wrong, it can lead to serious diseases such as lupus and psoriasis. Professor Hans Haecker and his team at the University of Utah in the US are investigating the molecular components of cells that drive our inflammatory response, and screening for molecules that may help treat chronic inflammatory diseases.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of Toll-like receptors (TLRs) in recognizing pathogens and initiating the immune response, including the first stage of inflammation.
Gain insight into the various research methods employed, including the investigation of inflammation and immune system functioning, drug screening, and preclinical and clinical trials.

pathways.title: How Do Nutrients and Pollutants Travel From Rivers to the Sea?
pathways.description: The Queen Mary University of London’s Professor Kate Spencer and Dr Jonathan Wheatland are on a quest to understand how suspended particulate matter carries vital nutrients and harmful pollutants from rivers to seas.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you should know more about the composition, behavior, and impact of suspended particulate matter (SPM) on aquatic ecosystems
Better understand the importance of environmental geochemistry in studying the impact of human activity on the environment
Learn valuable tips for pursuing a career in science and the applications of environmental geochemistry
Learn key terminology and techniques related to research around Suspended Particulate Matter (SPMs)

pathways.title: Climate Science Champions
pathways.description: Each of us has a story. Science can inform and guide decisions on important societal issues. Here are eight brief stories of USGS scientists working to understand and address climate change.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Explore a variety of careers involved in climate science research.
Gain a clearer understanding of what climate change related scientific careers could entail on a day-to-day basis.
Learn from scientists about their field of research.

pathways.title: Training Astronauts for Lunar Exploration
pathways.description: Becoming an astronaut could be one of the most exciting yet difficult careers on Earth. From exposure to the hostile space environment to conducting tasks in challenging places, astronauts need extensive training before being launched beyond the Earth's atmosphere. This pathway summarizes some of the challenges and considerations faced by astronauts training for the Artemis program.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand why astronauts have to train for years before going to space.
You will learn about the skills and tools used by astronauts.
You will discover how environments on Earth are used to mimic conditions in space.
You will understand the importance of self-care and teamwork for astronauts.

pathways.title: Why Do Children Differ in Their Academic Achievements?
pathways.description: Dr. Emma Meaburn describes the day-to-day life of an academic scientist who researches what genes do and how they change. Watch the video to find out more about behavioural genetics and how her experience as a fostered child inspired her to pursue her career.

pathways.title: Inferential Statistics and Regression Analysis
pathways.description: Inferential statistics is a crucial tool in data science for making predictions and decisions based on sample data, especially when accessing complete population data is impractical. These methods enable scientists to draw conclusions and make informed decisions from limited information. Key concepts include statistical inference, hypothesis testing, correlation, linear regression, and analysis of variance (ANOVA), all of which provide foundational knowledge for analyzing relationships between variables and making predictions. These topics are introduced in Chapter 4 of the OpenStax Principles of Data Science textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to make educated guesses about a population by analyzing sample data.
Use hypothesis testing to determine if a claim is supported by data.
Measure and interpret the strength of relationships between two variables using correlation.
Use linear regression to create and interpret models that predict outcomes.
Apply ANOVA to compare multiple groups and identify significant differences.

pathways.title: Chemical Reactions and Matter – The Behavior of Molecules
pathways.description: Chemistry is more than just atoms—it’s about how they interact! This pathway explores molecular interactions, the behavior of gases, and the states of matter. You will learn about intermolecular forces, diffusion, pressure, and solution chemistry through engaging simulations and problem-solving activities.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the different states of matter and how molecules interact.
Analyze gas properties, including pressure, volume, and temperature.
Explain diffusion, intermolecular forces, and their impact on matter.
Calculate molecular properties using real-world examples.
Explore how gases and liquids behave in different conditions.

pathways.title: Using Nanotechnology to Overcome the Adhesion of the Bacterial Pathogen Staphylococcus Aureus
pathways.description: Professor Yves Dufrêne is a researcher in nanobiophysics with an interest in methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus Aureus (MRSA) bacterial strains. Based at the Louvain Institute of Biomolecular Science and Technology in Belgium, his work focuses on finding new means of thwarting these pathogens and their ability to stick to medical devices and cause life-threatening infections.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you will understand the significance of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) strains and their antibiotic resistance.
Recognize the impact of MRSA infections, particularly biofilm-related ones, in hospital-acquired infections and patient outcomes.

Familiarize with atomic force microscopy (AFM) techniques developed by Professor Dufrêne's team to study the structural, adhesive and mechanical properties of microbes, particularly adhesion proteins and their interactions with human proteins.

pathways.title: Humans of HBI
pathways.description: This pathway showcases some of the individuals that make up the Harvard Brain Science Initiative.

pathways.title: Sex Determination
pathways.description: This pathway explores topics related to sex determination, including the differences between autosomes and sex chromosomes, different mechanisms for sex determination, and how these mechanisms determine sex in a range of organisms, such as humans.
learning_objectives: Describe different mechanisms of sex determination.
Explain the mechanism of sex determination in humans.

pathways.title: Data Management and Information Systems Business Strategies
pathways.description: Discover how businesses use technology to solve problems, improve processes, and create exciting digital products. From streaming services to virtual concerts, learn how data, apps, and innovation power the digital world—and explore the strategies companies use to stay competitive in a fast-changing, tech-driven marketplace. This pathway is Chapter 2 of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain key frameworks and practices for managing organizational data, including governance, quality, and alignment with business strategy.
Evaluate how organizations generate value through information technology, including strategies for innovation, efficiency, and business-IT alignment.
Compare and critique digital business models, examining how different models leverage IT to deliver, capture, and sustain value.
Analyze how information systems support business processes, from documentation and optimization to reengineering and digital transformation.
Describe the stages of digital innovation, and identify how organizations can adopt, develop, and commercialize technologies to remain competitive in a digital economy.

pathways.title: What Happens When Plastics Break Down Into Microplastics and Nanoplastics?
pathways.description: Microplastics and their even smaller cousins, nanoplastics, are a growing concern for environmental and human health. At the FAMU-FSU College of Engineering in Florida, USA, and the University of Alberta in Canada, environmental engineers Dr Jeffrey Farner and Dr Olubukola Alimi are studying what happens as plastics degrade into microplastics and nanoplastics, and how this pollution interacts with other substances.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms such as adsorb, Brownian motion, colloid, microplastic, nanoplastic, plastic polymer, and weathering.
Recall the primary sources and processes that contribute to the formation of microplastics and nanoplastics.
Explain how plastic degrades into microplastics and nanoplastics through weathering.
Summarize how nanoplastics interact with biological barriers and ecosystems.

pathways.title: Understanding DNA and Its Role in Life
pathways.description: DNA is the blueprint for all living organisms. It determines who we are, from physical traits to disease risks. This course explores DNA’s structure, replication, and role in heredity, protein synthesis, biotechnology, and forensics—building molecular biology knowledge for health and society applications.
learning_objectives: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to describe the structure of DNA and its components.
Understand how DNA carries genetic information.
Explain the role of DNA in determining individual traits and inheritance.

pathways.title: Arthropod and Biological Control of Pests
pathways.description: Arthropods make up about 85% of all known animal species, showcasing incredible diversity. Describe the characteristics, development, and ecological importance of arthropods, and evaluate methods of pest control, including integrated pest management and biological control strategies.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the general characteristics and classification of arthropods as invertebrate animals.
Differentiate between complete and incomplete metamorphosis in insects and explain their life cycle stages.
Explain the ecological and economic importance of arthropods in agriculture and the environment.
Evaluate pest control methods, including integrated pest management and biological control using arthropods.

pathways.title: Sources of Direct Current
pathways.description: Electricity powers our world, and understanding its sources is essential for modern technology. This pathway explores the fundamentals of electricity, direct current (DC), and alternating current (AC). You will learn about the history of electricity, key contributors like Edison and Galvani, and how electrical circuits function. By the end of this pathway, you will understand how DC power is generated and used in electrical systems.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the basic principles of electricity and its historical development.
Explain the differences between direct current (DC) and alternating current (AC).
Analyze how electrical circuits function and how voltage changes impact energy transmission.
Apply knowledge of electrical sources to real-world power distribution and usage.

pathways.title: From Bitter Water to Sacred Science: Matter's Role in Clean Water
pathways.description:
Explore the nature of matter and the fundamental principles of chemical bonding with this pathway accompanied by an Explore Worksheet and Experiment Worksheet. The recommended learning time spans 6 days of lessons (270-315 minutes) and focuses on the water cycle, the differences between mixtures and pure substances, the subatomic particles that make up atoms, as well as how these particles form the basis for the periodic table arrangement.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 45-60 minutes, items 2-6 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 45 minutes, items 7-8 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 45 minutes, items 9-13 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 90-120 minutes, item 14 of the pathway.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explore the nature of matter as consisting of atoms that are made up of subatomic particles consisting of protons, neutrons, and electrons, and that changing the number of subatomic particles can have different effects on the atom itself, from changing it to a different type of matter, or to an ion or isotope of the same type of matter.

Be able to relate the nature of matter to the purification of water by connecting how atoms can chemically combine to form molecules like water, which can interact with other molecules without chemically combining with them (a type of substance known as a mixture); pure water consists of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom, but is usually found as a mixture with other molecules dissolved into it.

Determine the subatomic properties of an atom through studying the periodic table arrangement, and how the outer number of electrons in an atom is the determining factor in how atoms chemically combine, ultimately predicting how water can form from two hydrogens and one oxygen.
Be able to learn to design, build, and test a Syntrichia Hydrotap, a portable device that would allow students to capture purified atmospheric water vapor to provide a source of potable water.

pathways.title: Ecosystem Ecology
pathways.description: Uncover the intricate link between global health and the environment. Examine our fragile energy resources, the paradox of inaction, and the demand for creative solutions to safeguard humanity. Learn about the challenges of harmonizing society with the environment as well as ongoing debates on decoupling and integrative solutions.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the intricate connection between global health and the environment.
Explore the concept of agroecology, grasping its principles and significance in promoting sustainable agriculture and a healthier environment.
Gain the knowledge and skills to contribute to global health and environmental preservation efforts, fostering a sense of responsibility towards the planet's well-being.

pathways.title: The Power of Language
pathways.description: The brain's capacity to process intricate thoughts and abstract ideas paves the way for collaboration and strategizing in large groups. In this pathway, you will gain insights into the evolution of language in social groups, discover key brain areas responsible for language processing, and learn to demonstrate how language works through a classroom presentation. By the end, you'll appreciate the vital role of teachers and tutors in vocal language learning.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how language evolved to communicate in social groups.
You will be able to name the key brain areas for language processing and their functions.
You will demonstrate/educate on how language works in a classroom presentation.
You will be able to discuss how teachers and tutors are key for vocal language learning.

pathways.title: Cooler Communities: Land, Climate, and Community Change
pathways.description: Land surface temperatures differ across parts of a city, and this 12-lesson pathway explores why. Through the lessons, students will investigate the urban heat island effect, vegetation, water cycle, greenhouse effect, and human impacts using maps, data analysis, and design thinking, while developing models and proposing sustainable community solutions.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to develop and use a model to describe how unequal heating and rotation of the Earth cause patterns of atmospheric and oceanic circulation.
Apply scientific principles to design a method for monitoring and minimizing human impact on the environment.
Construct an argument supported by evidence for how increases in human population and per-capita consumption of natural resources impact Earth’s systems.
Ask questions to clarify evidence of the factors that have caused the rise in global temperatures over the past century.
Plan an investigation to determine the relationships among the energy transferred, type of matter, mass, and the change in the kinetic energy of particles.
Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how environmental and genetic factors influence the growth of organisms.

pathways.title: NASA | Biofluids
pathways.description: The human body is accustomed to Earth's gravity - what happens to important bodily processes, like blood flow, when gravity changes? This pathway discusses how blood flow is impacted by microgravity environments, and how these effects are studied.
learning_objectives: Examine the composition and functions of blood and other bodily fluids.
Develop a fundamental understanding of fluid shifts and their effects on the body.
Understand how the cardiovascular system responds to a microgravity environment.

pathways.title: Invasive Species and Ecosystem Disruption
pathways.description: Invasive species are one of the most significant drivers of ecological change. Explore the biological, ecological, and human dimensions of invasive species, with a focus on how they affect plant communities and local ecosystems.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Differentiate between the terms introduced species, exotic species, and invasive species.
Describe historical and modern human roles in the global spread of plant and animal species, including intentional and accidental introductions.
Explain the ecological characteristics that enable certain plant species to become invasive.
Identify examples of invasive plant and animal species and evaluate their impact on native biodiversity.
Evaluate the effects of invasive species on native plant communities.
Examine how botanists use field data, plant collections, GPS tracking, and remote sensing to monitor invasive species and assess restoration potential.
Evaluate the risks and benefits of management strategies that are used to control invasive species.
Discuss the ethical and ecological complexities involved in managing invasive species, including the role of climate change, human responsibility, and conservation priorities.

pathways.title: Probability: How Can We Use Evidence to Reason about Chances?
pathways.description: This pathway will deepen your understanding of how evidence can help us to reason accurately about chances and uncertainty. Using visualizations and relatable examples from everyday life, you’ll explore foundational probability concepts, like independence, conditional probability, and base rates. By learning to identify and avoid common mistakes, such as the gambler’s fallacy and conjunction fallacy, you’ll enhance your ability to reason critically. Finally, you’ll practice applying these insights to realistic contexts, from assessing health risks to interpreting the reliability of test results, helping you to make better-informed decisions about probability.
learning_objectives: Describe basic concepts of probability, including conditional probability, independence, and base rates.
Evaluate real-world evidence of probability claims, recognizing common reasoning errors such as the gambler's fallacy and the conjunction fallacy.
Visualize and interpret probabilities and their relationships using diagrams and statistical tools.
Apply probabilistic reasoning to make informed decisions about practical situations involving uncertainty, such as interpreting medical tests and health risks.

pathways.title: Hello, Houston?! Satellite Science: Waves of Communication
pathways.description:
Navigate the world of satellites and electromagnetic waves to uncover how technology connects us. Engage in virtual experiments and model-making to explore the science of communication with the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 6 days of lessons (220 minutes).
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2 & 3, 60 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 9-11 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 45 minutes, found in the Student Guide.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 10 minutes, item 12 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to collect and analyze data about how electromagnetic waves travel.
Explain how communication technology evolves to meet human demands.
Engage in virtual experiments to enhance critical thinking skills.
Explore the relationships between humans' ever changing need to communicate quickly and effectively.
Explore related careers, and ask relevant questions.

pathways.title: How Can We Detect and Prevent Brain Injuries?
pathways.description: Inside your skull sits your brain, an incredible computer that controls everything you do. The skull does its best to protect our brains from injury, but, sometimes, it is not enough. Professor Christian Franck, from the University of Wisconsin-Madison in the US, leads the PANTHER programme, researching new ways of detecting and preventing traumatic brain injuries.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will be able to comprehend the significance of early detection of asymptomatic brain injuries.
You will evaluate the potential impact of PANTHER in preventing brain injuries.
You will explain the distinction between primary and secondary injuries in TBIs.

pathways.title: Concrete Change: The Innovative Chemistry of Sustainable Cement
pathways.description: The global cement industry is gigantic, so finding ways to reduce its impact on the climate is essential for a sustainable future. At the University of Sheffield in the UK, Dr Theodore Hanein and his team are investigating alternative methods for cement production. This includes replacing raw materials with waste products from other industries and reducing the carbon dioxide released during cement manufacture.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will understand the environmental challenges associated with traditional cement production.
You will analyze the potential benefits and challenges of substituting natural raw materials with by-products in cement manufacturing.
You will the the chemistry behind traditional cement production, especially the CO2-emitting process of clinker formation.
You will evaluate the role of carbon capture and storage (CCS) in reducing CO2 emissions from cement production and assess its potential benefits and drawbacks.

pathways.title: Detecting Toxic Substances for a Greener and Healthier World
pathways.description: Dr Wenping Yin, based at Monash University in Australia, forms part of a team working at the ARC Centre of Excellence in Exciton Science. Her work is focused on photoluminescent chemical sensors which can detect toxic substances. The findings will make the world a safer place.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you should be able to understand the significance of materials science and its impact on human society.
Describe how detecting toxic substances can contribute to making the world greener, and improve human health
Analyze the potential applications of this research

pathways.title: Critically Evaluating Information
pathways.description: Cognitive bias, like motivated reasoning, can lead individuals to accept false information that aligns with their beliefs. This pathway covers critical thinking strategies to identify data misrepresentation, detect deepfakes, analyze cognitive bias, evaluate scientific claims, and verify online content.
learning_objectives: Recognize how data can be used to persuade or mislead by identifying common misrepresentations in charts, graphs, and statistics.
Understand the risks of generative AI and deepfakes in spreading misinformation, and describe methods used to detect and protect against them.
Understand why neither data nor its interpretation is neutral, and assess the role of context and presentation in shaping perception.
Identify signs of bias in language, framing, and reasoning by recognizing the impact of cognitive heuristics and motivated reasoning.
Apply critical thinking techniques, such as click restraint and lateral reading, to avoid algorithmic bias and confirm the accuracy of online content.

pathways.title: Working with Stakeholders in Data-Driven Roles
pathways.description: Collaborating with stakeholders is a critical skill across data-driven and technology-focused fields, where effective problem definition and clear communication support better decisions and solutions. This pathway introduces essential concepts in business process management, systems analysis, user requirements gathering, and development frameworks such as Agile and the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).
learning_objectives: Describe the purpose and importance of documentation in information systems and analytics workflows, including how documentation supports system design, communication, and validation.
Explain the role of business processes in organizational success and describe how Business Process Management (BPM) supports the analysis, modeling, and optimization of those processes.
Use data-driven techniques and stakeholder input to evaluate and improve business processes and systems.
Analyze business and industry use cases using structured approaches to identify stakeholder needs, define system requirements, and propose data-informed solutions.

pathways.title: Electromagnetic Radiation: How Can Light Cause and Cure Cancer?
pathways.description:
Over 6 days (270–345 minutes total), you will explore how electromagnetic radiation interacts with matter in different ways. Through simulations, videos, texts, case studies and the accompanying student guide, you'll investigate different types of radiation, test how frequency affects interactions, and evaluate the reliability of published claims about radiation absorption.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45-60 minutes, item 1 of the pathway and activities in the student guide.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 60-90 minutes, items 2-5 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3-4, 60-90 minutes, item 6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 60 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 45 minutes, items 7-8 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify and differentiate between the various types of electromagnetic radiation.
Experiment with how different frequencies of electromagnetic radiation interact with different types of matter.
Assess the validity and reliability of published claims about the effects of electromagnetic radiation when absorbed by matter.

pathways.title: Understanding Forces
pathways.description: A force is a push or pull that can change the motion of an object. It has both magnitude and direction, making it a vector quantity.  Explore Newton’s laws, inertia, friction, gravity, and dark energy to understand motion from everyday objects to galaxies and the expanding universe.
learning_objectives: Explain Newton’s laws of motion and apply them to describe how forces affect the motion of objects on Earth and in space.
Investigate inertia and mass to understand resistance to changes in motion, including real-life examples such as vehicles, friction, and safety devices.
Analyze the role of friction as a force opposing motion, and compare how different surfaces (e.g., ice, concrete) affect the speed and stopping of moving objects.
Construct and interpret free-body diagrams to represent balanced and unbalanced forces, net force, and predict resulting motion.
Apply Newton’s third law of motion to everyday and scientific contexts, such as swimming, walking, rockets, and planetary interactions.
Explore gravitational force as described by Newton’s Law of Universal Gravitation, and use it to compare motions of celestial bodies and planetary orbits.
Discuss current scientific ideas about galaxies, dark matter, and dark energy, connecting them to Newtonian mechanics and the expansion of the universe.

pathways.title: Health Equity: Socioeconomic Factors and Policy Solutions
pathways.description: Understanding how socioeconomic factors like poverty, education, and employment impact health is essential to addressing health disparities. By analyzing globalization, trade policies, and environmental determinants of health, we can propose interventions that promote health equity, support sustainable development goals (SDGs), and improve healthcare access across diverse populations.
subject_tags: Economics:Firms, Work, and the Labor Market
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the relationship between health outcomes and socioeconomic factors, such as poverty, education, and employment.
Analyze the impact of globalization, trade policies, and economic systems on health equity and access to healthcare.
Evaluate strategies for promoting sustainable development goals (SDGs) related to health and well-being.
Discuss the concept of social determinants of health and their implications for policy and practice.
Propose interventions to address socioeconomic inequalities and improve health outcomes at the individual, community, and population levels.

pathways.title: Global Health: Equity, Policy, and Cultural Competence
pathways.description: Explore the core concepts of global health, focusing on its role in addressing health disparities and promoting equity worldwide. You will examine the interconnectedness of health issues across borders and the key stakeholders like the WHO involved in global health initiatives. Analyze historical and contemporary challenges in global health governance, and develop cultural competence and ethical awareness essential for effective global health practice.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define global health and explain its significance in addressing health disparities and promoting equity.
You will understand the interconnectedness of health issues across borders and the role of globalization in shaping health outcomes.
You will be familiar with key stakeholders and organizations involved in global health initiatives, such as the World Health Organization (WHO) and non-governmental organizations (NGOs).
You will evaluate historical and contemporary challenges in global health governance and policymaking.
You will interact with examples of cultural competence and ethical considerations in global health practice.

pathways.title: Understanding Leptin: How Can Changes in Body Weight Affect Reproduction?
pathways.description: Dr Carol Elias, a physiologist and neuroscientist at the University of Michigan Medical School in the US, is exploring how a hormone called leptin influences metabolism and reproductive health. She is seeking to uncover the mysteries behind conditions such as obesity, diabetes, and infertility.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand and describe the role of leptin in regulating energy balance and reproductive health, explaining how disruptions in leptin signaling can lead to metabolic and reproductive disorders.
Analyze the use of genetically modified mouse models in leptin research to identify the physiological processes influenced by leptin and its receptors within the brain.
Evaluate the ethical considerations of using animal models in scientific research, particularly in the context of studying hormonal effects on metabolism and reproduction.

pathways.title: Scaffolding New Material
pathways.description:
Every instructor who intends for his/her students to achieve ambitious learning objectives by the end of a semester must first answer the question: “How do I get them there?” Just as physical scaffolding enables a work crew to reach new heights at a construction site, instructional scaffolding supports students as they begin to grapple with progressively more challenging skills. Through the use of strategies such as modeling a professor’s own thinking, co-constructing common knowledge as a class, and building off of student contributions, instructors provide the initial support necessary to lead students to master more advanced skills and reach new heights in their learning.
How can we organize our lectures so that student understanding and interest increase over time? How might we use exemplary student thinking and behaviors to illustrate overarching learning goals? After having taught material, how can we check in real-time whether students truly understand it? In this pathway, featured professors detail how they scaffold learning so students work gradually toward deeper, more complex understandings.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will define the concept of instructional scaffolding and explain its role in supporting student learning and skill development
Apply instructional scaffolding techniques, such as modeling expert thinking and building on student contributions, to facilitate the gradual acquisition of complex skills.
Design a structured lecture or lesson plan that incorporates scaffolding strategies to enhance student understanding and maintain engagement over time.
Assess and adjust scaffolding methods in real-time to ensure that they are effectively aiding students in reaching higher levels of comprehension and application of the material.

pathways.title: Climate Challenge: Be Part of the Solution
pathways.description: This pathway provides insight into how climate change is already impacting and will impact communities in urban and rural areas around the world. It describes the concrete actions individuals, communities, and Aga Khan Development Network staff can take to adapt to and mitigate climate change.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will understand why climate actions are urgent.
Learners will be able to define climate resilience.
Learners will be able to discuss the scientific evidence of climate change impacts on human life and ecosystems.
Learners will be able to identify climate change risks and design measures that address them.
Learners will be able to understand greenhouse gases (GHGs) and identify actions that reduce their emissions or absorb them from the atmosphere.

pathways.title: Interdisciplinary Approaches
pathways.description: Follow thought-provoking discussions on optimization, planetary boundaries, ecological interdependence, climate economics, sustainability sciences, and the critical zone. Uncover insights, redefine paradigms, and contribute to a sustainable future.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will explore the concept of planetary boundaries, examining both technical and social perspectives.
Understand the perils of over-optimization, including the drawbacks of distorted success metrics, rebound effects, and their ecological consequences.
Understand its multidisciplinary importance in bridging natural and social sciences, and its impact on environmental understanding and policy-making.

pathways.title: Understanding Lyme disease with Dr Catherine Brissette
pathways.description: Dr Catherine Brissette, based at the Department of Biomedical Sciences at the University of North Dakota in the US, is engaged in a project that seeks to understand more about Lyme disease. The findings could lead to the development of novel therapies to treat and prevent infection.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway students will be able to explain the causes, symptoms, and treatment of Lyme disease, as well as identify potential long-term complications associated with the disease.
Explain the nature of Lyme disease as a bacterial infection, understand the factors contributing to its severity and persistence.

Analyze the impact of Dr. Brissette's research on the scientific field, synthesize the role of scientific findings in advancing understanding, and evaluate the societal implications and potential solutions for diversity and equality in the scientific community.

pathways.title: Why Thinking Like a Scientist Helps Students Tackle Real-Life Problems
pathways.description: Dr Erin Peters-Burton is the Director of the Center for Social Equity Through Science Education, George Mason University, USA. She has developed a metacognitive prompting intervention which enables teachers to help students think like a scientist.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you should: understand the concept of self-regulated learning (SRL) and its components, including metacognition, strategic action, and motivation to learn.
Apply the principles of self-regulated learning to real-life scenarios and consider how they can be implemented beyond the classroom setting.
Explore the concept of computational thinking and its potential application in helping high school students understand data analysis in more detail.

pathways.title: Building and Training Human Tendons in the Lab
pathways.description: Tendon issues affect millions of people worldwide, and treatment options are far from guaranteed to be effective. Tissue engineering involves building human tissues in the lab, which can then be used to replace faulty tissues in patients. Professor Pierre-Alexis Mouthuy, based at the University of Oxford in the UK, is taking this a step further with tendon building, by using humanoid robotic arms and soft bioreactor chambers to ‘train’ tendons to deal with the stresses they will encounter in the human body.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will learn more about tendon tissue engineering. How researchers are exploring tissue engineering as a potential solution for successful tendon repair or replacement procedures.
By the end of this pathway you will know how scientists are creating an environment that closely resembles the conditions in the body is crucial for growing functional tendon tissue.
By the end of this pathway you will learn about the use of robotics in tissue engineering.

pathways.title: Curiosity Drives Discovery
pathways.description: Curiosity fuels the human pursuit of knowledge, fostering breakthroughs and expanding our understanding of the world. This pathway explores the role of curiosity and imagination in human cognition, with a special focus on their influence within neuroscience. You'll explore how music enhances the brain's imaginative capacity, understand curiosity's contribution to civilization's development, and discover how curiosity propels human knowledge advancement in neuroscience, illustrated through innovative research.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand that curiosity and imagination is a hallmark of human cognition.
You will explore the role of music in expanding the human brain's imagination.
You will understand the role of curiosity in the development of advanced civilization.
You will learn about how curiosity drives the advancement of human knowledge in neuroscience.

pathways.title: Why Gravitational Waves Are of Supermassive Importance
pathways.description: Dr Paul Lasky, Senior Lecturer in the School of Physics and Astronomy at Monash University in Australia, tells us why the recent detection of gravitational waves from outer space has had such a huge impact on his research and science as a whole.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how gravitational waves are related to black holes, and what they are
Learn about the work that gravitational-wave astrophysicists are doing, and the paths that lead them to this niche field
Learn about the impact of gravitational-wave research on our understanding of black holes

pathways.title: Does Science Have All the Answers?
pathways.description: Dr Siobhan Maderson, from Aberystwyth University, UK, has been investigating how traditional environmental knowledge is often overlooked by scientists and politicians. Her research into the knowledge of beekeepers could help put sustainable, nature-friendly food on our plates.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will be able to define Traditional Environmental Knowledge (TEK).
You will be able to describe the benefits of combining TEK with scientific data.
You will understand the value of incorporating TEK into environmental management practices.

pathways.title: "The first systemic crisis of the Anthropocene" and "Coping with an ecological systemic crisis" (by the AFD)
pathways.description:
"The first systemic crisis of the Anthropocene" (Léa Lugassy, Antoine Godin, Devrim Yilmaz)
The COVID crisis is a triple crisis. Aside from the international health crisis, which has a strong ecological connection and which has so far specifically affected so-called developed economies, the moment has become a global financial crisis and an oil and commodities crisis of acute intensity in the developing world. This course will highlight the interconnectedness of these three dimensions. The first lecture will concentrate on the immediate impacts of the crisis. The health crisis is itself the combination of accelerated ecological degradations due to deeply unbalanced modes of development, and the starving of public health systems. The disinvestment in public goods by national and international public bodies observed in the last decades strikes back. This socio-ecological crisis has generated a full-blown pandemic with financial and economic impacts at a scale not seen in at least a century. Its uncertain timing, completely dependent on the virus dynamics, enters in deep contradiction with the current governance system, making any assumptions about a recovery dynamics highly speculative. This global shock of biological and economic notions of time certainly makes it a systemic crisis of the Anthropocene. In the developing world, the crisis triggered massive international capital flows movement away from emerging markets and toward developed countries. Moreover, the sudden decrease in oil and commodities demand exacerbated tensions notably among oil market actors driving the price for short-term WTI futures in negative territories. Consequently, many emerging markets and developing economies are now facing potential disruptive impacts such as exchange rate tensions, speculative attacks, and possible debt restructuring. The macrofinancial mechanisms at hand will be highlighted. 
"Coping with an ecological systemic crisis" (Achilleas Mantes, Etienne Espagne, Guilherme Magacho)
The second lecture will propose a prospective stance. Keeping the objectives of the Paris Agreement under reach amounts to a complete retrofit of past mitigation and adaptation strategies. If the crisis itself generated an unprecedented reduction in GHGs, to date, most of these exit policies are cementing a highly – and old -- carbonated productive sector, as was observed in 2008 in the aftermath of the financial crisis. On the other hand focusing on GHG emission reductions only to cope with the Paris Agreement without touching the financial and economic institutions at the heart of the fossil economy bears the risk of social unrest. Avoiding the next crisis of the Anthropocene imposes to navigate between these two reefs.

pathways.title: Communication Impact in IT and Data Science
pathways.description: Clear communication is essential in information technology and data-driven fields, where insights, decisions, and systems depend on effective collaboration and reporting. This pathway introduces key communication skills used across IT and data science, including collaborative digital tools, professional email practices, audience-focused reporting, and understanding the business value of IT.
learning_objectives: Explain how communication skills contribute to the effectiveness and career success of information technology (IT) and information systems (IS) professionals.
Describe core communication competencies for data analysts and IT professionals, including active listening, technical writing, virtual collaboration, and audience-tailored messaging.
Apply best practices for professional communication in digital formats, including email, messaging, and virtual meeting tools.
Analyze how audience needs (e.g., experts, executives, non-specialists) shape the language, format, and structure of technical reporting in data analytics.
Illustrate how effective communication practices support collaboration, drive innovation, and improve business outcomes in IT-driven organizations.

pathways.title: Decision-Making Using Machine Learning Basics
pathways.description: Machine learning models like decision trees and logistic regression are widely used to make data-driven predictions in fields such as healthcare and education. Explore foundational machine learning techniques, including classification, regression, clustering, and decision trees. Apply key algorithms like logistic regression, k-means, DBScan, and naïve Bayes to real-world data. This pathway is Chapter 2 of the Principles of Data Science textbook
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised machine learning techniques and describe how they are used in real-world decision-making.
Use classification algorithms, such as logistic regression and naïve Bayes, to predict categorical outcomes from labeled data.
Compare clustering algorithms like k-means and DBScan by evaluating their performance on datasets with varying structure and density.
Construct and evaluate decision trees, including the application of pruning techniques to prevent overfitting.
Interpret and calculate model evaluation metrics such as accuracy, mean absolute error (MAE), and entropy to assess predictive performance.

pathways.title: Community Health Promotion: Strategies and Partnerships
pathways.description: Discover how public health experts use disease surveillance and infrastructure to predict and manage outbreaks like COVID-19. Explore the role of community partnerships and technology in advancing health equity. Learn about health disparities through case studies to enhance public health strategies and outcomes.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the principles of community-based health promotion and the importance of community engagement in addressing health issues.
Analyze the role of policies and regulations in shaping health behaviors, environments, and access to healthcare services.
Evaluate the effectiveness of health promotion programs and interventions in diverse cultural and socioeconomic contexts.
Discuss strategies for building partnerships and coalitions to support collaborative approaches to health promotion.
Propose evidence-based interventions and policies to address priority health issues and improve population health outcomes.

pathways.title: Health Behaviors: Risks, Influences, and Interventions
pathways.description: Understand how biopsychosocial factors—ranging from genetics and stress to social determinants of health—contribute to complex health outcomes like obesity, alcohol use disorders, and chronic disease. This pathway emphasizes the role of environment, behavior, and biology in human health.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify common behavioral risk factors for health, such as tobacco use, alcohol consumption, physical inactivity, and risky sexual behavior.
Analyze the social, cultural, and environmental influences on health behaviors and lifestyle choices.
Evaluate the impact of health promotion and behavioral interventions in preventing disease and promoting healthy lifestyles.
Discuss the role of education, media, and social marketing in promoting positive health behaviors.
Propose strategies for addressing behavioral risk factors and promoting health behavior change at the individual, community, and population levels.

pathways.title: How Does Physics Allow Us to Look Inside the Body?
pathways.description: Biomedical imaging techniques enable doctors and scientists to view inside the human body, allowing them to diagnose medical conditions and uncover the biological processes that are essential for life. At the University of California Davis, USA, Professor Simon Cherry and Professor Ramsey Badawi have developed the world’s first full-body scanner, which has the potential to revolutionise medicine and biomedical research.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), positron emission tomography (PET), and radioisotope.
Describe the process of radioactive decay and its role in PET imaging.
Interpret the significance of positron-electron annihilation in PET scanning.
Identify the main components and principles behind PET scanning.

pathways.title: Responding to Students
pathways.description:
Class discussions often mirror the form of a tennis match between professor and students: The Professor serves a question to the student, the student sends the answer back to the professor, the professor moves on to the next student, and so on. Sometimes, however, it makes sense to volley continuously with one student so they might explain and expand their thinking further. Other discussions might better function as volleyball matches, where ideas are passed among students before the professor is brought back into the action. Whatever the situation calls for, one thing is clear -- how instructors respond (or refraining from responding) to students holds powerful consequences for a discussion’s flow, participation, and productivity.
How can we use our own voices more purposefully when responding to students? How do we facilitate a collaborative discussion where students genuinely discuss ideas among themselves? When might we insert our own expertise in a student-centered discussion versus letting that conversation proceed uninterrupted? How can we encourage students to disagree with one another while maintaining safe, supportive, and respectful classroom communities? In this pathway, featured professors discuss how they create the conditions for and encourage student-to-student discourse.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will demonstrate understanding of various discussion formats, such as the tennis match and volleyball match models, and their impact on classroom discourse.
Apply strategies for using instructor responses purposefully to guide and enhance the flow and productivity of class discussions.
Analyze the role of instructor intervention in student-centered discussions and make decisions about when to contribute expertise versus when to allow student dialogue to continue independently.
Design and implement methods for creating a classroom environment where students can respectfully challenge each other's ideas, fostering a culture of collaborative and critical discourse.

pathways.title: Space Fit: Galactic Health for Astronaut Well-Being
pathways.description:
Explore the effects microgravity has on the overall physical and mental well-being of astronauts with this pathway and accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (170-190 minutes) and focuses on microgravity, space exploration, and the overall physical and mental well-being of humans. Plus, explore a potential STEM career related to these topics.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2 & 3, 40-60 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 40 minutes, items 9-10 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, found in the Student Guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 10 minutes, item 11 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will investigate and interpret data on how being alone and confined affects humans in space.
Explain how regular exercise contributes to health by designing a fitness device for astronauts.
Understand how staying fit and feeling good are connected.
Discover space-related jobs and form thoughtful questions.

pathways.title: Protecting Crops From Nematodes
pathways.description: Just like animals, plants are bombarded by all sorts of pathogens and parasites. Currently, one particular species of nematode worm is threatening to cause widespread issues for a variety of important crops. At Clemson University, South Carolina, USA, Dr Paula Agudelo and her team are employing cutting-edge science from a wealth of different fields to develop an array of solutions to defend crops against these worms.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway learners will be able to understand the consequences of Meloidogyne enterolobii (Me) infestation on agriculture.
Learners will understand the practical implications of nematode infestation on producers, processors, and distributors.
Learners will evaluate the potential consequences of nematodes becoming pests in agricultural systems.
Learners will understand the adaptability of nematodes across diverse environments, hosts, and climates.

pathways.title: Why Worms Matter: The Importance of Nematode Diversity for Studying Biology and Evolution
pathways.description: Dr. Erik Andersen aims to capture the genetic diversity of Caenorhabditis elegans, a tiny worm used by geneticists worldwide to deepen our understanding of human biology. Despite its small size, this worm has had a significant impact, with six Nobel prizes awarded for discoveries made using it. By incorporating C. elegans diversity into research, more breakthroughs can be achieved, and you can contribute to Dr. Andersen's mission of advancing genetics.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the importance of capturing and studying genetic diversity in model organisms for advancing genetic research and enhancing our understanding of human biology.
Understand the significance of C. elegans as a model organism, recognize the importance of genetic diversity in nematodes.

Students will be able to explain the significance of genetic diversity in nematodes and understand the advantages of using wild strains of Caenorhabditis elegans in genetic research as a model organism.

pathways.title: Can We Use Mud to Understand Climate Change?
pathways.description: Professor David Thornalley from University College London, in the UK, has been using data from ocean floor mud to understand how the Atlantic Ocean has changed over thousands of years into the past and how it may change in the future due to climate change. His data show that the ocean’s circulation has been weakening, probably as a result of human-caused warming.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand what the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) is, and how it is influenced by climate change.
You will be able to explain the key aspects of climate science, and how oceans are a key part of the climate system.
You will understand how sampling can be used to supplement information gathered from remote sensing.

pathways.title: Measuring Surface Water
pathways.description: "As an environmentalist and a scientist, I feel the best way to achieve environmental change is through sound science,". Learn more about the work of a hydrologist to gather streamflow and flood data.
learning_objectives: Gain a better understanding of streamflow data gathering
Explore the field of hydrology and related careers
Gain insight into the workday of a hydrologist

pathways.title: En-ROADS Simulator Tour: Energy, Energy Efficiency, and Electrification
pathways.description: Discover how the En-ROADS simulator demonstrates the impact of energy systems, efficiency, and electrification on climate change, emissions reduction, and sustainable development to support informed climate action planning.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will be able to analyze the role of different energy sources, including fossil fuels and renewables, in global emissions reduction.
Evaluate the effectiveness of energy efficiency and electrification strategies for supporting sustainable development and climate action.
Apply systems thinking to model energy-related climate scenarios using En-ROADS.
Interpret simulation results to inform decisions about climate policies and energy transitions.

pathways.title: Visualizing Data
pathways.description: Data tells stories about the world around us—but understanding those stories requires the right tools and techniques. This chapter explores how data scientists visualize complex information using time series graphs, probability distributions, and geospatial heatmaps. You'll learn how Python can turn raw data into meaningful patterns, predictions, and insights—whether you're tracking trends over time, estimating probabilities, or mapping data across locations. This pathway is Chapter 9 of the Principles of Data Science textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to apply data visualization techniques—such as histograms, boxplots, and time series graphs—to uncover trends, patterns, and outliers in real-world datasets using Python.
Develop skills in communicating complex data insights effectively by choosing appropriate visual formats, including geospatial maps, 3D plots, and probability distribution graphs.
Construct compelling data narratives using structured visual encoding strategies that enhance the clarity and impact of scientific and statistical insights.

Interpret and create advanced data visualizations—such as correlation heatmaps and labeled scatterplots—to support data-driven decision-making across fields like business, education, and public policy.

pathways.title: Reporting Results
pathways.description: Data science reports must clearly state assumptions and justify them to ensure model reliability and transparency. This pathway teaches essential skills in model validation, sensitivity analysis, and writing actionable executive summaries. This pathway is Chapter 10 of the Principles of Data Science textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to compose technical reports that clearly document methods, data analyses, and results, adapting communication style and visualizations to effectively engage diverse audiences while ensuring accessibility and transparency.

Analyze models by articulating assumptions, evaluating errors, performing sensitivity analysis, and summarizing model strengths and weaknesses to improve reliability and transparency in data-driven investigations.
Summarize data science findings by writing clear executive summaries that highlight key methods, insights, and actionable recommendations for diverse audiences.

pathways.title: Cloud Computing and Managing the Cloud Infrastructure
pathways.description: Cloud computing offers on-demand access to IT resources over the internet without requiring user-managed infrastructure. This pathway introduces key concepts such as service and deployment models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), virtualization, SDNs, pricing, applications, and ethical considerations. This pathway is Chapter 7 of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain how cloud computing uses technology to store, process, and transmit information efficiently, and evaluate its impact on organizational operations and technological innovation.

Analyze how technological advancements in cloud computing support data storage, processing, and delivery, enabling scalable infrastructure and services that impact global communication, innovation, and decision-making in modern systems.
Explain how advances in computing, virtualization, and cybersecurity technologies enable data storage, access, and protection in modern cloud-based systems used across industries.
Evaluate how cloud-based technologies support data management, system integration, and decision-making in organizations, emphasizing the role of computing in solving real-world problems.

Evaluate how advances in computing and data systems impact society, privacy, and sustainability, integrating ethical considerations and environmental effects in the design and use of cloud-based technologies.

pathways.title: A Cure for Education: How Can Genuine Scientific Experiences Inspire Future Scientists?
pathways.description: What is it like to be a research scientist conducting hands-on experiments in a working lab? How well can science lessons prepare – and encourage – students for careers in STEM? Dr Ying Gao, assistant professor from Nantong University, Xinglin College, in China and currently based at Jackson State University in the US, is investigating how course-based undergraduate research experiences (CUREs) can transform the learning experience and encourage students to think like real scientists.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of equity in science education.
Discover how course-based undergraduate research experiences (CUREs) prepare students for STEM careers.

pathways.title: How Does Exercise Help Recovering Stroke Patients?
pathways.description: The after-effects of a stroke can severely alter a person’s quality of life. Effects range from mobility issues to changes in brain function. However, the brain is surprisingly adaptable and often, with the right approaches, these lost functions can be partly or completely restored. At Western University in Canada, Dr Sue Peters is investigating the role of exercise in improving post-stroke recovery, and has found some promising results.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms related to stroke rehabilitation, including neuroplasticity, neurotransmitter, growth factor, and rehabilitation.
You will identify the primary causes of a stroke and how it affects brain function.
You will explain how a stroke disrupts brain function and leads to mobility challenges.
You will use knowledge of neuroplasticity to explain how targeted exercises can improve brain function and mobility.

pathways.title: What Makes Nursing a Dream Career?
pathways.description: Nursing is a dynamic and rewarding career that plays a vital role in global healthcare, offering opportunities in hospitals, research, education, and community health. This pathway explores the importance of nursing, the skills and qualities needed, the challenges of workforce shortages, and the various career specializations available. Gain insights into evidence-informed practice, interdisciplinary collaboration, and future advancements shaping the profession.
learning_objectives: Identify the different career paths and specializations available in nursing.
Explain the impact of nursing shortages on healthcare systems and patient care.

pathways.title: Digestive and Alimentary Canal System
pathways.description: The gastrointestinal tract is a 30-foot tube from mouth to anus that powers digestion and absorption. Discover how the human digestive system works as a coordinated network of organs that break down food, absorb nutrients, and remove waste. Explore its structure, functions, regulation, and the vital roles of accessory organs and intestinal bacteria in maintaining health.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to escribe the structure and functions of the major organs of the human digestive system, including accessory organs.
Explain the processes of ingestion, digestion, absorption, and elimination, highlighting the role of enzymes, hormones, and peristalsis.
Analyze the importance of intestinal bacteria and the large intestine in maintaining health, including water balance, vitamin production, and waste removal.
Relate the regulation of the digestive system to nervous and hormonal control mechanisms.
Evaluate common digestive disorders and their effects on nutrient absorption and homeostasis.

pathways.title: Radioactivity: Discovery, Concepts and Applications
pathways.description: Radioactivity is one of the most fascinating discoveries in modern science, with applications in medicine, energy, and nuclear physics. This pathway explores the pioneers of radioactivity, the fundamental concepts of nuclear decay, and real-world applications. You will learn about alpha, beta, and gamma radiation, conservation laws, half-life, and nuclear energy. Through historical accounts, conceptual texts, and practice problems, you will gain a comprehensive understanding of radioactivity and its impact on science and society.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the contributions of key scientists like Marie Curie, Henri Becquerel, and Wilhelm Röntgen in the discovery of radioactivity.
Understand the types of nuclear radiation (alpha, beta, and gamma) and their properties.
Apply conservation laws to analyze nuclear decay and reactions.
Calculate half-life and radioactive decay using given formulas.
Explore real-world applications of radioactivity in medicine, energy, and scientific research.

pathways.title: Health Determinants: Interactions and Equity Solutions
pathways.description: This pathway introduces learners to the concept of health disparities, exploring the various biological, behavioral, social, and environmental determinants of health. Learners will analyze the interactions between these determinants and their impact on population health. They will also explore ways to address health disparities and promote health equity.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify and categorize the determinants of health, including biological, behavioral, social, and environmental factors.
Analyze the complex interactions between different health determinants and their impact on population health.
Evaluate the role of individual behaviors and lifestyle choices in shaping health outcomes and disease risk.
Discuss the importance of addressing social and environmental determinants of health to achieve health equity.
Propose multi-sectoral approaches and interventions to address key health determinants and improve population health outcomes.

pathways.title: Empowering People Through Health Education
pathways.description: To make informed decisions about our own healthcare, we need to understand how our bodies work and the illnesses that can affect them. Unfortunately, diseases can be complicated, and information about them is not always clear or accessible. Dr Susan Creary is a paediatrician from the Hematology, Oncology and Blood and Marrow Transplant (BMT) Clinic at Nationwide Children’s Hospital in Columbus, USA. She and her team have created an education programme to spread awareness and understanding of sickle cell trait and what it means for individuals and families who inherit it.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to explain the difference between sickle cell trait and sickle cell disease.
Describe how sickle cell disease affects the shape of red blood cells and their function.
Discuss the challenges of teaching parents about sickle cell trait and sickle cell disease.
Critically assess the importance of early detection and education in managing sickle cell disease.

pathways.title: Ingenious Solutions for Outwitting Plant Pests
pathways.description: Pests and pathogens can be the bane of plant producers’ lives. Fortunately, there are a range of inventive remedies out there. Dr Elizabeth Lamb, from Cornell University in the US, works closely with growers to find optimal pest control solutions depending on their specific situation, making the most of biological methods such as natural predators.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define key terms related to plant science and integrated pest management (IPM), such as agronomy, entomology, cultivar, biological control, and pathology.
Recall the main components of IPM and its fundamental principles.
Explain the significance of integrated pest management (IPM) in agriculture and horticulture.
Describe the differences between cultural, physical, biological, and chemical methods of pest management.
Evaluate the effectiveness of different pest control methods in various agricultural settings.

pathways.title: How Can Statistical Models Answer Ecology’s Big Questions?
pathways.description: Ecology involves studying the interactions between living things and their environment. These interconnections can be very complex, leading to the creation of some hefty datasets. At the Université de Sherbrooke in Canada, Professor Guillaume Blanchet specialises in building sophisticated statistical models that can analyse these datasets, producing fascinating and unexpected results.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to explain the role of statistical models in quantitative ecology.
Define ecology, ecosystem, endangered species, invasive species, and quantitative ecology.
Summarize the responsibilities and tasks of quantitative ecologists in analyzing ecological data.
Assess the impact of quantitative ecological research on conservation efforts and biodiversity management.

pathways.title: Lunar Leap
pathways.description:
Join forces with Astronaut Alex to conquer a lunar challenge! Design a probe launcher using Newton's Third Law, explore forces and motion, and craft arguments on gravity in this hands-on, space-themed learning journey with the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 6 days of lessons (290 minutes) - experiment, brainstorm, and solve real-world challenges while exploring Newton's Laws of Motion.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 50 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 60 minutes, items 3-4 of the pathway
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3-4, 80 minutes, items 5-6 of the pathway
• EXPLAIN: Day 5-6, 60 minutes, items 7-9 of the pathway
• EXTEND: Day 6, 40 minutes, item 10 of the pathway
Before we embark on our lunar adventure, let’s solidify our foundation with the Lunar Leap: Basics of Forces pathway, ensuring we're fully equipped to tackle the thrilling challenges ahead with Newton's Laws of Motion.
learning_objectives: By journey's end, you'll be able to identify key ideas behind Newton's Third Law and how it helps us shoot objects into the air, like launching probes on the Moon.
Describe how Newton's Third Law works and why it's important for sending things flying, using Astronaut Alex's moon probe challenge as an example.
Use Newton's Third Law in cool experiments and computer activities to see how forces make objects move and interact.
Examine how Newton's Third Law is used in real-life by designing a moon-ready catapult for Astronaut Alex's mission.
Evaluate how well Newton's Third Law works when creating an awesome, imaginary space launcher.
Craft your own space launcher design, putting Newton's Third Law into action for a space-tastic adventure.

pathways.title: Ways of Knowing: How Can Traditional Knowledge Enrich Geoscience Education?
pathways.description: Indigenous communities have inhabited their lands for tens of thousands of years and developed unique connections with their environments, which are passed down through stories containing a deep and intricate understanding of the world. However, western science often ignores this traditional knowledge, marginalising Indigenous scientists and discouraging Indigenous students from pursuing careers in STEM. Dr Judith Brown Clarke, from Stony Brook University, and Dr Wendy K’ah Skaahluwaa Todd, from the University of Minnesota Duluth, USA, hope to increase the participation and retention of Indigenous students in geoscience, by incorporating traditional knowledge in geoscience education and research.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway learners will understand how traditional knowledge, informed by local phenomena, offers a holistic view compared to Western science's focus on individual components.
Learners will evaluate the problems in current geoscience teaching and research methods, including the impact on Indigenous communities and intellectual property.
Learners will evaluate the role of geoscience careers in empowering Indigenous communities, considering the importance of preserving natural resources and advocating for cultural respect.

pathways.title: Conservation Technology and Innovation
pathways.description: Explore our relationship with nature and the balance between technology and sustainability. Let's learn, discuss, and shape a sustainable future together.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the ecological consequences of over-optimization and rebound effects.
Appreciate the concepts of ecological interdependence, human detachment from nature, and the foundations of environmental ethics.
Delve into the intricate balance between technology advancement and sustainable resource usage.

pathways.title: From Platinum to Smartphone: How Math Models Can Be Used to Streamline Supply Chains
pathways.description: Whether it’s the rubber in your Nike trainers, the metals in your iPhone or the sugar in your Coca-Cola, raw materials such as these go through a series of steps in a supply chain to end up in a shop near you. But what happens if one of these steps fails? At Lehigh University, Professor Larry Snyder is using operations research and machine learning to protect these vital supply chains.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway students will be able to describe the types of problems that operations research is used to solve in the fields of disaster relief, healthcare, and airline management.

Students will be able to understand how computers can learn to solve complex problems in machine learning, and identify the advantages of training computers to solve problems over having humans solve them.
Finally, students will be able to explain how operations research can be used in supply chain management to solve problems related to the newsvendor problem and supply chain disruption.

pathways.title: 101 Things to See in Space – And All From Your Living Room
pathways.description: What if we could all explore space like the crew of the Starship Enterprise? The National Schools’ Observatory is giving everyone the chance to do just that with the world’s largest robotic telescope – and it sends images of planets and galaxies to the people who use it
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you should learn about the field of astrophysics
Learn about the opportunities presented by The National School's Observatory
Understand how telescopes function

pathways.title: Building a Camera to See the First Galaxies
pathways.description: As astronomers look deeper into space, they require better instruments to do so. A joint team of scientists and engineers at Cardiff University, UK, and the National Institute of Astrophysics, Optics and Electronics, Mexico, have risen to this challenge. With the new technology in their ground-breaking camera, we could soon be learning more about the formation of the universe’s biggest stars and oldest galaxies.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the challenges associated with designing a camera that measures light at millimetre wavelengths.
You will learn about the potential discoveries that MUSCAT could make and its importance in understanding the Universe.
You will understand why some celestial objects are more difficult to see than others.

pathways.title: Muscles and Soft Tissue of the Shoulder and Arm
pathways.description: Videos detailing the muscles of the shoulder. Watch Bones and Joints of the Shoulder and Girdle to learn more about the shoulder.

pathways.title: Bones and Joints of the Shoulder and Girdle
pathways.description: Videos detailing the bones and joints of the shoulder and girdle. Watch Muscles and Soft Tissue of the Shoulder and Arm to learn more about the anatomy of the arm.

pathways.title: The disaster of negligence coronacrisis in Brazil (3 parts)
pathways.description: In the whole world, Brazil is being recognized as the most problematic country in the combat to COVID-19. This may not be taken as a surprise, since the president himself stimulates the population to get back to the normal life, by saying that COVID-19 is an ordinary flu. Hence, the national government has no coherent policy to cope with the sanitary aspects of the pandemic. The social inequality is amplified the sanitary drama, due to poor conditions of housing and sanitation for a large part of the population. As for the economic dimension, the first response of the Ministry of Economics was simply making pressure for the deepening of the neoliberal economic reforms, in a move that is totally opposed to what is being done in the whole globe. After a lot of hesitation and political pressure from the opposition, a set of economic policies has been approved in the Congress and implemented by the government, avoiding a complete breakdown in the economic activity - especially due to the impact of the emergency income in reducing poverty and avoiding a radical decline in the aggregate demand. But the current government is planning a new fiscal consolidation in 2021, which will limit the capacity of the State to create a new and stronger social policy. This module will therefore discuss the Brazilian situation as a typical case of negligence in a time of crises. It will also discuss the impact of an out-of-date agenda, that together with the negationist posture of the federal government, has put lives and the future of the country at great risk.

pathways.title: Atomic Structure and Models
pathways.description: Atoms are the fundamental building blocks of matter. They are the smallest particles of an element that retain its chemical identity. Each atom consists of a nucleus containing protons and neutrons, surrounded by electrons that orbit in energy levels. Understanding atoms is essential because they form the basis of all chemical reactions and the structure of matter—solids, liquids, and gases.
learning_objectives: Describe the structure of the atom, including the arrangement and properties of protons, neutrons, and electrons.
