Explain how atomic number, mass number, and isotopes relate to the composition of atoms.
Compare and evaluate early atomic models (Rutherford and Bohr), highlighting their contributions and limitations.
Explain electron energy levels and their role in atomic structure and emission spectra.
Describe how attractive and repulsive forces influence atomic interactions and bonding.

pathways.title: The Climate Action Simulation Game: Mastering En-ROADS
pathways.description: The Climate Action Simulation Game using the En-ROADS simulator empowers participants to negotiate climate policies, explore emissions reductions, and experience stakeholder perspectives, building systems thinking and climate leadership skills.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain how different climate policies and actions affect greenhouse gas emissions and global temperature using the En-ROADS simulator.
Analyze stakeholder perspectives and interests in climate negotiations.
Evaluate the effectiveness of policy solutions for emissions reduction and climate resilience.
Collaborate with peers to develop a scenario that addresses climate change while considering equity and economic impacts.

pathways.title: Principles of Data Science: Appendices
pathways.description: Python, R, and Excel are widely used tools in data science for analyzing, visualizing, and modeling data. This pathway introduces learners to essential computational tools, statistical methods, and machine learning algorithms, offering step-by-step examples across probability, regression, and time series forecasting. The four texts form the appendices for the Principles of Data Science textbook.
learning_objectives: Use spreadsheet software to organize, analyze, and visualize data, applying functions and formulas to identify patterns and support evidence-based claims in scientific investigations.

Use a statistical programming language to organize, analyze, visualize, and model data, applying functions and regression techniques to support evidence-based conclusions in scientific investigations.
Apply Python algorithms to collect, analyze, model, and visualize data, supporting evidence-based reasoning in scientific inquiries and decision-making.
Use Python functions and libraries to analyze, visualize, and model data, applying computational tools to support scientific investigations and draw evidence-based conclusions.

pathways.title: Global Information Systems
pathways.description: Global information systems (GIS) play a vital role in supporting multinational business operations across borders. This pathway introduces the foundational concepts of GIS and outlines how globalization and technological advancements have redefined information systems to meet global challenges. This chapter is part of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the role and strategic importance of global information systems in supporting international business operations, data coordination, and decision-making.
Analyze different global business models and logistics frameworks that rely on information systems to manage risks, supply chains, and cross-border activities.
Evaluate how cultural factors influence information system design and team collaboration in global enterprises, including communication styles, management practices, and adoption rates.
Identify and apply key terminology and core concepts related to global information systems, including infrastructure, digital transformation, and governance models.
Interpret case scenarios and assessment questions to apply your knowledge of global information systems in real-world business and technology contexts.

pathways.title: How Do Hurricanes Impact Forest Ecosystems?
pathways.description: The island of Puerto Rico has diverse landscapes, making it a natural laboratory for ecologists. From mountain rainforests to coastal coral reefs, it contains a range of ecosystems that are teeming with life. The Luquillo Long Term Ecological Research Program and Luquillo Experimental Forest are based at El Verde Field Station in El Yunque National Forest. Here, ecologists are studying how and why forest ecosystems are changing, including how they respond to hurricanes and droughts.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will identify the different types of ecosystems found in Puerto Rico, including rainforests, mangrove swamps, and coral reefs.
You will describe the Luquillo Long Term Ecological Research Program and its focus on understanding forest ecosystem responses to disturbances.
You will differentiate between the impacts of natural disturbances and human interference on forest ecosystems.
You will evaluate the importance of biodiversity and ecosystem health for human well-being.

pathways.title: How Are Flexible Electronic Devices Solving Healthcare Challenges?
pathways.description: Advances in healthcare are not only made by healthcare professionals. Led by Dr Radu Sporea, a team of electronics engineers and surgeons at the University of Surrey in the UK and Gachon University in South Korea is developing a wearable electronic device to monitor a patient’s blood flow as they recover from an operation.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key electronics terms such as amplification, flexible electronics, LED, photosensor, semiconductor, and transistor.
You will identify the primary function of a wearable blood flow monitoring device.
You will explain how optical blood flow monitoring works using LEDs and photosensors.
You will summarize the challenges faced by engineers when developing wearable medical devices.

pathways.title: Elephant Ivory Cache
pathways.description: Explore how to integrate ecology, ethics, and biotechnology into a biology classroom by basing a collaborative lab experience on real research on African elephant conservation. Students discover how modern biomolecular research tools can help conserve a species, in this case the African elephant. Students also consider the perspectives of various stakeholders and the historical context of conservation in Africa to better understand the challenges of solving the problem of elephant poaching.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will understand how DNA is inherited over generations, and how minor changes in DNA can be used to differentiate between closely related populations within a species.
Recognize that certain keystone species have a disproportionate impact on other species.
Understand how DNA technology can be used to protect endangered species.
Interrogate scientific evidence and ask critical questions from information provided.

pathways.title: Global Health: Demographics, Disparities, and Equity
pathways.description: This pathway delves into the relationship between world populations and disease, focusing on key demographic indicators and their implications for global health. Learners will evaluate health disparities based on factors like gender, socioeconomic status, and geography. The pathway also emphasizes the importance of addressing the specific health needs of vulnerable populations and proposes strategies for promoting health equity across diverse groups.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe demographic indicators, such as population size, growth rate, and age structure, and their implications for global health.
Analyze the impact of migration, urbanization, and displacement on health outcomes and access to healthcare services.
Evaluate disparities in health outcomes based on factors such as gender, socioeconomic status, ethnicity, and geography.
Discuss the concept of vulnerable populations and the importance of addressing their specific health needs.
Propose strategies for promoting health equity and reducing disparities among different population groups.

pathways.title: The Importance of Memory in Severe Mental Illness
pathways.description: While we all sometimes forget where we put our phone or keys, memory problems can have much more serious consequences. Together with his collaborators, Professor Jack Mellor from the University of Bristol, UK, is investigating how impairments in a person’s ability to form memories can contribute to the symptoms of psychiatric disorders. If they can uncover the biological mechanisms behind conditions such as schizophrenia, they hope to open new opportunities for mental health treatments.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define key terms related to neuroscience, such as neuron, synapse, synaptic plasticity, electrophysiology, and optogenetics.
Recall the role of synaptic plasticity in memory formation and learning.
Explain how memories are encoded in the brain through the connections between neurons.
Use the concepts of synaptic plasticity to predict how a new experience might be encoded as a memory.

pathways.title: From School to University: How Can Students Adapt Better to Change?
pathways.description: Are you worried about leaving school or eager to start on the next chapter of your life? Whether you are anxious about moving away from home or excited to make new friends, times of transition commonly cause a range of emotions. At the University of Waterloo in Canada, psychology researcher Dr Jenna Gilchrist is studying how high school students adapt to life at university, and how techniques such as exercising self-compassion can help them regulate their emotions.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will understand the role of self-compassion in promoting emotional regulation among university students.
Analyze the potential impact of self-compassion on student retention and success in higher education.
Evaluate the importance of emotional well-being in academic adaptation and how it affects dropout rates.

pathways.title: Harnessing the Power of Music to Improve Mental Health
pathways.description: Engaging with music has proven positive impacts on mental health and well-being, yet musical interventions are rarely used in healthcare settings. Professor Gilles Comeau, from the University of Ottawa in Canada, hopes to change this. He has established the Music and Mental Health Research Clinic to explore the relationships between music and mental health and to develop ways of integrating music into healthcare services.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway you will be able to, describe the various benefits of engaging with music on both physical and mental health.
Explain how music engagement affects cognitive function and social cohesion.
Analyze the challenges faced by individuals with mental health issues or physical limitations in accessing musical activities.
Evaluate the impact of music participation programs on the overall well-being of participants, considering both short-term and long-term outcomes.

pathways.title: Simulations
pathways.description:
Where cases present students with relevant situations from the field to apply their learning, simulations take this application a step further. Simulations are active learning exercises that thrust students directly into the action they will likely encounter in the professional world. Interactive, immersive, and intense, simulations present students with realistic situations that push them to enact their learning in context, then manage the consequences of their individual and collective decision-making. And what happens before and after simulations is just as important as what happens during them. Planning simulations and preparing students for them remain critical for the activities to hold relevance and purpose. In addition, debriefing simulations after their enactment creates opportunities for students to deepen their experiences and better position them in conceptual schema. Though designing simulations is challenging and involved work for instructors, their payoff cannot be understated. Purposeful, well-executed simulations deliver students conceptual knowledge through hands-on experience which may prove more memorable than learning material seated at a table or from a textbook.
How do we design simulations that involve many students simultaneously and remain open to multiple solution pathways and/or pitfalls? What is the role of the facilitator in such learning environments? How do we extend and deepen students’ learning beyond visceral simulation activities? In this pathway, Richard Schwartzstein and Brian Mandell discuss how they design, facilitate, and debrief simulations in their respective, field-based classrooms.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to recall the key components and stages involved in designing and implementing educational simulations.
Comprehend the importance of role assignments and how they contribute to the effectiveness of simulation-based learning.
Demonstrate the ability to plan and execute a simulation, incorporating peer feedback and debriefing methods to reinforce the learning experience.
Critically evaluate the outcomes of a simulation to identify areas of success and opportunities for improvement.
Assess the emotional and educational impact of simulations on participants and make informed decisions to enhance future learning experiences.

pathways.title: Modern Convnets
pathways.description: Dive into the realm of modern convolutional neural networks (convnets) and their fascinating applications in AI. Learn from Alexander Ecker as he delves into the intricacies image recognition capabilities, face recognition, and the ethical considerations of artificial intelligence. You will explore the transformative concept of transfer learning and how convnets optimize learning efficiency by reducing parameters.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will gain insight into the core concepts of convolutional neural networks.
You will understand the principles of transfer learning and its significance in deep learning.
You will trace the historical evolution of convolutional neural networks and their transformative impact on AI.
You will investigate the concept of deep networks and their implications for convnet progress.
You will examine the ethical considerations surrounding face recognition technology.

pathways.title: The COVID in India: High on lockdown, low on welfare, and timid on economic stimulus (3 parts)
pathways.description: The COVID-19 crisis caught India unprepared when the first case was detected on 30 January in the state of Kerala. The government took nearly one and a half months to initiate measures to curb the spread of the virus. Clearly, it underestimated the magnitude of an impending crisis. For a long time, the government took a false pride in low number of cases and insisted that community transmission has not taken place. In the hindsight, it clearly appears to be a case of low testing and misplaced confidence. On March 24, Prime Minister Narendra Modi announced a national lockdown for 21 days. This appeared to be a bold and reasonable step at that time. However, the government extended it several times without parallel supporting measures to rescue vulnerable population who lost their livelihood during the lockdown. The lockdown measures were strict and police went overboard to implement them. But the loss of livelihood forced migrant workers to march back to their villages and hometown. Millions of people started walking on highways defying government orders. This was tragic and clearly implied a failure of the government policies. After several lockdown extensions, a fatigue set in among people, and demands for opening up grew all over. The government had no choice but to gradually allow people to travel and businesses to function with social distancing protocols. By January 2021, the cases of COVID dropped significantly in India. The vaccination has also started, and there is a huge relief among the people now. Isn’t it an irony that a national lockdown was announced when there were merely 564 cases, and restrictions were lifted when there are nearly 150,000 confirmed? What does this mean? A failure of the lockdown? Where did the government go wrong? Were there enough supporting economic stimulus and welfare packages for various sections? What is the current status of COVID crisis and how is the government responding to it? How has media reacted to this crisis? How do I see the coming few weeks in India? These are some of the questions that I will try to answer in my short presentation.

pathways.title: Saving Lives with Coding: The Global Impact of an Undergraduate Project
pathways.description: Every piece of digital technology that we use, from smartphones to gaming consoles, relies on code that is written and designed by people. At the University of Florida, USA, Professor Angelos Barmpoutis has developed a course-based undergraduate research experience, or CURE, that gives students the opportunity to design code for digital education resources that are used in schools across the US and beyond.
learning_objectives: Describe what software development is and summarize why coding is increasingly important across disciplines, including AI.
Explain how user-centered design, scalability, and real-world constraints influence software development, using examples from educational app design.
Explain what software development work can look like in interdisciplinary settings and connect career advice from the reading to your own future learning goals.
Apply computational thinking and engineering design practices to build, test, and refine user-centered software using real user feedback.

pathways.title: Animal Collective: How Group Behavior Can Produce Surprising problems...and Solutions!
pathways.description:
Students will draw their own social networks, ponder the meaning of complexity and begin to notice unexpected patterns that emerge around them, unnoticed. Through hands-on activities and digital experiments, students will discover how complexity research enriches our daily lives.
Driving Questions:
• Why do humans and other animals live in groups?
• How do simple behavioral rules lead to emergence of novel properties at the group level? (i.e. How do animal flocks, market trends, and viral videos emerge from animal collective behaviors?)
• How do scientists use computer simulations and other tools to study emergence?
Hooks:
• Creative, art-infused "draw your own social network activity".
• Active movement flocking simulation.
• Mind-blowing interactive simulations of fish schooling and social network dynamics.
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to define complexity in the context of social and natural systems.
Identify key characteristics of emergent properties in biological and social systems.
Recognize how flocking and schooling behaviors emerge from simple rules.
Describe how social networks represent complexity in human interactions.
Explain how emergence arises from simple behavioral rules.
Construct a personal social media network diagram to visualize complexity.
Differentiate between real-world and simulated complexity in nature.

pathways.title: Can Statistics Help to Uncover the Ocean’s Secrets?
pathways.description: There are still many mysteries about the ocean. While this vast store of water is known to support life and help mitigate the impacts of climate change, scientists are constantly making new discoveries about how the ocean functions. At Dalhousie University in Nova Scotia, Canada, Professor Joanna Mills Flemming and her team are developing new statistical methods to study ocean data, uncovering insights into fish populations and how the ocean’s inhabitants will respond to climate change.
learning_objectives: Identify key concepts related to ocean research, such as ocean data, population dynamics, and statistical modeling.
Describe the importance of statistical methods in studying marine ecosystems and their application to real-world ocean-related problems.
Analyze the challenges of ocean data collection due to the ocean's vastness and depth.

pathways.title: Learning Objectives and Sequencing
pathways.description: Refine your teaching skills with effective strategies for crafting engaging lesson objectives and seamless sequencing. This pathway delves into creating clear, measurable objectives and planning lessons with purposeful pacing and transitions to deliver impactful learning experiences tailored to diverse student needs.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will Identify the components of effective learning objectives, including specificity, measurability, relevance, and achievability, for lesson planning.
You will evaluate the pacing and structure of a lesson to ensure clear transitions and engagement throughout the learning process.
You will utilize observable indicators to assess and improve teaching practices related to learning objectives, sequencing, and classroom management.

pathways.title: Lunar Luminescence
pathways.description:
Become a junior scientist and join a space adventure! Help Astronaut Alex unlock the secrets of light in the lunar landscape in this learning pathway, with the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 7 days of lessons (285 minutes) - experiment, brainstorm, and solve real-world challenges while exploring light waves through hands-on activities and simulations.
Pathway breakdown:
• BASICS: Day 1, 10 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• ENGAGE: Day 1-2, 60 minutes, item 2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 75 minutes, items 3-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4-5, 80 minutes, item 5 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 6-7, 60 minutes, item 6 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: 3 Days, 120 minutes, items 7-8 of the pathway and the EXTEND Guide: Harnessing Solar Energy
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will be able to identify factors influencing the behavior of light, such as reflection, absorption, and transmission.
Describe the relationship between material properties and the behavior of light.
Apply knowledge of light interaction to the specific context of lunar materials.
Critically analyze the behavior of light on the lunar surface.
Assess the effectiveness of different materials in influencing the behavior of light.
Construct a predictive model for the behavior of light on the lunar surface.

pathways.title: Lunar Legacy: Cosmic Communications
pathways.description:
Embark on a space science adventure, exploring wave properties, digital signals, and problem-solving to design a lunar communication system with the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 6 days (260 minutes) - experiment, brainstorm, and solve real-world challenges while exploring the differences between analog and digital signals through hands-on activities and simulations.
Pathway breakdown:
• BASICS: Day 1, 30 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• ENGAGE: Day 1-2, 45 minutes, item 2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 3-4, 60 minutes, items 3-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 5-6, 80 minutes, items 5-6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 6, 45 minutes, item 7 of the pathway and the Student Guide.
• EXTEND: Day 7-9, 120 minutes. Learning Pathway: NASA | Designing the James Webb Space Telescope
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the characteristics of waves, such as amplitude, frequency, and wavelength, and their role in transmitting information for space communication.
Understand why digital signals are preferred over analog signals for sending clear and reliable communication in space exploration.
Apply mathematical concepts to represent and analyze wave properties, enhancing comprehension of how waves carry information.
Use knowledge from multiple scientific disciplines to design an effective communication system for lunar missions, addressing real-world space challenges.
Engage in experiments and simulations to explore the practical applications of wave properties and digital signals, fostering critical thinking and technological proficiency.

pathways.title: Space Hydration Station: Designing the Future of Water in Zero Gravity
pathways.description:
Discover how astronauts recycle vital H2O, design your own space-ready filtration system, and unravel the secrets of water flow in the weightlessness of space with this learning pathway and the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days (210-230 minutes).
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2: 40-60 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3: 40 minutes, items 9-11 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5: 40 minutes, found in the Student Guide.
• EXTEND: 30 minutes, item 12 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to collect and analyze data about how humans reuse and treat wastewater to make it drinkable again.
You will demonstrate an understanding of patterns of movement of water in a zero gravity environment.
You will be able to engage in virtual experiments to enhance critical thinking skills.
You will explore the relationships between humans and water.

pathways.title: Lunar Light-Up: Engineering Bright Solutions for the Moon's Dark Poles
pathways.description:
Dive into a space-age quest to bring light to the Moon's shadowy South Pole! Design electrical circuits, master solar tech, and build a bright generator for astronauts on their Artemis adventure. This learning pathway with the accompanying Student Guide has a recommended learning time spanning 5 days of lessons (190 - 210 minutes).
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 40-60 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 9-10 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, found in the Student Guide.
• EXTEND: 10 minutes, item 11 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to make a functional model of a closed circuit.
You will be able to demonstrate understanding of how electrical current and solar panels work.
You will explore the relationships between light energy and electrical circuits.
You will will explore related careers, and ask relevant questions.

pathways.title: Meet the Crew! Why Building Trust With Your Team Is the Key to Success at NASA
pathways.description:
Research the effects of mental health and physical health on humans in space with this pathway and accompanying student guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (180-210 minutes). Students will learn how to collect and analyze data on how space affects humans and engage in virtual experiments to enhance their critical thinking skills. 
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 30-40 minutes, items 3-6 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 7-8 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 30 minutes, item 9 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to collect and analyze data about the effects space travel has on humans.
Students will be able to demonstrate an understanding of the value of communication and how teamwork leads to success.

Students will be able to engage in virtual experiments and collaborative discussion to enhance critical thinking skills, explore the relationships between mental health and human well being, related careers exploration, and ask relevant questions.

pathways.title: How Can Music Technology Improve the Well-Being of People Living With Dementia?
pathways.description: What does music mean to you? Whether you play an instrument or enjoy listening to your favourite band, there are many ways to engage with music. But what happens if you begin to lose your memories of music or ability to play an instrument? Dr Jennifer MacRitchie is a musician and electronic engineer at the University of Sheffield, UK. She is collaborating with researchers Dr Justin Christensen and Jon Pigrem, to develop new digital music technologies to improve music accessibility for people living with dementia.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to: Understand the significance of music in enhancing the well-being of individuals living with dementia.
Learners will be able to gain an understanding of the importance of interdisciplinary collaboration in leveraging technology for musical well-being	.

pathways.title: Environmental Policy and Governance
pathways.description: Uncover the movement for environmental justice, delve into historical climate policy shifts, learn how industry influences science and regulation, and discover the journey of France's energy transition. Explore the origins of sustainability sciences and connect environmental and social concerns. Uncover the movement for environmental justice, delve into historical climate policy shifts, learn how industry influences science and regulation, and discover the journey of France's energy transition.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will learn about the principles of environmental justice, identifying key historical and global examples.
Explore the possibilities of transforming the global financial system to address environmental and economic crises.
Gain insights into France's energy transition journey, comprehending the sufficiency approach, public status complexities, and historical decisions.

pathways.title: Convnets and DL thinking
pathways.description: Explore Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) in image recognition, deep learning, and data science. We will cover the intricacies of convolution, pooling, and hierarchical architectures, as well as the role of cost functions in neural network design. We will also cover the important use of embeddings and loss functions in face recognition.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will have an understanding of neural network architectures, including their role in simplifying complex models and efficiently handling large image datasets.
You will explore convolution, padding, stride, and pooling's impact on translation invariance and feature detection.
You will acquire skills to build hierarchical neural network architectures, using convolution, ReLU, pooling, and fully connected layers.
You will explore neural networks in face recognition, including embeddings, cost functions, and triplet loss functions.

pathways.title: Making Ill Health in Old Age a Thing of the Past
pathways.description: Deborah Dunn-Walters is Professor of Immunology at the University of Surrey. Her research is focused on B cells and the role they play in the immune system, particularly as we age. The findings could lead to improved treatments that enable us to lead healthier lives for longer
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you should understand the basic functions of the immune system
Learn about the work of immunologists like Professor Deborah Dunn-Walters and their contributions to understanding the immune system.

Explore various career paths in immunology, including research, immunotherapy, diagnosis, and management of immune-related diseases, and gain awareness of the potential salary ranges and job prospects in the field.

pathways.title: Lighting up the Quantum Future: From Basic Quantum Science to New Technologies
pathways.description: Join Dr. Thomas Volz from Macquarie University as he explores the behavior of photons, nanodiamonds, polaritons, and spin. Learn about superposition, the foundation of quantum computing, and the study of quantum emitter systems in materials. Explore the applications of quantum light and Dr. Volz's research projects, including his work on advanced optical spectrometers.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the principles of quantum physics and its significance in studying matter at the atomic and subatomic scale.
Explore the behavior and characteristics of quantum emitters and their role in generating quantum light.
Learn about the potential applications of quantum light and its significance in quantum information processing, such as quantum computing and quantum communication.

pathways.title: Water Use in the United States
pathways.description: The USGS produces national estimates of water withdrawal and consumptive water use. Withdrawal estimates are currently being finalized for a 20-year period from 2000 to 2020 for the three largest water use categories nationally (public supply, self-supplied thermoelectric power, and self-supplied crop irrigation). Six additional categories of use (self-supplied industrial, domestic, mining, livestock, aquaculture, and golf irrigation) will be available near the end of 2024.
learning_objectives: Gain a better understanding of how water is used in the United States
Exploring the links and effects between water use and climate change
Study data gathered by the USGS to understand water use over time

pathways.title: NASA | Total Solar Eclipses
pathways.description: Total solar eclipses occur when the Moon goes between the Earth and Sun and blocks the light of the Sun. This rare event is fascinating to watch, but it is important to take safety precautions when doing so. This pathway guides learners to understand the science of solar eclipses and how to be safe while watching them.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will understand what an eclipse is.
Learners will be able to differentiate between different types of eclipses.
Learners will know about the different stages of a total solar eclipse.
Learners will be able to use household materials to model a solar eclipse.

pathways.title: Circulatory System and the Heart
pathways.description: This pathway explores the circulatory system and its individual components. It includes several hands-on activities that provide the opportunity to model the human cardiovascular system, asses factors that affect blood flow rate, and investigate heart rate recovery times.
learning_objectives: Describe the different components of the cardiovascular/circulatory system.
Explain the function and purpose of the circulatory system.
Name at least two factors that affect the blood flow rate.

pathways.title: Environmental Factors Affecting Plant Growth
pathways.description: Explore how key environmental and biological factors influence plant growth and survival through the lens of a botanist. This pathway investigates how light, water, temperature, nutrients, soil composition, and ecological interactions shape plant development.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Explain the role of light on plant growth.
Identify how temperature, moisture, and seasonal changes affect plant physiology and development.
Analyze how nutrients, including macro- and micronutrients, affect plant growth and survival.

pathways.title: Xiao Xiao, PhD - Creative Technologist
pathways.description: Dr. Xiao Xiao is someone who broke the mold and through her career brought together her love for both music and science. She uses art and technology to study the human mind.

pathways.title: Nonvascular and Vascular Plants
pathways.description: Plants can be classified based on whether or not they have complex vascular tissue that is used to transport water and nutrients. Nonvascular plants—liverworts, mosses, and hornworts—do not have complex vascular tissue, while all other plants are known as vascular plants as they have complex vascular tissue. Explore this in more detail by learning about roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and tissues of vascular plants to understand structure-function relationships and classify plant parts. This pathway supports career exploration in botany, specifically plant anatomy and physiology. Ideal for biology, botany, or life science classes.
learning_objectives: Identify and describe key vascular plant organs—roots, stems, leaves, and flowers—and explain how their structures are used to classify plant species.
Differentiate between 2 major plant groups: vascular and nonvascular plants.
Relate structural adaptations in plant organs to their function and habitat, and apply this knowledge to support plant identification in the field.
Demonstrate basic dissection techniques to explore internal and external flower structures.

pathways.title: Projectile Motion
pathways.description: Projectile motion involves analyzing the trajectory of an object under gravity's influence. Understanding how initial velocity, angle, and forces affect motion supports key physics concepts. In this pathway on projectile motion, you will explore the trajectory of objects under gravity's influence. By examining initial velocity, angle, and forces, you will apply key physics principles to analyze and predict motion, solving real-world problems in diverse contexts.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the properties of projectile motion and the impact of gravity, initial velocity, and angle on an object’s trajectory.
Apply kinematic equations and vector analysis to solve real-world problems involving two-dimensional projectile motion.
Analyze the independence of vertical and horizontal motions in projectile scenarios, using vector diagrams and equations.
Predict the outcome of projectile motion under varying conditions, including air resistance, to solve applied physics problems.

pathways.title: Teacher Sensitivity
pathways.description: Teacher sensitivity helps create an inclusive and supportive learning environment. This pathway introduces educators to the principle of teacher sensitivity, shows real-life scenarios of the profound impact it has on teacher-student relationships, academic achievement, and emotional well-being. Through engaging narratives and reflective exercises, you'll better understand what it takes to be an inclusive and engaging educator.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the concept of teacher sensitivity.
You will analyze the effectiveness of teacher sensitivity in fostering a positive learning environment, considering its impact on student behavior, engagement, and peer interactions.

You will evaluate the effectiveness of seating assignments and group work in supporting positive interactions among students from different backgrounds, considering their impact on classroom dynamics and student engagement.

pathways.title: Virus Alarm Game
pathways.description: The Virus Alarm Game takes you on a journey through the world of virology by putting you in the shoes of a city government during the outbreak of a novel disease. In this pathway you will find the necessary activity sheets and game materials to complete these interactive lessons.
learning_objectives: Understand how viruses can spread in a population and cause a pandemic to occur.
Understand the role of epidemiologists in controlling the spread of a virus in a pandemic.
Understand how a pandemic can effect the economy.

pathways.title: Alcohol: A Shot to the Liver
pathways.description:
Embark on an investigative journey into the realm of chemical kinetics with this educational pathway and its accompanying Explore Worksheet and Experiment Worksheet. The curriculum spans 5 days of instruction (285-360 minutes), delving into the effects of temperature, reactant concentration, and catalysts on reaction rates through dynamic simulations and hands-on experiments. Discover the relationship between reaction rates, kinetic energy, and molecular collisions.







Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 60-90 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 45 minutes, items 3-7 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 45 minutes, item 8 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 45-60 minutes, item 9-10 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 90-120 minutes, item 11 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will conduct (and ultimately design) a set of experimental procedures to test the effects of temperature, reactant concentration, and catalyst presence on chemical reaction rates through the use of simulations and hands-on experiments.

Be able to relate chemical reaction rates and the accompanying changes in kinetic energy to the frequency of collisions occurring at the molecular level and the identical amount of energy that is either stored or released through the formation or breaking of chemical bonds involved in these reactions through the use of a simulation.

Be able to apply their knowledge of reaction rates through a study of alcohol and its effects on human liver chemistry, specifically through the context of the liver enzyme catalase and the effects of alcohol concentration on the catalysis reaction of hydrogen peroxide into hydrogen and oxygen.

pathways.title: NASA | Studying Microgravity for Space Exploration
pathways.description: Creating a weightless environment on Earth is one of the primary ways NASA seeks to understand how materials and reactions behave in microgravity, without needing to go into space. In this pathway, you will learn what microgravity is and why understanding its effects is crucial for successful missions into space.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will develop an understanding of what microgravity is.
Gain knowledge of Drop Tower facilities that conduct tests in a microgravity environment.

pathways.title: Community and Global Ecology
pathways.description:
This AP Biology content examines how species interactions, energy flow, and environmental change shape the structure and stability of ecological communities. Learners will explore how competition, predation, symbiosis, and resource availability influence species composition and community dynamics. They will also investigate how environmental pressures, invasive species, and human activities disrupt ecosystems, and how geological and meteorological events drive long-term ecological change. Learners will analyze key patterns in community and ecosystem energy dynamics and predict the effects of interspecific ecological interactions. Coming soon: new interactive content on NPP and energy flow between producers and consumers!
AP® is a registered trademark of the College Board, which has not reviewed this resource.
learning_objectives: Interpret how interactions within and between populations shape community structure, including species composition, diversity, and energy availability.

Explain the relationship between ecosystem diversity and its resilience to changes in the environment, including how the addition or removal of any part of an ecosystem will affect its overall short-term and long-term structure.
Analyze the interaction between the environment and random or preexisting variations in populations.
Describe how invasive species disrupt community and ecosystem dynamics.
Identify human, geological, and meteorological activities that drive changes in ecosystem structures and dynamics.

pathways.title: Review Questions Related To Urogenital System Infections
pathways.description: These questions check for understanding of anatomy & normal microbiota of the urogenital tract; bacterial infections of the urinary system; bacterial infections of the reproductive system; viral infections of the reproductive system; fungal infections of the reproductive system; and protozoan infections of the urogenital system.

pathways.title: The Rockin’ Rock Cycle - Cycling of Materials on Earth
pathways.description:
Discover the dynamic Earth through hands-on experiments and creative storytelling in this learning pathway that is accompanied by a student guide. The activities span 560 minutes over 9 days. Explore the rock cycle, witnessing firsthand how heat, pressure, and erosion shape igneous, metamorphic, and sedimentary rocks. By designing characters and crafting engaging narratives that incorporate key geological processes and vocabulary, gain a deep understanding of Earth's geological history and the forces that transform it.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1: 60 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-4: 200 minutes, item 2 in the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 5-6; 120 minutes, item 3 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 7-8; 120 minutes, items 4-5 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 9; 60 minutes, items 6-9 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, compare and contrast different types of rocks and explain that adding energy, pressure, and water can change these types of rocks into new forms. Describe this process as a continuous cycle.
Model the processes involved in the rock cycle including melting, crystallization, and weathering.

pathways.title: Asexual Reproduction
pathways.description: How do some organisms reproduce without a mate? This learning sequence introduces asexual reproduction and explores its role in maintaining essential species.
learning_objectives: Explain why asexual reproduction results in offspring with identical genetic information.
Compare cell division in prokaryotes and eukaryotes.

pathways.title: Forces
pathways.description: Forces are fundamental to understanding motion and interactions in the physical world. This pathway explores the concept of force, Newton’s laws, torque, and real-world applications in muscles, joints, and everyday movement. Through videos, simulations, and interactive activities, you will visualize and analyze how forces interact, how they affect motion, and how to calculate net force and torque.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define force and explain how it influences motion.
Differentiate between balanced and unbalanced forces and their effects on objects.
Analyze how forces act in one-dimensional motion, including friction, acceleration, and net force.
Apply torque concepts to biological systems, such as muscles and joints.
Interpret free-body diagrams and predict the movement of objects under various forces.

pathways.title: BLOCK 2 - Impacts and structural changes provoked by the Coronacrisis
pathways.description:
Welcome to this first MOOC series on “Rethinking the economy after the coronavirus”. This series has been initiated by the EPOG+ Master programme during the first lockdown period, with the objective of understanding the socio-economic consequences of the COVID and the perspectives for policy makers to reorientate our development model. Taking benefit of a very qualified network of scholars, this MOOC series contains several lectures, with very diversified but totally complementary contents, coping with some of the aspects we collectively understood as vital aspects for the understanding of the current crisis, not only in its surface, but in its structural dimension. First of all, it is crucial to discuss the ecological roots of Coronocrisis. Connected to that, we have lectures highlighting that Coronacrisis is a multidimensional crisis, having deep impacts on the world economy and society, and allowing some scholars to claim that it will possibly provoke structural changes. Finally, it is essential to discuss the economic policies that are being implemented worldwide to face the Coronocrisis. As you can see, it is a very dense and extremely instigating MOOC series and we hope you will enjoy it!
BLOCK 1 - The ecological roots of Coronacrisis
BLOCK 2 – Impacts and structural changes provoked by Coronacrisis
BLOCK 3 – The economy and the coronavirus in the global South
BLOCK 4 – Rethinking economic policy after the coronavirus

pathways.title: Muscular System
pathways.description: Muscle contractions are triggered by calcium ions binding to troponin, enabling myosin to pull actin filaments. Explain the structure and function of skeletal muscles, including muscle types, fascicle arrangement, and the biochemical processes involved in muscle contraction and voluntary movement in the human body.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify major skeletal muscles and describe their specific roles in body movement.
Explain the structure and function of skeletal muscles in relation to the muscular system.
Describe the biochemical and neurological processes involved in voluntary muscle contraction.

pathways.title: COVID-19 Infocomics
pathways.description: This series of infocomics is a collaboration between the SITN Blog and the SITN Graphics team to answer some of the most common questions about COVID-19.
learning_objectives: Examine the features of coronaviruses, including sars-cov-2.
Identify the benefits of hand washing and wearing masks in preventing the spread of covid-19.
Examine how the immune system fights covid-19 infection.

pathways.title: Fuel For Thought: A Gravity Slingshot to Mars and Back
pathways.description:
Explore NASA's plan for deep space exploration beyond the Moon, which will employ the Orion spacecraft, with this pathway accompanied by Explore: A Gravity Slingshot to Mars and Experiment: Gravity and Orbits Lab. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (180 minutes). Utilizing a variety of physical science concepts, from the effects of forces on energy states to factors that affect gravitational forces between objects and how these can be harnessed to perform a maneuver known as a “gravity slingshot” around the Moon, employing its gravitational field to affect the velocity, kinetic energy, and trajectory of your ship in an effort to conserve fuel and avoid a one-way trip to the red planet.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 45 minutes, items 3-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 45 minutes, item 5 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 45 minutes, items 6-9 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 45 minutes, item 10 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to quantify changes in energy that can result from forces exerted between colliding objects, and identify factors (i.e. mass and distance) that affect gravitational forces between object pairs.

Be able to explain how modulations in gravitational forces through mass changes in paired celestial objects (i.e. the Earth and Sun) can cause changes in other parameters in these objects (i.e. velocity and orbital pathway).

Be able to design a device to simulate the effects of gravity between a pair of objects in order to allow one of those objects to reach a desired destination through modulations in velocity and trajectory.

pathways.title: Water Resources
pathways.description: Unit 8 of The Habitable Planet Textbook. Earth's water resources, including rivers, lakes, oceans, and underground aquifers, are under stress in many regions. Humans need water for drinking, sanitation, agriculture, and industry; and contaminated water can spread illnesses and disease vectors, so clean water is both an environmental and a public health issue. In this unit, learn how water is distributed around the globe; how it cycles among the oceans, atmosphere, and land; and how human activities are affecting our finite supply of usable water.

pathways.title: Basic Electronics Circuit Components
pathways.description: Explore basic electronics circuit components and their functions through videos and simulations. Learn to identify resistors, capacitors, transistors, and switches, and construct simple electronic circuits using real-life applications of current, voltage, and resistance in physics.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify and describe basic electronic components such as resistors, capacitors, diodes, and transistors.
Explain the function and working principles of each component in an electronic circuit.
Construct and test basic electronic circuits using simulation tools or practical setups.
Apply Ohm’s Law and concepts of current, voltage, and resistance in designing functional circuits.

pathways.title: Energy Storage in the 21st Century
pathways.description: For nearly three decades, lithium-ion batteries have been the dominant form of rechargeable energy storage. But is that about to change? Prof Jenny Pringle at Deakin University, Australia, and her collaborators at storEnergy are developing the next generation of cleaner and safer batteries.
learning_objectives: In this learning pathway you will investigate and evaluate the advancements and environmental implications of battery technology to support society's transition from fossil fuels to renewable energy sources.
Explore career pathways in materials science, recognizing the role of personal interests and societal needs in shaping scientific research and technology development.

Analyze challenges in the transport industry, understand electrochemical devices and electrolytes, and investigate how batteries function, including the role of current lithium-ion technology and the need for improved energy storage solutions.

pathways.title: The Role of Earthquakes in Burying Carbon for Good
pathways.description: Carbon is the cornerstone of life on Earth and is constantly being transferred between the atmosphere and the Earth’s surface. This carbon cycle is one of Earth’s most important natural processes and continues to surprise us. Professor Robert Hilton of Durham University has been investigating an intriguing carbon cycle “shortcut”: the role of earthquakes in sequestering carbon.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will learn how scientist can use carbon-14 to analyze when sediment was formed.
You will understand how radiocarbon dating can be used to identify when earthquakes happened.
You will learn how scientists are studying the carbon cycle to better understand it.

pathways.title: Earth's Place in the Universe
pathways.description: This core idea focuses on understanding the universe and its temporal and spatial scales. Explaining composition, history of Earth and the universe, and the forces and processes of how the solar system operates are also emphasized in this core idea. This pathway is designed for NGSS standard, MS-ESS1.
learning_objectives: Develop and use a model of the Earth-sun-moon system to describe the cyclic patterns of lunar phases, eclipses of the sun and moon, and seasons. (MS-ESS1-1)
Develop and use a model to describe the role of gravity in the motions within galaxies and the solar system. (MS-ESS1-2)
Analyze and interpret data to determine scale properties of objects in the solar system. (MS-ESS1-3)
Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence from rock strata for how the geologic time scale is used to organize Earth's 4.6-billion-year-old history. (MS-ESS1-4)

pathways.title: Reproductive System
pathways.description: This pathway explores the structures and organs' functions involved in the human reproductive system, bringing an offspring of their kind. Describe the structure and function of the human reproductive system, explain the processes of sexual reproduction and fertilization, and identify key reproductive terms and events.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structures and functions of the male and female reproductive systems.
Explain the processes of gamete formation, fertilization, and early embryonic development.
Analyze the role of hormones in regulating human reproductive cycles and sexual development.

pathways.title: Earth's Systems
pathways.description: This core idea focuses on understanding Earth’s individual systems and how these systems are interrelated causing continual change over time. Explaining Earth’s internal motions and the role of water in the planet’s systems and surface processes are also emphasized in this core idea. This pathway is designed for NGSS standard, MS-ESS2.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Develop a model to describe the cycling of Earth's materials and the flow of energy that drives this process. (MS-ESS2-1)
Construct an explanation based on evidence for how geoscience processes have changed Earth's surface at varying time and spatial scales. (MS-ESS2-2)
Analyze and interpret data on the distribution of fossils and rocks, continental shapes, and seafloor structures to provide evidence of the past plate motions. (MS-ESS2-3)
Develop a model to describe the cycling of water through Earth's systems driven by energy from the sun and the force of gravity. (MS-ESS2-4)
Collect data to provide evidence for how the motions and complex interactions of air masses result in changes in weather conditions. (MS-ESS2-5)
Develop and use a model to describe how unequal heating and rotation of the Earth cause patterns of atmospheric and oceanic circulation that determine regional climates. (MS-ESS2-6)

pathways.title: Mastering Math Concepts: Form 1–3
pathways.description: Master core mathematics concepts from Form 1 to Form 3 with this Ghana Education Service-aligned pathway. Explore number systems, integers, fractions, algebra, functions, geometry, and measurement through interactive simulations and videos. Strengthen problem-solving, logical reasoning, and analytical thinking to excel in the West African Senior School Certificate Examination (WASSCE). Build a strong mathematical foundation for advanced studies in physics, engineering, economics, and data science.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will apply number operations and properties to perform calculations with integers, fractions, and rational numbers in real-life and mathematical contexts.
Manipulate and solve algebraic expressions and equations, including linear and quadratic forms, to model and analyze mathematical relationships.
Represent and interpret functions and patterns using tables, graphs, and symbolic rules to describe relationships between variables.
Use geometric concepts and measurement principles to calculate area, perimeter, and related quantities, demonstrating spatial reasoning and problem-solving skills.

pathways.title: Population Dynamics: A Tale of Mice and Men
pathways.description:
Explore population dynamics through this learning pathway and accompanying student guide. With a recommended learning time of 345 minutes over 8 days, delve into the complex interplay between ecosystems, wildlife, and human health. Explore the principles of limiting factors, carrying capacity, and population dynamics and investigate how manipulating different factors impacts the relationship between white-footed mice and ticks carrying Lyme disease, and, ultimately, the transmission of the disease to humans.
Pathway breakdown:








• ENGAGE: Day 1; 45 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-4; 135 minutes, items 2-6 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 5-6; 90 minutes, items 7-8 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 7; 45 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 8; 30 minutes, items 9-10 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define basic concepts of population dynamics.
Differentiate and identify examples of density-dependent and density-dependent limiting factors on population growth.
Explain ways in which populations of different species, including humans, are interconnected.
Hypothesize about how changes in different ecological factors might impact populations within an ecosystem.
Describe how knowing about population dynamics helps experts manage the needs of wildlife and citizens living in the same space.

pathways.title: Matter and its Interactions
pathways.description: This core idea focuses on understanding the types of atoms present and the interactions between and within atoms. Explaining and predicting the states, properties, and reactions of matter are also emphasized in this core idea. This pathway is designed for NGSS standard, MS-PS1.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Develop models to describe the atomic composition of simple molecules and extended structures. (MS-PS1-1)
Analyze and interpret data on the properties of substances before and after the substances interact to determine if a chemical reaction has occurred. (MS-PS1-2)
Gather and make sense of information to describe that synthetic materials come from natural resources and impact society. (MS-PS1-3)
Develop a model that predicts and describes changes in particle motion, temperature, and state of a pure substance when thermal energy is added or removed. (MS-PS1-4)
Develop and use a model to describe how the total number of atoms does not change in a chemical reaction and thus mass is conserved. (MS-PS1-5)
Undertake a design project to construct, test, and modify a device that either releases or absorbs thermal energy by chemical processes. (MS-PS1-6)

pathways.title: Solving Quadratic Equations
pathways.description: Learn to solve quadratic equations using factoring, graphing, completing the square, and the quadratic formula, and apply these methods to real-world problems involving motion, geometry, and business.
learning_objectives: Identify and classify quadratic equations in standard form and recognize their key features in equations and graphs.
Solve quadratic equations using factoring, completing the square, and the quadratic formula.
Interpret the graph of a quadratic function to find roots, vertex, and axis of symmetry.
Apply quadratic equations to real-life contexts, including motion and geometry problems.

pathways.title: Using Agrobiology to Ensure Sustainable Food Production
pathways.description: Dr Valeria Faggioli and Dr Mayra Osorio are working on separate projects under the sustainable food production branch of the CABANA programme, which aims to accelerate the implementation of data-driven biology to overcome a range of environmental challenges in Latin America.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand more about the importance of bioinformatics in investigating sustainable food production.
You will grasp the impact of human activities, both direct and indirect, on the natural world.

pathways.title: Blood Circulation System
pathways.description: Explore the human circulatory system by studying blood vessels, blood flow, blood pressure, and capillary exchange. Understand how vascular regulation maintains homeostasis, a key outcome for high school biology and physiology learning.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the structure and function of human blood vessels and circulatory pathways.
Explain the mechanisms of blood flow, blood pressure, resistance, and capillary exchange.
Analyze homeostatic regulation of the vascular system.
Compare circulatory adaptations across different animals and stages of human development, including fetal circulation.

pathways.title: Exploring Photosynthesis
pathways.description: Chloroplasts in plant cells convert sunlight into chemical energy through photosynthesis, producing glucose and oxygen. Explore the stages of photosynthesis, focusing on light-dependent reactions and the Calvin cycle. Interact with different media, experiment in virtual labs, and demonstrate understanding through assessments.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the role of chloroplast structures, including thylakoids, grana, and stroma, in capturing and converting light energy during photosynthesis.
Differentiate between light-dependent and light-independent (Calvin cycle) reactions, explaining how each contributes to the production of glucose and oxygen.
Investigate how environmental factors such as light intensity and distance from a light source affect the rate of photosynthesis, using experimental evidence from bean and Elodea plant studies.
Explain the relationship between photosynthesis and plant growth, including the effects of light availability on plant development and energy transformation.
Analyze the interdependence between plants and other organisms through the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide in ecosystems.

pathways.title: The Economy and the Coronavirus - The Case Of India
pathways.description:
The pathway adresses various challenges for the economy is facing in times of coronovirus. It is also discussing the perspectives for an post-coronavirus economy.
This sub-pathway deals in particular with India.

pathways.title: Why Do Cities Experience Urban Heat Islands?
pathways.description: This pathway is focused on energy and how it relates to earth’s system. It is centered around an anchoring phenomenon known as Urban Heat Islands where factors cause areas of a city to undergo differential heating. Students have a chance to look at data from their neighborhood to generate questions related to why some areas have higher land surface temperature than others. Students are led through lessons where they are able to answer questions that will deepen their understanding of why this phenomenon occurs. Additionally, students will continuously revisit an “Earth scene model” to understand how Earth’s systems relate to the phenomenon.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will remember key concepts such as urban heat islands, albedo, and heat capacity.
Understand how solar radiation and energy transfer influence temperature differences across city environments.
Apply data from local neighborhoods to explain variations in land surface temperature.
Analyze the effects of different materials, colors, and urban design on heat absorption and retention.
Evaluate possible solutions, including vegetation, reflective materials, and city planning, to mitigate urban heat island impacts.

pathways.title: Motion and Stability: Forces and Interactions
pathways.description: This core idea focuses on understanding forces between objects and explaining how their motions change. Explaining and predicting stability or instability in systems at various scales are also emphasized in this core idea. This pathway is designed for NGSS standard, MS-PS2.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Apply Newton’s Third Law to design a solution to a problem involving the motion of two colliding objects. (MS-PS2-1)
Plan an investigation to provide evidence that the change in an object’s motion depends on the sum of the forces on the object and the mass of the object. (MS-PS2-2)
Ask questions about data to determine the factors that affect the strength of electric and magnetic forces. (MS-PS2-3)
Construct and present arguments using evidence to support the claim that gravitational interactions are attractive and depend on the masses of interacting objects. (MS-PS2-4)

Conduct an investigation and evaluate the experimental design to provide evidence that fields exist between objects exerting forces on each other even though the objects are not in contact. (MS-PS2-5)

pathways.title: NASA | What is Biophysics?
pathways.description: Biophysics is a cross-disciplinary field that explores how the application of physics can be used to understand biological systems. In this pathway, you'll discover what biophysics is and explore examples of NASA's research in this area.
learning_objectives: Build a foundational understanding of biophysics.
Explore key biophysics research conducted by NASA and its applications in space.

pathways.title: Uncovering the Secrets of the Amazon
pathways.description: Dr Guilherme Oliveira, based at the Vale Institute of Technology in Brazil, leads a team working with Amazonian biodiversity. Together, they conduct genomic analysis on plants, animals and microorganisms in a habitat that is extremely diverse and still relatively unknown.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand how DNA barcoding and eDNA is used to identify and protect biodiversity.
Learn the importance of the roles of environmental genomics and bioinformatics in collating knowledge of organisms and ecosystems.

pathways.title: NASA | Biomechanics
pathways.description: In microgravity, astronauts experience significant bone loss due to reduced mechanical loading on the skeleton. This phenomenon highlights the importance of biomechanics—the study of how muscles and bones interact to support, move, and protect the body. In this pathway, students explore the structure and function of the musculoskeletal system, and how research in extreme environments like space is advancing our understanding of human health on Earth and beyond.
learning_objectives: Describe how bones and muscles work together to produce movement and maintain posture in the human body.
Explore the effects of microgravity on the human body’s structure, including changes in bones and muscles during spaceflight.
Evaluate countermeasures for preserving bone and muscle health in microgravity using scientific evidence.

pathways.title: NASA | Elements of Webb
pathways.description: Welcome to 'Elements of Webb,' an illuminating exploration of NASA's James Webb Space Telescope (JWST). Journey into the groundbreaking engineering and intricate materials that power the JWST. Delve into the telescope's extraordinary design, driven by scientific precision. From the remarkable beryllium mirrors to the intricacies of gold coatings, we journey into the heart of a Utah mine to unravel the rarity of these materials. Hosted by Sophia Roberts, this program presents material science.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the basic features of the James Webb Space Telescope, its size, and compare it with its predecessor, the Hubble Space Telescope.
You will explain the importance of gold-coated mirrors in enhancing infrared observations.
You will describe the characteristics and importance of beryllium in the James Webb Space Telescope.
You will explain how the James Webb Space Telescope employs spectroscopy for scientific investigations.

pathways.title: NASA | Metals
pathways.description: Metals build our world—and launch us beyond it. Every rocket, rover, and robot starts with the right material. Explore how atomic bonding, microstructure, and alloying determine the strength and behavior of metals like aluminum and steel. Discover key applications of metals and how innovations in 3D printing and smart alloys are enabling self-healing structures and high-performance systems for space, aviation, and other extreme environments.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: Explain how atomic bonding, microstructure, and alloying affect metal properties.
Compare the mechanical behavior of metals and ceramics under stress.
Describe alloy types, structures, and their applications.
Explain how 3D printing and smart alloys enable advanced materials for extreme environments.
Evaluate NASA’s new materials for improving spacecraft and aircraft durability.

pathways.title: NASA | Colloids
pathways.description: Explore NASA’s Advanced Colloids Experiment (ACE) and Preliminary Advanced Colloids Experiment (PACE) to study the unique properties of colloids in microgravity, driving innovations in space exploration and advancing our understanding of their applications in research.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define colloids, and differentiate them from solutions and suspensions.
Explain key properties of colloids, including the Tyndall effect.
Develop an understanding of the various experiments conducted at NASA that study colloids, and analyze the aaplications of colloids in space exploration.

pathways.title: Channel Islands
pathways.description: This pathway will equip you with the knowledge of the native flora and fauna that can be found on the channel islands, and the vital role they play in the island’s ecosystems.
learning_objectives: Learn more about the endangered Island Bush-Mallow.
Learn more about the feral horses you will find on Santa Rosa Island.
Learn more how the Island Phacelia, a tiny flower found only on Santa Rosa and San Miguel Islands, is being threatened by invasive grasses.
Learn more about how scientists are studying how the native plants of the Channel Islands play an important role in the water cycle of the island.

pathways.title: Reproduction and Reproductive System
pathways.description: The reproductive system is a biological system responsible for producing, nurturing, and transporting reproductive cells necessary for the continuation of species. In humans, the reproductive system differs between males and females, each with specialized organs and functions. The male reproductive system primarily produces and delivers sperm, while the female reproductive system produces eggs, facilitates fertilization, and supports fetal development. Understanding the reproductive system is essential for comprehending human development, reproductive health, and the role of hormones in sexual maturation and function.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able identify and describe the structure and function of the male and female reproductive organs.
Explain the process of fertilization, including the role of gametes and reproductive hormones.
Discuss common reproductive health issues and the importance of reproductive hygiene.

pathways.title: Coping With an Ecological Systemic Crisis (by the AFD)
pathways.description: The second lecture will propose a prospective stance. Keeping the objectives of the Paris Agreement under reach amounts to a complete retrofit of past mitigation and adaptation strategies. If the crisis itself generated an unprecedented reduction in GHGs, to date, most of these exit policies are cementing a highly – and old -- carbonated productive sector, as was observed in 2008 in the aftermath of the financial crisis. On the other hand focusing on GHG emission reductions only to cope with the Paris Agreement without touching the financial and economic institutions at the heart of the fossil economy bears the risk of social unrest. Avoiding the next crisis of the Anthropocene imposes to navigate between these two reefs.

pathways.title: Mastering Thermal Energy Management with Chemistry
pathways.description:
Figure out how to regulate thermal temperature through chemical reactions in this learning pathway, with the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 8 days of lessons (290 minutes) - experiment, brainstorm, and solve real-world challenges while exploring chain reactions, energy forms, and energy changes through hands-on activities and simulations.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 60 minutes, items 2-4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4-5, 80 minutes, items 5-6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 6, 45 minutes, items 7-8 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 7-8, 60 minutes, item 9 of the pathway.
Before we begin, let's prepare with "Chemical Reactions and Energy Essentials" to make sure we're ready take our chemistry knowledge to the next level!
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will be able to explain what bond energy is and why it's important for figuring out if a reaction gives off heat (exothermic) or soaks it up (endothermic).
Tell apart reactions that warm things up from those that cool things down.
Guess what will happen to energy when chemical bonds break or form.
Check out how well energy moves around in our pretend experiments and think of ways to make it better.
Come up with cool ideas to make energy change from one form to another in the best way possible.

pathways.title: Case-Based Collaborative Learning
pathways.description:
Learning conceptual material in the abstract works for some students. For others, traditional lectures and textbook learning can be alienating. By contrast, Case-Based Collaborative Learning (CBCL) provides a structured, student-centered approach to learning challenging material within realistic scenarios from the field. In the CBCL classroom, students identify and wrestle with concepts as they appear in reality. In the true-to-life, well-crafted case, these concepts may be buried amid relevant and/or irrelevant details. In the field, concepts rarely surface in “textbook” form, and problems of practice are increasingly confronted by teams of professionals who bring different levels of expertise to a given situation. Accordingly, Case-Based Collaborative Learning presents student groups with relevant field-specific puzzles and affords them a clear structure for solving these puzzles. Students work through complex material together, apply the concepts they gleaned individually the night before, and reach new understandings as a group. 
How do we craft cases and structure students’ experiences of them in a way that avoids straightforward answers, invites debate, and encourages collaboration? Given that cases are likely to stimulate many questions from students, how do we help them to progress in a case without quelling their curiosity and enthusiasm? How do we foster safe learning environments that value students’ diverse academic backgrounds and afford them the flexibility to wrestle with course material in context? In these videos, featured instructor Dr. Barbara Cockrill discusses her approach and strategies for using case-based collaborative learning with first-year medical students.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will interpret the significance of presenting material in a realistic context and explain how CBCL facilitates learning through engagement with field-specific scenarios.
Apply strategies for crafting cases that encourage debate and collaboration without providing straightforward answers, in order to stimulate student curiosity and active participation.
Evaluate different approaches to maintaining student enthusiasm and inquiry during case discussions, while also guiding them towards meaningful progress in understanding complex material.

Design a CBCL session that incorporates diverse student backgrounds, fosters a safe learning environment, and enables students to apply concepts to practical situations, reflecting the collaborative nature of problem-solving in professional settings.

pathways.title: Farming on Permafrost
pathways.description: Climate change is creating new opportunities for agriculture, by providing longer and warmer growing seasons, and thawing frozen ground. But farmers in permafrost regions face many challenges, including subsidence in their fields. Dr Melissa Ward Jones, a permafrost geomorphologist at the University of Alaska Fairbanks, USA, has established Permafrost Grown, a research project investigating the interactions between permafrost and agriculture.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway learners will be able to understand and define the concept of permafrost, including its characteristics and the conditions that lead to its formation.
Learners will be able to evaluate the impact of agriculture on permafrost thaw, considering the clearing of boreal forests and the use of farming practices that warm the soil.
Learners will be able to identify the key components of permafrost studies, including ground ice distribution and soil properties during thawing.
Learners will be able to define a food system and its diverse components, from farming to disposal.
