pathways.title: NASA | Launch of the James Webb Space Telescope
pathways.description: Explore the James Webb Space Telescope's remarkable journey, from conception to launch and arrival at Lagrange point 2. Uncovering the endeavor's global collaboration, cutting-edge technology, and profound contributions to astrophysics.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the importance of launch vehicles and launch site selection in space missions.
Have gained insight into the final stages of spacecraft preparation for space missions; the clean room environments and protective measures.
Understand why the James Webb Space Telescope has unique orbit around the second Lagrange point (L2).
Design and build an air-powered rocket and learn about the role rockets play in space exploration.

pathways.title: Atmospheric Pollution
pathways.description: In this pathway, learners identify patterns and predict trends in atmospheric pollution using graphs and tables. They explore how data communicates environmental concepts, including types of air pollution, impacts on ecosystems and human health, changes over time, and the testing hypotheses about the causes and consequences of pollution and climate change.
learning_objectives: Identify the sources and effects of atmospheric pollution.
Construct graphs to visualize data reporting the change in atmospheric pollution over time.
Formulate and test hypotheses about how atmospheric pollution will change over time and the impact this will have on ecosystems and populations.

pathways.title: Fundamentals of Physics: Forces, Waves, and Circuits
pathways.description: Explore the fundamental principles of physics, from forces and motion to waves, electricity, and gravity. This pathway provides a quick yet comprehensive look at how matter, energy, space, and time interact—unraveling the science behind everyday life and the universe itself.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the fundamental principles of forces, motion, and energy in everyday life.
Explain how Newton’s Laws of Motion and gravity influence the movement of objects.
Analyze the behavior of waves, light, and sound, including reflection, refraction, and wave interference.
Investigate the relationship between voltage, resistance, and current in an electric circuit using Ohm’s Law.
Apply the scientific method to design and interpret experiments that test physical laws and principles.
Explore how physics concepts such as projectile motion, pendulum motion, and energy conservation shape our understanding of the universe.

pathways.title: Magnetic Mysteries: Basics of Magnetism
pathways.description:
Explore the invisible forces of magnetism with our learning pathway. Understand magnets, their interactions, basic physics principles, Earth's magnetic field, and material properties to navigate magnetic challenges in science and lunar exploration.
After mastering these magnetic concepts, you'll be ready to tackle the "Magnetic Poles Mystery on the Moon: Protecting Lunar Base Equipment" pathway, where we'll apply what you've learned to real-world scenarios on the Moon!
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify and describe the properties and types of magnets and their uses.
Explain the principles of magnetic attraction and repulsion between different materials.
Apply Newton's Laws of Motion to understand the effects of magnetic forces on objects.
Describe the origin and significance of Earth's magnetic field and its effects on technology and navigation.
Classify materials based on their magnetic properties and interactions with magnetic fields.
Predict the behavior of magnetic materials in various practical and experimental scenarios.

pathways.title: How Do Bacteria Help and Harm Crops?
pathways.description: Over half of the world’s population depends on rice as a staple food. However, some bacteria cause diseases that can drastically reduce rice harvests, potentially leading to famine. Meanwhile, other bacteria are beneficial for soybean plants, as they help produce important nutrients. Understanding how bacteria interact with plants is, therefore, essential for preventing plant disease and increasing crop yields. This is why, at Louisiana State University in the US, Professor Jong Hyun Ham is studying the impacts of bacteria on rice and soybean.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway learners will be able to identify the significance of rice and soybean as crucial global crops.
Learners will recognize the impact of microbial interactions on plant health and understand the harm caused by Burkholderia glumae to rice crops.
Learners will explain the global importance of plant pathologists in addressing issues like food security and safety.

pathways.title: How Are Advances in Technology Improving Dietary Research?
pathways.description: To uncover how diet influences health, dietitians and nutritionists need easy but accurate ways to assess what people eat. However, current methods of dietary assessment are either time-consuming to record or rely on participant memory for recalling what they eat. To overcome these challenges, dietitians Professor Deborah Kerr and Associate Professor Carol Boushey have teamed up with computer engineer Associate Professor Fengqing (Maggie) Zhu. By combining their skills and expertise, they are developing new technologies to improve dietary assessment.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will be able to comprehend the interdisciplinary approach involving dietitians, nutrition scientists, and computer engineers to address these challenges.
You will analyze the advantages and disadvantages of the TADA system in dietary assessment.
You will understand the collaborative nature of interdisciplinary research in the field of dietetics and technology.

pathways.title: The Pandemic as Structural Crisis (“Virus Economics”) (Course and Evaluation)
pathways.description: The pandemic requires each country of the planet to reorganize its society in terms of quadrangular balancing acts between virus spread containment, public-health capacity, macro-economic stabilization policies, and business survival amidst depressed levels of activity, a process we may term virus economics. These inter-twined balancing acts of virus economics stress-test capitalism on the micro-, meso- (sectoral), macro-, and meta- (global) levels like no other event has in decades, a unique structural crisis. Various heterodox approaches in economic theory, notably the French Régulation Theory (“théorie de le régulation”), provide useful tools and concepts to analyze structural crises and put them in a historic context. Reconnecting RT theory back to its Marxist and (Post-)Keynesian roots allows us to frame the pandemic, with its balancing acts of virus economics, as the end of an era – the neo-liberal era of finance-led capitalism rooted in the dollar standard and US hegemony. Several scenarios going forward are possible, depending on politics and policy on a global scale.

pathways.title: The Economy and the Coronavirus - The Case Of Latin American Countries
pathways.description:
The pathway adresses various challenges for the economy is facing in times of coronovirus. It is also discussing the perspectives for an post-coronavirus economy.
This sub-pathway deals in particular with Latin American countries.

pathways.title: Establishing Inclusivity and Belonging
pathways.description: Build a brave and welcoming classroom community. Glean insights from inclusive classroom strategies: building rapport, setting and modeling norms, valuing students' strengths, encouraging diverse interactions, and fostering trust through consistent teamwork.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to build a positive atmosphere by connecting through student interests, appreciating their knowledge, and maintaining open communication.
Create a friendly learning space with inclusive language, recognizing students' contributions, and reducing barriers for risk-taking.
Encourage an inclusive classroom by setting collaborative goals that lead to challenging discussions, personal growth, and academic learning.
Set clear rules on day one to foster an inclusive space where students feel valued and responsible.
Promote inclusivity by publicly empowering students for their diverse strengths, be it academic or social.
Encourage diversity and collaboration by mixing students from varied backgrounds, fostering trust, and valuing everyone's input.
Ensure safety and collaboration with stable small groups, trust-building, open discussions, and a secure learning environment.

pathways.title: Can Gardening Help Children Grow?
pathways.description: Gardens provide practical learning sites and opportunities for students to improve their social competencies and emotional well-being. Garden-based therapy and garden-based learning have been proven effective in reducing stress levels and encouraging physical exercise, engaging students in practical outdoor projects, and deepening their learning. Explore the benefits and applications of incorporating a garden in the educational sphere.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you will better understand the benefits of school gardens as a tool for garden-based therapy and learning.
Evaluate the potential impact of school gardens on student well-being.
Identify the unique characteristics of the Sonoran Desert in Arizona, USA.

pathways.title: Hydroelectricity
pathways.description: So just how do we get electricity from water? Read on to learn about the process, it's advantages, and disadvantages.
learning_objectives: Explore the advantages and disadvantages of hydroelectric power
Gain an understanding of how hydroelectric power works

pathways.title: Wade Campbell - Archaeologist
pathways.description: Wade Campbell is an archaeologist, teacher, and researcher. One of his research areas of interest is the history of the American Southwest, where he studies records, land, and artefacts to better understand the interactions between the Navajo, Spanish, and other groups that lived in the area.

pathways.title: What Lessons Can We Learn From Past Pandemics?
pathways.description: The mortality rate of the 1918 influenza pandemic was several times higher than for COVID-19. At the University of Missouri, USA, Taylor van Doren and Professor Lisa Sattenspiel are investigating the impacts of the 1918 pandemic in Newfoundland, Canada, from a biological anthropology perspective. Their research sheds light on the significant role that social inequalities play in determining the outcomes of a pandemic.
learning_objectives: After completing the pathway, you will be able to analyze the role of social inequalities in influencing mortality outcomes during pandemics.
You will understand the interdisciplinary nature of biological anthropology and its application in understanding the interaction between biology and culture in the context of infectious diseases.

pathways.title: Energy Flow in An Ecosystem
pathways.description: Discover how ecosystems sustain themselves through the continuous flow of energy and cycling of nutrients. Explore trophic levels, food chains, and ecological pyramids to understand how producers, consumers, and decomposers interact to maintain ecological balance and energy transfer within the environment.
learning_objectives: Describe the flow of energy and cycling of nutrients in an ecosystem.
Identify and classify organisms according to their trophic levels in food chains and food webs.
Explain how energy is transferred and lost between trophic levels using ecological pyramids.
Analyze feeding relationships within an ecosystem to show interdependence among organisms.

pathways.title: The Role of Chemistry in Renewable Energy
pathways.description: Using just over 1% of the Sahara Desert for solar panels could power the world. This pathway explores renewable energy technologies, electricity grids, and storage systems, showing how they reduce carbon emissions and promote sustainable development.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the importance of renewable energy in fighting climate change.
Explain how solar energy is captured using photovoltaic cells and chemistry's role in improving them.
Describe the processes for producing biofuels, like fermentation and transesterification.
Identify methods for producing hydrogen and its significance as a clean energy source.
Discuss the importance of energy storage solutions, especially batteries, for renewable energy.
Explain carbon capture and utilization and its role in reducing emissions.
Recognize the links between chemistry and renewable energy technologies.
Evaluate the benefits and challenges of adopting renewable energy solutions.

pathways.title: Types of Motions
pathways.description: Motion is all around us, from the path of a thrown ball to the spinning of a wheel. This pathway explores different types of motion, including projectile motion, rotational motion, angular momentum, and torque. Through videos, texts, and glossaries, you will learn the fundamental concepts governing motion, how forces influence movement, and how different equations describe motion in two and three dimensions.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to differentiate between different types of motion, including projectile and rotational motion.
Analyze the forces that influence rotational motion, including rotational inertia and torque.
Apply kinematic equations to describe projectile motion and rotational kinematics.
Understand angular momentum and its conservation in various physical systems.
Interpret real-world applications of motion, from spinning objects to moving projectiles.

pathways.title: Using Community Data and Analytics to Build Resilience to Flooding
pathways.description: As the impacts of climate change worsen and urbanisation continues, flooding is becoming an increasingly serious problem for communities around the world. Professor João Porto de Albuquerque, at the University of Glasgow, UK, is leading the Waterproofing Data project, empowering communities in Brazil and helping them better predict and prepare for flooding events.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway students will be able to explain the importance of community engagement in generating data for flood early-warning systems.

Describe the impacts of digital technology on data collection and communication in marginalized neighborhoods, and discuss the potential of citizen science in improving community resilience to natural hazards.

Analyze and evaluate the vulnerability of urban regions to flooding, apply methods to reduce flood risk, and demonstrate practical skills in data collection and visualization using GIS software and rain gauge measurements.

pathways.title: Waves and their Applications in Technologies for Information Transfer
pathways.description: This core idea focuses on understanding how waves transfer energy from place to place without the overall displacement of matter. Explaining mechanisms for the transfer of energy and transmitting and storing information via waves are also emphasized in this core idea. This pathway is designed for NGSS standard, MS-PS4.
learning_objectives: Use mathematical representations to describe a simple model for waves that includes how the amplitude of a wave is related to the energy in a wave. (MS-PS4-1)
Develop and use a model to describe that waves are reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through various materials. (MS-PS4-2)
Integrate qualitative scientific and technical information to support the claim that digitized signals are a more reliable way to encode and transmit information than analog signals. (MS-PS4-3)

pathways.title: Studying Earthquakes to Save Lives
pathways.description: Earthquakes occur often, and they can wreak havoc on infrastructure such as buildings and roads. Of course, earthquakes are a natural occurrence, but how can city planners and construction companies prepare for them and minimise their impact? Dr Alex Copley from the University of Cambridge thinks a deep understanding of earthquakes, both past and present, is key
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you should understand the causes and effects of earthquakes
Learn about he Earth's structure and the concept of tectonic plates
Learn about the importance of earthquake monitoring.
Understand the value of historical data in predicting potential future earthquakes.

pathways.title: Earth and Human Activity
pathways.description: This core idea focuses on understanding human interactions with Earth. Explaining how natural resources and natural hazards affect people and how humans affect Earth’s processes are also emphasized in this core idea. This pathway is designed for NGSS standard, MS-PS3.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Construct a scientific explanation based on evidence for how the uneven distributions of Earth's mineral, energy, and groundwater resources are the result of past and current geoscience processes. (MS-ESS3-1)
Analyze and interpret data on natural hazards to forecast future catastrophic events and inform the development of technologies to mitigate their effects. (MS-ESS3-2)
Apply scientific principles to design a method for monitoring and minimizing a human impact on the environment. (MS-ESS3-3)
Construct an argument supported by evidence for how increases in human population and per-capita consumption of natural resources impact Earth's systems. (MS-ESS3-4)
Ask questions to clarify evidence of the factors that have caused the rise in global temperatures over the past century. (MS-ESS3-5)

pathways.title: Electric Crystals: Exploring How Crystal Symmetry Can Power Everyday Life
pathways.description:
Crystals aren't magic, but they are amazing! In this student-driven, lecture-free lesson, students will do card sorts, build hands-on models, solve engineering design puzzles, and more! The whole experience is connected through a beautifully crafted comic and supporting videos.
Driving Question(s):
• What are crystals and how do they form?
• How is molecular microstructure connected to functionality and properties we can easily observe?
Essential Question(s):
Why are abstract concepts like geometry important for “real life”?
Hook(s):
An engaging, original comic weaves together background information, hands-on activities, puzzles, and problem sets. Quick videos stoke curiosity and provide support for activities.
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to define the characteristics of crystalline solids, including the concept of the repeating "unit cell"
Construct a model of a table salt crystal to illustrate the concept of a unit cell.
Differentiate between crystalline and amorphous solids based on structure and properties.
Assess the implications of crystal structure in relation to material behavior and performance.
Interpret how microscopic structures influence macroscopic properties in solid materials.

pathways.title: Mastering Motion: Newton’s Laws Unveiled
pathways.description: Newton's laws of motion comprise three fundamental laws that outline the relationship between the forces applied to an object and the motion of that object. Apply Newton’s laws of motion and universal gravitation to analyze forces, predict motion, and explain real-world phenomena from everyday mechanics to astronomical systems.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe Newton’s three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation, with examples from everyday life, technology, and space.
Use Newton’s laws to study forces like gravity, friction, tension, and acceleration, and predict how objects will move.
Read and create free-body diagrams, motion graphs, and simulations to show how forces affect velocity, acceleration, and balance.
Explore real-world and space examples—such as rockets, airplanes, runaway stars, and orbits—using Newton’s laws and universal gravitation.
Think critically about scientific claims and solve problems in mechanics through observation, prediction, and reasoning.

pathways.title: The Fastest Electric Motor Ever Is Another Step Towards a Sustainable Future
pathways.description: Professor Rukmi Dutta and her team at the University of New South Wales, Australia, have developed a new electric motor that runs at high speeds and uses less power, which ultimately means less pollution.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you will better understand and define the key terminology of electrical engineering.
Understand what an electrical motor is and how it functions.
Learn more about the benefits that electrical engineering can offer to the world.

pathways.title: Yellowstone
pathways.description: Explore Yellowstone in this collection of videos discussing the geographic features that make Yellowstone a volcano. Discover how scientists go about studying Yellowstone's past and monitoring its present.
learning_objectives: Discover why scientists classify Yellowstone as a volcano.
Learn and understand how scientists monitor volcanic activity at Yellowstone.
Learn more about past volcanic eruptions at Yellowstone.

pathways.title: Hearing Health: The Fundamentals
pathways.description:
The World Health Organization estimates that 2.5 billion people will experience hearing loss by 2050. This pathway introduces how the ear detects and converts sound, outlining core structures such as the outer ear, ossicles, cochlea, and auditory nerve. You will also learn about degrees of hearing loss, key causes, treatment options, ear-health considerations, noise-related risks, and the developmental and social impacts on children.
Acknowledgments
Thank you for the invaluable contributions of our board members, partners, and colleagues in the development of this educational material: Dr. Marlene Bagatto, Ora Buerkli, Dr. Patricia Castellanos de Muñoz, and Dr. Diego Santana-Hernández.
Research and Script: Émilie Porry
Content Development and Design: Patricia Duchesneau
learning_objectives: Understand the basics of ear and hearing health
Have a better understanding of the challenges that a child with hearing loss may encounter at school and at  home
Have access to tips and tools to provide assistance to children with hearing loss or ear disease by improving listening conditions in the classroom

pathways.title: Fundamentals of Information Systems
pathways.description: Information systems consist of interconnected components that manage data and digital products to support organizational functions such as decision-making and communication. This content provides a comprehensive introduction to IS fundamentals, frameworks, global trends, and career competencies—making it ideal for learners exploring careers in business technology or educators teaching introductory IS or IT courses. This pathway is Chapter 1 of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms related to information systems, frameworks, standards, and professional competencies
List industry-recognized frameworks (e.g., Agile, COBIT, ITIL) and standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 27001, TOGAF) used in IS.
Explain the function and purpose of information systems in organizational settings.
Assess the value of industry standards and frameworks in maintaining effective and secure IS operations.
Distinguish between the functions of IS and IT in business and technology environments.

pathways.title: Genetic Rescue to the Rescue - Preventing Extinction Through Gene Flow
pathways.description:
Students will deepen their understanding of basic genetics concepts as they dive into real data sets from ongoing research. They will hone critical thinking and communication skills and come away with clear examples of how science can lead to positive impacts on wildlife populations.
Driving Question:
Can genetic rescue (a conservation strategy involving movement of individuals across populations) prevent the extinction of endangered species?
Essential Question:
How do we use scientific methods to measure and develop solutions for our negative impacts on the world?
Hook:
Numerous puzzles, group activities, compelling videos, and active discussions draw students into real-world problems.
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
learning_objectives: At the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define key terms such as gene, trait, genotype, phenotype, allele, and inbreeding.
Identify species such as the Florida panther and explain what genetic rescue means.
Explain why genetic variation is essential for species survival and evolution.
Describe the concept of the extinction vortex in endangered populations.
Use concept maps to organize knowledge about genetic rescue and extinction risks.

pathways.title: Electromagnetic Induction
pathways.description: Electromagnetic induction is the production of voltage known as induced EMF due to a changing magnetic field or magnetic flux linkage. This has contributed to many car inventions and has been used mainly in generating electricity through generators or windmills. Explore the principles of electromagnetic induction, including Faraday’s law, Lenz’s law, motional emf, and RLC circuits, to understand electricity, magnetism, and real-world technological applications.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define magnetic flux and explain its role in electromagnetic induction.
Apply Faraday’s law and Lenz’s law to predict and explain induced emf.
Analyze motional emf and its applications in devices such as generators and wind turbines.
Explain the operation of transformers and their importance in power distribution.
Solve problems involving inductance, RL circuits, and RLC circuits in alternating current systems.
Relate electromagnetic induction to practical applications in energy production, electrical safety, and modern technology.

pathways.title: Assessment Strategies
pathways.description: Student assessment strategies help teachers to measure students’ progress and evaluate the effect classroom experiences are having on learning. This pathway will define different assessment strategies and provide an opportunity to see them in practice in a classroom.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the significance of assessment strategies in measuring student progress and evaluating the effectiveness of classroom experiences on learning.
You will evaluate the impact of student self-assessment and peer assessment on promoting student ownership of learning.
You will analyze the reasons behind the students' preferences for specific activities and presentations, considering factors like engagement and interest.
You  discuss formative assessment techniques in a classroom setting, considering student diversity and individual learning needs.

pathways.title: Groupings of Mammals
pathways.description: Carl Linnaeus, known as the father of modern taxonomy, developed the system for classifying living organisms into hierarchical groups such as genus and species. Discover how mammals evolved and diversified over geological time. Explore taxonomy, fossil evidence, and anatomical adaptations.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the basis of classifying living organisms using taxonomy, emphasizing Carl Linnaeus’s system and its relevance to modern biological classification.
Describe the distinguishing features and adaptations of mammals, including structural, physiological, and behavioral traits that support survival in different environments.
Outline the major groups of mammals—monotremes, marsupials, and placentals—and give examples of each.
Discuss the evolutionary history of mammals using fossil evidence to trace their origin, diversification, and relationship to other vertebrates.
Analyze how environmental and climatic changes influenced mammalian evolution and human origins.

pathways.title: Can You Hear Me Now? Cell Phone Communication and Coulomb’s Law
pathways.description:
Explore cell phone signal transmission and electromagnetic waves through engaging videos, interactive simulations, and hands-on experiments. This pathway is accompanied by a student guide and spans 6 days (315-345 minutes). You will cover signal strength, wave properties, and communication technology, fostering a deep understanding of physics and telecommunications concepts.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1 & 2, Timing: 75 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, Timing: 30 minutes, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 90 - 120 minutes, items 2-8 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 60 minutes, in the student guide and item 9 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 60 minutes, items 10-12 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will use simulations to determine Coulomb’s Law and describe and predict electrostatic forces between objects.
Develop and use a model of two objects interacting through electric and magnetic fields.
Be able communicate information about how some technological devices use the principles of wave behavior and wave interactions to transmit information.

pathways.title: Hazards to Deep Space Astronauts
pathways.description:
Teach students about the five challenges that make spaceflight dangerous with these curriculum-aligned activities. Protect a potato "astronaut" from radiation, research water consumption rates, design an exercise device, and more. These engaging activities will help Grade 5-8 students gain a better understanding of the challenges astronauts face in space and develop critical thinking and problem-solving skills.
Product Number: NP-2021-08-2975-HQ
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the basics of how to protect an astronaut from radiation.
You will form practice writing a proposal to a fictional NASA panel and "sell" your idea for a research topic.
You will understand how to monitor water consumption rates to predict whether or not astronauts will have enough for their journey.
You will learn how to design a functional exercise device to mitigate bone loss and muscular atrophy.
You will learn how to design an enclosed, self-sustainable habitat for astronauts on a deep space mission.

pathways.title: Keeping COVID Away! An educational comic and coloring/drawing activity sheet for kids
pathways.description: Learn about Covid - What is it doing in town? How can we protect ourselves and our friends? - with this visual story and coloring/drawing activity for kids.
learning_objectives: Recall what the coronavirus is and how we can protect ourselves and our friends.
Illustrate how you are protecting yourself and friends during the pandemic.

pathways.title: Growth and Development
pathways.description: Why do some of our traits change throughout life? This learning sequence explores the factors that affect how our cells and bodies develop and transform over time.
learning_objectives: Explain how environmental and genetic factors influence the growth of organisms.
Predict how an external factor might affect development by influencing DNA.

pathways.title: Understanding Ocean and Coastal Acidification: For Teachers
pathways.description: Dive into the newly updated module, launched in January 2025, designed for grades 9–12. Students will examine the connections between carbon dioxide, ocean pH, and aragonite saturation state to understand why ocean pH is changing and how it impacts marine chemistry and ecosystems. This 5-level module features interactive maps, graphs, and videos, incorporating the latest NOAA data to reveal the science behind these changes and their global effects.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to predict how rising atmospheric carbon dioxide affects ocean pH using real data.
Use data to analyze the relationship between ocean carbon dioxide levels and pH changes.
Compare and contrast ocean and coastal acidification using real data.
Evaluate the impact of acidification on shell-building marine organisms.
Design and conduct an investigation on ocean acidification in a selected ecosystem.

pathways.title: Colorful Solutions: Chemical Engineering Dyes to Brighten Lives
pathways.description:
Students will learn to think like chemical engineers as they delve into the dye technologies that allow us to express ourselves through colorful clothing. They will read and discuss complex texts, analyze concept maps and graphs, and develop an appreciation for indigenous (Māori) dye technology.
Driving Question(s):
How have different dye technologies been used to produce colorful clothing across cultures and over time?
Essential Question(s):
How can a career in chemical, genetic, or process engineering create opportunities to solve complex societal problems?
Hook(s):
• Part 1 begins with a warm-up “Dye-versity Audit,” In which students will quantify the number of dyes they brought to class on their person and reflect on what they mean in terms of self-expression. The rest of Part 1 focuses on narratives designed to appeal to students’ curiosity.
• Part 2 involves a card-based puzzle activity.
• Part 3 puts students directly in the decision-maker position, giving them an opportunity to choose a course of action based on their own values and reasoning.
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to understand the role of chemical engineers in developing economical processes that convert raw materials into useful products.
Apply scientific methods to analyze and solve problems related to dye production.
Analyze complex historical and contemporary texts to synthesize information about natural and synthetic dye processes.
Evaluate the tradeoffs between synthetic and natural dye production with respect to cost, sustainability, scalability, and cultural significance, including Māori dye technology.

pathways.title: NASA | Fluid-like Materials
pathways.description: This pathway explores materials that behave like both liquids and solids, such as liquid crystals, polymers, and non-Newtonian fluids. You'll learn how these materials flow, respond to stress, and change behavior in microgravity through experiments on Earth and in space.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain how certain materials, such as liquid crystals and non-Newtonian fluids, can exhibit both solid-like and liquid-like properties.
Describe how microgravity affects the behavior of fluid-like materials and the implications for technology and scientific research.
Analyze the role of polymers and other adaptable materials in everyday applications and advanced scientific experiments.

pathways.title: How Can We Develop More Effective Vaccines?
pathways.description: When the COVID-19 pandemic struck the world in 2020, our everyday lives were put on hold until immunologists and vaccinologists discovered how to protect us. Incredibly, multiple vaccines for the disease were developed and approved for use within record-breaking time. At the University of Colorado Anschutz School of Medicine in the US, Professor Ross Kedl and his team are studying the immune system in the hope of exploiting it to develop more effective vaccines.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to describe the role of B cells, T cells, and antibodies in the immune response.
Summarize the process by which the innate immune system recognizes and responds to different types of pathogens.
Explain the distinction between extracellular and intracellular infections and how the immune system responds to each type.
Compare and contrast the mechanisms by which B cells and T cells protect the body against infections.

pathways.title: Balancing and Pacing
pathways.description:
A truly balanced discussion promotes equity by amplifying the voices of all students rather than just the outspoken few. This seldom occurs by chance. Rather, it is the result of careful planning and intentional strategies. We would additionally be hard-pressed to find an instructor who has not struggled with pacing class discussions. In addition to keeping an eye on the clock, this demands quick thinking and real-time adjustments, nimble reframing of questions, and nonstop judgment calls. Without attention to pacing, lesson objectives might not be met. Meanwhile, without some healthy flexibility, those spontaneous moments of collective discovery characteristic of productive class discussions may be abruptly halted.   
Which students in our classrooms tend to dominate the conversation? What strategies might help us get more voices into the mix? When should we chime in? How do we pivot the dialogue when necessary? In this pathway, featured professors share strategies for facilitating more equitable and well-paced discussions in their classrooms.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will identify and describe various strategies for promoting equity in classroom discussions, ensuring all students have the opportunity to contribute.
Apply techniques for effectively pacing classroom discussions to meet lesson objectives while allowing for spontaneous, productive dialogue.
Evaluate the effectiveness of different methods for managing dominant voices and encouraging quieter students to participate in class discussions.
Design a plan for facilitating classroom discussions that incorporates intentional strategies for real-time adjustments and inclusive participation.

pathways.title: Thermal Insulation Trouble
pathways.description:
Become a junior space engineer and join a space adventure! Figure out how to help Astronaut Alex fix important thermal insulation material in this learning pathway, with the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 7 days of lessons (330 minutes) - experiment, brainstorm, and solve real-world challenges while exploring energy forms, energy changes, and heat storage through hands-on activities and simulations.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1-2, 60 minutes, item 1 of the pathway and activity in the student guide.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 80 minutes, items 2-3 of the pathway and activity in the student guide.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4-5, 80 minutes, Items 4-5 of the pathway and activity in the student guide.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5-6, 60 minutes, items 6-7 of the pathway and activity in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 6-7, 50 minutes, item 8 of the pathway.
Before we begin, let's explore the Basics of Insulation and Energy Transfer pathway to make sure we're equipped to takcle thermal insulation challenges on the Moon!
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to identify the importance of thermal stability in space experiments and explain why maintaining stable temperatures through insulation is critical for energy conservation.
You will explore and describe the specific thermal challenges presented by the Moon's surface, analyzing how energy transfer and environmental conditions affect the stability of temperatures.
You will perform experiments to test the effectiveness of different insulation materials under simulated lunar conditions, understanding the principles of thermal energy transfer.
You will collect and analyze experimental data to determine the factors that influence the performance of insulation materials in maintaining thermal stability on the Moon.

You will apply your knowledge of thermal insulation and energy transfer to create an innovative insulation solution that addresses the challenges of extreme lunar temperatures, demonstrating an understanding of energy conservation in space.

pathways.title: NASA | Space Optics
pathways.description: Explore the evolution of optical science—from fundamental principles to advanced laser systems transforming deep space exploration. This pathway builds a foundation in geometric optics by examining how light travels, reflects, and focuses, then connects these core concepts to space telescopes and high-precision laser communications. Discover how optical technologies enable faster, more efficient data transmission for future missions to Mars and beyond.
learning_objectives: Describe the properties of light and how it travels
Explain how optical communication differs from traditional radio frequency systems.
Evaluate the advantages and challenges of using laser communications for deep space missions.

pathways.title: Hearing Health: Hearing Loss at School
pathways.description:
The World Health Organization reports that 430 million people currently live with disabling hearing loss worldwide. This pathway outlines the global and local impacts of hearing loss, the importance of school-based screening, and how teachers can support children through evidence-based classroom strategies and hearing health awareness.
Acknowledgments
Thank you for the invaluable contributions of our board members, partners, and colleagues in the development of this educational material: Dr. Marlene Bagatto, Ora Buerkli, Dr. Patricia Castellanos de Muñoz, and Dr. Diego Santana-Hernández.
Research and Script: Émilie Porry
Content Development and Design: Patricia Duchesneau
learning_objectives: Understand the basics of ear and hearing health.
Have a better understanding of the challenges that a child with hearing loss may encounter at school and at home.
Have access to tips and tools to provide assistance to children with hearing loss or ear disease by improving listening conditions in the classroom.

pathways.title: Does City Life Negatively Affect Wildlife?
pathways.description: If you have ever seen the beautiful sight of hundreds of starlings sweeping through the sky in unison, you will know how at ease these birds look in their environment, even in an urban setting. Starlings seem to have adapted well to cities, but how healthy is this for them? At Kennesaw State University in the US, Dr Sarah Guindre-Parker is an ecologist investigating the effects of urban life on starlings – and the implications for us all.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you'll be able to remember what starlings look like, where they like to live, and why they're important for studying pollution from heavy metals.
Describe how city growth and pollution can hurt animals, like how toxic metals in the environment can change starlings' actions and make them sick.
Look at how Dr. Sarah Guindre-Parker and her team figure out what heavy metals do to starlings.
Think about what Dr. Guindre-Parker's discoveries on starlings and metal pollution mean for building cities, protecting animals, and keeping people healthy.

pathways.title: How Can Experiments With Yeast Genetics Help Make the Perfect Pint?
pathways.description: Molecular biology depends on laboratory experiments to uncover the secrets of organisms at the molecular level. Despite being a relatively young field, it brings with it a huge array of possibilities for better understanding the world and for applications to society. One such application is the genetic modification of brewer’s yeast to improve the final product: a cool glass of ale or stout. Dr Michael Wolyniak of Hampden-Sydney College challenges his students to investigate the yeast genome to develop their own practical skills whilst simultaneously benefiting the brewery round the corner
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will explain how experiments with yeast genetics can contribute to improving the quality of beer
Describe the process of gene disruption and its role in altering yeast characteristics.
Discuss the potential benefits and challenges of genetic modification in the context of improving brewery products.

pathways.title: Take a Deep Breath: Investigating Air Quality
pathways.description: The air is full of aerosolised microscopic matter that enter our lungs with every breath. The BioAirNet team is investigating aerosols of biological origin, what they mean for our health, and how we can improve the air quality of the places we spend time in.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define and explain air quality terminology, with a focus on bioaerosols and their potential effects on human health.
Students will identify the different scientific disciplines involved in the study of air quality.
Learners will be able to identify and apply practical steps to improve indoor air quality in their homes.

pathways.title: Deep Learning and AI Basics
pathways.description: Deep learning drives innovations in natural language processing, computer vision, and autonomous systems. This pathway introduces how neural networks function through layers of neurons, activation functions, and tunable parameters. You will learn how models are trained using backpropagation, gradient descent, and loss functions to improve performance. Real-world examples, such as image classification and anomaly detection, illustrate how deep learning solves complex problems in artificial intelligence. This pathway is Chapter 7 of the Principles of Data Science textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the structure and function of artificial neural networks, including input, hidden, and output layers, as well as the role of neurons, weights, and biases.
Compare common activation functions, and describe how they influence a neuron's output in classification tasks.
Apply the perceptron learning rule and backpropagation algorithm to adjust weights and biases in a neural network during training.
Interpret how loss functions quantify model error and guide optimization using gradient descent.
Analyze real-world applications of deep learning, such as image recognition, NLP, recommendation systems, and anomaly detection, to understand the societal impact of AI.

pathways.title: Fundamentals of Motion and Measurement
pathways.description: Understanding the principles of motion and measurement is essential in physics. This pathway explores physical quantities, SI units, motion concepts, and Newton’s laws. You will learn about fundamental and derived units, velocity and speed, and the forces governing motion, including Newton’s second law and universal gravitation. By the end of this pathway, you will be able to apply measurement principles to motion analysis and understand the forces acting on objects in different environments.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe SI units, metric prefixes, and the role of fundamental and derived units in physics.
Analyze time, velocity, and speed as key motion parameters.
Explain Newton’s second law of motion and how force, mass, and acceleration are related.
Understand the concept of gravitational and inertial mass and apply Newton’s universal law of gravitation.
Apply unit conversion and dimensional analysis to solve real-world physics problems.

pathways.title: An Age-Old Question
pathways.description: As we reach the later years of our lives, our skin will wrinkle and our hair will turn grey. We may have mixed feelings about ageing, but it is a natural part of life. Although we may never be able to prevent ageing, some scientists think that we may be able to slow it down. Dr Hua Bai from Iowa State University in the US is studying a process in our cells that is thought to contribute to ageing. He hopes that this research may help us find new ways of slowing down the ageing process.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway learners will be able to discuss the methods and techniques used by Dr. Hua Bai and his team to study peroxisomes.
Learners will be able to analyze the impact of peroxisomal dysfunction on the aging process.
Learners will be able to explain the role of peroxisomes in cellular metabolism and the importance of peroxisomal function in maintaining overall health and well-being.

pathways.title: Plant Polymers as Plastic Alternatives
pathways.description: Plastics are cheap and versatile but have a long-lasting environmental impact, and the race is on to roll out effective alternatives. Professor Jeffrey Catchmark and his team at Pennsylvania State University in the US believe they have one such solution, using common plant-based materials to create sustainable food packaging.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will understand the concept of biological engineering and its application in creating sustainable alternatives to plastic.
Identify the skills and knowledge necessary for a career in biological engineering, including academic subjects and personal values.
Be able to define polysaccharides and discuss the benefits and disadvantages of plastics.
Be able to evaluate the role of research teams in preventing greenwashing and maintaining good relationships with companies.

pathways.title: How Can Electronics Engineers Help to Power the Green Transition?
pathways.description: You have probably heard of Silicon Valley in California, home of tech inventions and all things electronics. But at the University of Warwick in the UK, Professor Peter Gammon is using a different material to drive innovation in electrical devices. Will Silicon Valley one day become Silicon Carbide Valley?
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will gain a better understanding of power electronics and its role in moving energy, as well as the differences between power electronics and electrical electronics.
You will understand the potential of using alternative materials in electronics engineering to reduce energy wastage and contribute to carbon emission reduction efforts.
You will identify the presence of transistors in household electronic devices and explain their role in electrical circuits.

pathways.title: What Are Engineers Learning From Insects?
pathways.description: Based at Temple University in the US, Professor Parsaoran Hutapea and his mechanical engineering research team have taken inspiration from honeybee and mosquito stingers to design a new surgical needle that is more accurate and less likely to cause tissue damage.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to understand the principles of reducing tissue damage and improving precision in surgical procedures through mechanical engineering innovations.
Understand the applications of bioinspired engineering.

analyze the benefits of interdisciplinary collaboration in mechanical engineering, evaluate the effectiveness of new needle designs, and demonstrate creativity by designing their own bioinspired object or device.

pathways.title: MAGNETISM
pathways.description: Magnetism is the physical phenomenon by which certain materials exert attractive or repulsive forces on other materials due to the motion of electric charges, primarily electrons. Explore magnetic fields, electromagnetism, and forces on charged particles, applying right-hand rules and understanding ferromagnetic materials.
learning_objectives: Explain magnetism and magnetic fields by describing magnetic poles, field patterns, and the behavior of permanent and induced magnets.
Describe the relationship between electricity and magnetism, including how electric currents produce magnetic fields and how magnetic fields affect moving charges and current-carrying conductors.
Apply right-hand rules and relevant equations to determine the direction and magnitude of magnetic forces on charges and conductors in magnetic fields.
Explain magnetism in materials by describing ferromagnetism, magnetic domains, and how materials become magnetized or demagnetized.
Analyze real-world applications of electromagnetism, including electromagnets, Earth’s magnetic field, and their role in technology and everyday life.

pathways.title: Plant Nutrient Strategies
pathways.description: How do plants get the nutrients they need to grow, thrive, and adapt to their environments? Explore the essential macro- and micronutrients required for plant health, including how these nutrients are absorbed, used, and monitored.
learning_objectives: Identify the macro- and micronutrients required for plant growth.
Differentiate between organic and inorganic sources of nutrients.
Explain how plants obtain nutrients from soil, air, and water.
Describe nutrient uptake adaptations in plants, including mycorrhizal associations, nitrogen fixation, and parasitism.
Analyze how alternative growing systems like hydroponics supply nutrients to plants and compare their advantages to soil-based systems.
Interpret visual and experimental data to evaluate plant responses to nutrient deficiencies or varying nutrient conditions.

pathways.title: Can We Reveal Earth’s Secrets by Dating Faults?
pathways.description: Understanding the Earth’s geological history is crucial, not only for revealing its hidden evolutionary past but also for predicting future events. Dr Dawn Kellett, a research scientist at the Geological Survey of Canada, focuses on methods to accurately determine the ages of geological faults, providing invaluable insights into Earth’s geological evolution.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms such as isotopes, decay, mass spectrometry, minerals, and tectonic plates.
Identify the major geological processes that contribute to the formation of faults and mountain ranges.
Explain how radioactive decay is used to determine the age of minerals and rocks.
Predict how changes in fault activity could affect natural disasters or resource distribution.

pathways.title: How Can Robots Help Us Walk?
pathways.description: Prosthetic legs have enabled thousands of people with a disability walk, but artificial limbs can be stiff and uncomfortable to use. Dr Elliott Rouse, of the University of Michigan, is using robotics to help us understand how people walk and develop new prostheses that can react and adapt to our motion
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway students should understand the limitations of conventional prosthetic legs and the challenges faced by individuals using them.
Define key terms related to biomedical engineering, such as prosthesis, amputation, bionics, biomechanics, exoskeleton, and locomotion.
Explore the ethical considerations related to designing and controlling wearable assistive technologies, including human perception and user preferences.

pathways.title: NASA | Applications of Biophysics
pathways.description: Biomaterials have many applications in medicine, engineering, and space technology. Learn about these applications and the exciting biomaterials research taking place at NASA.
learning_objectives: Define and understand the properties of biomaterials.
Explore NASA’s research on biomaterials and their role in space exploration.
Examine various applications of biomaterials in medicine, engineering, and space technology.

pathways.title: Biodiversity in Practice
pathways.description: Biodiversity is the foundation of all ecosystems and a vital concern for scientists. Explore the multifaceted concept of biodiversity—its levels, patterns, and processes—while highlighting the key environmental and human factors that influence it.
learning_objectives: Define biodiversity.
Define and identify biodiversity hotspots around the globe.
Explain the need for preserving biodiversity.
Evaluate conservation strategies such as protected areas, legal frameworks, habitat restoration, and seed banking, and explain how these strategies support long-term ecosystem resilience.

pathways.title: Gathering Accurate Information
pathways.description: In today's digital landscape, it is vital that scientists navigate reliable sources with confidence. This pathway equips learners with essential skills in digital literacy, scientific research analysis, and media evaluation. Through videos, case studies, and practical techniques like lateral reading, fact-checking, and evidence assessment, learners gain tools to navigate misinformation and engage critically with scientific content online.
learning_objectives: Enhance scientific literacy by distinguishing between reliable and unreliable evidence in digital media, including text, photos, and videos.
Develop the ability to interpret and communicate scientific research by analyzing the structure and content of peer-reviewed journal articles.
Learn to critically evaluate digital sources by applying fact-checking techniques, lateral reading, and source verification strategies.
Apply strategies for identifying misinformation and constructing accurate, evidence-based messages for diverse audiences in STEM contexts.

pathways.title: Database Management Systems
pathways.description: As data powers modern decision-making, understanding how it's organized, managed, and protected is essential. This pathway introduces key concepts in data classification, database design, and cloud-based systems, covering structured and unstructured data, database models, and mobile integration. Learn how industries like healthcare and e-commerce leverage these systems, and gain the skills to build efficient, scalable, and secure data solutions. This pathway is Chapter 3 of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this section, you will be able to define data and differentiate between types of data.
Identify tools and techniques for managing data with database systems.
Describe the design process and structure for a database management system.
Discuss the considerations for cloud-based databases and their architecture.

pathways.title: Decoding RNA Mysteries: A New Era for Biology and Medicine
pathways.description: RNA modifications critically influence the structure and function of RNA molecules, impacting health, biotechnology and agriculture. To overcome the challenges of sequencing RNA molecules and their modifications, we urgently need advances in sequencing methods and technologies. The US National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine has assembled a committee of 16 professionals with a wide range of expertise related to RNA sequencing and RNA modifications, who call for a large-scale effort to revolutionize our understanding of RNA biology and unlock its potential. Part of the team is featured below.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms related to RNA biology, including epitranscriptome, gene expression, transcription, and RNA modification.
List the major applications of RNA modifications in medicine, agriculture, and biotechnology.
Explain how RNA modifications influence gene expression, protein synthesis, and cellular function.
Summarize the role of RNA modifications in vaccine development, using mRNA COVID-19 vaccines as an example.

pathways.title: NASA | What is Soft Matter?
pathways.description: Embark on a fascinating journey into the squishy, stretchy, and surprising world of soft matter, where physics meets the fantastic! Discover why NASA takes these materials to space, and learn how their unique behaviors in low gravity could unlock new possibilities for science and technology.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to understand the fundamental properties and behaviors of Soft Matter.
Understand why Soft Matter is studied, including why its studied in low gravity environments.

pathways.title: Measurements and Matter
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the states of matter, phase changes, and key variables that influence physical properties. By the end of this pathway, you will understand how to apply metric prefixes, analyze physical states, and perform unit conversions essential for scientific study.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the states of matter and how phase changes occur under different conditions.
Identify key variables that influence the behavior of solids, liquids, and gases.
Analyze how pressure, volume, and temperature interact in phase transitions.
Understand the metric system, including metric prefixes and their significance in scientific measurements.

pathways.title: Earth's Erosional Wonders: Must-Visit Geological Destinations
pathways.description:
Take a journey through Earth's water cycles with this pathway and accompanying student guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (190-210 minutes) with focus on groundwater and aquifers, and unravel their crucial role in the water cycle. Try out virtual experiments about the water model and the hydraulic fracturing model. Plus, explore a potential STEM career related to these topics.
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 40-60 minutes, items 3-9 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 60 minutes, items 10-12 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 6, 10 minutes, item 13 of the pathway
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will understand how weathering and erosion shaped Earth’s surface.
Explore how groundwater, streamflow, springs, infiltration and seepage plays a vital role in Earth’s water cycle.
Understand how groundwater is the saturated zone of soil/rock below the land surface.
Learn how there are large amounts of water stored beneath the Earth’s crust.

Learners will be able to engage in virtual experiments and collaborative discussion to enhance critical thinking skills, explore the relationships between water flowing erosional features, related careers exploration, and ask relevant questions.

pathways.title: Biodiversity Conservation
pathways.description: Discover the vital principles of ecological sustainability. Dive into core concepts like trade-offs, diversity, and competition. Gain insights into how these principles relate to climate change, biodiversity, and innovative solutions. Empower yourself to shape a more sustainable future.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the core concepts of ecological sustainability, including trade-offs, diversity, and competition, and their significance for maintaining natural ecosystems.
Be able to recognize the importance of incorporating ecological sustainability concepts into the development of effective public policies and economic strategies.
Gain insights into the practical implications of these ecological principles for addressing pressing global challenges, such as climate change, biodiversity conservation, and disease dynamics.
Recognize the key components and processes that define the critical zone, including interactions between geology, biology, hydrology, and climate.

pathways.title: A Bestiary of Loss
pathways.description: In the midst of a staggering mass extinction event caused by humans, explore the alarming statistics and stories of species lost throughout history. Discover the profound impact of collective human behaviors on biodiversity and the urgent need for action in today's environmental landscape.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will have acquired knowledge about various species that have gone extinct.
Learners will be able to recognize the various forces and actors that contributed to the extinction of specific animal species.
Learners will be encouraged to reflect on the critical role they play in addressing biodiversity conservation.

pathways.title: NASA | Deployment of the James Webb Space Telescope
pathways.description: To unfold the history of the universe we must first unfold the largest primary mirror, the largest sun shield, and the most powerful space telescope ever built. Find out why the James Webb Space Telescope orbits at the second Lagrange point, the challenges of deploying the telescope, and why its sunshield is so groundbreaking.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the complex engineering and unfolding process of the James Webb Space Telescope.
Understand that the James Webb Space Telescope's location at the second Lagrange point (L2) allows it to effectively shield itself from the Sun and Earth's heat and light and why this is important.
Be able to explain the features and function of James Webb's sunshield.
Be inspired to explore valuable skills for future mechanical engineering careers.

pathways.title: How Can We Tackle Energy Poverty in Refugee Camps?
pathways.description: Professor Elena Gaura, Associate Dean of Research at Coventry University in the UK, believes that, “engineering can change lives because it gives people hope”. She is principal investigator of the HEED Project, a humanitarian endeavour focused on increasing access to affordable and sustainable energy for displaced people.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how the energy poverty crises is addressed by involving end-users in the design and implementation of energy solutions in their countries.
You will understand what pervasive computing is, and how it connects devices to improve day-to-day experiences.

pathways.title: Pathogen-Busting Nanosurfaces Inspired by Insect Wings
pathways.description: Keeping surfaces clean is more complex than you might expect. Pathogens can adhere to and multiply on almost any surface – but not all. Professor Prasad Yarlagadda, from the Queensland University of Technology in Australia, is taking inspiration from insect wings to design nanosurfaces that destroy any bacteria or viruses that land on them. This has major implications for the next generation of super-sterile materials, especially within the medical field.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you will have a better understanding of how researches are using hydrothermal synthesis to create nanostructures for antibacterial properties.
Learn more about the field of smart systems engineering, and the endless possibilities and opportunities this field has to offer.

pathways.title: Pacific H2O: A Choose-Your-Own STEM Adventure
pathways.description:
Students will gain a new appreciation for water as they reflect, mind-map, embody different STEM roles, and perform a choose-your-own-adventure chemistry lab in this 4 x 45-minute science unit for Grades 9–13. Throughout, they will be exposed to Pacific languages, proverbs, and STEM solutions in this unique learning experience.
Driving Question(s):
1. In what ways do you and your community rely on clean water?
2. What technologies do engineers use to purify water and how can we use them in the classroom?
3. How do Pacific Island nations, surrounded by saltwater, provide fresh, clean water to the public?
Hook(s):
Lessons are threaded with Pacific languages and perspectives that will fascinate and engage students. Students will enjoy developing mind maps about water, doing a lab in their own way, with the choose-your-own-adventure app in lesson 2, and having many opportunities to connect content to Pacific cultures and real world problems.
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to define key terms related to water purification and Pacific Island environments.
Recall steps in the water testing and purification process demonstrated in the lab experience.
Describe how clean water access affects individuals and communities, particularly in Pacific Island nations.
Explain the role of engineers and scientists in developing water purification technologies.
Use a mind map to visually represent personal and community connections to water.
Compare different water purification methods and their effectiveness in Pacific contexts.

pathways.title: NASA | Foams and Gels
pathways.description: Foams play an essential role in our everyday lives, from shaving cream to fire-resistant insulation, aerospace materials, and food production. This pathway explores how foams form, evolve, and are optimized for scientific and commercial applications. Discover how gravity influences foam behavior, with groundbreaking experiments aboard the International Space Station.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the role of foams in scientific, industrial, and everyday applications.
You will analyze how gravity and microgravity impact foam stability and structure.
You will evaluate the benefits of foam technology in aerospace, healthcare, and manufacturing.
You will apply knowledge of foam science to create experiments and demonstrations, such as the "elephant toothpaste" reaction.
You will assess how space-based research contributes to advancements in foam technology.

pathways.title: How Are Strokes, Dementia and Depression Linked?
pathways.description: At Western University in Canada, Dr Jamie Fleet and Dr Stephanie Frisbee are collaborating to improve health outcomes for people affected by stroke, depression and dementia.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define stroke, TIA (Transient Ischemic Attack), and dementia.
You will identify key risk factors associated with cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases.
You will explain how stroke, depression, and dementia are interconnected.
You will examine the reasons why follow-up care gaps exist after a stroke or TIA.

pathways.title: Data and Communication in the World of the Future
pathways.description: As our lives become increasingly intertwined with technology, we need increasingly sophisticated ways of managing data and communicating. Professor Justin Coon and his team at the University of Oxford in the UK are working on a variety of research projects, involving molecular communication, data compression and prioritisation, and safeguarding the incoming ‘internet of things’.
learning_objectives: After completing the pathway, you will understand innovative research projects in engineering and communication, including molecular communication, graph compression, information value, and network security.
Explore the impact of these projects on various fields and everyday life.
Evaluate challenges and opportunities in the future of data and communication.

pathways.title: Glowing Diamonds Are a Quantum Scientist’s Best Friend
pathways.description: Dr Lachlan Rogers, based at the University of Newcastle in Australia, is investigating diamond colour centres. The ultimate aim is to enable the development of new quantum technologies that will revolutionise many aspects of life.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you will understand the importance of the diamond center color in quantum technology.
Learn how researchers are using diamond color centers in quantum technology to develop new applications in quantum computing and quantum metrology.
Be able to describe the basics of quantum optics and explain the potential impact of quantum technologies.

pathways.title: Metal Corrosion and Protection
pathways.description: Do you wonder why some metals and household items lose their shine while others stay protected? This pathway explores corrosion, oxidation, and electroplating, revealing how metals like iron and copper react, rust, or resist degradation. Connect these redox processes to material selection, protection, and real-world engineering applications.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define corrosion and describe its chemical basis as an oxidation-reduction process affecting metals.
Differentiate between corrosion, rusting, and passivation in common metals such as iron and copper.
Explain methods used to prevent or slow corrosion, including painting, galvanization, alloying, and cathodic protection.
Describe the process and applications of electroplating as an electrolytic reaction used to protect or enhance metals.
Relate the physical and chemical properties of metals to their conductivity, malleability, and tendency to corrode.
Analyze how understanding corrosion and metal properties informs material selection and engineering design in everyday contexts.

pathways.title: Photon Engineers: Tinkering with Light
pathways.description:
Embark on a journey through time and space to understand what light really is and how humans use it in this 5 x 45-minute science pathway for Grades 6–9. From starting fires with sunlight, to sending messages to other planets, to current research in photonics, students will become more curious about light in their daily lives!
Driving Question(s):
1. How can light be used to measure, transmit, and control information?
2. How does light behave in various media?
Essential Question(s):
• How has human curiosity driven innovation throughout history?
• What are the benefits and challenges of communicating with light?
Hook(s):
Puzzles, hands-on games, and original illustrations create a dynamic lesson supported by engaging videos.
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to define key vocabulary related to light, lenses, waves, and photonics (e.g., reflection, refraction, amplitude, focal point, photon, electromagnetic spectrum).
Explain how light behaves in different media (e.g., air, water, glass) and how that behavior supports phenomena such as magnification and distortion.
Describe how light waves can be used to transmit information.
Construct simple wave models (kinesthetic or drawn) to illustrate amplitude, frequency, and wave transmission.
Compare how different lenses (concave vs. convex) and materials (transparent vs. opaque) affect the path of light.

pathways.title: Time Series and Forecasting
pathways.description: Chapter 5 of the OpenStax Principles of Data Science textbook introduces you to time series analysis—a key method for understanding how data changes over time. Through real-world examples like stock prices and weather patterns, you will learn to identify trends, seasonal effects, and random fluctuations. The chapter also covers forecasting techniques and how to evaluate their accuracy, providing essential tools for making informed predictions.
learning_objectives: Understand what time series data is and recognize its real-world applications.
Identify components of time series, including trends, seasonality, and noise.
Apply smoothing techniques like moving averages to reveal underlying patterns.
Use forecasting models, such as ARIMA, to predict future data points.
Evaluate forecast accuracy using error metrics and prediction intervals.

pathways.title: NASA | Solid-like Materials
pathways.description: Why do Brazil nuts end up on top of the mix? Why do footprints on wet sand seem to dry the ground? How can a pile of snow turn into an avalanche—and what does that have to do with exploring the Moon? This learning pathway dives into the curious, surprising, and essential physics of granular materials—substances like sand, nuts, soil, and snow that behave like both solids and fluids. Through hands-on investigations, real-world analogies, and even NASA rover testing, you will explore the physical principles that govern granular behavior in everyday life and in planetary exploration.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will be able to describe the physical properties of granular materials and explain their dual fluid-solid behavior under different forces.
Conduct investigations and interpret real-world phenomena like the Brazil nut effect, non-Newtonian flow, and the angle of repose.
Apply concepts from granular physics to real-world challenges in engineering and space exploration, including NASA’s rover development.

pathways.title: Heating Effects of Electricity: Current, Voltage, and Circuits
pathways.description: Electricity generates heat when it flows through a conductor, a principle used in everyday applications like electric heaters, fuses, and appliances. This pathway explores electric current, voltage, resistance, and circuit types, providing a foundation for understanding how electricity produces heat and how it is controlled in electrical systems. By the end of this pathway, you will grasp the relationships between current, voltage, and resistance and how these factors contribute to heating effects in circuits.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the relationship between electric current, voltage, and resistance in circuits.
Analyze the differences between series and parallel circuits and their impact on power distribution.
Understand how electrical energy is converted into heat and applied in real-world systems.
Describe how electrical shocks occur and the dangers of high current versus high voltage.

pathways.title: How Can We Reduce Fat in Fried Foods?
pathways.description: Deep-fried food is immensely popular. Although embraced worldwide for its deliciousness, its appeal is tinged with health concerns stemming from the significant absorption of oil during the frying process. Dr Reza Tahergorabi, an associate professor at North Carolina Agriculture & Technical State University in the US, is exploring oleogel as a new frying medium to reduce the fat content in fried foods.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define key terms related to food science, including by-products, frying medium, gelling agents, oleogel, oxidative stability, and shelf life.
Describe the role of edible food coatings in food preservation and fat reduction in fried foods.
Explain how oleogels address the issue of excessive dietary fat intake in fried foods and contribute to healthier food options.
Evaluate the effectiveness of different frying mediums, such as oleogels, in enhancing the nutritional quality of fried products.
Analyze the environmental impact of using by-products in edible coatings and its contribution to sustainability in the food industry.

pathways.title: Engaging Students
pathways.description:
Oftentimes before students can engage deeply with complex ideas, they must be engaged by their instructor. This becomes challenging considering the mismatch between the length of a typical lecture period (one to three hours) and the average student's attention span in a traditional lecture class (about 15 minutes). As a result, instructors must be creative in getting and keeping student engagement. While some instructors design lectures that resemble choreographed performances to capture students’ attention, this is certainly not the only way to engage an academic audience. Engaging lecturers draw on a range of strategies to captivate their students.
