They infuse lectures with activities, sequence materials to pique students’ interest, and allow student contributions to shape discussions, building rapport and fostering meaningful analysis of course content in the process.
What types of strategies and activities can we use in lectures so that student attention does not wane? How can we make certain that students leave class with the most important takeaways? How do we ensure that student contributions and questions are acknowledged in a way that encourages them without derailing our lesson plans? In this pathway, featured professors discuss how they engage students in their lecture-based courses.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will identify and summarize a variety of strategies and activities that can be integrated into lectures to maintain student engagement throughout the class period.
Design and implement a lecture plan that incorporates techniques to capture and sustain student attention, ensuring alignment with key learning outcomes.
Evaluate the effectiveness of different engagement strategies in enhancing student understanding and retention of course material.
Apply methods for acknowledging and incorporating student contributions into lectures without deviating from planned lesson objectives.

pathways.title: Feedback Strategies
pathways.description: Feedback is vital for bridging the gap between current and desired performance levels, guiding students effectively toward achieving learning objectives. This pathway delves into the transformative power of effective feedback, exploring diverse strategies from positive reinforcement to precise guidance. Learn how to integrate these strategies into teaching approaches to foster student growth and development.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the importance of feedback for student engagement and learning, summarizing its value for both individual and group contexts.
You will evaluate suitable feedback for students encountering task difficulties and the effectiveness of peer assistance or modeling for supporting struggling students.
You will develop a feedback protocol for peer-to-peer feedback sessions that encourages constructive criticism and supports student learning objectives.

pathways.title: Policy responses to the Coronavirus in Latin America (3 parts)
pathways.description: The module will discuss the new role of the state in fostering resilience and economic recovery after the pandemic in the Latin American countries. It will also list some policy measures adopted in the region to mitigate its impacts. The consequences for political conflict and political instability will be mentioned.

pathways.title: NASA | Granular Materials
pathways.description: Granular materials — like sand, rice, or powdered pharmaceuticals — are everywhere, yet their behavior is poorly understood. In some ways behaving like liquids, in other ways behaving like solids, such materials have unique properties and pose unique questions to answer. This pathway introduces granular materials and the research that NASA is undertaking with granular materials in space.
learning_objectives: Understand what granular materials are and how they behave.
Discover how NASA studies granular materials in space.

pathways.title: Worldwide Weather: Why Do Storms Move In Predictable Patterns Around The World?
pathways.description:
Students explore why storms tend to follow specific global paths by investigating how sunlight heats Earth unevenly and how air moves in response. They examine global temperature patterns, model atmospheric circulation, and explore the Coriolis effect to explain how large-scale wind patterns influence weather movement across the planet.
This learning sequence contains lessons 12 - 15 of the GLOBE Weather Curriculum.
Accompanying Learning Sequences:
• From Cloud to Storm (Lessons 1 - 6)
• A Front Headed Your Way (Lessons 7 - 11)
• Snow Day (Culminating Challenge Tasks)
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to describe how unequal solar heating creates temperature differences around the globe.
Explain how warm air rises near the equator and cooler air replaces it, forming circulation patterns.
Model how global convection cells form and drive air movement in the atmosphere.
Analyze how the Coriolis effect curves wind and storm paths across Earth.
Use evidence and models to explain global storm movement in the tropics and midlatitudes.

pathways.title: From Cloud to Storm: What Causes an Isolated Storm?
pathways.description:
Students start with an investigation of short-lived, isolated storms, learning how they typically occur in the afternoon, the relationship between isolated storms and air temperature, and what determines whether growing clouds will cause precipitation.
This learning sequence contains lessons 1 - 6 of the GLOBE Weather Curriculum.
Accompanying Learning Sequences:
• A Front Headed Your Way (Lessons 7 - 11)
• Worldwide Weather (Lessons 12 - 15)
• Snow Day (Culminating Challenge Tasks)
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to use models and observations to describe how temperature and humidity contribute to storm formation.
Explain how convection moves warm, moist air upward to form clouds and precipitation.
Analyze real-world data to identify patterns that lead to isolated storms.
Communicate findings using diagrams, simulations, and weather data.

pathways.title: Food and Climate Change
pathways.description: Delve into the intricate relationship between climate change, food production, and food security. Students analyze their own food choices, learn how climate change and food production impact each other, understand food insecurity and solutions, and as a culminating project, create a concept map that connects all their learning.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will analyze the relationship between food and climate change.
Evaluate the environmental impact of food choices.
Investigate global and local challenges in food systems.
Propose and design sustainable solutions.
Develop systems thinking and communication skills.

pathways.title: Systems Analysis, Design, and Development
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the core principles, tools, and strategies involved in systems analysis and design. Discover how information systems intersect with daily life, and what goes into designing these systems. This chapter is part of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: Understand the principles, roles, and methodologies involved in systems analysis and design, including Agile and user-centered approaches.
Identify and apply key tools and techniques used in the design and development of information systems, such as data flow diagrams, data dictionaries, and iterative feedback methods.
Evaluate the importance of clearly defined user requirements, effective communication, and system design considerations in achieving successful project outcomes.

pathways.title: How Do Motor Proteins Help Cells Maintain Their Structure and Internal Organisation?
pathways.description: How do cells receive developmental cues and other specialised signals? How do your airways prevent dangerous particles from reaching your lungs? And how do sperm swim? The answer to all these: cilia. In the Cellular Organization Lab at Illinois State University in the US, Dr Martin Engelke and a team of cellular and molecular biologists are investigating the role that motor proteins play in building and maintaining these crucial cell structures.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms cilia, kinesin-2, intraflagellar transport (IFT), and motor proteins.
Identify the differences between motile and non-motile cilia.
Recall the role of kinesin-2 in maintaining cilia structure.
Explain how IFT enables cilia formation and maintenance.

pathways.title: How the Latest Tech Is Unveiling the Brain’s Complex Functionality
pathways.description: Understanding how our brains work in health and disease is hugely challenging, not least because all interactions have to be performed on a delicate, complex, actively working brain. Magnetoencephalography (MEG) is a powerful non-invasive technique that detects brain function by measuring the magnetic fields produced by the brain’s electrical activity. With this technique, it is possible to study and diagnose many brain functions and mental health disorders. Dr Orang Alem, Dr Svenja Knappe, Dr Jeramy Hughes and their team at FieldLine Medical have developed a novel and wearable MEG device using their quantum sensor technology.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define magnetoencephalography (MEG) and explain its purpose in neuroscience.
You will explain the meaning of cryogenic cooling and its role in traditional MEG devices.
You will discuss how wearable MEG devices might improve diagnosis and treatment of brain conditions.
You will differentiate between MEG, EEG, and MRI in terms of brain activity monitoring.

pathways.title: How Is Climate Change Affecting Accounting and Business?
pathways.description: Climate change is creating unprecedented risks for businesses around the world, which is making investors nervous. A key way that businesses can address this anxiety is through disclosing how they impact, and are impacted by, climate change. Dr Sanjay Banerjee of the University of Alberta in Canada is researching how businesses are going about this, and what this means for future policy and regulation.
learning_objectives: Explain how climate change presents both physical and transition risks for businesses.
Examine how institutional investors influence corporate climate risk disclosures.
Differentiate between voluntary and mandatory climate disclosures, and analyze their effectiveness in promoting corporate transparency.

pathways.title: What Can Arctic Rocks Teach Us About Life on Mars?
pathways.description: On top of snow-capped mountains high in the Canadian Arctic, giant slabs of rock may hold the answers to an age-old question: is there life on Mars? Éloïse Brassard, a PhD student at the Université de Sherbrooke in Canada, studies a type of rock formation known as gossans. She aims to understand their geological characteristics and develop methods for detecting similar formations on Mars using satellite imagery.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will have a greater understanding of the field of geomatics.
You will understand how studying geological formations on Earth can give us more information about the surface of Mars.
You will know what a gossan is and what makes them unique.

pathways.title: The Slow and Silent Earthquakes That Are Shaking up Seismology
pathways.description: The tectonic plates that make up the Earth’s crust are constantly shifting. When they collide, they can cause cataclysmic earthquakes and tsunamis; but sometimes, the effects are far less obvious. Dr Rebecca Bell of Imperial College London is researching how these silent earthquakes might affect the Earth – and us.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will identify and recall the characteristics of tectonic plates and the structure of Earth's crust, including the ductile mantle and the rigid crust.
Explain the concept of slow slip events and their potential implications for natural disasters, such as earthquakes and tsunamis
Apply your knowledge of geology to interpret the significance of using sound waves in the research of slow slip events and how this technique can contribute to earthquake prediction efforts.

pathways.title: The Amazing Complexity of Ecological Metacommunities
pathways.description: Ecosystems are hugely complex. Every animal, plant, fungus and microbe is interacting with countless others, as well as with their ever-changing environment. Understanding these intertwining relationships is highly challenging, but Dr Mathew Leibold, from the University of Florida in the US, believes it is possible. He champions the concept of ‘metacommunities’, which can help us appreciate how ecosystems function and are affected by human activity, and how we can conserve our natural world.
learning_objectives: By the end of the learning pathway, learners will be able to explain the concept of metacommunities and their significance in understanding biodiversity patterns.
Describe the interdependence of species within ecosystems and how biodiversity contributes to ecosystem stability.
Apply the concept of metacommunities to analyze how different environmental conditions influence species interactions and biodiversity.
Critically assess the implications of biodiversity loss for ecosystem resilience and human well-being.

pathways.title: Creating an Emotional Climate
pathways.description: Refine your teaching approach by mastering effective techniques for fostering a positive emotional climate in your classroom. This lesson series introduces the concept of emotional climate, exploring how it influences teacher-student interactions and student-student dynamics. You'll learn to foster a warm, cohesive environment where all students feel validated, respected, and motivated to participate actively.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the concept of emotional climate and its impact on classroom dynamics.
You will identify observable indicators of a positive emotional climate, including teacher-student interactions and student engagement.
You will explore strategies for fostering inclusivity, empathy, and respect for diversity in the classroom.

pathways.title: Creating an Inclusive Learning Environment Guide
pathways.description: Discover strategies for addressing real-world ethical dilemmas faced by educators, providing actionable guidance to nurture inclusive learning environments. This pathway explore methods to combat bullying, promote collaboration, and cater to the needs of diverse learners. Dive into the ten essential dimensions of inclusive learning environments, covering everything from defining learning goals and organizing content to creating effective assessment techniques.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the Inclusive Classroom Guide as a capacity-building tool for educators, focusing on quality and inclusive teaching practices, while incorporating pluralism and ethics to enhance teacher-student and student-student relationships.
You will analyze the significance of each dimension of inclusive learning environments for promoting a positive emotional climate and instructional quality.

You will evaluate the effectiveness of development dialogues as a means of promoting teacher reflection and enhancing classroom inclusivity, considering their impact on professional growth and instructional practices.

pathways.title: Differentiated Teaching
pathways.description: Differentiated teaching strategies require teachers to adjust their teaching practices to help extend the knowledge and skills of every student, regardless of their starting point. This pathway will define differentiation and provide an opportunity to see differentiated teaching strategies in practice in a classroom.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will have a greater comprehension of differentiated teaching practices.
You will understand the importance of identifying individual student needs and providing appropriate support.
You will know how to utilize personalized work plans tailored to each student's needs and preferences to accommodate diverse learning styles and abilities.

pathways.title: How Can Diabetes Lead to Vision Loss, and How Can This Be Prevented?
pathways.description: Diabetic retinopathy is the leading cause of blindness among working-age adults in the developed world. Dr David Antonetti, a molecular and cell biologist at the University of Michigan, USA, works in the field of ophthalmology to investigate how diabetes causes eye issues. He hopes his research will lead to the development of new treatments for diabetes-related vision loss.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the link between diabetes and vision loss caused by diabetic retinopathy.
You will describe the role of the blood-retinal barrier in maintaining the neural environment of the retina.
You will analyze the intricate relationship between cytokine signaling and the modifications observed in the blood-retinal barrier in individuals with diabetes.

pathways.title: Dissecting Supermassive Black Holes
pathways.description: Explore the relationship between supermassive black holes and their host galaxies, and learn how jets and winds from these cosmic giants impact both local and distant star formation. Delve into the intricate features surrounding active supermassive black holes, also known as active galactic nuclei.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the effects of supermassive black holes on their host galaxies and the larger cosmic environment.
Explain the role of supermassive blackholes in star formation.
Know the structure of a supermassive black hole.

pathways.title: How Can We Treat Multi-Drug Resistant Tuberculosis?
pathways.description: Professor Andrew Nunn is an epidemiologist at the Medical Research Council Clinical Trials Unit at University College London in the UK. His research focuses on clinical trials of drugs that have the potential to prevent and treat infectious diseases. He is currently working to develop a treatment regimen for a form of tuberculosis that is resistant to the most effective drugs commonly used.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to analyze the importance of well-conducted clinical trials in making life-changing progress in the field of epidemiology and identify potential contributions towards such progress.

You will be able to explain key concepts related to tuberculosis (TB) and its treatment, including drug resistance, efficacy, effectiveness, adherence, adverse effects, reproducibility, generalizability, superiority trials, and non-inferiority trials.

Understand the challenges posed by multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB) and the significance of clinical trials, such as the STREAM trial, in developing more effective treatment regimens for MDR-TB.

pathways.title: Paid to Study, Research and Communicate Science
pathways.description: Undergraduate scientists at the University of Saint Francis are receiving scholarships from the National Science Foundation to learn and share their knowledge with children, teenagers, the public and industry – and it’s all in the name of Britain’s first scientist, Roger Bacon.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway you should learn about the concept of science communication.
Understand the value of practical application of scientific concepts and how it enhances their understanding of the subject.
Learn the process of designing and conducting research projects, including formulating scientific questions, data collection, and analysis.

pathways.title: Storage Solutions: Can We Reimagine the Trade-Offs That Limit Flash Storage Devices?
pathways.description: Experts from the International Data Group have predicted that by 2025, every person who is connected to the internet will engage with digital data almost 5,000 times every day. This will result in an extraordinary amount of data, and a lot of it will need to be stored somewhere. Most of the data that we currently create, like photos, videos and word documents, are stored on flash drives on our phones, laptops or portable USB devices. Dr Bryan S. Kim, from Syracuse University in the US, is studying some of the innovative methods that are being developed to help improve information storage.
learning_objectives: Learn how to embrace innovation in a rapidly evolving technology-driven world. Students will learn how to be open to new ideas and techniques to improve efficiency.
Understand trade-offs, as engineering solutions often involve trade-offs.
Learn how a capacity-variant storage system can reduce capacity as cells deteriorate, maintaining performance and reliability.

pathways.title: Bringing Life Back to Kenya’s Grasslands
pathways.description: Land degradation causes soils to become less fertile, resulting in lower productivity in agricultural regions and decreased biodiversity. Led by Professor Mariana Rufino at Lancaster University, UK, an international team of researchers, including Dr Joseph Hitimana at the University of Kabianga in Kenya, is working with Kenyan farmers to find solutions to this problem. The ReDEAL project is utilising a community-focused, bottom-up approach to help provide Kenyan farmers with the tools and knowledge to restore their soils.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the concept of land degradation and its impact on ecosystems and agriculture.
Identify the differences in perspectives between farmers and scientists in addressing land degradation and recognize the need for interdisciplinary collaboration in restoration efforts.
Apply knowledge of bottom-up empowerment and community engagement to develop a proposal to persuade farmers, land managers, and governments to change land management practices in a specific region.

pathways.title: Magma Detectives: Studying Volcanic Arcs to Find Valuable Minerals in Earth’s Crust
pathways.description: Mining companies are having to look deeper in the Earth’s crust for copper and other precious metals, but how do they know where to look? Scientists working on the FAMOS research project are searching for clues in the minerals formed in the magma plumbing systems of ancient volcanoes.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway  learners will be able to explain the importance of copper in modern society and understand the challenges facing mining companies in sourcing this vital metal
learners will be able to define geochemistry, describe its purpose, and understand its role in helping scientists to gain a better understanding of the formation of the Earth
Discuss why sophisticated exploration tools are necessary for mining companies to find ore deposits deep in the Earth's crust

pathways.title: Microplastic Pollution: How Bad Is It and What Can We Do to Solve It?
pathways.description: Discover the extent of microplastic pollution in Georgia's coastal waters and the research efforts led by Professor Jay Brandes and Dodie Sanders from the Skidaway Institute of Oceanography and the University of Georgia Marine Extension. Learn about the detrimental effects of microplastics on aquatic ecosystems, their sources, and the challenges in removing them. Explore practical strategies to reduce microplastic pollution and the role of citizen science in monitoring and combating this issue.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the significance of microplastic pollution in coastal waters and its impact on aquatic ecosystems.
Explore the sources and pathways of microplastics, including their entry into the water system through activities like washing plastic-based textiles.
Learn about the research methods used to measure and analyze microplastics in water samples, including visual examination with microscopes.
Discover practical strategies and the importance of citizen science in reducing microplastic pollution.

pathways.title: How Can Vaccinating Pigs Protect People?
pathways.description: Many disease outbreaks, including Covid-19, are caused by viruses that have jumped from animals to humans. At the Pirbright Institute in the UK, Dr Rebecca McLean and Professor Simon Graham are studying one particular virus which did this in 1998 and continues to pose a threat today. They have developed and tested potential vaccines which, if delivered to pigs, could prevent future outbreaks of Nipah virus in humans.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand key terms associated with vaccine development
Know more about the Nipha Virus and the efforts being made to contain it

pathways.title: How Can We Improve the Cost-Effectiveness of Medical Trials?
pathways.description: Before a new treatment can be given to patients, it must first undergo medical trials, to ensure it is safe and effective. Dr Howard Thom is a health economist at the University of Bristol, UK, who hopes to improve this time-consuming process by encouraging the use of ‘adaptive trials’. These trials could reduce unnecessary research, save time and money, lead to better decision making and, ultimately, improve patient care.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will learn the key terminology related to health economics
Understand the role that health economists play in the healthcare system
Learn about the structure of healthcare trails

pathways.title: The Great Dying: Unpicking the Permo-Triassic Extinction Event
pathways.description: 252 million years ago, over 90% of all species on Earth were wiped out. Led by Professor Paul Wignall at the University of Leeds in the UK, a transnational project involving British and Chinese research teams is investigating this colossal and ancient crime scene, attempting to understand the relationships between species extinctions and environmental changes. Their conclusions could affect how we approach today’s climate and biodiversity crisis.
learning_objectives: Learn how ecoPT is studying the Permo-Triassic extinction event to understand how different types of organisms recovered and eventually diversified into many different species.
Understand the importance of fossilized pollen and spores in studying past vegetation and ecosystem responses to environmental change.
Learn about the different types of organism studied in palaeobiology, and how their diversity changes over time during extinction events.

pathways.title: How Do Sea Level Oscillations Contribute to Sea Level Extremes?
pathways.description: Discover the importance of studying sub-hourly sea level oscillations and how Dr Jadranka Šepić and her team are working to fill in knowledge gaps on this topic. Find out which data they are collecting and how they plan to analyze it. Read on to understand the science behind sea level extremes and the impact of climate change on coastlines.
learning_objectives: Students will be able to define key terms related to sea level, such as extreme sea levels, mean sea level, meltwater, atmospheric gravity wave, storm surge, and seismic.
Students will understand the contribution of sea level oscillations to sea level extremes.
Students will understand how rising sea levels due to global warming and climate change are increasing flood risks associated with extreme sea levels.

pathways.title: Forget Everything You Thought You Knew
pathways.description: Dr Fabio Costa, based at the University of Queensland in Australia, is investigating causal relations in quantum physics. The ultimate aim is to determine how quantum physics might change our notion of cause and effect, and the findings could lead to improvements in quantum computing.
learning_objectives: In this learning pathway you will explore how the theory of quantum physics has evolved and has now become a forefront in the advancement of technology.
Learn what it take to become a quantum physicist, and what it entails to pursue such a career.
Understand what quantum superposition by using an example of tossing a quantum coin and catching a bus.

pathways.title: Introduction to Computational Biology
pathways.description: Computational biology uses models built from experimental data to analyze and predict biological systems. This pathway introduces programming tools, probability, statistics, and genomic databases central to bioinformatics.
learning_objectives: Learn how programming and statistics combine to answer biological questions using real data.
Develop intuition for randomness, noise, and variability in biological measurements.
Gain skills in choosing appropriate models and statistical tests for biological data.
Understand how interdisciplinary thinking drives careers in computational biology.

pathways.title: How and Where Do Minerals Form?
pathways.description:
Did you know that there are more than 5,000 different kinds of minerals on Earth? Where do they come from? Some form deep underground, while some form on the surface. Some form in just seconds, while others take thousands or millions of years to form. One thing all minerals have in common is that they come from nature. Minerals can form anywhere. 
Explore these five types of environments: Igneous, pegmatitic, metamorphic, hydrothermal, and weathering. How do they produce Earth's amazing variety of minerals?

pathways.title: NASA | Cold Atom Physics
pathways.description: Here, we introduce learners to the physics of matter—from classical states to counterintuitive quantum systems—through the lens of research on the ISS. Explore states of matter, Bose–Einstein condensates, radiation pressure, and the ultra-cold atom experiments conducted in NASA’s Cold Atom Lab. Together, these resources build a foundational understanding of particle behavior, energy, and quantum phenomena in extreme environments.
learning_objectives: Compare the major states of matter, including classical and quantum states.
Understand how Bose–Einstein condensates form and why ultra-cold temperatures produce unique quantum behaviors.
See why a microgravity environment is necessary for in-depth research on the movement of atoms and molecules.
Be able to describe how radiation pressure slows atoms and molecules and its role in laser cooling.

pathways.title: Understanding Business Strategies for Science Communicators
pathways.description: Did you know the internet held 40 zettabytes of data by 2020? That's a lot of information to sort through! Luckily there are tools to help you make your own content stand out in a sea of data. This pathway covers essential digital business strategies for science communicators, including social media marketing, content management systems (CMS), web analytics, and marketing metrics. Learners will explore data-driven decision-making, audience engagement, SEO, KPIs, and effective content publishing.
learning_objectives: Recognize the role of web analytics and key performance indicators in measuring the effectiveness of digital marketing strategies.
Describe the importance of marketing metrics and digital communication tools in developing successful science communication strategies.
Understand how social media platforms and content management systems support business communication and audience engagement in STEM fields.
Explain how data-driven decision making and A/B testing contribute to optimizing online content and business outcomes.

pathways.title: Strategic Marketing and Planning
pathways.description: Marketing is a strategic driver of growth across sectors, from startups to global brands. This pathway introduces science communicators to core business strategy concepts—marketing plans, strategic planning, SMART goals, data science fundamentals, and social media branding. Ideal for educators and learners in science communication, entrepreneurship, and business development, it builds career-ready, cross-disciplinary skills.
learning_objectives: Recognize how data science principles and tools, such as SWOT analysis and gap analysis, inform decision-making and performance evaluation in business.
Describe the importance of social media platforms in building brand presence and engaging diverse audiences through cost-effective digital marketing strategies.
Explain the purpose of SMART goals and mission statements in creating actionable and measurable plans that drive growth and innovation in science communication and entrepreneurship.
Understand the key components of marketing and strategic planning that support effective business strategies aligned with organizational goals in STEM contexts.

pathways.title: Engaging Public Audiences as a Science Communicator
pathways.description: Understanding your audience is essential for building a successful career in science communication. This pathway equips aspiring and practicing science communicators with practical tools—from crafting compelling elevator pitches to using social media effectively—to share stories in science with clarity and impact. Featuring expert insights and interactive content, it supports learners in enhancing public engagement with science.
learning_objectives: Identify audience-focused communication strategies to present scientific ideas clearly and effectively across a variety of audiences.
Develop the ability to develop and deliver concise, engaging summaries of scientific work tailored to different contexts, including public, academic, and digital settings.

pathways.title: Nitro-Go-Round: The Cycle That Feeds the World
pathways.description: Explore the Nitro-Go-Round, an engaging pathway that reveals how nitrogen moves through the atmosphere, soil, water, plants, and animals to sustain life. Learn key processes—nitrogen fixation, nitrification, ammonification, and denitrification—and examine how human activities like fertilizer use impact ecosystems.
learning_objectives: Describe how nitrogen cycles through Earth’s systems and supports life.
Identify the roles of microorganisms in nitrogen transformation processes such as nitrogen fixation and denitrification.
Explain the importance of nitrogen in agriculture and ecosystem productivity.
Assess the effects of human activities on the nitrogen cycle and propose sustainable practices to maintain environmental balance.

pathways.title: Quadratic Functions: Graphs and Applications
pathways.description: Practice graphing quadratic functions using vertices, intercepts, and symmetry. Apply factoring, the quadratic formula, and completing the square to solve equations. Explore real-world applications like business profits and draining water with quadratic models.
learning_objectives: Graph quadratic functions using vertex, axis of symmetry, and intercepts.
Solve quadratic equations using factoring, quadratic formula, and completing the square.
Interpret x- and y-intercepts from quadratic equations in standard form.
Analyze the effect of coefficients on parabola orientation and width.
Apply quadratic functions to real-world contexts, such as business and water flow.
Use symmetry properties to find roots and verify solutions.

pathways.title: Guardian Frogs of Borneo: Why Exceptions Matter in Biology and Everyday Life
pathways.description:
Out of 8,000+ frog species, Guardian Frogs are one of the first to show females that call & males that care for offspring. Students will gain wonder and appreciation for how diverse animal mating systems are while doing fun activities and analyzing real data.
Driving Question(s):
• Across animal species, is there truth to the stereotype that males are “competitive,” while females are “caring”, or is this an oversimplification?
• Are the smooth guardian frogs of Borneo the first known species of frog with “competitive females” and “caring males”?
Essential Question(s):
• How can we use scientific methods to identify a general pattern?
• Why do exceptions matter in science and in life?
Hooks
• Part 1: Highly interactive card-based activity that simulates data collection + amazing animal videos
• Part 2: Atmospheric videos of Borneo, first-person narrative of an early career Latina scientist + use of authentic data
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to identify key terms related to sex differences, mating systems, and parental care in animals.
Recall the key behaviors observed in the Smooth Guardian Frog (Limnonectes palavanensis).
Explain why the Guardian Frog’s behaviors are considered exceptional in the context of animal mating systems.
Distinguish between typical and atypical sex-role behaviors across multiple animal species using provided data.
Compare and contrast male and female calling behaviors in frogs based on authentic scientific evidence.
Design a persuasive written argument explaining why conserving the Smooth Guardian Frog matters, using data and scientific reasoning.

pathways.title: How Can New Biomedical Imaging Techniques Help Us Understand Strokes?
pathways.description: Strokes can be devastating. Depending on their severity, they can cause issues with movement and muscle control, a loss of feeling and sensation, and degenerative diseases such as dementia. Understanding how strokes affect the brain can help researchers develop therapeutic interventions that aid recovery. Dr Jonathan Thiessen and Dr Shawn Whitehead from Western University, in Canada, are studying how to combine positron emission tomography (PET) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to gain a better understanding of the effects of strokes and how to treat them.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms such as biomedical imaging, stroke, dementia, PET, and MRI.
Identify the role of positron emission tomography (PET) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) in medical diagnostics.
Explain the differences and similarities between PET and MRI in brain imaging.
Describe the impact of strokes on brain function and their link to dementia.

pathways.title: NASA | Earth Action
pathways.description:
Earth Day is commemorated around the world each year on April 22.  These resources aim to help students learn more about their local environments and make an impact in their communities, as well as protect and improve life on Earth.
Join NASA researchers and discover the secrets of the universe, search for life elsewhere, and protect and improve life on Earth and in space via Citizen Science Earth Projects.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand how your actions play a part in improving life on Earth.
You will identify the importance of modeling future climate patterns in order to create sustainable action plans.
You will recognize the importance of clean water, fresh air, and diverse ecosystems in maintaining a healthy Earth.

pathways.title: NASA | Earth’s Ocean and Water Resources
pathways.description: For decades, researchers have observed Earth’s ocean and water resources to understand their fluctuations, observe their connection to habitability, and provide critical data to protect our planet's crucial waterways. NASA and its partners leverage Earth observation data to enhance water management and facilitate innovative solutions to stressors on one of our most precious natural resources.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the importance of monitoring Earth's water resources.
You will identify the impacts of sea level rise as a result of climate change.
You will understand the importance of data collection in visualizing and managing water resources.
You will engage with real water resource data to predict future sea level rise.

pathways.title: NASA | Aerosols and Atmosphere
pathways.description: Did you know that every time you take a breath, it’s nearly certain that you’ll inhale tens of millions of solid particles and liquid droplets? Explore this pathway to help you learn about how researchers and communities use data to understand the human impacts on the air and atmosphere.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the impact of aerosols on Earth's atmosphere and climate.
You will explain the importance of the Plankton, Aerosol, Cloud, ocean Ecosystem (PACE) mission and the data it collects.
You will understand how data collection can be used to improve the accuracy of air quality forecasts.

pathways.title: Can You Protect Astronauts From the Heat of Re-Entry?
pathways.description:
What distinguishes heat from temperature? Find the answer to this question as you explore thermal dynamics through hands-on experiments, testing conductivity to design a NASA heat shield for the Artemis mission, while modeling heat transfer and investigating advanced materials for future space exploration and related careers. This learning pathway spans 360 minutes over 7 days and is accompanied by a student guide.












Pathway breakdown:








• ENGAGE: Day 1: 40 minutes in class, item 1 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 60 minutes in class, items 2-6 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 60 minutes in class, item 7 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4-6: 120 minutes in class, items 8-11 of the pathway and student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 7: 60 minutes in class, items 12-13 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the effect of thermal energy on a substance.
Apply the physical and chemical properties of a substance to predict how it will react to thermal energy.
Understand and explain how heat travels.

pathways.title: Effective Facilitation
pathways.description: Effective facilitation is essential for promoting student learning and achievement. It involves fostering a dynamic and inclusive environment where students are motivated to participate and learn. This pathway provides interactive and collaborative learning strategies to cultivate a culture of inquiry and creativity, while promoting inclusivity and diversity among students.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will recognize the importance of effective facilitation in promoting student engagement and learning.
You will identify observable indicators of effective facilitation, such as student engagement, multi-modal instruction, and open-ended questioning.
You will explore various instructional techniques and activities to create engaging and interactive learning experiences.

pathways.title: Framing the Discussion
pathways.description:
To an observer, effective discussions can look seamless. A question or two seems gets the conversation started, and students take it from there, sharing insightful perspectives, building on each other’s ideas, succinctly articulating the instructor’s key takeaways, all in perfect time. But the “behind-the-scenes” work to frame a discussion -- creating a space where daring, student-centered conversations can flourish; being clear about discussion objectives; asking the right, generative questions -- requires significant up-front planning.
What steps can we take in advance and in the moment to facilitate rich and objective-driven discussions? How can we keep up with a dynamic conversation while guaranteeing our objectives are met? When should discussion call for small groups instead of the whole class? How can students themselves play a role in framing the discussion? In this pathway, featured professors discuss their approaches to ensuring they get the most out of discussions and focus them meaningfully.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will analyze and apply effective techniques for initiating and sustaining productive classroom discussions, as outlined by experienced educators.
Create a structured plan for facilitating discussions that align with specific learning objectives and encourage student-centered learning.
Evaluate the benefits and challenges of using small group discussions versus whole class discussions in various educational settings.
Synthesize and implement strategies for crafting generative questions that promote deep engagement and critical thinking among students.

pathways.title: Exploring Lunar Rover Motion
pathways.description:
Become a junior space scientist and join a space adventure! Help Astronaut Alex to decipher the crucial connection between mass and a lunar rover's navigational prowess in this learning pathway, with the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 7 days of lessons (275 minutes) - experiment, brainstorm, and solve real-world challenges while exploring Newton's Laws of Motion with the following activities and simulation.
Pathway breakdown:
• BASICS: Items 1-2 of the pathway.
• ENGAGE: Day 1-2, 60 minutes, item 3 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2-3, 75 minutes, items 4-6 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 4-5, 80 minutes, item 7 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 6-7, 60 minutes, items 8-9 of the pathway.
Extend your learning by checking out the Meet the Martians learning pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will figure out why changing the weight on a rover can make it move differently on the Moon.
You will get to know Newton's Laws of Motion about how things move and use them to predict what will happen to objects on the Moon and Mars.
You will run your own experiments with a computer simulation to see how pushing or pulling can speed up or slow down a rover's journey.
You will build a mini rover that can handle different weights.
You will be able to explain how mass affects a rover's trip across the Moon, using graphs, pictures, and your own words.

pathways.title: Case Teaching
pathways.description:
The professional real world is complex and filled with uncertainty. Rather than avoiding this complexity, case-based instruction centers around cases that tell the stories of real-life protagonists facing difficult decisions. Cases often end with a straightforward question: Which path should the protagonist take? To answer this question, students carefully read the case and its documents before class. They then spend class time discussing the context, analyzing the data, and debating potential courses of action the protagonist could take. As a facilitator of the conversation, the case instructor tracks and guides the conversation, pushing students to further clarify their ideas, teasing out disagreements, and introducing useful frameworks for analyzing the case. The end of a case discussion usually involves a "reveal," where the actual outcome is shared and students have an opportunity to reflect on their predictions. For these reasons, case-based discussion classrooms are an exciting mix: they are intensely grounded in specifics yet centered around an open-ended question. Effective case teaching instructors prepare intensely for sessions but try to always follow their students’ lead as well. 
How do you plan a case-session that will cover the key concepts but is flexible enough to follow a spontaneous conversation? How can instructors effectively follow up with students to ensure their students’ ideas have been clearly articulated and explored? How can student disagreement be harnessed for deeper understanding? How can instructors make sure that all students have an opportunity to be heard in the classroom discussion? In these videos, featured instructor Julie Battilana discusses her approach and strategies for using case teaching with graduate students across the university.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will identify and recall the key components of case-based instruction, including the structure of a case discussion and the role of the instructor in facilitating the conversation.
Explain the importance of case preparation for students and instructors, and describe how real-world complexity is integrated into case-based learning.
Demonstrate the ability to plan a case-session that incorporates essential concepts while allowing for flexibility to accommodate spontaneous class discussions.

Assess the techniques used by instructors to ensure that all students are heard and that their ideas are clearly articulated and explored, including the use of "warm calls" and participation tracking.

Design a comprehensive strategy for leading a case-based discussion that encourages student engagement, manages classroom dynamics, and aligns with learning objectives, drawing on the insights and methods discussed.

pathways.title: Saved by the Light on the Moon
pathways.description:
Explore energy transformations with this educational pathway accompanying the student guide which spans 6 days (270-300 minutes) of lessons. Dive into kinetic and thermal energy through engaging videos, informative texts, teaching guides, and interactive simulations. Learn how these concepts can help design a solar hot water heater, potentially aiding future lunar missions.
Pathway breakdown: 
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 45 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 45 minutes, items 3-5 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 45 minutes, items 6 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 45 minutes, items 7-9 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 5 & 6, 90-120 minutes, item 10 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to relate temperature as a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles within a system and demonstrate how changes in temperature result from increases in the average kinetic energy of molecules in that system.

Be able to demonstrate how energy transformations can result in a transfer of energy from one system component to another. An influx in thermal energy (heat) can lead to a rise in the average kinetic energy of the particles in a system component, which can be measured as an increase in temperature.
Be able to design, build, and test a solar hot water heater to maximize temperature changes within a system.

pathways.title: Human Ecology and Anthropogenic Impacts
pathways.description: Delve into disaster risk reduction, global health, and innovative solutions. Investigate climate risk insurance with a sociological perspective and challenge paradigms. Join the conversation for a resilient future.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the complexities of disaster risk reduction, including exposure, vulnerability, and social inequalities.
Explore the interconnectedness of global health and the environment.
Explore the importance of ecosystem resilience in the face of climate change.
Understand the concept of environmental justice and its fundamental definition as the right to fair treatment in environmental matters.
Understand the concept of the "critical zone" as the thin, habitable layer between Earth's surface and the atmosphere.

pathways.title: James Webb Space Telescope - First Science Snippets
pathways.description: NASA's James Webb Space Telescope is designed to measure infrared wavelengths from distant stars, planets and galaxies. Discover how Webb science allows us to unravel the mysteries of the cosmos, and begins our journey in anserwing the question: are we alone in the universe?
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will learn the importance of calibrating to instrumentation on the James Webb Space Telescope.
Understand how the James Webb Space Telescope uses spectroscopy to learn how stars are formed.
Discover the enormous impact the first scientific data from the James Webb Space Telescope, and how it helps us learn more about our universe.

pathways.title: Flights of Fancy: Can We Unravel the Mystery of Flight?
pathways.description: Charles Darwin’s theory of natural selection is one of the most important and ground-breaking scientific concepts ever constructed. Even so, there are some aspects of evolution that scientists are still trying to understand. Dr Ashley Heers from California State University in Los Angeles, USA, is studying juvenile birds to unravel one such mystery that has had evolutionary biologists puzzled for decades: the evolution of flight.
learning_objectives: After completing the pathway, you will understand the concept of natural selection and its role in the evolution of species, with a focus on the development of wings for flight.
Explore the function and significance of rudimentary locomotor structures in birds, and how studying these structures can provide insights into the evolutionary history of flight.

pathways.title: Your Pain Is My Pain: What Makes Us Feel Empathy?
pathways.description: The ability to feel what others are feeling, a phenomenon known as empathy, is a key part of people’s social behaviour. Despite us knowing how empathy feels, the neural processes behind empathy have long remained unknown. Dr Valeria Gazzola and Professor Christian Keysers, of The Social Brain Lab based in the Netherlands Institute for Neuroscience, employ the latest techniques in neuroscience to uncover how this process plays out in the brain.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the methods used in neuroscience research, such as functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), to study empathy at the brain level.
Describe the use of fMRI in studying brain activity.
Synthesize knowledge by designing an experiment to investigate differences in levels of empathy among human individuals while considering the variables that need to be controlled.

pathways.title: Imaging the Invisible: How Can Research Software and Imaging Techniques Help Scientists Study the Things We Can’t See?
pathways.description: From microscopic plankton to individual atoms, the subjects of many scientific studies need special devices to be seen. Scientific imaging devices, like electron microscopes, have become increasingly powerful in recent years, allowing scientists to study their subjects in even more detail. These devices need to be partnered with software to allow the images they produce to be analysed. Dr Joanna Leng, from the University of Leeds in the UK, is a research software engineer who designs and develops the software that allows scientific imaging devices to be used to their full potential.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway students will learn the importance of the power of scientific imaging.
By the end of this pathway students will recognize the importance of computer simulation.
By the end of this pathway students will consider research computing and software engineering as valuable career paths.

pathways.title: Manipulating the Brain’s ‘Gatekeepers’ Could Thwart Obesity and Prevent Dementia
pathways.description: Dr Vincent Prevot is a neuroendocrinologist based at the Inserm in Lille, France. He collaborates with Professor Markus Schwaninger and Dr Ruben Nogueiras on the WATCH project, which could help prevent, stall or reverse age-related diseases.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the role of tanycytes in regulating signal access from tissues to the brain, and how this communication impacts brain function.
You will understand the concept of metabolism and how tanycytes can impact metabolic diseases by facilitating the transport of messages to the appropriate neuronal circuits.
You will be able to evaluate the potential benefits and societal impact of the WATCH team's research on tanycytes and the hypothalamus.
You will develop a proposal for the funding of a similar research project.

pathways.title: The Milk of Human Kindness
pathways.description: Exploring the realm of medical research unveils its crucial impact on understanding human health, improving treatments, and addressing pressing issues. This pathway emphasizes the rewards and challenges of medical research in human milk supplementation, including the importance of translating research into real impact and the need for awareness and critical thinking regarding funding sources and conflicts of interest in the field.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the importance of human milk for the development of premature and vulnerable babies.
You will recognize the benefits of donor human milk (DHM) and how milk banks work, including screening donors and treating milk, and distribution to hospitals and families.
You will learn about the unique composition of human milk, and how it varies in response to the needs of the baby.

pathways.title: Can Quantum Physics Make the Internet More Secure?
pathways.description: Associate Professor Jacquiline (Jacq) Romero, a quantum physicist at the University of Queensland in Australia, is conducting experiments with photons to investigate quantum entanglement. Her research is helping to develop the quantum internet – the internet of the future will be very different from the internet today.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will better understand the difference between the regular internet and the quantum internet, including how the quantum internet transmits qubits and not bits like regular internet.
Understand the significance of quantum physics as a field of study and its potential impact on technology.
Learn what entanglement is, how to define entanglement, and how it works in quantum physics.

pathways.title: A History of Harvard Medical School
pathways.description: Distinguished Professor of Medicine and Medical Education discusses the founding of Harvard Medical School and its various developmental phases.

pathways.title: Education Matters
pathways.description: This pathway provides a glimpse into the personal and educational experiences that influenced Harvard Medical School students and educators in their careers. These individuals discuss their journeys into the medical field, including the challenges they faced and the factors that motivated them to pursue their careers. Through their stories, they offer a window into the passion and dedication that drives their work in medicine and healthcare.

pathways.title: NASA | Dusty Plasmas
pathways.description: Plasma is the most abundant state of matter in the universe, and it behaves very differently from solids, liquids, and gases. This pathway guides learners from foundational concepts like what plasma is and how it forms, to hands-on demonstrations, space-based experiments, and microgravity research that models atomic interactions. Explore plasma in everyday phenomena, laboratory investigations, and NASA missions, building a conceptual bridge between atomic theory, electromagnetism, and space science.
learning_objectives: Understand that plasma is a distinct state of matter.
Compare plasma behavior with solids, liquids, and gases under different energy conditions.
Analyze how electromagnetic forces govern plasma behavior in both laboratory settings and space environments such as the ionosphere.
Evaluate experimental design strategies used to study plasma, including microgravity research, scaled particle modeling, and sounding rocket missions.

pathways.title: Inspiring High School Students in Clinical Research
pathways.description: Working as a clinical researcher can have massive, positive effects on other people’s lives, giving them the opportunity to live longer and healthier. However, the field is in desperate need of more trained research professionals. The NYU Grossman School of Medicine at New York University Langone Health (NYULH) in New York, USA, has developed an awareness initiative to teach high school and undergraduate students more about this field, with the aim of inspiring the next generation of clinical researchers.
learning_objectives: Explain how clinical and translational research improve patient outcomes and advance healthcare.
Use principles of study design to outline a basic clinical research investigation addressing a health issue.
Examine how health equity and community engagement influence clinical trial participation and outcomes.
Assess the effectiveness of translational research strategies in moving scientific discoveries into real-world medical practice.

pathways.title: NASA | Semiconductors
pathways.description: Semiconductors are often called the “brains” of modern electronics, driving progress in energy, communication, and aerospace technology. This pathway explores how silicon forms the foundation of these systems, enabling innovations that advance sustainability on Earth while powering exploration beyond our planet.
learning_objectives: Define semiconductors and describe their role in modern electronics.
Explain how silicon is modified to function as a semiconductor.
Describe how solar panels convert light into usable energy using semiconductor principles.
Compare different types of semiconductor technologies and their efficiency in extreme environments

pathways.title: Why Do People Working In STEM Stay In Their Jobs – And Why Should We Care?
pathways.description: Dr Kohyar Kiazad and his colleagues are looking into reasons why people working in STEM stay in – not leave – their jobs. With the Australian Government investing heavily in this sector, the researchers’ unique approach could help policymakers and organisations understand how to strengthen the STEM workforce and retain key staff.
subject_tags: Economics:Firms, Work, and the Labor Market
learning_objectives: By the end of the learning pathway, learners will be able to define the concept of job embeddedness and explain its significance in employee retention.
Summarize the key factors that contribute to STEM professionals staying in their jobs, including both work and non-work influences.
Explain why the reasons for staying in a job are not simply the inverse of reasons for leaving.
Apply the theory of job embeddedness to a hypothetical scenario involving a STEM professional considering a career change.
Differentiate between intrinsic and extrinsic motivators for STEM professionals as revealed in the research.

pathways.title: A Front Headed Your Way: What Other Types of Storms Cause Precipitation?
pathways.description:
Students investigate how cold fronts create a different kind of storm from the isolated storms they studied earlier. Using real weather data, models, and simulations, they explore how temperature, humidity, air pressure, and air movement interact to form and move cold fronts. The sequence concludes with an analysis of the 2013 Colorado flood to explain how a stalled front can lead to extreme precipitation.
This learning sequence contains lessons 7 - 11 of the GLOBE Weather Curriculum.
Accompanying Learning Sequences:
• From Cloud to Storm (Lessons 1 - 6)
• Worldwide Weather (Lessons 12 - 15)
• Snow Day (Culminating Challenge Tasks)
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, learners will be able to identify and compare the characteristics of isolated storms and cold front storms using videos, models, and real-world data.
Analyze patterns in temperature, humidity, and air pressure before, during, and after the passage of a cold front.
Explain how differences in air density and pressure cause cold fronts to move and produce precipitation.
Use models and maps to describe how warm air is lifted along a cold front, leading to cloud and storm formation.
Apply scientific reasoning to explain how a stalled front caused extreme precipitation in the 2013 Colorado flood.

pathways.title: Exploring Function Transformations and Optics
pathways.description: Students will learn to graph reflections of functions across axes, apply Snell’s law to refraction, analyze wavelength and speed changes in media, and demonstrate prism-based light dispersion into colors.
learning_objectives: Identify and represent reflections of functions across the x-axis and y-axis.
Explain refraction of light at media boundaries using Snell’s law.
Analyze how wavelength and speed of light vary in different media.
Demonstrate how prisms disperse white light into constituent colors.

pathways.title: Information Systems Project Management
pathways.description: Project management is a multidisciplinary way of ensuring that projects are on time, within budget, and in scope, and that deliverables are of good quality and risks are minimal. This pathway introduces foundational project management concepts, major methodologies like PMBOK, Agile, and PRINCE2, essential tools like risk assessment and work breakdown structures, and the five project stages. It also highlights career relevance across industries, making it ideal for exploring project-based learning or career development in business, IT, or healthcare. This pathway is Chapter 9 of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key project management methodologies such as PMBOK, Agile, and PRINCE2.
List the five phases of the project management lifecycle.
Describe the purpose and importance of using structured project management in multidisciplinary fields.
Explain the differences between predictive, adaptive, and incremental project development approaches.
Distinguish between different project management frameworks based on their strengths and suitability for various project types.

pathways.title: Emerging Technologies and Frontiers of Information Systems
pathways.description: This pathway introduces you to the concept of emerging technologies, their convergence with frontiers of information systems, and their societal and global impact. Through real-world examples, you will explore how innovation shapes technologies like AI, blockchain, AR/VR, and IoT, and examine both the opportunities and risks they present. The pathway also emphasizes the importance of research, ethics, and accessibility in technology development and application. This pathway is Chapter 10 of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define emerging technologies and provide examples of their application
Analyze how frontiers of information systems intersect with emerging technologies
Explore the societal and global implications of emerging technologies in various sectors
Evaluate the role of research and innovation in developing and sustaining emerging technologies
Examine ethical, accessibility, and cybersecurity considerations
Apply critical thinking to assess the benefits, risks, and challenges of innovative technologies

pathways.title: Information Systems Security Risk Management
pathways.description: This pathway introduces the core principles, tools, and strategies essential to securing digital information and managing cybersecurity risks in today’s interconnected world. Learn how data travels across networks, the threats that endanger it, and the defenses used to safeguard it—from firewalls and encryption to continuous monitoring. Explore legal, ethical, and compliance considerations in cybersecurity, and gain insight into real-world practices for building strong risk management plans. Finally, discover career pathways in cybersecurity and the certifications that can set you apart. Test your understanding with review questions designed to prepare you for further study or a career in this high-demand field. This pathway is Chapter 5 of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the foundations of information and network security, including key principles such as the CIA triad and the role of devices like routers and switches.
Identify common cyber threats and vulnerabilities, and describe technologies and countermeasures used to defend against them.
Explain key security frameworks and risk management strategies, including how to build, monitor, and improve an ISRM (Information Security and Risk Management) plan.
Evaluate career roles in cybersecurity, and explore the certifications and degree programs that support career advancement in the field.
Apply acquired knowledge through a set of review questions that reinforce core concepts in cybersecurity, risk management, and professional development.

pathways.title: Exploring How Arts and Culture Benefit Our Health
pathways.description: While we might hold the instinctive feeling that engaging with arts and culture is good for our health and wellbeing, investigating the extent of the benefits involved is no easy task. At the Centre for Performance Science at the Royal College of Music in the UK, Aaron Williamon, Rosie Perkins and Neta Spiro set out to do just this through a variety of research techniques. Their results, brought together in the HEartS project, have some important lessons for valuing and engaging with the arts.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms such as closed questions, open questions, and demographics.
You will list the four main ways the HEartS survey respondents linked arts participation with social connectedness.
You will describe how the COVID-19 pandemic influenced people's engagement with the arts.
You will develop a short survey to assess how classmates engage with the arts and its impact on their well-being.

pathways.title: Exposing Environmental Injustice Through Maps and Stories
pathways.description: Often, it is society’s poorest and most vulnerable people that are most exposed to environmental harm. This injustice is the subject of much research, but getting the evidence needed to bring about major change can be challenging. Dr Stephanie Rutherford, at Trent University, and Dr Michael Classens, at the University of Toronto, have teamed up with two local organisations in the Canadian city of Nogojiwanong/ Peterborough to document the relationship between social vulnerability and environmental risk in the area, with the aim of driving policy change.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will have a better understanding of the concept of environmental justice.
You will be able to identify environmental injustices, both locally and worldwide.
You will understand how mapping these injustices makes them more visible, therefore making them subject to policy changes.

pathways.title: How Wearable Tech Can Combat Parkinson’s Disease
pathways.description: Every time we move around, our body is performing a complex array of tasks to move us in the direction we intend to go and to keep us balanced as we do so. A number of diseases, such as Parkinson’s disease, damage this process. At the Balance Disorders Laboratory at Oregon Health & Science University in the US, Professor Fay Horak is investigating how exercise regimes and wearable technologies can help people with Parkinson’s disease to regain mobility and autonomy.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to identify and describe the primary functions of the body's balance system and how these can be affected by Parkinson's disease.
Explain the rationale behind using exercise regimes and wearable technologies to assist individuals with Parkinson's disease in improving their balance and mobility.
Demonstrate how specific exercises or technologies can be applied to support balance and mobility in individuals with Parkinson's disease.

pathways.title: Can Microalgae Improve Honeybee Health?
pathways.description: Thanks to their work as pollinators, bees play an essential role in agriculture. However, modern agricultural practices and climate change mean many bees are becoming malnourished. In the US Department of Agriculture’s Honey Bee Breeding, Genetics, and Physiology Research Laboratory, Dr Vincent Ricigliano is improving bee health by developing pollen substitutes and edible vaccines using microalgae.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to list the primary reasons for bee malnutrition, including the impacts of modern agricultural practices and climate change.

Explain the significance of bees as pollinators in agriculture and how Dr. Ricigliano's development of microalgae-based pollen substitutes and edible vaccines could improve bee health and pollination services.

design a simple experiment to test the effectiveness of a microalgae-based dietary supplement on bee health, modeling the research methods used in the USDA Honey Bee Breeding, Genetics, and Physiology Research Laboratory.

pathways.title: Creating Narratives of Resilience With People Affected by Cancer
pathways.description: The psychological toll that accompanies living with cancer can be immense. To aid the mental well-being of people affected by cancer, Dr. Laura Béres, from King’s University College at Western University, and Tracey Jones, from Wellspring London and Region Cancer Support Centre, in Canada, successfully sought research funding for the development of the Journey Through Words programme, which uses the practices of narrative therapy and narrative medicine to help foster stories of resilience and hope in people with cancer and their families.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will recall the key concepts of narrative therapy and narrative medicine and their relevance to psychosocial oncology.
Explain how storytelling and creative writing can be used as therapeutic tools to support emotional well-being in cancer patients and their families.
Demonstrate the use of narrative therapy techniques in a group setting to facilitate sharing and resilience among individuals affected by cancer.

pathways.title: Classroom Agreement
pathways.description: Classroom agreement is about creating a learning environment that reinforces clearly defined expectations and procedures through mutual agreement. This pathway defines classroom agreement and provides an opportunity to see strategies demonstrated in a classroom.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the principle behind, and importance of a classroom agreement.
You will be able to provide examples of techniques to communicate high expectations, promote self-monitoring behavior, and implement positive discipline strategies.
You will understand the importance of promoting student leadership within the classroom environment.

pathways.title: Combining Science and Community Action to Combat Environmental Injustice
pathways.description: The Salton Sea, a polluted lake in southern California, USA, is rapidly drying up as a result of changes in climate and water usage. As the muddy lakebed becomes exposed, toxic dust is blown into surrounding communities, where residents have high rates of asthma. Scientists from the University of Southern California are working with community partners at Comite Civico del Valle to investigate how this air pollution is impacting children’s respiratory health and to empower affected communities to advocate for change and fight for environmental justice.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to describe the historical background and geographical context of the Salton Sea, including its formation, location, and surrounding communities.
Explain the environmental and health impacts of the shrinking Salton Sea on surrounding communities, including the correlation between air pollution and respiratory diseases.
Summarize the community-driven approach of the AIRE study and its collaboration between various stakeholders such as community organizations, schools, and scientific researchers.

Compare and contrast the environmental justice issues faced by marginalized communities around the Salton Sea with other similar environmental crises globally, highlighting common patterns and disparities.
Critique the ethical implications of conducting research on environmental justice, considering factors such as community consent, data ownership, and potential conflicts of interest.

pathways.title: How Can We Improve Cranberry Production?
pathways.description: Indigenous Peoples have been using cranberries in food, medicine and fabric dye from as early as the 1500s, and these fruits are still widely used and produced across the US today. However, fruit rot diseases can cause tremendous damage to these plants, leading to substantial yield losses if they are not properly managed. At the University of Wisconsin-Madison in the US, Dr Leslie Holland is a plant pathologist working to understand fruit rot and how we can better protect cranberries.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to describe the factors influencing the growth and spread of fungal pathogens in cranberry beds.
Explain why cranberry beds provide conducive environments for fungal pathogens.
Analyze the potential effects of climate change on cranberry production and disease management.
Assess the importance of interdisciplinary approaches in plant pathology, considering the complexities of managing cranberry diseases.

pathways.title: Building Community
pathways.description:
For students to feel empowered to share ideas freely, instructors must work to foster a classroom culture that is both caring and challenging, safe and supportive. Such a culture seldom materializes by happenstance, and building it must be a sustained effort throughout the semester. With community in mind, effective instructors consider how to set up the physical environment, how to encourage intellectual risk-taking, and how to model appropriate discussion behaviors, such as active listening and responding to one another respectfully.
How might we get to know our students better so we understand their prior experiences and content knowledge? How do we set a tone in which students are unafraid to share ideas, even if they may not be “right”? What steps can we take so that in-class discussion flows more like a conversation wherein students respond to each other’s ideas rather than simply looking to their professor for affirmation? In this pathway, featured professors describe actions they take to build community in their discussion- and lecture-based classrooms.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will recognize and articulate the importance of fostering a caring and challenging classroom culture to promote intellectual risk-taking and student engagement.
Develop strategies to create a physical and social classroom environment that encourages active participation and respectful dialogue among students.
Analyze the impact of instructor actions on building community within the classroom and enhancing the quality of in-class discussions.
Implement techniques to facilitate discussions that flow like conversations, where students respond to each other's ideas and feel comfortable sharing their own, regardless of correctness.

pathways.title: Deepening Understanding
pathways.description:
The challenge of narrowing a vast body of research and knowledge into a semester’s worth of material can be daunting, no matter the field of study. On top of that challenge, the effective instructor endeavors to awaken in students the same type of engagement that enlivens scholars in the field. Application and extension exercises help instructors go beyond mere transmission of knowledge by adding the depth and inspiration necessary to transform students into scholars. Such exercises additionally equip students with the tools and practice to transfer knowledge, preparing them to flexibly apply what they have learned to diverse real-world situations.
In a lecture course, how do we get every student talking and thinking, not just those who reliably raise their hands? What steps can we take to get students thinking less like students fulfilling a science or history requirement and more like actual scientists or historians? How can we get students to think about applying their learning to new problems and contexts? How might we facilitate learning experiences wherein students discover new knowledge themselves rather than simply imparting knowledge to them? In this pathway, featured professors describe strategies they use to deepen students’ understanding of course content.
