learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will analyze the process of transforming complex research into digestible course material while fostering a scholarly approach to learning among students.
Design and implement application and extension exercises that encourage students to apply course concepts to real-world situations and think critically like professionals in the field.
Evaluate the effectiveness of various instructional strategies in promoting active student participation and deeper understanding of course content.
Facilitate learning opportunities that enable students to independently discover and synthesize new knowledge, transitioning from passive recipients to active learners.

pathways.title: Team-Based Learning
pathways.description:
In classes that utilize a Team-Based Learning approach, students grapple with challenging problems that they can only solve by working together to learn and apply key class concepts. To achieve this, teams must be intentionally designed, and class norms and expectations for a safe and welcoming learning environment must be strongly reinforced. When this happens, students can learn from and with each other in ways that do not just facilitate content mastery but also foster social skills for future success in an increasingly collaborative world. 
How do we assign productive teams where students can learn from each other and grow together? How do we intentionally foster team-building in ways that are integrated with learning course concepts? How do we scaffold the social norms and social skills necessary to enable students to self-manage any conflict that may arise? How can we leverage team-based learning in summative assessments to decrease the anxiety associated with test taking? In these case studies, featured instructor Eric Mazur discusses his approach and strategies for using team-based learning with undergraduates at Harvard’s School of Engineering and Applied Sciences.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will learn to identify and apply team-based learning benefits in STEM.
Implement strategies to build teamwork skills for the modern workforce.
Analyze team composition's impact on learning outcomes.
Create diverse teams using surveys and demographic data.
Promote team autonomy and accountability in learning.
Understand how autonomy boosts motivation and social skills.

pathways.title: Pluralism and Ethics
pathways.description: Pluralism is all about recognizing and respecting human differences, while ethics relates to the values, principles, and personal moral qualities that guide our behaviour. This pathway introduces the concepts of pluralism and ethics, and some personal qualities and competencies that are linked to pluralistic values and attitudes to help educators be as effective as can be.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the relationship between pluralism, ethics, and educational frameworks, and their impact on fostering inclusivity and respect in the classroom.
You will evaluate the effectiveness of educational approaches in developing critical personal qualities and competencies for pluralism and ethics.

pathways.title: Lunar Leap: Basics of Forces
pathways.description:
Embark on a cosmic journey to grasp the essentials of physics, from Newton's fundamental laws to the enigmatic forces shaping our universe, all while uncovering the Moon's pivotal role in space exploration.
After mastering the laws of motion, you'll be able to tackle the Lunar Leap pathway, where you'll apply what you've learned to real-world scenarios on the Moon!
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to identify and summarize Newton's Laws of Motion and correct common misconceptions about these laws.
Describe the four fundamental forces of nature and their roles in the universe.
Explain the concept of inertia and Newton's First Law of Motion in the context of motion and mass.
Analyze the significance of the Moon in relation to Earth and understand its basic properties and formation.
Apply the concepts of force and motion to anticipate the challenges of lunar exploration and experimentation.

pathways.title: Ecology in Action: Interactions, Energy, and Impact
pathways.description: This pathway explores the fundamentals of ecology, focusing on how organisms interact with one another and with their environments. Topics covered include the levels of ecological organization, biotic and abiotic factors, energy flow, nutrient cycling, population dynamics, community ecology, and the effects of human actions on ecosystems. Examine ecological principles, symbiotic relationships, and global environmental challenges like climate change and habitat loss.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define and explain the interactions between biotic and abiotic components in an ecosystem and their role in supporting life.
Analyze the concept of biodiversity and evaluate its significance in maintaining ecological balance and ecosystem resilience.
Assess the impact of human activities, such as pollution and habitat destruction, on ecosystem dynamics and sustainability.
Understand and illustrate the concepts of food chains and food webs, and analyze the flow of energy and matter through trophic levels within an ecosystem.

pathways.title: Is Your Tap Water Dangerous?
pathways.description:
Explore how minerals can affect water with this pathway and accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (160-180 minutes) and focuses on the hardness of water. Understand how water gains its taste and hardness based on the minerals in it, and learn how to assess which minerals and metals make water dangerous to drink.

Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 50 minutes, items 1-3 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 50-60 minutes, item 4 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3, 50-60 minutes, found in the student guide.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4, 50 minutes, found in the student guide.
• EXTEND: Day 5, 60 minutes, items 5-7 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will learn that hardness of water is determined by amount magnesium and calcium present.
Determine that corrosive water is characterized by the presence of lead and calcium present in the water.
Apply your knowledge of ions to show metals ionize and are attracted to OH- in water.

pathways.title: Fostering Love and Knowledge of Agriculture and the Environment
pathways.description: Agriculture is what feeds the world, yet many people have a very limited knowledge of how their food grows and where it comes from. Diana E. Collingwood, Assistant Commissioner at the US Virgin Islands Department of Agriculture, is on a mission to change this, not just to inspire the next generation of agriculturalists, but also to help existing farmers overcome the challenges of their important work.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will learn about the key challenges facing agriculture in the US Virgin Islands, enhancing their understanding of global food security issues.
Learners will gain an understanding of the importance of sustainable practices in agriculture, enabling them to appreciate the balance between productivity and environmental preservation.
Learners will learn about the diversity of careers within the field of agriculture and align their interests with potential career paths.

pathways.title: How We Read: The Neuroscience Behind Literacy
pathways.description: Reading and writing are essential skills for modern life, but how often do you think about how your brain processes written information? Based at the University of Alberta in Canada, Professor Jacqueline Cummine is helping decode how we read – in particular, the important role of our senses – and using these findings to help people who struggle with literacy skills.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, learners will comprehend the function of different brain regions in reading, reinforcing key concepts in neuroscience and enhancing their understanding of the brain's role in literacy skills.

Learners will explore the impact of skill-based and goal-based training programs on adults with dyslexia, recognizing the potential of such interventions for promoting neuroplasticity and improving reading performance.
Learners will explore various career paths in cognitive neuropsychology, understanding the diverse opportunities available in academia, healthcare, government, industry, and education.
Learners will be able to define and explain the role of cognitive neuropsychology and phonemes in the process of reading.

pathways.title: Understanding Cells and Fostering Love for Science
pathways.description: Dr. Verónica Segarra is a cell biologist based at High Point University in North Carolina, USA. In addition to her research on how cells respond to changing environments, she is also passionate about developing the next generation of scientists
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the importance of studying cellular biology using baker's yeast and its relevance in understanding cellular functions and adaptations to stress.
Understand the interdisciplinary nature of scientific research and its applications in various industries.
Recognize the importance of fostering a sense of belonging and diversity in scientific fields.

pathways.title: Bedside to Bench to Bedside: A Unique Way to Treat Lung Injury
pathways.description: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) is a severe lung injury that results from multiple different causes including pneumonia, COVID-19 and sepsis. Using human lung models and a ‘bedside to bench to bedside’ approach, physician-scientist Dr Julie Bastarache, of Vanderbilt University Medical Center in the US, is working to understand and treat ARDS in patients suffering from lung damage.
learning_objectives: After completing the pathway, you will understand the pathophysiology of Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) and the underlying mechanisms that contribute to lung damage.
Explore the research approach of physician-scientists, specifically the "bedside to bench to bedside" method, and how it contributes to understanding and treating ARDS.
Identify the role of cell-free hemoglobin (CFH) in causing lung damage in patients with ARDS, and comprehend the underlying mechanisms by which CFH leads to respiratory failure.

pathways.title: Combining Medicine and Research to Treat Children With Tuberculosis
pathways.description: Dr James Seddon is a clinician scientist, working both as a medical doctor at St Mary’s Hospital and as a researcher at Imperial College London in the UK and Stellenbosch University in South Africa. He is conducting a clinical trial to test the effectiveness of a new drug at preventing children who have been exposed to multidrug-resistant tuberculosis from developing the disease. He also hopes to develop a test to identify children at greater risk of developing tuberculosis. James’s research could dramatically reduce the number of children who die from tuberculosis each year
learning_objectives: After the completing the pathway you will understand the importance of clinical trials in evaluating the safety and efficacy of new drugs.
You will know how gene expression tests can be used to identify individuals at higher risk of developing tuberculosis (TB) disease.

pathways.title: How Modelling Can Shed Light on Memory Retrieval?
pathways.description: Computational thinking describes the way we break down problems or draw conclusions from observations. We use it in everyday life, but Professor Osman Yaşar, based at the Computational Math, Science, and Technology Institute at the State University of New York in the US, believes we can learn to use it more effectively. He trains teachers to teach students to think in ways that improve their memory and problem-solving abilities, setting them up for shining careers in STEM.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will be able to define and explain the concept of computational thinking and its significance in problem-solving and cognition
Explain the benefits of teaching computational thinking and scientific thinking skills to enhance performance in academic, professional, and personal settings.

You will develop your skills in inductive and deductive thinking, as you construct models and apply deductive reasoning to modify and refine your creations, fostering computational and scientific thinking abilities.

pathways.title: Monitoring the Canopy Temperature of Forests
pathways.description: Dr Sophie Fauset, from the University of Plymouth in the UK, and Dr Shalom D. Addo-Danso, of the CSIR-Forestry Research Institute of Ghana, are collaborating on a global project that seeks to monitor the canopy temperature of forests. The findings will help develop understanding of how forests are responding to climate change and, hopefully, be an impetus for action.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the importance of monitoring canopy temperature in forests and its implications for climate change and ecological processes.
Gain insights into the fields of environmental science and ecology, including their significance in understanding and addressing environmental challenges.

Explore the significance of forests in the global climate system, understand the assumptions made by Earth system models, and recognize the importance of studying leaf temperatures in relation to ecological processes and climate change.

pathways.title: How Can We Make Future Places Healthier Spaces?
pathways.description: Professor Richard Harper, based at Lancaster University in the UK, is the principal investigator of a project called the Future Places Centre. This project is investigating how computing, the Internet of Things and data science can generate information that people can use to make their future places healthier.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway understand the impact of technology, computing and the Internet of Things (IoT) on our daily lives and how these changes can provide an opportunity to (re)imagine how our lives and the spaces in which we live might be enhanced.

Explore the potential of IoT and data science in facilitating the development of smart homes and healthy living practices, as well as in shaping and improving future places for healthier, more sustainable living.
Apply their knowledge of the project to suggest how data gathered from people and places can shape the future relationship between individuals and space.
Synthesize how technology can be used to communicate the aims of these spaces and make people aware of these future places.

pathways.title: Shining a Light on the Role of Trace Metals in Neurodegenerative Diseases
pathways.description: A team of researchers with expertise in physics, engineering and biology are finding out whether trace metals in the brain are linked to neurodegenerative diseases. Using innovative synchrotron techniques at Diamond Light Source, the UK’s national synchrotron facility, their work is paving the way for improved diagnosis and treatment.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the importance of studying changes in metal homeostasis and their role in the development of neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease.

Learn about the synchrotron technique of X-ray spectro-microscopy and its use in studying metal homeostasis in the brain, to potentially detect changes in metal chemistry and provide an earlier and more accurate diagnosis.
Understand the use of synchrotron techniques in investigating the role of trace metals in neurodegenerative diseases and the potential of synchrotron in various research fields.

pathways.title: Can Modern Technology Uncover the Secrets of Evolution?
pathways.description: Explore how modern technology, specifically micro-computed tomography (μCT), unlocks the mysteries of evolution by studying the Elgin Reptiles. Discover exciting findings about their anatomy, behavior, and their role in understanding mass extinctions, evolutionary lineages, and ecosystems. Delve into the interdisciplinary nature of paleontology and potential career paths in the field. Follow Dr. Davide Foffa, a research associate at National Museums Scotland, and his colleagues as they examine these fossils using digital technologies for the first time, leading to thrilling new discoveries.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will understand the importance of the Elgin Reptiles in unraveling the mysteries of evolution.
Examine the impact of mass extinctions on life on Earth and how species recover from such events.
Recognize the value of fossils in reconstructing past ecosystems and studying biodiversity crises.
Discover the interdisciplinary nature of palaeontology and the various career paths within the field.

pathways.title: Behind the Screens: The Crystals That Flow Like Rain Down a Windowpane
pathways.description: Liquid crystals are found all around us – in your phone, laptop and TV screens. Dr Akhshay Bhadwal, an experimental physicist at Nottingham Trent University, UK, and Dr Joseph Cousins, a mathematician at the Universities of Glasgow and Strathclyde, UK, have teamed up to understand how these unusual materials behave.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will learn about soft matter physics and the concept of nematics
Understand the idea of liquid crystals and how they fit into the field
Understand key terminology used in the study of nematics and soft matter physics

pathways.title: How Are Flexible Electronic Devices Solving Healthcare Challenges?
pathways.description: Advances in healthcare are not only made by healthcare professionals. Led by Dr Radu Sporea, a team of electronics engineers and surgeons at the University of Surrey in the UK and Gachon University in South Korea is developing a wearable electronic device to monitor a patient’s blood flow as they recover from an operation.
learning_objectives: Understand how flexible electronic components (LEDs, photosensors, transistors) work together to monitor physiological signals.
Analyze the engineering trade-offs involved in designing wearable medical devices, including comfort, energy use, cost, and reliability.
Evaluate the role of interdisciplinary collaboration in developing effective healthcare technologies.

pathways.title: Can Lifestyle Choices Protect Your Brain as You Age?
pathways.description: As we grow older, maintaining cognitive health becomes increasingly important. Dr Tina Brinkley, a gerontologist at Wake Forest University School of Medicine in the US, is researching how our everyday choices – like what we eat, how we move, and how we connect with others – can protect our brains from cognitive decline. Her work explores the links between lifestyle and brain health in older adults, offering hope for preventing diseases like Alzheimer’s.
learning_objectives: Understand key concepts in gerontology, including cognitive decline, dementia, and healthy ageing.
Assess the role of gerontology in addressing health disparities and demographic shifts.
See education pathways and careers that are related to ageing, health and public health research.
Analyze how lifestyle factors such as diet, physical activity, and social engagement affect brain health.

pathways.title: How Food Insecurity Affects Health – And How Healthcare Can Help
pathways.description: Globally, 2.4 billion people are unable to access or afford sufficient food to meet their nutritional needs1. Even in the US, one of the wealthiest countries in the world, 13.5% of households – or 44 million people – are affected by food insecurity2. At Wake Forest University School of Medicine in North Carolina, USA, Dr Deepak Palakshappa studies how food insecurity affects people’s health, and how healthcare systems can help address these issues.
learning_objectives: Describe how social determinants of health, including food insecurity, influence population health outcomes and healthcare delivery.
Explain how food insecurity functions as a social determinant of health and how public health systems respond to its impacts.
Analyze how social, economic, and environmental determinants contribute to food insecurity and shape associated health outcomes.

Evaluate how social determinants of health drive inequities related to food insecurity and assess strategies used by healthcare systems and public health organizations to improve population-level health outcomes.
Propose evidence-based interventions to reduce food-insecurity-related health disparities and improve population health.

pathways.title: Fairywrens and the Art of Inquiry
pathways.description:
Spark your scientific thinking by observing cute Australian bird behavior! Fun, interactive videos feature real biologists and student inquiry & observation. Analyze the scientists' real data to get a window into ongoing research on the evolution of group size in fairywrens.
Driving Question(s):
• How do scientists pick a question to investigate?
• How does a scientific question develop while collecting data?
Hook(s):
• Learn alongside actual scientists as they observe the same data as you!
• Includes creative, interactive videos and think-alouds with scientists that model how students can ask better questions.
learning_objectives: Observe how scientists ask questions about phenomena, models, and data.
Develop skills to ask questions in science.

pathways.title: NASA | What is Material Science?
pathways.description: Material science is crucial in keeping NASA's equipment well-maintained and suited to its environment. This pathway will introduce material science and some of the research NASA is undertaking in this field.
learning_objectives: Understand how NASA's materials research helps keep their equipment maintained and useful.
Explain how the environment of space impacts the materials used for NASA research.
Describe some of the material science research taking place at NASA.

pathways.title: The En-ROADS Climate Workshop: Mastering En-ROADS
pathways.description: Experience the Climate Action Simulation Game using the En-ROADS simulator to explore emissions reduction, climate policy negotiation, and systems thinking, building skills to design effective climate change solutions.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will explain how climate policies affect emissions using the En-ROADS simulator.
Analyze trade-offs in climate policy negotiations.
Evaluate effective solutions for emissions reduction and resilience.
Apply systems thinking to climate scenarios.

pathways.title: Unexpected Ways That Paint Prevents Corrosion
pathways.description: Our modern lifestyles depend on the material objects around us remaining strong and free from damage. Protective paints and coatings are effective at preventing corrosion damage, but how they do this – and how these mechanisms can be optimised – are less well understood. The sustainable coatings by rational design (SusCoRD) project connects materials science academics with commercial scientists to tackle these questions.
learning_objectives: By the end of the learning pathway, learners will be able to define key materials science terms such as corrosion, thermodynamically stable, electrochemical, nanocomposite, and rational design.
Identify the three primary theories explaining how corrosion-protective paints function.
Explain the process of corrosion as an electrochemical reaction and how it impacts metal structures.
Use examples from the article to model how rational design can improve material development and reduce hazardous components.
Compare and contrast the three theories of corrosion protection and assess their relative significance based on research findings.

pathways.title: Can Animated Stories Help You to Better Understand Your Mental Health Needs?
pathways.description: What’s Up With Everyone? is a campaign run in conjunction with Aardman, research teams from various universities, and clinical and charitable partners to provide support for mental health literacy among young people. They believe that by sharing ideas relating to mental health and well-being in an engaging and popular format, animations can make a positive difference to the lives of young people
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to evaluate the influence of environmental and social factors on mental health and behavior.
Construct an explanation based on evidence for how public communication campaigns can promote health literacy and behavior change.
Analyze how social and emotional factors, like peer influence and media engagement, affect mental health awareness and behavior.
Evaluate how group behaviors can promote individual well-being and support community mental health.

pathways.title: Understanding Intellectual Property and Copyright
pathways.description: A large proportion of the U.S. economy is driven by intellectual property industries, making it essential learning for science communicators. This pathway explores copyright, patents, trademarks, fair use, IP enforcement, and international law, providing a clear, engaging foundation in intellectual property rights, media ethics, and creative ownership—vital for ethical and legal science communication in the digital age.
learning_objectives: Understand the foundational concepts of intellectual property—including copyright, patents, and trademarks—and their role in protecting innovation in STEM fields.
Recognize the ethical and legal considerations related to the use of copyrighted materials, including fair use and public domain guidelines, in scientific communication.
Identify the impact of intellectual property law on global collaboration, knowledge sharing, and access to scientific advancements.
Explore how digital platforms and emerging technologies influence the enforcement and evolution of intellectual property laws in science communication.

pathways.title: Market Analysis for STEM Professionals
pathways.description: Learn how to identify unmet needs through market research, set SMART goals, and align strategies with effective marketing plans. This pathway bridges business strategy, data science, and marketing fundamentals—empowering STEM professionals to apply analytical thinking, audience targeting, and strategic planning in real-world communication and outreach.
learning_objectives: Learn how to conduct foundational market research to identify unmet needs, consumer behavior, and audience segmentation relevant to science-based industries.
Develop SMART goals and align them with mission and vision statements to guide strategy in science-focused ventures.

pathways.title: Active Listening and Constructive Feedback: Skills for Scientific Dialogue
pathways.description: Science is highly collaborative, which makes communication skills like active listening, constructive feedback, and thoughtful questioning essential for success in science communication careers. This pathway helps aspiring and practicing science communicators develop these core competencies to build trust, foster dialogue, and engage diverse audiences more effectively across academic, media, and public settings.
learning_objectives: Understand how to respond to feedback and dialogue with openness and professionalism, using constructive language and reflective thinking to support collaboration and effective communication.
Learn to identify and extract key ideas from feedback or conversations in research, academic, or mentoring contexts to improve personal performance and enhance scientific communication.
Develop active listening strategies—including paraphrasing, interpreting verbal and non-verbal cues, and asking meaningful questions—to build rapport and deepen understanding in scientific dialogue.

pathways.title: Connecting and Communicating with Stakeholders
pathways.description: Networking is an invaluable skill in many careers, including STEM. This pathway focuses on stakeholder communication, helping learners develop essential skills for presenting scientific information clearly and persuasively. Topics include writing for diverse audiences, structuring arguments, designing effective slides, and delivering impactful presentations—equipping young professionals to engage stakeholders across professional, academic, and public settings.
learning_objectives: Learn techniques that communicate complex information effectively to stakeholders with varying levels of expertise.
Learn best practices for virtual communication to maintain professionalism and clarity in online stakeholder engagements.
Learn to apply communication strategies to build rapport and trust with diverse audiences through public speaking and engaging slide presentations.
Develop communication and collaboration skills with stakeholders by identifying and understanding strategies for professional networking in STEM.
Construct clear and logical scientific arguments using claims, evidence, and reasoning to support communication with stakeholders.

pathways.title: I Like That! How Perception, Emotion, and Cognition Shape Our Preferences
pathways.description:
Designed for Grades 5–9, this pathway engages students in conducting experiments and analyzing real scientific data to learn about themselves, exploring how the eyes and brain interact to shape preferences, and applying that knowledge to fish research and character design.
Driving Question(s):
1. What makes humans and other animals perceive something as beautiful?
2. How does the way our brain processes visual input from our eyes affect how we perceive and interact with the world?
Essential Question(s):
How can science help deepen our understanding of the Arts, Humanities, and ourselves?
Hook(s):
Warm-up puzzles, fun student-driven experiments, and original artwork are woven into this data-centered lesson.
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to define key terms such as perception, cognition, emotion, typicality, and preference as they relate to visual interpretation and decision-making.
Explain how the eyes and brain work together to process visual stimuli, and how this process influences preferences in both humans and animals.
Describe the role of cone cells in the retina and their connection to color perception and aesthetic judgment.
Analyze patterns in raw experimental data to infer how perceptual elements influence aesthetic judgment in humans and animals.
Assess the relationship between perception, cognition, emotion, and aesthetic appeal in both natural and artistic contexts.

pathways.title: Ethics, Sustainability, and Social Issues in Information Systems
pathways.description: From powering eco-friendly businesses to saving lives in hospitals, information systems have a far-reaching impact on our world. This chapter explores how ethical thinking, sustainability, and responsible innovation shape the development and use of technology—covering topics like artificial intelligence, intellectual property, and health informatics to ensure that progress benefits both people and the planet. This chapter is part of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key ethical theories (utilitarianism, deontology, virtue ethics) and explain their relevance to information systems.
Recall key concepts such as Green IS, Lean IS, Sustainable IS, and corporate social responsibility (CSR).
Describe how sustainability principles apply to information systems and supply chain management.
Use sustainability principles to evaluate the environmental, social, and economic impacts of information system practices.
Compare and contrast Green IS, Lean IS, and Sustainable IS models in terms of their approaches to minimizing impact and maximizing efficiency.

pathways.title: NASA | Earth’s Cryosphere
pathways.description: The cryosphere encompasses components of Earth that contain water in its frozen state: ice sheets, ice caps, glaciers, sea ice, snow, lake ice, and permafrost. While these components exist at many locations on Earth, they are most abundant in the polar regions. NASA cryospheric data, including land ice height, ice thickness, glacial extent, and glacial mass, provides insight into how changes in these regions contribute to sea level rise, impact water resources for agriculture and human consumption, and even lead to changes in Earth’s rotation.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define Earth's cryosphere.
You will understand the importance of glaciers and icecaps in maintaining a healthy Earth.
You will identify how NASA monitors the health of Earth's cryosphere.

pathways.title: How Do Young People Manage Their Career Pathways?
pathways.description: Finding a good job that is well-paid and satisfying is becoming increasingly difficult for many young people. Professor Jason Heyes, from the Sheffield University Management School, in the UK, is investigating the difficulties faced by young people moving between jobs, and which policies are most effective to improve their opportunities.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms related to employment relations, such as casualisation, labour market transition, qualitative methods, and quantitative methods.
Explain the difference between quantitative and qualitative research methods in studying employment transitions.
Summarize how labour market transitions affect young people’s job quality, wages, and career growth.
Identify real-world examples of casualisation and underemployment and analyze their impact on young workers.

pathways.title: Can Ocean Alkalinity Enhancement Reduce Atmospheric Carbon Dioxide?
pathways.description: We must urgently and dramatically reduce our carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions. However, even if we completely cut emissions tomorrow, the atmosphere will still hold the majority of the CO2 emitted over the past century. This will continue to affect the climate, so scientists are exploring how to enhance the natural, but slow, removal of CO2 from the atmosphere. At Dalhousie University in Canada, oceanographers Professor Katja Fennel, Dr Dariia Atamanchuk and Professor Ruth Musgrave are investigating the effectiveness and implications of ocean alkalinity enhancement, a CO2 removal technique that mimics the natural process of rock weathering.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key terms related to carbon dioxide removal (CDR), including ocean alkalinity enhancement (OAE), biogeochemical models, brucite, and carbon reservoirs.
You will recall the natural rock weathering process and how it contributes to ocean alkalinity.
You will describe the role of the ocean as a carbon reservoir and its impact on climate change mitigation.
You will predict potential changes in ocean alkalinity and marine ecosystems based on OAE trials.
You will compare the natural process of rock weathering with the artificial introduction of brucite in OAE.

pathways.title: Power to the People: How Electrical Engineering Can Empower the Navajo Nation
pathways.description: The Navajo Nation lies across more than 25,000 square miles of desert and scrubland in the southwest US. The Navajo People, or Diné as they refer to themselves, have long fought for self-determination. Through the Navajo Technical University, the Navajo have the power to educate their young people in accordance with their traditions, cultures, and beliefs. However, a lack of Navajo engineering faculty means a lack of role models for engineering students. Dr Peter Romine has developed an engineering graduate program to train Navajo engineers who will inspire the next generation.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to dentify the geographical extent of the Navajo Nation and recall the significance of the term 'Diné' as it relates to the Navajo people.
Demonstrate how NTU's Electrical Engineering Graduate Program contributes to decolonizing education by applying Diné philosophies and values to the curriculum and teaching methods.

Assess the significance of increasing Navajo representation among the faculty at NTU, particularly in the field of electrical engineering, and the potential impact this has on student inspiration and the development of community role models.

pathways.title: Asteroid Intercept: Designing for Collision Safety
pathways.description:
Embark on a space engineering adventure! Design safety features for a probe to catch an asteroid, using Newton's laws with the accompanying Student Guide. Get ready for videos, simulations, and cool experiments in this action-packed science journey! The recommended learning time spans 7 days of lessons (350 minutes).
Pathway breakdown:
• PRE-LEARNING: Day 1, 20 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• ENGAGE: Day 1-2, 40 minutes, item 3 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 50 minutes, items 4-5 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3-4, 80 minutes, items 6-7 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 5, 40 minutes, item 8 of the pathway.
• EXTEND: Day 6-7, 120 minutes, item 9 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, you will be able to use Newton's cool "action-reaction" law to make sure a space probe can safely bounce off an asteroid without getting smashed.
Create awesome designs to keep the space probe from getting wrecked if it bumps into the speedy asteroid.
Think like a space detective to figure out what could go wrong during the probe's asteroid adventure and how to fix it.
Play with computer simulations to see what happens when things crash in space, learning about the forces that make stuff move or stop.
Put on your engineer hat to plan a space probe mission that can safely meet an asteroid and help us learn more about our universe.

pathways.title: Ethical and Cultural Dimensions
pathways.description: Rethink ecological challenges, transitioning to strategic solutions like managing "negative commons". Learn from history, and catalyze global finance reform for environmental and economic crises. Plus, delve into environmental justice's global impact.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will explore the historical precedents and tactics employed by leaders to challenge powerful financial sectors for addressing environmental and economic crises.
Learn about the principles of environmental justice, recognizing its social and ecological significance on a global scale.
Understand the concept of "Ecological Redirection" and its implications.
Explore the historical detachment of humans from nature and its consequences on environmental degradation and sustainability.

pathways.title: What Skills Do You Need to Succeed in School?
pathways.description: Your academic identity plays a key role in your education. Students with low self-confidence in their abilities are more likely to struggle in school and withdraw from university. To address this, Dr Cheryl Talley founded Project Knowledge, a research project and academic intervention at Virginia State University, USA, which has since expanded to a local high school. Using psychology theories and methods, this landmark programme provides students with the skills and confidence they need to succeed.
learning_objectives: After completing the pathway, you will be able to recognize the factors that contribute to students staying in university, including protective factors and and risk factors.
You will recognize the value of mentoring and building trusting relationships in fostering a positive academic identity and promoting academic success.
You will know and understand the different branches of psychology.

pathways.title: How Research Fields Are Joining Forces to Protect Healthcare Systems
pathways.description: Healthcare-associated infections and antimicrobial resistance are two of the greatest threats to global healthcare systems. Dealing with them cannot rely upon medicine alone. Instead, social, technological and computational innovations need to be integrated. An interdisciplinary team of researchers from Imperial College London, UK, and the UK Health Security Agency is rising to this challenge.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain the concept of antimicrobial stewardship and describe its importance in reducing antimicrobial resistance.
You will be able to analyse the interdisciplinary approaches necessary to address antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and healthcare-associated infections (HCAIs).
You will understand how to design effective communication strategies to raise awareness and prevent the spread of AMR and HCAIs.

pathways.title: USGS Scientist Spotlight Series
pathways.description: The Scientist Spotlight series highlights some of the many talented scientists working with the CASC network. CASC scientists work with wildlife managers and stakeholders to better understand and prepare for climate change impacts affecting a range of plant and animal species, from coastal fishes to alpine forests and all ecosystems in between.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: Explore the work being undertaken by USGS scientists
Understand the need for adaptive management plans for resources
Learn how scientists work with stakeholders to create collaborative relationships.

pathways.title: How Do Children See the World?
pathways.description: Dr. Tessa Dekker researches the development of vision in children. She is also a scan operator at the Birkbeck-UCL Centre for Neuroimaging (BUCNI). Scientists go to BUCNI when they want to use state of the art magnetic imaging facilities for their research. Read about some of the tools she uses in her research below.

pathways.title: The Hidden Chemicals Affecting Primates’ Hormones
pathways.description: In many of the world’s rainforests, human activities threaten the survival of our closest living relatives: primates. Some of these activities, such as deforestation and hunting, are well documented and heavily criticised, while others, such as the output of chemical pollution, remain largely hidden. At Indiana University Bloomington in the US, Dr Michael D. Wasserman, head of the Primate Environmental Endocrinology Lab (PEEL), is investigating how chemicals in the environment, including pollutants such as pesticides and flame retardants, impact the survival of primates.
learning_objectives: Explain how endocrine-disrupting chemicals affect primate hormone systems and physiology.
Analyze how pollution and habitat change influence primate health and reproduction.
Evaluate how field and laboratory methods are used to study chemical exposure in wild primates.
Propose a conservation strategy to reduce chemical threats to primate populations.

pathways.title: How Can We Measure Time?
pathways.description: Learning to tell the time is one of the key skills we are taught as children; but take a moment to think about it, and you may find the concept of time slipping away from you. How do we know how long a second is? Where did minutes come from? How do we actually measure time? At the University of Queensland in Australia, Stefan Zeppetzauer is using quantum mechanics to investigate time – one of the most familiar but least understood concepts in physics!
learning_objectives: Apply the concepts of oscillation and frequency to explain how pendulum, quartz, atomic, and quantum clocks measure time.
Compare different types of clocks and analyze how factors like noise, temperature, and measurement methods affect their accuracy.
Evaluate why extremely precise time measurement is important for technologies such as GPS and discuss the broader importance of studying time in science.
Design and communicate an idea for a future timekeeping device or a new application of quantum engineering, using scientific reasoning to justify their design choices.

pathways.title: What Do Quantum Physicists Know About Gravitational Waves and Dark Matter?
pathways.description: Did you know that dark matter makes up over 85% of the Universe’s mass, but scientists have never been able to detect it? And that some gravitational waves are proving just as elusive? At The University of Western Australia, quantum physicists Dr William Campbell and Emma Paterson are aiming to solve these mysteries.
learning_objectives: Describe what gravitational waves and dark matter are and why scientists think they exist.
Explain how quantum technologies (e.g., cryogenic detectors, resonators, SQUIDs) help scientists detect extremely small signals from space.
Analyze the differences between high- and low-frequency gravitational waves and the challenges involved in detecting them.
Evaluate the importance of quantum research for both scientific discovery and real-world applications such as healthcare and technology.
Communicate complex quantum physics concepts clearly using analogies, models, or visual explanations.

pathways.title: Foundations of Genetics
pathways.description: Genetic differences can arise from a single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP), showing how small changes in DNA can influence traits, health, and evolution. Explore how genetic information is stored, inherited, and expressed through DNA, RNA, and proteins. Through simulations and applications such as PCR, gel electrophoresis, and genetic testing, students investigate mutations, genetic variation, and genotype-phenotype relationships, while building foundational skills in heredity, genomics, and biotechnology and exploring real-world connections to genetic disorders and careers.
learning_objectives: Explain the structure and role of DNA, genes, and chromosomes in the inheritance of traits.
Describe different types and causes of genetic mutations, including spontaneous and induced mutations, and their effects on proteins and phenotypes.
Analyze how genetic variation (e.g., SNPs and chromosomal abnormalities such as nondisjunction) contributes to diversity and disease.
Investigate the relationship between genotype and phenotype using examples of genetic disorders.
Describe and apply basic biotechnology techniques (e.g., PCR, gel electrophoresis, restriction enzymes) used in genetic analysis and testing.
Evaluate the role, benefits, and ethical considerations of genetic testing in society.
Demonstrate understanding of inheritance patterns using genetic crosses, key terminology, and probability.

pathways.title: Killer Whale Conversations and Conservation: Eavesdropping With Artificial Intelligence
pathways.description: Killer whales, or orcas, are some of the most intelligent and socially complex animals on the planet. At Simon Fraser University in Canada, Dr Ruth Joy and Dr Kaitlin Palmer are part of the HALLO project, researching how the specific sounds these whales make to communicate can be used to track them as they move through the sea. By using deep learning AI techniques to identify which whales are making which sounds, they aim to alert nearby ships when certain endangered whale populations are nearby.
learning_objectives: Identify the purpose of the HALLO project and describe how killer whale vocalisations are used in conservation research.
Explain how bioacoustics and deep learning AI are applied to classify killer whale calls and monitor endangered populations.
Interpret acoustic data (e.g., spectrograms) to distinguish between different killer whale call types.
Evaluate the effectiveness and ethical implications of using AI-driven ecological monitoring to inform real-time conservation decisions.

pathways.title: Commuters on Computers: How is Technology Changing The Way We Live in Our Cities?
pathways.description: Smartphones, air pods and the internet are now essential items in many city-dwellers’ survival packs. In the hustle and bustle of the morning rush-hour, unplugged commuters are a rare sight. For better or for worse, the physical and digital worlds of our cities are becoming increasingly intertwined. Dr Philipp Rode and Sudeep Bhargava, from the London School of Economics and Political Science in the UK, are investigating how these so-called hybrid cities are changing, and what they will look like in the future.
learning_objectives: Understand how digital technologies contribute to the creation of hybrid cities and influence everyday urban life.
Apply urban studies concepts to real-world examples of technology use in cities, such as commuting or public spaces.
Analyze the social, environmental, and governance impacts of increasing technology use in urban environments.
Evaluate the benefits and challenges of emerging urban technologies, including autonomous systems, for social cohesion and equity.

pathways.title: Machine Learning and Genomics
pathways.description: Machine learning fits computational models to data to recognize patterns and make predictions without explicit programming. This pathway introduces supervised learning, regression, neural networks, deep learning, and model evaluation in biological contexts.
learning_objectives: Learn how models translate noisy biological data into predictive insights.
Understand strengths and limits of different algorithms, from regression to deep learning.
Develop intuition for feature selection, overfitting, and model evaluation.
Gain career-relevant skills at the intersection of biology, data science, and AI.

pathways.title: Biological Data Analysis and Systems
pathways.description: Bioinformatics integrates data cleaning, visualization, sequencing, and network-based modeling to analyze large-scale biological datasets. This pathway explains how raw biological data are transformed into interpretable results using statistics, programming, and systems biology concepts.
learning_objectives: Develop skills in cleaning, standardizing, and validating large biological datasets.
Learn how sequencing data becomes quantitative evidence for biological questions.
Understand how networks and systems thinking reveal interactions beyond single genes.
Gain computational habits essential for careers in bioinformatics and data-driven biology.

pathways.title: Why is Geriatric Cardiology a Rewarding Field to Work In?
pathways.description: Dr Maria Octavia Rangel from Wake Forest University School of Medicine in the US has been making significant changes to the lives of older adults with heart problems ever since she was studying for her master’s degree. By blending clinical practice, clinical research, and teaching and mentoring the next generation of medical professionals, Octavia uses her passion for improving the lives of older adults to improve treatment, medication and rehabilitation.
learning_objectives: Identify key age-related changes in cardiovascular physiology that affect heart disease in older adults.
Explain how diagnostic tools such as echocardiography, ECGs, and regadenoson stress tests are used to assess cardiac function.
Demonstrate how principles of personalized medicine are applied when treating patients with heart failure or complex geriatric syndromes.
Examine how clinical research methods inform decisions about treatment safety, rehabilitation strategies, and medical interventions.
Evaluate patient-centered care plans for geriatric patients and propose improvements based on physiological, ethical, and psychosocial considerations.

pathways.title: NASA | Ceramic and Glass
pathways.description: Ceramics and glass are among humanity’s oldest materials, yet they remain at the forefront of modern science and technology. This pathway highlights how innovations in heat-resistant ceramics and advanced glass, from aerospace coatings to everyday devices, enable NASA to push the boundaries of strength, sustainability, and exploration on Earth and in space.
learning_objectives: Describe how the microstructure of ceramics and glass influences their behavior under different conditions.
understand how ceramic and glass innovations solve engineering problems.
Compare traditional materials to advanced ceramic composites.
Evaluate how NASA’s use of ceramic coatings and glass innovations supports advancements in space exploration.

pathways.title: How Are Control Engineers Improving the Sustainability of Irrigated Agriculture?
pathways.description: Globally, less than 60% of the water diverted into irrigation networks is used productively. This inefficiency is partly due to outdated infrastructure and manual operating practices that can result in wasteful spillage at the end of the open-water distribution channels and poor service to farmers. In Australia, control engineers from the University of Melbourne and Rubicon Water have developed a system that automatically adjusts water supply to more efficiently meet demand.
learning_objectives: Define key terms related to irrigation control systems, such as actuator, compensator, feedback control, setpoint, and sensor.
Explain how feedback control systems like TCC use sensors and actuators to regulate water levels in irrigation networks.
Compare the performance of manual vs. automated irrigation systems in terms of water efficiency and service to farmers.
Assess the advantages and limitations of using feedback control in large-scale water distribution systems like those in agriculture.

pathways.title: How to Facilitate En-ROADS Climate Workshops
pathways.description: Gain the skills to confidently facilitate En-ROADS climate workshops and simulations, empowering communities to explore climate solutions, policy impacts, and emissions reduction using the En-ROADS simulator for effective climate action.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to describe the role of an En-ROADS facilitator in climate workshops and simulations.
Explain how to guide participants in using the En-ROADS simulator to explore climate policy impacts.
Apply systems thinking to support climate action discussions using En-ROADS.
Demonstrate effective facilitation strategies to engage participants in emissions reduction planning.

pathways.title: Snow Day: Challenge Tasks
pathways.description:
In this culminating task, students apply what they’ve learned about weather from Learning Sequences 1–3 to analyze a real winter storm that crossed the U.S. in February 2017. Through three data-driven challenges, they explain differences in rain and snow across California, explore snowfall patterns in the Rockies, and predict which communities in the Midwest may need to close schools due to heavy snow. Students use weather maps, models, and data to demonstrate their understanding of atmospheric processes and storm movement.
Accompanying Learning Sequences:
1. From Cloud to Storm (Lessons 1 - 6)
2. A Front Headed Your Way (Lessons 7 - 11)
3. Worldwide Weather (Lessons 12 - 15)
learning_objectives: By the end of the learning pathway, learners will be able to use models of temperature, pressure, and moisture to explain rain and snow formation in a winter storm.
Analyze real storm data to identify weather patterns and precipitation outcomes.
Predict storm movement using prevailing winds, fronts, and pressure systems.
Communicate how geography and atmospheric conditions influence local weather.
Apply scientific reasoning to determine community-level weather impacts and prepare for severe weather.

pathways.title: Digital Collaboration
pathways.description: Effective communication and collaboration are core skills in the evolving career of a science communicator, requiring fluency in digital tools and professional etiquette. This pathway equips learners with key strategies for online collaboration, professional emailing, and digital calendar use—essential for success in scientific outreach and academic engagement.
learning_objectives: Gain awareness of digital literacy practices for selecting and using appropriate online tools to organize, communicate, and collaborate within scientific and academic contexts.
Recognize effective email communication techniques for initiating and maintaining respectful contact with academic and research professionals.
Understand best practices for virtual communication to maintain professionalism and clarity in online stakeholder engagements.
Explore digital collaboration strategies that support efficient teamwork in STEM through shared documents, video conferencing, and calendar tools.

pathways.title: Navigating the Social Media Landscape
pathways.description: Billions of people use social media worldwide, making it a powerful tool for science communication and public engagement. This pathway explores the role of social media in business, how to develop your scientific voice online, the power of storytelling in science, and strategies for navigating digital content critically.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of social media in STEM fields by identifying how digital platforms influence public engagement and science communication.
Recognize effective strategies for building a science communication presence online, including tone, storytelling, and audience interaction.
Identify challenges and opportunities in navigating digital information environments, with attention to misinformation and responsible content sharing.
Explore the impact of neuroscience storytelling in public education by examining narrative techniques used to connect science with everyday experiences.

pathways.title: Investigating Sea Level For Teachers
pathways.description: This pathway, for grades 6-8, provides a step-by-step guide for teaching students about sea level using real data, with five lessons ranging from 45 to 90 minutes each. It covers key concepts such as calculating mean sea level, analyzing satellite altimetry, exploring tide data, investigating storm impacts, and designing independent research projects. Designed to build data literacy and critical thinking skills, the pathway supports student inquiry at multiple levels.
subject_tags: Environmental Science:Climate Change and Global Warming
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to calculate the mean sea level using real data and interpret deviations from the mean.
Analyze satellite data to identify global variations in sea level and construct explanations for these variations.
Generate and interpret tide charts, calculating means and describing tidal patterns.
Analyze tide data to understand storm impacts on coastal sea levels.
Design and conduct a research investigation using real sea level data to answer a student-developed question.

pathways.title: Ethics Throughout the Data Science Cycle
pathways.description: Data can reveal powerful truths—but only if used responsibly. This pathway explores the ethical side of data science, including how to collect, analyze, and report data while respecting privacy, ensuring fairness, and avoiding bias. You'll discover how ethical practices help build trust and protect people in an increasingly data-driven world. This pathway is Chapter 8 of the Principles of Data Science textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define  key terms such as data privacy, informed consent, pseudonymization, k-anonymity, data governance, and intellectual property in the context of data science.
Recall major data privacy regulations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, FERPA) and their primary functions.
Explain the importance of ethical considerations in each stage of the data science lifecycle.
Use appropriate tools (e.g., Google Colab, Tableau) to securely share and visualize ethically collected and processed data.
Differentiate between anonymized and pseudonymized data, and assess when each is appropriate.

pathways.title: Females Singing to Be Heard: Challenging Long-Held Assumptions About Birdsong Through Data Visualization
pathways.description:
Students will learn how data visuals tell a story. They will analyze real bird song data to try to understand why female singing behavior has been overlooked in one of the most common birds in the world. They will also gain a deeper insight into the scientific peer review process. This pathway is aimed at Grades 5-8 and 9-12 and consists of three 45-minute activities.
Driving Question(s):
What is the best way to visualize data?
Essential Question(s):
• How do our assumptions (biases) affect our conclusions?
• What are the pros and cons of scatterplots vs. histograms?
Hook(s):
This lesson focuses a lot on puzzles called ciphers and uses a goofy video showing connections between histograms and dot plots to engage students.
Explore the research background, learning standards, and credits that shape this pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, students will be able to define key terms such as scatterplot, histogram, bias, and peer review.
Recall the main findings of the bird song study regarding male and female singing behaviors.
Explain how data visualization (scatterplots and histograms) tells different stories about the same dataset.
Compare and contrast the advantages and disadvantages of scatterplots versus histograms for representing bird song data.
Develop an argument explaining how biases can lead to overlooked phenomena in scientific research, supported by examples from the bird song study.

pathways.title: Enterprise Security, Data Privacy, and Risk Management
pathways.description: Did you know your smart fridge could be hacked? As more devices connect to the internet, protecting personal data becomes more challenging—and more important. This pathway explores how organizations defend against digital threats, follow global privacy laws like GDPR, and take responsibility for keeping your information safe. This pathway aligns with Chapter 6 of the Foundations of Information Systems textbook.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define key concepts in data privacy and data security, including their importance for enterprise trust, compliance, and risk reduction.
Identify common vulnerabilities in web applications and IoT technologies and match them with corresponding threats and technical countermeasures.
Analyze global frameworks such as GDPR, ISO/IEC 27701, and NIST, and compare how they are applied across industries and regions.
Evaluate enterprise policies and protocols to determine alignment with global data protection frameworks, and develop strategies for implementing and monitoring compliance improvements.

pathways.title: Can Brain Cooling Revolutionise Stroke Treatment?
pathways.description: A stroke is a life-threatening condition in which oxygen is prevented from reaching the brain, causing brain cells to die. At Western University in Canada, Dr Ting-Yim Lee, a biomedical engineer, has teamed up with Dr Marat Slessarev, a clinical doctor, to develop an innovative new device that can cool a stroke patient’s brain, thereby reducing the amount of damage caused.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to define cerebral blood flow, metabolic coupling, and metabolic processes in the context of brain function.
Recall the role of randomized controlled trials in testing medical interventions.
Explain how brain cooling can help stroke patients by reducing metabolic processes and inflammation.
Describe the function of the Vortex tube IntraNasal Cooling Instrument (VINCI) in regulating brain temperature.
Compare the survival rates of stroke-induced pigs with and without brain cooling treatment.

pathways.title: Looking to the Future With Edge Computing
pathways.description: Edge computing ‘brings the cloud closer’ – enabling small and simple technological devices to perform complex functions. Professor Mahadev Satyanarayanan, at Carnegie Mellon University in the US, and Professor Nigel Davies, at Lancaster University in the UK, are working on making this life-changing technology part of our everyday reality.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to define key concepts in computer science, including bandwidth, cloud computing, edge computing, Internet of Things (IoT), latency, and cognitive assistance.
Summarize the differences between cloud computing and edge computing, including their respective advantages and limitations.
Describe the potential applications of edge computing, such as wearable cognitive assistance and technology-mediated serendipity.
Compare and contrast the performance of cloud computing and edge computing in terms of latency, bandwidth, and suitability for real-time applications.

pathways.title: Growing New and Beginning Farmers
pathways.description: Beginning a career in farming is not simple or straightforward. As well as learning how to care for crops and livestock, farmers need to have solid business acumen, understand complex regulations, and know how to make their practices as environmentally friendly as possible. Based at Alabama A&M University’s Small Farms Research Center, in the US, E’licia L. Chaverest heads up the ‘New and Beginning Farmers Program’, which supports projects that educate and train farmers in how to run an effective and sustainable farming operation.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway, learners will be able to identify key challenges faced by new and beginning farmers, including access to resources, legal issues such as heir property, and financial literacy barriers.
Explain the significance of supporting minority, socially disadvantaged, and veteran farmers in agriculture.
Apply farm planning techniques to develop a sustainable agricultural business model.
Evaluate the impact of heir property issues and legal barriers on the agricultural industry, particularly for historically underserved farmers.

pathways.title: Astro-Architects: Crafting Capsules for Cosmic Comfort and Safety
pathways.description:
Explore NASA's space capsules, dive into recovery techniques, and unleash your creativity by designing and building an interactive model of your very own futuristic astronaut capsule in this learning pathway with the accompanying Student Guide. The recommended learning time spans 5 days of lessons (190-210 minutes).
Pathway breakdown:
• ENGAGE: Day 1, 40 minutes, items 1-2 of the pathway.
• EXPLORE: Day 2, 40-60 minutes, items 3-8 of the pathway.
• EXPERIMENT: Day 3 & 4, 60 minutes, items 9-10 of the pathway.
• EXPLAIN: Day 4 & 5, 40 minutes, found in the Student Guide.
• EXTEND: 10 minutes, item 11 of the pathway.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to collect and analyze data about NASA current capsule designs.
You will engage with virtual experiments to enhance critical thinking skills.
You will be able to explain the complexity of space travel by accurately creating a model of a space capsule.

pathways.title: How Can First-Year STEM University Students Be Better Supported?
pathways.description: Going to university is a huge change from going to high school. At the University of Pittsburgh at Greensburg in the US, biologists Barbara Barnhart and Dr Olivia Long are using their Science Seminar programme to ease this transition for first year students studying biology, chemistry and biochemistry degrees.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway students will gain insights into the process of career planning in the STEM field, understanding the importance of early preparation and informed decision-making.
Learners will be able to apply the key learning strategies recommended by Dr Long and Barbara, such as active reading, question asking, and reflection on biases, in their own academic pursuits.
Educators will be prepared with strategies to stimulate active student participation in class discussions and debates, thereby enhancing student engagement and learning outcomes.

pathways.title: How Can Data Management Tools Help Us Discover New Treatments for a Chronic Disease?
pathways.description: Myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) is a complex disease that can take a huge toll on a person’s everyday life. There is still much we do not know about the disease, and researchers are working hard to learn more about what causes it. Dr Linda Brown, an epidemiologist, and Dr Megan Carnes, a genomics research scientist, from RTI International in the US have developed a suite of data management tools to help ME/CFS researchers to collaborate in the hope of finding new treatments.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway students will be able to explain the role of epidemiologists in studying the causes, distribution, control, and prevention of diseases.
Understand the significance of genomic science in studying an organism's genome, identifying new treatments or biomarkers, and enhancing our understanding of disease biology.
Learn about the importance of health literacy and effective communication in improving health outcomes.
Learn about the significance of data sharing and analysis in scientific research, using the example of the mapMECFS platform.

Participate in activities that prompt them to research a specific disease, its public health impact, and the role of epidemiology in addressing it, as well as engage in career exploration by reaching out to a professional in a field of interest.

pathways.title: Mech-Ing Endoscopies Safer by Using Robots You Can Swallow
pathways.description: Dr Mark Rentschler, based at the University of Colorado Boulder in the US, leads a team focused on developing mobile robotic capsule endoscopes. These robots could improve procedures for patients and save lives
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will understand the cutting-edge development of mobile robotic capsule endoscopes for surgical procedures in the digestive system.
Explore the potential impact of autonomous capsule robots on patient care and treatment of diseases like colorectal cancer.
Recognize the interdisciplinary nature of mechanical engineering, its challenges, and its applications in the medical field.

pathways.title: How One Massive Study Is Uncovering the Secrets of UK Society
pathways.description: The Understanding Society project, run by the University of Essex in the UK, is a gigantic long term study that interviews households yearly to explore a huge array of life experiences. Dr Alita Nandi and Nicole D. James both work on the project, ensuring the substantial quantities of data collected can be used by researchers to shed light on societal trends within the UK.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway students will be able to analyze the importance of longitudinal studies in understanding societal trends and their impact on well-being and equality.
Students will be able to differentiate between the two approaches, understand their respective uses in research, and evaluate the strengths and limitations of each approach.
Assess the significance of large sample sizes and diversity in research, explain the importance of anonymizing data in research ethics, and apply research findings to real-world scenarios.

pathways.title: Care at a Distance: Adapting During the Pandemic
pathways.description: The COVID-19 pandemic has tested healthcare facilities to their limits. One effect has been a transition towards remote consultations, which raises unique challenges for accurate diagnoses. Based at the University of Oxford’s Medical Sciences Division, in the UK, Professor Trish Greenhalgh and PHD student Laiba Husain have been developing methods to ensure remote care is as effective as possible.
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway you will understand the challenges and strategies involved in adapting primary healthcare to remote consultations, including the development of assessment tools and data analysis techniques

You will gain an understanding of the role of research, data analysis, collaboration, and patient partnership in improving healthcare delivery, and explore the potential future developments in integrated care.

You will critically evaluate the ethical considerations, challenges, and potential benefits associated with remote primary care and examine the implications of the COVID-19 pandemic on healthcare practices.

pathways.title: What if We Could Develop a Sensor That Can Detect Pollutants Like Heavy Metals?
pathways.description: Dr Helen Bridle, an associate professor at Heriot-Watt University, is conducting research on developing novel sensors to explore the relationship between antimicrobial resistance and pollutants in India. Her team hopes to understand how pollutants like heavy metals and additives impact antibiotic resistance in the water environment to find solutions for minimizing AMR, a serious global public health threat.
learning_objectives: By the end of the pathway you will be able to describe the concept of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and explain why it is a significant global health issue.
You will be able to analyze the factors contributing to the heavy usage of antibiotics in India, including lack of regulation and awareness.
Explain how water, particularly wastewater, plays a role in the spread and transfer of AMR, and how pollutants like heavy metals and additives may contribute to AMR.
Identify the novel sensor technologies being used by Dr. Helen Bridle's team to detect and monitor pollutants in water, and explain how these sensors work.
